Her kindness further charmed Soulanges, especially after the misery he had endured at the ball. He took her hand and kissed it gratefully, for gratitude often accompanies love.“Hortense, what is that on your finger that hurt my lip?” he asked, laughing. “It’s my diamond, which you said you lost, and which I have found.”General Montcornet did not marry Madame de Vaudremont, despite their brief understanding; she became one of the victims of the tragic fire that marred the ball hosted by the Austrian ambassador for Napoleon’s marriage to the daughter of Emperor Joseph II.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################In September 1835 Mademoiselle du Rouvre, the only daughter of the Marquis du Rouvre, married Comte Adam Mitgislas Laginski, a Polish exile. Though her father had squandered much of his fortune, Clémentine remained a substantial heiress through her mother’s family; relatives’ settlements secured her a steady income upon marriage.Comte Adam, while exiled, was not a charge on the French government. He came from an illustrious Polish family but met prejudice in Paris, where Polish nobility was often poorly regarded. At first he appeared a student enjoying Parisian life after losing political prospects. The Polish community in France was split between republican and noble exiles, complicating social relations and making Adam the subject of Parisian jokes and speculation about his wealth and status.In 1834 Adam bought a house in the rue de la Pépinière, and by 1836 he had established himself in Parisian society, having fallen in love with Clémentine. Their marriage elevated his standing and he became seen as a fashionable figure. The couple’s home was luxurious, blending architectural styles and filled with fine decor, reflecting the tastes and resources of the rising bourgeoisie.Clémentine, now a countess, noticed Captain Paz, a factotum who managed much of their affairs while remaining largely unseen. She questioned Adam about Paz, who handled everything from finances to the care of horses. Adam explained that Paz was a nobleman from a poor branch of the Paz family who had fought valiantly for Poland and become a close friend through shared hardship.Paz’s loyalty and discretion were evident; he ran the household with remarkable efficiency. Adam admired Paz’s character, saying their friendship rested on mutual respect and understanding. Despite social hierarchy, Adam regarded Paz as an equal, their bond strengthened by shared experiences. Paz once declined money Adam offered, underlining their deep friendship. As the couple prepared for a day out, Clémentine’s affection for both men grew. The narrative highlights loyalty, friendship, and the complexities of social status in 19th‑century Paris, showing how personal connections can defy societal norms.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Paz (Thaddeus) lived reclusively in Paris until the Comtesse Laginska, Clementine, was seen riding with him in the Bois de Boulogne. She insisted he dine with her and Adam; Thaddeus, dressed for the occasion, behaved like a subordinate—listening more than speaking. Adam urged him to be livelier, but Thaddeus’s reserve concealed a deeper struggle: he was in love with Clementine and tried to hide it.After dinner they went to the opera, where Thaddeus remained detached. Clementine, offended by his lack of engagement, complained; Adam excused him as obdurate. Inwardly tormented by unrequited love, Thaddeus’s longing only deepened when Clementine returned from a trip. He took a bittersweet satisfaction in being indispensable to their household—secure that their domestic life depended on his management—even as that role heightened his private yearning.Clementine, growing more beautiful and confident in Parisian society, focused on her status and left little room for thoughts of Thaddeus. Still, she occasionally noticed him—once inviting him into their carriage, an act that flustered him and charged their brief interactions with unspoken feeling. Thaddeus took solace in his duties and the stability they provided, yet his devotion remained a source of inner turmoil. He reflected on the nature of his love: profound, unrewarded, and sustained by watching Clementine’s happiness from a distance.At one reception Clementine hosted, Thaddeus’s absence passed unnoticed; he returned to solitary habits, content with silent devotion but aware his love was both joy and suffering. He admired her as she sought attention, contended with rivals, and often neglected Adam. Throughout, Thaddeus quietly sustained their household while privately enduring the ache of desire.In May, as Clementine prepared to go to her uncle’s estate, she saw Thaddeus again and, on impulse, insisted he join them for dinner. He hesitated but came. Trying to be more engaging, he was still hampered by shyness and duty; yet Clementine began to view him differently. Their conversation grew more intimate, and moments bristled with possibility. Still, loyalty to Adam restrained Thaddeus from acting on his feelings. After the meal Clementine voiced discontent with Adam and a wish for greater passion. Thaddeus wanted to confess but held back, sensing the peril such an admission posed to their complicated triangle.To disguise his true attachment, Thaddeus invented a story of being in love with a circus performer named Malaga—a graceful, captivating figure who represented a simpler, more honest affection than the polished Parisian world. The tale let him express longing without betraying Clementine. His visits to Malaga became a refuge from his unfulfilled passion: he provided for her and ensured her comfort, yet the relationship never deepened into genuine romance.As Malaga’s circumstances changed, she grew suspicious of Thaddeus’s motives, and misunderstandings followed. Despite the ambiguity, he remained her benefactor out of compassion and perhaps a need to enact a love that could not be declared. Then Malaga sent him a heartfelt letter pleading over their separation and yearning for his presence. Her despair struck Thaddeus; the letter echoed the same ache he felt for Clementine and crystallized his realization: his devotion to Clementine was unwavering, and he would remain torn among silent adoration, practical duty, and the sacrifices demanded by the intertwined lives around him.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Clementine returned home, and Paz saw her again, more beautiful than ever. After dinner the count and countess discussed Thaddeus, who had left a letter from Malaga with Adam. Adam lamented Thaddeus’s misfortune, fearing he would lose everything because of his involvement with a circus performer. Clementine expressed disdain for such women, while Adam defended Thaddeus, noting his financial sacrifices for Malaga.Paz, who had asked Adam for money to help Malaga, was seen by the countess as a potential ruin for Adam. Adam believed Thaddeus would eventually leave Malaga if it served his interests; the countess remained skeptical.Days later Paz noticed a change in Clementine’s behavior toward him and felt her contempt, which plunged him into despair. Convinced he had caused her to despise him, he nevertheless found solace in silently admiring her at social events and dedicating himself to her well-being and the household. His love was profound, yet he felt unworthy of her respect. He fell ill from this emotional turmoil but eventually recovered.Meanwhile Adam lost a significant sum gambling and asked Thaddeus to conceal it by attributing the loss to Malaga. Thaddeus reluctantly agreed, burdened by obligation. When Clementine discovered Thaddeus’s financial generosity toward Paz she expressed frustration, questioning his loyalty. Feeling the weight of her expectations, Thaddeus resolved to manage her affairs more effectively and began teaching her household management so she could run things independently.As spring approached, Adam fell seriously ill, and Thaddeus devoted himself to nursing him. Clementine, aware of Adam’s precarious condition, sought the best medical care. Thaddeus’s dedication was unwavering; through attentive care he managed to keep the count alive. After weeks of nursing Adam’s condition improved and Thaddeus received praise, yet he was tormented by his feelings for Clementine and by the certainty that he could never have her. He decided to leave for the Caucasus, believing it the only escape from unrequited love.Before departing Thaddeus wrote a heartfelt letter to Clementine, confessing his deep feelings and the sacrifices he had made for her happiness, and acknowledging that remaining in Paris would only prolong his pain. Clementine, on receiving the letter, reflected on her feelings and the loss of a devoted friend. Adam, unaware of the full extent of Thaddeus’s sacrifices, lamented the loss, believing their bond strong enough to withstand trials.In the years that followed Clementine often thought of Thaddeus, wondering about his fate. She remained unaware of the depth of his devotion, while he continued to live in the shadows, forever changed by his love. Over time Clementine became involved in a new romantic entanglement, but the memory of Thaddeus lingered as a bittersweet reminder of a love that could never be.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################The Marquise de Listomere embodies the Restoration spirit, balancing religious devotion with worldly elegance. Married seven years to the unremarkable Marquis de Listomere, she maintains a virtuous facade—perhaps to support his political ambitions. While some women defer judgment until he becomes a peer, the marquise’s conduct remains strictly principled.The marquis is notable for his lack of qualities rather than any merit. A silent deputy who votes correctly but lacks charisma, he is nicknamed “dull weather.” His unremarkable nature is seen as a safe match for a virtuous woman, since he is incapable of folly.Despite her reserve, the marquise commands both respect and scorn from men who attempt to flirt; she returns only contempt. She converses with agreeable men without arousing gossip, unlike those who pursue ulterior motives. She does not affect coyness; she is genuinely principled.The narrator admires her presence: neither plain nor conventionally pretty, she has a captivating air. Her animated eyes reveal a deeper soul, and she charms without seeking it. This quality shows in her recent encounter with Eugène de Rastignac, a clever young man who combines modesty with liveliness.Rastignac, adept at social navigation, speaks with the marquise at a ball on topics from opera to women’s duties. Their exchange indicates mutual interest, though he abruptly leaves her side afterward. The next morning, writing letters in a comfortable room, Rastignac finds himself preoccupied with thoughts of her and increasingly absorbed in feelings that verge on romance, before his friend Horace interrupts a conversation that ranges as far as a possible expedition to Morea.While dressing, the marquise receives a letter from Rastignac. It begins with affectionate terms, stirring curiosity about his intent. As she reads, an initial impulse to discard it yields to a desire to understand his sentiments. The letter’s conclusion leaves her fatigued; she reflects on Rastignac’s impertinence and the implications of his words.The episode captures the tension between her virtuous exterior and stirrings of emotion within, setting up the complexities of her character and relationships.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Madame de Listomere forbade her porter to admit Monsieur de Rastignac and resolved to treat him with more than disdain; his recent insolence exceeded previous offenses she had tolerated. She had considered keeping a letter he sent but ultimately burned it. "Madame received a fine love-letter," Caroline remarked to the housemaid, who was surprised.That evening Madame de Listomere went to a party at the Marquis de Beauseant expecting Rastignac; by two in the morning she realized he would not come, despite intending to confront him with coldness. Stendhal called this mental process "crystallization."Four days later Rastignac scolded his valet, Joseph, for misplacing letters. Joseph, distracted, finally remembered one was for Madame de Listomere and the other for Rastignac's lawyer. When Rastignac learned the letter had not been for Madame de Nucingen, he laughed at his own wandering heart.Eugène’s errors began when he planned to amuse Madame de Listomere by discussing the mix-up and worsened them by staying away for several days, letting her resentment harden.When he finally called, the porter said Madame de Listomere was out. The Marquis, unaware of tensions, invited him in. As they climbed the stairs Rastignac noted several social faux pas. On seeing him, Madame de Listomere blushed; he thought, "What! that fortress, too?" and adjusted his cravat. Monsieur de Listomere remained absorbed in his newspaper, oblivious.Madame de Listomere quickly composed herself. When Eugène asked after her health she assured him she was well. He suggested his visit might be unwelcome and blamed the marquis's introduction; she replied that her husband did not know all her secrets, hinting at their complicated intimacy.Eugène tried to lighten the mood, suggesting silence as his best excuse. She promised to forget, keeping her dignity. When he mentioned the letter she feigned offense but could not hide amusement. He insisted he meant no harm and that the letter was not for her; she pretended to be hurt but warned him against further mistakes.Monsieur de Listomere, still engrossed in his paper, interrupted to announce a mutual acquaintance's death. The ensuing talk exposed the marquise's irritation with Eugène’s earlier remarks. He naively suggested she might be trying to extract a secret from him, which only increased her curiosity.When he named Madame de Nucingen her demeanor shifted: she rang for wood, signaling his visit should end, and coldly confronted him about how his pen could have written her name. Discomfited, Eugène stammered and departed.Days later Madame de Listomere confirmed Eugène's claim about the letter's true recipient. She withdrew from society; the marquis explained her absence as a stomach ailment, while her physician diagnosed a nervous condition she used to justify seclusion. The episode underscores the complexities of social interaction and misunderstanding, especially in romantic contexts, and reveals characters' emotional reactions and social roles.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY CONT. ########################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################At Paris, balls often split into two gatherings: an official party of superficial pleasantries and a more intimate follow-up, where genuine conversation and laughter flourish. The latter, often hosted by women like Madame d’Espard and Mademoiselle des Touches, preserves the old spirit of French wit and allows free-flowing ideas and camaraderie.One evening at Mademoiselle des Touches' salon, notable figures including the Minister de Marsay gathered for lively discourse. The charged atmosphere showcased Parisian conversational style: anecdotes and confessions flowed, creating a vivid tapestry of social interaction.Conversation turned to de Marsay's early experiences with love, particularly a passionate affair with a woman six years his senior. He recounted youthful infatuation marked by intense devotion and secrecy; to shield their relationship he kept a façade of indifference. He described their careful dance of appearances and emotions—riding past her carriage and exchanging silent signals through the flowers she carried. Their bond thrived on mutual admiration and the thrill of concealment, while he remained blind to the possibility of betrayal.A turning point came when he fell ill and sent a note to cancel a meeting. Overcome with jealousy but determined, he visited her despite his condition and saw her with another man, which ignited a storm of feeling. Yet her calm demeanor and tender concern briefly soothed his doubts when they met.His narrative then darkened as he grappled with betrayal, feeling a profound internal shift—the end of innocence and the rise of a more cynical view of love. He reflected on revenge and passion’s complexities, ultimately asserting that true love should transcend the desire for vengeance.De Marsay's story resonated with his audience, prompting reflection on love, jealousy, and the societal expectations that shape relationships; it served as a poignant reminder of trust’s fragility and love’s transformative power, leaving a lasting impression.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Cured of my cold and my pure love, I embarked on an adventure with a charming heroine whose beauty sharply contrasted with that of my deceptive angel. I avoided quarrels with this clever woman, suspecting that true love might not equal the pleasures of her skilled deceit; such refined hypocrisy can be as good as virtue.I sought a skilled artist in the Rue Boucher to make a keepsake from my hair for my new angel. He had a monopoly on hair mementos and showed me work surpassing fairy tales, explaining trends like marking linen with hair. When I recognized a handkerchief he had worked on, my doubts about the authenticity of women’s affections were confirmed. I left the shop still believing in pleasure but skeptical about love.Two months later I was in the boudoir of this ethereal being, holding her hand as we explored sentiment. In a tender moment I seized the chance to elicit her most delightful lies. Charlotte professed her love, claiming I was the only man in her world and that she had been seeking ways to bind me to her eternally.When I asked plainly, “When are you to marry the Duke?” her startled reaction revealed guilt. She feigned astonishment, but I pressed on, suggesting she should marry him since he was rich and devout. Charlotte fell to her knees, insisting she loved no one but me and swore before God. I confessed my doubts about her fidelity; she quickly turned the accusation on me, claiming I no longer loved her. She sought to escape, but curiosity made her ask what I had seen. I revealed that I had noticed her attending mass with the Duke several times, which she dismissed as jealousy.I argued her marriage to the Duke would elevate her socially and advised her to embrace the role; the Duke was virtuous and would expect a prude. Charlotte, in tears, lamented that if I had spoken sooner we could have been happy. I maintained it was too late; she had gone too far with the Duke. She worried about the Duke’s suspicions; I reassured her he likely had none, though she needed to prove her devotion. As she paced the boudoir her demeanor shifted: she held out her hand, called me noble and charming, and vowed never to forget me.Her tactics were admirable, and I played the distressed lover. She then asked what would become of me, and I revealed advances to another woman. This startled Charlotte, who accused me of unfaithfulness; I countered that she had too much wit for her heart to suffer. Charlotte declared disillusionment with love, claiming women could never be truly loved. I acknowledged her anger but argued that treating love as a solemn bond in society was like romanticizing an actress. She accused me of betrayal; I feigned submission and asked if she would remember her grievances once she became a duchess. Her bitterness confirmed she still felt wronged, and I realized I had successfully distanced myself. I had studied the lives of successful men with women and believed I had mastered the art of retreat.The women in the room reacted with sympathy for Charlotte, while de Marsay wore a slight smile. The conversation shifted to the decline of social distinctions. Lady Dudley noted women often did not view their marriages as acts of inconstancy. De Marsay lamented the erosion of the aristocracy, saying duchesses and marquises were disappearing and the Civil Code had leveled hierarchies.Blondet remarked that aristocracy now began with viscountesses, while de Marsay thought countesses might survive in some form. He reflected on how the last great ladies had vanished with the splendor of the previous century, and the Code had inadvertently created a new class of women. The Comte de Vandenesse observed that the current structure allowed anyone to claim nobility; Lord Dudley added that property had not been organized, leading to the aristocracy’s decline.Joseph Bridau said the beauty of womanhood had diminished and the great lady’s fan was broken. Daniel d’Arthez noted the aristocracy had retreated, leaving behind women who might once have shaped society. The conversation turned to the modern “perfect lady,” a product of the era: she embodied good taste and grace but lacked the grandeur of past great ladies.Mademoiselle des Touches questioned the notion of progress, while Madame de Nucingen argued a woman’s worth was no longer tied solely to lineage but to character and presentation. Count Adam Laginski asked what constituted a “perfect lady.” Émile Blondet described her as someone who exudes elegance and refinement, effortlessly commanding respect and admiration—graceful and poised in various social settings.The discussion highlighted the complexities of modern femininity, where the “perfect lady” navigates societal expectations with skill and charm, blending beauty and intellect to captivate those around her.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“Now, you will not talk for half an hour with a bourgeoise without her mentioning her husband,” Blondet observed gravely. “Yet a married lady will hide her husband so skillfully you might never find him. You may only see her glance at a middle-aged man with a decoration who bows and goes; she has ordered her carriage and departs.“You are not the rose, but you have been with the rose, and you go to bed under a golden canopy of a delightful dream that may linger even after Sleep has opened the ivory gates of dreams.“At home she sees no one before four; she keeps you waiting. Her house is tasteful; her luxury is daily and renewed. There are no dusty glass shades or rags—warm stairs, flowers everywhere—flowers being the only permissible gift, and only from certain people since they mean a promise. Fashionable trifles are scattered, but not museum-fashion. She sits by the fire in a low chair and does not rise to greet you. At the ball she was our creditor; at home she owes you the pleasure of her wit. These are her arts. She seeks in you someone to expand her sphere, an object for her attention. To lure you into her drawing-room she is enchantingly charming. This shows how isolated women are and why they want a little world where they can shine like constellations. Conversation tends to generalities.“Yes,” de Marsay agreed, “you have found the flaw of our age. The epigram—an entire volume in a word—no longer attacks people or things but petty events that fade quickly.”“Hence,” Blondet went on, “a lady’s intelligence, if she has any, lies in casting doubt on everything. Here is the contrast between two women: the townswoman is virtuous, the lady hesitates and wrestles with virtue. That uncertainty is one of the last graces left her. She hardly goes to church but will discuss religion; if you show free thought, she’ll try to convert you with a stock of platitudes understood by these women: ‘For shame! I thought you had too much sense to attack religion. Society totters, and you deprive it of support. Religion is property and the future of our children! Individualism is the disease of the age, religion the only remedy; it unites families your laws sunder,’ and so on. She then glides into a neo-Christian, politically tinged discourse that is neither Catholic nor Protestant—but very moral—a muddle of modern doctrines at odds with each other.”The women laughed at Blondet’s satire.“This explanation, Count Adam,” Blondet told the Pole, “shows that the ‘perfect lady’ embodies both intellectual and political muddle, surrounded by flashy, ephemeral products of an industry that replaces itself. When you leave her you think: she certainly has superior ideas! You believe it because she has probed your heart delicately and asked your secrets; she feigns ignorance to learn everything. There are things she never knows even when she does. You alone feel uneasy, knowing nothing of her heart. The great ladies of old flaunted affairs; today’s lady arranges her passions like a musical score, with rests and notes, avoiding anything that would compromise love, her husband, or her children’s future. Name, place, and fortune no longer shield her; the aristocracy no longer protects. She lacks the lofty antagonism of the past; instead of crushing, she risks being crushed. She masters Jesuitical compromises between propriety and anonymous passion. She fears her servants as an Englishwoman fears a divorce trial. So free at a ball, charming in public, she is a slave at home; she is never independent except in secrecy or in theory. Her task is to remain a lady.“For today, a repudiated woman, reduced to a meager allowance without a carriage, luxury, or opera box is neither wife nor maid nor townswoman; she drifts into chattelhood. The Carmelites will not take a married woman; it would be bigamy. Would her lover want her then? That is the question. Thus your perfect lady may invite calumny but not slander.”“It is all horribly true,” said the Princesse de Cadignan.“And so,” Blondet continued, “our ‘perfect lady’ stands between English hypocrisy and eighteenth-century frankness—a bastard system symptomatic of an age where nothing that emerges resembles what has vanished, where transition leads nowhere and everything is graduated; the great figures fade into the background, and distinction becomes merely personal. I doubt any woman, even near a throne, can, before twenty-five, acquire that encyclopedic knowledge of trifles, the practice of maneuvering, the small yet important arts—the musical tones and harmony of dress, angelic mischief and innocent cunning, speech and silence, wit and obtuseness, diplomacy and ignorance—that make a perfect lady.”“And where,” Mademoiselle des Touches asked, “would you place the female author? Is she a perfect lady, a woman comme il faut?”“When she has no genius, she is a woman comme il n’en faut pas,” Blondet replied, looking in a way that might praise Camille Maupin. “That epigram is not mine but Napoleon’s.”“You need not hate Napoleon for that,” Canalis interposed. “He was weakly jealous of literary genius—he had his faults. Who can explain Napoleon? A man with arms folded who did everything—the greatest force, most concentrated, most caustic; a singular will who spread armed civilization without fixing it. He could do everything because he willed everything: a prodigious phenomenon of will, conquering illness by battle yet destined to die sick in bed; a man with a code and a sword in his brain, word and deed; a clear spirit seeing everything but his own fall; a capricious politician risking men, yet sparing three diplomats—Talleyrand, Pozzo, Metternich—whose deaths might have saved the Empire. Nature gave him, as a rare gift, a heart in a bronze frame; mirthful and kind among women at midnight, the next morning he moved Europe as a girl might splash water. Hypocritical and generous; liking tawdriness and simplicity; without taste, yet protecting the arts; truly great in everything by temperament: Caesar at twenty-five, Cromwell at thirty. Then, like my grocer in Père Lachaise, a good husband and father. He improvised public works, empires, kings, codes, verses, a romance—with breadth over precision. He aimed to make all Europe France; having changed laws of gravitation, he left us poorer. He who took an empire by his name lost it at its frontier in blood. A man of thought and action who understood Desaix and Fouché.”“All despotism and all justice at the right moments. The true king!” de Marsay declared.“Ah! what a pleasure to digest what you talk about,” Baron de Nucingen remarked.“But is our banquet of talk a common one?” Joseph Bridau asked. “If you paid for the charms of conversation as for music or dancing, your fortune would be inadequate! There is no second performance of the same flash of wit.”“And are we really so diminished as these gentlemen think?” the Princesse de Cadignan asked the women with a skeptical smile. “Because under a regime that makes everything small you prefer small dishes, small rooms, small pictures, small articles, small newspapers, small books, does it follow that women have grown smaller? Why should the human heart change because you change your coat? Passions remain the same in all ages. I know of beautiful devotion and sublime sufferings that lack the publicity—the glory—that once lent luster to some women’s errors. But even without saving a king one is no less an Agnès Sorel. Do you think our Marquise d’Espard is inferior to Madame Doublet or Madame du Deffand, in whose rooms so much evil was done? Is not Taglioni a match for Camargo? Malibran for Saint-Huberti? Are our poets not superior to those of the eighteenth century? If now, under merchant rule, we lack a defining style, did the Empire not have its stamp as Louis XV’s age did, and was its splendor not fabulous? Have the sciences lost anything?”“I agree, madame; the women of this age are truly great,” Comte de Vandenesse replied. “When posterity looks back, will not Madame Récamier appear as fine as any past beauty? We have made so much history that historians will be lacking. The age of Louis XIV had but one Madame de Sévigné; we have a thousand in Paris who write better privately than she published. Whether called ‘perfect lady’ or great lady, she will always be the woman among women.“Blondet painted the fascinations of a woman of the day; but when needed, this creature who bristles or shows off, who echoes Mr. This and Mr. That, can be heroic. Your faults, mesdames, are more poetic because they are surrounded by greater perils. I have seen much—perhaps too late—but when the illegality of your feelings might be excused, I always saw what you call Providence inevitably overwhelming those we deem light women.”“I hope,” Madame de Vandenesse said, “that we can be great in other ways—”“Oh, let the Comte preach to us!” Madame de Serizy cried.“With all the more reason because he has preached by example,” the Baronne de Nucingen added.“On my honor!” General de Montriveau said, “in all the dramas—a word you like,” he added, glancing at Blondet—“where God’s finger has been visible, the most frightful I ever knew was almost by my act—”“Tell us!” Lady Barimore cried. “I love to shudder!”“It is the taste of a virtuous woman,” de Marsay observed, looking at Lord Dudley’s lovely daughter.“During the 1812 campaign,” General de Montriveau began, “I was the involuntary cause of a terrible disaster which may interest Doctor Bianchon, since while you study the body, you care for the mind; it may help solve will’s problems.“I was on my second campaign, enjoying danger like a young foolish lieutenant. At the Beresina the army lost discipline and obedience; it was a medley of nations making their way south. Men would drive off a general in rags if he brought no wood or food. After crossing the river, disorder worsened. I had wandered from Zembin’s marshes seeking shelter. Finding none, I finally saw a wretched little Polish farm—think of the meanest wooden houses of Lower Normandy. These dwellings were a single room, one end partitioned as a forage store.“In twilight I saw faint smoke; hoping to find kinder comrades, I approached. Inside, the table was laid. Several officers and a woman—common enough—were eating potatoes, some horseflesh grilled, frozen beetroots. I recognized artillery captains from my regiment. I was welcomed with shouts that would have amazed me on the other side of the Beresina; but at that moment the cold was less keen; my comrades had food, warmth, and straw; we asked for little then. I sat on a bundle of straw.“At the table’s end, by the door to the hay partition, sat my old colonel—one of the most extraordinary men I met among the mixed ranks. He was Italian. When the South is truly fine, it is sublime. His fairness under lamplight was exquisite. My colonel, like many officers borrowed from Eugene’s army, was tall—well proportioned, perhaps stout, but prodigiously powerful and greyhound-like. His abundant black curls contrasted with a white, almost feminine complexion; small hands, a shapely foot, a pleasant mouth, and an aquiline nose that would turn white with anger, which happened often. His irascibility was beyond belief; no one could remain calm near him. He had taken a fancy to me and thought all I did was right. When angry his brow knit into a horseshoe delta; the muscles quivered and his strength became boundless.“He spoke with a guttural roll that, though sometimes a grace, conveyed incredible force when excited. When calm his blue eyes were angelic and his brow charming. In parade he eclipsed others; even d’Orsay paled before him. Passion thrives on contrast, so you need not ask whether he was irresistible to women—he could be—but singularly he was not a lady-killer.“To show his violence: we once were dragging guns up a narrow road when another artillery regiment crossed us. Their colonel wanted our captain to give way; our captain refused. Their nearest gun wheel struck our captain’s right leg, breaking it and throwing him off his horse. Our Italian colonel, nearby, sensed a quarrel; he galloped among the guns, risking everything, and reached the captain shouting ‘Help!’ No, our colonel was no longer human—foam rose, he roared. Incapable of speaking, he beckoned his opponent into the wood and drew his sword. In two seconds the other colonel lay with his skull split. The soldiers of the opponent’s regiment backed away—yes, quickly.“The injured captain had an Italian wife, a beautiful Sicilian from Messina, who was not indifferent to our colonel. This aggravated his rage; he felt pledged to protect the husband as he would the woman.“In the Zembin hovel where I was received that night the captain sat opposite me; his wife sat at the other end facing the Colonel. Rosina was small, dark, with almond eyes full of Southern fire; though dust-covered and ragged, exhausted, she retained womanly graces—rose mouth, white teeth, an outline suggesting love despite misery. Rosina’s frame was fragile-looking but wiry. Her husband, a Piedmontese gentleman, had an ironical simplicity on his face that betrayed a quiet cruelty; a thin, restless underlip that turned down at the corners made me uneasy.“The conversation was flat when I arrived. We exchanged campaign misfortunes. After the Colonel finished he wiped his mustache, gave a black look at the Italian woman, said ‘Rosina?’ and went into the hay to sleep. The meaning was clear. The young wife’s gesture expressed the annoyance she could not show—an offense to her dignity and to her husband—but also a presentiment. She stayed seated.“A minute later, apparently when the Colonel was snug, he repeated, ‘Rosina?’ The tone was more brutally demanding. Rosina turned pale, rose, passed behind us, and went to him. We all sat in absolute silence; I began to laugh, and then they laughed too. ‘Tu ridi?—you laugh?’ said the husband. ‘On my honor, old comrade,’ I replied, serious again, ‘I confess I was wrong; I ask your pardon a thousand times, and if you are not satisfied with my apologies I am ready to give you satisfaction.’ ‘Oh! it is not you who are wrong; it is I!’ he answered coldly. We all lay down and slept.“Next morning each set out alone, with the selfishness that made our rout one of the most horrible dramas of self-seeking, melancholy, and horror. Yet about seven or eight hundred paces away we met again and walked on together like geese. Necessity drove us all.“At a knoll where we could still see the farmhouse we heard sounds like lions and bulls—a noise like no known cry—and a woman’s feeble scream. We looked around in terror; we no longer saw the house, only a huge bonfire. The farmhouse was barricaded and ablaze. Swirls of smoke carried hoarse cries and a pungent smell. A few yards behind, the captain quietly joined our caravan; we gazed at him in silence; he pointed to his breast with his right forefinger and, waving his left toward the fire, said, ‘Son’io.’ We all walked on without asking a word.”“There is nothing more terrible than the revolt of a sheep,” de Marsay remarked.“It would be frightful to leave with that vision in mind,” Madame de Montcornet said. “I shall dream of it—”“And what was the punishment of Monsieur de Marsay’s ‘First’?” Lord Dudley asked, smiling.“When the English jest, their foils have the buttons on,” Blondet replied.“Monsieur Bianchon can tell us, for he saw her dying,” de Marsay said, turning to me.“Yes,” I answered; “and her death was one of the most beautiful I ever witnessed. The Duke and I had spent the night by the dying woman’s pillow; pulmonary consumption in its last stage left no hope; she had taken the sacrament the day before. The Duke fell asleep. The Duchess woke about four, and with a friendly smile signed to me to let him rest while she faced death. She had grown incredibly thin, but her face kept its sublime outline; the pallor made her skin like porcelain lit from within. Her bright eyes contrasted with this languid beauty; her countenance showed expressive calm and pity for the Duke, a lofty tenderness that death seemed only to enlarge. The silence was complete; the room, softly lamp-lit, resembled any sickroom at the hour of death.“At that moment the clock struck. The Duke awoke, despairing at having slept through a few of the last moments; his gesture might have been misread by any but the dying woman. A busy statesman, always thinking of France’s interests, had strange odd ways that make genius seem mad to the unseeing. He sat by her, looked fixedly. The dying woman took his hand weakly and said in a low, agitated voice, ‘My poor dear, who is left to understand you now?’ Then she died looking at him.”“The stories the doctor tells always leave a deep impression,” Comte de Vandenesse remarked.“But a sweet one,” Mademoiselle des Touches said, rising.PARIS, June 1839–42.ADDENDUMThe following characters appear in other stories of the Human Comedy:Bianchon, Horace: Father Goriot; MIDDLE OF THE STORY. This marks the story's middle.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################“Ah! madame,” said the doctor, “I have appalling stories; but each has its proper hour—Chamfort told the Duc de Fronsac, ‘Between your sally and the present moment lie ten bottles of champagne.’”“But it is two in the morning, and Rosina’s story has prepared us,” said the mistress of the house.“Tell us, Monsieur Bianchon!” cried everyone.The doctor bowed and began.About a hundred paces from Vendôme, on the Loir, stands an old brown house with very high roofs, isolated without even a tannery or tavern nearby. In front is a garden sloping to the river; neat box borders and espaliers have run wild; willows in the stream half hide the house; fruit trees neglected for ten years no longer bear. An arbor’s wreck and a table remain. One wall bears a sundial with the motto, “Ultimam cogita.” The roof is dilapidated; shutters closed; balconies hung with swallows’ nests; doors forever shut. Weeds and rust mark the stonework; moon and weather have eaten the wood. Only birds, cats, polecats, rats, and mice disturb the silence. An invisible hand has written across it all: “Mystery.”From the street you see a large gate riddled with holes; through them the courtyard shows the same ruin: cracked walls, disjointed steps, broken gutters. What calamity befell it? What curse rests here? The empty house is an enigma.It was once a small fief called La Grande Bretèche. During the doctor’s stay in Vendôme, while attending a rich patient, the sight of the strange dwelling became one of his chief pleasures. It was better than a ruin: it contained an unrevealed thought. He often climbed the wild hedge into the ownerless garden, scratched by briars, lingering for hours. He loved to invent romances and melancholy to match the scene; had he known the real cause of the neglect, the unwritten poetry would have been lost. The house suggested provincial life, contemplative and grave-like; sometimes he imagined lepers there, sometimes the Atridae; he wept there; he never laughed.Often involuntary terrors seized him—a wood-pigeon’s hum above sounded ominous; damp earth and reptiles required caution; an icy chill could settle as if a hand had lain on his shoulder. One evening a weathercock’s creak made him shudder; finishing a gloomy drama, he returned to his inn lost in gloomy thoughts. After supper his hostess entered with an air of mystery and announced, “Monsieur, here is Monsieur Regnault.”“Who is Monsieur Regnault?” he asked.The hostess left and a tall, slim man in black appeared—hat in hand, like an usher, with a receding forehead, a small pointed head, and a colorless face. His coat was shabby, but he wore a diamond in his shirt frill and gold rings in his ears.“Monsieur,” the doctor asked, “whom have I the honor of addressing?”“Dear me, it is very cold. I am Monsieur Regnault,” the man answered, rubbing his hands. “I am notary at Vendôme.”The doctor encouraged himself to ask questions.Regnault spoke at length. He warned the doctor that walking in La Grande Bretèche’s garden constituted trespass and, as executor under the will of the late Comtesse de Merret, he begged him to discontinue the practice. Bound to the testatrix’s will, he could not allow repairs or entry. He had never set foot in the house since the will was read; they had merely verified doors and windows to assess taxes. “Ah! my dear sir,” he said, “her will made a great commotion in the town.”Regnault, once head-clerk in a Paris office ruined by bankruptcy, had come to Vendôme after failing to buy a Paris practice. He married into local ties; three months after being licensed he was summoned one evening by Madame la Comtesse de Merret to her château. She had been living at Merret after the Count went to Paris and died miserably two months before Regnault arrived. The Count and Countess had behaved eccentrically for months, admitting no visitors; Madame lived on the ground floor and Monsieur on the first; she was little seen except at church and had become altered. People whispered she was not quite right. When she left La Grande Bretèche for Merret she had dismantled the house and, it was said, burned furniture.Curiosity brought Regnault to the château that night. The maid had told him the Countess had received the Sacrament and might not live through the night. He crossed great, lofty, dark, cold rooms to the state bedroom. The chamber was old-fashioned, papered in brown, diabolically cold and thick with dust; a single old Argand lamp lighted it and a high tester bed hung with chintz dominated the scene. A small table bore an “Imitation of Christ” and the lamp; a deep armchair and two small chairs completed the scant furnishing. There was no fire. The room seemed a romance’s stage—funereal.Under the lamp, Madame de Merret lay in the bed. She was yellow as wax and narrow as two folded hands; her lace cap showed abundant but white hair. Sitting up with difficulty, her large black eyes were dimmed by fever; her forehead clammy; her fleshless hands like bones beneath soft skin—veins and muscles visible. She must once have been handsome; now she was a phantom. Those who wrapped her later said none had ever lived so emaciated. Her pale violet lips barely moved when she spoke.Although Regnault had often tended the dying, the lonely, silent woman in her vast château affected him deeply. He stood motionless, absorbed in her. At last her large eyes moved; with great effort she tried to raise her right hand and murmured in a breath, “I have waited for you with the greatest impatience.” A bright flush rose on her cheeks; speaking was a great effort.Regnault began to answer, but the housekeeper, leaning close, warned, “Do not speak; Madame la Comtesse is not in a state to bear the slightest noise.” He sat. After a few moments the Countess summoned strength to slide her right hand under the bolster and, with a last effort, withdrew a sealed paper, drops of perspiration on her brow. “I place my will in your hands—Oh! God! Oh!” Her hand seized a crucifix; she lifted it to her lips and died.The expression in her eyes haunted Regnault; joy had been in her last glance. He brought away the will. When opened, it named him executor and left the whole property to the Vendôme hospital except for a few legacies. As for La Grande Bretèche, it ordered that the house be left, for fifty years from her death, in the state it then occupied: absolutely no entry into the apartments, no repairs of any sort, and watchmen to enforce the decree if necessary. After fifty years, if her will has been faithfully executed, the house would become the property of Regnault’s heirs; otherwise it reverts to legal heirs only after complying with conditions in a codicil sealed until the expiration of the fifty years. The will had not been contested.Regnault’s recital made the doctor feel he still saw the dying woman. When asked whether he knew the motives behind the testatrix’s strange directions, Regnault, with comic reticence, refused to criticize a benefactor who had given him a diamond. Still, the doctor soon coaxed from him the town’s gossip and the judgments of Vendôme’s political sages—contradictory and diffuse opinions that nearly put him to sleep. The notary left pleased, making a wry joke on the stairs about living forty-five years longer and taking a pinch of snuff.Later, the innkeeper, Madame Lepas, a buxom Flemish woman with an innkeeper’s quick scrutiny, asked if Regnault had told the story of La Grande Bretèche. When the doctor repeated the tale, she leaned forward eagerly. He suspected she knew more than she first let on; she confessed she had a secret that tormented her conscience and wished to consult him, believing he was a man of judgment and incapable of betraying her. Though she swore she would not tell what could compromise him, she asked him to listen.Madame Lepas then related another thread of the story. When the Emperor sent Spaniards and prisoners to the region, she had been required to lodge, at the government’s expense, a young Spaniard on parole who had to present himself daily to the sub-prefect. He was a Spanish grandee—something like Bagos de Férédia, she recorded his name in her books. He was a handsome little man, barely five-foot-two or three, well made, with small, delicate hands he groomed carefully; thick black hair, a flame in his eye, a somewhat coppery complexion she admired despite the common prejudice against Spanish looks. He wore the finest linen she had ever seen—even finer than that of the nobles and princes she had lodged. He ate little but had polite, amiable ways. He spoke little and rarely answered, which she attributed to a Spanish mannerism.He read his breviary like a priest and attended mass regularly, and he always took a place within two yards of Madame de Merret’s chapel. At first no one suspected a purpose in this. He never looked up from his book. In the evenings he walked among the ruins of the old castle on the hill—his only amusement, which reminded him, people said, of his native Spain. “They say that Spain is all hills!” she concluded.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################One evening soon after he arrived he stayed out late. I was uneasy when he didn’t return until midnight, but we had learned to accept his whims; he took the key and we didn’t wait. He lived in a house we owned on Rue des Casernes. One of our stable-boys thought he had seen the Spanish Grandee swimming in the river. When he did come back, I warned him about the weeds, and he seemed annoyed at being seen.Eventually, one morning we discovered he hadn’t returned at all. Searching his belongings, I found a note in his drawer and fifty doubloons—about five thousand francs—plus a sealed box of diamonds worth ten thousand francs. The note said that if he didn’t come back, he left this money and the diamonds to fund masses in gratitude for his escape.My husband went looking for him. Curiously, he returned with the Spaniard’s clothes, found under a stone on a breakwater near La Grande Bretèche. He went early, unseen. After reading the letter, he burned the clothes and, following Count Férédia’s wishes, we announced the Spaniard had escaped.The sub-prefect sent the constabulary after him, but he was never caught. Lepas suspected the Spaniard had drowned. I, however, thought he was linked to the affair of Madame de Merret, especially since Rosalie told me the crucifix her mistress had cherished and been buried with was ebony and silver; early in his stay Monsieur Férédia had a similar crucifix, which I never saw again. Now, monsieur, should I feel remorse about the Spaniard’s fifteen thousand francs? Aren’t they truly mine?Certainly. But have you ever questioned Rosalie? I asked.“Oh, I have, sir. But what can be done? That girl is like a wall. She knows something, but it’s impossible to make her talk.”After a brief talk, my hostess left me with vague sinister thoughts, romantic curiosity, and a religious dread—the feeling one has in entering a dark church at night, with a faint light under a high vault. La Grande Bretèche—with its overgrown grass, shuttered windows, rusty ironwork, and empty rooms—appeared vividly. I felt compelled to explore that mysterious house to reach the heart of this solemn story, this drama that had claimed three lives.Rosalie became the most intriguing person in Vendôme to me. Watching her, I sensed something inward beneath her robust health. There was an element of sorrow or hope in her soul; her demeanor suggested a secret, like devout souls who pray incessantly or a woman who has killed her child and forever hears its last cry. Yet she was simple and clumsy, her vacant smile free of criminality; she looked innocent in her large checked kerchief tucked into a lilac-and-white-striped gown. I resolved not to leave Vendôme without learning La Grande Bretèche’s full story. To achieve this, I would make advances with Rosalie if necessary.“Rosalie!” I called one evening.“Your servant, sir?”“You’re not married?” She flinched.“Oh! There’s no shortage of men if I ever fancy being miserable!” she laughed, quickly regaining composure; every woman, from highest lady to inn-servant, has a natural presence of mind.“Yes; you’re attractive enough not to lack suitors! But tell me, Rosalie, why did you become an inn-servant after leaving Madame de Merret? Didn’t she leave you a small annuity?”“Oh yes, sir. But my position here is the best in all Vendôme.”Her reply seemed evasive. Rosalie appeared central to this romance; she seemed bound to its core. It wasn’t an ordinary courtship affair—this girl held the last chapter of a romance. From that moment my attention fixed on her. By studying her I noticed many admirable qualities; she was clean, neat, and undeniably attractive, soon embodying all the charms desire can give a woman, whatever her station. A fortnight after the notary’s visit, one morning I said to Rosalie:“Come, tell me all you know about Madame de Merret.”“Oh!” she replied, “I will tell you, but keep it a secret.”“Of course, my child; I will guard your secrets with a thief’s honor, the most loyal known.”“If it’s all the same to you,” she said, “I’d prefer it be with your own.”She adjusted her head-kerchief and settled to tell the tale; one needs a certain attitude to tell a story. The best tales are told at a proper hour—just as we are here at table. No one tells a story well standing or fasting.If I reproduced Rosalie’s diffuse eloquence, a whole volume would scarce suffice. Since the event she recounted sits between the notary’s gossip and Madame Lepas’s account, I will summarize it succinctly.Madame de Merret’s bedchamber at La Grande Bretèche was on the ground floor. A small built-in cupboard about four feet deep served as her wardrobe. Three months before the evening in question, Madame de Merret had been so ill that her husband had begun sleeping on the first floor. One evening he returned two hours later than usual from the club, delayed by animated discussions and losses at billiards. His wife assumed he was in bed. For some time Monsieur de Merret had relied on Rosalie to tell him if his wife was in bed; her affirmative replies led him to his room, trusting her completely. That evening, however, he went to check on his wife to share his misfortunes and perhaps find comfort; he had noticed her especially lovely at dinner and thought her recovery had enhanced her beauty.Upon entering, he thought he heard the closet door shut, but when he entered, Madame de Merret stood alone by the fireplace. He felt a suspicion, and noticed anxiety in her eyes.“You’re very late,” she said, her voice slightly husky.Monsieur de Merret remained silent as Rosalie entered, which startled him. He began pacing with folded arms.“Have you received bad news, or are you unwell?” his wife asked timidly while Rosalie helped her undress. He did not answer.“You can go, Rosalie,” Madame de Merret said; “I can manage my curl-papers myself.” She wanted to be alone with her husband, sensing disaster. Once Rosalie left—or was thought to have left, for she lingered in the passage—Monsieur de Merret confronted his wife, declaring coldly, “Madame, there’s someone in your cupboard!” She calmly replied, “No, monsieur.”This “No” pierced Monsieur de Merret’s heart; he did not believe it, yet his wife looked more saintly than ever. He moved toward the closet door, but Madame de Merret took his hand and stopped him. In strange emotion she said, “Remember, if you find no one there, everything must end between us.”The dignity of her stance filled him with deep respect and inspired a resolve that could be described as fierce.“No, Josephine,” he said, “I won’t open it. Regardless of the outcome, we would be forever parted. I know your soul’s purity; you wouldn’t commit a deadly sin to save your life.” At these words Madame de Merret looked haggard. “Here is your crucifix,” he continued. “Swear to me before God that there’s no one in that closet; I will believe you—I will never open that door.”Madame de Merret took the crucifix and swore, “I swear it.”“Louder,” he insisted; “repeat: ‘I swear before God that there is nobody in that closet.’” She repeated the words without flinching.“That will do,” said Monsieur de Merret coldly. After a pause he added, “You have a fine piece of work there, which I’ve never seen before,” examining the ebony-and-silver crucifix.“I found it at Duvivier’s; last year when that troop of Spanish prisoners passed through Vendôme he bought it from a Spanish monk.”“Indeed,” said Monsieur de Merret, hanging the crucifix back on its nail, and he rang for Rosalie.He waited for her to come. “Go to bed, all of you,” he told the household, and called Jean, his coachman and confidential servant, close. He whispered, “When they’re all asleep—mind, asleep—you understand?—come down and tell me.”Monsieur de Merret kept his gaze on his wife as he gave orders, returning to her at the fireside to discuss billiards and the club. When Rosalie returned she found them conversing amiably.Not long before, Monsieur de Merret had had new ceilings put in all the ground-floor reception rooms. Plaster was scarce in Vendôme, and he had ordered a large quantity, knowing he could sell any surplus. This circumstance supplied the means for his plan.“Gorenflot is here, sir,” Rosalie whispered.“Tell him to come in,” he said aloud.Madame de Merret paled at the sight of the mason.“Gorenflot,” her husband said, “go fetch some bricks from the coach-house; bring enough to wall up the cupboard door; you can use the leftover plaster as cement.” Pulling Rosalie and the mason close, he whispered, “Listen, Gorenflot, you’ll sleep here tonight; tomorrow morning I’ll give you a passport to go abroad to a place I’ll specify. I’ll give you six thousand francs for your journey. You must live in that town for ten years; if you don’t like it you may settle elsewhere, but it must be in the same country. Go through Paris and wait there until I join you. I’ll give you an agreement for six thousand francs more, paid on your return if you fulfill the conditions. In return, you must keep silent about what you do tonight. Rosalie, I’ll secure ten thousand francs for you, payable on your wedding day, provided you marry Gorenflot; but to marry, you must stay silent. If not, no wedding gift!”“Rosalie,” Madame de Merret said, “come and brush my hair.”Her husband paced quietly, watching the door, the mason, and his wife without revealing suspicion. Gorenflot made some noise. Madame de Merret caught a moment when he was unloading bricks to whisper to Rosalie: “My dear child, I’ll give you a thousand francs a year if you tell Gorenflot to leave a crack at the bottom.” Then she added casually, “You’d better help him.”Monsieur and Madame de Merret kept silent while Gorenflot began walling the cupboard. Their silence was deliberate; the husband did not want to give his wife any ambiguous chance. From her side it was either pride or prudence. When the wall was half built, the crafty mason, taking advantage of his master’s back being turned, broke one of the two panes at the top of the door with his pick. Madame de Merret understood Rosalie had urged this. They all then saw the face of a dark, gloomy man with black hair and fiery eyes.Before her husband turned around, the poor woman nodded to the stranger, signaling “Hope.”At four o’clock, as dawn broke in September, the work was finished. The mason was placed in charge of Jean, and Monsieur de Merret slept in his wife’s room.The next morning he mentioned casually, “Oh, by the way, I must go to the Maire for the passport.” He donned his hat, took a few steps toward the door, paused, and grabbed the crucifix. His wife trembled with joy; “He will go to Duvivier’s,” she thought.As soon as he left, Madame de Merret rang for Rosalie and cried in a terrifying voice, “The pick! Bring the pick! We must work. I saw how Gorenflot did it yesterday; we have time to make a gap and build it up again.”In an instant Rosalie brought her mistress a cleaver; with a frenzy she began demolishing the wall. She had removed a few bricks when, turning to deliver a stronger blow, she saw Monsieur de Merret behind her. She fainted.“Lay madame on her bed,” he said coldly.Foreseeing what would happen in his absence, he had set a trap; he had only written to the Maire and summoned Duvivier. The jeweler arrived just as the disorder was being repaired.“Duvivier,” Monsieur de Merret asked, “didn’t you buy any crucifixes from the Spaniards who passed through town?”“No, monsieur.”“Very well; thank you,” he said, casting a fierce glance at his wife. “Jean,” he added, turning to his valet, “you can serve my meals here in Madame de Merret’s room. She is ill, and I won’t leave her until she recovers.”The cruel man remained in his wife’s room for twenty days. In the early days, when there were noises from the cupboard and Josephine wished to intercede for the man dying inside, he silenced her: “You swore on the Cross that there was no one there.”After this story all the ladies rose from the table, breaking the spell Bianchon had cast over them. Some of them had shivered at those final words.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################During the Restoration the Archbishop of Besançon was among the few who frequented the drawing-room of the devout Baronne de Watteville. Monsieur de Watteville, descended from the infamous Watteville, was gentle and unassuming by contrast. After living quietly in La Franche-Comté he married Mademoiselle de Rupt, heiress of the Rupt family; their alliance, arranged in 1802, was formalized in 1815 after the second Restoration, and she brought a substantial income. After her grandparents’ deaths Madame de Watteville became more devout, and her piety influenced Besançon’s upper circles.Monsieur, thin and unremarkable, spent his days as a turner and collector of local geological specimens; some thought him noble in spirit. Early on he recognized he would never dominate his wife and so immersed himself in work and hobbies. The Rupt residence, reflecting both families’ status, boasted elegant furnishings and fine food, and Monsieur often acted as his own butler. Madame’s wealth contrasted with her husband’s modest estate, and their close ties to the Archbishop attracted several notable abbés.At a ceremonial dinner in September 1834 guests welcomed Monsieur l’Abbé de Grancey, who announced a favorable verdict in a lawsuit. The Vicar-General called the victory unexpected in the present political climate and credited a young lawyer, Monsieur Savaron, for the success. When Madame asked about him, the Vicar-General said Savaron lived nearby but his origins were unknown, which piqued interest. Amedée de Soulas, present that evening, would later prove significant.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In all ages France and England have exchanged cultural influences, especially in language and fashion, often bypassing customs. What is "English" in Paris is "French" in London, reflecting mutual borrowing: the English national anthem, "God Save the King," was composed by Lulli for a French play, and hoop skirts that were once ridiculed became dominant across Europe. By 1815 English long waists were mocked in Paris, yet French styles soon evolved. More recently England contributed words to French such as "dandy" and "lion," the latter popularized by Alfred de Musset and inspiring a vogue for stylish young men that spread from Paris to the provinces.In 1834 Besançon had its own "lion" in Amedee-Sylvain de Soulas, a young man of Spanish descent who felt out of place in the dull military town. He lived modestly in a sparsely furnished apartment but maintained a lavish appearance, employing a young servant, Babylas, as his "tiger." Amedee dressed Babylas fashionably and spent extravagantly on his wardrobe while surviving on limited income. Though perceived as a spendthrift, he was shrewd about budgetting, often dining out to avoid household expenses. His grooming and clothes were a façade intended to impress the local aristocracy and win the favor of Mademoiselle Rosalie de Watteville, a wealthy heiress. His travels and knowledge of Parisian culture gave him a reputation for worldliness despite financial strain.Besançon was conservative and resistant to change; the local aristocracy—"the Colony"—maintained strict social hierarchies. Amedee’s distinctive fashions, like trouser-straps, set him apart and reinforced his status as a "lion." The town’s elites, eager to seem modern to visiting Parisians, welcomed his cosmopolitan touch.Amedee aimed to marry Rosalie, who had been raised in a strict religious household with little exposure to the wider world. At eighteen she was modest and unassuming, educated under her mother’s watchful eye; domestic skills were emphasized over social graces and she was sheltered from contemporary influences. Despite a passive demeanor, Rosalie had a strong spirit stemming from her family lineage. Her mother, Madame de Watteville, was fiercely protective and controlling, convinced her daughter’s honor was at stake. This produced tension as Amedee courted Rosalie: the Baroness’s strictness caused Rosalie to suppress any feelings, creating a complex, fraught relationship marked by maternal jealousy and uncertainty.As Amedee pursued Rosalie he navigated Besançon’s social intricacies, aiming for a favorable match while preserving his reputation. The elite found him charming and talk of an engagement circulated, yet Rosalie’s upbringing and her mother’s influence complicated his efforts, since she remained largely unaware of the romantic expectations around her. Amedee de Soulas’s life in Besançon thus exemplifies the interplay of social ambition, cultural exchange, and provincial constraint as he strives to win Rosalie de Watteville amid rigid social structures.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“Madame,” said Monsieur de Soulas to the Baroness as he waited for his soup to cool, “one morning a gentleman from Paris arrived at the Hotel National and took the first‑floor rooms in Mademoiselle Galard’s house on Rue du Perron. He registered at the mairie and had his card, ‘ALBERT SAVARON,’ put on the list of barristers at the Court.”“The Savaron name is famous,” Mademoiselle de Watteville observed.“He’s a Frenchman, not of the Brabant line,” Amedee de Soulas replied. “If he claims the Savaron arms he must add a bar‑sinister. The only remaining Brabant heir is a rich Miss de Savarus.”“The bar‑sinister denotes a bastard, but even a bastard of a Comte de Savarus is still noble,” Rosalie added.“Enough,” the Baroness interrupted. “You insisted she learn heraldry,” Monsieur de Watteville said with a smile. “She knows it well.”“Please continue,” the Baroness encouraged Monsieur de Soulas.“In a town like Besançon everything is catalogued,” he went on. “Albert Savaron was received without fuss by local lawyers, who wondered who had sent him and what he hoped to do—especially since he left his card with judges instead of calling. Three days later no one paid him much mind. He hired Jerome, the late Monsieur Galard’s servant, who could cook a little. Savaron kept to himself; no one had seen much of him.”“Does he go to mass?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked.“He attends the early service at Saint‑Pierre on Sundays. He rises very early, works until eight, breakfasts, walks in his garden—fifty or sixty turns—dines, and is in bed by six or seven.”“How do you know all this?” Madame de Chavoncourt wanted to know.“I live on Rue Neuve, opposite his house; my servant speaks with Jerome.”“And you gossip with Babylas?” someone laughed.“How did you come to hire a stranger for your defense?” the Baroness asked the Vicar‑General.“The President of the Court assigned Savaron to defend a half‑witted peasant accused of forgery. Savaron proved the man innocent and exposed the real culprits, who were arrested. That defense impressed the Court and the jury. Then a merchant put a difficult case in his hands, which he won. Monsieur Berryer advised us to employ him, promising success. I met him and had confidence in him—and I was right.”“Is he extraordinary?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked.“Certainly,” the Vicar‑General replied.“Tell us more,” urged Madame de Watteville.“The first time I met him,” the Abbe de Grancey recounted, “he received me in an outer room lined with books, modestly furnished. I had not expected a young advocate. His dress was odd—black dressing gown, red cord, red slippers, red waistcoat, red smoking cap.”“The devil’s colors!” Madame de Watteville exclaimed.“Yes,” the Abbe continued, “but he has a magnificent head. Thick black hair streaked with gray, a round white throat, a broad forehead marked by thought, an olive complexion, a square nose, and eyes like an eagle. He is passionate yet calm; his voice is sweet but can thunder.”“Monsieur Savaron is of average height, with hands like a prelate,” the Abbe added.“The second time I saw him he received me in his shabby bedroom; the third I found him having a frugal breakfast in his library. He is no ordinary man; there is something heavy on him. His face is terrible and gentle at once; he seems to carry a great burden.”“Why would such an eloquent man leave Paris for Besançon?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked. “Doesn’t he know how little a stranger can do here?”“After seeing his head and hearing him reply to a leading Paris lawyer,” the Abbe said, “I believe he will yet make a name.”“Why speak of him? You’ve paid your bill,” Madame de Watteville remarked, noting her daughter’s rapt attention.The conversation moved on, but the Abbe’s portrait of Savaron had taken root in Rosalie. For the first time she encountered someone who seemed truly exceptional, in stark contrast to the dull Count who had courted her. He presented an enigma: eloquent, serious, isolated, and apparently burdened. She replayed the Abbe’s words in her mind, particularly the description of his forehead and hands, and felt a new, restless curiosity.“Rosalie, why aren’t you eating?” her mother asked.“I’m not hungry, mamma,” she answered, lost in thought.She realized she had seen a lighted window from her bed that belonged to him. “I must see him,” she decided.“Is the Chapter’s lawsuit settled?” she asked the Abbe, trying to appear casual.“It might go to the Court of Appeal, but our adversaries will hesitate,” he said.After dinner, Rosalie listened for any mention of Savaron, but there was little more than congratulations to the Abbe. Eager for bedtime, she planned to wake between two and three to glimpse Savaron’s windows. When the hour came she felt a thrill watching the candlelight through the trees and thought of the furniture and color of his rooms. “While the world sleeps, he is awake—like God,” she imagined.The question of educating girls—the tension between informing them and restraining them—was briefly noted: too much enlightenment risks precociousness; too much suppression breeds rebellion. For Rosalie, the Abbe’s portrait had torn aside the usual restraint and kindled a desire—part romantic, part intellectual—to know this man.The next morning, while dressing, she saw Albert walking in the garden beside the Hotel de Rupt. “At least I can see him here,” she thought. Determined to enter his house and hear his voice, she planned to engineer an opportunity.She spent the day embroidering with the air of someone distracted. She decided to go to confession, timing herself to leave just before the eight o’clock mass, hoping to encounter Albert. She visited her father in his workshop and suggested he carve larger pieces and build a grotto in the garden to display his work. He delighted in the idea; she warned him not to tell her mother.She imagined the grotto with an observatory that would give her a view into Albert’s private room. Young girls often contrive elaborate lengths for the sake of a man, even if he never knows.When Sunday arrived Rosalie dressed with care; her maid Mariette smiled at her nervousness. She arrived at church before mass and took a place where she might see Albert enter. He had a solemn, burdened air; his gaze was intense. To Rosalie, everything about him now seemed filled with meaning. Boldly she stood in his path to exchange glances, and a warmth surged through her.After confession she found a seat to watch him; by the time Mass ended he had gone. “The Vicar‑General was right,” she thought. “He is unhappy. Why has this eagle come to Besançon? I must know everything!” With renewed resolve she turned her thoughts to the grotto plan.“Mamma,” she said one day, “my father has an idea for a Belvedere in the garden. Do you approve?”“I approve of whatever amuses your father,” Madame de Watteville replied dryly.“From there we’ll be able to see into Monsieur de Soulas’ rooms, and he may see us—perhaps remarks will be made—”“Do you presume to guide your parents, Rosalie?” her mother snapped.“I’ll say no more, mamma,” she answered. But the plan pressed on. Monsieur de Watteville and their carpenter devised a grotto hidden from one neighbor yet giving a view toward Savaron’s windows. Periwinkles, clematis, ivy, honeysuckle, and Virginia creeper would frame a rustic interior with a mirror, ottoman, and bark‑inlaid table. A chandelier carved by Monsieur de Watteville himself completed the idea. The garden project was commissioned and rumors spread: the Wattevilles were building a lovely belvedere—“a thousand crowns!” Madame de Chavoncourt exclaimed.Rosalie felt a flush of pride. No one suspected that the supposed simple‑witted girl had planned the arrangement chiefly to see Savaron’s private study.Meanwhile Savaron’s brilliant defense of the Cathedral Chapter was soon forgotten amid envy from other lawyers. He remained secluded, avoiding social gatherings, which increased his anonymity. Yet he kept winning complex cases: three successes at the Commercial Tribunal brought him four prominent merchant clients who recognized his legal skill.On the day the Wattevilles inaugurated their Belvedere, Savaron was launching an enterprise of his own. Quietly, through connections among local merchants, he planned a fortnightly paper, the Eastern Review. It was financed by forty shares of five hundred francs each subscribed by his first ten clients, who saw the need to promote Besançon as a trade center.To compete with Strasbourg and Dijon, Besançon needed to become a center of commerce and enlightenment. Savaron wanted to challenge the literary dominance of the West and bring light to Eastern France. His ideas appealed to merchants who fancied they were furthering their own interests.Savaron kept his name out of the public foreground. He let his chief client, Monsieur Boucher, handle accounts, while Savaron retained editorial control and a share of any profits. The Review invited contributions from various regions and, with a low subscription price, quickly gained five hundred subscribers.To avoid bruising local pride, Savaron invited Alfred Boucher, Monsieur Boucher’s eager son, to manage the literary side. Savaron subtly guided Alfred’s contributions while letting him believe he was in charge. The merchants were pleased: with a few more subscribers the publication would yield dividends for shareholders, while the editor remained unpaid.After the third issue the Review began to be noticed by other French publications; Savaron read them at home. The third number contained a tale attributed to him that stirred talk in Madame de Chavoncourt’s drawing room, though some of Besançon’s higher circles dismissed it for its liberal sentiments.“Papa,” Rosalie said, “there’s a Review published in Besançon; you should subscribe and keep it in your room—mamma won’t let me read it, but you can lend it to me.”Monsieur de Watteville, eager to please Rosalie, subscribed and lent her the four issues. That night she devoured the tale—the first she had ever read. Given her limited reading experience, its effect on her was profound. She hoped to find in it the sentiments or even the life of Albert. From the opening pages she felt convinced it reflected him; critics would later note that the author had drawn from his own experiences as if confessing.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In 1823 two young men left Lucerne for a holiday around the lake of the Four Cantons. The journey offered striking mountain, river, and meadow views. Passing the hamlet of Gersau, one friend, Rodolphe, was captivated by a newly built house and a woman at the window and decided to stay; his friend Leopold continued the trip.Rodolphe and Leopold had studied law together: Leopold was destined to be a notary in Paris, while Rodolphe, the illegitimate son of a nobleman, had a more unsettled background. His mother had sacrificed everything to secure his education, hoping to improve his prospects. Passionate and sensitive, Rodolphe was often led by desire, a contrast to Leopold’s calm demeanor.In Gersau Rodolphe lodged with the Bergmanns, who had taken him in. He learned the woman at the window was Fanny Lovelace, who had moved there with her elderly father for his health. Moderately well off, they lived simply; Fanny supported her father by translating and writing. Rodolphe was taken by her beauty and musical talent.The next day he sought permission to visit the Bergmanns’ gardens; though initially hesitant they allowed him to explore. There he noticed a deaf-mute girl, Gina, whose presence suggested a distinctive past. Eager to meet Fanny, Rodolphe encountered the Bergmanns’ protectiveness.That evening he heard Fanny singing and was enchanted. He later met Fanny and Gina, but their conversation was cut short when an unknown assailant attacked and wounded him. Fanny and Gina aided him and he was taken to safety.While recovering, Rodolphe received devastating news: his mother had died. The loss plunged him into deep depression, but he found solace in Fanny’s company. They bonded over shared interests, and Rodolphe confessed his feelings, only to learn Fanny was married to an older man, Emilio. Despite this, Fanny’s affection for Rodolphe grew and they shared intimate moments.Fanny revealed that her husband was not as old as he seemed and that she had married him under duress. Rodolphe was torn between his love for her and the reality of her marriage. Fanny encouraged him to pursue his ambitions, believing in his potential.Their relationship deepened, yet Rodolphe struggled with jealousy and insecurity, especially when news arrived of Tito, a young man from Fanny’s past.
