Tito’s presence provoked rivalry in Rodolphe, who feared losing her.Despite these challenges, Rodolphe and Fanny’s connection remained strong. They shared dreams of freedom and a future together, but the complexities of their circumstances—Rodolphe’s ambition and Fanny’s loyalty to her husband—created persistent tension. Rodolphe wrestled with the demands of love, ambition, and the sacrifices required for happiness. The beauty of their surroundings mirrored the intensity of their emotions, yet the realities of life threatened to overshadow their dreams.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For twenty days Rodolphe stayed at the Bergmanns’, watching Francesca almost unconsciously. He saw in her the youthful imprudence and unbroken spirit of a woman who sometimes resisted love and sometimes yielded. The old man treated her like a father; her gratitude awakened a native nobility that intrigued Rodolphe and made her an enigma.Those days blended secret joys and melancholy; their quarrels were as delightful as their reconciliations. Rodolphe grew captively fond of Francesca’s steady tenderness, which often showed a jealous exactness over trifles.One evening, when Francesca declared she wanted to leave Gersau because it lacked luxury, Rodolphe asked if she cared for luxury. She answered passionately, equating her fondness for luxury with a love of art and beauty, insisting she had never complained during hardships. With a mischievous air she argued that it was natural for ordinary people to aspire to greatness, revealing her charming character.She then begged Rodolphe not to accompany them to Geneva, citing town gossip and her wish to shield her husband’s reputation. Though she affirmed her love for Rodolphe, she demanded it remain concealed; she kissed him and left him astonished.The next day the Bergmanns’ lodgers departed early. Restless, Rodolphe went to Vevay and then to Geneva at the end of October, taking a long route. To avoid the town’s discomforts he rented a room in Eaux-Vives. His first inquiry was after Italian refugees, especially a bookseller named Lamporani; the landlord replied that the only new arrivals were Prince and Princess Colonna and Prince and Princess Gandolphini.On his first walk to the Villa Diodati the beauty of the place struck him. Approaching, he saw Francesca in an elegant carriage with an old lady and felt dizzy with surprise. Learning they were at the Colonna residence, a veil seemed to lift: the implications of their past interactions became clear. He feared he had been a mere whim for Francesca, struggling with the reality of her noble standing.He sent his card to Prince Gandolphini and was received by the false Lamporani, who invited him to a concert that evening. Though curious, Rodolphe refrained from requesting an audience with the Princess and returned to Eaux-Vives with mingled anticipation and anxiety. As the evening approached his passion deepened and he felt compelled to elevate his social position to match hers.When he finally saw her at the concert she was dazzling; their eyes met in an electric instant. Francesca’s beauty and grace captivated him and he felt a profound connection, convinced she had remained faithful to their past despite her rank. After the concert he was introduced to the Colonnas and to Francesca, who playfully styled herself “Princess Rodolphini.” The jest revived memories of Gersau; he was overwhelmed by her voice and the intimacy of their proximity.In the ensuing days Francesca perceived Rodolphe’s deep love, which for her felt natural. She said their affection should be faithful and enduring, whatever the distance. Rodolphe, however, was driven by ambition; he wanted to achieve greatness so Francesca could be proud of him.Francesca herself combined gaiety and tenderness. Once destined for a cloister, family misfortunes had compelled her to marry Prince Gandolphini to preserve her family’s standing. Yet despite her noble birth she had acquired a taste for independence and liberal ideas, inclining her toward revolutionary sentiments.Rodolphe spent the winter in Geneva where time flew in the company of the intelligent and beautiful Francesca. But with spring came inner turmoil: Francesca’s calmness contrasted with his emotional unrest. He wished she would show more feeling, believing Italian women to be more expressive. One day on the terrace he voiced a bitter complaint about the happiness of others, singling out a woman of noble lineage; his words wounded Francesca, and she withdrew. Rodolphe followed and found her in tears. In a moment of passion he kissed her, but she repelled him and asserted her dignity.Francesca then ordered him to leave, declaring they would not meet again until Naples. Her command was firm; Rodolphe, recognizing the strength of her will, complied.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################On his return to Paris, Rodolphe discovered a portrait of Princess Gandolphini by Schinner, who had refused several women. Rodolphe doubted the Prince could persuade him, but Francesca charmed Schinner into painting an original for Rodolphe and a duplicate for Emilio. She announced it in a delightful letter, beginning a regular correspondence between Rodolphe and Francesca—their sole indulgence.Ambitious and in love, Rodolphe invested all he had in a venture that failed through deceit, costing him three years' struggle. The Villele ministry fell just as he faced ruin. Seeking solace he turned to politics, but first went to Naples to recover at Villa Gandolphini, where the Prince and Princess had been restored. His three-month respite healed him and renewed his courage and hope.Rodolphe then tried to rebuild his fortune. His talents were noticed and a high post was promised for his dedication and past efforts, but the July 1830 storm dashed his ambitions again. Only Francesca and God witnessed his brave, daring attempts—he had fine qualities but was unlucky in fortune. Sustained by love, he returned to new challenges, guided by a faithful presence and devoted heart. Lovers! Pray for him!############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################As Mademoiselle de Watteville finished her narrative, Rosalie's cheeks burned and she wept. The novel — recounted through a man's experiences — was her first encounter with such a love story and stirred deep emotion. Her agitation sprang from jealousy of Francesca Colonna, whom she believed to be Albert's beloved.Consumed by curiosity and a fierce desire to win Albert, Rosalie, insecure about her looks and lack of musical talent, feared he would never love her and resolved to learn the truth. Her imagination hatched schemes typical of a young woman raised under strict rules: sneaking into Albert's garden, writing to him, or contriving encounters at her father's land dispute to draw him into her drawing-room.Watching Albert's window, she observed something that might reveal his secrets: Jérôme, Albert's servant, being helped over the wall by Mariette, the maid. Rosalie noted Mariette's plainness but also her steadiness and means, which explained Jérôme's interest. The incident taught Rosalie a dangerous lesson: her mother's rigid upbringing could be undermined by a servant's choices.The next morning Rosalie accompanied Mariette to Mass and seized the chance to question her about Jérôme. She confronted Mariette about their secret meetings and, to her surprise, offered support in return for access to the letters Albert passed through Jérôme. Relieved, Mariette agreed.Rosalie's obsession deepened as she read a letter from Albert to his friend Léopold revealing his noble lineage and past struggles. She was enthralled by his ambitions and the complexity of his character, intensifying her feelings. The letter portrayed a man striving for success while devoted to the woman he loved—Francesca.As she continued to read Albert's correspondence, Rosalie grew more aware of her inadequacy and the moral cost of spying. She wrestled with guilt over invading his privacy but rationalized it as proof of love. Her passion slid into infatuation, prompting penances and self-imposed suffering intended to demonstrate devotion.One evening Mariette brought a letter meant for Francesca, plunging Rosalie into jealousy and doubt. She hesitated but yielded to the urge to read it. The letter confirmed Albert's enduring love for Francesca, outlining his ambitions and hopes for a shared future.Rosalie's heart ached as she absorbed its contents and grasped the depth of his feeling for another. Though she had once resolved to respect their relationship, she found herself torn between admiration for their fidelity and her own longing. In a clear moment she chose to step back, seeing the nobility of their bond and acknowledging her shortcomings.Preparing to return the letter with Mariette, Rosalie reflected on her feelings and the nature of love. She felt sorrow and resignation, recognizing that true love often requires sacrifice, and began to accept the reality of her unrequited affection.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Rosalie spent the winter of 1834–35 in turmoil; by April, at nineteen, she fantasized about triumphing over the Duchesse d’Argaiolo. In solitude that imagined struggle fed her passion and dark thoughts, prompting numerous plans. Many young women like Rosalie exist, and this story functions as a cautionary tale.That winter Albert de Savarus made notable advances in Besançon, eagerly awaiting the Chamber’s dissolution. He’d secured the backing of a wealthy contractor tied to the moderate party. The Romans had invested heavily in town water supplies, and Besançon drew water from Arcier, a distant hill. Restoring an aqueduct to drink the Romans’ water seemed absurd where the Doubs flowed, but persuading citizens would produce large expenses benefiting the contractor.Savarus argued the river water was undrinkable and promoted Arcier water. Local Review articles echoed him, and townspeople across political lines bought the romance of drinking like the Romans. The issue became a focal point driven by hidden interests. Opponents were dismissed as old-fashioned while Savarus’s projects dominated discussion. After eighteen months he had stirred the stagnant town and gained influence without real popularity.He also won several lawsuits for local priests over the winter, and his ambition consumed him. His apparent indifference to money masked a form of moral usury: he expected a far greater reward. In October 1834 he helped a struggling merchant with funds from Léopold Hannequin, which also qualified him for election. The Abbé de Grancey, who saw Savarus’s ambition clearly, remarked on his remarkable nature and hinted he’d chosen a wrong turn in life.Rosalie resolved to introduce Savarus to the society of the Hôtel de Rupt, initially content to observe him from afar. The Wattevilles’ estate, les Rouxey, produced ten thousand francs a year, but management was neglected by the Baron, who left it to an old servant, Modinier. The Baron’s indifference sprang from his wife’s substantial income; the family often retreated to les Rouxey, a picturesque spot shaped by the famous Watteville.Watteville had built a dam and lake, enhancing the estate, but his heirs maintained a dubious claim over the village of Riceys, which historically enjoyed rights to the land. The Baron’s actions left a legacy of usurpation, and his descendants continued to assert control over the village.Rosalie’s efforts were complex and subtle. In May 1835 she left Besançon with her father for les Rouxey; the lake’s beauty captivated her and she associated it with love. During a suggested boat trip her father confided about legal troubles with Riceys over land rights. Rosalie urged him to hire Savarus as their lawyer, stressing the importance of securing the Dent de Vilard for the estate. She argued a lawsuit would enliven their lives and insisted on bringing Savarus to les Rouxey, revealing her determination to secure her future there and to create beautiful gardens.Four days later the Abbé de Grancey visited Savarus to persuade him to represent the Wattevilles. Savarus hesitated, fearing such alignment would compromise his neutrality. The Abbé urged him to weigh the political advantages of backing the Wattevilles, especially for votes.Rosalie, meanwhile, was determined to keep Savarus in Besançon and prevent his election. She wrote to the préfet, revealing Savarus’s ambitions, but the préfet disregarded her. Rosalie understood the political landscape and sought to manipulate it to her advantage.As the election approached, a meeting of influential men in Besançon decided to nominate Savarus as a candidate, much to his surprise; he had been initially unaware of these machinations. The Abbé, who had been watching, warned Savarus of potential betrayals and advised him to be transparent about his past.Savarus delivered a passionate speech outlining his vision and ambitions, captivating his audience. Yet he faced challenges: the préfet worked to undermine his support, and his voter base began to erode. Despite efforts, the political tide shifted against him and he struggled to maintain his position.On the day of the first voting the results were disappointing for Savarus; he felt the weight of his ambitions and the pressure of the election, returned home in despair, and reflected on his love for Francesca and the uncertainty of his future.Just as hope faded, the Abbé de Grancey arrived with encouraging news. The priest had worked behind the scenes to secure support for Savarus, urging him to visit the Wattevilles and promising their case would be resolved in time for the election. The Abbé revealed that winning the Rouxey lawsuit could secure crucial votes and encouraged Savarus to pursue a marriage with Sidonie de Chavoncourt to solidify his political standing. Initially resistant, Savarus recognized the potential benefits.The Abbé’s guidance proved invaluable, and Savarus prepared to navigate the complexities of both love and politics, understanding that his future depended on choices he would have to make in the coming days.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################By nine the next evening Madame la Baronne de Watteville’s rooms were full of Besançon’s aristocracy, gathered over the unusual decision to go to the polls for the daughter of the Rupts. The man to be presented was the former Master of Appeals, a secretary to a loyal minister of the Elder Branch. Madame de Chavoncourt arrived with her younger daughter Sidonie, elegantly dressed; her elder daughter, confident in her relationship, had made no effort—an omission noticed in a small town. The Abbe de Grancey moved among groups, listening and offering incisive remarks that steered the talk.“If the Elder Branch returns,” he asked an elderly statesman, “what politicians would they find?” They spoke of Berryer, isolated on his bench and likely to unsettle the Government, of the Duc de Fitz-James in Toulouse, and how a vote for Monsieur Savarus might draw Republicans toward them rather than the Moderates.By half past nine Albert had not arrived, and Madame de Watteville took his lateness as a personal slight. Madame de Chavoncourt urged calm, suggesting he might be detained by a consultation. Rosalie, watching the two, observed her mother’s indulgence toward Albert, which hinted at a possible match between him and Sidonie.At ten the delay grew embarrassing; some gentlemen began to play cards. The Abbe de Grancey, perplexed, went to a window where Rosalie stood and exclaimed, “He must be dead!” The Vicar-General, Monsieur de Watteville, and Rosalie went into the garden to check Albert’s rooms, which were dark and silent.“Jerome!” Rosalie called to the servant. When he confirmed Albert had left in a post-chaise, the Abbe cried, “He is ruined! Or he is happy!” Rosalie’s face, however, betrayed a joy the Vicar-General noticed and pretended to ignore, wondering if she was implicated.Back in the drawing-room Monsieur de Watteville announced Albert’s unexplained departure. By eleven-thirty only about fifteen guests remained: Madame de Chavoncourt, the Abbe de Godenars, the two Chavoncourt girls, Monsieur de Vauchelles, the Abbe de Grancey, Rosalie, Amedee de Soulas—a retired magistrate who had backed Albert’s election—and a few others. The Abbe stationed himself near the Baroness to watch Rosalie, whose ordinarily pale face showed agitation.“What could have happened to Monsieur de Savarus?” Madame de Chavoncourt wondered aloud. At that moment a servant brought a letter for the Abbe de Grancey on a silver tray. The Baroness urged him to read it. As he did he saw Rosalie turn pale and recognized the handwriting; after exchanging a look with her that betrayed feeling, he folded the letter and slipped it into his pocket.“She is in love with Albert Savarus!” he concluded inwardly. As he prepared to leave, Rosalie intercepted him. “Monsieur de Grancey, it was from Albert!” she cried. When he asked how she knew, her passionate reply—“I love him! What is the matter?”—laid bare her feeling. He told her Albert was withdrawing from the election. Stunned, Rosalie begged him to keep the news secret, realizing that if the election ended, so too might her sister’s prospect of marrying Albert.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################On her way to Mass, Mademoiselle de Watteville learned from Mariette that Albert had abruptly disappeared. An elderly Parisian had arrived at the Hotel National and called on Monsieur Savaron. The lawyer turned pale at the card; they met for over an hour. Afterwards the gentleman left with a large package, and Savaron, distraught, went to the Prefecture, bought an old carriage, ordered post-horses, left his keys, and told his servant to find other employment.Savaron’s sudden flight caused a stir in Besançon; the Prefect quickly granted him a passport. The affair fed local prejudice against outsiders, and Albert was soon forgotten by most — except Girardet, the Vicar-General, and Rosalie, who alone knew the truth about the Duc d’Argaiolo’s death.By April 1836 there was still no news of Albert. The Baroness de Watteville urged Rosalie to marry, noting how much she had changed. The Baron agreed to give Rosalie the family estate, les Rouxey, but the Baroness fretted over finances and arranged a match with Monsieur de Soulas, which Rosalie firmly rejected, insisting she was content as she was. The conflict escalated; a summer at les Rouxey made Rosalie’s refusal public. After Mariette and Jerome married, the Baron improved the estate, which the Baroness resented. As tensions rose, the Vicar-General tried to mediate.One day Rosalie confessed to him that she had interfered in Albert’s relationship with Francesca, the Duchess d’Argaiolo: she had intercepted letters and engineered their separation. He was horrified, insisting on the moral gravity of her actions, urging her to seek forgiveness and to grasp the consequences she had set in motion.Rosalie’s guilt deepened when she learned of Albert’s fate. A Paris clerk representing Albert came to sell his effects, revealing that Albert had renounced the world and entered a monastery. The Vicar-General wrote to the monastery and received a letter from Albert expressing complete forgiveness for Rosalie and his wish to remain in solitude.When the Vicar-General shared this, he urged reconciliation with her mother and acceptance of Monsieur de Soulas. Rosalie remained defiant, unwilling to sacrifice her mother’s happiness. The Vicar-General’s death left a void; soon after, Madame de Watteville married Monsieur de Soulas, believing Rosalie’s support.Now legally of age, Rosalie encouraged the marriage and thus secured her independence. With a substantial fortune she entered Parisian society intent on confronting the Duchess d’Argaiolo. At a ball she publicly revealed her role in Albert’s downfall, leaving the Duchess shaken.Rosalie returned to Besançon, having exposed the truth, yet the emotional toll of her actions weighed heavily. The tale of love, betrayal, and the consequences of ambition unfolded, leaving a lasting impact on all involved.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Mademoiselle de Watteville lived alone at les Rouxey—riding, hunting, and improving her estate—making her known in the Eastern provinces. Madame de Soulas, mother of two, looks younger than her husband, Monsieur de Soulas, who has grown old. He told young Chavoncourt, "My fortune has cost me dear. To know a bigot, it is unfortunately necessary to marry her!"Famed for eccentric "vagaries," Mademoiselle de Watteville annually visited the walls of the Grande Chartreuse, perhaps following a grand-uncle who once burst through monastery walls. In 1841 she left Besançon—said to marry, though motives were unclear—and returned after a Loire steamboat boiler explosion. The blast so gravely injured her that she lost her right arm and left leg and suffered disfiguring facial scars. Her health declined; in constant pain, she now lives in religious observance at the Chartreuse of les Rouxey.PARIS, May 1842.ADDENDUMThe following characters appear in other stories of the Human Comedy:- Beauseant, Vicomtesse de: Father Goriot; The Deserted Woman- Genovese: Massimilla Doni- Hannequin, Leopold: Beatrix; Cousin Betty; Cousin Pons- Jeanrenaud: The Commission in Lunacy- Nueil, Gaston de: The Deserted Woman- Rhetore, Duc Alphonse de: A Bachelor’s Establishment; A Distinguished Provincial at Paris; Scenes from a Courtesan’s Life; Letters of Two Brides; The Member for Arcis- Savaron de Savarus and Albert Savaron de: The Quest of the Absolute- Schinner, Hippolyte: The Purse; A Bachelor’s Establishment; Pierre Grassou; A Start in Life; The Government Clerks; Modeste Mignon; The Imaginary Mistress; The Unconscious Humorists- Tinti, Clarina: Massimilla Doni############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ##################################### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Produced by John Bickers, and DagnyTHE MARRIAGE CONTRACTBy Honore De BalzacTranslated by Katharine Prescott WormeleyDEDICATIONTo Rossini.THE MARRIAGE CONTRACTCHAPTER I. PRO AND CONMonsieur de Manerville was a respectable Norman gentleman whom the Maréchal de Richelieu married to a wealthy Bordeaux heiress when the old duke administered Guienne. The Norman sold his Bessin estate, became a Gascon by moving to his wife’s château de Lanstrac, and purchased the post of major of the Gate Guards toward the end of Louis XV’s reign. Having escaped the Revolution, he lived until 1813.In late 1790 he went to Martinique, leaving his Gascogne property under the care of Mathias, a notary’s clerk who sympathized with, if not wholly adopted, revolutionary ideas. On his return the Comte de Manerville found his estates intact and well-managed—the Gascon’s energy grafted onto the Norman’s steadiness.Madame de Manerville died in 1810. The old man, hardened by youthful dissipation, became penurious and niggardly, making almost nothing over to his only son.Paul de Manerville returned from the college of Vendôme in 1810 and lived under strict paternal discipline for three years. The seventy-year-old despot shaped a young heart and character still unformed; Paul, naturally bold in the Gascon tradition, dared not resist his father and lost that formative faculty of resistance. His suppressed feelings hardened inwardly; later, when they collided with societal maxims, he could reason rightly but act wrongly. He might fight passionately over a word or look yet tremble at dismissing a servant—his timidity showing in struggles requiring steady will. Capable of bold deeds to escape persecution, he would not have prevented it by sustained opposition. Timid in thought, bold in action, he retained a simplicity that made him a dupe and a voluntary victim of things against which other souls revolt. Imprisoned by his father’s mansion and lacking money to consort with peers, he envied their pleasures without sharing them.Each evening the old man took him in a shabby carriage to royalist houses, where the relics of parliamentary and martial nobility gathered—landed aristocrats crushed by the wealth of maritime cities, priding themselves on disdain for commerce’s display. Too young to grasp such social distinctions, Paul was bored by these ancients, unaware that those early connections would later secure the preeminence Frenchmen seek.He found some compensation in the manual exercises his father insisted upon. The old gentleman believed that fencing, riding, pistol practice, tennis, and good manners—frivolous accomplishments of old nobility—made a modern young man a gentleman. Thus Paul took fencing lessons, rode, practiced at a pistol gallery, and spent the rest of his time reading novels, his father forbidding the abstruse studies then becoming necessary.So monotonous a life would have killed him had not his father’s death delivered him when it became intolerable. Paul then found himself possessor of considerable capital accumulated by paternal avarice, with well-kept estates. But he disliked Bordeaux; Lanstrac, the summer hunting place, did not captivate him.Once the estate was settled, the young heir, eager for enjoyment, invested in consols, entrusted estate administration to Mathias, and spent six years abroad: attached to the French embassy at Naples, secretary at Madrid, then in London—making a European tour.After seeing the world, losing illusions, and dissipating his father’s capital, Paul had to draw upon the territorial revenues Mathias had been saving. Seized by a virtuously named impulse, he resolved to leave Paris, return to Bordeaux, regulate his affairs, live as a country gentleman at Lanstrac, improve his property, marry, and eventually pursue a deputy’s seat.Paul was a count; nobility had matrimonial value again; he could and ought to make a good marriage. In exchange for seven hundred thousand francs squandered in six years he had gained what cannot be bought—experience: the knowledge drawn from social apprenticeship, love affairs, duels, lost bets, disillusions and pleasures. He had become what is called elegant. Despite extravagance he had not become a mere man of fashion. In that burlesque army of the worldlings, the man of elegance ranks above the mere fashionable man. Paul enjoyed that reputation and sustained it: his servants were well dressed, his equipages noted, his suppers in vogue; his bachelor establishment rivaled the finest Paris houses.He had not debauched any woman; he gambled openly, was honest enough not to mislead, left no billets-doux about, and kept no chest of love-letters. Not willing to tap his Guienne property, he lacked the bold extravagance that attracts attention. He did not borrow, though he foolishly lent to friends who deserted him. He seemed methodical in his dissipations. The secret of his character lay in his father’s tyranny, which had made him, as it were, a social mongrel.One morning he told his friend de Marsay, soon to be celebrated,—“My dear fellow, life has a meaning.”“You must be twenty-seven to find it,” de Marsay laughed.“Well, I am twenty-seven; and because I am twenty-seven I mean to live the country-gentleman life at Lanstrac. I’ll move my belongings to Bordeaux, and spend three months a year in Paris in this house I shall keep.”“Will you marry?”“I will marry.”“I’m your friend,” de Marsay said after a pause, “and I tell you: settle down into a worthy father and husband and you’ll be ridiculous for the rest of your days. If you could be happy and ridiculous, it might be bearable; but you won’t be happy. You don’t have the strong wrist to manage a household. I’ll do you justice: you are a perfect horseman—no one knows how to set a horse—but marriage is another thing. I see you led by Madame la Comtesse de Manerville, going where you would not, oftener at a gallop than a trot, and presently unhorsed—broken. Listen. You still have some forty-odd thousand francs a year from your Gironde property. Take your horses and servants and furnish your house in Bordeaux; you can be king of Bordeaux, proclaim our Paris edicts; be the correspondent of our follies. Play the provincial rake; commit follies that may bring celebrity. But don’t marry. Who marries nowadays? Merchants for capital, peasants for children to work, brokers and notaries for wives’ dots, miserable kings to continue dynasties. We are exempt from the pack, and you want to join it! Why marry? If you wed an heiress as rich as yourself, eighty thousand francs for two is not the same as forty for one; two become three or four with children. Do you love the old Manerville race enough to be hampered by it? Consider what parents must be. Marriage, my old Paul, is the silliest social immolation; our children alone profit and don’t know its price until their horses nibble the flowers on our grave. Do you regret your father—the old tyrant who made your youth wretched? How can you be sure your children will love you? The care you take and the necessary sternness will lessen their affection. Children love a weak or prodigal father, whom they later despise. You’ll live between fear and contempt. No man becomes a good head of a family simply by willing it. Look at our friends and name one you’d like as a son. Marriage breeds many who dishonor their names. Children, my dear Paul, are the most difficult merchandise to keep. You think yours will be angels. Fine. Have you measured the gulf between bachelor and married life? As a bachelor you limit your ridiculousness; as a married man you fall into its infinitude. A bachelor makes his own happiness; married, you must take it as it comes. Marry and you’ll grow a blockhead; you’ll calculate dowries; you’ll talk morality; you’ll become a social academician. It’s pitiable! The old bachelor hoarding his spoon is blessed compared with the married man. I speak not merely of annoyances, bores, coercions and tyrannies of constant companionship; to relate them would repeat Boileau. Still, I’ll forgive your absurd idea if you promise to marry ‘en grand seigneur’: entail your property; have two legitimate children; give your wife a house and household distinct from yours; meet her only in society, never return from a journey without sending her a courier. Two hundred thousand francs a year will suffice, and your antecedents will enable you to marry some rich Englishwoman hungry for a title. That’s an aristocratic life thoroughly French; it retains a woman’s respect and a man’s distinction. Thus the Comte de Manerville may advise his epoch, place himself above the world, be minister or ambassador. Ridicule can never touch him; he gains social advantages of marriage while keeping bachelor privileges.”“But, my good friend, I am not de Marsay,” said Paul; “I am Paul de Manerville, a worthy father and husband, deputy of the Centre, possibly peer of France—a destiny commonplace; but I am modest and I resign myself.”“Yes, but your wife,” the pitiless de Marsay went on, “will she resign herself?”“My wife, my dear fellow, will do as I wish.”“Ah! my poor friend, is that where you are? Adieu, Paul. Henceforth I refuse to respect you. One word more; I cannot agree coldly to your abdication. Look where the strength of our position lies. A bachelor with only six thousand francs a year left has at least his reputation for elegance and memory of success. Life still offers many chances to the unmarried man. You can aim at anything. But marriage is the social ‘Thus far shalt thou go and no farther.’ Once married you can never be anything but what you then are—unless your wife deigns to care for you.”“But,” said Paul, “you crush me with exceptional theories. I am tired of living for others; of having horses merely to exhibit them; of doing everything for what may be said; of wasting my substance to keep fools from crying, ‘Dear! Paul is still driving the same carriage. What has he done with his fortune?’ Believe me, Henri, I admire your power, but I don't envy it. You judge life like a statesman, place yourself above prejudices, and grasp the profits of situations in which I would find only ill luck. Your cool deductions are, to the masses, shockingly immoral. I belong to the masses. I must play by society’s rules. While placing yourself above things on peaks of ice, you still have feelings; as for me I should freeze to death. The life of the great majority, to which I belong, is made of emotions I now need. Often a man coquets with a dozen women and obtains none. Whatever his strength, he is crushed in convulsions. For my part, I like the peaceful chances and changes of life; I want a woman always at my side.”“A trifle indecorous, your marriage!” exclaimed de Marsay.Paul was not disconcerted: “Laugh if you like; I shall feel happy when my valet says in the morning, ‘Madame is awaiting monsieur for breakfast’; happier still at night, to return to find a heart—”“Altogether indecorous,” said de Marsay. “You are not yet moral enough to marry.”“But,” Paul continued, “a heart to confide my interests and secrets. I wish a close union with a woman so our affection shall not depend on a yes or no, nor be open to love’s disillusions. I have the courage to become, as you say, a worthy husband and father. I feel fitted for family joys; I want to submit myself to society’s conditions; I desire a wife and children.”“You remind me of a hive of honey-bees! But go your way; you’ll be a dupe all your life. Ha! you wish to marry to have a wife! In other words, you wish to solve to your own profit the most difficult problem created by bourgeois morals of the Revolution; and, more, you mean to begin by a life of retirement. Do you think your wife won’t crave the life you say you despise? If you won’t accept the noble conjugality I’ve outlined, at least hear my final advice. Remain a bachelor thirteen years; amuse yourself; at forty, on your first gout attack, marry a widow of thirty-six; then you may be happy. If you take a young girl now, you’ll die a madman.”“Ah ça! tell me why!” cried Paul, piqued.“My dear fellow,” de Marsay replied, “Boileau’s satire on women is common enough. But the problem of marriage is not where Boileau puts it. Do you suppose marriage equals love and that being a man suffices to make a wife love you? Have you learned nothing in your boudoir experience but agreeable memories? Everything in our bachelor life leads to fatal errors in married life unless one profoundly observes the human heart. In youth a man triumphs over women ready to be triumphed over. Obstacles created by law, sentiment and natural defenses engender sensations that deceive the superficial about future married relations, where obstacles no longer exist. The wife submits to love instead of permitting it and often repulses pleasure. The whole aspect of life changes. The bachelor, free and without care, need never fear repulsion; in marriage, repulsion is nearly certain and irreparable. A lover may sometimes change a woman’s unfavorable decision, but such a change is the husband’s Waterloo. Like Napoleon, the husband achieves victories which cannot undo the first defeat. The woman, flattered by perseverance as a lover, calls the same ardor brutality in a husband. You, who talk of marrying, have you reflected on the Civil Code? I never loved that Law-school’s gossip, but I see the Code’s application. The Code makes woman a ward; it treats her like a minor. How must we govern children? By fear. In that word, Paul, is the curb of the beast. Now feel your pulse! Have you the strength to play the tyrant—you, so gentle, so confiding? This is science—Anthropology. Ah! if I had not solved life’s mystery by pleasure, if I did not despise those who think instead of act, I’d write a book on modern marriages and stick a lantern on that heap of sharp stones where votaries of the social ‘multiplicamini’ lie. But does humanity require my time? Besides, isn’t ink better used to snare hearts with love-letters? Well, shall you bring the Comtesse de Manerville here and let us see her?”“Perhaps,” said Paul.“We shall still be friends,” said de Marsay.“If—” replied Paul.“Don’t be uneasy; we will treat you politely, as Maison-Rouge treated the English at Fontenoy.”############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Though the foregoing conversation affected the Comte de Manerville, he carried out his intentions and returned to Bordeaux in the winter of 1821.Restoring and furnishing his family mansion sustained the elegant reputation that preceded him. Reintroduced by old connections to Bordeaux’s royalist society—which his opinions, name, and fortune bound him to—he quickly took a fashionable pre‑eminence. The Faubourg Saint‑Germain of Bordeaux admired his knowledge of life and Parisian accomplishments. An old marquise revived a courtly phrase, calling him “the pink of fashion”; the liberal clique used it satirically, the royalists sincerely.Paul de Manerville fulfilled his flowery title. In public he seemed superior, and at ease displayed the fine qualities that accompanied his defects. His wit was mild; his manners not supercilious; his intercourse with women expressed a temperate respect they liked. His foppery was merely a carefulness of appearance that pleased. He respected rank, granted youth a certain freedom, and though adept with sword and pistol, was noted for a feminine gentleness. Medium height and plumpness did not prevent his playing the part of a Bordelais Brummell: fair complexion, handsome hands and feet, blue eyes fringed by long lashes, black hair, graceful motions, and a chest voice that vibrated in listeners’ hearts harmonized with his sobriquet. Paul was a delicate flower needing careful culture—one more to receive than to give happiness, with something of the woman in his nature—one to whom conjugal love should come as a providence.Such a character creates private difficulties but commands public attraction. Consequently Paul succeeded greatly in the narrow provincial circle, where his half‑tinted mind was better appreciated than it would have been in Paris.Restoring his town house and the château de Lanstrac—into which he introduced English country‑house comfort—consumed the capital the notary had saved over six years. Reduced now to his strict income of about forty thousand a year, he believed himself prudent in regulating his household accordingly.After flaunting his equipages, entertaining the town’s most distinguished young men, and giving hunting parties at Lanstrac, Paul saw that provincial life would not do without marriage. He was too young for stinginess or for speculative improvements provincials undertake to secure children; he needed the variety of Parisian life. A name to preserve, property to transmit, a household to create social relations, and a weariness of irregular connections were motives, but the chief was his secret love for Bordeaux’s queen, the great beauty Mademoiselle Evangelista.Around the century’s beginning a rich Spaniard named Evangelista had settled in Bordeaux; letters of recommendation and large fortune opened noble salons to him. His wife, a Creole from the Casa‑Reale, lived as a great lady, ignorant of money’s value and indulged by an adoring husband. From 1800 to 1812 the Evangelistas were objects of interest. The Spaniard died in 1813, leaving Madame Evangelista a thirty‑two‑year‑old widow with an immense fortune and a prettiest little girl of eleven who became an accomplished young woman. The Restoration shifted social positions; royalist ranks cleared and old families left Bordeaux. Lacking her husband’s direction, Madame Evangelista resolved not to change her mode of living: by squandering large sums on brilliant fêtes and a quasi‑regal life she maintained the public belief in her wealth while her capital steadily diminished.Natalie, now nineteen, seemed the richest match in Bordeaux, but proposals had not come. Accustomed to indulgence, she wore cashmeres and jewels and lived in a luxury that alarmed the calculating provincials. A fatal remark circulated: “None but a prince can afford to marry Mademoiselle Evangelista.” Mothers jealous of Natalie’s beauty and tyrannical elegance spread treacherous warnings—her costly toilette, horses, maid, Mechlin laces—deterring suitors. Pampered as she was, Natalie knew nothing of the value of money or household maintenance; she bounded through life unbridled and unshod.Six months after Paul’s arrival the Pink of Fashion and the Queen of Balls met in Bordeaux’s highest society. They exchanged apparently cold glances, each thinking the other charming. Madame Evangelista muttered, “He will be my son‑in‑law.” Paul thought, “She will be my wife.”The Evangelistas’ reputed wealth lingered in Paul’s memory, so their pecuniary condition was known to him. When society’s flattering remarks about Natalie ended with warnings about extravagance, Paul replied with disdain that shut down provincial pettiness. He brought to Bordeaux a certain Britannic reserve and Byronic pessimism, together with English plate, boot‑polish, ponies, yellow gloves, cigars, and a habit of galloping—thus avoiding discouragements that deterred other suitors. Madame Evangelista asked him to formal dinners; he accepted, pleased to breathe the grandeur and luxury he loved.When he rode by in his tilbury or on his English horse, young men said, “There’s a lucky man. He is rich and handsome, and is to marry, so they say, Mademoiselle Evangelista.” Society, which never does good without doing evil, credited Natalie with a dowry of a million and bestowed her, almost before consent, upon Paul. Their fortunes were thought to match: Paul’s habits of luxury and his newly arranged house made him seem uniquely fitted to meet the pecuniary difficulties of marrying such a daughter of a Creole great lady. Royalist friends flattered him: “Everyone gives you Mademoiselle Evangelista. You could not find, even in Paris, a more delightful girl. Her mother is intelligent and adroit; she will be a great help in public life. You must soon bring the matter to a close.”“That is all very well,” replied Paul, anxious to keep freedom, “but I must be sure that the conclusion shall be a happy one.”He therefore went often to the Hôtel Evangelista, partly to occupy idle hours harder for him than for most men. Madame Evangelista, at forty, had the beauty of a glorious summer sunset. Her spotless reputation was much discussed; her temperament combined the force and seductiveness of Spanish and Creole women. She had black eyes and hair, a swaying form and a complexion like muslin over crimson—white suffused with color. Full and rounded, she possessed the nonchalance of strength united with vivacity, sometimes the carriage of a queen. Men were taken by her conversation; she had the genius for schemes necessity bestows, advancing from concession to concession and retreating when asked for too much. Though ignorant of facts, her familiarity with courts and illustrious families gave a superficial but impressive education. She received society with natural grace and dignity, and her reputed virtue lent authority to her actions.Mother and daughter had a true friendship that went beyond maternal and filial bonds; perpetual contact produced no jar. Madame Evangelista’s perpetual widowhood was not explained solely by maternal devotion: she had once loved a man who recovered titles and property under the Restoration, courted her to marry in 1814, and severed the connection by 1816. She nursed a resentment—“Odiate e aspettate”—and relished a revengeful triumph when he later ruined himself. This misadventure and pride kept her from remarrying and turned her motherly devotion into an ambition to place her daughter at the heart of Parisian society.Madame Evangelista divined Paul’s nature and concealed her own. He was precisely the man she wanted for a son‑in‑law, an instrument for future influence. Through his mother he belonged to the Maulincour family; his connection to the Baronne de Maulincour and her grandson Auguste’s army position made Paul an excellent introducer for the Evangelistas into Parisian society. The widow, who disliked residing in Bordeaux, longed to shine in Restoration Paris, where political fortune lay. She looked to Paul as a means to that end; many men serve as screens for secret feminine ambitions, and Madame Evangelista had more than one interest in holding her daughter’s husband.Paul was captivated. The mother charmed him by seeming to seek no influence while subtly magnifying herself and her daughter in his eyes. He valued himself more for their appreciation; he fancied himself clever when his remarks pleased Natalie and flattered when the mother’s praise appeared involuntary. So kind and welcoming were they that he spent most of his time at the Hôtel Evangelista.A year after his return, though Paul had not declared himself, his attentions made the world consider him a suitor. Neither mother nor daughter pressed marriage. Natalie preserved the reserve of a great lady who can charm without allowing intimacy—a reserve that pleased Paul, timid and disliking sudden proposals. He therefore committed himself inwardly all the more. The mother’s remark that superior women sometimes place ambition above earlier emotions seduced him: “That woman is fitted,” he thought, “to advance me in diplomacy before I am even made a deputy.”Paul was an optimist; he saw everything to advantage and did not suspect that an ambitious mother‑in‑law might become a tyrant. He pictured himself married each evening and embraced wedlock cheerfully—bachelor life seemed dull. “Marriage,” he mused, “is disagreeable for people without means, but half its troubles disappear before wealth.” Every day some favorable consideration swelled his sense of advantage.“No matter what position I attain, Natalie will always be on the level of her part,” he told himself. “How many Empire men I’ve seen who suffered horribly through their wives! It is a great condition of happiness not to feel one’s pride or vanity wounded by the companion we have chosen. A man can never be really unhappy with a well‑bred wife; she will never make him ridiculous.” He taxed his memory among Faubourg Saint‑Germain women to see Natalie as their equal; all comparisons favored her, resting on his imagination, which followed his desires. Paris would have shown other types to balance his mind; in Bordeaux Natalie had no rivals and appeared when Paul was under an age‑old tyrant: the domination of an idea.Thus propinquity, self‑love, and a real passion whose outlet was marriage led Paul into an irrational love he prudently kept to himself. He tried to study Natalie, mindful of de Marsay’s warnings. But luxury breeds indifference; Paul never imagined the gulf of ruin beneath the ladies’ habits. Moreover, there are no rules to predict conjugal harmony; young people display favorable weather only, oblivious to life’s common gray days. Each shows a favorable mask; later they blame marriage for evils inherent in life.To detect in Natalie any sign of future imperfections would have required not only physiognomy but a kind of personal science few possess. Her face, like most young girls’, was impenetrable. The sculptural calm of virgin faces—Justice, Innocence—marked purity; such placid expression had not been clouded by passion or betrayal. If that expression were assumed, then no young woman remained behind it.Yet any clever analyst might find in Natalie indications of future difficulties. Her beauty arose from extreme regularity of feature and proportion—perfection that often foreshadows coldness of organization. Superior nature usually shows slight imperfections that become luminous attractions; perfect harmony often indicates a mixed, colder nature. Her round waist signaled strength and a will liable to obstinacy; statue‑like hands suggested an inclination for illogical domination; meeting eyebrows hinted at jealousy that in inferior minds becomes hatred. The “hate and wait” of her mother lived in her. Her eyes were blackish brown with orange streaks, her hair a ruddy auburn—an external contrast giving charm but not necessarily soul. Where outlines lack soft roundness, beauty is not of the soul. Such roses of youth will drop their leaves, revealing hardness and dryness beneath admired nobility.Though her face had some august outlines, her slightly full chin suggested passions that might grow violent; her astringent mouth expressed haughty pride consistent with hand, chin, brows, and figure. Her pure voice, though seductive, had metallic tones hinting at Casa‑Reale temperament. These marks suggested violent passions without tenderness,—sudden devotions, irreconcilable dislikes, a mind not deeply intelligent, and a desire to rule natural in those who feel their station inferior to their pretensions.These defects lay in Natalie like ore in a mine and would only surface under life’s shocks. Meantime grace, freshness, distinction, sacred ignorance, and youthful sweetness gave a glamour that misled unthinking minds. Her mother taught her the art of agreeable talk, replying to arguments with clever jests and concealing the subsoil of her mind with volubility. Natalie had the charm of those who never knew suffering: frank, gay, natural, expecting pleasure from marriage and answering objections with jest.How could Paul, dazzled by such beauty, foresee the woman she might be at thirty? Besides, happiness in such a marriage was not impossible. Embryo defects could be stifled by proper training, especially in a young girl who loved him. But to render ductile an intractable woman the iron wrist de Marsay recommended was necessary. Fear inspired by love is an instrument to manage women; he who loves fears, and thus is nearer affection than hatred.Had Paul the coolness, firmness, and judgment required for this struggle? Did Natalie love him? Like most young girls, she mistook instinct and the pleasure of his external appearance for love; she knew nothing of marriage or its demands. Would she have the tact to distinguish his noble qualities among lesser defects, or would she magnify the latter and forget the former, as inexperienced wives often do? There comes a time when wives pardon defects in a husband who spares them annoyances; what conciliating power or wise experience would uphold this home? Paul, weak where even a strong man runs risks, might yield to tyranny instead of establishing his rule. Could he say “No”?The subject here is not merely the change from bachelor to married man—a picture that would itself offer rich drama—but the great comedy that precedes all conjugal pairing in France: the discussion before a notary about the marriage contract, played in families noble and bourgeois, where human passions are excited by small interests as by large ones. This scene, neglected by dramatists though full of wit, controlled Paul’s future life and was awaited by Madame Evangelista with terror. It is to this comedy—the notarial negotiation—that we now turn.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################At the beginning of the winter of 1822, Paul de Manerville, through his great-aunt the Baronne de Maulincour, formally asked for Mademoiselle Natalie Evangelista’s hand. The baroness stayed in Médoc until late October to assist him and returned with good news.“My child,” she reported, “the affair is won. Madame Evangelista gives nothing of her own to her daughter; Mademoiselle Natalie’s dowry is her patrimony. Marry her. Men with a name and estate must marry to continue the family. Consult Maitre Mathias; the marriage contract is a sacred duty.”Troubled by his aunt’s hint that Madame Evangelista was a “sly cat,” Paul followed her counsel and engaged Maitre Mathias to draw the contract. He approached Madonna Evangelista with cautious courtesy to avoid offending her.“Of course,” she said when he mentioned his notary. “Notaries of both families usually agree.” Inwardly anxious, she concealed the truth: legally she owed her daughter one-third of Monsieur Evangelista’s fortune—about 1,156,000 francs—but lacked liquid means to pay it. If Paul learned this through his notary he might withdraw and spoil her plan. Confident she could secure Natalie’s happiness and find an escape if necessary, she resolved to conceal the difficulty.Madame Evangelista summoned her own notary, Maitre Solonet, a young, ambitious royalist dandy noted for gallantry and political zeal. Flattered to be received, he heard the situation bluntly.“Mademoiselle Natalie’s dowry is her patrimony,” Solonet said. “How have you comported yourself toward Monsieur de Manerville? In matters like these deportment matters more than law. Is the Comte de Manerville in love with Mademoiselle Natalie?”“He adores her,” the mother answered. Solonet counseled display and charm. “Make her look her best tonight. The marriage-contract dress is half the battle,” he declared.Madame Evangelista dressed Natalie herself. In white tulle with pink ribbons and hair à la Sévigné, Natalie looked charming. Her mother probed her feelings for Paul.“If you and I had to part forever would you still persist in the marriage?”“I should give it up,” Natalie replied.“You do not love him,” her mother said, kissing her brow.“But I love him,” Natalie insisted.Madame Evangelista warned that if Paul hesitated at sight of them in all their glory, she would break off the match and seek another husband. Natalie, loyal and curious, promised that if property difficulties separated them she alone would bear the blame.Paul arrived with his habitual bouquet. He and Natalie spoke fondly of the future—Lanstrac, Paris, children—while awaiting the notaries. But the business of marriage soon intruded. For Paul the alliance seemed simple; yet the legal and financial details were a minefield. The two notaries embodied different eras and temperaments.Maitre Mathias, Paul’s notary, was an elderly professional with forty years’ experience: slight, odd in appearance, respected for uprightness, painstaking care, and long faithful service. Solonet, by contrast, was young, fashionable, mixing politics with practice, eager for profit and advancement. When they met at Madame Evangelista’s salon they represented “former times and the present day.”Solonet, eager to secure his client’s ends, had pre-drawn a contract favoring the widow. Mathias insisted on details and clarity. He catalogued Paul’s property—Lanstrac (23,000 francs rental), farms and vineyards, houses in Bordeaux and Paris, furniture—totaling significant landed wealth. Solonet countered that the Evangelista inheritance had been invested in Five-per-cent funds and amounted effectively to about 800,000 francs, that the Paris house added value, and that the mother could convey titles while reserving a life interest.Mathias challenged vague claims and demanded inventories. Solonet replied the family, being Spanish and Creole, had not followed French formalities at the father’s death and had reinvested English securities in Paris, making strict accounting difficult. When Mathias pressed the difference between land and marketable funds, the conversation turned heated. Solonet aimed for a settlement preserving the widow’s respectability while releasing Natalie’s patrimony; Mathias sought to protect Paul’s estate and his children’s future.Natalie, playing with a fan, watched and then, moved by loyalty, told Paul in private that if property difficulties separated them she alone would bear the blame. Paul, not yet told of Mathias’s alarm, pressed her hand and returned to the group. Mathias, concerned, warned Paul in a private aside that there was little ready dowry; he advised postponement to reflect.Solonet quickly offered a way out: sell the Paris house, reckon the Five-per-cents as nearly at par, and convey titles into Natalie’s name while reserving a life interest for Madame Evangelista. This would give immediate recognition of a large patrimony and close the guardianship account. Mathias saw a trap: the scheme would acknowledge receipt of about 1,156,000 francs, binding Paul to account to his children for that sum later while the family actually had less secure capital. He protested that the arrangement would impoverish Paul and his heirs and leave him liable under misfortune.Madame Evangelista, provoked, threatened to break off the marriage and leave Bordeaux; she appealed to Spanish ways and the sentimental value of her name as a kind of dowry. The argument grew tense: Paul, torn between love and prudence, nearly yielded to emotion. Mathias, seeing Paul’s distress and Madame Evangelista’s ambition—she wanted Paul to become a peer of France—suddenly conceived a legal remedy that could reconcile interests.Mathias proposed that to secure Paul’s future public prospects and protect his estate, the parties entail Lanstrac and the Paris house and include eight hundred thousand francs of Natalie’s patrimony in that entail by purchasing adjoining domains and having them included in the royal ordinance required for an entail. In other words, apply the convertible portion of Natalie’s fortune to create inalienable capital that would strengthen the family’s landed estate, thus satisfying Madame Evangelista’s desire to release Natalie’s patrimony and calming Paul’s and Mathias’s fear of immediate impoverishment. This would allow Mathias to accept the guardianship account’s deficiency.Solonet was surprised but recognized that the entail would placate distrust: an entail makes property inalienable and would reassure Paul and his advisers that the capital could not be wasted. Madame Evangelista, ignorant of legal technicalities, accepted Mathias’s plan because it seemed to secure Natalie’s patrimony and her own safety. Both she and Paul agreed that if the entail were created, the marriage should proceed.Mathias emphasized that the formalities required a royal ordinance and immediate action, but that consent to proceed would suffice for the parties to go on. Natalie, a minor, was not party to the formal bargaining beyond assent.It was arranged that Mathias would draw up the contract, Solonet the guardianship account and release, and both documents should be signed days before the marriage. The notaries left together, each satisfied professionally: Solonet for getting his client married under an appearance of security, Mathias for having devised a legal mechanism to protect Manerville.Left alone, Paul and Madame Evangelista reconciled their earlier sharp words. Paul was ecstatic at the prospect that, with the entail and a combined household, he and Natalie could have an income near one hundred thousand francs and he might obtain a peerage. Madame Evangelista, relieved, expressed gratitude and tenderness; she proposed to give Natalie her diamonds and assured Paul she would sell her house and find means to secure herself without injuring her daughter’s patrimony.Natalie reacted with emotional generosity, insisting her father’s diamonds remain but accepting that her mother’s sacrifice might be required. Madame Evangelista planned to have the jewels appraised and to sell plate and other items. She spoke of moving winters to Paris, of Lanstrac as their home, and of Solonet’s aid in settling debts. She fell asleep convinced she had achieved her ends: Natalie married to a man she could influence, a household assured, and an ambitious plan for Paul’s political advancement.Thus the first day of negotiation ended with apparent satisfaction on all sides: Paul convinced, Madame Evangelista appeased, Natalie content, Mathias relieved that a protective entail might save Manerville, and Solonet pleased at having guided the match. Yet beneath this harmony lay seeds of distrust and competing designs—Madame Evangelista believing herself triumphant, Mathias guarding his client’s legacy, Solonet enjoying the victory, and Paul dreaming of a bright future of love and ambition.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The next day Elie Magus, then in Bordeaux, obeyed Madame Evangelista’s summons. Rumors that Comte Paul was marrying Mademoiselle Natalie made people assume she wanted jewels for the bride; instead she had him value the mother-in-law’s property, suspecting the diamonds’ worth would be part of the marriage contract. She instructed him to appraise them as if for a private buyer, though they would not be sold. Magus explained how experts distinguish Asiatic from Brazilian diamonds: Golconda and Visapur stones have superior whiteness and brilliance, while Brazilian stones have a yellow tinge. He valued Madame Evangelista’s necklace and earrings, entirely Asiatic diamonds, at 250,000 francs, and called the “Discreto” one of the finest private diamonds, worth 100,000 francs. She asked if she could obtain that money immediately.Magus replied that a dealer would pay only seventy-five thousand for the brilliant and one hundred and sixty thousand for the necklace and earrings, explaining the dealers’ margin: rarer gems may remain unsold longer, so buying prices are lower than selling prices; owning them ties up capital. He illustrated the practical cost of wearing diamonds and, when asked about the rest, valued other jewels—Brazilian diamonds, pearls, rubies—at about one hundred thousand to a dealer, though they might resell for a hundred and fifty thousand. Madame Evangelista declared she would keep them; Magus warned that investing the income from that capital could replace similar diamonds in five years while preserving the principal.The conference leaked. Servants overheard raised voices; gossip exaggerated practical difficulties into scandal: had Madame Evangelista sold her house? Was the marriage off? Notaries denied any collapse, blaming only delay in constituting the entail, but society persisted. Young women and thwarted provincial mothers, envious after two decades of Evangelista magnificence, spread and embellished the tale. Officials at the prefecture hinted the notaries’ language implied trouble. People speculated about quibbles over the entail and were outraged at the idea that a wealthy widow might lack funds to pay her daughter’s patrimony. Bets were laid; yet no one told Paul or Madame Evangelista the gossip. Paul had business at Lanstrac and gave a hunting-party as a kind of farewell to bachelor life; society interpreted this as evidence of trouble.Madame de Gyas, hoping to marry her daughter, felt obliged to sound out the Evangelistas and approached them with a long face as if to disclose misfortune. Natalie and her mother laughed at the recital of gossip but recognized the motives: provincial mothers, thwarted by lack of dowry or fortune, might imagine such plots. The Marquise launched into a spirited harangue about mothers desperate to secure husbands for daughters, likening them to predatory creatures and mocking public judgment of private marriage clauses. Madame Evangelista, preparing to leave the city, could safely mock both friends and enemies and enjoyed making remarks that stung.When a footman announced Paul—who had ridden twelve miles to spend an hour with Natalie—she greeted him; he dismissed the gossip with laughter. “These worthy people have found out, perhaps, that there will be no wedding festivities,” he said, and suggested pacifying them with a ball on the day they signed the contract: a public show to refute the rumors. Madame Evangelista took him at his word and invited all Bordeaux to witness the contract signature; she intended the last fête to silence the gossip.Preparations for the camellia fête consumed a month. Camellias banked staircases and supper-rooms; partitions were removed to enlarge rooms; estates near Lanstrac were purchased, banns published, and doubts dissipated. For a while the earlier stormy conference faded—after all, as Madame Evangelista had said, it had seemed only the notaries’ affair.Yet on the morning of the signing a flash of memory returned to Madame Evangelista: Mathias’s earlier words when he had yielded to Solonet’s conditions—“Questa coda non e di questo gatto”—suggested someone else would bear the cost. If Mathias had seen compensation, it could not be Paul. Could her daughter’s fortune be made to meet the price? She resolved to seek explanations for the contract’s tenor. This day would profoundly affect Paul de Manerville’s conjugal life.Despite the winter, the courtyard was tented, shrubs and banked camellias decorated the house, and the ballroom and supper-room were fitted for the fairyland Bordeaux expected. About eight, as the final contract was discussed, a dense crowd gathered to see the ladies in full dress. The two notaries had dined with the bridal pair and their mother; Mathias’s head-clerk was present to receive signatures and guard against surreptitious readings.No bridal toilette equalled Natalie’s; she was superb. Madame Evangelista, in cherry velvet, wore her pearl necklace clasped with the “Discreto,” a visible contradiction to calumnies of ruin. Paul and Natalie sat together by the fire, inattentive to the reading, whispering and touching; Natalie toyed with a peacock-feather fan Paul had given her. Seated by the notaries, Madame Evangelista listened as Solonet read the guardianship account: Natalie’s share of Monsieur Evangelista’s three-million-plus estate amounted to eleven hundred and fifty-six thousand francs. “Come, listen, listen, my children; this is your marriage contract,” she said.The clerical routine proceeded: statements of property, general deeds of gift in case of death without issue, deed of gift of a life interest and a quarter of capital allowed by the Code, constitution of a common fund, settlement of the diamonds on the wife, the library and horses on the husband, and then the constitution of the entail. When all but signatures remained, Madame Evangelista asked what an entail would ultimately mean. Solonet explained generally, but Mathias bluntly defined the entail’s practical effect: if the wife died first leaving several children including a son, Monsieur de Manerville would owe those children only three hundred and sixty thousand francs, from which he would deduct his fourth in life-interest and his fourth in capital, reducing his debt to about one hundred and sixty thousand francs, excluding savings and profits from the common fund. If the husband died first leaving a male heir, Madame de Manerville’s right would be three hundred and sixty thousand francs plus her deeds of gift of property not in the entail, the diamonds settled on her, and profits and savings from the common fund.The consequence struck Madame Evangelista: “My daughter is ruined,” she whispered. Both notaries overheard. Mathias, with gentle irony, replied that constituting an indestructible fortune for her family could hardly be called ruin. The younger notary, seeing her face, whispered bluntly that they had tried to trick them out of three hundred thousand francs but actually the children had secured eight hundred thousand—arguing that compared with that security, any supposed loss was only four hundred thousand from their interests for the children’s benefit. He advised her to break the marriage immediately or carry it through.Silence followed. Natalie, unaware she had lost half her fortune, and Paul, ignorant the Manerville house had gained, conversed lightly. Solonet and Madame Evangelista exchanged glances—one masking indifference, the other curbing bitter feeling. Madame Evangelista’s remorse and indignation at having compromised her honor in Natalie’s interest turned to fury when she realized she would be the loser and that Mathias must have foreseen this and enlightened Paul. The thought that the old notary might soon be lauded for his sagacity and turn Paul against her was intolerable. She faced a dilemma: three months earlier she might have broken the match; now banns were published, friends had arrived for the fête, and postponing would bring public scandal. She felt trapped and knew she must yield.Her anger surfaced: her chin trembled; Solonet noticed the muscles play and read the birth of a mortal hatred. She turned to Mathias, accusing him of incapacity or worse, of seeing the entail’s effect and not warning her sooner. The younger notary drew her aside, thinking of his fees but speaking coolly: she had overstepped legal craft for her own sake; if she did not want Comte Paul, she need not accept him—the contract was not yet signed; she could postpone it and blame some error in Paris. He suggested she might find another count if title were the chief aim. She protested that she could not chance her honor. He insisted that Mademoiselle Natalie’s happiness mattered most and argued that she, as wife, could dominate the home and lead Paul by the nose; if she played her cards right, the count’s fate was within her power. This mollified Madame Evangelista; she declared she would be grateful.Mathias and Solonet then amended the contract to protect the wife’s interests further. The young notary proposed—and Mathias concurred—clauses to ensure that if Manerville died without children or left only daughters, the entailed property should pass under the general deed of gift between husband and wife or that the entail be declared void. Solonet added this clause in the margin. Madame Evangelista listened, dropped her eyes while Mathias read the added sentence aloud.“We will now sign,” she said, her voice betraying violent emotion. She thought: she would not allow her daughter to be ruined; she intended that Natalie should have the name, title, and fortune. If Natalie later loved another, Paul would be driven out of France. Her mind resolved on measures to keep her daughter rich and free. Mathias took Paul aside and explained the saving stipulation—that he held the documents and that the contract contained a rescript for the diamonds; Paul must ask for them and could use them toward payments. Mathias spoke in practical terms: the first payment of two hundred thousand francs was due after formalities; use the diamonds; the house could be sold to pay the rest; plant vineyards to raise income. Paul pressed Mathias’s hand affectionately; Madame Evangelista noticed and suspected collusion. Rage and hatred overwhelmed her.After verifying signatures and initials, Mathias looked at the mother-in-law. Seeing Paul say nothing of the diamonds, Mathias remarked there could be no doubt about their transfer now they were family. Solonet urged it was more regular for Madame Evangelista to make them over immediately since Monsieur de Manerville had become responsible for guardianship funds and one never knew who might live or die. Paul protested that immediate transfer would be insulting; Madame Evangelista, angered at what she took as an insult masked in Mathias’s words, cried she would tear the contract up if he did not take the diamonds.She left in fury, nearly mad with impotence. Natalie whispered to Paul to take them: her mother was angry and she would know why that night; they must pacify her. Calmed, Madame Evangelista kept the necklace and earrings she wore and brought the other jewels, which Magus had valued at 150,000 francs. Mathias and Solonet examined them and found they would meet the first payments. Solonet assured Paul he would lose nothing on the spot; Mathias said the jewels would meet the first payment on the estate purchase and the contract costs.Hatred fed on small things; Madame Evangelista took Paul’s reluctance to accept the jewels as hypocrisy. She interpreted his embarrassment—he did not want them—as hypocrisy and flung a look that seemed to say “Take your property.” Paul, eager to end the dispute, told Natalie to lock the jewels away as her own gift. Natalie stowed them in a console drawer. At that moment carriages and voices swelled; salons filled and the fête began.During the ball, whispers and judgments circulated among guests: speculation about the marriage’s financial and social results, about Madame Evangelista’s supposed penury, and about Mathias’s hand in safe-guarding the family. Some predicted future domestic friction; others doubted whether Mademoiselle Evangelista, a merchant’s daughter, could hold her place socially. Card-players took tables, the young danced, supper was served, and the ball lasted till morning.After Paul left, Madame Evangelista and Natalie retired. Though overtired, they exchanged a few words. Madame Evangelista confessed she had acted out of tender devotion and unscrupulous business ignorance to secure Natalie’s happiness; she had trampled her pride. Natalie, half asleep, spoke warmly of Paul’s innocence; she had no idea her mother’s fears or the implications of the contract. In the dark, Madame Evangelista felt the first stirrings of avarice induced by passion and hate: a sudden resolve to secure her daughter’s entire fortune by reclaiming her husband’s property for Natalie. She told herself honor required it; devotion to Natalie made her shrewd and calculating. She planned to invest capital in the Funds and to manage matters carefully. Passion and hatred had transformed the once-prodigal woman into a miser and a plotter. She fell asleep muttering, “To-morrow!” Her mind, in sleep, marshalled ideas and arranged a plan to rule Paul’s life—a plan she began to put into action the next day.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Though the feast had driven away Paul’s anxious thoughts, they returned to torment him in bed.“It seems to me,” he told himself, “that without that good Mathias my mother-in-law would have tricked me. Yet why should she deceive me? Are we not joining fortunes? Well, in two days Natalie will be my wife; our money relations are defined. Nevertheless, I’ll be upon my guard. Suppose Mathias was right? If so, I needn’t marry my mother-in-law.”In this second struggle over the contract Paul’s future had already shifted, though he did not know it. Of the two persons he was marrying, one—the cleverer—had become his mortal enemy, already planning to withdraw her interests from the common fund. Paul, incapable of reading a Creole temperament, did not suspect her craftiness. The Creole blends European intellect, tropical violence of passion, and an apathetic Eastern indifference; graceful but dangerous, like an unwatched child. She broods over desires until she hatches them; impassioned, she plans. Naïve yet perfidious from her upbringing among Negroes, bolstered by Spanish obstinacy and glazed by French politeness—this described Madame Evangelista.Dormant for sixteen years in married ease and social trifles, that nature now flamed: she was about to lose her dearest object and needed a new outlet. Natalie would be apart from her in three days. Vanquished on other counts, Madame Evangelista had this one clear day—the last day a daughter spends with her mother. A few words could sway two lives; Natalie believed her blindly. No institution can prevent a crime that kills by words—the weakness of social law; the great world uses venomous ideas instead of knives.The next morning, mid-day, Madame Evangelista half-sat on Natalie’s bed. In that waking hour they caressed and recalled a life without discord.“Poor darling!” the mother wept. “How can I be calm when I think that after I’ve fulfilled your every wish you will belong to a man you must obey?”“Oh, mamma, obeying!” Natalie made a graceful gesture of mock rebellion. “You are joking. Father gratified your caprices. I am loved too.”“Yes, Paul loves you; but conjugal love evaporates if a married woman is not careful. The influence a wife should have depends on how she begins. You need the best advice.”“But you will be with us.”“Possibly. Last night I reflected on the dangers of our being together. If my presence injures you—if the little acts that should slowly establish your authority are attributed to me—your home will be a hell. At the first frown of your husband I, proud as I am, should instantly leave. If I were driven to leave, better not to enter at all. I should never forgive your husband for causing trouble between us. Whereas, when you are once mistress, when your husband is to you what your father was to me, that danger disappears. Though painful, you must become absolute sovereign of your home.”“Then why did you say just now I must obey him?”“My dear, to rule you must seem to do what he wishes. If you were not told this, impulsive opposition might destroy your future. Paul is a weak man; he might allow a friend or another woman to rule him. Prevent such disasters by making yourself his ruler from the start. Is it not better he be governed by you than by others?”“Yes, certainly,” Natalie said. “I shall think only of his happiness.”“And it is my privilege to think only of yours,” the mother answered. “I will not leave you without a compass among the reefs you must steer.”“But, mamma, are we not strong enough together, you and I, to stay beside him? Paul loves you.”“Oh! He fears me more than he loves me. Watch him when I tell him I shall not go to Paris; you will see, though he hides it, his inward joy.”“Why should he feel so?”“I will tell him myself and before you.”“But suppose I marry on condition that you do not leave me?” urged Natalie.“Our separation is necessary,” Madame Evangelista declared. “Several considerations have changed my future. I am poor now. You will lead a brilliant life in Paris, and I could not live with you suitably without spending my little remaining capital. If I go to Lanstrac, I can manage your property there and restore my fortune by economy.”“You, mamma! You practise economy!” Natalie laughed. “Do you mean to leave me for such reasons? Paul is not selfish.”“Ah!” Madame Evangelista’s tone suggested more; the girl’s heart throbbed. “Those discussions about the contract have made me distrustful. I have my doubts about him—But don’t be troubled, dear child,” she added, kissing her, “I will not leave you long. Whenever my return can take place without making difficulty, whenever Paul can rightly judge me, we will resume our happy life—”“Oh, mother, how can you think of living without your Natalie?”“Because, dear angel, I shall live for her. A mother’s heart will be satisfied thinking I contribute to your happiness.”“But must I be alone with Paul, already? What will become of me? What must I do or not do?”“Poor child!
