Do you think I would utterly abandon you for your first battle? We will write three times a week like lovers. Nothing will happen to you that I shall not know, and I can save you from misfortune. Besides, it would be ridiculous if I never came to see you; it would suggest dislike or disrespect to your husband. I shall always spend a month or two every year with you in Paris.”“Alone, already alone, with him!” cried Natalie.“But you wish to be his wife?”“Yes, I wish it. Tell me how to behave—you who did what you pleased with my father. I’ll obey you blindly.”Madame Evangelista kissed her forehead; she had willed and awaited this request.“Child, my counsel must adapt to circumstances. All men are not alike; I can only give general advice.”“Tell me, quickly, all you know.”“First, the cause of failure for married women who wish to keep their husbands’ hearts—and to keep hearts and rule them is one and the same—is perpetual intercourse. This never existed in old times but has been introduced by the mania for family. Since the Revolution bourgeois manners invaded aristocratic homes. Rousseau's heresy pretended all women had the same rights and faculties; as if a grandee’s wife had anything in common with women of the people! Well-bred women have stuck at home; life became involved; perfect harmony is almost impossible. Perpetual contact is as dangerous for parents and children as for husband and wife. Few souls survive omnipresence. Erect between yourself and Paul the barriers of society; go to balls and operas; go out mornings, dine out evenings, pay visits constantly, and give little time to your husband. By this you keep your value. When two beings live on sentiment alone, its resources are soon exhausted; indifference, satiety, contempt succeed. Be ever young and new to him. Even if he wearies you, you must never weary him. The faculty of being bored without showing it is a condition of all power. You cannot diversify happiness by property or family cares; if you don’t make him share your social interests and amuse him, you will fall into apathy—the SPLEEN of love. A man will always love the woman who amuses him and keeps him happy. To give happiness and to receive it are two feminine lines separated by a gulf.”“Dear mother, I am listening, but I don’t understand one word,” Natalie said.“If you love Paul so much that you do all he asks, if you make your happiness depend on him, all is over; you will never be mistress of your home, and rules will not help.”“That is plainer; but how to apply it?” laughed Natalie. “I have the theory; practice will come.”“My poor Ninie,” the mother said, sadly, “things will teach you—and remember, Natalie: we all have a destiny as women. A woman is born to be a woman of the world and a charming hostess, as a man is born to be a general or a poet. Your vocation is to please. Your education formed you for society. Nowadays women should be educated for the salon as once for the gynoecium. You were not born to be mother and steward. If you have children, I hope they will not spoil your figure at once; nothing is so bourgeois as having a child immediately. If they come two or three years later, governesses and tutors will bring them up. YOU are to be the lady who represents luxury and pleasure. But make your superiority visible only in things that flatter a man’s self-love; hide superiority acquired in greater matters.”“But you frighten me, mamma,” cried Natalie. “How can I remember all these precepts? How shall I, such a child and heedless, reflect and calculate before acting?”“But, my dear little girl, I tell you today what you must learn later by experience bought with faults and regrets.”“How must I begin?” asked Natalie artlessly.“Instinct will guide you,” her mother answered. “At this moment Paul desires you more than he loves you; love born of desire is hope; love after satisfaction is reality. There lies your power. What woman is not loved before marriage? Be so afterward and you will remain so always. Paul is weak, trained to habit. If he yields once he will yield always. A woman ardently desired can ask all things; do not waste the first hours of your sway on trifles. Use the empire his first emotions give you to accustom him to obedience. When you make him yield, choose some unreasonable point to test his concession. What victory is there in making him agree to a reasonable thing? Like the Castilian proverb says, take the bull by the horns; when a bull sees his defense useless he is beaten. When your husband does a foolish thing for you, you can govern him.”“Why so?”“Because marriage lasts a lifetime, and a husband is not a man like other men. Therefore never give yourself into his power in everything. Keep reserve in speech and action. You may be cold to him without danger; you can modify coldness at will. Nothing is easier to maintain than dignity. The words ‘It is not becoming in your wife to do thus’ are a talisman. A woman’s life lies in the words ‘I will not.’ They are the final argument; feminine power is in them, used on real occasions. They govern far beyond argument. I reigned over your father by his faith in me. If your husband believes in you, you can do all things with him. To inspire that belief you must make him think you understand him. A woman can always make a man think he is loved; to make him admit he is understood is harder. I tell you this now, for tomorrow life with its complications begins. Bear in mind that difficulty. The only means of harmonizing two wills is to arrange from the first that there shall be but one; and that will must be yours. Many say a wife creates unhappiness by wavering; but she becomes mistress only by controlling events instead of bearing them.”Natalie kissed her mother’s hands with tears of gratitude. Quick to feel but not practically wise, she still returned to a personal plea:“Dear mamma,” she said, “a few days ago you spoke of Paul’s advancement and said you alone could promote it; why then do you abandon us?”“I did not then know the extent of my obligations nor the amount of my debts,” the mother replied, concealing her real motive. “Besides, a year hence I can take up that matter again. Come, let us dress; Paul will be here soon. Be as sweet and caressing as when we first discussed this fatal contract; to-day we must save the last fragments of our fortune, and I must win for you something to which I am superstitiously attached.”“What is it?”“The ‘Discreto.’”Paul arrived about four. Though trying to look gracious, traces of the earlier counsels showed on his face.“Mathias has told him!” Madame Evangelista thought, resolving to defeat the notary. “My dear son,” she said, “you left your diamonds in the console drawer; I frankly confess I would rather not see again the things that threatened to bring a cloud between us. Besides, as Monsieur Mathias said, they ought to be sold at once to meet the first payment on the estates you have purchased.”“They are not mine,” Paul said. “I gave them to Natalie, and when you see them upon her you will forget the pain they caused you.”Madame Evangelista took his hand and pressed it with a tear.“Listen, my dear children,” she said, looking from Paul to Natalie; “I find myself obliged to sell my pearl necklace and ear-rings. Yes, Paul, it is necessary; I do not choose to put a penny of my fortune into an annuity; I know my duty to you. But I have a weakness; to sell the ‘Discreto’ seems a disaster. To sell a diamond that bears the name of Philip the Second and once adorned his royal hand—no, I cannot! Elie Magus values my necklace and ear-rings at a hundred and some odd thousand francs without the clasps. Will you exchange the jewels I made over to you for these? You will gain by the transaction. I am not selfish. Instead of those mere fancies, your wife will have fine diamonds she can really enjoy. Isn’t it better I sell ornaments that will go out of fashion, and that you keep these priceless stones in the family?”“But, mother, consider yourself,” Paul said.“I,” she answered, “I want such things no longer. Yes, Paul, I am going to be your bailiff at Lanstrac. It would be folly to go to Paris when I ought to be here to liquidate my property and settle my affairs. I shall grow miserly for my grandchildren.”“Dear mother,” Paul, moved, said, “ought I to accept without paying you the difference?”“Good heavens! are you not my dearest interests? Do you suppose I shall not find happiness thinking, as I sit by the chimney, ‘Natalie is dazzling tonight at the Duchesse de Berry’s ball’? When she wears my diamond she will have a little vanity that adds to a woman’s happiness. Nothing saddens a woman more than repressed vanity; I’ve never seen an ill-dressed woman amiable.”“What was Mathias thinking?” Paul wondered. “Well then, mamma,” he said low, “I accept.”“But I am confounded!” Natalie cried.At that moment Solonet arrived: among Bordeaux speculators he’d found two contractors for the house grounds.“They offer two hundred and fifty thousand francs,” he said; “but if you consent I can make them give three hundred thousand. There are three acres.”“My husband paid two hundred thousand for the place, therefore I consent,” Natalie replied. “But reserve the furniture and mirrors.”“Ah!” Solonet said. “You are beginning to understand business.”“Alas! I must,” she sighed.“I am told many are coming to your midnight service,” Solonet added, sensing his presence was inopportune.Madame Evangelista accompanied him to the salon door and said low: “I now have personal property to the amount of two hundred and fifty thousand francs; if I get two hundred thousand for my share of the house it will make capital I need to invest. I count on you. I shall probably live at Lanstrac.”The notary kissed her hand; her tone made him fancy the alliance might extend beyond business.“You can count on me,” he replied. “I can find investments in merchandise with no risk and good profits.”“Adieu until tomorrow,” she said. “You are to be our witness, with Monsieur le Marquis de Gyas.”“My dear mother,” Paul asked when she returned, “why do you refuse to come to Paris? Natalie is provoked, as if I caused your decision.”“I have thought it over, my children, and I am sure I should hamper you. You would feel obliged to make me a third in all you did, and young people have ideas I might unintentionally thwart. Go to Paris. I do not wish to exercise over the Comtesse de Manerville the gentle authority I held over Natalie. I desire to leave her wholly to you. Don’t you see, Paul, there are habits between us which must be broken? My influence ought to yield to yours. I want you to love me and to believe I have your interests at heart. Young husbands grow jealous of a wife’s love for her mother. Perhaps rightly. When you are thoroughly united, my living with you would not be feared. I have seen many homes destroyed by blind mothers who became intolerable. Let me make one more sacrifice for your happiness. I have given you my fortune, and now I desire to resign my last vanities as a woman. Your notary Mathias is getting old. He cannot look after your estates as I will. I will be your bailiff; I will create for myself those occupations which are the pleasures of old age. Later, if necessary, I will come to Paris and second you in your ambitions. Come, Paul, be frank; my proposal suits you, does it not?”Paul would not admit it, but he was delighted to get his liberty. Mathias’s suspicions about Madame Evangelista were dissipated by this talk, which she prolonged.“My mother was right,” Natalie thought, watching Paul. “He is glad to know I am separated from her—why?”That “why” was the first note of rising distrust; did it prove the power of her maternal instructions?Certain people stop at single proofs; Paul’s confiding nature relied on effects, not causes. His weakness sprang more from kindness and belief in good than from moral defect.Natalie was sad and thoughtful, not knowing what to do without her mother. Paul, confident in love, smiled at her sadness, thinking Paris and marriage would soon dispel it. Madame Evangelista saw this with satisfaction: she had already taken two great steps—her daughter had the diamonds that cost Paul two hundred thousand francs; and she had gained her point of leaving the two to themselves with no other guide than their illogical love. Her revenge was preparing, unknown to Natalie, who would become its accomplice. Did Natalie love Paul? That question, still undecided, might modify her projects, for she loved her daughter too much to disregard her happiness. Paul’s future thus still depended on himself: if he could make his wife love him, he was saved.The next day, at midnight, after a formal dinner for the four witnesses—dinner Madame Evangelista had given—the bridal pair, accompanied by friends, heard mass by torchlight before an inquisitive crowd. A marriage at night suggests an omen; light symbolizes life and pleasure, lacking at a midnight wedding. Darkness chills, its black arches sadden. Natalie, parted from her mother, wept—seized by the doubts that grip any heart entering a new career filled with pitfalls. She was cold and wanted a mantle. The air and manner of Madame Evangelista and the bridal pair excited comment among the elegant crowd.“Solonet tells me the bride and bridegroom leave for Paris tomorrow, all alone.”“Madame Evangelista was to live with them, I thought.”“Count Paul has got rid of her already.”“What a mistake!” said the Marquise de Gyas. “To shut the door on the mother is to open it to a lover. Doesn’t he know what a mother is?”“He has been very hard on Madame Evangelista; the poor woman sold her house and diamonds, and is going to live at Lanstrac.”“Natalie looks very sad.”“Would you like to be made to take a journey the day after your marriage?”“It is awkward.”“I am glad I came,” said a lady. “I am now convinced of the necessity of a wedding’s pomps; a scene like this is bare and sad. If I may say, this marriage seems indecent.”Madame Evangelista took Natalie in her carriage and accompanied her alone to Paul’s house.“Well, mother, it is done!” said Natalie.“Remember, my dear, my last advice, and you will be happy. Be his wife, not his mistress.”When Natalie retired, the mother flung herself with tears into the arms of her son-in-law—an apparently provincial display she allowed herself for effect. Amid tears and speeches, she obtained from Paul concessions all husbands make.The next day she put the married pair into their carriage, accompanied them to the ferry crossing the Gironde toward Paris, and with a look and a word enabled her daughter to see that if Paul had won the contract-game, her revenge was beginning. Natalie was already reducing her husband to perfect obedience.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Five years later, on an autumn afternoon, Comte Paul de Manerville, cloaked and bearing a bowed, mysterious air, arrived at the house of his old friend Maître Mathias in Bordeaux.Mathias, retired and too old to practice, was out on urgent business; his housekeeper Josette showed the count to Madame Mathias’s room. Fatigued by his journey, Paul slept until evening. When Mathias returned he climbed to the room and watched him sleep, as a mother watches a child; Josette followed, hands on hips.“It is a year today, Josette, since I heard my dear wife’s last sigh; I little thought then I should see the count half dead,” murmured Mathias.“Poor man! he moans in his sleep,” said Josette.“Sac à papier!” the notary muttered. “At any rate I have saved the title to Lanstrac for him, and Ausac, Saint-Froult, and his house, though the usufruct is gone.” He counted on his fingers. “Five years! since his aunt, Madame de Maulincour, asked for the hand of that little crocodile who has ruined him—as I expected.”Leaning on his cane, the gouty old man paced the garden until Paul awoke. At supper Mathias, struck by Paul’s outward calm but noting signs of mental strain that had aged him, urged him to sit.“Dear Mathias, you too have troubles,” Paul said.“Mine are natural, Monsieur le comte; but yours—”“We will speak of that over supper.”“If I had not a son in the magistracy and a married daughter,” Mathias began, “I would have done more than offer hospitality. Why come to Bordeaux when placards announce seizure of your farms at Grassol and Guadet, the vineyard of Belle-Rose and the family mansion? To see my name on those yellow posters—ah! a notary of forty-one years’ standing must feel ashamed.” He opened the paper: “‘At the request of Dame Natalie Evangelista, wife of Paul-François-Joseph, Comte de Manerville, separated from him as to worldly goods and chattels by the Lower court of the department of the Seine—’”“Yes, and now separated in body,” Paul answered.“Ah!” cried Mathias.“Oh! against my wife’s will,” Paul hastened. “I had to deceive her; she didn’t know I was leaving.”“You have left her?”“My passage is taken; I sail for Calcutta on the Belle-Amélie.”“Two days hence! Then we shall never meet again,” Mathias lamented.“You are only seventy-three and have the gout, Mathias. When I return I shall be forty and rebuild my fortune. I intend to make a noble fortune in seven years. All is possible.”“You? to undertake commerce!” Mathias protested.“I am no longer ‘Monsieur le comte,’ dear Mathias. I sail under the name Camille. Commerce is my final chance. I start with a sum to redeem my future on a large scale.”“Where is that money?”“A friend will send it.”The notary dropped his fork; his look expressed grief at Paul’s illusion. “I have been fifty years in the notariat,” he said, “and I never knew a ruined man whose friend would lend him money.”“You don’t know de Marsay. He has sold some investments already; to-morrow you will receive from him a bill of exchange for one hundred and fifty thousand francs.”Mathias brightened. “If that be so, cannot your friend settle your difficulties here? You could live at Lanstrac five or six years on your wife’s income and recover.”“No assignment or economy could pay off fifteen hundred thousand francs of debt, in which my wife is involved to the amount of five hundred and fifty thousand.”“You cannot mean you have incurred a million and a half of debt in four years?”“Nothing is more certain, Mathias. I gave those diamonds to my wife; I spent the 150,000 from selling Madame Evangelista’s house on the Paris house; I used other money for payments required by the marriage contract. I sold Natalie’s forty thousand a year in the Funds at eighty-seven, so I became two hundred thousand francs in debt within a month after marriage. That left us sixty-seven thousand francs a year; but we spent three times that annually. Add usurers’ interest and other payments and you have a million.”“Br-r-r!” said Mathias. “Go on. What next?”“I wanted to complete my wife’s set of jewels. I paid a hundred thousand for a coronet of diamond wheat-ears. There’s eleven hundred thousand. And now I find I owe my wife’s fortune, which amounts to three hundred and sixty-six thousand francs of her dot.”“If Madame la comtesse had surrendered her diamonds and you had pledged your income you could have pacified creditors and paid them,” Mathias insisted.“When a man is down, Mathias, when his property is mortgaged, his wife’s claims take precedence, and he has notes of a hundred thousand francs to pay, what you propose is not possible.”“This is dreadful! Would you sell Belle-Rose with the 1825 vintage still in the cellars?”“I cannot help myself.”“Belle-Rose is worth six hundred thousand francs.”“Natalie will buy it in; I advised her to do so.”“I might push the price to seven hundred thousand, and the farms are worth a hundred thousand each.”“If the house in Bordeaux could be sold for two hundred thousand—”“Solonet will give more; he wants it. He has made a handsome property gambling on the Funds; he has sold his practice for three hundred thousand and marries a mulatto woman. People say he speculates for your mother-in-law with her funds.”“She has greatly improved Lanstrac and repaid me for the use of it.”“I shouldn’t have thought her capable of that.”“She is so kind and devoted,” Paul said; “she has always paid Natalie’s debts during the three months she spent with us in Paris.”“She could afford to do so; she lives on Lanstrac. They say she bought Grainrouge between Lanstrac and Grassol, paying one hundred thousand francs down.”“She is as handsome as ever,” Paul added; “country life preserves her. I don’t mean to go to Lanstrac to bid her good-bye; her heart would bleed for me too much.”“You would go in vain; she is now in Paris.”“No doubt she left as I did.”“No complaint,” Paul resumed; “I am truly loved—by two women who outdo each other in devotion; they are jealous of each other. This great affection has been my ruin. How could I fail to satisfy my wife’s every caprice? Impossible to restrain myself. Neither could I accept sacrifice on her part. We might live at Lanstrac and part with her diamonds, but I would sooner go to India and work for a fortune than tear Natalie from the life she enjoys. So I proposed a separation as to property. Women are angels who should not be mixed in sordid interests.”Mathias listened in doubt and amazement.“You have no children, I think.”“Fortunately, none,” Paul replied.“That is not my idea of marriage,” the notary said. “A wife should share good and evil. Your marriage contract made it incumbent upon you to have a son to consolidate the entail. Why not? Madame Evangelista was strong and healthy. In those noble families the wife thought it her duty to bear children. A wife is not an instrument of pleasure but the honor and virtue of her household.”“You don’t know women, my good Mathias,” Paul returned. “To be happy we must love them as they want to be loved. Is there not something brutal in depriving a wife of her charms at once, spoiling her beauty before she has enjoyed it?”“If you had children your wife would not have dissipated your fortune; she would have stayed at home and cared for them.”“If you were right,” Paul said, “I should be more unhappy than I am. Do not aggravate me by preaching after my fall. Let me go without the pang of looking back on my mistakes.”The next day Mathias received a bill of exchange for one hundred and fifty thousand francs from de Marsay.“You see,” Paul said, “he does not write to me. He begins by obliging me. Henri’s nature is the most imperfectly perfect, the most illegally beautiful that I know. If you knew with what superiority that man can rise above sentiments and self-interest, you would be astonished.”Mathias tried to deter Paul from leaving but found his decision fixed, and after hearing many cogent reasons, ceased his efforts.By a fortunate circumstance the wind was fair and the Belle-Amélie sailed the next day. The quay was lined with relations, friends, and idle persons. Many who had known Manerville came to see the spectacle his ruin made of him. Curiosity replaced the old fashion; his disaster made him as celebrated as his former wealth. Mathias accompanied Paul to the quay and suffered as he heard the remarks:—“Who could recognize that dandy, the Pink of Fashion, now ruined, going to India to look for luck?”“How did he ruin himself? Paris, women, play, the Bourse—”“Manerville was always a poor creature; soft as papier-mâché.”Paul wrung Mathias’s hand and went aboard. Mathias stood on the pier, watching his client lean against the shrouds, defying the crowd with a contemptuous glance. As the sailors weighed anchor, Mathias made signals with his handkerchief; Josette hurried to him, excited. Paul asked the captain to wait and sent a boat. Too feeble to go himself, Mathias put two letters into the sailor’s hands.“My friend,” he said, “this packet” (showing one letter) “is important; it has just arrived by courier from Paris in thirty-five hours. Tell Monsieur le comte; it may change his plans.”“Would he come ashore?”“Possibly, my friend,” the notary answered imprudently.The Breton sailor, valuing his time, preferred not to return and gave Paul the letters without stating the message. Recognizing the handwriting of his wife and de Marsay, Paul supposed their content; anxious not to be influenced, he put the letters unread into his pocket.Absorbed in sad thoughts, he waved farewell; the spires of Bordeaux shrinking in the distance. Night brought doubts. He searched the future and found uncertainty and danger, and asked himself whether courage would hold. A vague dread seized him when he thought of Natalie alone; he repented. Sea-sickness then dissolved his will: he was carried to his cabin and stayed three days, vomiting and sleeping under the sailors’ grog. On the first morning he felt better, went on deck, and found the letters in his pockets. He opened his wife’s letter first.To understand the Comtesse de Manerville’s letter it is necessary to recall the one Paul had written the day he left Paris.From Paul de Manerville to his wife:My beloved,—When you read this I shall be far away, perhaps already on the vessel for India, where I go to repair my shattered fortune.I lacked courage to tell you of my departure; I deceived you because it was best. You would have wished to sacrifice your fortune; I could not suffer that. When I return with millions I shall lay them at your feet, as your father did for your mother. I love you madly, Natalie; my love is the only accomplice in my disaster. As money diminished, my delight grew; each fragment of my fortune turned into some pleasure for you and gave me untold happiness. I have hidden my sacrifices; now I reveal them, for when you hold this paper I shall be far away. I leave you free in all things; amuse yourself, go to theatres, balls, enjoy society. I beg you to trust de Marsay; I place liquidation of my affairs in his hands. If he advances sums ask you to repay them. I do not leave you to him, but to yourself.I cannot write more; I count your breaths. Ah! I should infuse my blood into your veins! Keep me as a religion in your heart. I go like a man vowed to a great enterprise; the fire of your eyes will light my way. Adieu, my precious love. Your poor Pink of Fashion is blown away, but the wings of good luck shall waft him back. I shall never leave you.From the Comtesse de Manerville to her husband:Dear, beloved one,—Your letter plunged me into affliction. Had you the right to take this course without consulting me? Do you not belong to me? If you must go, why should I not follow? You make me feel deprived of rights. I was right when I refused that separation as to property which my mother and you pressed. What did I say then? I warned it would cast a reflection on you and ruin your credit. I was forced to yield.My dear Paul, never have you been so noble in my eyes as now. To despair of nothing and start courageously to seek fortune—only your strength could do it. I sit at your feet. A man who avows his weakness with such good faith, who rebuilds his fortune for the same motive that wrecked it, is sublime. Fear nothing; do not doubt your Natalie. I shall go to live at Lanstrac with my mother; I will economize and pay your debts. From this day I am another woman; I bid farewell to society and pleasures you cannot share. Besides, Paul, you will have a noble reason to make your fortune—perhaps I am with child; I fear not to give you false hopes, but I am certain to-day that we shall have a child—yes, a son must it not be?This morning my mother brought thirty thousand francs by post to help you. I felt joy. Then Augustine brought me your letter—when we had slept together. I read it weeping. The richest fortunes of the heart and the momentary ruin of all interests! To lose you at a moment when I admired your greatness. I wished to follow you, but my mother said it was madness in my state; I was taken ill and write now.My mother is doing all she can to stop calumnies. The Vandenesses defend you; but de Marsay treats attacks satirically. I will obey you, make him my friend. My diamonds shall be pledged; mamma and I will use our utmost resources. She blames you for not telling her of your embarrassments; she would not have bought Grainrouge had she known. She fears India for your health. She entreats you to be sober and not be trapped by women—that made me laugh. I am as sure of you as of myself. You will return rich and faithful. Who will take care of my delicate flower? I am here and may suffer while you suffer far away alone. I shall live at Lanstrac with my mother and my child and await you, sending daily prayers. Remember our love is a talisman. Notwithstanding my grief, we have our son to brighten the future.Adieu, my beloved; our love and prayers follow you. The tears on this paper tell what I cannot write. I kiss you.Your Natalie.Paul read and re-read his wife’s ardent letter. It threw him into reverie, alternately assailed by memories and fresh assurances of love. Exclusive tenderness carries weakness—jealousy and uneasiness rising with happiness; yet his heart kept returning to faith: the lover’s conviction that silenced doubt.To understand what followed we must give the letter Paul had written to de Marsay as he left, and de Marsay’s reply received through Mathias.From Paul to Henri de Marsay:Henri,—I am ruined. When you read this I shall be on the point of sailing from Bordeaux to Calcutta on the Belle-Amélie.You will find with your notary a deed which needs only your signature to lease my house to you for six years at a nominal rent. Send a duplicate to my wife. I lease the income of my share of the entailed property for four years, amounting to 150,000 francs, which I beg you to lend me and send by bill of exchange to Maître Mathias. My wife will endorse the paper. If the revenues do not pay the loan quickly enough, we will settle on my return. The sum is necessary for me to seek fortune in India; I expect to receive it in Bordeaux the night before I sail.I have hidden my ruin. Before seizure news reached Paris I tried to save myself with 100,000 francs from notes and a last gamble at play. I lost.How have I ruined myself? By my own will. From the first month of marriage I saw I could not keep up the style but shut my eyes; I could not say, “We must leave Paris and live at Lanstrac.” I ruined myself for her like a man for a mistress. I am neither fool nor weak—going to India to reconstruct my fortune, instead of committing suicide, is not weakness.I shall return rich or never return at all. I confide to you my wife; being childless, a lover might be dangerous. I love her madly; I would forgive an infidelity rather than chain her to misery. But you know Natalie; you will be her guardian, chaperon, adviser, banker if need be. I count on you.Send letters by cover to the Governor at Calcutta. I hope to find you the same de Marsay on my return.From Henri de Marsay to Paul de Manerville:So, Monsieur le comte, you have made a wreck! Monsieur l’ambassadeur has gone to the bottom! Why have you kept away from me? If you had told me I would have made your position plain. Your wife refused to endorse. May that unseal your eyes! Your notes were protested at Lecuyer’s instigation, former head-clerk to Solonet. That usurer is proxy for your mother-in-law, who actually holds your notes for 100,000 francs, of which she gave you only 70,000. Compared with Madame Evangelista, papa Gobseck is mild. Your vineyard Belle-Rose will fall into your wife’s hands, paid for by her mother; the farms Guadet and Grassol and mortgages on your Bordeaux house already belong to her via straw men arranged by Solonet.These two women have calculated, evidently, that you are now at sea; I send this by courier so you may have time to follow advice.I made Lecuyer talk and uncovered a domestic conspiracy hatched against you. This evening I shall pay my compliments to your mother-in-law and wife at the Spanish embassy; I shall pay court to Madame Evangelista and say sly things about you—nothing overt. How did you make her such an enemy? If you had loved her before marrying her daughter you might now be peer of France, Duc de Manerville.You should have come to me at marriage; I’d have protected you. Your wife maintained the social life with ease; she is made for it; she is a fashionable woman—dress, balls, theatre—and in love with a public life. To starve that out of such a nature a man of iron was needed. Vandenesse stirred a chord in your wife you never touched. Where you lavished gifts, Felix awakened love. Her mother is naturally on the side of the lover. Your dissipations and easy nature have opened the way for your wife and mother-in-law to get rid of you by this ruin. The mission you gave me is null and void—“consummatum est.”Forgive my tone; I treat you like a man of the world. This blow should make you turn and possess marriage; you are freed of one evil. You now have the brand on your shoulder of a galley-slave; turn it to systematic opposition to society. I love you, Paul—read what I have yet to say.I am turning to politics; within five years I aim for a ministry or ambassadorship. Several of us—Ronquerolles, Montriveau, the Grandlieus, La Roche-Hugon, Serisy, Feraud, Granville—have allied against the “parti-prêtre.” We intend to form an oligarchy to direct the nation.A fortunate accident set the question of my embarking on this career; my mother, after thirty-five years, decided to find me a wife—an English heiress, Miss Dinah Stevens, with 240,000 francs a year. She is puritanic and will manage house, estates, servants, and will adore her husband. We will make settlements; she devotes part of her fortune to an entail bearing an income of 240,000 and purchases a house entailed. The settlement credited to me is a million francs. I shall sell out of the funds and invest in land. Thus in two years I may have six hundred thousand francs a year in real estate. If I reach the top I will not refuse you a helping hand.Here is my proposal. Let the Belle-Amélie sail without you; return at once to Paris; I’ll arrange a duel with Vandenesse in which you will have the first shot, and you can wing him like a pigeon. In France the husband who shoots his rival becomes respectable. Kill Vandenesse, your wife and mother-in-law tremble, society trembles, and you recover your position. I hold 100,000 francs for you to pay principal debts; you can sell what property you have left with power of redemption, for you will soon obtain an office enabling you to pay creditors. Once enlightened as to your wife’s character you can rule her. I will make your mother-in-law supple. Renounce this expatriation—it is vain. Stay, return to Paris, and we will play the game again: you and I gamblers on the green baize of politics, with loans in order. Come; you will find in me a friend.Paul ended de Marsay’s letter as if struck by a pickaxe. It demolished his hopes, illusions, and love. The ship had passed beyond the Azores. In the midst of devastation a cold impotent anger seized him.“What had I done to them?” he said.That is the question of fools and feeble beings who see nothing and foresee nothing. Then Paul cried aloud, “Henri! Henri!” Many men would have been driven mad; Paul went to bed and slept that heavy sleep that follows immense disasters—the sleep that seized Napoleon after Waterloo.ADDENDUMThe following personages appear in other stories of the Human Comedy.Casa-Real, Duc de — The Quest of the AbsoluteClaes, Josephine de Temninck, Madame — The Quest of the AbsoluteMagus, Elie — The Vendetta; THAT WHICH WAS LACKING TO PIERROTIN’S HAPPINESSRailroads were beginning to change travel to and from Paris, making many small enterprises seem antiquarian. The picturesque "coucous" that once ran from the Place de la Concorde and the Cours-la-Reine, common a century earlier and still numerous in 1830, were scarce by 1842 except as suburban curiosities. In 1820 the "Environs of Paris" did not all enjoy regular stage-coach service.The Touchards had built a successful messagerie from the rue du Faubourg-Saint-Denis, but short-run coucous still competed on nearby routes. Parisian taste for country trips sustained local operators—often innkeepers along the road—who won custom by familiarity, lower charges, and accommodating practices that the Touchards’ formal system could not match. These rivals sometimes ignored regulations, overloaded their coaches, and kept the affection of the masses; travelers preferred a familiar coachman and would wait for an old vehicle rather than take a stranger.One much-contested line was Paris to Beaumont-sur-Oise—a lucrative route that in 1822 had three competing enterprises. Touchard tried to monopolize it by lowering fares and improving coaches, but short-run lines serving towns and villages like Saint-Denis and Pierrefitte remained productive. On the main road toward England, a turning at a place called the Cave leads to the delightful Oise valley and the little town of Isle-Adam, once the seat of the extinct family of Isle-Adam and formerly a Bourbon-Conti residence. Isle-Adam lies near Nogent and Parmain, villages noted for quarries whose stone built many fine Paris structures. Though the region had fine sites and châteaux, it was reached in 1822 by only two coaches that generally worked in harmony.A simple reason explains this exception: from the Cave the paved road made by the Princes of Conti turns six miles off the main line to Isle-Adam and ends there, a detour a speculator would not take. In 1822 the Conti highway was the only road to Isle-Adam, so Pierrotin and his colleague reigned on the route, beloved by the countryfolk and often preferred even by those on the main road as far as the Cave. The two proprietors maintained a friendly rivalry; when one left Isle-Adam the other returned from Paris, and in the capital they economized by sharing the same yard, hotel, stable, coach-house, office, and clerk. Their hotel, at the corner of the rue d’Enghien, was the Lion d’Argent, long accustomed to lodging coachmen and vehicles.Although advertised as departing at fixed hours, Pierrotin and his rival were indulgent, a practice that won peasant gratitude and sometimes embarrassed strangers. The afternoon trip listed for four would often not go until half-past; the morning at eight seldom left before nine. In summer the rules relaxed for country customers; this elasticity sometimes let Pierrotin take two fares for one place if a countryman arrived early and sat in a seat booked by a late traveler. He and his colleague excused such practices by citing hard times, winter losses, the need for new coaches, or the imperative of keeping to printed tariffs—displays rarely shown unless a traveler demanded them.Pierrotin, about forty and a father, had been discharged from the cavalry at the great demobilization of 1815 and succeeded his father, who had long driven a coucou between Paris and Isle-Adam. He married an inn-keeper’s daughter and expanded the business into a regular service noted for intelligence and a certain military precision. Active and decisive, Pierrotin’s vivacious face lent sly shrewdness to his weather-stained features. He had the facility of speech learned by seeing life; his rough voice could soften before bourgeois passengers. His work clothing—stout nailed boots from Isle-Adam, bottle-green velveteen trousers and waistcoat, and a blue blouse embroidered on collar, shoulder-straps, and cuffs—was practical; a visor cap shaded his head. His military schooling left him respect for social superiority and obedience to the upper classes; he mingled little with the lesser bourgeoisie and always respected women. By "trundling the world," he came to regard his human passengers as parcels requiring less attention than cargo, the essential object of a coaching business.Alert to postwar changes, Pierrotin refused to be outdone by rivals. Since the previous summer he had talked of a large coach ordered from Farry, Breilmann, and Company—renowned makers who had adopted square English springs—an addition prompted by an influx of travelers. Pierrotin’s establishment consisted of two vehicles. One, used in winter and the only one he reported to the tax-gatherer, was the coucou inherited from his father. Its rounded body provided two hard metallic seats that might, by stretching the law, hold six—the seats divided by a removable wooden bar Pierrotin called "a back," a device passengers hated for its awkwardness. Though each seat was legally for three, it was not uncommon to see eight persons jammed onto the two seats. Pierrotin maintained travelers were more comfortable in a compact mass; when fewer occupied a seat they bumped each other and risked hitting their hats against the roof on jolting roads.In front was a wooden bench for the driver, where three travelers—called "rabbits"—could perch; Pierrotin sometimes placed four rabbits on the bench and sat on a box beneath the coach-body, which he kept full of straw and parcels. The coucou was painted yellow with a barber’s-blue band and bore in silver "Isle-Adam, Paris" and "Line to Isle-Adam" across the back. On great occasions it could carry three more passengers in a square compartment under an awning—allowed only for regulars and then avoided near gendarmerie posts where overloads might be reported. Thus on busy nights Pierrotin’s coucou could "trundle" fifteen travelers, pairing Rougeot, his stout old horse, with Bichette, a small mare of which he was fond.The other vehicle was a comical four-wheel coach holding seventeen though legally allowed fourteen. It rattled so that Isle-Adam folk would say, "Here comes Pierrotin!" when he had barely emerged from the forest. It had an "interior" for six, a front cabriolet called the "coupe" closed with awkward sashes, and an "imperial" hood holding six under leather curtains. Pierrotin sat on a nearly hidden seat below the coupe. He paid tax only on the coucou, rated for six, taking out special permits when he ran the four-wheeler—common practice then, for taxes on vehicles had been timidly enforced at first and proprietors often cheated.Often in summer the two coaches ran together, carrying thirty-two passengers though Pierrotin paid tax on six. On lucky days the convoy left Faubourg Saint-Denis at half-past four and reached Isle-Adam by ten at night, proudly proclaiming "We went at a fine pace!" To do the twenty-seven miles in five hours he sometimes omitted stops at smaller villages like Saint-Brice, Moisselles, and La Cave.The Lion d’Argent’s deep plot extended back through a long courtyard to the stables; its passage under the second floor could shelter several coaches. In 1822 the offices of all lines starting there were kept by the innkeeper’s wife, who received fares, booked passengers, and stowed packages in her vast kitchen—a patriarchal system travelers accepted. If early they waited beneath the kitchen chimney, in the passage, or in the nearby Cafe de l’Echiquier.On an early autumn Saturday in 1822 Pierrotin stood with hands in his blouse beneath the Lion d’Argent porte-cochere, watching the courtyard and through it the stables. Daumartin’s diligence had just gone, and it was past eight. Pierrotin fretted: only four passengers booked on a fine Saturday. He told his hostler to harness anyway, lamenting no parcels and the hard fate of coachmen. The hostler noted there was scarcely room left but the cabriolet; Pierrotin reminded him of the new coach arriving the next day and insisted he needed at least eighteen passengers for it. He boasted it would make Touchard envious—painted red and gold and drawing three horses; he had bought a mate for Rougeot and planned a showy team.Yet Pierrotin’s buoyant talk disguised anxiety. The coach-builders required a two-thousand-franc preliminary payment; Pierrotin had exhausted resources and credit—his wife, father-in-law, and friends had bled for him. He had paid two thousand already and owed another thousand plus five hundred for Rougeot’s mate and three hundred for new harnesses on three months’ credit. Driven by despair and vanity, he had publicly announced the coach would start the next day, hoping the builders would accept fifteen hundred instead of the two thousand five hundred still due. He considered asking Monsieur Moreau, steward at Presles, to take his note for six months.At this moment a footman in livery, carrying a leather portmanteau and returning from the Touchard establishment where he had gone too late for places, asked whether Pierrotin would wait a quarter-hour to take his master. Pierrotin agreed and received the trunk—well buckled, with a brass plate bearing a coat of arms. He wrapped it in hay and put it in the boot, then invited the valet into the Cafe de l’Echiquier for two absinthes. The valet explained his master only came to the district once a year and preferred to travel in his own carriage; this time he was going to Presles to see Monsieur Moreau. Pierrotin recognized the name: Monsieur le Comte de Serizy."But listen!" said the footman. "There’s a special order. If you have any of the country neighbors in your coach you are not to call him Monsieur le comte; he wants to travel 'en cognito,' and will pay a handsome pourboire if not recognized."Pierrotin wondered whether the secret journey related to a recent affair for which Pere Leger, a farmer, had come to Paris. The valet mentioned a countermanded order: last night the Daumont carriage had been ordered to leave at seven for Presles, but Monsieur le Comte had changed his mind at the last minute; Augustin, his valet de chambre, attributed the switch to the visit of a lady who called the previous night and again that morning—perhaps from the country. Pierrotin speculated whether the lady had said anything against Monsieur Moreau, whom Pierrotin greatly admired as an honest and capable steward. He had done errands for the Moreaus, received fees, and even fetched and returned the children; every time he carried someone to the chateau he was allowed to drive up to the gate—a benefit he valued.The valet, however, was more cynical. They said Monsieur Moreau had little when Monsieur le Comte made him steward of Presles; the valet hinted that masters were annoying and advised Pierrotin, "Every man for himself. Do what Monsieur le Comte asks, and you will be paid well." He stretched his arm to indicate a generous tip.This prudent counsel cooled Pierrotin’s ardor for borrowing from Moreau. The footman’s presence and his mention of the Comte and his steward introduced the social actors central to the drama about to unfold in Pierrotin’s vehicle. A rapid glance at the lives of the Comte de Serizy and his steward, Moreau, is necessary to understand what is to follow.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Monsieur Huguet de Serisy descends from the celebrated président Huguet, ennobled under François I. The family arms are party per pale or and sable, with an orle and two lozenges counterchanged; the motto, “i, semper melius eris,” and the distaff supporters reflect the modest pride of bourgeois families ennobled when magistrates held office—the pun on “eris,” with the initial “i” and final “s” of “melius,” giving Serisy, the estate-name.The present count’s father had been a parlement president before the Revolution. The count himself, at twenty-two, became councillor of State at the Grand Council in 1787 and wrote admirable memoranda on delicate diplomatic matters. He did not emigrate during the Revolution, spending the years on his Serizy estate near Arpajon, where his father’s standing protected him. After tending his dying father in 1794, he was elected to the Council of Five Hundred and accepted legislative duties to divert his grief. After 18th Brumaire, like many old parliamentary families, Monsieur de Serizy accepted Napoleon’s overtures: appointed to the Council of State, he reformed a disorganized department and became useful to the regime.He was soon called to a ministry, made count and senator by the Emperor, and served as proconsul to two kingdoms. In 1806, at forty, he married the sister of the former Marquis de Ronquerolles, a widow at twenty of General Gaubert, who had left her property. The marriage matched his rank, increased his fortune, and linked him to the former Marquis de Rouvre, later a count and chamberlain under the Emperor.Weary and ill in 1814, the Comte de Serizy resigned, left his department, and came to Paris. Napoleon at first suspected defection but accepted the count’s illness as cause. Though never disgraced, Serizy felt wronged by Napoleon. When the Bourbons returned, Louis XVIII, whom the count regarded as his legitimate sovereign, trusted him with private affairs and made him a cabinet minister. On March 20 the count did not go to Ghent; he told Napoleon he remained faithful to the Bourbons, would not accept a peerage during the Hundred Days, and stayed at his estate.After Napoleon’s second fall he resumed office as privy councillor, became vice-president of the Council of State, and acted as liquidator of claims and indemnities demanded by foreign powers. Unambitious personally but influential, little of importance occurred without his counsel; he avoided court and was seldom seen in salons. A life of incessant work had formed his habit: rising at four to toil until mid-day, attending duties in the afternoon, and sleeping by nine. He held the grand cross of the Legion of Honor and multiple foreign orders. No man was less conspicuous or more useful. Public honors mattered little to him; but such devotion rarely lacks a serious motive. His conduct had a cruel cause.He had loved his wife before marrying her; the passion endured through the secret unhappiness of wedding a self-possessed widow who used her liberty, while he treated her with indulgent deference. His constant toil shielded him from a pain he silenced with a diplomat’s care for secrets. He knew jealousy would seem ridiculous in a society that would not believe an old statesman’s conjugal passion. How his wife fascinated him, why he suffered without resistance, why he did not fight, and why he still hoped would require a long history; here they are only hinted at. One cruel fact was that his labor had caused a skin malady which repelled his wife. Kind and considerate, he permitted her to be mistress of herself and their home. She received all Paris, spent time in the country, and returned as though still a widow. He managed her fortune and supplied her luxury like a steward. The countess admired his mind and could please him by approving him; an hour’s talk in his study made him pliable. Like old seigneurs, he protected her so loyally that any disrespect toward her would have seemed an unforgivable injury. The world admired him; Madame de Serizy benefited by that admiration. She was ungrateful, yet mingled charm with ingratitude and sometimes soothed his wounds.This explains the sudden incognito journey of a minister of State.A rich farmer of Beaumont-sur-Oise named Leger leased and cultivated a farm whose fields projected into and troubled the Comte de Serizy’s Presles estate. The farm belonged to a burgher, Margueron. The lease made to Leger in 1799, before the agricultural boom, was about to expire, and Margueron refused Leger’s offers to renew. Monsieur de Serizy wanted the farm to stop the annoyance of enclosed fields and heard Margueron’s chief ambition was to have his only son, then a mere tax-gatherer, appointed special collector of finances at Beaumont. Farmer Leger, knowing he could sell parcels to the count at a high price, planned to buy and resell to profit.Two days before our narrative, anxious to settle the matter, Monsieur de Serizy summoned his notary Alexandre Crottat and his lawyer Derville to examine the situation. Though Derville and Crottat distrusted the count’s steward—prompted by a disturbing letter from him—the count defended Moreau, who had served him faithfully for seventeen years.“Very well,” Derville said, “then go to Presles yourself and invite Margueron to dinner. Crottat will take a deed drawn, leaving only necessary lines blank for property description and titles. Take part of the purchase money in a Bank of France check, and the appointment of the son to the collectorship. If you don’t settle this now the farm will slip through your fingers. You don’t know the trickery of peasants. Peasants against diplomat, and the diplomat succumbs.”Crottat agreed, and, as suggested by his valet Pierrotin, the count adopted the plan. The preceding evening he had sent Moreau a line by diligence to Beaumont, telling him to invite Margueron to dinner so they might close the purchase.Before this affair, the count had ordered the château of Presles restored and refurnished; for a year Grindot, an architect then in fashion, had made weekly visits. While concluding the purchase the count intended to examine restorations and new furniture as a surprise for his wife when he brought her to Presles; he had put some personal pride into the work. Why the count, who planned to drive to Presles in his own carriage in the evening, should set out early the next morning incognito in Pierrotin’s coucou needs explanation.A few words on the steward Moreau are necessary.Moreau was the son of a provincial attorney who became syndic-attorney at Versailles during the Revolution. In that post Moreau the elder almost saved the Serizys’ lives and property; he belonged to Danton’s party, was pursued by Robespierre, and was executed at Versailles. Moreau the son, inheriting his father’s doctrines and friendships, was implicated in a conspiracy against the First Consul. Monsieur de Serizy, wishing to repay debts, enabled Moreau, under sentence of death, to escape; in 1804 he sought his pardon, obtained it, offered him a government post, and finally made him private secretary.Some time after his patron’s marriage, Moreau fell in love with the countess’s waiting-woman and married her. To avoid the annoyances of that position at court, Moreau asked to manage the Presles estate, where his wife could play lady and neither would suffer wounded pride. The count wanted a trustworthy man at Presles because his wife preferred Serizy, an estate only fifteen miles from Paris. For three or four years Moreau thus held the key of the count’s affairs; he was intelligent, had studied law in his father’s office, and the count granted his request.“You can never advance in life,” the count told Moreau, “for you have broken your neck; but you can be happy, and I will take care that you are so.”He gave Moreau a salary of three thousand francs, a lodge near the château, all the wood needed from estate timber, oats, hay, and straw for two horses, and a right to the gardens’ produce. A sub-prefect was not as well provided for.For the first eight years Moreau managed the estate conscientiously; he took interest in it. The count, visiting now and then to examine property and purchases, admired Moreau’s loyalty and rewarded him liberally.But after the birth of Moreau’s third child—a daughter—he felt secure and ceased to see his advantages as gifts. About 1816, the steward, who had until then taken only what he needed, accepted twenty-five thousand francs from a wood-merchant as inducement to lease timber cutting for twelve years. Moreau argued that he could have no pension, was father of a family, and the count owed him that sum as a gift after ten years’ management; with sixty thousand francs already saved, this would let him buy a farm worth 125,000 francs in Champagne, above Isle-Adam on the Oise. Political events prevented the count and neighbors from noticing this investment, made in Madame Moreau’s name as if she had inherited from an aunt.Once he tasted possession, Moreau, while keeping an appearance of integrity, seized opportunities to increase his fortune clandestinely; the interests of his three children served as a poultice to his conscience. Nevertheless, by local custom he shared with the count in profits due to his management. This swelling of his fortune troubled his conscience. Alert and thoroughly understanding the count’s interests, Moreau watched for good purchases, gaining a larger percentage. Presles returned a net revenue of seventy thousand francs. The countryside said for thirty miles: “Monsieur de Serizy has a second self in Moreau.”Prudent, Moreau invested yearly after 1817, piling up savings secretly. He often played the poor man so well that the count sent his sons to the school Henri IV. At present Moreau held 120,000 francs invested in the Consolidated thirds, paying five percent and quoted at eighty francs. These hidden 120,000 and his Champagne farm, enlarged by purchases, amounted to about 280,000 francs, giving an income of some 16,000 francs.Such was the steward’s position when the Comte de Serizy wished to buy the Moulineaux farm—ownership indispensable to his comfort. The farm consisted of ninety-six parcels bordering Presles and often running into it, causing disputes about hedges, ditches, and tree-cutting. Any other than a cabinet minister would probably have faced lawsuits. Pere Leger wished to buy the property only to resell it to the count at a handsome advance. To secure that sum he sought an understanding with Moreau. Three days before the crucial Sunday he talked with the steward and showed that Moreau could make the count invest at two and a half percent and thus pocket forty thousand francs off Leger’s offer.“I tell you what,” Moreau said to his wife as he went to bed, “if I make fifty thousand francs out of the Moulineaux affair—and I certainly shall, for the count will give me ten thousand as a fee—we’ll retire to Isle-Adam and live in the Pavillon de Nogent.”This “pavillon,” built by the Prince de Conti for a mistress, was well furnished.“That will suit me,” his wife replied. “The Dutchman there has put it in order and now returns to India; he might let us have it for thirty thousand francs.”“We shall be close to Champagne,” said Moreau. “I hope to buy the farm and mill of Mours for a hundred thousand francs. That would give us ten thousand a year. Nogent is one of the most delightful residences in the valley; and we should still have an income of ten thousand from the Grand-Livre.”“But why not ask for the post of juge-de-paix at Isle-Adam? That would give influence and fifteen hundred a year.”“Well, I did think of it.”With these plans, Moreau, hearing the count was coming to Presles and wished him to invite Margueron to dinner on Saturday, sent an express to the count’s head-valet enclosing a letter to his master, which the messenger failed to deliver before Monsieur de Serizy retired. Augustin, however, placed it on his master’s desk in the morning. In the letter Moreau begged the count not to come, but to trust him entirely; he added that Margueron was no longer willing to sell the whole at once and talked of cutting the farm into lots. It was necessary to circumvent this plan, and perhaps employ a third party to make the purchase.Everybody has enemies. The steward and his wife had offended a retired officer, Monsieur de Reybert, and his wife, who lived near Presles. From pin-prick speeches matters had advanced to dagger thrusts. Monsieur de Reybert sought vengeance; he aimed to make Moreau lose his situation and gain it himself. Reybert had spied on the steward for two years. The same conveyance that took Moreau’s letter to the count carried Madame de Reybert to Paris, whom her husband had despatched. There she so earnestly asked to see the count that although sent away at nine, she was ushered into his study the next morning at seven.“Monsieur,” she said to the cabinet minister, “we cannot write anonymous letters, so I have come in person. I am Madame de Reybert, née de Corroy. My husband is a retired officer with a pension of six hundred francs, and we live at Presles, where your steward has offered us insult after insult, though we are persons of good station. Monsieur de Reybert, who is not intriguing, retired in 1816 after twenty years—always at a distance from the Emperor. You know how difficult it is for soldiers not under the eye of their master to obtain promotion; my husband’s integrity displeased superiors. He has watched your steward for three years, aware of his dishonesty and intending to have him dismissed. We have been frank with you. Moreau plans to get an extra hundred thousand francs out of you, to be divided between the notary, the farmer Leger, and Moreau. You have written Moreau to invite Margueron, and you are going to Presles today; but Margueron will be ill, and Leger is so certain of buying he is now in Paris with the money. If we have enlightened you, and you want an upright steward, take my husband; though noble, he will serve you as he has served the State. Your steward has made two hundred and fifty thousand francs from his place; he is not to be pitied.”The count thanked Madame de Reybert coldly, giving the holy-water of courts, for he despised backbiting; but he remembered Derville’s doubts and felt shaken. He then saw Moreau’s letter and read it. In its assurances of devotion and blaming implied distrust, he read truth.“Corruption has come to him with fortune—as it always does!” he said.The count then questioned Madame de Reybert, less for information than to observe her, and wrote a short note to his notary telling him not to send his head-clerk to Presles as requested, but to come himself in time for dinner.“Though Monsieur le comte,” Madame de Reybert concluded, “may have judged me unfavorably for acting without my husband, he ought to be convinced we obtained this information in a natural and honorable manner; the most sensitive conscience cannot take exception to it.”So saying, Madame de Reybert, née de Corroy, stood erect. She presented a face seamed with smallpox, a flat spare figure, two light eager eyes, fair hair plastered on an anxious forehead, a small drawn bonnet of faded green taffetas lined with pink, a white gown with violet spots, and leather shoes. The count recognized the wife of some poor, half-pay captain: a puritan, earnest in virtue but mindful of comfort and eager for a good situation.“You say your husband has a pension of six hundred francs,” he remarked.“Yes, monsieur.”“You were born a Corroy?”“Yes, monsieur,—a noble family of Metz, where my husband belongs.”“In what regiment did Monsieur de Reybert serve?”“The 7th artillery.”“Good,” said the count, writing the number.He had thought of entrusting estate management to some retired officer, about whom he could obtain exact information from the minister of war.“Madame,” he resumed, ringing for his valet, “return to Presles this afternoon with my notary, who is going down for dinner and to whom I have recommended you. Here is his address. I am going myself secretly to Presles, and will send for Monsieur de Reybert to come and speak to me.”Thus Monsieur de Serizy’s journey by public conveyance and his valet’s injunction to conceal his name and rank had not needlessly alarmed Pierrotin. That worthy had forebodings of a danger about to swoop down upon one of his best customers.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################As Pierrotin emerged from the Café de l’Échiquier after tending the valet, he saw in the gateway of the Lion d’Argent a lady and a young man he recognized as customers; the lady, neck outstretched and anxious, looked for him. She wore a dyed black-silk gown, a brown bonnet, an old cashmere shawl, raw-silk stockings, low shoes, and carried a straw bag. Once beautiful, she seemed about forty; her blue eyes, stripped of happiness’s fire, showed she had long renounced the world. Her dress and manner indicated a mother wholly devoted to home and son. The bonnet’s faded strings and worn shape betrayed its age; the shawl was fastened with a broken needle stuck into sealing-wax. She waited impatiently for Pierrotin, wishing to entrust her son—doubtless travelling for the first time—to his care.The son completed the picture. If the mother mended gloves, the son wore an olive-green coat with sleeves too short, showing he had grown; his blue trousers, patched by his mother, revealed a brighter patch when the coat-tails parted.“Don’t rub your gloves that way, you’ll spoil them,” she said as Pierrotin appeared. “Is this the conductor? Ah! Pierrotin, is it you?” she exclaimed, leaving her son and taking the coachman aside.“I hope you’re well, Madame Clapart,” he replied, with a blend of respect and familiarity.“Yes, Pierrotin, very well. Please take good care of my Oscar; he is travelling alone for the first time.”“Oh! so he is going alone to Monsieur Moreau!” Pierrotin cried, to learn whether he was really going there.“Yes,” said the mother.“Then Madame Moreau is willing?” returned Pierrotin, with a sly look.“Ah!” said the mother, “it will not be all roses for him, poor child! But his future absolutely requires that I should send him.”This answer struck Pierrotin. He hesitated to reveal his fears for the steward to Madame Clapart, while she feared harming her son by asking for too much care. During this brief deliberation, covered by remarks on the weather, journey, and stopping places, we must explain the ties that united Madame Clapart with Pierrotin and authorized their remarks.Often—three or four times a month—Pierrotin, on his way to Paris, would meet the steward near La Cave. As soon as the vehicle arrived, Moreau would signal a gardener, who, with Pierrotin’s help, would put on the coach one or two baskets containing fruits, vegetables, chickens, eggs, butter, and game. The steward always paid the carriage and Pierrotin’s fee, adding money for the toll at the barrière if the baskets contained dutiable goods. These parcels were never addressed. On the first occasion, the steward had given Madame Clapart’s address by word of mouth to the discreet Pierrotin, requesting he never deliver the packages to others. Pierrotin, imagining an intrigue between the steward and some pretty girl, went to number 7 rue de la Cerisaie and found Madame Clapart instead of the beauty he expected.Drivers of public conveyances enter many homes and learn many secrets; social accident often places them in intimate proximity, but being without education and poor observers, they are not usually dangerous. Nevertheless, after months Pierrotin was puzzled to explain Moreau and Madame Clapart’s exact relation from what he saw of the household. Though lodgings were not dear in the Arsenal quarter, Madame Clapart lived on a third floor at the end of a courtyard in a house once belonging to a great family when the nobility inhabited the old Tournelles and Saint-Paul sites. Toward the late sixteenth century those grandeurs were divided into the present streets—Cerisaie, Beautreillis, des Lions, etc. Madame Clapart’s flat, panelled with ancient carvings, consisted of three rooms—dining-room, salon, and bedroom; above was the kitchen and Oscar’s bedroom. Opposite the entrance, on the square landing, was a door opening into a rough stone tower built with a well for the staircase; that back room was where Moreau slept when in Paris.Pierrotin had seen in the first room, where he deposited the hampers, six wooden chairs with straw seats, a table, and a sideboard; the windows had discolored curtains. In the salon he noticed old Empire furniture now shabby; the bedroom matched the salon and dining-room. The coarsely painted woodwork blurred the mouldings; the floors, never waxed, had the gray tone of boarding-schools. When Pierrotin visited Monsieur and Madame Clapart at meals he noted that the china and glass betrayed the utmost poverty; yet the forks and spoons were silver.Monsieur Clapart, in a shabby surtout and broken slippers, always wore green spectacles and, when he removed his shabby cap, showed a pointed skull with a few dirty filaments hardly to be called hair. Timorous yet tyrannical, he looked wan.In that dreary north-facing apartment, with only a vine and a well to look on, Madame Clapart bore herself like a queen and moved like a woman unaccustomed to walking. Often, while thanking Pierrotin, she gave him glances to touch a sensitive observer and would slip a twelve-sous piece into his hand; her voice then became charming. Pierrotin had never seen Oscar because the boy was always at school when his business took him to the house.Here is the sad story Pierrotin could not have discovered by asking the portress, who only knew the Claparts paid 250 francs a year, had no servant but a charwoman coming a few hours each morning, that Madame Clapart did some washing herself, and paid postage daily as she could not let money accumulate.There seldom exists a criminal wholly criminal; nor a dishonest nature entirely dishonest. A man may cheat his master yet do good at times. If Moreau’s faults can be excused, it might be for his persistent kindness to a woman whose favours he once claimed and in whose house he had hidden when condemned to death in 1797.This woman, celebrated under the Directory for a liaison with a powerful protector, married through that protection a purveyor named Husson, who made millions from government contracts but whom Napoleon ruined in 1802. Husson became insane and threw himself into the Seine, leaving Madame Husson pregnant. Moreau, intimately allied with her, was then condemned to death and could not marry her; forced to leave France, he watched over her. Madame Husson, at twenty-two, married a clerk named Clapart, aged twenty-seven, rumored to be a rising man. But Clapart, though coarse and handsome, had no intelligence. Expecting wealth, he feigned passion but could not supply the wants she had known. He filled a Treasury post badly, giving a salary of eighteen hundred francs, which was all the household had to live on. When Moreau returned as secretary to the Comte de Serizy he learned of Madame Husson’s plight and obtained, before his own marriage, an appointment for her as head waiting-woman to Madame Mère, the Emperor’s mother. Despite such protection, Clapart was never promoted; his incapacity showed.Ruined in 1815 by the fall of the Empire, the brilliant Aspasia of the Directory had no resources other than Clapart’s clerk’s salary of twelve hundred francs from a job procured by the Comte de Serizy. Moreau, the only protector of a woman he had known in opulence, obtained a half-scholarship for her son, Oscar Husson, at the school of Henri IV, and sent her regularly, by Pierrotin, such supplies from the estate at Presles as he could decently offer to a household in distress.Oscar was his mother’s whole life and future. The poor woman’s only fault was exaggerated tenderness for her boy—the bête-noire of his stepfather. Oscar, unfortunately, had a foolish overweening conceit his mother did not perceive despite the stepfather’s sarcasms. This conceit so worried Monsieur Moreau that he begged Madame Clapart to send the boy to him for a month so he might study his character and find a suitable career; Moreau thought of proposing Oscar as his successor to the count.To give each thing its due, we should show the causes of Oscar’s silly vanity: he was born in Madame Mère’s household, dazzled in childhood by imperial splendors. His imagination retained those images and nursed hopes of regaining a golden past. The natural boastfulness of schoolboys, resting on these memories and on maternal tales of former splendour, developed excessively. Oscar, leaving school, had borne many humiliations as a scholarship pupil among paying students unless the scholar can impose respect by physical superiority.This mixture of departed splendor, faded beauty, blind maternal love, and heroic suffering made the mother a pathetic figure in Paris.Incapable of understanding Moreau’s real attachment to her, and of that woman’s for the man who saved her in 1797, Pierrotin did not communicate his suspicion of danger to Moreau. His thought, “We have enough to do in this world to look after ourselves,” returned to mind, along with his obedience to what he called the “chefs de file”—men of rank. Besides, Pierrotin’s head was full of his own stings like coins in a purse. So his “Very good, madame,” “Certainly, madame,” in reply to the poor mother showed his desire to escape her long instructions.“You will be sure to place the packages so they can’t get wet if the weather changes.”“I’ve a hood,” replied Pierrotin. “Besides, see with what care they are placed.”“Oscar, don’t stay more than two weeks, no matter how much they may ask you,” continued Madame Clapart, returning to her son. “You can’t please Madame Moreau, whatever you do; besides, you must be home by the end of September. We are to go to Belleville, you know, to your uncle Cardot.”“Yes, mamma.”“Above all,” she said in a low voice, “never speak about servants; keep thinking all the time that Madame Moreau was once a waiting-maid.”“Yes, mamma.”Like all youths whose vanity is excessively ticklish, Oscar seemed annoyed at being lectured on the threshold of the Lion d’Argent.“Well, now good-bye, mamma. We shall start soon; there’s the horse all harnessed.”The mother, forgetting she was in the street, embraced Oscar and smiling took a little roll from her basket.“Tiens! you were forgetting your roll and the chocolate! My child, don’t take anything at the inns; they’ll charge you ten times the value.”Oscar would have liked his mother farther off as she stuffed the roll and chocolate into his pocket. Two young men a few years older than Oscar, better dressed and without a mother hanging on them—complete in independence—witnessed the scene.“He said mamma!” cried one, laughing.The words reached Oscar and drove him to say, “Good-bye, mother!” in a tone of terrible impatience.Madame Clapart spoke too loudly and seemed desirous to show her tenderness. “What is the matter with you, Oscar?” she asked. “I don’t know what to make of you,” she added severely, then resumed in her tender voice, “Listen, my Oscar—do not talk and show off; keep your tongue in check. You are not advanced enough to judge persons; there is nothing more dangerous than speaking in public conveyances. Besides, in a diligence well-bred persons always keep silence.”The two young men had walked to the other end of the establishment and returned with boot-heels tapping. To rid himself of his mother, Oscar took an heroic measure that showed vanity stimulating intellect.“Mamma,” he said, “you are standing in a draught, and you may take cold. Besides, I am going to get into the coach.”The lad touched a tender spot; his mother caught him to her bosom, kissed him as if he were starting on a long journey, and went with him to the vehicle with tears. “Don’t forget to give five francs to the servants when you come away,” she said; “write me three times at least during the fifteen days; behave properly, and remember all I have told you. You have linen enough; don’t send any to the wash. And above all, remember Monsieur Moreau’s kindness; mind him as you would a father, and follow his advice.”As he got into the coach Oscar’s blue woollen stockings became visible as his trousers pulled up, and the new patch showed through the parting coat-tails. The smiles of the two young men, on whom these signs of respectable indigence were not lost, were fresh wounds to his vanity.“The first place was engaged for Oscar,” said the mother to Pierrotin. “Take the back seat,” she said to the boy with a loving smile.Oh how Oscar regretted that trouble and sorrow had destroyed his mother’s beauty and that poverty prevented her being better dressed! One young man, wearing top-boots and spurs, nudged the other to notice Oscar’s mother; the other twirled his moustache as if to say, “Rather a pretty figure!”“How shall I ever get rid of mamma?” thought Oscar.“What’s the matter?” asked Madame Clapart.He pretended not to hear. Perhaps she lacked tact; all absorbing sentiments have much egoism.“Georges, do you like children when travelling?” asked one young man of the other.“Yes, my good Amaury, if they are weaned, and are named Oscar, and have chocolate.”These remarks, uttered in half-tones, allowed Oscar to hear or not; his face would indicate how much fun they might make of him. Oscar chose not to hear and looked to see if his mother, weighing on him like a nightmare, was still there. He compared his companion’s dress with his own and felt that his mother’s toilet counted for much in the youths’ smiles. “If they would only take themselves off!” he thought.Instead, Amaury tapped the coucou’s wheel and remarked to Georges, “And so, my friend, you are really going to trust your future to this fragile bark?”“I must,” replied Georges, fatalistically.Oscar sighed as he noted the jaunty hat of his companion, revealing blond hair; he had black hair cut close by order of his stepfather. The chubby healthy face of the vain lad contrasted with the long pale one of the other. The latter’s broad forehead and filled chest showed a cashmere waistcoat; his tight iron-gray trousers and frogged overcoat looked elegant; the sight galled Oscar like an ugly woman seeing a pretty one. The click of the stranger’s boot-heels echoed in his heart; he felt hampered by his clothes—which were probably his stepfather’s—while the other seemed at ease.“That fellow must have heaps of francs in his trousers pocket,” thought Oscar.The young man turned. Oscar saw a gold chain and a watch; from then on the young man assumed the proportions of a personage.Living in rue de la Cerisaie since 1815, taken to and from school by his stepfather and seldom seeing anything but his mother’s poverty, Oscar had little comparison. Brought up strictly on Moreau’s advice, he seldom went to the theatre except to the Ambigu-Comique. His stepfather still wore his watch in the fob with a heavy gold chain and various ornaments; Oscar considered that old-fashioned finery the “ne plus ultra.” He was bewildered by the revelation of superior, negligent elegance: spotless gloves, a gold-headed switch cane twirled with grace.Oscar had reached that last quarter of adolescence when little things cause immense joys and miseries—a period when youth prefers misfortune to a ridiculous suit and torments itself about neckcloths and the desire to appear a man. Then the lad swells; his swagger is portentous because it boasts about nothings. Yet a lad who envies a well-dressed fool may also admire talent. Such defects, unless rooted in the heart, prove only youthful exuberance. For a nineteen-year-old only child kept at home by poverty and adored by an indulgent mother to envy a twenty-two-year-old in a frogged surtout is a peccadillo common to all ranks.But Oscar passed from peccadillo to ill feeling. He felt humiliated, angry with the youth he envied, and secretly desired to show he was as good.The two young fellows continued walking up and down from the gate to the stables; each time they turned they looked at Oscar curled in his corner. Oscar, convinced their jokes concerned him, affected indifference. He began to hum a chorus lately in vogue among liberals ending, “‘Tis Voltaire’s fault, ‘tis Rousseau’s fault.”“Tiens! perhaps he is one of the chorus at the Opera,” said Amaury.This exasperated Oscar, who bounded up, pulled out the wooden “back,” and called to Pierrotin: “When do we start?”“Presently,” said Pierrotin, whip in hand, gazing toward rue d’Enghien.At this moment a young man arrived with a true gamin followed by a porter dragging a hand-cart. The young man spoke confidentially to Pierrotin, who nodded and called his porter.
