The porter helped unload the cart, which contained two trunks, buckets, brushes, oddly shaped boxes, and innumerable packages that the gamin stowed with such speed Oscar, then smiling at his mother across the street, noticed none of the paraphernalia that might reveal his new companion’s profession.The gamin, about sixteen, wore a gray blouse buckled by a leather belt, a cap jauntily perched, curly brown hair on his shoulders, a black-silk cravat around a very white neck, bright gray eyes, and a vivacious, mischievous face—Figaro-like. His gestures and mocking eye showed a lively intellect born of early vocation. Unconcerned about costume, he looked at his unpolished boots as a quizzical effect. “I’m in style,” he said, shaking himself and addressing his companion.The companion’s glance showed authority over the adept; a practised eye would call the gamin a “rapin.” “Behave yourself, Mistigris,” said his master.The master was a slight pale young man with thick black hair worn fantastically. His abundant hair suited an enormous head with a vast forehead proclaiming precocious intellect. His strained, harassed face was hollowed as if from chronic malady, poverty, or recent grief. His shabby American-green surtout was clean and brushed; a black waistcoat buttoned to the throat almost concealed a scarlet neckerchief; black trousers flapped his thin legs; muddy boots showed he had come on foot. With a rapid look he seized the Lion d’Argent scene, then glanced at Mistigris’s satirical stare.“Charming!” said Mistigris.“Yes, very,” replied the other.“We seem to have got here too early,” continued Mistigris. “Couldn’t we get a mouthful somewhere? My stomach, like Nature, abhors a vacuum.”“Have we time for a cup of coffee?” the artist asked Pierrotin.“Yes, but don’t be long,” answered Pierrotin.“Good; that means a quarter of an hour,” said Mistigris.The pair disappeared. Nine o’clock was striking in the hotel kitchen. Georges thought it reasonable to remonstrate with Pierrotin.“Hey! my friend; with such wheels as these (striking the clumsy tires with his cane) one might expect punctuality. I have devilishly pressing business or I wouldn’t trust my bones to it. And that horse you call Rougeot doesn’t look fit to make up for lost time.”“We are going to harness Bichette while those gentlemen take their coffee,” replied Pierrotin. “Go and ask,” he said to his porter, “if Père Léger is coming with us—”“Where is your Père Léger?” asked Georges.“Over the way, at number 50. He couldn’t get a place in the Beaumont diligence,” said Pierrotin, making no answer to his customer; then he disappeared in search of Bichette.Georges, after shaking hands with his friend, got into the coach, handling an important portfolio which he placed beneath the seat cushion. He took the opposite corner to Oscar on the same seat.“This Père Léger troubles me,” he said.“They can’t take away our places,” replied Oscar. “I have number one.”“And I number two,” said Georges.Just as Pierrotin reappeared having harnessed Bichette, the porter returned with a stout man whose weight could not be less than two hundred and fifty pounds. Père Léger belonged to the farmer species—square back, protuberant stomach, powdered pigtail, little blue linen coat, white gaiters above the knee fastened over velveteen breeches with silver buckles, and hob-nailed shoes. He held a small polished reddish stick with a large knob and a leather thong.“And you are called Père Léger?” asked Georges very seriously as the farmer attempted to step up.“At your service,” replied the farmer with a face like Louis XVIII, fat rubicund cheeks, and a large nose; his smiling eyes sunken in rolls of fat. “Come, a helping hand, my lad!” he said to Pierrotin.The farmer was hoisted in by Pierrotin and the porter amid cries of “Houp la! hi! ha! hoist!” uttered by Georges.“Oh! I’m not going far; only to La Cave,” said the farmer, good-humoredly.In France everyone takes a joke.“Take the back seat,” said Pierrotin, “there’ll be six of you.”“Where’s your other horse?” demanded Georges. “Is it as mythical as the third post-horse.”“There she is,” said Pierrotin, pointing to the little mare coming along alone.“He calls that insect a horse!” exclaimed Georges.“Oh! she’s good, that little mare,” said the farmer, now seated. “Your servant, gentlemen. Well, Pierrotin, how soon do you start?”“I have two travellers in there after a cup of coffee,” replied Pierrotin.The hollow-cheeked young man and his page reappeared.“Come, let’s start!” was the general cry.“We are going to start,” replied Pierrotin. “Now, then, make ready,” he said to the porter, who removed the stones that stopped the wheels.Pierrotin took Rougeot by the bridle and gave that guttural cry, “Ket, ket!” to urge the two animals; though stiff, they pulled the coach to the Lion d’Argent door. Then Pierrotin gazed up rue d’Enghien and disappeared, leaving the coach to the porter.“Ah ca! is he subject to such attacks—that master of yours?” said Mistigris to the porter.“He has gone to fetch his feed from the stable,” replied the porter, versed in tricks to keep passengers calm.“Well, after all,” said Mistigris, “‘art is long, but life is short’--to Bichette.”At that time the studios fancied mutilating or transposing proverbs; it was a triumph to change letters or words to keep the semblance while making a fantastic meaning.“Patience, Mistigris!” said his master; “‘come wheel, come whoa.’”Pierrotin returned bringing the Comte de Serizy, who had come through rue de l’Échiquier and with whom Pierrotin had doubtless conversed.“Père Léger,” said Pierrotin, looking into the coach, “will you give your place to Monsieur le comte? That will balance the carriage better.”“We sha’n’t be off for an hour if you go on this way,” cried Georges. “We shall have to take down this infernal bar, which cost such trouble to put up. Why should everyone move for the man who comes last? We all have a right to the places we took. What place has monsieur engaged? Come, find that out! Haven’t you a way-book, a register, or something? What place has Monsieur Lecomte engaged—count of what, I’d like to know.”“Monsieur le comte,” said Pierrotin, visibly troubled, “I am afraid you will be uncomfortable.”“Why didn’t you keep better count of us?” said Mistigris. “‘Short counts make good ends.’”“Mistigris, behave yourself,” said his master.Monsieur de Serizy was evidently taken by people in the coach for a bourgeois named Lecomte.“Don’t disturb anyone,” he said to Pierrotin. “I will sit with you in front.”“Come, Mistigris,” said the master to his rapin, “remember the respect you owe to age; you don’t know how shockingly old you may be yourself some day. ‘Travel deforms youth.’ Give your place to monsieur.”Mistigris opened the leather curtain and jumped out with frog-like agility.“You mustn’t be a rabbit, august old man,” he said to the count.“Mistigris, ‘ars est celare bonum,’” said his master.“I thank you very much, monsieur,” said the count to Mistigris’s master, next to whom he now sat.The minister of State cast a sagacious glance round the coach interior, affronting both Oscar and Georges.“When persons want to be master of a coach they should engage all the places,” remarked Georges.Certain now of his incognito, the Comte de Serizy made no reply but assumed a good-natured bourgeois air.“Suppose you were late, wouldn’t you be glad the coach waited for you?” said the farmer to the two young men.Pierrotin still looked up and down the street, whip in hand, apparently reluctant to mount the hard seat where Mistigris fidgeted.“If you expect someone else, I am not the last,” said the count.“I agree to that reasoning,” said Mistigris.Georges and Oscar began to laugh impertinently.“The old fellow doesn’t know much,” whispered Georges to Oscar, who was delighted at this apparent union between himself and the object of his envy.“Parbleu!” cried Pierrotin, “I shouldn’t be sorry for two more passengers.”“I haven’t paid; I’ll get out,” said Georges, alarmed.“What are you waiting for, Pierrotin?” asked Père Léger.Whereupon Pierrotin shouted a certain “Hi!” in which Bichette and Rougeot recognized a definitive resolution and sprang toward the faubourg at a pace soon to slacken.The count had a burning red face with inflamed portions highlighted by snow-white hair. These blotches injured his naturally noble air; careful examination was needed to find in his green-gray eyes the shrewdness of a magistrate, the wisdom of a statesman, and a legislator’s knowledge. His face was flat, the nose depressed into it. The hat hid the grace of his forehead. There was enough odd contrast—silvery hair, burning face, thick black brows—to amuse youths.He wore a long blue overcoat buttoned military-fashion to the throat, a white cravat, cotton wool in his ears, a high shirt-collar making a white patch on each cheek. His black trousers covered his boots whose toes were barely seen. No decoration adorned him; doeskin gloves concealed his hands. Nothing betrayed a peer of France and one of the kingdom’s most useful statesmen.Père Léger had never seen the count, who knew the farmer only by name. When the count entered the carriage he cast a glance about him that affronted Georges and Oscar but was truly looking for the head-clerk of his notary in case he had been forced, like himself, into Pierrotin’s vehicle, intending to caution him about his incognito. Feeling reassured by Oscar, Père Léger, and the quasi-military air, waxed moustaches, and adventurer look of Georges, he concluded his note had reached his notary Alexandre Crottat in time to keep the clerk from leaving.“Père Léger,” said Pierrotin when they reached the steep faubourg Saint-Denis at rue de la Fidélité, “suppose we get out, hey?”“I’ll get out, too,” said the count, hearing Léger’s name.“Goodness! if this is how we go we shall do fourteen miles in fifteen days!” cried Georges.“It isn’t my fault,” said Pierrotin, “if a passenger wishes to get out.”“Ten louis for you if you keep the secret of my being here as I told you before,” said the count in a low voice, taking Pierrotin by the arm.“Oh, my thousand francs!” thought Pierrotin as he winked at Monsieur de Serizy, meaning, “Rely on me.”Oscar and Georges stayed in the coach.“Look here, Pierrotin, since Pierrotin you are,” cried Georges when they were stowed away again, “if you don’t mean to go faster than this, say so! I’ll pay my fare and take a post-horse at Saint-Denis; I have important business.”“Oh! he’ll go well enough,” said Père Léger. “Besides, the distance isn’t great.”“I am never more than half an hour late,” asserted Pierrotin.“Well, you are not wheeling the Pope in this old barrow,” said Georges, “so, get on.”“Perhaps he’s afraid of shaking monsieur,” said Mistigris looking at the count. “But you shouldn’t have preferences, Pierrotin, it isn’t right.”“Coucous and the Charter make all Frenchmen equals,” said Georges.“Oh! be easy,” said Père Léger; “we are sure to get to La Chapelle by mid-day,”—La Chapelle being the village beyond the Barrière of Saint-Denis.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Those who travel in public conveyances know strangers do not at once begin to talk; there is first a period of mutual examination. Once each thinks he has sized up his companions, the most talkative starts and the conversation livens—especially in French stage-coaches, where everyone is eager to laugh and show wit.A twenty-two-year-old, Georges, was tempted to display his cleverness. He had sized up his fellow passengers: the count as a second-rate manufacturer or chandler; the shabby young man with Mistigris as insignificant; Oscar as a ninny; Pere Léger as an excellent dupe. He resolved to amuse himself at their expense and, as the coucou descended from La Chapelle toward Saint-Denis, ran over possible personae—Etienne, Béranger, a Carbonaro, the son of Marshal Ney, a disguised Russian prince, Cousin, Lord Byron—finally choosing a swaggering, Eastern-adventurer role. Mistigris’s banter and Pierrotin’s exclamations opened the talk.Assuming the air of someone just returned from the Levant, Georges said he had been ill three months and had only just left his bed after a “germ” of suppressed plague. This produced alarm and gibes. He launched into tales of Egypt and Greece: service under Ali, pacha of Janina, a quarrel with him, and Oriental living that had disordered his liver.When Pere Léger asked if he had been a soldier and how old he was, Georges answered twenty-nine and sketched a patched military history: enlisted in 1813, present at Hanau, promoted to sergeant-major, made sub-lieutenant at Montereau and decorated by the Emperor; he hinted at service at Mont-Saint-Jean in 1815, expressed disgust with France, then said he went abroad with comrades to serve a pacha in Egypt. He declared he wouldn’t renounce his religion for Islam and told of receiving a thousand talari from the pacha.The passengers were captivated. Georges boasted of being grandson of the famous Czerni-Georges, who had fought the Porte; his father had died in Paris leaving his mother pregnant. Their property had been seized; his mother had remarried a purveyor, Monsieur Yung, now dead; she had left Georges estranged from his stepfather—supposed reasons for his enlisting as a private in 1813. Back in Greece, Ali Tebelen welcomed him as descendant of Czerni-Georges; Georges claimed Ali gave him a harem—five wives and ten slaves—but the women were stupid and the harem little better than a herd of goats; he preferred French grisettes.They were entering Saint-Denis and stopped at a tavern famed for its cheese-cakes. The count, suspecting the youth had left something in the coach, returned and found beneath the cushion a portfolio labeled “Maitre Crottat, notary.” He took out the deed of sale for the farm at Moulineaux, pocketed it, and went into the inn, concluding Georges was Crottat’s second clerk sent as head-clerk. Georges meanwhile had paid for cheese-cakes and wine for Pere Léger and Oscar, basking in their admiration, and tried to pick up Mistigris and his master.When Pierrotin returned, Georges piled on: he had been made commander of Ali’s cavalry and had distinguished himself against Chosrew pacha, cutting his line and receiving Ali’s gratitude and costly gifts. Back at Janina Ali proposed degrading acts—drowning a wife, renouncing his religion—but Georges refused and fled. He claimed Chosrew later captured Ali by sea with English help; Chosrew had become a naval commander and, when he recognized Georges at Smyrna, nearly executed him. Monsieur de Rivière, the French ambassador, intervened, saving him and recovering two thousand talari, though most treasures were taken by the admiral.Georges said he had witnessed the execution of Smyrna’s governor and then gone to Spain, becoming aide-de-camp to Mina with the rank of colonel for the constitutional cause. The count reacted sharply, warning that the Court of Peers was then investigating conspiracies and punishing French soldiers who bore arms against France or engaged in foreign intrigues. He added that if he were a magistrate he would have ordered the gendarmes at Pierrefitte to arrest the supposed aide-de-camp and summon the coach’s occupants to testify. The remark silenced Georges as they passed the gendarmerie guard-house.Conversation turned to cultivation in Eastern lands. Georges, with his habitual flippancy, described them as too busy cutting each other’s heads off to farm like the French: onions, rice, tobacco, dates, rugs at Smyrna, Latakia tobacco, opium from poppies—customs that left the fellahs barely enough after taxes. Pere Léger, puzzled, asked about sheep and fields; Georges offered evasive explanations about crops growing with little cultivation. He mocked bureaucracy and contrasted despotic plunder with French stamped papers. The count, drawing from this talk, advised landowners to sell, warning taxes in Italy were heavy too.Mistigris and Schinner—the supposed painter—joined in. Schinner claimed to be a humble painter returned from Rome after winning the grand prix and sent to Italy at the government’s expense. He insisted he’d go merely as a decorator to a château. The count indulged this mock-importance of artists, arguing that great ceilings and the love that inspired them were worth sacrifices. Oscar, jealous and awkward, listened, eager to be somebody.Pierrotin, the driver, joked about carrying sovereigns and the pourboires he expected, and fretted about a debt for a new coach. The passengers exchanged banter about ceilings, Venice, Rome, and a near-murder Schinner claimed to have endured there. Mistigris hinted Lord Byron had been involved; Schinner demurred, not wishing to speak of Byron.Schinner then related a long account of Dalmatia, Zara, and a Greek beauty named Zena. He had stayed with an apothecary, seen Zena on a neighboring balcony, and, despite an ugly duenna and a jealous seventy-year-old husband—an Uscoque pirate with millions, a missing ear and eye—had exchanged looks and stolen walks with her. He detailed the dangerous delicacy of their flirtation: ramparts, the duenna who wouldn’t leave, and growing tension. He finally entered the pirate’s house and found Zena; they walked the ramparts together. One day he returned to find the street full of people; he was seized, garroted, gagged, and dragged before magistrates accused of murdering the pirate. He was stoned and nearly killed by a maddened populace.He learned that Zena had poisoned the pirate with opium to free herself for walks with him; the duenna had overdone the dose, implicating him. He insisted he knew nothing of the murder. The mayor and Austrian commissary of police released him with orders to return to Rome, while Zena, who had let the heirs take most of the Uscoque’s fortune, was sentenced to two years’ seclusion in a convent; Schinner hoped to paint her portrait someday. Mistigris complained that Schinner had left him penniless in Venice, forcing him to paint cheap portraits to reach Rome.Georges chimed in with comments on Dalmatian maraschino and the follies of youth. Pere Léger repeatedly asked about farming and crops, receiving brief, often evasive replies. The count maintained a guarded attitude, sometimes smiling at fashionable braggadocio and sometimes revealing surprising knowledge—for example, when Georges mentioned names tied to Smyrna that the count recognized, making the youth seem less sure of his invented recollections.Throughout the journey the characters parried between bluster and information: Georges’s ostentatious Eastern adventures mixed fact and fabrication; the count, discreet but observant, suspected a clerk posing as a colonel; Schinner’s romance showed the dangers beneath exotic talk; Mistigris supplied cynical comments; Pierrotin kept an eye on profit; Pere Léger asked homely questions; Oscar gaped and yearned to be more. Conversation, prompted by the coach’s enforced intimacy and the French habit of talk, ran on—at times diverting, at times revealing—until they moved on toward Pierrefitte and beyond.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Pierrotin’s coach descended into the large village of Saint-Brice, where he usually stopped to rest his horses. It was about half-past one. At the innkeeper’s cry—“Here’s Pere Leger”—the travelers prepared for breakfast.“Always once a day,” said the fat farmer. Georges, twirling his cane, provoked poor Oscar by producing a small straw case of cigars and lighting one on the threshold. Oscar, puffing and coughing, tried to look jaunty; Schinner and Georges showed him proper technique, while the painter and Georges mocked his awkwardness. Mistigris, seizing Oscar’s cigar, smoked with relish. Oscar fumed inwardly at Georges’s spending—Alicante, cheesecakes, cigars—and thought his shabby clothes and patched stockings betrayed him. Mistigris, sharing the embarrassment, revealed his own patched socks; the count, standing apart beneath the porte-cochere, smiled at the youngsters’ folly while envying their easy gaiety.In the kitchen, the innkeeper and Pere Leger spoke low of Les Moulineaux. The innkeeper guessed Pere Leger had arranged to buy it, and offered to lend money. Pere Leger answered calmly that he had done for the count. He explained his plan: having secured forty thousand “pictures on coin” from the King to present to Moreau—presumably a middleman—he would buy Les Moulineaux outright for 260,000 francs and then sell it to the Count de Serizy for 360,000, profiting handsomely while keeping appearances by letting the count propose him as tenant. The farm brought in 6,000 francs rent now; Pere Leger would take it at 7,500 for eighteen years and show it as a sound investment. The innkeeper approved; the count, listening unseen, felt he had found evidence he sought but wondered if it was only talk and whether Moreau, the steward, was implicated.Pierrotin came out to water the horses. The count, fearing indiscretion, whispered to him to keep his name secret in Saint-Brice and Isle-Adam, and promised Pierrotin a thousand francs the next morning if he did. Pierrotin, flattered and pale with happiness, agreed and then, pretending he had a fish to deliver, refused the innkeeper’s invite to breakfast and urged Pere Leger to come on. Pere Leger accepted, planning to harness a horse the innkeeper wished to sell and overtake Pierrotin.To the count’s surprise, Pierrotin rebridled, and Schinner and Mistigris walked on. When Pierrotin caught up with the others, Pere Leger and Georges arrived too, Pere Leger boasting that he was the farmer of Les Moulineaux. Conversation resumed: Georges puffed cigars and asked about careers. Oscar, indignant at being called “little young man,” declared himself destined for diplomacy. The others laughed; Georges praised his experience with cigars and travel, and Schinner tongue-in-cheek tried to coach Oscar in courtly manners. Oscar, swelling with pretension, boasted of his family, his private tutor—Abbe Loraux—and his acquaintance with the Comte de Serizy and his son, whom he rode with. The claim surprised Pere Leger and the others.When the count, interested, asked Oscar for information about Serizy, Oscar obliged: the count was said to suffer from a hideous skin disease; his wife, who couldn’t endure him, was the true power; the count was in love with her but as pitiable as Arnolphe in Moliere—so Oscar. He advised seeking the marquis rather than the count. The companions laughed at Oscar’s intimate knowledge; the count, shocked at the gossip about the Comtesse de Serizy, interposed sharply: he was a friend of her brother, the Marquis de Ronquerolles, and would not tolerate disparagement of the countess. He warned them against speaking ill of people and declared he knew many influential persons and even Monsieur Grindot, architect of Presles. Then, asking Pierrotin to stop at the next inn, he left the coach, taking a path through the woods toward what everyone perceived as the chateau.Pierrotin drove through Moisselles and the little group fell quiet. The count drew Pierrotin aside in the inn-yard and asked about Oscar. Pierrotin explained the youth was the son of a poor woman from rue de la Cerisaie, Madame Husson, who often received fruit and game from Presles. Pere Leger teased that Pierrotin thought Oscar of some noble house, and the three young men sat mortified, realizing how their boastful talk might have misled the count.When the coach entered the road to Presles, conversation resumed in fits of bravado and rebuke: Georges warned Oscar that if the man they’d encountered was the Count de Serizy, he had cause to be ashamed. Oscar, remembering his mother’s counsel and feeling premonitions of trouble, tried to maintain composure. They approached the estate: the chateau of Presles rose, splendid in the valley. The count made a light farewell, wishing success to the colonel, the painter, and Oscar, and urged the colonel to reconcile with the King; he praised Schinner but forbade him from domestic visits, and lauded Oscar’s imagined knowledge of statesmen. Then he told Pierrotin to set him out and to fetch him the same time next day, and went along a wood-path, leaving his companions embarrassed.Pere Leger, suddenly recognizing the path to Les Moulineaux, confronted Georges—who quickly slipped away so the farmer couldn’t find him. Pierrotin pulled into the iron gates and drove into Presles. Oscar declared he would go straight to the chateau and flung himself out in a narrow path, uncertain where to go. Pere Leger shouted directions, and Oscar, compelled, entered the grand courtyard. To Oscar’s astonishment, Mistigris and his master Schinner were to be installed at the chateau: Pierrotin had been instructed to deposit their packages and the youths’ luggage with the concierge. In ten minutes Pierrotin discharged the painter’s implements and Oscar’s effects, including a small leather portmanteau, which the concierge’s wife took in charge. Then Pierrotin drove out through the forest road to Isle-Adam, his face aglow with the thought of the thousand francs he would soon receive and of the profit from his shrewd secrecy. Nothing more was needed for his happiness: on the morrow he would have his thousand francs and could begin paying for a new coach.Throughout, the youthful boastfulness, the farmer’s scheme, the count’s discreet surveillance, Pierrotin’s opportunism, and the travelers’ social pretensions intersected: the count’s secrecy and influence, Pere Leger’s plot against the count, Oscar’s bluster and folly, and Pierrotin’s double gain all came to light at Presles, leaving the coach’s occupants in varying states of triumph, confusion, and shame.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Oscar, abashed, skulked behind a clump of trees in the courtyard watching his two traveling companions when Monsieur Moreau appeared on the guard-room portico. Dressed in a long blue overcoat and top-boots and carrying a riding-whip, he greeted Oscar warmly and then turned to Mistigris and his master.“Good-day, messieurs,” he said. “You are, no doubt, the two painters Monsieur Grindot told me to expect.” He whistled; the concierge came.“Take these gentlemen to rooms 14 and 15. Madame Moreau will give you the keys. Go with them to show the way; make fires if necessary, and take up all their things. I have orders from Monsieur le comte,” he added to the artists, “to invite you to my table; we dine at five, as in Paris. If you like hunting, you will find plenty to amuse you; we hunt over twelve thousand acres of forest, not counting our own domain.”Oscar, the painter, and Mistigris exchanged subdued glances; Mistigris murmured, “‘Veni, vidi, cecidi,—I came, I saw, I slaughtered.’” Oscar followed the steward briskly through the park.“Jacques,” Moreau called to one of his children, “run and tell your mother that little Husson has come, and say I must go to Les Moulineaux for a moment.”The steward, about fifty, medium height and dark-complexioned, seemed abrupt but kind; his decisive manner and affectionate penetration had always made Oscar feel small in his presence. Having been trained by his mother to magnify the steward, Oscar felt a new unease: he feared Moreau might harm him.“Well, my Oscar, you don’t look pleased to be here,” said Moreau. “You’ll find plenty of amusement; you shall learn to ride and to shoot and hunt.”“I don’t know any of those things,” Oscar replied.“But I brought you here to learn them.”“Mamma told me only to stay two weeks because of Madame Moreau.”“Oh! we’ll see about that,” Moreau said, slightly wounded that his authority was doubted.Moreau’s youngest son, an active twelve-year-old, ran up.“Come,” said his father, “take Oscar to your mother.”Moreau himself went by the shortest path to the gamekeeper’s house between the park and the forest.The steward’s pavilion, built shortly before the Revolution and attached to the estate when the count’s father bought it, was a neat Louis XV freestone house set above ground. The ground floor included a salon, bedroom, dining-room and billiard-room; two suites were divided by a staircase and peristyle. Madame Moreau had turned an adjoining chamber into a boudoir. Furnished with pieces from the chateau’s old furniture and decorated with blue-and-white damask, Persian rug and a tent-style boudoir, the rooms suggested country luxury. Around the house Madame Moreau had laid out a garden opening into the park and improved the entrance with an iron gateway.The Moreaus cleverly concealed their dependence on the count: they appeared as comfortable, independent managers of a friend’s property. The count and countess rarely came to Presles to check them. Perquisites granted by Monsieur de Serizy—milk, eggs, poultry, game, fruits, forage, vegetables, wood—were enough to keep horses and servants; they bought only butcher’s meat, wines, and colonial supplies. The poultry-maid baked bread; Moreau paid his butcher with farm pigs after reserving some for himself. He stacked three hundred tons of hay while accounting for a hundred, farmed poultry and cattle at the estate’s expense, and used stable manure for the chateau gardens—small appropriations with plausible pretexts.Madame Moreau employed local girls as maid and cook; a discharged soldier handled heavy labor. In neighboring towns, people either didn’t know or pretended not to know her past as a waiting-maid. Moreau’s influence secured favors—appointments and decorations—that made the couple welcome at every bourgeois festivity. The rector and mayor of Presles played cards with them every evening; hospitality followed feathering one’s nest.Estelle, Madame Moreau, a pretty, affected woman of about thirty-six, wore Paris fashions and expensive boots and avoided walking except in fine weather. Though allotted but five hundred francs a year for her toilette, she spent it to appear like a princess, resenting being called “the steward’s wife at Presles” and aspiring to be taken for the chateau’s mistress. She patronized villagers in the tone of a great lady and depended on her husband to manage stewardship and fortune. Confident in their comfortable existence of seventeen years, she feared the count’s plan to restore the chateau and urged Moreau to partner with Léger about Les Moulineaux so they might retire to Isle-Adam—she did not want to return to servitude.A rancorous enmity with the Reyberts grew from a slight Madame de Reybert had inflicted when Estelle presumed precedence on arriving at Presles, and for four years the Reyberts, cut dead by Estelle, brooded on revenge.The Moreaus, friendly with Grindot the architect, learned two painters were coming to finish chateau decorations—the principal paintings already done by Schinner. Schinner’s reputation had brought respect: he and his wife lived in the chateau by the count’s orders, and the neighborhood vied to pay them attention. To regain a little local prestige, Madame Moreau resolved to receive and praise the artist she expected, presenting him as Schinner’s equal.For two days Estelle prepared, reserving her finest dress for Saturday, certain the artist would arrive for dinner. She put on a pink-striped gown, velvet ribbons, bronze shoes and a superb Leghorn bonnet trimmed with roses—Parisian elegance made for display. After ordering a choice dinner and reviewing her rooms, she stood near a flower-bed in the chateau courtyard holding a rose-colored parasol to be seen as mistress on the arrival of the Beaumont coach. When the coach from Pierrotin left only Mistigris’s packages with the concierge, she withdrew disappointed, feeling too fine to sit idle in her salon awaiting the expected carriage, which in fact left Paris an hour after Pierrotin.The two artists, after being sent to their rooms to dress for dinner, rigged themselves up and, piloted by Jacques Moreau, went to the steward’s lodge.“Mamma,” Jacques said, “here are the two artists sent by Monsieur Schinner.”Madame Moreau, agreeably surprised, rose and prepared to display her graces.“Make yourselves at home. In the country, be at ease; life is too insipid otherwise. We have already had Monsieur Schinner with us,” she purred.Mistigris glanced slyly at his companion.“You know him, of course?” Estelle asked.“Who does not know him, madame?” said the painter.“Knows him like his double,” Mistigris added.“Joseph Bridau,” the painter replied, wondering what sort of woman she was.Mistigris, inwardly rebelling at her patronizing tone, waited for a cue—artists seize such phrases to sketch the ridiculous. Estelle’s clumsy manners and revealing words made amusement easy.“You say you love art, madame; perhaps you cultivate it successfully,” Bridau said.“No. My education was commercial; but I have so profound and delicate a sense of art that Monsieur Schinner always asked me, when he had finished a piece, to give my opinion,” she answered.“Didn’t he propose to ‘croquer’ you?” Bridau asked. “Painters are eager after handsome women.”“What may you mean by such language?” she replied.“In the studios we say croquer for sketching,” Mistigris explained, “and we are always wanting to croquer beautiful heads. That’s the origin of ‘She is pretty enough to eat.’”“I was not aware of the origin,” she said with a sweet glance.“My pupil here,” Bridau added, “Monsieur Leon de Lora, shows talent for portraiture. He’d be happy to leave you a souvenir by painting your charming head, madame.”Bridau signaled Mistigris: “Come, sail in.” Leon slid to the sofa beside Estelle and took her hand, which she allowed.“Oh! madame, if you’d offer a surprise to your husband and give me secret sittings I would surpass myself,” Leon cooed. “You are so beautiful, so fresh, so charming!”“We must paint your dear children in arabesques,” Bridau interrupted.“I would rather have them in the salon; but perhaps I am indiscreet,” she coquetted.“Beauty, madame, is a sovereign whom all painters worship,” Bridau declared.“They are both charming,” thought Madame Moreau. “Do you enjoy driving? Shall I take you through the woods after dinner in my carriage?”“Oh! oh! oh!” cried Mistigris ecstatically. “Why, Presles will be our terrestrial paradise.”“With an Eve, a fair, young, fascinating woman,” added Bridau.As Estelle bridled, Rosalie burst in.“Madame! The count is at the chateau.”“Has he asked for me?” said Estelle.“No; but he wants his trunk and the key of his apartment.”“Then give them to him,” she said impatiently, hiding her real trouble.“Mamma! here’s Oscar Husson,” said the youngest son, bringing in Oscar, who turned red seeing the two artists in evening dress.“Oh! so you have come, my little Oscar,” Estelle said stiffly. “I hope you will now go and dress,” she added contemptuously. “Your mother, I presume, has not accustomed you to dine in such clothes as those.”“Oh!” cried the cruel Mistigris, “a future diplomatist knows that ‘two coats are better than none.’”“How do you mean, future diplomatist?” exclaimed Madame Moreau.Poor Oscar had tears in his eyes as he looked between Joseph and Leon.“Merely a joke in traveling,” Joseph replied to spare him.“The boy wanted to be funny like the rest of us, and he blagued, that’s all,” Mistigris said.“Madame,” Rosalie returned, “his Excellency orders dinner for eight at six. What shall we do?”During Estelle’s conference with her head-woman the two artists and Oscar watched in consternation.“His Excellency! who is he?” said Joseph Bridau.“Why, Monsieur le Comte de Serizy, of course,” replied Jacques.“Could he have been the man in the coucou?” Leon asked.“Oh!” Oscar cried, “the Comte always travels in his own carriage with four horses.”“How did the Comte get here?” Joseph asked Madame Moreau when she returned, much discomfited.“I cannot explain it,” she said. “I do not know what brought him—and Moreau not here!”“His Excellency wishes Monsieur Schinner to come to the chateau,” said the gardener. “And he begs Monsieur Schinner to dine with him; also Monsieur Mistigris.”“Done for!” cried the rapin laughing. “He whom we took for a bourgeois in the coucou was the count.”Oscar nearly froze; his throat tightened.“And you, who talked to him about his wife’s lovers and his skin diseases!” Mistigris turned on Oscar.“What does he mean?” exclaimed Madame Moreau, puzzled, as the artists left laughing at Oscar’s expression.Oscar remained dumb, confounded, hearing nothing though Madame Moreau questioned and shook him by the arm. Rosalie reappeared with orders; the household bustled; the master had fallen upon his house like a bombshell.From the top of the hill near La Cave, where he left the coach, the count had taken the wood-path to the gamekeeper’s house. The keeper was amazed to see his master.“Is Moreau here? I see his horse,” the count asked.“No, monseigneur; he meant to go to Moulineaux before dinner and left his horse here while he went to the chateau.”“If you value your place,” said the count, “take that horse and ride at once to Beaumont to deliver a note I shall write.”He entered the lodge, wrote a line folded to prevent opening without detection, and gave it to the man as soon as he mounted.“Not a word to anyone,” he said. “And as for you, madame,” he added to the keeper’s wife, “if Moreau comes for his horse, tell him I have taken it.”The count crossed the park and entered the chateau through the iron gates. Worn by public life and emotions, he felt wounded by the idea that Moreau had deceived him; thinking on Pere Leger’s revelations and gossip, he judged Moreau less a conspirator than a traitor who had revealed secrets.When Moreau arrived at the lodge and asked for his horse, the keeper’s wife said the count had taken it.“Monsieur le comte!” cried Moreau. “Whom do you mean?”“The Comte de Serizy, our master,” she replied. Unable to understand, Moreau ran to the chateau, where he learned the count was dressing.“Seven persons invited to dinner!” Rosalie cried when she saw him.Moreau went through the offices to his house. He met the poultry-girl in an altercation with a handsome young man.“Monsieur le comte particularly told me a colonel, an aide-de-camp of Mina,” insisted the girl.“I am not a colonel,” replied Georges.“Isn’t your name Georges?” asked the girl.“What’s all this?” said the steward, intervening.“Monsieur, my name is Georges Marest; I am the son of a rich ironmonger in the rue Saint-Martin; I come on business to Monsieur le Comte de Serizy from Maître Crottat, a notary, whose second clerk I am.”“And I,” said the girl, “am telling him that monseigneur said: ‘There’ll come a colonel named Czerni-Georges, aide-de-camp to Mina; he’ll come by Pierrotin’s coach; if he asks for me, show him into the waiting-room.’”“Evidently,” said the clerk, “the count is the traveler we took for a bourgeois in the coucou; if it hadn’t been for the politeness of a young man he’d have come as a rabbit.”“A rabbit in Pierrotin’s coucou!” exclaimed Moreau and the girl.“I’m sure of it from what she says,” Georges added. “I blagué the travelers with stories of Egypt and Greece and Spain,” he explained. “Wearing spurs, I took myself out for a colonel: pure nonsense!”“Tell me,” the steward said, “what did that traveler look like?”“Face like a brick, snow-white hair, and black eyebrows.”“That is he!”“Then I’m lost!” cried Georges.“Why?”“Oh, I chaffed him about his decorations.”“Pooh! he’s a good fellow; you probably amused him. Come at once to the chateau. I’ll go in and see his Excellency. Where did you say he left the coach?”“At the top of the mountain.”“I don’t know what to make of it!”“After all,” Georges thought, “though I did blague him, I didn’t say anything insulting.”“Why have you come here?” asked the steward.“I’ve brought the deed of sale for the farm at Moulineaux, ready for signature.”“Good heavens!” Moreau exclaimed, bewildered.Moreau felt his heart beat painfully. After rapping on his master’s door he heard:—“Is that you, Monsieur Moreau?”“Yes, monseigneur.”“Come in.”The count, dressed in evening clothes and wearing the grand cross of the Legion and the Golden Fleece, sat composedly. He had arranged his hair himself, likely to impress Monsieur Margueron.“Well, monsieur,” he said to Moreau, “we have not concluded that purchase from Margueron.”“He asks too much.”“Why is he not coming to dinner as I requested?”“Monseigneur, he is ill.”“Are you sure?”“I have just come from there.”“Monsieur,” the count said sternly, “what would you do with a man you trusted if, after seeing you dress wounds you desired secret, he should reveal your misfortunes and laugh at your malady with a strumpet?”“I would thrash him,” Moreau answered.“And if he betrayed your confidence and robbed you?”“I would detect him and send him to the galleys.”“Monsieur Moreau, listen. You have spoken of my infirmities to Madame Clapart, laughed at her house and, with her, over my attachment to the Comtesse de Serizy; her son, little Husson, told many matters about my treatment in a public conveyance at my presence. He dared calumniate my wife. Besides, Pere Leger, who was in the coach, revealed the plan by the notary at Beaumont, you, and he, concerning Les Moulineaux. If you went to Monsieur Margueron it was to tell him to feign illness. He is so little ill that he is coming here to dinner. Now, monsieur, I could pardon you having made two hundred and fifty thousand francs from your position in seventeen years—I can understand that. You might each time have asked me and I would have given it; but that you, who knew my toil for our country and my love for Madame de Serizy—that you should gossip before a boy, holding up my secrets and affections to ridicule—”“Monseigneur!”“It is unpardonable. To injure a man’s interest is one thing; to stab his heart is another! Oh! you do not know what you have done!”The count put his head in his hands and was silent for a while.“I leave you what you have gained,” he said after a time, “and I shall forget you. For my sake, for my dignity, and for your honor, we will part decently; I cannot forget what your father did for mine. You will explain the stewardship duties to Monsieur de Reybert, who succeeds you. Be calm, as I am. Give no opportunity for fools to talk. Above all, no recrimination or petty meanness. Though you no longer possess my confidence, behave with the decorum of well-bred persons. As for that miserable boy who wounded me to death, I will not have him sleep at Presles; send him to the inn; I will not answer for my temper if I see him.”“I do not deserve such gentleness, monseigneur,” Moreau answered with tears. “Yes, you are right; if I had been utterly dishonest I should now be worth five hundred thousand francs instead of half. I offer to give you an account of my fortune with all details. But let me tell you what actually took place. It was from heart to heart that I spoke of you to Madame Clapart. As for my wife, I never said one word of these matters—”“Enough,” said the count, conviction complete. “All is irrevocable. Put your affairs and mine in order. You can stay in the pavilion until October. Monsieur and Madame de Reybert will lodge in the chateau for now; endeavor to keep on terms with them, like well-bred persons who hate each other, but maintain appearances.”They went downstairs; Moreau white as the count’s hair, the count calm and dignified.During this interview the Beaumont coach, which left Paris at one, had stopped before the chateau gates and deposited Maître Crottat, the notary, who, per the count’s orders, was shown into the salon where he found his clerk, subdued, and the two painters, all painfully self-conscious. Monsieur de Reybert, crabbed and fifty, was there with old Margueron and the notary of Beaumont holding a bundle of deeds.Seeing the count in evening dress and orders, Georges Marest felt a twinge, Joseph Bridau quivered, but Mistigris, conscious of Sunday clothes and with nothing on his conscience, remarked loud enough:—“Well, he looks a great deal better like that.”“Little scamp,” said the count, catching him by the ear, “we are both in the decoration business. I hope you recognize your own work, my dear Schinner,” he added, pointing to the salon ceiling.“Monseigneur,” the artist replied, “I did wrong to take such a celebrated name out of mere bravado; but this day will oblige me to do fine things for you, and so bring credit on my own name of Joseph Bridau.”“You took up my defense,” said the count hastily; “I hope you will dine with me, as well as my lively friend Mistigris.”“Your Excellency doesn’t know to what you expose yourself,” the saucy rapin said; “‘facilis descensus victuali,’ as we say at the Black Hen.”“Bridau!” the minister cried struck by a thought. “Are you related to one of the devoted toilers under the Empire, the head of a bureau who fell a victim to his zeal?”“His son, monseigneur,” replied Joseph, bowing.“Then you are most welcome,” the count said, taking Bridau’s hand. “I knew your father; count on me as on—on an uncle in America,” he laughed. “But you are too young to have pupils of your own; to whom does Mistigris belong?”“To my friend Schinner, who lent him to me,” Joseph answered. “Mistigris’ name is Leon de Lora. Monseigneur, if you knew my father, will you think of his other son, accused of plotting against the State and soon to be tried before the Court of Peers?”“Ah! that’s true,” the count said. “Yes, I will think about it. As for Colonel Czerni-Georges, the friend of Ali Pacha, and Mina’s aide-de-camp—” he continued, walking up to Georges.“He! that’s my second clerk!” cried Crottat.“You are mistaken, Maître Crottat,” the count assumed sternness. “A clerk who intends to be a notary does not leave important deeds in a diligence at the mercy of travelers; nor spend twenty francs between Paris and Moisselles; nor expose himself to be arrested as a deserter—”“Monseigneur,” said Georges, “I may have amused myself with the bourgeois in the diligence, but—”“Let his Excellency finish,” said the notary, digging his elbow into his clerk’s ribs.“A notary,” the count continued, “should practice discretion and caution; he should be incapable of such a blunder as taking a peer of France for a tallow-chandler—”“I am willing to be blamed for my faults,” Georges said; “but I never left my deeds—”“Now you are committing the fault of contradicting a minister, a gentleman, an old man, and a client,” the count said. “Give me that deed of sale.”Georges turned papers in his portfolio.“That will do; don’t disarrange those papers,” the count said, taking the deed from his pocket. “Here is what you seek.”Crottat turned it back and forth, astonished to receive it from his client’s hands.“What does this mean, monsieur?” he asked Georges.“If I had not taken it,” said the count, “Pere Leger—who showed by his agricultural questions he minds his business—might have seized that paper and guessed my purpose. You must dine with me on condition you describe the execution of the Muslim of Smyrna, and finish the memoirs of some client you know so well.”“Schlague for blague!” Leon whispered to Bridau.“Gentlemen,” the count said to the two notaries and Messieurs Margueron and de Reybert, “let us go into the next room and conclude this business before dinner, because, as my friend Mistigris would say: ‘Qui esurit constentit.’”“Well, he is very good-natured,” Leon said to Georges after the count left the room.“Yes, he may be,” Georges replied, “but my master isn’t; he’ll request me to blaguer elsewhere.”“Never mind, you like travel,” said Bridau.“What a dressing that boy will get from Monsieur and Madame Moreau!” cried Mistigris.“Little idiot!” said Georges. “If not for him the count would have been amused. Well, the lesson is good; if ever again I’m caught bragging in a public coach—”“It is stupid,” Joseph said.“And common,” Mistigris added. “‘Vulgarity is the brother of pretension.’”While the sale was settled between Monsieur Margueron and the Comte de Serizy, assisted by their notaries and Monsieur de Reybert, the ex-steward walked slowly to his house, entered the salon, and sat down without noticing anything. Little Husson slipped into a corner, frightened by the steward’s livid face.“Eh! my friend!” Estelle said, coming in tired. “What is the matter?”“My dear, we are lost—lost beyond recovery. I am no longer steward of Presles, no longer in the count’s confidence.”“Why not?”“Pere Leger, who was in Pierrotin’s coach, told the count all about Les Moulineaux. But that is not what cost me his favor.”“What then?”“Oscar spoke ill of the comtesse, and told of the count’s diseases.”“Oscar!” cried Madame Moreau. “Ah! your sin is found out. It was well worth while to warm that young serpent in your bosom. How often I have told you—”“Enough!” Moreau said in a strained voice.At that moment they discovered Oscar cowering. Moreau swooped on the lad, seized him by the collar, dragged him to a window. “Speak! what did you say to monseigneur in that coach? What demon let loose your tongue, you who keep a doltish silence whenever I speak to you? Why did you do it?” he cried violently.Too bewildered to weep, Oscar was dumb and motionless.“Come with me and beg his Excellency’s pardon,” said Moreau.“As if his Excellency cares a little for a toad like that!” cried the furious Estelle.“Come, I say, to the chateau,” repeated Moreau.Oscar collapsed like an inert mass.“Come!” cried Moreau, his anger rising.“No! no! mercy!” Oscar cried, unable to submit to a torture worse than death.Moreau seized the lad by his coat and dragged him through the yards, which rang with Oscar’s outcries and sobs. He hauled him up the portico and, with an arm made powerful by fury, flung him, bellowing and rigid, into the salon at the very feet of the count, who, having completed the purchase, was about to leave the salon for the dining-room with his guests.“On your knees, wretched boy! and ask pardon of him who gave food to your mind by obtaining your scholarship.”Oscar, face to the ground, foamed with rage and said nothing. The spectators were shocked. Moreau seemed out of his senses; his face crimson and bloodshot.“This young man is a mere lump of vanity,” the count said after waiting for Oscar’s excuses. “A proud man humbles himself because he sees grandeur in a certain self-abasement. I fear you will never make much of that lad.”So saying, his Excellency passed on. Moreau took Oscar home and ordered the horses immediately put to Madame Moreau’s caleche.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################While the horses were being harnessed, Moreau wrote to Madame Clapart:My dear,—Oscar has ruined me. During his journey in Pierrotin’s coach he spoke of Madame de Serizy’s behavior to his Excellency, who was travelling incognito, and actually told, to himself, the secret of his terrible malady. After dismissing me from my stewardship, the count told me not to let Oscar sleep at Presles, but to send him away immediately. Therefore, to obey his orders, the horses are being harnessed at this moment to my wife’s carriage, and Brochon, my stable-man, will take the miserable child to you to-night.We are, my wife and I, in a distress of mind which you may perhaps imagine, though I cannot describe it to you. I will see you in a few days, for I must take another course. I have three children, and I ought to consider their future. At present I do not know what to do; but I shall certainly endeavor to make the count aware of what seventeen years of the life of a man like myself is worth. Owning at the present moment about two hundred and fifty thousand francs, I want to raise myself to a fortune which may some day make me the equal of his Excellency. At this moment I feel within me the power to move mountains and vanquish insurmountable difficulties. What a lever is such a scene of bitter humiliation as I have just passed through! Whose blood has Oscar in his veins? His conduct has been that of a blockhead; up to this moment when I write to you, he has not said a word nor answered, even by a sign, the questions my wife and I have put to him. Will he become an idiot? or is he one already? Dear friend, why did you not instruct him as to his behavior before you sent him to me? How many misfortunes you would have spared me, had you brought him here yourself as I begged you to do. If Estelle alarmed you, you might have stayed at Moisselles. However, the thing is done, and there is no use talking about it.Adieu; I shall see you soon.Your devoted servant and friend,MoreauAt eight that evening Madame Clapart, just returned from a walk with her husband, was knitting winter socks for Oscar by candlelight. Monsieur Clapart expected Poiret, a friend who often came to play dominoes; he avoided cafés. In spite of small means, he prided himself on that habit.“I’m afraid Poiret came while we were out,” said Clapart.“No, the portress would have told us,” replied his wife.“She may have forgotten it.”“It wouldn’t be the first time,” said Clapart.“Well,” the poor woman said to change the subject, “Oscar must be at Presles by this time. How he will enjoy that fine house and the park.”“Oh! yes,” snarled Clapart, “you expect fine things of him; but there’ll be squabbles wherever he goes.”“Will you never cease to find fault with that poor child?” she cried. “What has he done to you? If some day we should live at our ease, we may owe it all to him; he has such a good heart—”“Our bones will be jelly long before that fellow makes his way,” said Clapart. “You don’t know your own child; he is conceited, boastful, deceitful, lazy, incapable of—”“Why don’t you go to meet Poiret?” said the mother, wounded.“A boy who has never won a prize at school!” continued Clapart, equating prizes with future success.“Did you win any?” asked his wife. “Oscar stood second in philosophy.”This silenced Clapart for a moment, then he resumed his complaints, including fear Moreau would set his wife against Oscar. “You don’t know what you’re doing,” he began—until the cracking of a postilion’s whip and the noise of a carriage stopping before the house stirred the street. Clapart looked out.“They have sent Oscar back to you in a post-chaise,” he cried, partly satisfied, partly uneasy.“Good heavens! what can have happened to him?” Madame Clapart trembled.Brochon entered, followed by Oscar and Poiret.“What has happened?” she asked the stable-man.“I don’t know, but Monsieur Moreau is no longer steward of Presles, and they say your son has caused it. His Excellency ordered that he should be sent home to you. Here’s a letter from poor Monsieur Moreau, madame, which will tell you all. You never saw a man so changed in a single day.”“Clapart, two glasses of wine for the postilion and for monsieur!” cried the mother, and flung herself into a chair to read the fatal letter. “Oscar,” she said, staggering toward his bed, “do you want to kill your mother? After all the cautions I gave you this morning—”She fainted from distress. When she recovered, her husband was shaking Oscar by the arm.“Will you answer me?” he demanded.“Go to bed, monsieur,” she said to her son. “Let him alone, Monsieur Clapart. Don’t drive him out of his senses; he is frightfully changed.”Oscar had slipped away to bed the instant he got the order. After a day of such emotion, despite his faults, he slept the sleep of the just. The next day the world seemed less changed; hunger replaced despair, and the violent mental suffering of the night had faded. Youth turns quickly from one impression to another; corporal punishment might have fixed the lesson, but discretion in administering it is crucial.Madame Clapart sent her husband out so she might be alone with Oscar. She was pitiable: eyes worn with tears, a face weary from sleeplessness, a feeble voice—everything showed suffering beyond what she could bear.When Oscar entered she motioned him to sit beside her and spoke in a gentle, grieved voice of the benefits they had received from Monsieur Moreau. For six years they had lived on his delicate charity; Monsieur Clapart’s salary and Oscar’s “demi-bourse” scholarship were due to the Comte de Serizy. Most of this would now cease. Clapart had no claim to a pension; when he could no longer keep his place, what would become of them?“For myself,” she said, “I could nurse the sick or be a housekeeper and support Monsieur Clapart; but you, Oscar, what could you do? You must earn your living. There are four careers for a young man like you—commerce, government employment, the professions, or the military. Commerce needs capital, and we have none. Public employment requires influence and a long probation—you have alienated our only protector. The professions demand years and fees, and again money for your maintenance while you learn. You are eighteen and strong; enlist in the army; it seems the only way you can earn your bread.”Oscar, ignorant of life and of earning a living, listened submissively. The word “army,” his mother’s tears, and her admonitions finally made him cry. Seeing his tears, Madame Clapart softened, and gave the familiar peroration mothers use in such scenes.“Well, Oscar, promise me you will be more discreet—repress your silly vanity,” she urged.Oscar promised, and she kissed him to console him.“In future,” she added, “you will follow my advice. We will go see your uncle Cardot; he owed much to your father, who gave him his sister Mademoiselle Husson with an enormous dowry, enabling him to make a fortune. Perhaps he might place you with Monsieur Camusot, his successor and son-in-law, in the rue des Bourdonnais. But your uncle has many claims on him—four children, the Cocon d’Or given to his eldest daughter, and other connections. He scarcely knows of our existence. Still, to-morrow we will go and see him; he is our last hope.”Monsieur Jean-Jerome-Severin Cardot had been a widower six years. As head-clerk of the Cocon d’Or, one of Paris’s oldest firms, he had bought the establishment in 1793 with the dowry money of Mademoiselle Husson, and made a large fortune. To provide for his children he had bought an annuity for himself and divided his capital among them; thus he lived on a thirty-thousand-franc annuity and had the comfort of seeing his children well provided.Uncle Cardot lived at Belleville, in a first-floor apartment with a southern exposure and a garden for a thousand francs a year. He lived simply, served by an old cook and a former maid of Madame Cardot, who expected small legacies. His apartment remained as his late wife had furnished it. He dined in Paris five days a week and returned home at midnight in a hackney-coach. He was a little old man, neatly dressed, polite to women, and fond of pleasure. He professed epicureanism, admired Voltaire and Beranger, and affected decorum; to his children he appeared grave and attentive, though he privately indulged pleasures and gaiety.He had not spoken of his income to his children, who supposed he had deprived himself for them, and thus redoubled their attentions. Only Camusot, his son-in-law, knew of the annuity; he considered Cardot’s conduct a generous blending of family duty and later enjoyment.Madame Clapart did not trouble the Cardots with complaints, seeing them only rarely. She had always clung to Uncle Cardot as a hope and took Oscar to see him during vacations. Cardot sometimes gave Oscar a dinner and a Gaiete visit and once had provided a school-suit and a silver cup and fork. Madame Clapart reminded him of these favors and of his nephew’s gratitude, though Cardot, shrewd and aware that Oscar was a posthumous child with no claim, had not been deeply moved.“Your son bears the name of Husson,” said Cardot when Madame Clapart and Oscar visited. “If my dear deceased wife were living she would wish to do something for the name of her father and of her brother—”“She loved her brother,” said Oscar’s mother.“All my fortune is given to my children,” he continued, “and I have now only an annuity; but I know the path on which you ought to start this young fellow. Let him study law; I’ll pay the costs. Put him in a lawyer’s office and let him learn the business; if he does well, and if I am still alive, each of my children shall lend him a quarter of the cost of a practice; I will be security for him. You will only have to feed and clothe him. Discretion, honesty, and work—live at home, go straight to the law-school, from there to a lawyer’s office; drudge night and day, and study at home. Become, by twenty-two, a second clerk; by twenty-four, head-clerk; be steady, and you will win all. If you don’t like the profession you might enter my son the notary’s office. Work, patience, discretion, honesty—those are your landmarks.”“God grant that you may live thirty years longer to see your fifth child realizing all we expect from him,” cried Madame Clapart, seizing Cardot’s hand.“Now come to breakfast,” replied the kind old man, leading Oscar by the ear.During the meal Cardot noted Oscar’s inexperience. “Send him here now and then,” he said to Madame Clapart as they parted; “I’ll form him for you.”This visit calmed the poor mother’s anxieties. For the next fortnight she took Oscar walking every day and watched him closely. By the end of October, while the household breakfasted on a humble meal, the formidable ex-steward entered and surprised this scene of poverty.“We are now living in Paris—but not as we lived at Presles,” said Moreau, explaining the change in their relations caused by Oscar’s folly. “I shall seldom be here myself; for I have gone into partnership with Pere Leger and Pere Margueron of Beaumont. We are speculating in land, and we have begun by purchasing the estate of Persan. I am the head of the concern, which has a capital of a million; part of which I have borrowed on my own securities. When I find a good thing, Pere Leger and I examine it; my partners have each a quarter and I a half in the profits; but I do nearly all the work, and for that reason I shall be constantly on the road. My wife lives here, in the faubourg du Roule, very plainly. When we see how the business turns out, if we risk only the profits, and if Oscar behaves himself, we may, perhaps, employ him.”“Ah! my friend, the catastrophe caused by my poor boy’s heedlessness may prove to be the cause of your making a brilliant fortune; for, really, you were burying your energy and your capacity at Presles.”Madame Clapart related her visit to Cardot to show Moreau that she and her son need no longer be a burden.“He is right, that old fellow,” said the ex-steward. “We must hold Oscar on that path with an iron hand, and he will end as a barrister or a notary. But he mustn’t leave the track; he must go straight through with it. Ha! I know how to help you. The legal business of land-agents is quite important, and I have heard of a lawyer who has just bought what is called a ‘titre nu’; that means a practice without clients. He is a young man, hard as an iron bar, eager for work, ferociously active. His name is Desroches. I’ll offer him our business on condition that he takes Oscar as a pupil; and I’ll ask him to let the boy live with him at nine hundred francs a year, of which I will pay three hundred, so that your son will cost you only six hundred francs, without his living, in future. If the boy ever means to become a man it can only be under a discipline like that. He’ll come out of that office, notary, solicitor, or barrister, as he may elect.”“Come, Oscar; thank our kind Monsieur Moreau, and don’t stand there like a stone post. All young men who commit follies have not the good fortune to meet with friends who still take an interest in their career, even after they have been injured by them.”“The best way to make your peace with me,” said Moreau, pressing Oscar’s hand, “is to work now with steady application, and to conduct yourself in future properly.”############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Ten days later Monsieur Moreau took Oscar to Maître Desroches, a twenty-six-year-old solicitor newly established in the rue de Béthisy in a large apartment at the end of a narrow courtyard, for which he paid little.Born to poor parents and brought up sternly, Desroches knew of Oscar’s condition and felt interest in him, though his apparent harshness limited the help he could show. Oscar, gaunt, with muddy skin and hair cropped like a clothes-brush, who spoke curtly and had a piercing eye and a gloomy vivacity, was terrified by the sight of his new master.“We work here day and night,” said the lawyer from his armchair behind a table piled with papers. “Monsieur Moreau, we won’t kill him; but he’ll have to go at our pace.” Though it was Sunday, the head-clerk appeared, pen in hand.“Monsieur Godeschal, here’s the pupil I told you of. Monsieur Moreau takes the liveliest interest in him. He will dine with us and sleep in the small attic next to your chamber. You will allot the exact time it takes to go to the law-school and back, so he does not lose five minutes on the way. See that he learns the Code and is proficient; after his work here you will give him authors to read. He will be under your immediate direction, and I shall keep an eye on him. They want to make him what you have made yourself, a capable head-clerk, so he can take such a place himself. Go with Monsieur Godeschal, my young friend; he’ll show you your lodging, and you can settle down.”Desroches praised his clerks—youths with only their ten fingers to rely upon—working as hard as a dozen ordinary fellows. He had taken Godeschal from Derville when the latter was only a second clerk, paid him a thousand francs a year with board, and admired his indefatigable honesty. “For the slightest fault of that kind a clerk leaves my office,” he declared. Moreau thought Oscar was in a good school.For two years Oscar lived in the rue de Béthisy, a den of petty law. Under this strict and capable supervision he kept rigid hours; his Paris life resembled that of a monk.At five in the morning, in all weathers, Godeschal woke and took Oscar to the office, where their master was already at work. Oscar ran errands and prepared law-school lessons; Godeschal and often Desroches pointed out authors and difficulties. He was not allowed to leave a single section of the Code until he had mastered it; Godeschal gave him preliminary examinations more searching and longer than those of the law-school. After brief classes he returned to the office; occasionally sent to the Palais, but always under Godeschal’s thumb, until dinner. Dinner was modest—one dish of meat, one of vegetables, and a salad; dessert, a piece of Gruyère cheese. After dinner Godeschal and Oscar returned to work until night. Once a month Oscar breakfasted with his uncle Cardot and spent Sundays with his mother. From time to time Moreau, when in Paris, took Oscar to dine in the Palais-Royal or to the theater in the evening. Oscar had been so snubbed by Godeschal and Desroches for attempts at elegance that he no longer gave thought to his clothes.“A good clerk,” Godeschal told him, “should have two black coats, one new, one old, a pair of black trousers, black stockings, and shoes. Boots cost too much; you can’t have boots till you are called to the bar. A clerk should never spend more than seven hundred francs a year. Good stout shirts of strong linen are what you want. Ha! when a man starts from nothing to reach fortune, he has to keep down to bare necessities. Look at Monsieur Desroches; he did what we are doing, and see where he is now.”Godeschal preached by example. He practised strict principles of honor, discretion, and honesty without ostentation; such conduct was natural to him. Eighteen months after Oscar’s installation the second clerk was, for the second time, slightly wrong in his accounts. Godeschal said to him before the others:“My dear Gaudet, go away of your own free will, that it may not be said that Monsieur Desroches has dismissed you. You have been careless or absent-minded, and neither of those defects can pass here. The master shall know nothing about the matter; that is all that I can do for a comrade.”At twenty Oscar became third clerk in the office. Though he earned no salary, he was lodged and fed while doing the work of the second clerk. Desroches employed two chief clerks, and the second’s work was unremitting toil. By the end of his second year in the law-school Oscar knew more than most licensed graduates; he did work at the Palais intelligently and argued some cases in chambers. Godeschal and Desroches were satisfied. Yet Oscar still showed, from time to time, a hankering after pleasure and a suppressed desire to shine.Moreau, satisfied with Oscar’s progress, relaxed his watchfulness; in July 1825, when Oscar passed his examinations with a spotless record, the land-agent gave him money to dress elegantly and Madame Clapart prepared his outfit splendidly.In November, when the courts reopened, Oscar occupied the chamber of the second clerk and did that work wholly, with a salary of eight hundred francs plus board and lodging. Uncle Cardot, who had privately inquired at Desroches about his nephew, promised Madame Clapart to look out for a practice for Oscar if he continued to do well.Despite these virtuous appearances, Oscar endured great inward strife. At times he thought of quitting a life so contrary to his tastes; he fancied galley-slaves happier. Galled by the collar of this iron system, wild desires seized him when he compared himself in the street with well-dressed young men. Sometimes he was driven toward women; then he resigned himself, only to fall into deeper disgust for life. Impelled by Godeschal’s example, he was forced rather than led into the rugged way.Godeschal watched Oscar and, by principle, kept him from temptation. Generally the young clerk had so little money he could not give way to excess. During the last year Godeschal had made five or six pleasure parties with Oscar, defraying expenses, feeling the rope by which he tethered the young kid must be slackened. These “pranks,” as he called them, helped Oscar endure existence, for there was little amusement in breakfasting with Uncle Cardot or seeing his mother, who lived even more penuriously than Desroches. Moreau could not be as familiar with Oscar as Godeschal could; perhaps Godeschal, sincere friend to young Husson, had been behind Moreau in safe efforts to initiate the poor youth into life’s mysteries. Oscar, grown prudent, had come to see the extent and character of the fault committed on that luckless journey; but his repressed fancies and youthful follies might still get the better of him. Nevertheless, the more knowledge he got of the world and its laws, the better his mind would form itself, and, provided Godeschal never lost sight of him, Moreau flattered himself that between them they could bring the son of Madame Clapart through in safety.“How is he getting on?” asked the land-agent of Godeschal on his return from a journey.“Always too much vanity,” replied Godeschal. “You give him fine clothes and fine linen, he wears the shirt-fronts of a stockbroker, and so my dainty coxcomb spends his Sundays in the Tuileries, looking out for adventures. What else can you expect? That’s youth. He torments me to present him to my sister, where he would see actresses, ballet-dancers, elegant young fops, spendthrifts wasting fortunes! His mind, I’m afraid, is not fitted for law. He can talk well, though; and if we could make him a barrister he might plead cases that were carefully prepared for him.”In November 1825, soon after Oscar had taken his new clerkship and was about to pass his licentiate examination, a new clerk arrived to fill the place vacated by Oscar’s promotion.This fourth clerk, Frédéric Marest, intended to enter the magistracy and was in his third year at the law school. A fine young man of twenty-three, he inherited about twelve thousand francs a year from a bachelor uncle, and was the son of Madame Marest, widow of a wealthy wood-merchant. Desiring to understand procedure and train to be a head-clerk in two years, he had placed himself in Desroches’ office to study; by thirty he hoped to be “procureur du roi.” Though Frédéric was cousin-german to Georges Marest, Oscar did not connect him with the grandson of Czerni-Georges.“Messieurs,” said Godeschal at breakfast, “I announce the arrival of a new jurisconsult; and as he is rich, rishissime, we will, I hope, make him pay a glorious entrance-fee.”“Forward, the book!” cried Oscar, nodding to the youngest clerk, “and pray let us be serious.”The youngest clerk climbed to the top shelf and produced a register covered with dust. We must explain this perennial joke of legal offices. In a clerical life where work is the rule, amusement is treasured; a hoax is a delightful rarity. The gravest clerk, at times, craves fun and quizzing, and a set of clerks will seize any hoax with keen appetite. This instinct explains Georges Marest’s behaviour in the coucou; the denizens of a studio and of a lawyer’s office are in this line superior to comedians.When Desroches bought a practice without clients he began a new dynasty, breaking the usual customs of receiving new-comers. Having taken an office where legal documents had never been scribbled, he bought new tables and white boxes edged with blue. His staff, clerks from other offices without mutual ties, were surprised to find themselves together. Godeschal, who had apprenticed under maître Derville, would not allow the precious tradition of the “welcome” to be lost. This “welcome” is a breakfast every neophyte gives to the ancients of the office.About the time Oscar arrived, during the first six months of Desroches’ installation, on a winter evening when work had been finished early and the clerks warmed themselves before the fire, Godeschal conceived and composed a Register “architriclino-basochien,” of great antiquity, contrived to appear genuine and saved from the fires of the Revolution. The work, approved by the clerks, began with a search through dealers in old paper for a proper register—paper with an eighteenth-century mark, bound in parchment, stamped like an order in council. The book was left about to gather mold and wear; the gilt edges were tarnished with surprising perfection. Then entries were forged. The first sixty pages were filled with fictional reports of receptions in an eighteenth-century legal style, as though the clerical guild of Basoche had been continuous since 1525.The Register opened with a mock solemnity: an eighteenth-century-styled invocation to Sainte-Geneviève, an assertion that since 1525 the clerks of this Practice had existed under her protection, and a declaration by clerks of a predecessor’s office that they renewed and continued the register and the archives. Names and dates—Malin, Grévin, Athanase Féret, Jacques Héret, Regnault de Saint-Jean-d’Angély, Bedeau—were appended, and an account of a long breakfast at Courtille that lasted until seven the next morning.Expertly engrossed, it contained twenty-seven reports of receptions of neophytes, the last in 1792. After a blank of fourteen years the register began again in 1806 with Bordin’s appointment as attorney before the first Court of the Seine. A deed proclaimed the reconstitution of the kingdom of Basoche, crediting Sainte-Geneviève and maître Bordin for the miraculous preservation of the archives, and recording clerks’ gratitude with a breakfast and theatre visit. The entries mixed legal formality with rollicking conviviality—detailed praise for dinners, wines, and the master’s generosity—creating the illusion of continuous clerical tradition.Under date June 1822, when Desroches took the oath, Godeschal’s declaration appeared, boasting of recovering the famous archives architriclino-basochien, seeking out a document dated 1786 connected with other proofs going back to 1525 and celebrating the “worship” of the Goddess Bottle and good living. Godeschal recorded a breakfast at the “Cheval Rouge,” recounting twelve bottles of twelve different wines, melons, and dishes, and thanked his sister Mariette, a celebrated dancer, for providing orchestra seats. The page bore stains of wine, pâtés, and candle-grease.Oscar’s own pretended reception, recorded with mock solemnity, lampooned his humble home repast with exaggerated praise: radishes, gherkins, anchovies, butter and olives for hors-d’œuvre; a “succulent soup of rice” credited to maternal solicitude and fine stews; a tongue of beef with tomatoes; a compote of pigeons; a timbale of macaroni with chocolate custards; and a dessert of eleven dishes, all washed down with sixteen bottles of choicest wines, Roussillon and Rhône outdoing Champagne and Burgundy. The statutes of their Order were parodied too: candidates must limit the magnificence of their welcome to the length of their purse; Madame Clapart’s liberality merited hurrahs. The entry purported to be signed by all the clerks.Three clerks had already been deceived by the Book, and three real “receptions of welcome” were falsely recorded in it. After each arrival the little sub-clerk placed the Archives Architriclino-Basochiennes on the new-comer’s desk; the clerks enjoyed the look on his face as he studied its facetious pages. Inter pocula each candidate learned the secret of the farce, and the revelation inspired him to hoax his successor.We see why Oscar, now a participator in the hoax, cried, “Forward, the book!”Ten minutes later a handsome young man with a fine figure and pleasant face presented himself, asked for Monsieur Desroches, and gave his name without hesitation.“I am Frédéric Marest,” he said, “and I come to take the place of third clerk.”“Monsieur Husson,” said Godeschal, “show monsieur his seat and tell him about the customs of the office.”The next day the new clerk found the register on his desk. He read a few pages, began to laugh, said nothing to the assembled clerks, and laid the book down again.“Messieurs,” he said at five o’clock, “I have a cousin who is head-clerk of the notary Maître Léopold Hannequin; I will ask his advice as to what I ought to do for my welcome.”“That looks ill,” cried Godeschal when Frédéric had gone, “he hasn’t the cut of a novice, that fellow!”“We’ll get some fun out of him yet,” said Oscar.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The next day at two a young man entered the office whom Oscar recognized as Georges Marest, now head-clerk to the notary Hannequin.“Ha! here’s the friend of Ali Pacha!” cried Oscar.“Hey! you here, Monsieur l’ambassadeur!” answered Georges, recognizing him.Godeschal asked if they knew one another.“We got into a scrape together about two years ago,” Georges said. “I had to leave Crottat and go to Hannequin.”“What was it?” asked Godeschal.“Oh, nothing,” replied Georges at Oscar’s signal. “We tried to hoax a peer of France and he bowled us over. So you want to jockey my cousin, do you?”“We jockey no one,” said Oscar with dignity, producing the famous register and pointing to a sentence of banishment passed on a refractory in 1788.Georges laughed, leafed through the archives, and then announced a fête. “Tomorrow (Sunday) you are bidden to the Rocher de Cancale at two.
