Afterwards I’ll take you to spend the evening with Madame la Marquise de las Florentinas y Cabirolos, where we’ll play cards and you’ll see the elite of the women of fashion. Therefore, gentlemen of the lower courts,” he added with notarial airs, “you will have to behave yourselves, and carry your wine like the seigneurs of the Regency.”The office cheered. Desroches emerged from his private room.“What’s all this about?” he asked. Seeing Georges, he suspected a plan to demoralize his clerks. He opened his cash-box and gave Oscar five hundred francs. “Go to the Palais and get from the registrar a copy of the decision in Vandernesse against Vandernesse; it must be served to-night if possible. I promised Simon twenty francs. Wait if the copy is not ready. Don’t let Derville stick a spoke in our wheel; Count Félix de Vandernesse is more powerful than his brother, our client, the ambassador. Keep your eyes open; if there’s the slightest hitch come straight back.”Oscar set off resolved to distinguish himself in his first entrusted affair as second clerk.After Georges and Oscar left, Godeschal probed the new clerk about the supposed marquise. Frédéric, cool and grave like a king’s attorney, sustained his cousin’s hoax. By his manner he convinced the office that the marquise might indeed be a Mexican-born widow of a Spanish grandee, courted by rich Georges. “She loves to laugh, sing, drink like me,” he whispered, quoting Béranger. “Georges is very rich: he inherited eighteen thousand francs a year and with twelve thousand from an uncle he has thirty thousand. He pays his debts and gives up the law. He hopes to be Marquis de las Florentinas; the young widow can give her title to her husband.”Though some clerks remained skeptical, the promise of a Rocher de Cancale breakfast and a fashionable evening put them in high spirits.In truth the Marquise de las Florentinas y Cabirolos was simply Mademoiselle Agathe‑Florentine Cabirolle, first danseuse at the Gaîté, whom uncle Cardot had taken up. After Madame Cardot’s death Cardot encountered Florentine leaving Coulon’s dancing-class when she was about thirteen. Charmed, he followed her home, learned she was a portress’s daughter, and soon installed mother and daughter in a modest apartment in rue de Crussol with mahogany furniture, carpets, a proper kitchen, and a monthly pension. Cardot, the generous patron, sent her to Vestris and watched her debut at sixteen in a melodrama’s ballet. He raised her stipend to five hundred francs; though no longer adored as an angel, he remained a “friend for life,” a second father.From 1820 to 1823 she moved among celebrated dancers—Mariette, Tullia, Florine, and Coralie. Cardot, now older, indulged a semi‑paternal devotion; he kept her close so he could still sing “Mère Godichon.” Over the years he saved eighty thousand francs. Expecting that Florentine would soon need the luxuries of a theatrical star, he had recently spent forty‑five thousand francs fitting up the former apartment of the late Coralie for her. The dance star’s lodgings on rue de Vendôme were magnificently furnished; she began to give dinners, spent three hundred francs a month on dress, kept a carriage, and had servants. An engagement at the Opera was in prospect. Cardot, who had been master of his household, now found himself under a capricious rule, but he loved his charge and intended to leave her a hundred thousand francs.Georges Marest, with thirty thousand francs a year and a handsome face, courted Florentine. A danseuse always wants a young man to take her out—dinners, boxes, drives, wines; the passion for such a star costs trifles to the favored mortal. Georges, newly wealthy after his uncle’s death, stopped thinking of becoming a notary. Frédéric, by contrast, persisted in pursuing public office.The clerks, sons of poor parents unused to the great world, dressed up to attend Georges’ breakfast and the promise of meeting the Mexican marquise. Oscar felt a mix of resentment toward Georges for a prior slight—he still recalled Moreau having flung him at the count’s feet—but he eagerly prepared to display his new clothes. “What luck,” he told Godeschal, “my mother had just made me a new coat, trousers, waistcoat; I have six frilled shirts!” Madame Clapart appeared with cravats and praised Godeschal as a model. Godeschal warned Oscar not to blunder as he had the previous night; Desroches had been furious that the judgment was not obtained. Godeschal had gone to the Palais himself at six that morning and secured a promise the copy would be ready by seven.Madame Clapart, proud of her son’s appointment and savings, had given him a gold watch and told him Cardot promised money for a conscript substitute. She also brought a pocket‑book from their friend Moreau. Oscar insisted on carrying the five hundred francs Desroches gave him to fetch the judgment; his mother, alarmed, urged leaving it with Godeschal, but the latter had already gone.Oscar lounged on the boulevards until Georges’ breakfast. Proud in his new clothes—blue waistcoat with palm pattern, black cashmere trousers, black coat, and a saved gilt‑top cane—he enjoyed prospects of the gay world. He tried to keep his aversion for Georges in check—an instinct to avoid those who had once injured him—but when at half‑past two he entered the Rocher de Cancale salon he forgot it. Present were an old captain of dragoons named Giroudeau, Finot the journalist who might secure Florentine’s Opera engagement, and du Bruel the author and friend of Tullia. Georges made himself charming.“You’ve taken to private diplomacy,” he said. “What difference between a lawyer and an ambassador? Ambassadors are attorneys of peoples. If I can ever be useful to you, let me know.”Oscar confessed Georges had once done him great harm. “Pooh!” said Georges. “It was Monsieur de Sérizy who behaved badly. His wife—I wouldn’t have her; and the count, Minister of State and peer of France, has a small mind. I don’t care a fig for him.” Oscar welcomed such slurs, which diminished his own fault in his eyes.The feast stretched long; courses took hours, dessert did not appear until eight. The wines were excellent. The clerks ate and drank like men newly freed from privation. Godeschal, after signing the report of the meal, left them to the wines and liqueurs. By half‑past ten Oscar was so intoxicated Georges packed him into a coach and sent him home, but ten others, also drunk, determined to walk to Florentine’s house. Only Georges, Giroudeau, du Bruel, and Finot could keep pace; the rest were carried by wines and carriages Georges sent for, and after an hour’s drive round the boulevards they returned by Bercy and the quays to rue de Vendôme.Florentine’s salon sparkled with stage princesses who, knowing of Frédéric’s joke, imitated the women of good society as they ate ices. Wax candles burned in candelabra; footmen in full livery served dainties on silver; Lyonnaise hangings and carpets dazzled the newcomers. Through Georges’ artful presentation and their own intoxication, the clerks believed in the Marquise de las Florentinas. Gold glittered on card‑tables in the bed‑chamber; in the salon women played vingt‑et‑un, kept by the celebrated Nathan.Presented to Florentine by Georges, Oscar at first did not recognize the dancer he had seen at the Gaîté in this aristocratically décolletée and lace‑swaddled figure. She received him with gracious manners, then took him to a vingt‑et‑un table and introduced him to the pretty Fanny Beaupré, who had just succeeded at the Porte‑Saint‑Martin.“My dear,” said Florentine, “allow me to present a charming youth whom you can take as a partner in the game.”“Ah! that will be delightful,” smiled the actress. “I am losing. Shall we go shares, monsieur?”Oscar sat, ashamed to mix his small silver coins with the actress’s gold. She put up five gold pieces; he produced his hundred francs in silver. In ten throws the actress lost the two hundred francs. She became banker; Florentine lent her five hundred francs obtained from Georges. Nathan had won twelve hundred francs, and the actress whispered to Oscar to bring them luck.Tempted by the opportunity and the atmosphere, Oscar took out the five hundred francs Desroches had given him. The first throws brought gains—two thousand francs—but a few turns later the gains vanished. Oscar wanted to feign illness and escape, but “honor” held him. Three more throws lost their stake. He drank iced punch; then, in a fit of desperation and hope, he accepted Florentine’s offer when she lent him a thousand francs to win back what he’d lost, advising him not to risk more than five hundred. She gave him the money to help him, yet the brigands Finot and Giroudeau won it from him. The company laughed over the “poor innocent.”By dawn, after “character dancing,” Florentine, tired, went to bed, oblivious to Oscar still asleep in the boudoir.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################About eleven the next morning a tremendous noise woke the unfortunate clerk. Hearing his uncle Cardot’s voice, he feigned sleep and buried his face in the yellow velvet cushions where he had passed the night.“Really, my little Florentine,” said the old gentleman, “this is neither right nor sensible; you danced last evening in ‘Les Ruines,’ and you have passed the night in an orgy. That’s deliberately going to lose your freshness. Besides, it was ungrateful to inaugurate this beautiful apartment without even letting me know. Who knows what has been going on here?”“Old monster!” cried Florentine. “Haven’t you a key that lets you in at all hours? My ball lasted till five in the morning, and you have the cruelty to come and wake me at eleven!”“Half-past eleven, Titine,” Cardot corrected meekly. “I came out early to order a dinner fit for an archbishop at Chevet’s. Just see how the carpets are stained! What sort of people did you have here?”“You needn’t complain, for Fanny Beaupré told me you were coming to dinner with Camusot, and to please you I’ve invited Tullia, du Bruel, Mariette, the Duc de Maufrigneuse, Florine, and Nathan. So you’ll have the four loveliest creatures ever seen behind the foot-lights; we’ll dance you a ‘pas de Zéphire.’”“It is enough to kill you to lead such a life!” cried Cardot. “And look at the broken glasses! What pillage! The antechamber actually makes me shudder—”He stopped, magnetized. Through the open door of the boudoir he saw the outline of a man in a black coat.“Ah, Mademoiselle Cabirolle!” he said.“Well, what?” she asked.Florentine’s eyes followed Cardot’s; then, seeing the clerk, she burst into fits of laughter that confused Cardot, and Oscar was compelled to rise; Florentine took him by the arm, still pealing at the conscience-stricken faces of uncle and nephew.“You here, nephew?”“Nephew? so he’s your nephew?” cried Florentine, with another burst of laughter. “You never told me about him. Why didn’t Mariette carry you off?” she said to Oscar, who stood petrified. “What can he do now, poor boy?”“Whatever he pleases!” said Cardot sharply, starting toward the door as if to go.“One moment, papa Cardot. You will be good enough to get your nephew out of a scrape into which I led him; for he played his master’s money and lost it, and I lent him a thousand francs to win it back, and he lost that too.”“Miserable boy! you lost fifteen hundred francs at play at your age?”“Oh, uncle, uncle!” cried Oscar, plunged into despair, dropping on his knees, hands clasped. “It is twelve o’clock! I am lost, dishonored! Monsieur Desroches will have no pity! He gave me the money for an important affair, in which his pride was concerned. I was to get a paper at the Palais in the case of Vandernesse versus Vandernesse! What will become of me? Oh, save me for the sake of my father and aunt! Come with me to Monsieur Desroches, and explain it to him; make some excuse—anything!”These words burst out through sobs that might have moved even a sphinx.“Old skinflint!” said the danseuse, crying. “Will you let your own nephew be dishonored—the son of the man to whom you owe your fortune? His name is Oscar Husson. Save him, or Titine will deny you forever!”“But how did he come here?” asked Cardot.“Don’t you see that the reason he forgot to go for those papers was because he was drunk and overslept? Georges and his cousin Frédéric took all the clerks in his office to a feast at the Rocher de Cancale.”Père Cardot looked at Florentine and hesitated.“Come, come,” she said, “you old monkey, wouldn’t I have hidden him better if there had been any other business?”“There, take your five hundred francs, you scamp!” said Cardot to his nephew, “and remember, that’s the last penny you’ll ever get from me. Go and make it up with your master if you can. I’ll return the thousand francs which you borrowed of mademoiselle; but I’ll never hear another word about you.”Oscar, not wishing to hear more, disappeared. Once in the street, he knew not where to go.Chance which destroys men and chance which saves them were both at work that morning. When Mariette reached home that night, alarmed at what might happen to the youth in whom her brother took interest, she wrote a hasty note to Godeschal, telling him what had happened to Oscar and enclosing a bank bill for five hundred francs to repair his loss. She charged her maid to carry the note to Desroches’ office before seven o’clock. Godeschal, rising at six and finding Oscar absent, guessed what had occurred. He took the five hundred francs from his own hoard and rushed to the Palais, where he obtained a copy of the judgment and returned in time to lay it before Desroches by eight o’clock.Desroches, who rose at four, was in his office by seven. Mariette’s maid, not finding her mistress’s brother in his bedroom, came down to the office and met Desroches, to whom she naturally offered the note.“Is it about business?” he said; “I am Monsieur Desroches.”“You can see, monsieur,” replied the maid.Desroches opened the letter and read it. Finding the five-hundred-franc note, he went into his private office, furious with his second clerk. About half-past seven he heard Godeschal dictating to the second head-clerk a copy of the document in question, and a few moments later the good fellow entered his master’s office with an air of triumph.“Did Oscar Husson fetch the paper this morning from Simon?” inquired Desroches.“Yes, monsieur.”“Who gave him the money?”“Why, you did, Saturday,” replied Godeschal.“Then it rains five-hundred-franc notes,” cried Desroches. “Look here, Godeschal, you are a fine fellow, but that little Husson does not deserve such generosity. I hate idiots, but I hate still more the men who will go wrong in spite of the fatherly care which watches over them.” He gave Godeschal Mariette’s letter and the banknote which she had sent. “You must excuse my having opened it,” he said, “but your sister’s maid told me it was on business. Dismiss Husson.”“Poor unhappy boy! what grief he has caused me!” said Godeschal. “That tall ne’er-do-well of a Georges Marest is his evil genius; he ought to flee him like the plague; if not, he’ll bring him to some third disgrace.”“What do you mean by that?” asked Desroches.Godeschal briefly related the affair of the journey to Presles.“Ah! yes,” said the lawyer, “I remember Joseph Bridau told me that story about the time it happened. It is to that meeting that we owe the favor Monsieur de Sérizy has since shown in the matter of Joseph’s brother, Philippe Bridau.”At this moment Moreau, to whom the Vandernesse case was important, entered the office. The marquis wished to sell the land in parcels and the count opposed such a sale. The land-agent therefore bore the first fire of Desroches’ wrath against his ex-second clerk and all the threatening prophecies which he fulminated against him. The result was that this sincere friend and protector of the unhappy youth came to the conclusion that his vanity was incorrigible.“Make him a barrister,” said Desroches. “He has only his last examination to pass. In that line, his defects might prove virtues, for self-love and vanity give tongues to half the attorneys.”Meanwhile Clapart, who was ill, was being nursed by his wife—a painful, unrewarded duty. The sick man tormented her; impoverished and crafty, he stung his wife with malicious teasing. By the way she boasted of Oscar whenever he obtained the slightest success, Clapart knew the extent of her secret uneasiness and took pains to rouse it whenever he could.“Well, Madame,” Clapart would say, “Oscar is doing better than I even hoped. That journey to Presles was only a heedlessness of youth. Where can you find young lads who do not commit such faults? Poor child! he bears his privations heroically! If his father had lived, he would never have had any. God grant he may know how to control his passions!”While these events occurred in the rue de Vendôme and the rue de Béthisy, Clapart, wrapped in an old dressing-gown, watched his wife in their bedroom as she prepared the family broth, Clapart’s “tisane,” and her own breakfast.“Mon Dieu! I wish I knew how the affair of yesterday ended. Oscar was to breakfast at the Rocher de Cancale and spend the evening with a marquise—”“Don’t trouble yourself! Sooner or later you’ll find out about your swan,” said her husband. “Do you really believe in that marquise? Pooh! A young man who has senses and a taste for extravagance like Oscar can find such ladies as that on every bush—if he pays for them. Some fine morning you’ll find yourself with a load of debt on your back.”“You are always trying to put me in despair!” cried Madame Clapart. “You complained that my son lived on your salary, and never has he cost you a penny. For two years you haven’t had the slightest cause of complaint against him; here he is second clerk, his uncle and Monsieur Moreau pay all expenses, and he earns, himself, a salary of eight hundred francs. If we have bread to eat in our old age we may owe it all to that dear boy. You are really too unjust—”“You call my foresight unjust, do you?” replied the invalid, crossly.Just then the bell rang. Madame Clapart ran to open the door, and remained in the outer room with Moreau, who had come to soften the blow which Oscar’s new folly would deal to the poor mother’s heart.“What! he gambled with the money of the office?” she cried, bursting into tears.“Didn’t I tell you so, hey?” said Clapart, appearing like a spectre at the salon’s door where curiosity had drawn him.“Oh! what shall we do with him?” said Madame Clapart, whose grief made her impervious to Clapart’s taunts.“If he bore my name,” replied Moreau, “I should wait composedly till he draws for the conscription, and if he gets a fatal number I should not provide him with a substitute. This is the second time your son has committed a folly out of sheer vanity. Well, vanity may inspire fine deeds in war and may advance him in the career of a soldier. Besides, six years of military service will put some lead into his head; and as he has only his last legal examination to pass, it won’t be much ill-luck for him if he doesn’t become a lawyer till he is twenty-six; that is, if he wants to continue in the law after paying, as they say, his tax of blood. By that time, at any rate, he will have been severely punished, he will have learned experience, and contracted habits of subordination. Before making his probation at the bar he will have gone through his probations in life.”“If that is your decision for a son,” said Madame Clapart, “I see that the heart of a father is not like that of a mother. My poor Oscar a common soldier!--”“Would you rather he flung himself headforemost into the Seine after committing an dishonorable action? He cannot now become a solicitor; do you think him steady and wise enough to be a barrister? No. While his reason is maturing, what will he become? A dissipated fellow. The discipline of the army will, at least, preserve him from that.”“Could he not go into some other office? His uncle Cardot has promised to pay for his substitute; Oscar is to dedicate his graduating thesis to him.”At that moment carriage-wheels were heard, and a hackney-coach containing Oscar and all his worldly belongings stopped before the door. The luckless young man came up at once.“Ah! here you are, Monsieur Joli-Cœur!” cried Clapart.Oscar kissed his mother and held out a hand to Moreau, which Moreau refused. Oscar replied with a reproachful look the boldness of which he had never shown before. Then he turned on Clapart.“Listen to me, monsieur,” said the youth, transformed into a man. “You worry my poor mother devilishly, and that’s your right, for she is, unfortunately, your wife. But as for me, it is another thing. I shall be of age in a few months; and you have no rights over me even as a minor. I have never asked anything of you. Thanks to Monsieur Moreau, I have never cost you one penny, and I owe you no gratitude. Therefore, I say, let me alone!”Clapart, hearing this apostrophe, slunk back to his sofa. The reasoning and inward fury of the young man, who had just received a lecture from his friend Godeschal, silenced the imbecile mind of the sick man.“A momentary temptation, such as you yourself would have yielded to at my age,” said Oscar to Moreau, “has made me commit a fault which Desroches thinks serious, though it is only a peccadillo. I am more provoked with myself for taking Florentine of the Gaieté for a marquise than I am for losing fifteen hundred francs after a little debauch in which everybody, even Godeschal, was half-seas over. This time, at least, I’ve hurt no one but myself. I’m cured of such things forever. If you are willing to help me, Monsieur Moreau, I swear to you that the six years I must still stay a clerk before I can get a practice shall be spent without—”“Stop there!” said Moreau. “I have three children, and I can make no promises.”“Never mind, never mind,” said Madame Clapart to her son, casting a reproachful glance at Moreau. “Your uncle Cardot—”“I have no longer an uncle Cardot,” replied Oscar, relating the scene at the rue de Vendôme.Madame Clapart, feeling her legs give way, staggered to a chair and fell as if struck by lightning.“All the miseries together!” she said, and fainted.Moreau took her in his arms and carried her to bed. Oscar remained motionless, as if crushed.“There is nothing left for you,” said Moreau when he returned, “but to make yourself a soldier. That idiot of a Clapart looks to me as though he couldn’t live three months, and then your mother will be without a penny. Ought I not, therefore, to reserve for her the little money I am able to give? It was impossible to tell you this before her. As a soldier, you’ll eat plain bread and reflect on life such as it is to those who are born into it without fortune.”“I may get a lucky number,” said Oscar.“Suppose you do, what then? Your mother has well fulfilled her duty towards you. She gave you an education; she placed you on the right road, and secured you a career. You have left it. Now, what can you do? Without money, nothing; as you know by this time. You are not a man who can begin a new trade by taking off your coat and going to work in your shirt-sleeves with the tools of an artisan. Besides, your mother loves you, and she would die to see you come to that.”Oscar sat down and no longer restrained his tears. At last he understood this language, so completely unintelligible to him since his first fault.“Men without means ought to be perfect,” added Moreau, not suspecting the profundity of that cruel sentence.“My fate will soon be decided,” said Oscar. “I draw my number the day after to-morrow. Between now and then I will decide upon my future.”Moreau, deeply distressed in spite of his stern bearing, left the household in the rue de la Cerisaie to its despair.Three days later Oscar drew the number twenty-seven. In his interest the former steward of Presles had courage to go to the Comte de Sérizy and ask for his influence to get Oscar into the cavalry. It happened that the count’s son, having left the École Polytechnique rather low in his class, had been appointed, as a favor, sub-lieutenant in a regiment of cavalry commanded by the Duc de Maufrigneuse. Oscar was thus, through the Comte de Sérizy’s intercession, drafted into that regiment, with the promise of promotion to quartermaster within a year. Chance had placed the ex-clerk under the command of the son of the Comte de Sérizy.Madame Clapart, after languishing for several days, became a victim to remorse. She attributed the sorrows of her second marriage and the misfortunes of her son to retribution for the pleasures of her youth. This belief hardened into conviction. The poor woman went, for the first time in forty years, to confess herself to the Abbé Gaudron, vicar of Saint-Paul’s, and took up devotion. But so ill-used and loving a soul as Madame Clapart’s could never be anything but simply pious. The Aspasia of the Directory wanted to expiate her sins in order to draw down God’s blessing on Oscar, and she henceforth vowed herself to works of the purest piety. She believed she had won heaven’s attention when she saved the life of Monsieur Clapart, who, thanks to her devotion, lived on to torture her; yet she chose to see his tyranny as a trial inflicted by One who loveth while He chasteneth.Oscar meantime behaved so well that by 1830 he was first sergeant of the company of the Vicomte de Sérizy, which gave him the rank of sub-lieutenant of the line. Oscar Husson was by that time twenty-five. As the Royal Guard, to which his regiment was attached, was always in or near Paris, he came to see his mother from time to time and tell her his griefs; he had sense enough to see he could hardly become an officer as matters then were. The cavalry grades were being taken up by the younger sons of noble families, and men without titles found promotion difficult. Oscar’s sole ambition was to leave the Guards and be appointed sub-lieutenant in a regiment of the line. In February 1830 Madame Clapart obtained this promotion for her son through the influence of Madame la Dauphine, granted to the Abbé Gaudron, now rector of Saint-Pauls.Although Oscar outwardly professed devotion to the Bourbons, in the depths of his heart he was a liberal. Therefore, in the struggle of 1830, he went over to the side of the people. This desertion, significant because of the crisis, brought him into public notice. During the triumphal excitement in August he was promoted lieutenant, received the cross of the Legion of Honor, and was attached as aide-de-camp to La Fayette, who made him captain in 1832. When La Fayette was removed from command of the National Guard, Oscar, whose devotion to the new dynasty amounted to fanaticism, was appointed major of a regiment sent to Africa at the time of the Prince-Royal’s first expedition. The Vicomte de Sérizy chanced to be lieutenant-colonel of this regiment. At the affair of the Makta, where the field had to be abandoned to the Arabs, Monsieur de Sérizy was left wounded under a dead horse. Oscar, discovering this, called to the squadron:“Messieurs, it is going to death, but we cannot abandon our colonel.”He dashed upon the enemy, and his electrified soldiers followed him. The Arabs, astonished by this furious return, allowed Oscar to seize the vicomte, whom he flung across his horse and carried off at full gallop, receiving two slashes from yataghans on his left arm.Oscar’s conduct was rewarded with the officer’s cross of the Legion of Honor and promotion to lieutenant-colonel. He took most affectionate care of the Vicomte de Sérizy, whose mother met him on the regiment’s arrival at Toulon, where, as we know, the young man died of his wounds.The Comtesse de Sérizy had not separated her son from the man who had shown him such devotion. Oscar himself was so seriously wounded that the surgeons whom the countess had brought from Paris deemed it best to amputate his left arm.Thus the Comte de Sérizy was led not only to forgive Oscar for his painful remarks on the journey to Presles, but to feel himself indebted to him on behalf of his son, now buried in the chapel of the château de Sérizy.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Years after the Makta affair, on a May morning beneath the porte-cochere of the Lion d’Argent in the rue de Faubourg Saint-Denis, an old lady in black, leaning on a bronzed, one-armed man of about thirty-four wearing a Legion of Honor rosette, waited for Pierrotin’s diligence. Pierrotin, master of the Oise line, would scarcely have recognized in this soldier the Oscar Husson he once escorted to Presles. Madame Husson, now a widow transformed by piety, was hardly recognizable either. Clapart, the idle lounger whose presence had led to the earlier disaster, was dead; his death had, in a way, served his wife by removing his evil influence. She now received a pension of fifteen hundred francs a year.The coach, roomy enough for nineteen passengers, was in good order; Pierrotin, still brisk at around fifty-six, supervised porters stowing luggage. Oscar told Pierrotin two places in the interieur were taken under the name of his servant Bellejambe, whom he said had reserved them. Pierrotin guessed he was the new collector of Beaumont, replacing Monsieur Margueron’s nephew. The officer, wishing to remain unknown for the moment, pressed his mother’s arm to keep her from speaking.A voice from the street made Oscar start: Georges Marest hailed, asking if there was a seat. Pierrotin rebuked him for the familiar tone. By voice alone, Oscar would not have recognized Georges. Once elegant, Georges was now almost bald, with tufts of hair above his ears, a pear-shaped belly, blotched skin and dull, vinous eyes; his clothes were shabby yet pretentious, and a battered cane and mismatched finery hinted at a desire to display a past rank despite decay.Georges asked about seats; Pierrotin answered the coupe was taken by a peer of France, the Baron de Canalis, his wife, and mother-in-law, leaving only one place in the interieur. Georges, recalling past adventures with the Comte de Serizy, accepted the interieur. He glanced at Oscar and his mother but did not recognize them: Oscar’s African bronzing, pronounced moustache and whiskers, strict dress, missing arm and rosette would have concealed his identity; Madame Clapart’s religious austerity hid the Aspasia of 1797.Pere Leger, an enormous old man in plain but good clothes and clearly a millionaire, came along and received Pierrotin’s respectful greeting. Georges jeered at his own past follies; Leger corrected his address and the two traded remarks about change and fortune. An old man named Reybert joined, followed by Joseph Bridau, the celebrated painter, whose fame and bearing were evident; neither Georges nor Oscar recognized him at first.A clerk read the passenger list: the Canalis party in the coupe; Bellejambe, two places in the interieur; de Reybert, three; Georges gave his name lowly at the clerk’s request. The rotunde filled with nurses, country folk, and shopkeepers; four young men were sent to the imperiale. The coach moved off, climbing the faubourg Saint-Denis, racing to Saint-Denis, then taking the road through the valley of Montmorency.At the turn into Montmorency, Georges broke the silence, joking about speed and displaying a silver watch. Leger corrected his title; Georges boasted he had “made the revolution of July,” admitting that it ruined him. Joseph Bridau teased him about new campaigns; conversation turned to familiar names and fortunes. Leger noted Pierrotin had become a principal entrepreneur of the Oise coaches, owning a hotel where diligences stopped and a helpful wife and daughter.They spoke of the old circle: Mistigris, now famed as Leon de Lora; the youth who had offended the count with foolish talk; the count himself, driven yet fragile—Bridau feared the count’s last journey would be from Presles to Isle-Adam for Bridau’s marriage. Reybert reported the count’s wife visited once a month and had recently had her niece marry a rich Pole, the Comte Laginski. Madame Clapart asked who would inherit Monsieur de Serizy’s property; Georges said the count’s wife would take it and bury him. Leger teased Georges about illusions; Georges defended the countess’s elegance.Conversation turned to Moreau, the steward the count had dismissed, now deputy from the Oise and owner of the Pointel estate between Presles and Beaumont—something Georges called “bad taste.” Monsieur de Reybert warned Georges to speak softly: Madame Moreau and her daughter, the Baronne de Canalis, were in the coupe. Georges speculated on what dowry could have induced the great orator to marry Moreau’s daughter; Leger guessed “something like two millions.” Georges sneered that the orator always liked millions.Oscar interrupted to reproach Georges for speaking ill in a public conveyance. Joseph Bridau glanced at Oscar, then recognized his rosette.“Monsieur is not an ambassador, but his rosette tells us he has made his way nobly,” Bridau said, noting that he and General Giroudeau had named him in their reports. Georges then recognized Oscar.“Faith! if it hadn’t been for your voice I should never have known you,” Georges exclaimed. Reybert recalled that Oscar had rescued Vicomte Jules de Serizy from the Arabs and that the count had obtained for him the collectorship at Beaumont as a stepping stone to Pontoise. Oscar confirmed this. Bridau invited Oscar to his marriage at Isle-Adam; Oscar accepted, asking whom Bridau would marry. Bridau answered: Mademoiselle Leger, Pere Leger’s granddaughter, the marriage arranged by the count to secure the painter’s future.Georges, eager for assistance, declared himself fortunate to have chosen that day to traverse the valley; he announced he worked for a recently formed company called “l’Espérance,” whose statutes were approved by royal ordinance. He explained it provided dowries to girls after ten years, annuities to old men, education for children—handling fortunes and appointing agents across France. Leger wryly likened it to an insurance company; Georges corrected him, calling himself the inspector-general charged with finding honest correspondents and agents, a delicate and difficult task.Pierrotin and Oscar joked; Georges curtly replied to Oscar’s question about how he’d lost his thirty thousand a year with, “As you lost your arm,” to which Oscar returned a bitter, sarcastic remark. Georges admitted he had too many shares to sell.They halted at Saint-Leu-Taverny to change horses and for passengers to disembark briefly. Oscar admired Pierrotin’s agility as he unhooked traces and tended the horses. Oscar introduced his mother to Pierrotin, who confessed he wouldn’t have known them and remarked that Africa darkens the skin. Madame Moreau de l’Oise, alighting from the coupe, heard the name and glanced disdainfully at Oscar and his mother. Oscar’s attempt to be gracious to Pierrotin was one of the last blunders vanity would lead him to commit: two months after settling in Beaumont-sur-Oise, Oscar began courting Pierrotin’s daughter, Georgette, whose dowry was one hundred and fifty thousand francs, and he married her in late winter, 1838.The narrative remarks that the Presles journey had taught Oscar discretion; his disaster at Florentine’s card-party had strengthened his honesty; military hardships taught him the social hierarchy and the acceptance of his lot. He became prudent and capable, content enough. The Comte de Serizy, before his death, secured him the collectorship at Pontoise; with the influence of Monsieur Moreau de l’Oise, the Comtesse de Serizy and the Baron de Canalis, he later obtained a receiver-generalship, and the Camusot family recognized him as a relation.Oscar is described finally as a commonplace, gentle, modest man who keeps to a middle course like his government, exciting neither envy nor contempt: the modern bourgeois.ADDENDUMThe following personages appear in other stories of the Human Comedy.Beaupre, Fanny — Modeste Mignon; Another Study of Woman; The Gondreville Mystery; A Daughter of Eve############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################DEDICATION(To a Polish Lady.)CHAPTER I. THE CHALETIn October 1829 the notary Simon Babylas Latournelle walks from Havre to Ingouville with his family and his head clerk Jean Butscha. He confides to his son Exupère a contrived plan to test whether Modeste Mignon has a lover: after the formalities at the Chalet, Exupère is to hurry back from a walk and let Modeste overhear him whisper to Dumay, “The young man has come.” The Latournelles—especially Madame Latournelle, pedantic and provincially grand—act as chaperones for Modeste under Dumay’s vigilance.The Chalet is a small ornamental cottage on the Ingouville ridge, once a porter’s lodge, now a charming dwelling leased to Jean-François Bernard Dumay: a stubborn Breton, former lieutenant, and faithful cashier of the ruined house of Mignon. Vilquin, the villa’s proud new owner, resents Dumay for clinging to his lease and occupying the bright red Chalet, a jewel in the garden. Despite offers and temptations, Dumay insists on preserving the Chalet as home for Madame Mignon and her daughter Modeste.CHAPTER II. A PORTRAIT FROM LIFEThe evening the Latournelles arrive finds a small, attentive company assembled: Gobenheim, a frugal young banker; the blind, stately Madame Mignon; her daughter Modeste; the faithful dwarf-like Butscha; Madame Latournelle; and Dumay and his wife. Dumay has been visibly agitated—he has even laid pistols on the chimney and kept watch with his two Pyrenean dogs—because he believes Modeste may be in love. The household’s anxiety, and Madame Mignon’s private conviction, set a trap around Modeste. Yet she remains absorbed in her embroidery, apparently unaware, while the others exchange furtive glances.Modeste is twenty: pale-gold-haired, delicate, thoughtful, and reserved. Her beauty is subtle rather than flamboyant—an inner chastity and poetic sensibility. She is devoted to her blind mother and seems to live a double life: outwardly dutiful and domestic, inwardly given to deep reading, music, and reverie. Dumay’s intensity derives from an oath to the absent father, Colonel Charles Mignon: guard Modeste’s honor as if life depended on it.CHAPTER III. PRELIMINARIESJean Dumay’s past is recounted: born at Vannes, son of a revolutionary official later executed, he fled to join the army and became close to Charles Mignon (Comte de La Bastie), a handsome Provençal officer. They were lifelong friends through campaigns and imprisonment. After the fall of the Empire, Mignon turned merchant in Havre, prospered, and made Dumay his cashier. Dumay’s loyalty, honesty, and thrift helped build the Mignon fortune; he married an American, set up comfortably, and was rewarded by the colonel with residence at the Chalet and a substantial account.In 1826 Mignon’s house collapses under commercial reverses. On the eve of his departure abroad to seek recovery, he charges Dumay and his friends to guard his wife and daughters. The colonel sails away, leaving Dumay, Latournelle, and Gobenheim to wind up affairs and protect the family. Their public notice of suspension of payment is dignified: the friends sell assets and pay debts, and Madame Mignon and Modeste retire to the Chalet, where Dumay vows to defend Modeste’s purity.CHAPTER IV. A SIMPLE STORYThe ruin coincides with private tragedy. Bettina Caroline, Modeste’s elder sister—once seduced by the dashing Charles d’Estourny—had been abandoned and ruined in spirit; she returned ill after the house’s failure and died young. Her death and the shame of the seduction haunt the family. On her deathbed Bettina entreated Modeste to be guarded against clandestine love: “Never give your heart without your hand; tell everything to papa and mamma.” She left Modeste a ring engraved “Think of Bettina, 1827,” to be kept until marriage.After the national crisis and the family catastrophe, Modeste and her mother live quietly at the Chalet. The community conjectures, criticizes, and sometimes ridicules them. Dumay, obsessive in his charge, keeps night watch, inspects the grounds, and trains his dogs; Madame Dumay and Madame Latournelle act as vigilant chaperones. The household routines and whist games mask Modeste’s inner life, which only the blind mother, by feeling and tenderness, perceives. Madame Mignon senses a change—subtle signs of happiness and expectation—and voices fears about a hidden lover.CHAPTER V. THE PROBLEM STILL UNSOLVEDOne evening the conspirators carry out Latournelle’s test. Exupère bursts in, announcing aloud, “The young man is here!” Dumay rushes for his pistols and runs out; the household trembles for Modeste’s safety. Yet she remains calm, explaining she is going to prepare her mother for bed. The trap fails to expose any interloper: Dumay finds no trace but his own fears. The friends debate whether Modeste has a lover. Latournelle and Dumay suggest she might be merely romantic, given to reading Byron and foreign poets; but Madame Mignon insists by intuition that Modeste is in love. The company is divided between suspicion, protectiveness, and the comic awkwardness of provincial surveillance.CHAPTER VI. A MAIDEN’S FIRST ROMANCEWe learn Modeste’s inner life. After Bettina’s fall and the father’s disgrace, Modeste devoted herself to reading and composing airy romances in her head—an inner theatre where she tried on lives and loves. She cultivated a Platonic longing: not for ordinary men, but for genius—poets and artists, a companion of rare sensibility. Her reading in French, English, and German nourished this idealism; she fasted, prayed, and imagined a saintly life as a path to fulfil her yearning. She refused commonplace suitors; her pride combined with hunger for a spiritual, intellectual attachment.Her reveries centred on two contemporary literary figures. A lithograph of Canalis in a bookseller’s window and a novel by Arthez set her imagination alight. She resolved to correspond with Canalis, whose lyrics had soothed her. With the help of Françoise Cochet, a maid, she arranged to receive replies “poste restante” at Havre, and wrote to the poet in an ingenuous, ardent note signed “O. d’Este M.”—a trembling overture.CHAPTER VII. A POET OF THE ANGELIC SCHOOLCanalis is sketched: a compact, urbane poet of the Faubourg Saint-Germain—ambitious, vain, yet gifted with charm. Comfortable incomes from official sinecures and literary patronage sustain him, but his vanity and careerism compromise poetic authenticity. He has a young secretary, Ernest de La Brière, modest and honest, who admires Canalis but gradually sees the poet’s ego and artifice.Canalis and La Brière open Modeste’s letter with different temperaments. Canalis, half-amused, half-cynical, doubts anonymous affection and the sincerity of fan letters; La Brière reads the note with sympathy and predicts it might touch a man’s heart. Canalis composes a long, prudential, paternalistic reply warning the unknown correspondent of the dangers of loving a poet and of incompatibilities between poetic life and domestic claims. He counsels prudence, the sanctity of family duties, and the blunt truth that poets may be vain, inconsistent, and ill-suited to domestic bliss. The letter, though elegant and sincere in tone, chills Modeste’s romantic intoxication.Modeste, who had waited with trembling hope, reads Canalis’s reply at midnight. The prose, thoughtful though it is, dashes the fevered images she cherished. She weeps, then moves from despair to anger: convinced the poet’s counsel is condescending and self-satisfied, she is determined to answer in a way that will force an inconsistency, to meet his pity with a spirited retort. Thus she begins, by the moonlit sea and a nightingale’s song, to compose a reply kindled less by love than by wounded vanity and the proud resolve of a young romantic who wants to be seen and understood.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################CHAPTER VIII. BLADE TO BLADETo Monsieur de Canalis:Monsieur,--You are certainly a great poet, and you are something more,--an honest man. After showing such loyal frankness to a young girl who was stepping to the verge of an abyss, have you enough left to answer without hypocrisy or evasion the following question?Would you have written the letter I now hold in answer to mine,--would your ideas, your language have been the same,--had some one whispered in your ear (what may prove true), Mademoiselle O. d’Este M. has six millions and does intend to have a dunce for a master?Admit the supposition for a moment. Be with me what you are with yourself; fear nothing. I am wiser than my twenty years; nothing that is frank can hurt you in my mind. When I have read your confidence, if you deign to make it, you shall receive from me an answer to your first letter.Having admired your talent, often so sublime, permit me to do homage to your delicacy and your integrity, which force me to remain always,Your humble servant, O. d’Este M.Ernest de La Briere held this letter and paced in turmoil. A Parisian might call Modeste a flirt, but La Briere’s conscience weighed Truth, Justice, and Honor. Truth suspected hypocrisy—would he have courted a rich woman? Justice noted that society pursues wealthy brides; Honor demanded candor.He could not assume she had six millions, yet the idea stung. Six days later Modeste received a cautious reply:Mademoiselle,--You are not a D’Este. The name is a feigned one to conceal your own. Do I owe the revelations which you solicit to a person who is untruthful about herself? Question for question: Are you of an illustrious family? or a noble family? or a middle-class family? Undoubtedly ethics cannot change, but obligations vary by station. Just as the sun lights scenes diversely, morality adapts to rank and position. The peccadillo of a soldier is a crime in a general. Observances differ for a gleaner, for a seamstress, for the daughter of a shopkeeper, for a bourgeoise, for the heiress of a noble family. A king must not stoop to pick up a coin, but a laborer should retrace his steps for ten sous; both obey economy, yet duties differ. A daughter of Este, worth six millions, may ride like an amazon into the presence of a poet and say, “I love poetry;” a daughter of the people would ridicule herself imitating her. To what class do you belong? Answer sincerely, and I will answer your question.As I have not the honor of knowing you personally, and yet am bound to you, in a measure, by poetic communion, I avoid commonplace compliments. Perhaps you already enjoy making a man of books squirm.Modeste promptly claimed noble origin: a count for a father, quarterings enough for any court. La Briere, uneasy in Paris’s intrusive society, went to Havre, obtained secrecy from the post-office, followed Francoise Cochet to Ingouville, and saw Modeste at the chalet window. Struck by her beauty, he learned the villa belonged to the Vilquins and that the d’Herouvilles were visiting.Back in Paris he wrote a long, honest letter admitting that had he known she possessed vast wealth he would have behaved differently: wealth tempts men to flatter and seek security rather than self-respect; success in such pursuit might bring position but not true happiness. He confessed spying at Havre and blushed at it; seeing her altered his view—she might be an exception, a poetic ideal. He swore to make his fortune and choose a companion without cupidity; he urged her, if of ordinary station, to obey social law, though he admired her soul. He counseled women to restrain public ambition for the sake of home, and closed with a warm, reverent farewell.This letter relieved Modeste; she saw her mistake and altered arrangements to secure the post, resolving to receive letters herself. La Briere’s confession moved her: to have touched such a noble heart vindicated her intuition. She rejoiced and dreamed of her father’s return to make them happy.CHAPTER IX. THE POWER OF THE UNSEENTo Monsieur de Canalis:My friend,--Suffer me that name,--you have delighted me; I would not have you other than you are in that letter, the first--oh, may it not be the last! Who but a poet could have excused and understood a young girl so delicately?I wish to speak with the same sincerity. First, be glad you do not know me. I am neither the hideous Mademoiselle Vilquin nor the withered d’Herouville. The Cardinal d’Herouville flourished a century earlier; I take no account of lieutenants-general and abbés writing trumpery verses.I do not live in Villa Vilquin; there is not a drop of that chilly counter-blood in me. I come partly from Germany, partly from southern France; my mind loves reverie, my blood has Provençal vivacity. I am noble on both sides; on my mother’s side I might be in the Almanach de Gotha. My precautions are taken. It is not in any man’s power, nor the law’s, to unmask my incognito. I shall remain veiled, unknown.As to my person and my “belongings,” be easy. I am at least as handsome as the little girl on whom your eyes chanced in Havre; and I do not call myself poverty-stricken, though ten sons of peers may not accompany me. I have seen the humiliating comedy of the heiress sought for her millions. Make no attempt to reach me. Though free as air, I am watched and guarded—by myself and by people of nerve who would not hesitate to stab you if you tried to penetrate my retreat. I do not say this to tempt you; I believe I need no such incentive.Now to the second edition of your sermon.Will you have a confession? When I saw you distrustful and mistaking me for Corinne, I thought muses had taken you into valleys of boarding-school Parnassus. You are safe with me; I may love poetry, but I have no little verses, and my stockings remain white. Should I ever say “Come!” you will not find an old maid or a poor ugly creature.Ah, my friend, if you only knew how I regret that you came to Havre! You have lowered the charm of my romance. God alone knew the treasure I reserved for a man noble and perspicacious enough to come having faith in my letters, to meet me with the simplicity of a child: that was my dream. Now you have spoiled my treasure! But I forgive you; you live in Paris and a man dwells in a poet.Because I tell you this will you think me some little girl of illusions? You, witty and wise, have not guessed that when I received your pedantic lesson I said to myself: “No, my first letter was not a pebble flung by a vagabond child but a net cautiously thrown by a fisherman hoping for a miraculous draught.”All you say about family has my approval. The man who pleases me will have my heart and life—with my parents’ consent; I will neither grieve nor surprise them. I reign over them, and they have no prejudice. I have built my fortress and fortified it with devotion; I have armor engraved “Disdain.” I detest calculating motives and worship the beautiful and ideal without being merely romantic; though I have been in dream. I recognize the truth of society’s demands.For now we are two friends. Why seek an unseen friend? Your person may be unknown, but your mind and heart I know; they please me, and within my soul are thoughts that need a man of genius for a confidant. I do not wish my poem to be wasted; I would have it known to you as to God. What a precious thing is a true comrade! Later, if our souls are sisters, an old serving-man shall lead you to the cottage, villa, castle—I don’t know what bower it will be—but you admit it is poetic, and that I am agreeable. Have I not left you free? Have I gone with jealous feet to watch you in Paris salons? No; I ask only a spiritual and mystic alliance. Come to me when you are unhappy, wounded, weary. Tell me all; I have balms for your ills. I am twenty, but I have the sense of fifty and, through another’s experience, I know love’s horrors and delights. I know baseness and infamy; yet I am honest. I have no illusions; I have beliefs and religion. See! I open the ball of our confidences.Whoever I marry—if I choose him—may go to the East Indies and find me at home, working at tapestry begun before he left, each stitch a verse of his poem. I have resolved never to follow my husband where he does not wish me; I will be the divinity of his hearth. That is my religion. But why should I not test and choose the man to whom I shall be like life to body? Is a woman who thwarts the man she loves anything but disease? By life I mean joyous health.But to return to your letter: it contains the prosaic sentiments necessary to family life; without them no happiness is possible. To act as an honest man, to think as a poet, to love as women love—that is what I longed for, and it is no longer chimera.Adieu, my friend. I am poor at present. That is partly why I cling to concealment. I have read your last verses in the “Revue” with delight now that I am initiated in your austere loftiness.Will it make you unhappy to know a young girl prays for you; that you are her solitary thought—except her father and mother? Can there be any reason for rejecting pages full of you, written for you, seen by no eye but yours? Send me your counterpart. Your confidences—if full and true—will suffice for my happiness.O. d’Este M.La Briere read, enchanted and alarmed: had he already fallen? The unseen correspondent fascinated him. For a young secretary newly out of Paris’s bustle, epistolary intimacy was a new intoxication. He answered with guarded earnestness, irony, and real feeling:Mademoiselle,--Is it loyal to enthrone yourself in a poor poet’s heart with intent to abandon him if he is not exactly what you wish, leaving him long regrets—showing a moment’s image of perfection, giving a foretaste of happiness? I was short-sighted to solicit this letter in which you begin to unfold your fabric of thoughts. A man can enamor easily of such a mysterious unknown: fearlessness, originality, imagination, feeling. I therefore make the most of my remaining sense to offer humble remonstrances.Do you really believe letters—more or less true, expressions of the moment—can replace the revelations of daily intercourse? Man is dual: an invisible life of heart, which letters may serve, and a material life, to which more importance is given than you know. These must harmonize in your ideal; and this is rare.The pure, disinterested homage of a solitary soul consoles the literary man; I thank you with an impulse equal to your own. But after this poetical exchange, what next? What do you expect? I have neither Byron's genius nor his halo of fictitious damnation. What could you have hoped from him? Pride devoured him; vanity discouraged friendship. I, less than he, may have character discordances, and make friendship a burden. In exchange for your reveries, what will you gain? The dissatisfaction of a life not wholly yours. The compact is madness. Let me tell you why.Your projected poem is a plagiarism. A young German girl adored Goethe with girlish intoxication, made him her god though he was married; Madame Goethe indulged her with sly good-nature. The young enthusiast later married a younger, handsomer man. If a girl had made herself handmaid to a man of genius and then retired in solitude, she might have lived forever inlaid in his glory. If that is sublime, what of the reverse? As I am neither Goethe nor Byron, I cannot expect a cult. I am not disposed to martyrdom. I have ambition and a heart; I am young and have a career. See me for what I am. The king’s bounty and ministers’ protection give me a sufficient living. I have the outward bearing of an ordinary man. If I drive to soirées, my wheels know nothing of invested capital. If I am not rich, neither am I in garret penury like worthier men.Ah, what prosaic conclusions your young enthusiasm will find to these visions. Let us stop. If I have seemed to you a terrestrial paragon, you have been to me a thing of light. May nothing tarnish this episode. Were we to continue, I might love you; I might conceive a passion that rends obstacles. Suppose I pleased you—would we not end in the common way—marriage, household, children? Therefore, adieu.CHAPTER X. THE MARRIAGE OF SOULSTo Monsieur de Canalis:My Friend,--Your letter gives me as much pain as pleasure. But perhaps one day we shall find nothing but pleasure in writing. Understand me. The soul speaks to God and asks; he is mute. I seek in you the answers God does not make. Cannot Mademoiselle de Gournay and Montaigne’s friendship be revived in us? Do you not remember Sismondi’s household in Geneva? The most lovely home ever known, I have been told; like the Marquis de Pescaire’s. Ah, friend, is it impossible that two hearts, two harps, should answer each other from a distance, vibrating in unison? Man alone is harp, musician, and listener. Do you think me jealous like ordinary women? I know you go into the world and meet the handsomest and wittiest women in Paris. May not some mermaid have clasped you and inspired your prosaic answer? There is something more beautiful than Parisian garlands; there grows a flower on Alpine peaks—men of genius—which fertilize humanity with lofty dews. I seek to cultivate that flower.Do me the honor to believe there is nothing low in me. Were I Bettina, I should never have become Madame von Arnim; had I been one of Byron’s loves, I should be in a cloister. You have touched me. You do not know me, but shall. I feel something sublime in me that I dare to name without vanity. God has put in my soul the roots of that Alpine flower; I cannot plant it in a window-pot and see it die. No, that cup of single beauty shall not be dragged through vulgar life; it is yours—yours before any eye has blighted it, yours forever! Yes, my poet, to you belong my thoughts—secret and gay; my heart is yours without reserve and with infinite affection. If you personally do not please me, I shall never marry. I can live in the life of the heart on your mind and sentiments. I will be what I am—your friend. Yours is a noble moral nature; I have recognized and appreciated it, and that suffices. Do not laugh at a young handmaiden who shrinks not from being the old companion of a poet—a sort of mother, housekeeper; the guide of his judgment and a source of his wealth. This handmaiden—devoted, precious—will be Friendship, pure, disinterested, to whom you will tell all. She knits by lamp-light and waits when the poet returns vexed. Such shall be my destiny if not that of a happy wife; I shall neither be an old maid nor childless—I shall make myself a mother by caring for others and by secret cooperation in a great man’s existence.I hate commonplaceness. If free and rich, I will never belong to a ninny because he is a peer’s son, nor to a merchant who might ruin us, nor to a handsome creature who would be a woman in the household, nor to any man who would make me blush daily. Make yourself easy. My father adores my wishes; he will not oppose them. If I please my poet, and he pleases me, our love shall be built so high as to be inaccessible to misfortune. I am an eaglet; you will see it in my eyes.I shall not repeat, but will compress: I should be the happiest of women imprisoned by love as I now am imprisoned by paternal care. Ah, friend, let us see our romance to a real end: listen to its argument:A young girl locked in a tower longs to run in the park; she invents a way out, jumps from the casement, scales the wall, frolics on the neighbor’s sward. That girl is my soul; the neighbor’s park is your genius. Is it not natural? Was there ever a neighbor who did not complain of broken trellises? I leave it to my poet to answer.But do you want better reasons? Here they are. Marriages usually defy common sense. Parents inquire: the Leander is not a thief, has necessary fortune, comes from college or law and wears gentlemanly clothes—so he is allowed to meet the girl, who is instructed to guard her tongue and not reveal heart or soul, smiling like a dancer at a pirouette. Commands say not to seem alarmingly well educated. If settlements agreed, the parents let the pair see each other briefly. Each is as dressed in soul as in body. This comedy, mixed with bouquets, jewels, and theatre-parties, is called “paying your addresses.” It revolts me: I want actual marriage to be the result of a prior long marriage of souls. A woman has only one moment when reflection and experience should govern her; she risks her all and is forced to be a spectator. I have the right, will, and power to make my own unhappiness, and I use them, as my mother did, who married generously by instinct. I know you are free, a poet, noble-looking. Be sure I would not choose one of your Apollo brothers who was already married. If my mother was won by beauty, why should I not be attracted by spirit and form united? Shall I not know you better by correspondence than by the vulgar receiving of addresses? This is the question.But my proceedings at least do not bind us personally. I know love has illusions and every illusion its to-morrow; hence many partings among lovers vowed for life. The proof of love lies in suffering and happiness. When, after these trials, two beings have shown each other defects and virtues and truly observed characters, they may go to the grave hand in hand. Who told you our little drama would have no future? In any case, shall we not have enjoyed our correspondence?I await your orders, monseigneur, and am with all my heart,Your handmaiden,O. d’Este M.La Briere answered with passion and trembling: he could no longer be only a friend; she must give hope or he must renounce. Her reply startled him: she had moved toward the possibility of a life united in soul, yet would not meet him without learning more. Her compact required him to write his life plainly, tell what he did day by day, and then she would see him. That step involved her entire future and her parents’ consent; she longed to know how far great spirits could bend to family life. She justified her boldness and proclaimed that, if she found in him genius and nobleness, she would be faithful, devoted, and the companion of his life—either as wife or as the devoted domestic friend of a man of genius. Her letter ended in affectionate banter of a girl who believed herself taciturn.CHAPTER XI. WHAT COMES OF CORRESPONDENCEMany letters between Ernest and Modeste in September are omitted; they depict a passionate, reciprocal intimacy. For La Briere they were treasures; for Modeste they became love. The correspondence knitted two souls: hers the ideal of devotion, his the complement of Canalis’s fame. This epistolary marriage fed both imaginations and made each the other's chief preoccupation.Modeste sent a decisive letter proposing a test: Canalis was to be at Havre the next Sunday, attend morning mass, walk round the nave without speaking, wear a white rose in his buttonhole, and return to Paris to await her reply. She warned she might not say what he wished, and that she would not accept without having seen him.This letter came just after the quarrel between Dumay and Modeste and the family’s anxious days. Modeste’s mother noticed her disturbance and sought assurances. Madame Mignon, blind and broken, feared that any breach with her husband or failure in duty would kill him. She entreated Modeste to promise never to leave her father, to allow him to choose a husband after proper inquiry, and to keep him informed. Modeste kissed her mother and swore never to marry without her father’s consent and to think always of Bettina.At that moment Dumay hurried up the hill to the chalet with wondrous news: three letters had ruined them; one letter might restore them. A sea-captain had arrived from the China seas with a letter for Jean Dumay from Charles Mignon. It announced that Charles had made at least seven millions through opium and indigo trade, had bought a brig called the "Mignon," and intended to return soon. He sent a large check to Dumay and intended to keep the true amount of his fortune secret on his return, arranging through Mongenod to secure La Bastie and entail it, so that one of his sons-in-law might inherit. He wished Dumay to be his bailiff. Charles declared his love for his family and asked Dumay to prepare them for his return.Madame Mignon said, dryly, “Your father is coming.” Modeste asked how she knew, and her mother answered, “Nothing else could make Dumay hurry himself.” Dumay announced that the colonel had never been ill and was coming back with a fine ship and a fortune. He wanted secrecy. He believed the colonel sought his lost daughter Bettina, and though she was dead, he would learn of that only upon his return.Madame Mignon feared the shock of learning Bettina’s death might kill her husband. Dumay urged Modeste to write to her father, prepare him for grief, and promised to deliver the letter in Paris. Modeste, afraid that Canalis and Dumay might meet, hurried to write to her poet and postpone any rendezvous. Before leaving, she asserted proudly to Dumay and her mother that her hand belonged to her family but her heart was her own; she swore she would never act contrary to her pledges to her sister and mother.Dumay, reassured, smiled at Modeste’s spirited declarations and praised her truthfulness. Madame Mignon, though joyful at the news of the father’s return, still felt deep sadness; she would see him but feared the tale he would bring.Thus the return of Charles Mignon transformed the family’s fortunes and cast new light on Modeste’s future. The correspondence between her and the poet had deepened into a potential union of minds, to be tested by a meeting and, above all, by the consent and prudence of family ties.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################At this moment Modeste, glad at her father’s return yet disconsolate like Perrette with broken eggs, paced her room. She had hoped her father would bring more than Dumay had announced. Nothing would satisfy her new ambition for the poet except at least half the six millions she had named in her second letter. Torn between two joys and the grief of comparative poverty, she sat at the piano and gave voice to her wishes.Dumay and his wife were in the garden under the windows, discussing the fortune and asking one another’s desires. Monsieur and Madame Dumay had no family beyond the Mignons; they agreed to go live in Provence if the Comte de La Bastie did, and to leave their money to whichever of Modeste’s children might need it most.“Listen to Modeste,” said Madame Mignon. “None but a girl in love can compose such airs without study.”Houses may burn, fortunes sink, fathers return from distant lands, empires crumble, cholera ravage cities, yet a maiden’s love persists as surely as nature pursues hers.Inspired, Modeste set certain stanzas to music—printed in the second volume of Dauriat’s edition—adapting lines to her melody, a liberty that shocked admirers of a poet famed for exact measures.Hear, arise! the lark is shaking  Sunlit wings that heavenward rise;Sleep no more; the violet, waking,  Wafts her incense to the skies.Flowers revived, their eyes unclosing,  See themselves in drops of dewIn each calyx-cup reposing,  Pearls of a day their mirror true.Breeze divine, the god of roses,  Passed by night to bless their bloom;See! for him each bud uncloses,  Glows, and yields its rich perfume.Then arise! the lark is shaking  Sunlit wings that heavenward rise;Nought is sleeping—Heart, awaking,  Lift thine incense to the skies.“It is very pretty,” said Madame Dumay. “Modeste is a musician, and that’s all.”“The devil is in her!” cried Dumay; suspicion crept into his heart.“She loves,” Madame Mignon insisted.Madame Mignon’s certainty infected Dumay and destroyed the comfort the return and prosperity had brought him. The Breton went back to Havre and his desk in Gobenheim’s counting-room heavy-hearted; before dinner he went to Latournelle to confess his fears and seek the notary’s advice.“Yes, my dear friend,” said Dumay on the notary’s steps. “I now agree with Madame; she loves—yes, I am sure of it; and the devil knows the rest. I am dishonored.”“Don’t make yourself unhappy, Dumay,” answered the little notary. “We can outwit the little puss; sooner or later every girl in love betrays herself—you may be sure. We will talk this evening.”Thus all devoted to the Mignon family were as troubled and uncertain as before the old soldier’s experiment. Dumay decided not to go to Paris to secure his patron’s announced fortune until he had ascertained Modeste’s heart. These friends, who prized feeling above interest, feared that if the daughter were not pure, the father would die of grief when he learned of Bettina’s death and his wife’s blindness. Dumay’s distress so occupied the Latournelles that they even forgot Exupere’s parting for Paris that morning. During dinner Monsieur and Madame Latournelle and Butscha turned the problem over and over.“If Modeste loved anyone in Havre she would have shown fear yesterday,” said Madame Latournelle; “her lover, therefore, lives elsewhere.”“She swore to her mother this morning,” said the notary, “in Dumay’s presence, that she had not exchanged a look or word with any living soul.”“Then she loves after my fashion!” exclaimed Butscha.“And how is that?” asked Madame Latournelle.“Madame,” said the little cripple, “I love alone and afar—as far as from here to the stars.”“How do you manage it, you silly fellow?” she laughed.“Ah, madame! what you call my hump is the socket of my wings.”Butscha’s seal bore a star beneath the words “Fulgens, sequar”—“Shining One, I follow thee”—the motto of the house of Chastillonet. A beautiful woman may be as distrustful as the ugliest, he observed; Modeste was clever enough to fear she might be loved only for her beauty.Butscha’s mind worked with the concentrated intensity often attributed to deformed persons: where others dissipate energy, a curvature seemed to store and project it in flashes of feeling and insight. Such beings sometimes possess peculiar faculties—whimsy, malice, or rare goodness—living inwardly like instruments awaiting a touch. This partly explained old legends of dwarfs and enchanted creatures.Nearly at the key to the puzzle, Butscha, with all the anxious solicitude of a hopeless lover, resolved to capture Modeste’s secret. He would lay a trap—an intercepted glance, a sudden quiver, some small surgeon’s probe. That evening he followed his patrons to the Chalet, clouded with care, and allied himself with Dumay against Monsieur and Madame Latournelle. During a brief moment of Modeste’s absence, about nine to prepare for her mother’s bedtime, the others spoke openly; the poor clerk, depressed by conviction of Modeste’s love, seemed remote.“Well, what’s the matter with you, Butscha?” cried Madame Latournelle; “one would think you hadn’t a friend in the world.”Tears stood in the poor fellow’s eyes; he was the son of a Swedish sailor, his mother dead.“I have no one in the world but you,” he answered. “Your compassion is part of your religion; I can never lose it—and I will never deserve to lose it.”This struck a sensitive chord in all present.“We love you, Monsieur Butscha,” said Madame Mignon, with feeling.“I have six hundred thousand francs,” cried Dumay, “and you shall be a notary and Latournelle’s successor.”The American wife took the poor hunchback’s hand.“What! six hundred thousand francs!” exclaimed Latournelle, prickling at Dumay’s words; “and you allow these ladies to live as they do! Modeste ought to have a fine horse; why doesn’t she continue lessons in music and painting—”“He has had the money only a few hours!” said the little wife.“Hush!” murmured Madame Mignon.While words were exchanged, Butscha’s august mistress turned to him.“My son,” she said, “you are so surrounded by true affection that I never thought how my thoughtless phrase might be construed; but you must thank me, for it has shown you what friends your noble qualities have won.”“Then you must have news from Monsieur Mignon,” resumed the notary.“He is on his way home,” said Madame Mignon; “but let us keep the secret. When my husband learns how faithful Butscha has been—how he has shown the warmest and most disinterested friendship when others gave the cold shoulder—he will not leave provision to you alone, Dumay. And so, my friend,” she added, turning her blind face toward Butscha, “you can begin at once to negotiate with Latournelle.”“He’s of legal age, twenty-five and a half. As for me, making the purchase easy for you will be paying a debt,” said the notary.Butscha kissed Madame Mignon’s hand; his face was wet with tears as Modeste opened the salon door.“What are you doing to my Black Dwarf?” she demanded. “Who is making him unhappy?”“Ah! Mademoiselle Mignon, do luckless fellows cradled in misfortune ever weep for grief? They have shown me as much affection as I could feel if they were my own relations. I’m to be a notary; I shall be rich. Ha! the poor Butscha may become the rich Butscha,” he cried.He struck his chest and sat before the fireplace, casting a glance at Modeste that slipped like a ray of light between his half-closed eyelids. He perceived, in that glance, a chance to interrogate his sovereign’s heart. Dumay thought the clerk dared aspire to Modeste and exchanged a rapid glance with the others, who began to eye the little man with mixed terror and curiosity.“I, too, have my dreams,” said Butscha, keeping his eyes on Modeste.The young girl lowered her eyelids with a movement that revealed much to the young man.“You love romance,” he said. “Let me tell you mine; tell me whether it could be. To me wealth would bring greater happiness than to others; the highest joy I can imagine is to enrich the one I loved. You, mademoiselle, tell me if a man can make himself beloved independently of his person, be he handsome or ugly, and be loved for his spirit only?”Modeste looked at Butscha with a piercing, questioning glance; she shared Dumay’s suspicion of his motive.“Let me be rich, and I will seek some beautiful poor girl, abandoned as I was, who has suffered. I will write to her and console her, be her guardian spirit; she shall read my heart and possess my two gifts—my gold, delicately offered, and my thought robed in splendor that birth denied my grotesque body. I shall remain hidden, like a cause science seeks. God himself may not be glorious to the eye. Naturally the maiden will be curious; she will wish to see me; I shall tell her I am a monster of ugliness; I shall picture myself hideous.”At these words Modeste gave Butscha a look that pierced him through. Had she spoken aloud “What do you know of my love?” she could not have been more explicit.“If I have the honor of being loved for the poem of my heart, if some day such love may make a woman think me only slightly deformed, shall I not be happier than the handsomest man—as happy as a man of genius beloved by some celestial being like yourself?”Color suffused Modeste’s face; the cripple read nearly all he sought.“Well, if that be so,” he went on, “if we enrich the one we love, if we please the spirit and withdraw the body, is that not the way? At any rate it is the dream of your poor dwarf—a dream of yesterday; for today your mother gives me the key to future wealth by promising means to buy a practice. But before I become another Gobenheim, I seek to know whether this dream could be carried out. What do you say, mademoiselle?”Modeste was so astonished she scarcely noticed the question. The lover’s trap was better baited than the soldier’s; she was speechless.“Poor Butscha!” whispered Madame Latournelle. “Do you think he is going mad?”“You want to realize Beauty and the Beast,” Modeste said at length; “but you forget the Beast turned into Prince Charming.”“Do you think so?” said the dwarf. “I have always thought that transformation meant the phenomenon of the soul made visible, obliterating form under spirit’s light. If I were not loved I should stay hidden, that is all. You and yours, madame,” he added to his mistress, “instead of a dwarf at your service, will have a life and fortune.”So saying, Butscha resumed his seat, remarking to the whist-players with assumed calm, “Whose deal is it?” but within he whispered sadly: “She wants to be loved for herself; she corresponds with some pretended great man; how far has it gone?”“Dear mamma, it is nearly ten o’clock,” said Modeste.Madame Mignon said good-night and went to bed.Those who love in secret may have Pyrenean hounds, mothers, Dumays, and Latournelles to spy upon them and yet be safe; but when it comes to a lover—ah! that is diamond against diamond, flame against flame, mind to mind, an equation whose terms are mutual.On Sunday morning Butscha arrived at the Chalet before Madame Latournelle and blockaded the house awaiting the postman.“Have you a letter for Mademoiselle Mignon?” he asked the humble functionary when he appeared.“No, monsieur, none.”“This house has been a good customer to the post of late,” remarked the clerk.Modeste heard and saw the colloquy from her chamber window, where she always posted herself behind the blinds at that hour to watch for the postman. She ran downstairs into the little garden and called imperatively, “Monsieur Butscha!”“Here am I, mademoiselle,” he said, reaching the gate as Modeste opened it.“Will you tell me whether among your various titles to a woman’s affection you count that of shameless spying, in which you are now engaged?” she demanded, aiming a queenly glance to crush her slave.“Yes, mademoiselle,” he answered proudly. “Ah! I never expected the grub could serve a star—but so it is. Would you rather your mother and Monsieur Dumay and Madame Latournelle had guessed your secret than one excluded from life who seeks to be one of those flowers you cut and wear for a moment? They all know you love; but I, I alone, know how. Use me as a vigilant watchdog; I will obey you, protect you, and never bark; neither will I condemn you. I ask only to be of service.
