Your father has made Dumay keeper of the hen-roost; take Butscha to watch outside—poor Butscha who asks for nothing, not so much as a bone.”“Well, I’ll give you a trial,” said Modeste, who most wished to rid herself of so clever a watcher. “Please go at once to all the hotels in Graville and Havre, and ask if a gentleman has arrived from England named Monsieur Arthur—”“Listen, mademoiselle,” Butscha interrupted respectfully. “I will go and take a walk on the seashore, for you don’t want me to go to church today; that’s what it is.”Modeste looked at him with stupefied astonishment.“Mademoiselle, you have wrapped your face in cotton-wool and a silk handkerchief, but nothing is the matter with you; you have put that thick veil on to see someone yourself without being seen.”“Where did you acquire such perspicacity?” cried Modeste, blushing.“Moreover, mademoiselle, you have not put on your corset; a cold in the head wouldn’t oblige you to disfigure your waist and wear half a dozen petticoats, nor hide your hands in old gloves, nor hide your pretty feet in hideous shoes, nor—”“That’s enough,” she said. “How am I to be certain you will obey me?”“My master must go to Sainte-Adresse. He does not like it, but he is so good he won’t deprive me of my Sunday; I will offer to go for him.”“Go, and I will trust you.”“You are sure I can do nothing for you in Havre?”“Nothing. Hear me, mysterious dwarf—look,” she said, pointing to the cloudless sky; “can you see a single trace of that bird that flew by just now? No; then my actions are pure as the air and leave no stain. Reassure Dumay and the Latournelles and my mother. That hand,” she held up a pretty delicate hand, the rosy fingers slightly turned, “will never be given, it will never even be kissed by what people call a lover until my father has returned.”“Why don’t you want me in church today?”“Do you venture to question me after all I have done and asked?”Butscha bowed and departed to find his master, rapturous at being taken into the service of his goddess.Half an hour later Monsieur and Madame Latournelle came to fetch Modeste, who complained of a horrible toothache.“I really have not had the courage to dress myself,” she said.“Then stay at home,” replied the worthy chaperone.“Oh, no!” said Modeste. “I would rather not. I have bundled myself up, and I don’t think it will do me any harm to go out.”And Mademoiselle Mignon marched off beside Latournelle, refusing his arm lest he question the outward trembling that betrayed her inward agitation at the thought of seeing her great poet. One look, the first—was it not about to decide her fate?############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Is there a more delightful moment in a man’s life than a first rendezvous? Do not the sensations hidden in our hearts find fresh expression? Can we again feel the nameless pleasures Ernest de La Briere knew when he sharpened his razors, laid out his finest shirt, an irreproachable collar, and his best clothes? We deify the garments associated with that supreme moment. We weave poetic fancies within us like women; when either party guesses them they take wing and fly. Are not such feelings like the bittersweet flower of wild fruits in the heart of a forest, the joy of scant sun-rays, the joy—as Canalis says in the “Maiden’s Song”—of the plant whose unclosing eyes see their image within the breast?Such emotions stirred in La Briere, showing that, like other poor fellows whose lives began in toil and care, he had never yet been loved. Arriving at Havre overnight, he had gone straight to bed to erase fatigue; after his bath he dressed in a costume carefully chosen to show him at his best. This is perhaps the right moment for a full-length portrait of him, to justify the last letter Modeste was to write.Born of a good family in Toulouse and allied by marriage to the minister who took him under his protection, Ernest had the air of good breeding from early education; the habit of managing business had given him a sedateness not yet pedantic—though pedantry often springs from premature gravity. He was of ordinary height; his face, winning by its delicacy and sweetness, was warm in flesh-tints though without color, relieved by a small moustache and an imperial à la Mazarin. Without this sign of virility he might have resembled a young woman in disguise: the shape of his face, the cut of his lips, the transparent ivory of his regular teeth were almost feminine. Add to these womanly points a manner of speech as gentle as the expression of the face, as gentle as the blue eyes with their Turkish eyelids, and you see why the minister sometimes called his young secretary Mademoiselle de La Briere. The full, clear forehead, framed by abundant black hair, was dreamy and in keeping with the generally melancholy cast of his features. The upper eyelid’s prominent arch, though beautifully cut, shadowed the glance and added a physical sadness produced by the droop of the lid. This inward doubt—called modesty—was expressed in his whole person.Perhaps his appearance can be better understood if we say the oval of his head required greater length, more space between chin and forehead; in short, the face had a compressed look. Hard work had already furrowed the brow between eyebrows that were too thick and too near together, like those of a jealous nature. Though then slight, La Briere belonged to temperaments that begin after thirty to take on unexpected flesh.To a student of French history the young man might suggest the royal, almost inconceivable figure of Louis XIII—melancholy modesty without cause; pallid beneath the crown; loving war and hunting but hating work; timid with his mistress to the extent of keeping away from her; so indifferent as to allow his friend’s head to be cut off—a figure explained only by remorse. Was he a Catholic Hamlet, or merely the victim of disease? In Ernest the undying worm was simple distrust of himself—the timidity of a man to whom no woman had ever said, “Ah, how I love thee!” and above all the spirit of self-devotion without an object. After the fall of his patron’s ministry, the poor fellow had leaped upon a rock covered with exquisite mosses named Canalis; he was still seeking a power to love, and this spaniel-like search for a master gave him outwardly the air of a king who has found his. This play of feeling and a general tone of suffering made his face more truly beautiful than he knew; he had long been annoyed to be classed among the “handsome disconsolate,” a class fallen from fashion in these times when every man seeks to blow his own trumpet.The self-distrustful Ernest rested his immediate hopes on the fashionable clothes he intended to wear. For this sacred interview where everything depended on the first impression he put on black trousers and carefully polished boots, a sulphur-colored waistcoat revealing an exquisitely fine shirt with opal buttons, a black cravat, and a small blue surtout that clung to his back and shoulders. The ribbon of the Legion of Honor was in his buttonhole. He wore well-fitting kid gloves of Florentine bronze, and carried his cane and hat in his left hand with a gesture and air worthy of the Grand Monarch, enabling him, as the precincts demanded, to show his bare head with light on his carefully arranged hair. He stood in the church porch before the service began, from which he could observe the Christians—more particularly the female Christians—who dipped their fingers in the holy water.An inward voice cried to Modeste as she entered, “It is he!” That surtout, and indeed the whole bearing of the young man, were essentially Parisian; the ribbon, the gloves, the cane, the perfume of his hair were not of Havre. So when La Briere turned to examine the tall imposing Madame Latournelle, the notary, and the bundled figure of Modeste, the poor child—though she had tutored herself for the event—received a violent blow when her eyes rested on this poetic figure, illuminated by the full light streaming through the open door. She could not be mistaken; a small white rose nearly hid the ribbon of the Legion. Would he recognize his unknown mistress muffled in an old bonnet with a double veil? Modeste was so in fear of love’s clairvoyance that she began to stoop in her walk like an old woman.“Wife,” said little Latournelle as they took their seats, “that gentleman does not belong to Havre.”“So many strangers come here,” answered his wife.“But,” said the notary, “strangers never come to look at a church like ours, which is less than two centuries old.”Ernest remained in the porch throughout the service without seeing any woman who realized his hopes. Modeste could not control the trembling of her limbs until Mass was nearly over. She was in the grasp of a joy that none but she could depict. At last she heard the footfall of a gentleman on the aisle pavement. After the service La Briere made a circuit of the church, where no one remained but the punctiliously pious, whom he proceeded to analyze. He noticed a prayer-book shaking violently in the hands of a veiled woman as he passed; since she alone hid her face his suspicions were aroused, and then confirmed by Modeste’s dress, which the lover's eye now scanned and noted. He left the church with the Latournelles and followed them at a distance to the rue Royale, where he saw them enter a house accompanied by Modeste, who customarily stayed with friends till vespers. After examining the little house, ornamented with scutcheons, he asked the owner’s name and learned it was Monsieur Latournelle, the chief notary in Havre. As Ernest lounged along the rue Royale hoping for a glimpse into the house, Modeste caught sight of him and declared herself too ill to go to vespers. Poor Ernest thus had his trouble for his pains. He dared not wander about Ingouville; moreover, he made it a point of honor to obey orders, and therefore returned to Paris, previously writing a letter which Francoise Cochet duly delivered next day with the Havre postmark.It was Monsieur and Madame Latournelle’s custom to dine at the Chalet every Sunday when they brought back Modeste after vespers. As soon as the invalid felt better they set out for Ingouville, accompanied by Butscha. Once home, the happy Modeste forgot her pretended illness and disguise, and dressed charmingly, humming as she came down to dinner,“Nought is sleeping—Heart! awaking,Lift thine incense to the skies.”Butscha shuddered slightly at sight of her, so changed did she seem. The wings of love were fastened to her shoulders; she had the air of a nymph, a Psyche; her cheeks glowed with the divine color of happiness.“Who wrote the words to which you have put that pretty music?” her mother asked.“Canalis, mamma,” she answered, flushing from throat to forehead.“Canalis!” cried the dwarf, to whom the voice and blush told what he had not yet guessed. “He, that great poet, does he write songs?”“They are only simple verses,” she said, “which I have ventured to set to German airs.”“No, no,” interrupted Madame Mignon, “the music is your own, my daughter.”Modeste, growing crimson, slipped into the garden, calling Butscha after her.“You can do me a great service,” she said. “Dumay is keeping a secret from my mother and me as to the fortune my father is bringing back. Did not Dumay send papa over five hundred thousand francs when he first went away? Yes. Well, papa is not the kind to stay four years and only double his capital. It seems he is coming back on a ship of his own, and Dumay’s share amounts to almost six hundred thousand francs.”“There is no need to question Dumay,” said Butscha. “Your father lost, as you know, about four millions when he went away, and he has doubtless recovered them. He would of course give Dumay ten per cent of his profits; the worthy man admitted the other day how much it was, and my master and I think the colonel’s fortune must now be six or seven millions—”“Oh, papa!” cried Modeste, crossing her hands on her breast and looking up, “twice you have given me life!”“Ah, mademoiselle!” said Butscha, “you love a poet. That kind of man is more or less a Narcissus. Will he know how to love you? A phrase-maker, always busy fitting words together, must be a bore. Mademoiselle, a poet is no more poetry than a seed is a flower.”“Butscha, I never saw so handsome a man.”“Beauty is a veil which often hides imperfections.”“He has the most angelic heart of heaven—”“I pray God you may be right,” said the dwarf, clasping his hands, “—and happy! That man shall have, as you have, a servant in Jean Butscha. I will not be notary; I shall give that up; I shall study the sciences.”“Why?”“Ah, mademoiselle, to train up your children, if you will deign to make me their tutor. But, oh! if you would only listen to some advice. Let me take up this matter; let me look into the life and habits of this man—find out if he is kind, bad-tempered, gentle, if he commands the respect you merit in a husband, if he can love utterly, preferring you to everything, even his own talent—”“What does that signify if I love him?”“Ah, true!” cried the dwarf.At that instant Madame Mignon was saying to her friends, “My daughter saw the man she loves this morning.”“Then it must have been that sulphur waistcoat which puzzled you so, Latournelle,” said his wife. “The young man had a pretty white rose in his buttonhole.”“Ah!” sighed the mother, “the sign of recognition.”“And he also wore the ribbon of an officer of the Legion of Honor. He is a charming young man. But we are all deceiving ourselves; Modeste never raised her veil, and her clothes were huddled on like a beggar-woman’s—”“And she said she was ill,” cried the notary; “but she has taken off her mufflings and is just as well as ever.”“It is incomprehensible!” said Dumay.“Not at all,” said the notary; “it is now as clear as day.”“My child,” said Madame Mignon to Modeste as she entered, followed by Butscha, “did you see a well-dressed young man at church this morning, with a white rose in his button-hole?”“I saw him,” said Butscha quickly, perceiving by everybody’s strained attention that Modeste might fall into a trap. “It was Grindot, the famous architect, with whom the town is in treaty for restoring the church. He has just come from Paris, and I met him this morning examining the exterior as I was on my way to Sainte-Adresse.”“Oh, an architect, was he? he puzzled me,” said Modeste, for whom Butscha had thus gained time to recover composure.Dumay looked askance at Butscha. Modeste, fully warned, recovered her impenetrable calm. Dumay’s distrust was thoroughly aroused, and he resolved to visit the mayor’s office early the next morning to ascertain if the architect had been in Havre the previous day. Butscha, on the other hand, was determined to go to Paris and learn more about Canalis.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Gobenheim came to play whist, calming the household’s excitement. Modeste waited impatiently for her mother’s bedtime to write; she only ever wrote at night. Here is the letter love dictated while the world slept.To Monsieur de Canalis,—Ah, my friend, my beloved! How false those portraits in the shop-windows are! I was foolish to delight in that lithograph. I am humbled to love one so handsome. No; Parisian women cannot have failed to adore you. I do not believe your claims of loneliness and obscure desire; you’ve been loved too well—your brow betrays it. Who am I to be called to life by you? For a moment my soul broke free from the body; the world spoke to me. The old church shone like an Italian cathedral; the organ sang angelic melodies. Havre’s pavements became a meadow; the sea spoke like an old friend. The roses seemed to smile as I left the church and I heard your name, Melchior, in the bells and clouds. I live because of you; my poet, more beautiful than cold Lord Byron. One glance of yours pierced my veil and sent warmth through me. My life will answer to your thought only. I know now music’s divine purpose—to utter love. Ah, to have genius and beauty is too much; a man should choose between them at birth.You have given me tenderness beyond dreaming, especially this month; sometimes I think it a dream. Yet you hide something—what woman can give you to me and not die? Jealousy has entered my heart. Strange revulsion—I would rather you be ugly.How foolish I was after coming home! Yellow dahlias recalled your waistcoat, white roses were my friends. I shall remember every trifle of that day—the atmosphere, a ray of sun on a pillar, the prayer your step interrupted, the incense, the priestly blessing as you passed me. The good Abbé Marcelin married us then! My happiness is beyond earth; I unmask. Come back soon; I unmask with joy.You have heard of the house of Mignon in Havre? Through misfortune I am its sole heiress. But do not look down on us; the Mignon de La Bastie arms will honor Canalis. Our motto is “Una fides, unus Dominus!”—one true faith and one master. My name is Modeste—you are not deceived by the signature “O. d’Este M.” Our fortune will be what made you so virtuous. Let it free our happiness; when fancy takes us, “Come, let us go in a comfortable carriage” without a thought of money. Thus Modeste Mignon can serve you, and her gold will be nobly used.As to your servant—you saw her once at her window. The Modeste of long ago is dead; this Modeste has received from you the life of life. Love, sanctioned by my father’s return rich, has raised me. I tremble lest I not deserve my fate. The king has come in his glory; my lord has now a subject who apologizes for liberties taken. My poet! I will be thy Mignon—happier than Goethe’s, for you will keep me in your heart. A nightingale in the park answers for you. If his note does not lie to me, tell me so. My father will pass through Paris; go to him, but do not tell him how I love you—let that be between us and God. I will tell my mother; she will justify me. You have the daughter’s confession; obtain my father’s consent. P.S.—Do not come to Havre without my father’s consent.Dumay caught Modeste up late and questioned her; she claimed to be writing to her father because he was leaving in the morning. That night she sealed two letters—one to Canalis, one to her father—but she mistakenly reversed the envelopes. François Cochet, terrified when he found an envelope addressed to Canalis among those mailed from Havre, brought a letter to Modeste and took away the one she had written to Canalis. The letter he brought was from Ernest de La Brière, who, in his embarrassment, confessed that the veiled, disguised woman he had seen might be Modeste. The letter revealed the secretary’s yearning; he wished to cast off his disguise and be honest.Modeste was unsettled and demanded an immediate explanation from Ernest.Meanwhile other events in Havre complicated matters. Dumay discovered no architect had been in town, and Butscha had gone to Paris, claiming his father had been found there. Dumay suspected a conspiracy and rushed to Latournelle and then to the Chalet. Latournelle had seen a shipping notice that Captain Mignon’s ship—the Bettina Mignon—had arrived from Marseilles, so the colonel must be in Paris. Dumay resolved to go after Butscha to learn what he knew about Modeste.Modeste, thinking Butscha had been sent to investigate her poet’s morals, fainted with alarm. She had given Dumay the letter she thought addressed to Canalis; Dumay, seeing the address “Monsieur le Baron de Canalis, rue de Paradis-Poissoniere,” cried out in surprise. Madame Mignon told them that the stanzas Modeste had set to music were by Canalis and that his portrait hung upstairs. Modeste snatched back the letter and vowed that if Dumay interfered with Canalis she would leave the house forever. Dumay left for Paris in haste, Monte to fetch Canalis or to pursue Butscha; the parents and friends rallied round the distraught Modeste.Modeste confessed everything to her mother—then sank between hope and shame. Her mother listened with a mixture of rebuke and understanding. Modeste read her letters aloud and wept. She declared her love pure, divine, and eternal, but then feared being exposed to vulgar scrutiny and the tearing of her “diaphanous wings.” She fell ill, chilled and feverish; her mother and friends tended her as Dumay raced to Paris. The narrative shifts to the city.Ernest de La Brière, modest and sensitive, was elated to be believed handsome by Modeste. But his joy turned to dread when he realized she thought him to be Canalis and that she had substantial fortune. Conscience made him determine to confess everything and to find Modeste’s father in Paris, so he sought the banker Mongenod to trace the colonel.Dumay, a Breton soldier by nature, disdained poets as idle, shabby fools—until he arrived at Canalis’s house and found evidence of rank: servants, a groom washing a carriage, a valet like a banker. Canalis was not a garret poet but president of the Court of Claims attached to the foreign ministry. Surrounded by tokens of patronage and honor, Dumay was taken aback and allowed Canalis to speak first.Canalis received Dumay with calm superiority. Dumay accused him of seducing the young and rich Modeste of Havre and demanded his intentions. Canalis feigned ignorance and declaimed about preserving the letters of young admirers in a precious casket, treating their devotion with veneration rather than suspicion. He allowed Dumay to search the casket to prove his point. Dumay, outraged but silenced by the poet’s manner, begged for help in investigating. Canalis promised to assist as opportunity allowed. Dumay left bewildered; Butscha, meanwhile, had cleverly been investigating Canalis in his own way.La Brière entered Canalis’s study and confessed to the poet that Modeste loved him, believing him to be Canalis, and that Modeste’s father, Colonel Mignon, supposedly had great wealth. He revealed he was the true correspondent and that his conscience impelled him to come clean. Canalis, who had in truth forgotten Modeste’s letter, masked disappointment with gentility and magnanimously offered to further Ernest’s hopes—yet inwardly considered the girl’s fortune a dazzling prize. La Brière, idealistic, swore himself to Modeste.The Comte de La Bastie—Colonel Mignon—sank into grief upon hearing of Bettina's death, his wife's infirmity, and Modeste’s secret. He wept inwardly, then received La Brière. Ernest, humble and honest, declared his love and explained the deception. When Mignon read the letters, he disclosed the principal error: Modeste would not have six million francs as rumored; at most two hundred thousand and questionable prospects. La Brière, relieved, felt his path to happiness cleared. Mignon, impressed by La Brière’s candor, set a test: both La Brière and Canalis should come to Havre for the last two weeks of October; Modeste would compare the “real” man and the “poet,” the illusion and the substance. Ernest must bring Canalis himself and refrain from revealing the earlier deception. Mignon would go to Havre and expected both to follow. La Brière accepted, broken but honorable.Canalis, having reflected, saw the possibility of rank and riches—the match might make him a peer. He was furious at missing an opportunity and at La Brière’s inadvertent advantage. But by the time La Brière returned the poet resolved to help him—and privately to compete. Both men, despite differing motives—La Brière by duty, Canalis by ambition—prepared to present themselves in Normandy. Mignon and Dumay set off to Havre; the colonel planned a lesson in character for his daughter.On arrival at the Chalet the household greeted the count’s return with joy. The colonel, tender with his wife, watched Modeste with delight; Modeste, pale but composed, lavished kisses on her father. Dumay’s silence and the colonel’s resolve unnerved her. The colonel told Modeste to rise early next day for a walk by the shore—there would be talk about her poems. On the walk he told her he knew all and more than she did. He reproached her for writing to a stranger without consulting family and for treating marriage as a poem rather than a matter of prudence. Modeste defended herself: she had been drawn by genius, not by a stranger. The colonel argued family oversight existed to spare girls from the dangers of romantic illusion. He insisted Modeste must choose between La Brière—the plain, honorable secretary—and Canalis—the famed poet she admired. To his surprise, Modeste swore to obey him in the matter of marriage. He warned her that the man she loved might be a deceiver; she insisted she loved an honest, candid soul. The colonel insisted the family must guide her and set a plan: she must see both candidates in the country among ordinary pursuits and choose freely. Modeste promised to obey.Disenchanted, Modeste fell from poetic heights into the cold plain of reality. Her imagination—the source of her idealizing love—found in daily life only humiliation; she became numb and morose. Once exultant, she now tasted savage pleasure in her suffering and withdrew into morbid melancholy. Yet the shock clarified her mind: her head cleared, malice and discernment grew. She developed a resolve to humiliate La Brière—revenge on the false Canalis—though her mother counseled courtesy.A third suitor soon arrived. Latournelle declared that the Duc d’Herouville, a titled but impecunious grand equerry and peer of France, had offered himself after seeing Modeste at the Vilquins. Rumor already exaggerated the Mignon fortune, and the duke’s family pressed the match. The duke, thin, pale, and timid but with courtly prestige, was as much a caricature of faded nobility as many such provincial peers; yet titles still had weight. The house gossip noted that Modeste, now thought an heiress, had revived attention and competition.Gobenheim and others speculated about Colonel Mignon’s wealth. The colonel, refusing to part with his chosen servants, installed them and began to live in a style that stirred local rumor. In time the arrival of Canalis and La Brière at a villa at Sanvic was announced; they would come to the Chalet the following evening. Canalis, anxious to make a strong impression, engaged Germain, his valet, to arrange a grand household, and in Havre Germain’s air and insistence on treating La Brière as a secretary suggested the social gap that would work against Ernest. Canalis sedulously cultivated effects—carriage painted with arms, servants, and airs; he planned to appear indifferent and disdainful to pique Modeste. La Brière, sincere and despondent, saw himself as prose against Canalis’s poetry. Nevertheless, both men planned to spend an evening at the Chalet.Canalis’s entrance at the Chalet was studied: he adopted pompous postures, declamatory diction, and the theatrical manner he had perfected under the Duchesse de Chaulieu’s flattery. His affectations, harmless in Paris, were conspicuous in provincial Havre. He lavished public homage on war and nation and on the local veterans, flattering Dumay and praising Napoleon’s soldiers with lofty rhetoric to win the provincials’ respect. He charmed the women and Modeste with talk of poetry and politics, arguing poets could become statesmen. Modeste, determined to worship him, sat as at a theater; La Brière, modest, felt eclipsed but listened to her. Canalis complimented La Brière exaggeratedly, calling him “Pylades” and praising his talents; poor Ernest, flattered, took it as a balm.The visit ended with an invitation to dinner. Modeste and the mothers debated whether Canalis was rich, and whether modish fame or real fortune mattered. The colonel suggested Modeste might appear in the splendor of an heiress if she ordered her Paris wardrobe. Many of the household, including Gobenheim, quipped about marriages and disguises. They expected more visitors soon.At night on the seashore, La Brière brooded on his hopeless love. Butscha, the hunchbacked clerk and unofficial guardian of Modeste’s reputation, revealed himself and spoke with surprising eloquence about devotion and study of women. He claimed to be one of three pledged to serve Modeste and to guard her happiness. He knew the local intrigues, the duchess’s vindictiveness, and the valet Germain’s ties; Butscha promised to watch Canalis and to see whether the great poet could love truly. La Brière, heartened by Butscha’s fidelity, treated him as a comrade. Butscha declared he studied love as a science and would soon reveal Canalis’s true character, and he intended to ensure Modeste’s happiness. La Brière resolved to make a friend of the dwarf.The next morning the two Parisian guests rode and explored the country; both felt the strain between devotion and ambition. Canalis smugly assumed an advantage in society; Ernest resolved on stoic endurance, to love Modeste quietly and honorably even if she chose another. Germain bought flamboyant horses and the ducal and Canalis households prepared to stake their reputations.Desplein, the famous surgeon, arrived, examining Madame Mignon’s cataract; he promised the mother she might regain sight. His brisk competence, studious manner, and a moment of gallantry toward Modeste impressed her—an encounter that revealed to her the difference between true genius and the pose of Canalis. She served his simple breakfast; he promised to help her mother see again, and his gravity and skill charmed her. The household hoped meanwhile for the forthcoming theatre of suitors, as Modeste prepared to see three men—the poet, the honest secretary, and the titled duke—determine in practice whether she would choose fame, substance, or rank.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################This visit of the great surgeon was the event of the day and left a luminous trace in Modeste’s soul. The young enthusiast admired the man whose life belonged to others and in whom long study of pain had destroyed manifestations of egoism. That evening, with Gobenheim, the Latournelles, Butscha, Canalis, Ernest, and the Duc d’Herouville in the salon, they all congratulated the Mignon family on the hopes Desplein encouraged. Conversation, with Modeste again predominant, turned on the man whose genius, appreciable chiefly by the faculty and men of science, drew praise and a characteristic remark from Gobenheim—“He makes a mint of money.”“They say he is very grasping,” added Canalis.Modeste’s praise of Desplein annoyed the poet. Vanity acts like a woman; both feel defrauded when love or praise is given to another. Canalis challenged the judgment of genius.“Do you think, monsieur,” said Modeste, smiling, “that we should judge genius by ordinary standards?”“Perhaps we ought first to define the man of genius,” replied Canalis. “One condition is invention—form, system, force. Napoleon invented war-making; Scott, Linnaeus, Cuvier renewed arts and sciences. But Desplein merely applies known laws skillfully; he is an executive genius, like Moscheles or Paganini—performers, not creators. To one belongs the immortal crown, to the other innumerable five-franc pieces.”Ernest de La Brière opposed this exclusiveness. “Genius must be estimated by utility,” he said. “Parmentier popularized the potato, Jacquard the loom: they change a state. In that sense Desplein is a man of genius; he follows a generation of sufferers whose pains he relieves.”Modeste opposed Ernest’s view. “If that be so, then the man who could discover a way to mow wheat without injuring the straw, by a machine that could do the work of ten men, would be a man of genius.”“Yes, my daughter,” said Madame Mignon; “and the poor would bless him—he that is blessed by the poor is blessed of God.”“That is putting utility above art,” Modeste objected.“Without utility what would become of art?” said Charles Mignon. “What would support it? You must be lodged and paid.”“Oh! my dear papa, such opinions are flat and antediluvian!” Modeste retorted insolently. Her impertinence astonished Madame Mignon, Madame Latournelle, and Dumay. Butscha looked to Monsieur Mignon for indignation.“A little more, young lady, and you will be wanting in respect for your father,” said the colonel, smiling.“I am your only child,” she answered saucily.“Child, indeed,” remarked the notary, significantly.“Monsieur,” Modeste turned upon him, “my father is delighted to have me for his governess; he gave me life and I give him knowledge; he will soon owe me something.”“There seems occasion for it,” said Madame Mignon.Canalis rose, standing before the fireplace in one of his grandest attitudes, and weighed in. “God has given food and clothing to man, but not art. We need the life of the soul as much as that of the body—hence, two utilities. True, an epic does not feed like a charity kitchen. But industrial perfections will not breathe life into a people nor tell futurity of a civilization. Works of genius are the summum of civilization; they presuppose utility. Nations cherish survival beyond death; the survival of a people is the work of men of genius. France, though excelled by England in commerce, leads the world in art and taste. We shall reign longer by books than by swords. In La Brière’s system, all that is glorious and lovely would be suppressed. All useful things are ugly; a kitchen is indispensable, but you live in the salon and adorn it. There is nothing beautiful but what seems useless.”“Rubbish! yes, that may be, but my rubbish is dear to me,” laughed the Duc d’Herouville.Butscha attacked Canalis. “Does art exist at all? Isn’t it a splendid lie? I don’t want a landscape painting when I can see one done by God. Happiness and virtue exist above art and genius.”“Bravo, Butscha!” cried Madame Latournelle.Canalis’s long rhetoric lost the country-folk. Modeste’s indifference toward La Brière and disrespect for her father depressed the young man. The duke took up Butscha’s argument, declaring Saint Theresa’s ecstasies superior to Lord Byron. Modeste countered, “Hers was purely personal poetry, whereas the genius of Byron and Molière benefits the world.”Charles Mignon teased Modeste. The exchange widened the gulf between Modeste and Ernest. La Brière’s quiet voice contrasted with the baron’s peremptory tone. Canalis asserted that every work of art implies a social utility: books employ paper-makers, printers, booksellers; a symphony or an opera needs hands and machinery. Works of genius have an expensive basis and are useful to labor. He spoke with fine metaphor and inward complacency, then returned unknowingly to La Brière’s position.“He will make an admirable constitutional minister,” said the little duke slyly.“Oh!” replied Canalis with a grand gesture, “what is the use of these discussions? All things are true and false according to surroundings.”“He is a poet,” whispered Gobenheim.Canalis, ten leagues above his audience, took the coolness as provincial ignorance, content that Modeste understood him. He spoke of the Duchesse de Chaulieu with rapture, declaring his gratitude and affection. Modeste, affected, said, “Who can think of a poet without a muse!”“He would be without a heart,” replied Canalis.Dumay reminded Canalis of having denied feeling to which Canalis ironically replied with a boast of loving as well as La Brière and being able to give his love literary form. His triumph led him to declaim; Modeste, swept into his labyrinth of fancy, thought, “He loves me!” Butscha noted Canalis’s declamations and inattention and later commented cruelly: Canalis’s habit of not listening often offended and wounded others. Not to listen is disrespect; Canalis often forgot to answer a speech, passing abruptly to what preoccupied him. That impertinence breeds hatred.Modeste begged Canalis to read some poems. He took the volume and cooed an imitation of Moore’s “Loves of the Angels” titled “Vitalis,” greeted by yawns. Gobenheim’s remark about whist raised a laugh; Canalis flung the volume impatiently on a table. The incident showed the peril of a drawing-room hero stepping out of his sphere.The whist game began with Canalis, the duke, Gobenheim, and Latournelle as partners. Modeste sat near the poet, much to Ernest’s disappointment. La Brière watched Modeste and the progress of Canalis’s fascination. He lacked the gift of seduction; there is always a comic element in the actor-poet’s art of feigning sentiment to win. Ernest imitated the duke, becoming gloomy and watchful, prey to concentrated jealousy; he could not obtain a glance from Modeste. He left the room with Butscha.“It is all over!” he said. “She is caught by him; I am more disagreeable to her, and she is right. Canalis is charming; there’s intellect in his silence, passion in his eyes, poetry in his rhodomontades.”“Is he an honest man?” asked Butscha.“Oh, yes,” La Brière replied. “He is loyal and chivalrous, capable of getting rid, under Modeste’s influence, of those affectations Madame de Chaulieu taught him.”“You are a fine fellow,” said the hunchback; “but will he love her?”“I don’t know,” answered La Brière.He asked whether Modeste had said anything about him. Butscha repeated Modeste’s speech about disguises. La Brière was beside himself, flung onto a bench, and wept. He described how he had tried to undeceive Modeste before she took off the mask and how her recent letter had been flattering to the individual he wished. Butscha urged him to show himself as he was and not let his rival practice his exercises. La Brière lamented his inability to speak when she was present, the strange physical sensations her looks caused.At this moment Modeste, followed by Canalis who had lost the rubber, came out with her father and Madame Dumay to breathe the starry night. La Brière left. The next day Modeste, favoring Canalis, walked alone with him across the gravel. The d’Herouville ladies interrupted, turning conversation to court appointments and the distinction of rank: “To have a duke for a son,” said one, “is a fortune.” Canalis, displeased, asked why the duke had had little success despite title; the ladies shot him venomous glances. Ten days later the tide at the Chalet turned against Canalis. The poet’s prodigality of wit tired those preferring the solid; forced to behave like an ordinary man, his faults were exposed. La Brière, by contrast, was sober, discreet, and gaining ground: modesty, simplicity, and utility won favor.Canalis, impulsive and ambitious, displayed impatience, depression, and capricious moods noted by Madame Latournelle and others. The attention shifted to La Brière. For two days the poet worked to fascinate Modeste alone, weaving passionate language. The d’Herouville ladies slandered him. At dinner the duke proposed arranging a hunt in a crown forest; glances among the d’Herouville group said, “The heiress is ours!” The poet, sensing danger, resolved to get a pledge then and there. Modeste, half-frightened, hinted at the calumnies to him and begged him to be silent; he promised.“These stabs of the tongue are fair in the great world,” he said. “They shock you; I laugh at them; I am pleased. These ladies must feel the duke’s interests are in peril.”Canalis entered his defence with warmth and passion and declared himself plainly with vows and protestations. Modeste listened.“Monsieur,” she said, “freedom granted me by my parents has allowed me to listen to you; but you must address them.”“But,” cried Canalis, “tell me that if I obtain their consent, you will ask nothing better than to obey them.”“I know beforehand,” she replied, “my father has fancies that may wound your family’s pride. He wishes his name borne by grandsons.”“Ah, dear Modeste, what sacrifices would I not make to commit my life to an angel like you.”“You will permit me not to decide in a moment the fate of my whole life,” she said, turning to rejoin the d’Herouville ladies.Butscha waylaid Modeste at the portico. “Mademoiselle, I do hope you don’t call him Melchior.”“I’m very near it, my Black Dwarf,” she said sweetly.“Good God!” exclaimed Butscha.“You?” she said, “would your La Brière accept me without a fortune?”“Ask your father,” replied Butscha. “I am ready to give not only my life but my honor for you. Has that sublime Canalis made the disinterested offer you flung as a reproach at poor Ernest?”“Yes.”“Do you believe it?”“That question undervalues the strength of my self-love.”“You are laughing, mademoiselle; then there’s no danger: I hope you are only making a fool of him.”“Pray what would you think of me, Monsieur Butscha, if I allowed myself to make fun of those who do me the honor to wish to marry me? Even if a girl affects to despise the most despicable attentions, she is flattered by them.”“Then I flatter you?” he said, illuminated.“You give me the most precious friendship—a feeling as disinterested as a mother’s for a child. Compare yourself to no one; even my father is obliged to be devoted to me.” She paused. “I cannot say I love you, in the sense men give the word, but what I give you is eternal.”“Then,” said Butscha, bowing, “suffer me to watch over you as a dragon guards a treasure. The poet covered you with lace-work and tinsel of his promises. If, as soon as he finds your small fortune, he changes, will you still marry him? shall you still esteem him?”“He would be another Francisque Althor,” she said bitterly.“Make him play that pretty little comedy, and—”“That I will! he shall play it through and through within three days—on Wednesday. Until then amuse yourself with the little tunes of the lyre so the discords may come out more distinctly.”Modeste returned to the salon where La Brière, sitting by the window, rose respectfully as she approached. He followed her with a furtive look; she sat by Canalis, who would have looked at her more boldly. La Brière and Butscha paced the terrace.“It is nine o’clock,” cried Ernest. “I shall start for Paris; I can get there by tomorrow morning. From you she will accept anything; let me give her a riding-whip in your name.”“Ah, you are happy,” said Butscha ruefully; “you have money!”He rode to Paris, secured a place on a mail-coach back, and bought a gold fox-head whip-handle with a ruby—seven thousand francs—engraved with La Bastie’s arms and placed in a morocco case stamped with interlaced M.’s. He returned Wednesday morning to breakfast with Canalis. Butscha met him and instructed Germain the valet to put the box on Modeste’s dressing-table.Canalis and Germain spoke apart. Germain reported gossip from the quays and fishermen indicating the brig connected with Monsieur Mignon had been sold to another’s account, and the cargo was worth only three or four hundred thousand francs at most. Canalis served champagne and took the gossip lightly. Butscha, hidden prompter, requested the secretary to say nothing about his trip and not to interfere. He had exploited an unfavorable feeling in Havre about Monsieur Mignon’s fortune by urging fishermen to speak ill; they obliged. Butscha was a lively slanderer, and over wine he poured forth scandalous gossip. He declared Modeste might have two or three hundred thousand in ready money and that Dumay’s property might add to her fortune—perhaps six hundred thousand francs when the old soldiers died.This coarse honesty sobered Canalis. To Butscha such a sum seemed colossal. La Brière, bound by a secrecy oath to the colonel, had not meant to disclose the earlier rumor to Canalis. Pressed, Ernest revealed that Colonel Mignon had told him in Paris he did not possess the colossal fortune spoken of; he intended to give two hundred thousand francs to his daughter in ready money, entailing his estates for descendants to retain the title. Canalis, who had expected to marry with a fortune to equal his own, was shaken.“Will you promise me on your word of honor to forget what I tell you and to behave to me as though this confidence had never been made?” Ernest asked.“I’ll swear that,” Canalis answered.Butscha dozed; Canalis brooded: “Fool! then take her,” he said, “and break your oath.” But he feared losing both love and office. Yet Butscha urged directness: go speak frankly to Colonel Mignon.Canalis rose to dress for the excursion. Butscha, pursued by servants’ laughter, took a kiosk and wallowed in triumph at having fooled a great man.Modeste appeared in a bottle-green habit, showing the gifted whip. “Did it come from you, Monsieur le duc?” she said, holding the handle toward him. “There was a card saying ‘Guess if you can.’ Françoise and Dumay credit Butscha; my father sent his with a turquoise top.”“I would give ten years of my old age,” said the duke, “to offer you that jewel.”“Ah, here comes the audacious giver!” cried Modeste as Canalis rode up. “La Brière rode at full gallop from Havre?”“Does your secretary take such liberties?” she asked, turning pale, and threw the whip to Françoise with scorn. “Give me your whip, papa.”“Poor Ernest, half-dead with fatigue!” said Canalis, overtaking her. “You are pitiless, mademoiselle. The poor fellow said, ‘I have but this one chance to remain in her memory.’”“And should you think well of a woman who could take presents from half the parish?” she returned.She received no answer; Canalis’s inattention attributed to horse’s feet. His admiration seemed too intense to be natural. At a cross-road overlooking the Seine, Canalis let Modeste and the duke pass, then joined the colonel.“Monsieur le comte,” he said, “you are an open-hearted soldier. I am twenty-nine, without landed estates, full of ambition. Mademoiselle Modeste pleases me. The question of money is of the utmost importance. I have about thirty thousand francs a year; if my wife brought me as much more, I should be about equal. Shall you give Mademoiselle a million?”“We have not reached that point,” said the colonel cautiously.“Then suppose we go no further. Give me your word of honor to say nothing, even to Mademoiselle Modeste, because my circumstances may change and I may ask for her without dowry.”“I promise,” said the colonel. “People exaggerate fortunes. I await accounts of my agents; the sale of my merchandise and ship is not yet concluded. I did say to La Brière in Paris I would guarantee two hundred thousand francs in ready money. I wish to entail my estates and enable grandchildren to inherit my arms and title.”Canalis barely listened. They reached a table-land with a view of the Seine valley. “Butscha was right; God is the greatest landscape painter,” he said.Modeste remarked on fictitious wants and vanities; Canalis called it a bucolic written on tablets of gold. The colonel invited the duke and Canalis to dine in riding-dress. That evening Modeste gave La Brière’s whip back: “Please ask Monsieur de La Brière to keep it until you know whether we are rich enough to buy it back.” The colonel kissed her forehead approvingly.Canalis, alarmed at the indecency of his earlier conduct and its consequences, tried to excuse himself to Modeste, pleading jealousy and the delicacy of a poet’s sensibility. “I attach more value to words and looks than ordinary men,” he said, “and demand infantile security.” Modeste countered, calling his demand excessive and resenting his mention of the duchess. “I admire you, my dear poet,” she said lightly, but rebuked his presumption. Their intercourse cooled; Canalis left early, and the field opened to the duke. Butscha, walking with Madame Latournelle, whispered in Modeste’s ear, “Was I right?”“Alas, yes,” she answered. “Anger got the better of me.”“Well, so much the better. When you are both so angry you can’t speak, I will make him desperately in love until you are deceived yourself.”“Come, Butscha; he is a great poet; he is a gentleman; he is a man of intellect.”“Your father’s millions are more to him than all that,” answered Butscha.“Eight millions!” cried Modeste.“My master, who sold his practice, is going to Provence to attend to buying lands your father’s agent suggested. The purchase is four millions; your father has agreed. You are to have a dot of two millions and another million for a Paris establishment. Count up.”“If it hadn’t been for that comedian Canalis you would have kept his whip thinking it came from me,” said Butscha, advocating La Brière.Modesty’s reaction sealed matters. Canalis, sleepless, composed a letter to the Duchesse de Chaulieu explaining his absence and his interference on La Brière’s behalf. He described Mademoiselle Modeste as “a rather pale, insignificant, thread-papery little thing” who liked literature and had coquetted with the duchess’s Melchior. He asked Eleonore to verify facts through banker Mongenod about Monsieur Mignon’s fortune and to help La Brière by transmission of a father-in-law’s title. His confidential reminiscences were too intimate.Meanwhile Butscha, under the name Jean Jacmin, had received and replied to a letter from his fictitious cousin Philoxene, and the duchess, wounded by Melchior’s silence, dictated and read replies and then summoned Mongenod to inquire about Monsieur Mignon’s fortune. Finding evidence that Modeste had wealth, Eleonore—who had felt betrayed and angry—quickly changed to generosity after reading Canalis’s letter: if he had not been faithless, she would give him the girl and her millions—provided Mademoiselle Mignon was as ugly as Canalis had written.The duke arrived. The duchess, eager, wrote to Diane de Maufrigneuse and to Mademoiselle d’Herouville to arrange an invitation and hinted to secure the hunt. Eleonore sent Canalis a sharp answer:My dear poet,—Mademoiselle de La Bastie is very beautiful; Mongenod has proved she has millions. I thought of marrying you to her; I am displeased at your want of confidence. You are a child. The duke thinks you less than a gentleman, reflecting on your mother’s honor. Now I intend to see things for myself and will manage to have you invited to Rosembray.Canalis flung the letter at Ernest across breakfast. “That’s the two thousandth love-letter I have had from that woman,” he cried. “Marry, try your luck! The worst marriage is better than this halter.”Caught between two lies, Canalis was at a loss. “Play by rule, and you lose!” he muttered, then resolved that if he must retire, his ten years of fidelity to Eleonore would ensure some good match. The hunt would unite passions and prove a truce among adversaries. Canalis wished to appear courteous and simply charming; he talked finances with Gobenheim and war with the colonel, biasing them in favor of La Brière. The d’Herouville ladies continued their intrigues to secure Modeste’s dot. Invitations from the Duc de Verneuil and the Master of the Hunt extended to the Basties to stay at Rosembray for a grand hunt.La Brière, full of dark presentiments, craved Modeste’s presence. The duke arrived to arrange Modeste’s departure; the colonel invited the suitors to breakfast the morning of the start. Canalis planned to reconquer Modeste and throw over the duchess, La Brière had resolved to bid eternal farewell, and each suitor prepared a last plea. The colonel pressed Modeste for decision.“Our position with the d’Herouville family will be intolerable at Rosembray,” he said. “Do you mean to be a duchess?”“No, father,” she answered.“Then do you love Canalis?”“No, papa, a thousand times no!” she exclaimed; the colonel rejoiced.“Ah, I chose in Paris the man of fixed principles and sound moralities,” he said slyly.“I was not even thinking of him, papa. Please leave me liberty to refuse the duke myself; after a glimpse of court life at Rosembray I will tell you my secret.”“Monsieur de La Brière,” cried the colonel, “I hope you are going to this hunt?”“No, colonel, I have come to take leave; I return to Paris—”“You have no curiosity,” said Modeste.“If that is all, you must stay to please me; I wish it,” said the colonel, moving away to meet Canalis and leaving daughter and suitor together.“Mademoiselle,” said La Brière, raising his eyes, “let me carry away with me your forgiveness. My life can never be happy; it will be full of remorse—”“Before we part forever,” said Modeste interrupting, “I wish to ask you one thing; though you once disguised yourself, I think you cannot be so base as to deceive me now. Will you be frank?”La Brière related the scene in the poet’s study and the challenge that followed. “You have said enough,” said Modeste, restraining emotion. “If you have not my heart, monsieur, you have at least my esteem.” These words gave him a violent shock; staggering, he leaned against a tree. Modeste left him but returned, taking his hand. “What is the matter?” she asked.“Forgive me—I thought you despised me.”“But,” she answered, distant, “I did not say that I loved you.”She left him. Yet the esteem she’d given filled him with joy. Modeste saw in La Brière’s sensitivity the possibility of a love that might fulfill the ideal every woman hopes for.Canalis approached Modeste later. “In spite of the little value you attach to my sentiments, my honor is concerned in effacing a stain under which I have suffered,” he said, and let her read lines from the duchess’s letter that proved Mongenod’s inquiry and intention to marry Modeste to La Brière—he tore that passage from the letter and gave the fragment to Modeste. “I cannot let you read the rest,” he added; “but a girl who could accuse me of ignoble motives is capable of suspecting collusion. Ah, Modeste,” he added with tears, “your poet has as much poetry in his heart as in his mind. Suspend judgment until you have seen the duchess.”“Oh, dear!” she said; “it seems they are all angels—and not marriageable; the duke is the only one that belongs to humanity.”Butscha appeared with a parcel. He had been busy with Spanish bits and had shown a piece to the colonel, who thanked him generously. The dwarf departed like a man who’d heard of a rich uncle.“My dear father,” Modeste said to the colonel in the salon, “I would like that beautiful whip—suppose you ask Monsieur de La Brière to exchange it for your picture by Van Ostade.” She looked furtively at Ernest as the colonel made the proposition; she thought, “He will be at the hunt.”Curiously, Modeste’s three lovers each went to Rosembray with hearts full of hope. Rosembray—bought by the Duc de Verneuil—was a chateau of the grand order, approached by an avenue of elms, presenting a courtyard with orange trees and a façade with fluted pilasters and a pediment carved by Coysevox. Its park extended to immense ponds, gardens in the French style, and thirty acres of woodland contiguous with other forests, royal and state—a noble landscape fitting the drama to come.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Modeste’s arrival at Rosembray created a stir. A carriage in royal livery drew up, escorted by the grand equerry, the colonel, Canalis, and La Briere on horseback, preceded by an outrider and followed by six servants — including Negroes and a mulatto — and the colonel’s britzka for two waiting-women and luggage. The four-horse carriage and the elegant postilions, inspected by the grand equerry, made Modeste feel she had entered a little Versailles. Dazzled and uneasy about meeting famed duchesses, she regretted having wished for a hunt.She descended to find a calm, plump old man in a blond wig, his face wearing a fatherly, almost monastic cheerfulness — the Duc de Verneuil, master of Rosembray. The duchess, pious, spare and erect and the mother of four, had an abbess’s grace and resembled Madame Latournelle. Mademoiselle d’Herouville greeted the duchess warmly. “Let me present our little angel, Mademoiselle de La Bastie,” she said.“We have heard so much of you,” the duchess replied. “We were eager to receive you.”“We regret the time lost,” the duke added. The grand equerry introduced the colonel as Monsieur le Comte de La Bastie. “You possess more than one treasure,” Monsieur de Verneuil observed to Modeste.The duchess led Modeste into a vast salon where a dozen women clustered about the fireplace. The men remained with the duke on the terrace except Canalis, who moved toward the superb Eleonore. The Duchesse de Chaulieu sat at an embroidery frame, teaching Mademoiselle de Verneuil how to shade a flower.Modeste felt the sting of Madame de Chaulieu’s cold, haughty stare. For an instant she saw nothing but that woman and perceived the cruelty women can show one another. Her involuntary admiration, which might have disarmed most, only inflamed Eleonore. The poet’s miscalculation had roused the proud lady’s anger: radiant of face yet menacingly reserved — a courtesy masking steel. Canalis’s attempts to soften things slid off like water; the duchess’s gracious manner concealed a severe, icy wrath. Modeste’s beauty and fine dress, rivalling Diane de Maugrigneuse’s, set Eleonore brooding.All the women had rushed to the window to see the new wonder descend from the royal carriage, attended by her three suitors. “Do not let us seem so curious,” Madame de Chaulieu had said, stung by Diane’s cry — “She is divine! where in the world does she come from?” — and they returned to their seats, composed though Eleonore’s heart seethed.Mademoiselle d’Herouville murmured to the Duchesse de Verneuil, “Eleonore receives her Melchior very ungraciously.”“The Duchesse de Maugrigneuse thinks there is a coolness between them,” Laure de Verneuil replied.Modeste asked why. The pious duchess signaled her daughter to be silent and explained that the great poet, Canalis, had left Madame de Chaulieu without a letter for more than two weeks after going to Havre on a supposed health trip. During that time the duchess had been trying to secure him the command of the Legion of Honor and a post at Baden. “Oh, that was shameful in Canalis; he owes everything to her,” Mademoiselle d’Herouville cried.“Why did not Madame de Chaulieu come to Havre?” Modeste asked innocently.“She would let herself be cut in pieces without a word,” the Duchesse de Verneuil answered. “Look at her — regal; she would smile like Mary Stuart after her head was cut off.” Prompted by a nudge, the duchess added that Diane said Madame de Chaulieu had made a cutting reply to Canalis’s first letter a few days ago.Modeste blushed for the man before her; she longed not to crush him but to revenge herself with a malicious act sharper than a dagger. She looked haughtily at the Duchesse de Chaulieu and then, daring the tense air, said, “Monsieur Melchior!”All the women sniffed and glanced between the duchess and the young girl ill-bred enough to disturb a lovers’ meeting. Diane de Maugrigneuse’s nod seemed to support Modeste. The women smiled at each other; they were enraged with a woman of fifty-six still handsome enough to claim the dues of youth. Melchior looked at Modeste with feverish impatience and made the gesture of a master to a valet, while the duchess lowered her head like a lioness disturbed—her eyes sending red flames toward the poet, each word a triple insult.“Monsieur Melchior!” Modeste repeated, demanding to be heard.“What, mademoiselle?” he snapped.He rose, remaining halfway between the embroidery frame and the fireplace where Modeste sat with the Duchesse de Verneuil. Bitter reflections entered his ambitious mind as he caught Eleonore’s glance. Obeying Modeste would end all with his protectress; disobeying would avow his slavery and lose the gains of his recent scheming. Modeste’s beauty and money stood against Eleonore’s rights and influence; the choice was as perilous as a matador’s. Canalis, palpitating, seemed near collapse.“Mademoiselle d’Herouville hurried me from the carriage, and I left behind me,” Modeste said to Canalis, “my handkerchief—”Canalis shrugged.“And,” she continued, ignoring him, “I had tied into one corner of it the key of a desk which contains the fragment of an important letter; have the kindness, Monsieur Melchior, to get it for me.”Caught between angel and tiger, and having turned livid, Canalis nearly complied — the tiger seemed less dangerous — when La Briere appeared at the door, to his relief like the archangel Gabriel.“Ernest, here, Mademoiselle de La Bastie wants you,” the poet said hurriedly, returning to his chair.Ernest rushed to Modeste without ceremony, saw only her, took his commission joyfully, and darted from the room with the secret approbation of every woman present.“If you speak to her, if you ever look at her, all is over between us,” the duchess whispered to the poet, not content with how Ernest’s arrival had ended the scene. “And remember, if I am not present, I leave eyes behind that will watch you.”Then she rose and walked toward Diane de Maugrigneuse’s group, stepping daintily like a deer. Though a little too stout for her height, Eleonore retained an exquisite delicacy and a nervous vigor that vitalized her flesh, giving her majestic ease. Her undyed abundant hair was simply plaited; she bared her white throat and shoulders and displayed the famed hand and arm with pardonable pride. Modeste and the other antagonists felt forced to admit, “She eclipses us.” Eleonore was one of those rare grandes dames whose carriage, bearing, and arts are like poetry; her superiority defied dissection.Madame de Chaulieu bowed to Helene and her aunt, then, in a pure even tone without emotion, said to Diane, “Is it not time to dress, duchess?” and made her exit with her daughter-in-law and Mademoiselle d’Herouville. As she left, she murmured to the old maid, who pressed her arm, “You are charming,” meaning, “I am grateful for the service you have just done us.” Mademoiselle d’Herouville returned to the salon as spy; her first glance made Canalis aware that the duchess’s threat was not empty. The poet discovered that his diplomatic skill was insufficient for such a struggle; his wit would have to find a more honest, if humbler, stand.When Ernest returned with Modeste’s handkerchief, the poet seized him and took him out on the terrace. “My dear friend,” he said, “I am not only the most unfortunate man in the world, but also the most ridiculous; I come to you to get me out of the hornet’s nest into which I have run. Modeste is a demon; she sees my difficulty and laughs at it. She has just spoken to me of a fragment of a letter of Madame de Chaulieu, which I foolishly gave her; if she shows it I can never make my peace with Eleonore. Will you at once ask Modeste to send me back that paper, and tell her, from me, that I make no pretensions to her hand? Say I count upon her delicacy, upon her propriety as a young girl, to behave to me as if we had never known each other. I beg her not to speak to me; I implore her to treat me harshly — though I hardly dare ask her to feign a jealous anger, which would help my interests amazingly. Go; I will wait here for an answer.”############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################On re-entering the salon, Ernest de La Briere found the Vicomte de Serizy, a young officer of the Havre guard who had come from Rosny to announce that Madame must be present at the opening of the Chambers. The importance attached to this constitutional solemnity—when Charles X delivered his speech surrounded by the royal family, Madame la Dauphine and Madame in their gallery—made the choice of emissary a mark of attention to Diane, who was adored by this charming young man, son of a minister, gentleman in ordinary of the chamber, only son and heir to an immense fortune. The Duchesse de Maufrigneuse tolerated his attentions mainly to call attention to the age of his mother, Madame de Serizy, who, whispered chronicles behind fans said, had deprived her of the heart of the handsome Lucien de Rubempre.“You will do us the pleasure, I hope, to remain at Rosembray,” said the severe duchess to the young officer.While listening to scandal, the devout lady shut her eyes to the derelictions of guests carefully chosen by the duke; it is surprising how much these excellent women will tolerate under pretense of bringing lost sheep back to the fold by indulgence.“We reckoned without our constitutional government,” said the grand equerry; “and Rosembray, Madame la duchesse, will lose a great honor.”“We shall be more at our ease,” said a tall thin old man, about seventy-five, dressed in blue cloth and wearing his hunting-cap by permission of the ladies. Resembling the Duc de Bourbon, he was the Prince de Cadignan, Master of the Hunt, one of the last great French lords. Just as La Briere was trying to slip behind the sofa to speak with Modeste, a man of thirty-eight, short, fat, and common in appearance, entered the room.“My son, the Prince de Loudon,” said the Duchesse de Verneuil to Modeste, who could not restrain her amazement at seeing the bearer of a historic name turned into such a commonplace figure.“Gaspard,” said the duchess, calling her son. He came at once, and his mother continued, motioning to Modeste, “Mademoiselle de La Bastie, my friend.”The heir presumptive, whose marriage with Desplein’s only daughter had lately been arranged, bowed to the young girl without seeming struck, as his father had been, by her beauty. Modeste could thus compare today’s youth with the old age of a past epoch; the old Prince de Cadignan had already shown a reverence that made her feel he rendered homage to womanhood as much as to royalty. The Duc de Rhetore, the eldest son of the Duchesse de Chaulieu, chiefly remarkable for impertinent manners, bowed rather cavalierly. The contrast between fathers and sons suggested that young men no longer felt themselves great beings and dispensed with the duties of greatness. The fathers retained the politeness of their vanished grandeur, like mountain-tops still gilded by the sun when all is twilight in the valley.Ernest at last slipped a word into Modeste’s ear, and she rose immediately.“My dear,” said the duchesse, thinking she was going to dress, and pulling a bell-rope, “they shall show you your apartment.”Ernest accompanied Modeste to the foot of the grand staircase, presenting the request of the luckless poet and trying to touch her feelings by describing Melchior’s agony.“You see, he loves—he is a captive who thought he could break his chain.”“Love in such a rapid seeker after fortune!” retorted Modeste.“Mademoiselle, you are at the entrance of life; you do not know its defiles. The inconsistencies of a man who falls under the dominion of a woman much older than himself should be forgiven, for he is really not accountable. Think how many sacrifices Canalis has made to her. He has sown too much seed of that kind to resign the harvest; the duchess represents to him ten years of devotion and happiness. You made him forget all that, and unfortunately, he has more vanity than pride; he did not reflect on what he was losing until he met Madame Chaulieu here today. If you really understood him, you would help him. He is a child, always mismanaging his life. You call him a seeker after fortune, but he seeks very badly; like all poets, he is a victim of sensations—childish, easily dazzled by anything that shines, and pursuing its glitter. He used to love horses and pictures, and he craved fame—well, he sold his pictures to buy armor and old Renaissance and Louis XV furniture; just now he is seeking political power. Admit that his hobbies are noble things.”“You have said enough,” replied Modeste; “come,” she added, seeing her father, whom she called with a motion of her head to give her his arm; “come with me, and I will give you that scrap of paper; you shall carry it to the great man and assure him of my condescension to his wishes, but on one condition—you must thank him in my name for the pleasure I have taken in seeing one of the finest German plays performed in my honor. I have learned that Goethe’s masterpiece is neither Faust nor Egmont—” and then, as Ernest looked at the malicious girl with a puzzled air, she added: “It is Torquato Tasso! Tell Monsieur de Canalis to re-read it,” she added smiling; “I particularly desire that you repeat to your friend word for word what I say; for it is not an epigram, it is the justification of his conduct—with this trifling difference, that he will, I trust, become more and more reasonable, thanks to the folly of his Eleonore.”The duchess’s head-woman conducted Modeste and her father to their apartment, where Francoise Cochet had already put everything in order, the elegance of which astounded the colonel, especially after he heard from Francoise that there were thirty other apartments in the chateau decorated with the same taste.“This is what I call a proper country-house,” said Modeste.“The Comte de La Bastie must build you one like it,” replied her father.“Here, monsieur,” said Modeste, giving the bit of paper to Ernest; “carry it to our friend and put him out of his misery.”The word our friend struck the young man’s heart. He looked at Modeste to see if there was anything real in the community of interests she seemed to admit, and she, understanding perfectly what his look meant, added, “Come, go at once, your friend is waiting.”La Briere colored excessively, and left the room in a state of doubt and anxiety less endurable than despair. For the true lover the path that approaches happiness is like the narrow way Catholic poetry calls the entrance to Paradise—a dark passage echoing with the last cries of earthly anguish.An hour later this illustrious company were assembled in the salon; some played whist, others conversed; the women had their embroideries, and all awaited the announcement of dinner. The Prince de Cadignan was drawing Monsieur Mignon out upon China and his campaigns under the empire, making him talk about the Portendueres, the L’Estorades, and the Maucombes, Provencal families; he blamed him for not seeking service and assured him nothing would be easier than to restore him to his rank as colonel of the Guard.“A man of your birth and your fortune ought not to belong to the present Opposition,” said the prince, smiling.This society of distinguished persons not only pleased Modeste but enabled her, during her stay, to acquire a perfection of manners she might otherwise have lacked. Show a clock to an embryo mechanic and you reveal the mechanism; Modeste had the instinct to appropriate the distinctive qualities of Madame de Maufrigneuse and Madame de Chaulieu. For her, the sight of these women was an education; whereas a bourgeois would have ridiculed their ways or made them absurd by clumsy imitation. A well-born, well-educated, right-minded young woman like Modeste fell naturally into connection with these people, and saw at once the differences separating the aristocratic world from the bourgeois world, the provinces from the faubourg Saint-Germain; she caught almost imperceptible shadings and perceived the grace of the grande dame without doubting she could acquire it. She noticed also that her father and La Briere appeared infinitely better in this Olympus than Canalis. The great poet, abdicating his real power—the mind—became nothing more than a courtier seeking a ministry, intriguing for an order, flattering the galaxy. Ernest de La Briere, without ambitions, could be himself; while Melchior became, to use a vulgar expression, a mere toady, courting the Prince de Loudon, the Duc de Rhetore, the Vicomte de Serizy, or the Duc de Maufrigneuse, like a man not free to assert himself, as Colonel Mignon did, proud of his campaigns and of Napoleon’s confidence. Modeste noted the strained efforts of the man of real talent seeking witticisms or compliments to please these grand personages. In a word, to Modeste’s eyes the peacock plucked out his tail-feathers.Toward the middle of the evening the young girl sat down with the grand equerry in a corner of the salon. She led him there purposely to end a suit she could no longer encourage if she wished to retain her self-respect.“Monsieur le duc, if you really knew me,” she said, “you would understand how deeply I am touched by your attentions. It is because of the profound respect I feel for your character, and the friendship which a soul like yours inspires in mine, that I cannot endure to wound your self-love. Before your arrival in Havre I loved sincerely, deeply, and forever one who is worthy of being loved, and my affection for whom is still a secret; but I wish you to know—and in saying this I am more sincere than most young girls—that had I not already formed this voluntary attachment, you would have been my choice, for I recognize your noble and beautiful qualities. A few words your aunt and sister have said to me as to your intentions lead me to make this frank avowal. If you think it desirable, a letter from my mother shall recall me, on pretense of her illness, tomorrow morning before the hunt begins. Without your consent I do not choose to be present at a fete I owe to your kindness, and where, if my secret should escape me, you might feel hurt and defrauded. You will ask why I have come here at all. I could not withstand the invitation. Be generous enough not to reproach me for what was almost a necessary curiosity. But this is not the chief, not the most delicate thing I have to say you. You have firm friends in my father and myself—more so than perhaps you realize; and as my fortune was the first cause that brought you to me, I wish to say—but without intending to use it as a sedative to calm the grief gallantry requires you to testify—that my father has thought over the affair of the marshes, his friend Dumay thinks your project feasible, and they have already taken steps to form a company. Gobenheim, Dumay, and my father have subscribed fifteen hundred thousand francs, and undertake to get the rest from capitalists who will feel it in their interest to take up the matter. If I have not the honor of becoming the Duchesse d’Herouville, I have almost the certainty of enabling you to choose her, free from all trammels in your choice, and in a higher sphere than mine. Oh! let me finish,” she cried, at a gesture from the duke.“Judging by my nephew’s emotion,” whispered Mademoiselle d’Herouville to her niece, “it is easy to see you have a sister.”“Monsieur le duc, all this was settled in my mind the day of our first ride, when I heard you deplore your situation. This is what I have wished to say to you. That day determined my future life. Though you did not make the conquest of a woman, you have at least gained faithful friends at Ingouville—if you will deign to accord us that title.”This little discourse, which Modeste had carefully thought over, was said with so much charm of soul that tears came to the grand equerry’s eyes; he seized her hand and kissed it.“Stay during the hunt,” he said; “my want of merit has accustomed me to these refusals; but while accepting your friendship and that of the colonel, you must let me satisfy myself by the judgment of competent scientific men, that the draining of those marshes will be no risk to the company you speak of, before I agree to the generous offer of your friends. You are a noble girl, and though my heart aches to think I can only be your friend, I will glory in that title, and prove it to you at all times and in all seasons.”“In that case, Monsieur le duc, let us keep our secret. My choice will not be known, at least I think not, until after my mother’s complete recovery. I should like our first blessing to come from her eyes.”############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“Ladies,” said the Prince de Cadignan, as the guests prepared to part for the night, “I know several of you intend to follow the hounds tomorrow, and it is my duty to tell you that if you would be Dianas you must rise, like Diana, with the dawn. The meet is for half-past eight. I have seen women display greater courage than men, but only for a few seconds; you will need resolution to keep on horseback the whole day, except for a breakfast halt, which we shall take, like true hunters and huntresses, on the nail. Are you still determined to show yourselves trained horse-women?”“Prince, it is necessary for me to do so,” said Modeste, adroitly.“I answer for myself,” said the Duchesse de Chaulieu.“And I for my daughter Diane; she is worthy of her name,” added the prince. “So you all persist? I shall arrange, for the sake of Madame and Mademoiselle de Verneuil and others who stay at home, to drive the stag to the further end of the pond.”“Make yourself easy, mesdames,” said the Prince de Loudon, after the Royal Huntsman left the room; “that breakfast ‘on the nail’ will take place under a comfortable tent.”At dawn the next day the weather promised a fine hunt: a gray veil showed blue patches the northwest wind would likely clear. As the party left the chateau, the Master of the Hunt, the Duc de Rhetore, and the Prince de Loudon rode in front, noting the chateau’s white mass against autumn foliage.“The ladies are fortunate in their weather,” remarked the Duc de Rhetore.“Oh, in spite of all their boasting,” replied the Prince de Cadignan, “I think they will let us hunt without them!”“So they might, if each had not a squire,” said the duke.Their attention was drawn by a loud altercation as they reached the open at the forest of Rosembray, the meet. The cause was plain: the Prince de Loudon, enamored of English ways, had brought his British hunting establishment and placed it under the Master of the Hunt. One of his men, a small fair Englishman—insolent, phlegmatic, neatly dressed—had stationed himself on one side. John Barry wore a short scarlet frock with buttons bearing the De Verneuil arms, white breeches, top-boots, a striped waistcoat, and carried a small hunting-whip and brass horn. He was first whipper-in to fifteen horses and sixty English hounds, which had cost the Duc de Verneuil a large sum.John found himself forestalled by three other whippers-in in charge of two royal packs brought in carts. They were the three best huntsmen of the Prince de Cadignan, a marked contrast to the Englishman. Each wore a broad three-cornered hat over a swarthy, wrinkled face; lean, sinewy, vigorous—men for whom sport was passion. They bore immense horns of Dampierre, wound with green worsted, but mainly controlled their hounds by eye and voice. Those noble animals, marked with white, black, or liver, had distinct looks; some young ones yelped impatiently, the old scarred hounds lay quiet with ears to the earth.As the Englishman came up, royal dogs and huntsmen looked at him as though to say, “If we cannot hunt by ourselves his Majesty’s service is insulted.”What began with jests grew fiercer between Monsieur Jacquin La Roulie, the old French whipper-in, and John Barry. The two princes guessed the subject from afar, and the Master of the Hunt, setting spurs to his horse, brought it to an end with authority.“Who drew the wood?”“I, monseigneur,” said the Englishman.“Very good,” said the Prince de Cadignan, proceeding to take Barry’s report.Dogs and men became silent and respectful before the Royal Huntsman, as each recognized his dignity. The prince laid out the day’s work; a hunt is like a battle, and the Master of Charles X.’s hounds was their Napoleon. Thanks to the admirable system he had introduced into French venery, he could concentrate on the science and strategy of it. He assigned the duty of driving the stag to water to the Prince de Loudon’s establishment, expecting the royal hounds to send the stag into the Crown forest on the horizon before the chateau.
