He soothed his old huntsmen’s pride by giving them the most arduous task, while employing the Englishman at his specialty, letting the two national systems face each other and show their strengths.“Does monseigneur wish us to wait any longer?” said La Roulie.“I know what you mean, old friend,” said the prince. “It is late, but—”“Here come the ladies,” said the second whipper-in.At that moment the cavalcade of sixteen riders approached, the four ladies in front with green veils. Modeste, accompanied by her father, the grand equerry, and La Briere, rode in advance beside the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse, whom the Vicomte de Serizy escorted. Behind them rode the Duchesse de Chaulieu, flanked by Canalis, on whom she smiled without rancor. When they reached the open where the huntsmen in red coats and brass bugles, surrounded by the hounds, made a tableau, the Duchesse de Chaulieu, despite her embonpoint, rode up to Modeste, finding it more dignified not to avoid the young woman to whom she had said nothing the evening before.When the Master of the Hunt finished his compliments to the ladies on their punctuality, Eleonore observed the magnificent whip glittering in Modeste’s little hand and graciously asked to see it.“I have never seen anything more beautiful,” she said, showing it to Diane de Maufrigneuse. “It suits its possessor,” she added, returning it to Modeste.“You must admit, Madame la duchesse,” answered Mademoiselle de La Bastie, with a tender malicious glance at La Briere, “that it is a rather strange gift from the hand of a future husband.”“I should take it,” said Madame de Maufrigneuse, “as a declaration of my rights, in remembrance of Louis XIV.”La Briere’s eyes filled, and for a moment he dropped his reins; a second glance from Modeste bade him not betray his happiness. The hunt began.The Duc d’Herouville took occasion to say in a low voice to his fortunate rival, “Monsieur, I hope that you will make your wife happy; if I can be of use to you, command me—I should be glad to contribute to the happiness of so charming a pair.”This great day, on which such vast interests of heart and fortune were decided, caused but one anxiety to the Master of the Hunt—whether the stag would cross the pond and be killed on the lawn before the house; huntsmen of his caliber are like chess-players who can foresee a checkmate. The happy old man attained his wish; the run was magnificent, and the ladies were freed from his attendance for the hunt of the next day but one—which, however, proved rainy.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The Duc de Verneuil’s guests stayed five days at Rosembray; on the last day the Gazette de France announced Baron de Canalis’s appointment as commander of the Legion of Honor and minister at Carlsruhe.Early in December, after Desplein’s operation, Madame de La Bastie regained her sight; seeing Ernest de La Briere, she pressed Modeste’s hand and whispered, “I should have chosen him myself.”By late February Latournelle had signed the Provence deeds, and the La Bastie family secured the king’s signature to the marriage contract and the ordinance transmitting their title and arms to La Briere, who took the name La Briere‑La Bastie. Letters‑patent entailed the La Bastie estate at the end of April. La Briere’s witnesses were Canalis and the minister he had served five years as secretary; Modeste’s were the Duc d’Herouville and Desplein, whom the Mignons remembered with gratitude.We shall again meet Monsieur and Madame de La Briere‑La Bastie; readers will see how sweet and easy married life with an educated woman can be. Modeste, avoiding pedantry, is the pride and happiness of her husband, family, and all around her.ADDENDUMPersonages appearing in other Human Comedy stories:Beaupre, Fanny: A Start in Life; The Muse of the Department; Cut off by poor roads and slow communication with Paris, these places treat modern civilization as a spectacle—astonished observers rather than participants. A moral archaeologist would find in many villages the manners of bygone reigns: Louis XV in Provence, Louis XIV in Poitou, and even older types in Brittany. Tradition preserves these traces better than historians: local memory clings to customs and vestiges of past splendors—former capitals of tiny feudal states now reduced to names on maps.Modern industry is eroding such distinctive local arts and crafts, turning unique products into mass-made goods and replacing the patrimony of individual artisans with impersonal production. Public buildings remain as retrospective monuments, but factories will soon be the new landmarks. Yet some towns maintain their ancient character. One of the most intact is Guerande, a Breton jewel positioned to command the tides and dunes, like the summit of a triangle whose other points are Croisic and Batz. Few provincial towns—perhaps Vitre and Avignon excepted—preserve the medieval type so thoroughly.Guerande is still ringed by strong walls, its moats full, battlements and loopholes unovergrown; even ivy has not mantled the towers. Three gates show rings of portcullises and an iron-clamped drawbridge that could still be raised. In 1820 the mayor was reproached for planting poplars along the moat to shade the promenade, though the esplanade had long been a mall where citizens strolled beneath elms. The houses have not altered their fronts, added stories, or been stuccoed; arcades rest on wooden columns, floor planks bend but do not break. Merchant houses are low, slate-faced, with carved wood figures, grotesque faces, and fantastic beasts—relics painters prize for their patina.The streets remain as they were centuries ago, though the population has thinned so much a traveler may walk alone through a deserted way where mullioned windows are boarded up to avoid the window-tax. A postern gives onto a wooded tract—one of Brittany’s luxuriantly verdant spots—seen through casemates once occupied by halberdiers. Every step reminds one of long-continued habits; medieval ideas and superstitions persist. A gendarme in a silver-laced hat would be an anachronism; modern objects and fashions are rare and adapt to immutable customs. The public square still swarms with Breton costumes that attract artists: the white linen of the paludiers (salt-gatherers), blue and brown peasant clothes, the sacred jewelry of women, sailors’ jerkins and varnished caps. Castes remain distinct—bourgeoisie, nobility, clergy, peasants, paludiers—so that revolutionary leveling found the masses too rugged to plane. Even after 1830 Guerande remained fervently Catholic, silent, and self-contained, where modern ideas have little access.Its geography explains much. Guerande overlooks salt-marshes producing the celebrated Guerande salt—whose quality Bretons link to their butter and sardines—and is linked to the rest of France by only two roads: one from Savenay that terminates at Saint-Nazaire, and another from Vannes. Land travel is limited; the more frequented route is by water from Saint-Nazaire. Between Guerande and Saint-Nazaire the mail-coach does not run; the road sees almost no passengers. These obstacles discourage travelers and encourage inhabitants to accept separation. Guerande, at the edge of the continent, leads nowhere and is glad to be left alone. Much of its salt production is managed at Croisic, which communicates with Guerande across dangerous quicksands and an arm of the sea; Croisic’s port handles commerce.Guerande is like the Herculaneum of feudality—preserved rather than destroyed. Approaching from Croisic across salt-marshes, its vast fortifications strike the traveler; from Saint-Nazaire its position and naive grace charm. The surrounding country is ravishing: hedges full of honeysuckle, roses, box, and plants that make the landscape seem an English garden. This rich nature, framed by an African-like belt of ocean-banked dunes, creates striking contrasts—the paludiers in white scattered among melancholy swamps recall Arabs in burrows. Guerande’s sleep-like silence, likened to Venice, lulls one; public conveyances are rare, reduced to a carrier’s springless wagon. Bernus, the carrier in 1829, was the town’s factotum, going and coming at will; the arrival of a carriage or a lady for sea-bathing was an event that drew peasants who came to barter and to buy the distinctive jewels and linens that mark Breton brides and households.Around Guerande, for a ten-mile circuit, it is always Guerande—the town of the treaty of 1365, the key of the coast, and a place that still claims a lost splendor. Artists and some bourgeois visitors feel a desire—like that aroused by Venice—to spend their days amid its peace, walking along the mall from gate to gate toward the sea. Its image lingers like that of an enchanting woman: towered, girdled, floating in a robe of dunes, scented with wild paths.Near Guerande’s church stands a mansion that epitomizes the town’s past—a symbol of a greatness destroyed. It belongs to the du Guaisnics, the noblest family of the province, as ancient as Brittany’s granite. In du Guesclin’s time they were counted among the utmost nobility; perhaps Druids in origin, they now subsist on a small income. The du Guaisnics (also spelled du Glaicquin) possess the manor in Guerande, the little castle of Guaisnic, and nominally hold lands once bringing substantial revenue. But their estates have long been pledged to farmers; despite the barony’s lands grossing perhaps sixty thousand francs under mismanaged cultivation, the family receives only two thousand francs a year. For two centuries they have been unable to repay advances and thus remain titular rather than actual proprietors—analogous to the ancien régime’s crown towards its engagistes. Before 1789 the Guaisnic fiefs were worth fifty thousand francs annually, but the National Assembly’s suppression of seigneurial dues on inheritance reduced their claims. In Paris the family is little regarded and laughed at, yet in Guerande the Baron du Guaisnic remains a great baron, a man standing beneath only the King.At the end of a damp, gloomy lane stands the mansion’s granite archway—wide enough for a horseman, indicating its antiquity. The oak gate, studded with enormous nails set in geometric patterns, opens under a semicircular arch carved with the Guaisnics’ arms: simple, proud, and ancient—Gules, a hand proper gonfaloned ermine, a sword argent in pale, with the motto FAC. The coronet above proclaims baronial rank. The hand holding the sword with vigor expresses chivalrous pride and a family’s readiness for action—“To DO,” a chivalrous motto, echoing the praise “You did well in the battle” once accorded to du Guesclin. The depth of the carving, sheltered by the arch, matches the depth of feeling this motto still arouses among those who know the Guaisnics.The gate opens into a somewhat spacious courtyard with stables on the right and kitchen offices on the left. The house is of freestone from cellar to garret. Its courtyard facade has a portico with double steps; carved vestiges—now worn—display the Hand bearing the sword. Granite steps are disjointed and mossy, yet solidity remains. The oak door, nail-studded and likely by a Venetian school artisan, opens into a noble hall leading to a portico and the garden.The hall is marvelously preserved: chestnut wainscot three feet high, Spanish leather wall hangings with gilding faded to red, a wooden painted ceiling with traces of gold and floral designs, and an enormous carved stone chimney-piece with giant wrought-iron andirons capable of holding a cart-load of wood. Furniture is all oak, each piece bearing the family arms. Hunting arms—three English guns, sabres, game-bags—hang from the walls alongside fishermen’s utensils. On one side a dining-room opens to the kitchen via a door cut through a corner tower; at the opposite corner a spiral staircase rises to the two upper stories.The dining-room is hung with fourteenth-century tapestries; their inscribed banderols, in the naive language of the fabliaux, attest their age. These tapestries, spared much light and framed in ebony-toned oak, set the room’s medieval tone. The ceiling beams are carved with foliage, and the painted planks between reveal golden garlands. On shelves, as in 1200, sit four old goblets, an embossed soup-tureen, two silver salt-cellars, and pewter plates and pitchers marked with the du Guaisnics’ arms and covered with hinged pewter lids. The fireplace here has been modernized in Louis XV style—an anachronism that would distress a poet but to the family is indifferent. On the mantel stands a tall tortoise-shell clock inlaid with brass flanked by silver candelabra of singular design. A large square table fills the center; chairs are turned wood with tapestry. A small round table by a garden window holds an odd lamp—an ostrich-egg-sized globe attached to a candlestick by a glass tube, its wick drawing nut-oil like a coiled tape-worm.Windows to the garden and courtyard are mullioned with hexagonal panes and draped with heavy brocatelle curtains of ancient crimson silk with gold reflections. The two upper stories contain only two rooms each—the master’s bedroom and the children’s—while guests lodged under the roof and servants above kitchens and stables. The pointed lead-protected roof has noble pointed windows nearly to the ridge, slender casings whose carvings have suffered from salty air; above each arch a vane grinds.A detail invaluable to the archaeologist: a spiral-stair tower stands at the corner of a great gable wall without windows, its stair descending to a little arched door opening on a gravel yard between house and stables. A companion, five-sided tower ending in a cupola with a bell-turret, repeats the form on the garden side instead of the pepper-pot roof of the sister tower. These towers connect on the first floor by a stone gallery supported by brackets ending in grotesque human heads; the gallery’s delicate balustrade and a stone dais hanging from the gable recall statues at church portals. One might imagine a woman walking this balcony to gaze over Guerande at sunlight gilding sands and ocean.The garden, remarkable for so old a place, covers half an acre, espaliered walls dividing vegetable squares bordered by cordons of fruit trees tended by Gasselin, the man-of-all-work. At the far end is a grotto with a seat, a central sun-dial, and gravelled paths. The garden facade lacks the courtyard towers but shows a slender spiral column from ground to roof that once bore the family banner—its iron socket now supporting only some weak plants. The presence of certain sculptural details, trefoils with four leaves rather than three, suggests Venetian influence in the architecture—Venetian art, corrupted by eastern contacts, sometimes betraying Christian symbolism by altering the trinity to quartet forms.This ancient dwelling’s charm prompts one to ask why such houses are no longer built. The reason is social change: mansions are sold, demolished, or parceled for streets; fathers are uncertain the next generation will retain the paternal home. Formerly, when a house was built, men thought themselves working for a family in perpetuity. That faith—both in self and God—produced prodigies: durable architecture, carefully ordered interiors, and a continuity of manners that now fade. The du Guaisnics still live by those traditions. Their rooms, courtyard, and external accessories preserve the spirit, grace, and candor of old Brittany. To understand the family’s surprising figures, one must first see the town and the house; things shape beings. There are public buildings whose very presence molds the character of nearby inhabitants; under the shadow of a cathedral, irreligion is harder to maintain. So too the Guaisnic mansion sustains a way of life. Were the Baron not still ready to take sword in hand, all this faithful portraiture would be untrue.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Early in May 1836 the family of Guenic comprised Monsieur and Madame du Guenic, Mademoiselle du Guenic (the baron’s elder sister), and an only son of twenty-one, Gaudebert-Calyste-Louis by an old family use; the father’s name was Gaudebert-Calyste-Charles. Only the given names varied: Saint Gaudebert and Saint Calyste were forever bound to protect the Guenics.The Baron du Guenic had left Guerande as soon as La Vendée and Brittany rose; he fought through the war with Charette, Cathelineau, La Rochejaquelein, d’Elbée, Bonchamps, and the Prince de Loudon. Prudently, before leaving, he had sold all his property to his sister, Mademoiselle Zéphirine du Guenic. After the leaders died, the baron, miraculously spared, refused to submit to Napoleon and kept resisting until 1802, when, defeated and nearly captured, he returned to Guerande, went to Croisic, and from there crossed to Ireland, true to the old Breton enmity with England.The people of Guerande feigned ignorance of him; for twenty years not a single indiscreet word was spoken. Mademoiselle collected the rents and sent them by fishermen. Monsieur du Guenic returned to Guerande in 1813 as quietly as if he had spent a season at Nantes. During his Dublin exile the old Breton, well past fifty, fell in love with an Irishwoman, Fanny O’Brien, daughter of a noble but poor family. She was twenty-one. The baron went to France for marriage papers, returned to Ireland, and about ten months later (early 1814) brought his wife to Guerande, where she gave him Calyste on the very day Louis XVIII landed at Calais—hence the young man’s final name, Louis.The old, loyal Breton was now seventy-three; his long guerrilla warfare, exile, and perilous sea crossings had prematurely aged him, making him seem a hundred. Thus the head of the house of Guenic matched the worn grandeur of their dwelling, built when a court still reigned at Guerande.Monsieur du Guenic was tall, straight, wiry, and lean. His oval face was a network of wrinkles over the cheekbones and brow, like faces Rembrandt loved to paint. The sun and watching his country had sunken his features, yet enough remained to touch the soul. The firm outline, the solemn lines, and the rigid, slightly wounded nose declared intrepidity without calculation, faith without reserve, obedience without discussion, fidelity without compromise, and love without inconstancy. In him, Breton granite had become man.The baron had no teeth; his lips, once red, were now violet and drawn inward, giving an expression both threatening and proud. His chin seemed to seek his nose, which, humped in the middle, betrayed his energy and Breton resistance. His skin, marbled with red blotches through wrinkles, indicated a sanguine temperament fitted to resist fatigue. His abundant white hair fell in curls on his shoulders. In his black eyes, despite the extinguished face, still glittered the last flames of a generous and loyal soul. Eyebrows and lashes had disappeared; shaving had become difficult, so he let his beard grow fan-shaped. One would admire his hands—large, broad, hairy, the hands of a soldier and guerrilla, hands that had handled sword and oar and fought among thorns and marshes. Close inspection might show recent marks attesting he had again joined the Vendée. These hands were a living commentary on the Guenic motto: Fac!His forehead, with golden temples and a narrow brow broadened by hair loss, added majesty to that noble ruin. His countenance, somewhat material but natural to the Breton type, showed a certain savagery and stolid calm, resembling Huguenot impassibility—a repose where animal nature dominates and thought is rare. Thinking seemed an effort; its seat was more the heart than the head; it led to acts rather than ideas. The mystery resolves: he had inborn faiths and sentiments, allowing him to dispense with much thought. Institutions and religion thought for him; he saved his mind, and his kind, for action. He drew thought from his heart like his sword from its scabbard, held aloft on his scutcheon, shining with sincerity. That explains the sale to his sister before the war—provision for all eventualities: death, confiscation, exile. The beauty of character in these two old people (the sister lived only by and for the brother) cannot be understood by the selfish morals of the present age. An archangel could not have found a thought of personal interest. In 1814, when the rector suggested the baron go to Paris to claim recompense from the Bourbons, the sister cried, “Oh, fy! does my brother need to hold out his hand like a beggar?” “It would be thought I served a king from interest,” said the baron. “Besides, it is for him to remember. Poor king! he must be weary indeed of those who harass him.”This loyal servant, who had spent life and means on Louis XVIII, received the rank of colonel, the cross of Saint-Louis, and a stipend of two thousand francs a year—news the baron accepted as the king’s remembrance. No one undeceived him; the gift had been made by the Duc de Feltre. As gratitude to the king, the baron held a siege at Guerande against General Travot; refusing to surrender the fortress, he evacuated into the woods with a band of Chouans who remained armed until the second Bourbon restoration. Guerande still remembers that siege.The Baron du Guenic was as illiterate as a peasant; he could read, write a little, and cipher, knew military art and heraldry, but, aside from his prayer-book, had not read three volumes in his life. His clothing was always the same: stout shoes, ribbed stockings, greenish velveteen breeches, a cloth waistcoat, and a loose coat with a collar from which hung the cross of Saint-Louis. A noble serenity reigned on his face; for the last year or so sleep—prelude to death—seemed to be preparing him for eternal rest. This somnolence did not alarm his wife, his blind sister, or his friends, whose medical knowledge was slight; to them these pauses were natural: the baron had done his duty.In this ancient mansion the absorbing interests were the fortunes of the dispossessed elder branch, the future of the exiled Bourbons, the Catholic religion, and political developments in Brittany. The sole personal interest was the attachment of all for the only son, Calyste, heir and sole hope of the du Guenic name.Some years earlier the old Vendean experienced a return of youth to train his son in manly exercises proper for a gentleman ready at any moment to take arms. When Calyste was sixteen, his father took him to the marshes and forest, teaching war’s rudiments through the chase, preaching by example—firm in saddle and sure in shot, indifferent to fatigue, intrepid before obstacles, bidding his son follow him into danger.So, when the Duchesse de Berry landed to attempt restoration for her son, the father judged it right to take his boy and join her, practicing the family motto. The baron started at night, saying nothing to his wife, taking his son under fire as if to a fête, accompanied by Gasselin, his sole vassal. The three were absent three months without sending news to the baroness, who read the Quotidienne with trembling, nor to the blind sister, whose nerve never faltered. The three guns on the walls had seen service recently. The baron, considering the enterprise useless, left the region before the affair of La Pénissière; otherwise the house of Guenic might have ended in hecatomb.On a stormy night after parting from Madame, the father, son, and servant returned, surprising the baroness and Mademoiselle du Guenic, though the blind sister recognized their steps in the lane. The baron looked around the anxious circle seated by the antique lamp and said in a tremulous voice, while Gasselin replaced guns and sabres, in words of feudal simplicity: “The barons did not all do their duty.” Then he kissed his wife and sister, sat in his old arm-chair, and ordered supper for his son, Gasselin, and himself. Gasselin had thrown himself before Calyste to protect him and received a sabre cut on his shoulder; the women scarcely thanked him. The baron and guests uttered neither curses nor complaints about conquerors—silence a Breton trait. In forty years no one ever heard contumely from the baron about his adversaries; it was for them to do their duty as he did his. This silence indicates an unalterable will.This last effort, a flash of waning energy, had caused the present weakness and somnolence of the old man. The fresh defeat and exile of the Bourbons, driven out as miraculously as restored, were a bitter sadness.About six on the evening this history begins, the baron, who by custom dined at four, fell asleep while his wife read the Quotidienne. His head rested against the arm-chair back beside the fireplace on the garden side.Near that gnarled ancient tree, and before the fire, the baroness sat—the type of those admirable women found most in England, Scotland, or Ireland—milk-white, golden-haired, the curls wound as if by angels, with a heavenly light in their hair. Fanny O’Brien was one of those sylphs—strong in tenderness, invincible under misfortune, soft as her voice, pure as her blue eyes; skin silken to the touch, beauty beyond painter or word. Still beautiful at forty-two, many would have counted themselves fortunate to marry her as she looked on the autumn display of flowers and fruit.The baroness held the paper in dimpled fingers, nails cut square like an antique statue’s. Half reclining, feet out to warm, she wore a black velvet gown for the chill. The corsage rose to the throat and molded the shoulders and bosom. Her hair was in ringlets in the English fashion, the rest twisted to the crown and pinned with a tortoiseshell comb. The color gleamed like burnished gold. She braided the short nape-locks—a racial sign—concealing the tiny braid in the coif, a detail showing the care she gave her person; it was her pride to please the old baron. When a woman displays in her own home the coquetry many reserve for outside, she is a noble mother and wife; she loves without calculation, does good in secret, and worships. In Fanny the Virgin’s halo seemed to preserve her beauty from time. Her skin had warm pearly tones; her broad well-modeled brow caught the light; turquoise eyes shone with incomparable sweetness; soft lashes and slightly sunken temples inspired mute melancholy. Her aquiline thin nose suggested high birth; pure lips wore happy smiles of inexhaustible loving-kindness. She had gained slight embonpoint lately, but hips and slender waist remained pleasing. Autumn had added perennial flowers to her summer beauty—nobler rounded arms, finer skin grain, fuller outlines. Above all, her open serene face and pure blue eyes expressed illimitable sympathy and angelic tenderness.At the other hearth, in an arm-chair, the octogenarian sister, like her brother in all except dress, sat listening and knitting stockings—the work requiring no sight. Both eyes had cataracts; she refused an operation despite her sister-in-law’s entreaties. Secretly, she said it was want of courage; truly, she would not let her brother spend twenty-five louis for her. That sum saved would be for the household’s good.These two old persons set off the baroness’ beauty. Mademoiselle Zéphirine, deprived of sight, was unaware of what eighty years had done to her face. Her pale hollow countenance, the white sightless eyes giving an appearance of death, and three or four solitary projecting teeth, framed by a little brown printed cotton hood quilted like a petticoat and trimmed with a ruche, seemed menacing. She wore a cotillon—short coarse skirt—over a quilted petticoat (a veritable mattress hiding double louis-d’or) and pockets sewn to a belt she unfastened each night and put on each morning like a garment. Her body was encased in the casaquin of Brittany, a spencer of the same cloth, with a pleated collar, the washing of which caused her only dispute with her sister-in-law—she changed it only once a week. From the large wadded sleeves came two withered yet vigorous arms, their brownish-red hands like stocking-machines eternally at work; from time to time she would thrust a long knitting needle between hood and hair to poke her white locks, thrusting it back carelessly without fear of harm. She was straight as a steeple; her erect and imposing carriage might be one of old age’s coquetries proving pride a necessary passion of life. Her smile was gay. She, too, had done her duty.As soon as the baroness saw her husband asleep, she ceased reading. A ray of sunshine stretched across the old room, burnishing black furniture, touching ceiling carvings, dancing on time-worn chests, and brightening the old oak table; Fanny’s voice and the light made music in the octogenarian blind sister’s heart. The ray sank into the melancholy tones of twilight; the baroness fell into deep meditation, a silence noted by her sister-in-law for the last two weeks, unexplained and unasked about. Mademoiselle continued to knit; the silence deepened until the clicking of needles was plainly heard.“You have dropped the paper, sister, but you are not asleep,” said Mademoiselle slyly.Mariotte then entered to light the lamp, which she placed on a small table by the fire, fetched her distaff and ball of thread, and sat on a stool in the window recess to spin. Gasselin busied himself about the offices, saw to the horses, readied the stable, and fed the two hunting-dogs; their joyful barking was the last noise to awaken the echoes in the dark walls. The two dogs and two horses were the only remaining vestiges of the house’s chivalry. An imaginative man sitting on the portico steps might have quivered to the hounds’ baying and the horses’ neighing.Gasselin was a short, thick, squat Breton, with black hair and sun-browned face, silent, slow, and obstinate as a mule, yet steady. He was forty-two and had been in the household twenty-five years, hired by Mademoiselle at fifteen on hearing of the marriage and the baron’s probable return. He considered himself part of the family, had played with Calyste, loved the horses and dogs, petted them, and wore a blue linen jacket with flapping pockets, matching waistcoat and trousers, blue stockings, and stout hob-nailed shoes; in cold he donned a goatskin.Mariotte, also over forty, was to woman what Gasselin was to man—same complexion, figure, lively black eyes. It is curious they never married; perhaps it seemed immoral, they were so like brother and sister. Mariotte’s wages were ninety francs a year; Gasselin’s, three hundred. But thousands elsewhere would not have tempted either to leave the Guenic household. Both were under Mademoiselle’s orders, who, from the war to the baron’s return, had ruled the house. When she learned the baron would bring home a mistress, she feared losing the household sceptre, yet she was agreeably surprised to find in Fanny O’Brien a woman of high birth who disliked petty household cares and would prefer plain fare to preparing costly food for herself. Fanny was capable of all duties, strong under necessary privations but without taste for commonplace work. When the baron begged his sister, in his wife’s name, to continue in charge, the old maid kissed the baroness like a sister, made a daughter of her, adored her, and rejoiced to keep control of household management. She ran the house on an almost inconceivable economy, relaxing only for great occasions—lying-in, Calyste’s needs, and similar events.Though the two servants were used to the stern regime and required no orders—masters’ interests were their own—Mademoiselle insisted on overseeing everything. She knew, without looking, how many nuts in the barn or oats in the bin, carrying on her belt a boatswain’s whistle to summon Mariotte with one note and Gasselin with two.Gasselin’s greatest happiness was tending the garden, producing fine fruits and vegetables; with so little to do he would have been lost without it. After grooming horses and cleaning the ground-floor rooms, he weeded and watched for a field-mouse or the grub of the cockchafer, joyfully presenting his catch to his masters. He liked going to Croisic on fast-days to buy fish cheaper than at Guerande.Thus no household was more united in interests, more bound together than this noble family devoted to duty. Masters and servants seemed made for one another. For twenty-five years there had been neither trouble nor discord. The only griefs were the boy’s petty ailments, the only terrors the events of 1814 and 1830. If the same tasks were performed at the same hours, and food obeyed seasons and times, this monotony, like nature’s, varied only by cloud and sun, and was sustained by affection in all hearts—the more fruitful because it sprang from natural causes.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When night had fairly fallen, Gasselin entered the hall and asked his master respectfully if he was still needed.“You can go out, or go to bed, after prayers,” replied the baron, waking, “unless Madame or my sister—”The two ladies signaled assent. Gasselin knelt as his masters rose to kneel on their chairs; Mariotte also knelt before her stool. Mademoiselle du Guenic then said the prayer aloud. After it was over, someone rapped at the door on the lane and Gasselin opened it.“I dare say it is Monsieur le curé; he usually comes first,” said Mariotte.Everyone recognized the rector’s foot on the portico steps. He bowed respectfully to the three occupants and addressed them with the unctuous civility of a priest. To the somewhat absent-minded greeting of the mistress of the house he replied with ecclesiastical inquiry.“Are you anxious or ill, Madame la baronne?” he asked.“Thank you, no,” she replied.Monsieur Grimont, about fifty, of middle height, lost in his cassock from which issued two stout shoes, showed a plump visage and generally pale skin with yellow spots. His dimpled hands and placid abbatial face suggested a Dutch burgomaster; his straight black hair and lively brown eyes recalled a Breton peasant. His gaiety, that of a man whose conscience is calm, admitted a joke. He lacked the unease of rectors whose power is contested; seen through Guerande, even the most irreligious traveler would recognize him as the sovereign of that Catholic town. Yet before the du Guenics he lowered his spiritual superiority; in their salon he was a chaplain in his seigneur’s house. In church his benediction always favored the Guenic chapel, where their mailed hand and device adorned the keystone.“I thought Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel had already arrived,” said the rector, sitting and taking the baroness’s hand to kiss it. “She is getting unpunctual. Can it be that dissipation is contagious? I see Monsieur le chevalier is again at Les Touches this evening.”“Don’t say anything about those visits before Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel,” cried the old maid eagerly.“Ah! mademoiselle,” remarked Mariotte, “you can’t prevent the town from gossiping.”“What do they say?” asked the baroness.“The young girls and the old women all say he is in love with Mademoiselle des Touches.”“A lad of Calyste’s make is playing his proper part in making the women love him,” said the baron.“Here comes Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel,” said Mariotte.The gravel in the courtyard crackled under discreet footsteps; a page with a lantern accompanied the coming lady. Seeing him, Mariotte moved her stool to the great hall to talk with him by the lantern’s gleam, economizing the hall’s single oribus candle.This elderly demoiselle was thin, dried-up, yellow as parchment, wrinkled, with gray eyes, prominent teeth, and the hands of a man. Short, slightly crooked, she stirred a great mass of petticoats when reaching a pocket; a loud jingling of keys and money echoed among her garments. She wore, dangling from one side, the keys of a good housekeeper, and from the other a silver snuff-box, thimble, knitting-needles, and other resonant implements. Instead of Zephirine’s wadded hood she had a green bonnet—once fresh, now yellowing—of a fashion preserved by her nieces and renewed every five years at Nantes. Her gowns, too, were made by her nieces to an immutable pattern. She used the old cane with its short hook and bore the ermine of ancient Breton dukes. In her and her sister the illustrious house of the Pen-Hoels ended. Her younger sister had married a Kergarouet who, despite disapproval, added Pen-Hoel to his name and called himself Vicomte de Kergarouet-Pen-Hoel.“Heaven has punished him,” said the old lady; “he has nothing but daughters, and the Kergarouet-Pen-Hoel name will be wiped out.”Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel had about seven thousand francs a year from rents. She had come into her property at thirty-six and managed it herself, inspecting it on horseback and displaying the firmness often seen in deformed persons. Her avarice was admired locally; she kept one woman-servant and the page, and her yearly expenses, excluding taxes, did not exceed a thousand francs. Thus she attracted the caresses of the Kergarouet-Pen-Hoels, who spent winters at Nantes and summers on the Loire estate. She was supposed ready to leave her fortune to whichever niece pleased her best; every three months one of the four demoiselles de Kergarouet-Pen-Hoel (the youngest twelve, the eldest twenty) came to spend a few days with her.A friend of Zephirine du Guenic, Jacqueline de Pen-Hoel, devoted to the Breton grandeur of the du Guenics, had planned since Calyste’s birth to transmit her property to the chevalier by marrying him to whichever niece the Vicomtesse de Kergarouet-Pen-Hoel would bestow. She dreamed of buying back some of the best Guenic property from the farmer engagistes. When avarice has an object it ceases to be a vice; it becomes a means of virtue. Perhaps Zephirine knew Jacqueline’s plan. Perhaps even the baroness, whose soul was occupied by love for her son and tenderness for his father, guessed it, seeing with what wily perseverance Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel brought her favorite niece, Charlotte de Kergarouet, now sixteen. The rector, Monsieur Grimont, was certainly in her confidence; he helped the old maid invest her savings.But even had Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel possessed three hundred thousand francs in gold, the du Guenics would never have shown any attention that could be construed as seeking her fortune. From true Breton pride, Jacqueline, glad of the supremacy accorded to Zephirine and the du Guenics, always showed herself honored by her relations with Madame du Guenic and her sister-in-law. She even concealed the sacrifice she made each evening in allowing her page to burn in the Guenic hall that singular gingerbread-colored candle called an oribus still used in western France.Thus this rich old maid was nobility, pride, and grandeur personified. At the moment of this narrative, the Abbe Grimont has revealed that on the evening when the old baron, the young chevalier, and Gasselin secretly departed to join MADAME (to the terror of the baroness and the great joy of all Bretons) Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel had given the baron ten thousand francs in gold—an immense sacrifice—to which the abbe added another ten thousand, a tithe collected by him—charging the old hero to offer the whole, in the name of the Pen-Hoels and of the parish of Guerande, to the mother of Henri V.Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel treated Calyste as if her intentions gave her rights over him; her plans seemed to authorize supervision. Not strict in gallantry—she had the indulgence of old women of the old school—she held in horror modern ways. Calyste might increase her esteem by a few Breton adventures but would lose it considerably if she saw him entangled in what she called innovations. She might disinter a little gold to pay for the results of a love-affair, but if Calyste drove a tilbury or spoke of a visit to Paris she would deem him dissipated and a spendthrift. To her, novel ideas meant the overthrow of crop succession, ruin under the name of improvements, mortgaged lands as the inevitable result of experiments. Prudence was the true method of making fortune; good management meant filling granaries and waiting for a rise rather than risking experiments. By singular chance she often made lucky sales that confirmed her principles. She was thought maliciously clever but was not quick-witted; methodical as a Dutchman, prudent as a cat, and persistent as a priest, those qualities in routine-bound Brittany were practically equivalent to intellect.“Will Monsieur du Halga join us this evening?” asked Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel, taking off her knitted mittens after the usual exchange of greetings.“Yes, mademoiselle; I met him taking his dog to walk on the mall,” replied the rector.“Ha! then our mouche will be lively to-night. Last evening we were only four.”At the word mouche the rector rose and took from a drawer a small round osier basket, a pile of ivory counters yellow with use, and a greasy pack of cards. He arranged counters before each player and set the basket beside the lamp with infantine eagerness and the manner of a man accustomed to this service.A firm knock at the outer gate echoed through the mansion. The page opened, and presently the long, lean, methodically clothed Chevalier du Halga, former flag-captain to Admiral de Kergarouet, outlined himself in black on the portico.“Welcome, chevalier!” cried Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel.“The altar is raised,” said the abbe.The chevalier was a man in poor health, who wore flannel for his rheumatism, a black-silk skull-cap to protect his head from fog, and a spencer to guard his chest from sudden gusts. He always carried a gold-headed cane and a little bitch named Thisbe. Fussy as a fine lady and speaking low to spare his voice, he had in earlier days been among the most intrepid officers of the old navy, winning the confidence of de Suffren and the friendship of the Comte de Portenduere. His splendid conduct as flag-captain to Admiral Kergarouet was written on his scarred face. Now no one would imagine the voice that ruled storms and the eye that compassed the sea.The chevalier never smoked, never swore; he was gentle and tranquil as a girl, as concerned about Thisbe’s caprices as an elderly dowager. He moved as an invalid, walking as if stepping on eggs, complaining of wind, sun, or mist, yet showed the whitest teeth in the reddest gums—a fact reassuring as to his maladies, which were rather expensive, consisting of four daily meals of monastic amplitude. His frame, like the baron’s, was bony and indestructibly strong, covered with a parchment-like skin retaining the tawny color from India, whence, however, he did not bring back facts or ideas. He had emigrated with his friends, lost his property, and now ended his days with the cross of Saint-Louis and a pension of two thousand francs from the Invalides de la Marine. The slight hypochondria that made him invent imaginary ills is explained by actual suffering during the emigration. He served in the Russian navy until Emperor Alexander ordered him employed against France; he then resigned and lived at Odessa near the Duc de Richelieu, with whom he returned to France. The duke obtained for this relic of the old Breton navy the pension that enabled him to live. On the death of Louis XVIII he returned to Guerande and eventually became mayor of the city.For the last fifteen years the rector, the chevalier, and Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel had regularly passed their evenings at the hôtel de Guenic, where other noble neighbors also came. It will be readily understood that the du Guenics were at the head of the faubourg Saint-Germain of the old Breton province, where no member of the new administration sent down by the government was ever allowed to penetrate. For the last six years the rector coughed when coming to the crucial words, Domine, salvum fac regem. Politics in Guerande remained at that point.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Mouche is played with five cards to each player and one turned up to show trumps. Each round a player may play or abstain; abstaining costs only his stake unless there are forfeits in the basket. If he plays and wins a trick he is paid pro rata to the stake; win none and he is mouche, paying the whole stake and swelling the next basket. Those who decline throw down their cards; their play is null. Players may exchange cards with the remainder of the pack in sequence, so the first two sometimes take most of it. The turned-up trump belongs to the dealer, who is last and may exchange it for any card in his hand. One powerful card, the knave of clubs, is called Mistigris.The game provokes cupidity, diplomacy, and poker faces. At the hotel du Guenic each player took twenty counters representing five sous, making the stake five farthings—a significant sum for this company. In extraordinary luck fifty sous could be won—more than anyone in Guerande spent in a day. Consequently Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel played mouche with the passion of a big-game hunter. Mademoiselle Zephirine, who shared with the baroness, attached equal importance, treating one farthing for a chance at five as a serious investment.An 1825 convention stated the game should cease when anyone losing ten sous wished to retire; politeness forbade continuing without him. The chevalier and the baron, however, found a way around this: when all but one wished to continue, du Halga, a rich free-spending sailor, would offer ten counters to Zephirine or Jacqueline, recompensing them if they won. The baron made similar offers under the pretext of continuing the game. The misers accepted, though not without pressing—a maidens' custom—but only after the baron and chevalier had won, or the offer would insult them.Mouche became brilliant when a Demoiselle de Kergarouet passed through with her aunt. The Kergarouets insisted on being called simply Kergarouet. The aunt treated the niece to mouche at du Guenic and ordered her to look amiable—easy in Calyste’s presence, whom the four Kergarouet girls adored. Brought up in modern ways, they cared little for five sous; stakes rose. These evenings excited the old blind sister, who signaled stakes to the baroness by pressing her foot a number of times. To play or not when the basket was full provoked inward struggle—cupidity versus fear. If Charlotte de Kergarouet was lucky, her aunt would nevertheless lecture her on reserve and modesty for appearing too decisive.These nightly games, carried on for twenty years, were sometimes interrupted by gossip or political discussion. Players often sat half an hour talking with cards fanned on their stomachs. If a counter was missing from the basket, everyone declared they had put in their share. Usually the chevalier made up the deficiency and was accused of being absent-minded about his health; when he paid, Zephirine and Jacqueline fretted they themselves might have forgotten. The company enjoyed the small suspicions. Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel would almost always accuse the rector of cheating when he won the basket.“It is singular,” he replied, “that I never cheat except when I win the trick.”Sometimes talk of royal misfortunes would distract the baron, and evenings could end without gain or loss but with emotion and complaints that mouche was dull. Once, after the Kergarouets arrived, they tried whist and boston at the baroness’s urging but could not understand them; on departure the new games were voted head-splitters and the company returned to mouche—tradition triumphing in Brittany.While the rector dealt, the baroness asked the Chevalier du Halga the same health questions as the night before. The chevalier made it a point to invent new ailments; he never complained of real wounds but of fancied pains—the doctors have found no remedy for illnesses that don't exist. “Yesterday the trouble was, I believe, in your legs,” said the rector. “It moves about,” said the chevalier. The exchange, with light banter from Zephirine and Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel, displayed the chevalier’s old wit and a life rich in memories.The conversation turned to the death of the Duc de Lenoncourt, and the baron remarked he would be next; his wife gently rebuked him. The company noticed the baron’s melancholy and afterward would say, “Monsieur du Guenic was sad to-night.” The phrase “The Baron du Guenic fails” opened many conversations.“How is Thisbe?” asked Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel. “The poor thing is like her master,” the chevalier replied, mimicking the dog and exposing his hand—an attempt by a neighbor to see if he had Mistigris or the trump. The baroness observed the rector’s nose grow pale—his sign of holding Mistigris—because of the pleasure that card gave him.“You had company today,” said the chevalier. “Yes, a cousin of my brother-in-law,” she replied; she told of the marriage of the Comtesse de Kergarouet, a Demoiselle de Fontaine. The chevalier, who knew the admiral, recognized the name. She then astonished them with tales about Mademoiselle des Touches—so strange she could hardly believe them; if true, Calyste would not be so constantly with her. The baron pricked up his ears.But Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel suddenly had Mistigris and could not continue. “Your play, monsieur le baron,” she said. Zephirine returned to knitting; the rector, informed about Calyste and Mademoiselle des Touches, refrained. The baron inquired again: “What is so extraordinary?” “She smokes,” Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel said. The chevalier commented dryly. Asked about her property, the old maid said she was squandering it.“The game is mine!” cried the baroness; she had a long run of trumps and Mistigris. The victory horrified Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel, who forgot Calyste and Mademoiselle des Touches for a moment. By nine only the baroness and rector remained; the others had gone to bed. The chevalier, as usual, accompanied Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel home, commenting on Zephirine’s delight in engulfing her gains. He had also noticed the baroness’ abstraction.At Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel’s door she confided the cause: “Calyste is lost unless we marry him promptly. He loves Mademoiselle des Touches, an actress!” “Then send for Charlotte.” “I have; my sister will receive my letter tomorrow,” she replied.From this sketch of a normal evening one may imagine the hubbub excited throughout Guerande by any stranger’s arrival, stay, or passage.When silence came from the baron’s and his sister’s rooms, the baroness looked at the rector, who toyed pensively with the counters. “I see that you begin to share my anxiety about Calyste,” she said. “Did you notice Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel’s displeased looks to-night?” he asked. She had the best intentions for Calyste; she loved him as though he were her son. His conduct in the Vendée beside his father and Madame's praises of his devotion had increased that affection. Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel intends to execute a deed of gift leaving her property to whichever niece Calyste marries. The rector acknowledged the mother’s hope for a richer Irish match but said it was wise to have two strings to the bow—Pen-Hoel’s savings and well-managed land were not to be despised. He then described Mademoiselle des Touches’ life: a stage-woman and writer, frequenter of theatres and artists, squandering her fortune on pamphleteers, painters, and musicians, under the false name Camille Maupin. She had turned Les Touches into an improper paradise of dissipation; more wine was drunk there in weeks than in all Guerande in a year. Last year the Demoiselles Bougniol let lodgings to men suspected of being Blues; they sang wicked songs and spent time at Les Touches. And this is whom Calyste adores; he would gallop to Nantes for an infamous book but not for the Church. She is not a royalist; if Calyste were called to fight for the cause and she wanted to keep him, the chevalier might let his own father go without him.“Oh, no!” exclaimed the baroness. The rector feared to test Calyste; the mother would suffer too much. “I will see Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel tomorrow,” he promised. “Don’t be too uneasy. The harm may not be as great as they say. I will find out the truth. Jacqueline trusts me. Calyste is our child and pupil—he will not let the devil inveigle him nor disturb his family’s plans. One fault is not vice.”“You are only informing me of details,” said the baroness. “Was I not the first to notice the change in my Calyste? A mother keenly feels being second in her son’s heart. This crisis is a trial of motherhood. I had hoped Calyste would love someone noble and beautiful—not a stage-player, a masquerader, an author who feigns sentiments, a creature who will deceive and make him unhappy! She has had adventures—”“With several men,” said the rector. “Yet this impious creature was born in Brittany! She dishonors her land. I shall preach upon her next Sunday.”“Don’t do that!” cried the baroness. “The peasants and paludiers might rush to Les Touches. Calyste is Breton; something dreadful might happen if he defended her as he would the Blessed Virgin.”“It is now ten o’clock; I must bid you good-night,” said the abbe, lighting his lantern. “Who could have told me that a young man brought up by you, trained by me to Christian ideas, a fervent Catholic, a child who has lived like a spotless lamb, would plunge into such mire?”“But is it certain?” asked the mother. “How could any woman help loving Calyste?”“What proof other than her staying on at Les Touches? In twenty-four years since coming of age she has never stayed so long; her visits were formerly few and short.”“A woman over forty!” exclaimed the baroness. “In Ireland they say such a woman is the most dangerous mistress for a young man.”“As to that I have no knowledge,” replied the rector, “and shall die in ignorance.”“And I, too, alas!” said the baroness. “I wish I had loved deeply, so I might counsel and comfort Calyste.”The rector did not cross the little court-yard alone; the baroness accompanied him to the gate, hoping to hear Calyste’s step. They heard the rector’s cautious tread grow fainter and the parsonage door close behind him.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################V. CALYSTEThe poor mother returned to the salon deeply distressed to find the whole town aware of what she had thought known to her alone. She trimmed the lamp-wick, took up her worsted-work, and awaited Calyste. The baroness hoped to induce her son to come home earlier and spend less time with Mademoiselle des Touches, but Calyste’s visits to Les Touches grew more frequent and later; the night before he returned at midnight.Absorbed in anxious thought, the baroness stitched rapidly. Piqued by a curiosity common to pure women, she wondered what charms these daughters of Baal possessed to draw men away from family and duty. Sometimes she longed to meet Mademoiselle des Touches and judge her soberly. Her mind measured the evils the age’s innovations—so dangerous for young souls, the rector had warned—might work on her only child, until then guileless and beautiful with a fresh innocence.Calyste, of noble Breton and Irish blood, had been raised with the utmost care. After infancy and early guidance by his mother, she entrusted him to the rector of Guerande for a thorough Christian education; thus his instruction was that of a seminary—Latin, Greek, cautious history, geography, and a limited selection of French writers. He knew little of modern literature or current science. The Church had continued the maternal work: Calyste remained a fervent Catholic, neither over-pious nor ridiculous.For this noble son the baroness desired a quiet life. She expected to inherit a few thousand pounds from an aunt; combined with the Guenics’ present fortune she hoped to find a wife bringing a suitable income. Charlotte de Kergarouet, a rich Irish girl, or any decent heiress would have suited her; she regarded marriage pragmatically, like those around her, ignorant of passionate love. Thus the thought that Mademoiselle des Touches threatened their prudential plans filled Fanny with foreboding.Hearing midnight strike with tears, the baroness feared Calyste might stay away; but his step soon sounded in the lane and she opened the gate joyfully.“Oh!” cried Calyste, grieved, “my darling mother, why did you sit up for me? I have a pass-key and the tinder-box.”“You know very well, my child, that I cannot sleep when you are out,” she said, kissing him.In the salon she studied his face for signs of the evening. Calyste stirred maternal pride: except for the dark, energetic eyes from his father, he resembled his mother—golden hair, lovable mouth, curving fingers, soft white skin; though slightly girlish in look, his strength was Herculean. His fatigue looked like painful thought, but his mother blamed passing excitement. She feared that the “beautiful pure cheeks” would belong to another woman and that passion would tarnish her son’s innocence.It may seem strange that in a family living on limited means the son had a velvet coat and the mother a velvet gown, but Fanny had wealthy London relations who sent gifts. Several sisters, married into wealth, took interest in Calyste and hoped to find him an heiress.“You stayed at Les Touches longer than last night, my dear one,” said the mother.“Yes, dear mother,” he answered, offering no explanation. The curt reply clouded her brow. Mothers who love as Fanny did tremble before their sons; they perceive what love will take and yet feel joy in their children’s happiness.“Go to bed, dear child,” she said, repressing her tears.The next day Calyste slept until mid-day; his mother would not wake him. Mariotte served breakfast in his bed. At one o’clock the family sat in the salon to read and prepare for the three-o’clock dinner. The baroness read the Quotidienne; as she finished a paragraph she heard Calyste’s steps above and dropped the paper.“Calyste must be going to dine again at Les Touches; he has dressed himself.”“He amuses himself, the dear boy,” said the old sister.Mariotte appeared.“What is it?” she said; “anything wanted?”“The chevalier dines at Les Touches; don’t cook the fish.”“But we are not sure yet,” said the baroness.“You seem annoyed, sister; I know it by your voice.” Fanny informed them that Monsieur Grimont had heard grave charges against Mademoiselle des Touches, who had so changed Calyste. The baron, amused, tried to dismiss it.“Suppose Monsieur le chevalier does love Mademoiselle des Touches, where’s the harm?” said Mariotte. “She has thirty thousand francs a year and is very handsome.”“A Guenic marry a des Touches!” exclaimed the old baron. “A des Touches could hardly be an actress,” added the old man in the tone of ancestral pride. The baroness answered that Camille Maupin wrote plays and books; the baron, surprised, shrugged. Mariotte, not inquisitive, left when Calyste entered.“Are you going again to Les Touches, my Calyste?” the baroness asked.“Les Touches is not respectable. Its mistress leads an irregular life; she will corrupt our Calyste. Already Camille Maupin has made him read many books; you knew all that and never said one word to your best friends!”“The chevalier is discreet,” said his father.“Too discreet,” said the jealous mother, noting a flush on her son’s brow.“My dear mother,” said Calyste, kneeling beside her, “I didn’t think it necessary to publish my defeat. Mademoiselle des Touches, or Camille Maupin, rejected my love more than eighteen months ago during her last stay at Les Touches. She laughed at me gently, saying she might very well be my mother and that a woman of forty loving a minor were a crime against nature; she tried to comfort me and offered her friendship nobly. She has more heart than talent; she is generous. I am now her child. On her return lately, hearing she loves another, I have resigned myself. Do not repeat calumnies against her. Camille is an artist; she has genius; she leads an exceptional life not to be judged like ordinary lives.”“My child,” said Fanny, “nothing can excuse a woman for not conducting herself as the Church requires. She fails in her duty to God and society by abjuring the gentle tenets of her sex. A woman sins even in going to a theatre; but to write the impieties that actors repeat, to roam the world, first with an enemy to the Pope, and then with a musician—Calyste, you can never persuade me such acts are deeds of faith, hope, or charity. Her fortune was given by God to do good; what good does she do with hers?”Calyste sprang up. “Mother,” he said, “Camille is my friend; I cannot hear her spoken of this way; I would give my very life for her.”“Your life!” said the baroness. “Your life is our life, the life of all of us.” The old sister, knitting rapidly, declared that education had caused the change: “Educated people did the evil—be educated if you choose!”“Calyste,” she added later in a voice from her heart, “your father has never opened books; he speaks Breton, he fought for God and king.” The family’s conflicting views left the baroness agitated. Calyste whispered that it was impossible to explain now but promised a night’s talk after which she would bless Mademoiselle des Touches. He took his leave. The baron offered to visit Les Touches himself and report; the remark had no savor yet hinted at future confrontation.VI. BIOGRAPHY OF CAMILLE MAUPINGuerande had seen Calyste frequently going to Les Touches and concluded Mademoiselle Félicité des Touches was passionately in love with the youth and worked sorceries. Rumor swelled; the rector and local notables, alarmed, related the strange life of the author who hid behind the masculine name Camille Maupin. To Abbé Grimont she was monstrous—a cross between a siren and an atheist—violating social laws that bound womanhood.Camille Maupin was the mask of Félicité des Touches, a Breton whose literary virility had long led the world to suppose her a man. The two volumes of plays published in 1822 had provoked literary revolution in the clash of classics and romantics; subsequently she wrote more plays and a novel that sustained her success. Explaining why Félicité became a man and an author, why she kept her freedom, and why she differed from Madame de Staël helps to understand this rare figure.Félicité des Touches became an orphan in 1793. Her property escaped confiscation though her father and brother were killed for the king; her mother died a few days after, leaving her to a sister, a nun of Chelles. Madame de Faucombe took the child to Faucombe, near Nantes. The populace of Nantes seized the nuns and Félicité on a false charge; Thermidor released them. Her aunt died of fear; another sister left France; the third entrusted Félicité to Monsieur de Faucombe, her great-uncle, who lived in Nantes.Monsieur de Faucombe, an old archaeologist who liked to be amused, left Félicité’s education to chance. She kept him company in his library and read widely. Thus she acquired theoretical knowledge of life without the innocence of ordinary upbringing. Her intellect was crammed with undigested learning; imagination ran riot while her personal life remained chaste. By eighteen she was as well-informed as a young man entering literary circles. This prodigious reading governed her passions more than convent life would have; it formed an unusual intellect without corrupting chastity—a surprising outcome to those who knew only convent routine.She helped Faucombe by writing three of his books; the strain affected her health, and doctors ordered horseback and society. She became an admirable horsewoman and recovered. At eighteen she entered society and produced sensation, called “the beautiful Mademoiselle des Touches.” Seeking the pleasures of a woman’s triumph, she discovered coldness toward tribute and contempt for trivial talk; resentment at family ridicule led her to cultivate coquetry and music. She studied with Steibelt at great expense and later in Paris perfected harmony, composing music for two operas whose true authorship is hidden.Provincial mediocrity soon bored her; she returned to books, piano, Beethoven, and Faucombe. In 1812, at twenty-one, Faucombe gave her the guardianship accounts and she took control of fifteen thousand francs from Les Touches, twelve thousand from Faucombe, and a capital saved during her minority. She learned finance and provincial administration, invested wisely at the right moment, and by young adulthood had fifty thousand francs a year to invest—force of will equal to a man of thirty. Habits of criticism enabled sober judgments about art and public affairs. Faucombe’s later illness kept her until his death as Europe convulsed under Napoleon.A Royalist, she hastened to Paris with the Bourbons’ return. Political commotions preserved her mind from personal entanglements. In 1815 she bought a fine house in rue Mont Blanc and settled as an independent woman. Accustomed to control her life, she adopted modes of thought and action considered masculine. By 1816 she knew nothing of marriage as a life-scheme; her superior mind refused the abdication married life requires. She valued independence and felt disgust for maternity’s duties. These facts explain Camille Maupin’s anomalies: having known no normal parental education and raised by an archaeologist among books, she had not played with dolls.Toward the close of 1817 Félicité felt a bodily lassitude and feared aging. Between marriage and love she wanted freedom; she was still susceptible to admiration. At forty she retained twenty-five’s vivacity; Nature favored her, and meditation preserved youth.A Breton type with darker features, Félicité’s face was longer than oval, like an Isis in bas-relief; olive in daylight, luminous by candlelight, it changed little with emotion. Heavy black hair, a broad forehead, and incisive eyes—pupils ringed with an orange circle—gave severity and depth to her glance. In passion her eyes could be sublime, in repose dull; silence and attentive look were her hallmarks. Her nose and mouth expressed disdain and tenderness in turn; the chin was firm, the bust ample, waist slim; arms and hands were strong and delicate. The head’s carriage was majestic, lips and nostrils offering expressive mobility. Yet overall the face seemed sad with constant meditation; she listened more than she spoke, startling by silence and a deep, attentive glance; no educated spectator could see her without thinking of Cleopatra.This anatomy of character suggested ambiguity: did she judge and not feel, or feel and judge at once? Her great mind made her often unpossessable and feared as a living symbol rather than an object of ordinary affection. Adventures of her life, partly rumored and not disavowed by Camille, reinforced such questions.Her beauty, fortune, and position aided her fame. In 1817 she opened her house to artists, authors, scientists, and publicists; her salon resembled Baron Gérard’s: men of rank mingled with distinguished talent and Parisian women. Ambitious mothers hoped to marry in; diplomats and men of distinction came for intellect’s amusements. Mademoiselle des Touches studied the comedies of passion and ambition on display among men. She despised accepted flatteries and laughed at things others took seriously.Ordinarily a woman feels, enjoys, and judges in succession; in Félicité this order reversed. Youth had been wrapped in knowledge and reflection. This transposition explained her life’s strangeness and talent and led inevitably to a first love late in life. A first secret affair had shown her the folly of valuing physical beauty as soul-beauty; she recovered with the help of a friend, a singular man—poet, critic, traveler—who took her to Italy to teach her Art and gave her frankness and literary grace. He roused her taste for German and English literature; in Rome in 1820 he deserted her for an Italian. That betrayal hardened her contempt for men and helped shape Camille Maupin’s public persona.Back in Paris, she collaborated with Conti, the great musician, for whom she wrote librettos. Her two volumes of plays made Camille Maupin famous; she then published a short novel, one of the period’s masterpieces and a dangerous work akin to “Adolphe.” Her pseudonym’s motives remain obscure; perhaps a woman’s wish to escape fame while using a man’s name for criticism played a part.Her celebrity grew through salon influence, wit, judgment, and learning. She became an authority; her sayings were quoted and she could not abandon the social role Paris bestowed. She was an exception: women admired her mind, men her beauty. Her friendships seemed platonic, and she was no noisy partisan; far from Madame de Staël’s politics, she joked about Camille Maupin, admiring rivals with composure.From 1817 to 1834 she visited Les Touches occasionally. Her first return followed the 1818 disillusion; she took lodgings in the village to contemplate herself, then had furniture sent from Paris to Les Touches. The public’s curiosity about her luxury filled the porter’s purse; visitors came. Later, after returning from Italy with Conti, appearances excited less interest. By the time of this story, news of her double existence had spread in Guerande; letters addressed to Camille Maupin at Les Touches arrived. In a region so Catholic and prejudiced, her singular life caused rumors that alarmed Abbé Grimont; such a life seemed improper to him.During her present stay at Les Touches she was not alone: Claude Vignon, a scornful, powerful writer, was her guest. She had received him among many authors and artists for seven years. Knowing his laziness and penury, she still appeared inclined to marry him—ambition, dread of solitary old age, and desire to secure a superior man with her fortune may have influenced this. Yet she was misleading both Calyste and Claude; her heart was undergoing the most violent convulsion possible for a soul as strong as hers. Too late she saw life lighted by youthful love, shining again in a heart she thought past such fires.Let us now see Camille’s convent where this was happening.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A few hundred yards from Guérande the soil of Brittany ends and the salt‑marshes and dunes begin. We descend into a desert left by the sea, by a rugged road through a ravine that has never seen a carriage. This desert holds waste tracts, ponds of unequal size, muddy banks where salt is made, and a little arm of the sea that separates the mainland from Croisic. Geographically Croisic is really a peninsula; but as it clings to Brittany only by beaches connecting it with the village of Batz (barren quicksands hard to cross), it may be more correct to call it an island.Where the road from Croisic to Guérande turns off from the main road stands a country‑house, surrounded by a large garden, remarkable for its trimmed and twisted pines, some shaped like sun‑shades, others stripped of branches, their reddened trunks where bark has peeled. These trees, victims of hurricanes and growing against wind and tide, prepare the mind for the strange sight of marshes and dunes that resemble a stiffened ocean. The house, of slaty stone with granite courses, has little architecture: a plain wall with regularly spaced windows, small leaded panes on the ground floor and larger panes above. Attics stretch under an enormously high pointed roof with two gables and two large dormers on each side. Under each gable’s triangular point a circular window opens its cyclopic eye, one facing the sea, the other Guérande. One façade looks toward the road to Guérande, the other toward the desert that ends at Croisic; beyond that little town is the open sea. A brook escapes through an opening in the park wall that skirts the road to Croisic, crosses the road, and is lost in the sands.The gray tones of the house harmonize with the scene it overlooks. The park is an oasis in the surrounding desert; at its entrance a mud‑hut shelters custom‑house officials. This house without much land (most of the estate is in Guérande) derives income from the marshes and some outlying farms—over ten thousand francs a year. Such is the fief of Les Touches, from which the Revolution lopped feudal rights. The paludiers, however, still call it “the château,” and might still say “seigneur” if the fief were not now in the female line. When Félicité set about restoring Les Touches she, artist that she was, did not alter the desolate exterior that gives it the look of a prison. The sole change was an entrance gate enlivened by two brick columns supporting an arch under which carriages pass into the court where she planted trees.The ground floor follows the arrangement of nearly all country houses built a hundred years before. It was evidently erected on the ruins of some old castle formerly perched there. A large paneled entrance‑hall, turned by Félicité into a billiard‑room, opens into an immense salon with six windows and the dining‑room. The kitchen communicates with the dining‑room through an office. Camille has shown noble simplicity in arranging this floor, avoiding splendid decoration. The salon, painted gray, is furnished in old mahogany with green silk coverings. The dining‑room contains four great mahogany buffets, horsehair chairs, and superb Audran engravings in mahogany frames. The old wooden staircase with heavy balusters is covered with a green carpet.Above are two suites separated by the staircase. Mademoiselle des Touches has taken the one looking toward the sea and marshes, arranging it with a small salon, a large chamber, and two cabinets, one a dressing‑room, the other a study and writing‑room. The other suite she has made into guest apartments, each with a bedroom, antechamber, and cabinet. The servants’ rooms are in the attic. The guest rooms are furnished with what is necessary, no more. A certain fantastic luxury is reserved for her own apartment. In that sombre, melancholy habitation, looking upon the sombre landscape, she gathered the most fantastic creations of art she could find. The little salon is hung with Gobelin tapestry framed in marvelously carved oak. The windows are draped with heavy silken hangings of a past age—brocade shot with crimson and gold against green and yellow—gathered into mighty pleats and trimmed with fringes, cords, and tassels fit for a church. This salon contains a chest or cabinet worth seven or eight thousand francs, a carved ebony table, a secretary with many drawers inlaid with ivory arabesques bought in Venice, and other noble Gothic furniture. Here too are pictures and articles of choice workmanship bought in 1818, when no one suspected their ultimate value. Her bedroom is strictly Louis XV: a carved white bedstead with Cupids scattering flowers, a canopy, blue silk hangings; the Pompadour dressing‑table; bits of singular furniture—a duchesse, a chaise‑longue, a stiff little sofa—silk window‑curtains lined with pink satin, tied with silken ropes, and a Savonnerie carpet; in short, the elegant dainties in which eighteenth‑century women lived and loved.The study, entirely contemporary, contrasts with the Louis XV gallantries and contains a charming collection of mahogany furniture; it resembles a boudoir. The bookshelves are full, but trivialities of a woman’s existence encumber it; amid them an inquisitive eye perceives, with uneasy surprise, pistols, a narghile, a riding‑whip, a hammock, a rifle, a man’s blouse, tobacco, pipes, a knapsack—a bizarre combination that paints Félicité.Every great soul entering that room would be struck by the peculiar beauty of the landscape spreading its broad savanna beyond the park, the last vegetation on the continent. The melancholy squares of water, divided by little paths of white salt crust along which the salt‑makers pass—dressed in white—to rake and gather the salt into mulons; a space whose saline exhalations prevent birds from crossing and stifle botanical effort; sands where the eye is soothed only by one hardy plant bearing rosy flowers and the Chartreux pansy; that lake of salt water, the sandy dunes, the view of Croisic, a miniature town afloat like Venice on the sea; and the mighty ocean tossing its foaming fringe upon the granite rocks—this sight uplifts and saddens; the sublime produces a regretful yearning for things unknown yet perceived by the soul on far‑off heights. These wild harmonies are for great spirits and great sorrows only.This desert scene, where at times the sun’s rays reflected by water and sands whiten the village of Batz and ripple pitilessly on the roofs of Croisic, filled Camille’s dreaming mind for days. She seldom looked to the cool, refreshing groves and meadows about Guérande. Her soul struggled under a horrible inward anguish.No sooner did Calyste see the vanes of the two gables rising beyond roadside furze and distorted pines than the air seemed lighter to him; Guérande was a prison; his life was at Les Touches. Who will not understand the attraction to a youth in his position? A love like Chérubin’s had flung him at the feet of a person who was a great and grand thing to him before he thought of her as a woman, and it had survived Félicité’s repeated and inexplicable refusals. This sentiment—more a need of loving than love itself—had not escaped Camille’s analysis; hence, possibly, her refusal—a generosity unperceived, of course, by Calyste.At Les Touches the ravished eyes of the ignorant young countryman beheld the riches of a new world; he heard another language. He listened to the poetic sounds of the finest music, that surpassing music of the nineteenth century, in which melody and harmony blend or struggle—music in which song and instrumentation reach a new perfection. He saw works of modern painters, the French school, now heir to Italy, Spain, and Flanders, where talent has become common and hearts, weary of talent, call aloud for genius. He read works of imagination, creations of modern literature that impressed his unused heart. In short, the great Nineteenth Century appeared to him in all its collective magnificence, its critical spirit, its desires for renovation in all directions, and its vast efforts—nearly all on the scale of the giant who cradled the century in his banners and sang hymns with the lullaby of cannon.Initiated by Félicité into all these things, which sometimes escape their creators, Calyste gratified at Les Touches the taste for the glorious, powerful at his age, and that artless admiration, the first love of adolescence, always irritated by criticism. It is natural that flame should rise! He listened to charming Parisian raillery, graceful satire revealing French wit and qualities of the French mind, and awakened in him a thousand ideas that might have slumbered forever in the soft torpor of his family life. For him, Mademoiselle des Touches was the mother of his intellect. She was so kind; a woman is always adorable to a man in whom she inspires love, even when she seems not to share it.At the present time Félicité was giving him music lessons. To him the grand apartments on the lower floor and her private rooms above, so coquettish, so artistic, were vivified by a light, a spirit, a supernatural atmosphere, strange and undefinable. The modern world with its poesy sharply contrasted with the dull patriarchal world of Guérande; the two systems faced one another.
