On one side all the thousand developments of Art, on the other the sameness of uncivilized Brittany. No one will therefore ask why the poor lad, bored like his mother with the pleasures of mouche, quivered as he approached the house, rang the bell, and crossed the court. Such emotions do not assail a mature man, trained to life’s ups and downs.As the door opened, Calyste, hearing the piano, supposed Camille was in the salon; but in the billiard‑hall he no longer heard it. He thought she must be playing on a small upright piano in her little salon. He ran up the stairs where the thick carpet smothered his steps; but he slowed as he neared the top, perceiving something unusual about the music. Félicité was playing for herself only; she was communing with her own being.Instead of entering, the young man sat on a Gothic seat covered with green velvet on the landing beneath a window artistically framed in carved wood. Nothing was more mysteriously melancholy than Camille’s improvisation; it seemed like the cry of a soul de profundis—from the depths of a grave! The heart of the young lover recognized the cry of despairing love, the prayer of a hidden plaint, the groan of repressed affliction. Camille had varied, modified, and lengthened the introduction to the cavatina “Mercy for thee, mercy for me!” which is nearly the whole of the fourth act of Robert le Diable. She now suddenly sang the words in a heart‑rending manner, and then as suddenly interrupted herself. Calyste entered, and saw the reason. Poor Camille Maupin! poor Félicité! She turned to him with a face bathed in tears, took out her handkerchief, dried them, and said simply, without affectation, “Good‑morning.” She was beautiful in her morning gown. On her head was one of those red chenille nets, much worn in those days, through which coils of black hair shone, escaping here and there. A short upper garment like a Greek peplum revealed cambric trousers with embroidered frills and the prettiest of Turkish slippers, red and gold.“What is the matter?” cried Calyste.“He has not returned,” she replied, going to a window and looking out upon the sands, the sea, and the marshes.This answer explained all. Camille was awaiting Claude Vignon.“You are anxious about him?” asked Calyste.“Yes,” she answered, with a sadness the lad was too naive to analyze.He started to leave the room.“Where are you going?” she asked.“To find him,” he replied.“Dear child!” she said, taking his hand and drawing him toward her with one of those moist glances which to a youthful soul are the best recompense. “You are distracted! Where could you find him on that wide shore?”“I will find him.”“Your mother would be in mortal terror. Stay. Besides, I choose it,” she said, seating him on the sofa. “Don’t pity me. The tears you see are the tears a woman likes to shed. We have a faculty that is not in man—that of abandoning ourselves to our nervous nature and driving our feelings to an extreme. By imagining certain situations and encouraging imagination we end in tears, and sometimes in serious illness or disorder. The fancies of women are not actions of the mind; they are of the heart. You have come just in time; solitude is bad for me. I am not the dupe of his professed desire to go to Croisic and see the rocks and dunes and salt‑marshes without me. He meant to leave us alone together; he is jealous, or rather he pretends jealousy, and you are young, you are handsome.”“Why not have told me this before? What must I do? Must I stay away?” asked Calyste, with difficulty restraining his tears, one rolling down his cheek and touching Félicité deeply.“You are an angel!” she cried. Then she gaily sang the “Stay! stay!” of Matilde in Guillaume Tell, taking all gravity from that magnificent answer of the princess to her subject. “He only wants to make me think he loves me better than he really does,” she said. “He knows how much I desire his happiness,” she went on, looking attentively at Calyste. “Perhaps he feels humiliated to be inferior to me there. Perhaps he suspects you and means to surprise us. But even if his only crime is to take his pleasure without me, and not associate me with the ideas this new place gives him, is that not enough? Ah! I am no more loved by that great brain than I was by the musician, by the poet, by the soldier! Sterne is right; names signify much; mine is a bitter sarcasm. I shall die without finding in any man the love that fills my heart, the poesy that is in my soul—”She stopped, her arms pendant, her head lying back on the cushions, her eyes fixed on a pattern of the carpet. The pain of great minds has something grandiose and imposing; it reveals a vast extent of soul which the spectator’s thought extends still further. Such souls share privileges of royalty whose affections belong to a people and so affect a world.“Why did you reject my—” said Calyste; but he could not end his sentence. Camille’s beautiful hand laid upon his eloquently interrupted him.“Nature changed her laws in granting me a dozen years of youth beyond my due,” she said. “I rejected your love from egotism. Sooner or later the difference in our ages must have parted us. I am thirteen years older than he, and even that is too much.”“You will be beautiful at sixty,” cried Calyste, heroically.“God grant it,” she answered, smiling. “Besides, dear child, I want to love. In spite of his cold heart, his lack of imagination, his cowardly indifference, and the envy which consumes him, I believe there is greatness behind those tatters; I hope to galvanize that heart, to save him from himself, to attach him to me. Alas! I have a clear‑seeing mind, but a blind heart.”She was terrible in her knowledge of herself. She suffered and analyzed her feelings as Cuvier and Dupuytren explained to friends the fatal advance of their disease and the progress of death in their bodies. Camille Maupin knew the passion within her as those men of science knew their own anatomy.“I have brought him here to judge him, and he is already bored,” she continued. “He pines for Paris, I tell him; the nostalgia of criticism is on him; he has no author to pluck, no system to undermine, no poet to drive to despair, and he dares not commit some debauch in this house that might lift for a moment the burden of his ennui. Alas! my love is not real enough, perhaps, to soothe his brain; I don’t intoxicate him! Make him drunk at dinner tonight and I shall know if I am right. I will say I am ill, and stay in my room.”Calyste turned scarlet from neck to forehead; even his ears burned.“Oh! forgive me,” she cried. “How can I heedlessly deprave your girlish innocence! Forgive me, Calyste—” She paused. “There are some superb, consistent natures who say at a certain age: ‘If I had my life to live over again, I would do the same things.’ I who do not think myself weak, I say, ‘I would be a woman like your mother, Calyste.’ To have a Calyste, oh! what happiness! I could be humble and submissive—and yet I have done no harm except to myself. But alas! dear child, a woman cannot stand alone in society except in a primitive state. Affections not in harmony with social or natural laws, affections that are not obligatory, in short, escape us. Suffering for suffering, as well be useful where we can. What care I for those children of my cousin Faucombe? I have not seen them these twenty years, and they are married to merchants. You are my son, who have never cost me the miseries of motherhood; I shall leave you my fortune and make you happy—at least, so far as money can do so, dear treasure of beauty and grace that nothing should ever change or blast.”“You would not take my love,” said Calyste, “and I shall return your fortune to your heirs.”“Child!” answered Camille, in a guttural voice, letting tears roll down her cheeks. “Will nothing save me from myself?” she added presently.“You said you had a history to tell me, and a letter to—” said the generous youth, wishing to divert her thoughts from her grief; but she did not let him finish.“You are right to remind me of that. I will be an honest woman before all else. I will sacrifice no one—Yes, it was too late yesterday, but today we have time,” she said cheerfully. “I will keep my promise; and while I tell you that history I will sit by the window and watch the road to the marshes.”Calyste arranged a great Gothic chair for her near the window and opened one sash. Camille Maupin, who shared the oriental taste of her illustrious sister‑author, took a magnificent Persian narghile given her by an ambassador. She filled the nipple with patchouli, cleaned the bochettino, perfumed the goose‑quill which she attached to the mouthpiece and used only once, set fire to the yellow leaves, placed the vase with its long neck enameled in blue and gold at some distance, and rang the bell for tea.“Will you have cigarettes?—Ah! I am always forgetting that you do not smoke. Purity such as yours is so rare! The hand of Eve herself, fresh from the hand of her Maker, is alone innocent enough to stroke your cheek.”Calyste colored; sitting on a stool at Camille’s feet, he did not see the deep emotion that seemed for a moment to overcome her.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“I promised you this tale of the past, and here it is,” said Camille.She told Calyste the letter came from the Marquise de Rochefide. The Rochefide family, less ancient than the Casterans, sought a prestigious match for their son; the Comtesse de Montcornet recommended Mademoiselle Béatrix-Maximilienne-Rose de Casteran, the youngest daughter of a father who intended to marry off his two daughters without dowries to preserve his estate for his son. Béatrix, raised at the château de Casteran, had married in 1828 at twenty. Camille called her what provincials call original—independent, enthusiastic, absorbed by art—a temperament dangerous for a woman, since only men have the support to keep balance at those edges.The dowager de Casteran approved the match. Rochefide seemed superior to Béatrix and the marriage linked his family to other nobles; his son had nearly gained a peerage under Charles X, lost after the July revolution. Rochefide, vain and foolish, mistook Béatrix’s refinement and reserve for frigidity and soon returned to a bachelor’s life. He assumed a proud, artistic wife would content herself with salon pleasures; but he combined vanity with a petty, lurking jealousy and sudden cruelty. Camille analyzed how this temperament and Béatrix’s admiration bred rivalry: Béatrix wanted her own salon and tried to draw Camille’s circle away, attracting only superficial acquaintances. Camille thought Béatrix thirsted for celebrity yet conceded the marquise had grandeur of soul, regal pride, clear ideas, and uncommon facility in art, theology, and music—though sometimes affected by arcane learning.Camille described Béatrix’s appearance: an ethereal blonde, slender and erect, with a long, pointed face whose skin varied; a bold forehead, pale sea-green eyes, a clever nose, a disdainful mouth, pale cheeks, a somewhat heavy chin, an exquisite waist, beautiful shoulders, but an underdeveloped bust. Her carriage and nobility of bearing made her striking in certain attire; in cherry velvet and lace she could seem divine. Camille admitted Béatrix was not faultlessly beautiful, yet left an ineffaceable impression.Félicité showed Calyste a painting by Mieris; Camille joked about the charms of blondes and warned Calyste not to fall for Béatrix from her portrait. Béatrix, Camille said, was severe in line, hard and elegant, sometimes revealing Southern passion; an angelic flash would appear and vanish. For three years after her marriage she judged the world from her height; the July revolution and social changes unsettled her. She embraced some new intellectual currents while wishing to protect the nobility; finding limited place for individual superiority, she chose personal happiness over fading social power.At Camille’s salon she met Gennaro Conti, a celebrated Neapolitan composer whose voice people adored as a soul rather than mere sound. Béatrix fell madly in love and took him from Camille. The act, painful for Camille, was fair play: Béatrix confided to Camille and made her arbiter of her choice. Camille admired Béatrix’s conduct—she preserved her dignity as a marquise and displayed a grandeur in womanhood that men often cannot appreciate. Yet Camille confessed she had been faithful to Conti in admiration and curiosity; she had tested him, knowing him charming but vain and hypocritical in love. Conti, she argued, was a consummate charlatan of feeling—insincere, theatrical, insatiable for applause; he posed as fanatically devoted to art while often self-centered. His warmth and chivalry deceive many women; Camille had probed and seen his false sentiment, and Conti now hated her for seeing through him. She had told Béatrix nothing.Camille had even received Conti and Béatrix together in her salon to keep the affair discreet, forcing Conti to dissimulate admirably. Béatrix soon told Camille she was leaving for Italy with Conti. Camille was not surprised: Béatrix felt united to him and, weary of social constraints, chose to escape into love. Conti was elated; Camille warned that he loved himself more than any woman. After a scene of fury he borrowed money from Camille for the journey. Béatrix left Rochefide a letter and went to Italy for two years; she wrote charming letters to Camille, the only woman she trusted. Conti, short on funds, accepted an offer to write a French opera, and Béatrix wrote from Genoa that they might soon return to Paris. Camille handed Calyste Béatrix’s letter.Claude Vignon then entered unexpectedly, casting an ominous presence. He was thirty-seven, prematurely bald, his once-handsome face grown haggard; he resembled a youthful Raphael but now his features had thickened and his expressions were worn by solitude. His pale blue eyes were clouded; his face suggested deep thought but little action. Camille described him as a critic whose intellect penetrated everything but whose energy failed in outward life: he demolishes but does not create. He was simple yet wily, indulgent in excesses, prone to paralysis—an arch-critic who contemplated and did not act. Camille liked the idea of making Claude more active; he fancied himself a politician and writer but habitually let opportunity slip away.Small talk followed, with veiled jests about entrances “by the door” or window-scaling as lover’s badges. Claude’s sardonic manner disturbed Calyste, who felt uneasy. Claude interrupted Calyste’s earnestness about the letter with indifferent, mocking comments about love, angering the young man. Calyste, rash and sincere, was reproached by Camille and Félicité, who tried to shield him. He left in a storm of emotion, kissed Camille’s hand, and wept; Claude smirked that the youth would love much. Félicité announced that Madame de Rochefide might arrive, perhaps with Conti, which interested Claude, who had seen a vessel offshore near Croisic that could bring them.Calyste, returning home, brooded on Béatrix’s letter and Camille’s portrait. At home, his mother Fanny, Aunt Zephirine, and servants created a cozy domestic scene. Calyste unfolded the letter again and imagined Béatrix as Camille had described. He told his mother how Béatrix had opened his mind, teaching him about Paris, music, literature, Italian, and social nuances; she had become, in effect, the mother of his intellect. Fanny feared the change but, moved by her son’s devotion, softened. Aunt Zephirine urged a prudent, bourgeois path: marriage to a good Breton girl, a tranquil life to secure the estate and heirs; practical counsel that contrasted with Calyste’s romantic ardor. Calyste flared against such domestic plans, exclaiming he could not be confined to a married monk’s life; his imagination had been inflamed by Parisian civilization, books, and women; he longed for passion, beauty, freedom. His mother, seeing his earnestness, embraced him and blessed his pursuit of happiness while pleading prudence.After dinner Calyste returned to Les Touches under pretext of returning the letter. Claude Vignon and Félicité remained at table; Félicité indulged Claude’s gourmandise and the dinner displayed Camille’s Parisian taste, a contrast to the Guénic family’s simpler comforts. Calyste presented the letter; Claude read only the first page and observed that Béatrix’s love for Conti seemed bound more by pride than passion: “She is held to Conti now by pride only; she is condemned to love him quand même.” Camille protested that Béatrix’s nature was too grand to change, and reminded them Conti would accompany her.Claude’s cynical remarks pierced Calyste. When Claude casually suggested Béatrix was the right match for Calyste, the youth was wounded. Félicité admonished Claude not to plant dangerous ideas in the impressionable young man. Claude, enjoying wordplay and satire, pursued discussions about youth’s infatuations with older women—an observation that both flattered and ensnared Camille and softened Calyste. He argued that young men often adore older, semi-dowager women because they possess experience, savoir-faire, dignity, and absolute devotion that captivates youth: their love, he said, is vast and unrelieved by vanity—their passions are deep and memorable. Félicité, pressed by Camille’s evident pleasure in the praise, felt both flattered and wary.The evening continued with wit and anecdotes; Claude and Félicité described Parisian life to Calyste, who was charmed—intellect had immense seduction for someone “all heart.” Claude predicted the Marquise and Conti might land at Croisic the next day, having possibly come on the Danish vessel he’d seen. Camille colored at the suggestion.Calyste arrived late that night to his anxious mother, insisting he’d never had so delightful an evening: genius, he rhapsodized, could create life and choice among women. His mother tenderly kissed him and reminded him that love might suffice. Calyste pledged he would love in her stead for those who might have adored him. Fanny counseled him to choose noble women if he must pursue love, and bade him be prudent though she could not bind his heart.Thus the scene closed with Calyste torn between domestic duty and the passionate horizons opened by Béatrix’s letter and Camille’s salon—a youth intoxicated by ideas, love, and the promise of Paris, watched over by a family that loved him and feared for his future.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################What young man full of restrained life and emotion would not go to Croisic to see Madame de Rochefide land and to examine her incognito? Calyste surprised his parents by leaving at dawn without waiting for breakfast. He moved with an agility almost new to him and kept close to the walls of Les Touches lest he be seen. He was ashamed of his ardor and afraid of being laughed at; Félicité and Vignon were so perspicacious. Young men imagine their foreheads transparent.At the shore, by a stone embankment before a shelter for bad crossings to Croisic, two boats arrived from Croisic laden with trunks and chests that marked travellers of distinction. In one boat a maid in a straw bonnet with a green veil and a man—only a maid and a footman—were present.“Are you going over to Croisic, Monsieur Calyste?” a boatman asked; annoyed at being called by name, Calyste shook his head.A chest covered with tarred cloth labeled MME. LA MARQUISE DE ROCHEFIDE caught his eye. The name shone before him like a talisman; he fancied there was something fateful in it. In the desert of his newly awakened desires his fancy fastened on the first woman presented. Béatrix thus inherited the love Camille had rejected.As the luggage landed Calyste glanced toward Croisic for another boat that might show him Béatrix—already the marble statue on which to hang his garlands. He stood with arms folded, lost in meditation. Remarkably, we often subject ourselves to sentiments by our own volition, deliberately bind ourselves; chance has less to do with it than we think.“Are you expecting someone to meet you?” the maid asked.“We were told they would fetch us from Les Touches. If they don’t come,” she added, “I don’t know how Madame la marquise will manage to dress for dinner. You had better go and find Mademoiselle des Touches. Oh! what a land of savages!”Calyste felt he had blundered.“Is your mistress going to Les Touches?” he inquired.“She is there; Mademoiselle came for her this morning at seven o’clock. Ah! here come the horses.”Calyste started toward Guérande with the lightness of a chamois, doubling to avoid retracing his steps or meeting servants of Les Touches. He met two of them on the narrow causeway through the marsh.“Shall I go in, or shall I not?” he thought when the pines of Les Touches came in sight. He was afraid, and continued to Guérande, where he finished his excursion on the mall and reflected.“She has no idea of my agitation,” he said to himself.His capricious thoughts were grapnels fastening his heart to the marquise. He had known none of these mysterious terrors and joys with Camille. Such vague emotions rise like poems in the untutored soul. Warmed by the first fires of imagination, souls like his pass through preparation and may reach in silence the heights of love without having met the object of their efforts.Presently Calyste saw the Chevalier du Halga and Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoël walking together on the mall. He heard them say his name and slipped aside to listen. The chevalier advised Pen-Hoël, if Charlotte de Kergarouët came, to keep her four or five months so the girl might fall in love with Calyste by daily contact, allowing them to marry next winter. “If you say two words about it to Charlotte she’ll say four to Calyste,” he added; “a girl of sixteen can certainly carry off the prize from a woman of forty.”The old people turned away and Calyste heard no more. Remembering what his mother had told him, he saw Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoël’s intention. The idea of a girl thus imposed upon him sent him with greater ardor into his imaginary love. He had never fancied Charlotte; he now felt repugnance at the thought. Calyste, raised in modest means and accustomed to restrained life, cared little for fortune; his thoughts belonged to the marquise. What was that little Charlotte beside the portrait Camille had drawn—more a sister than a rival?He did not return home until five. His mother handed him a letter from Mademoiselle des Touches:My dear Calyste,—The beautiful marquise has come; we count on you to help us celebrate her arrival. Claude, always sarcastic, declares that you will play Bice and that she will be Dante. It is for our honor as Bretons, and yours as a du Guenic to welcome a Casteran. Come soon.Your friend, Camille Maupin.Come as you are, without ceremony; otherwise you will put us to the blush.Calyste gave the letter to his mother and departed.“Who are the Casterans?” said Fanny to the baron.“An old Norman family, allied to William the Conqueror,” he replied. “They bear on a shield tierce fessed azure, gules and sable, a horse rearing argent, shod with gold. That beautiful creature for whom the Gars was killed at Fougères in 1800 was the daughter of a Casteran who made herself a nun and became an abbess after the Duc de Verneuil deserted her.”“And the Rochefides?”“I don’t know that name. I should have to see the blazon,” he replied.The baroness was somewhat reassured that the Marquise de Rochefide was of noble birth, but she felt her son exposed to new seductions.Calyste walked along with violent yet soft inward movements; his throat was tight, his heart swelled, his brain was full, fever possessing him. He tried to walk slowly, but some superior power hurried him. Such impetuosity is common in young men: a subtle fire makes Nature and woman seem radiant.He found the company assembled in Camille’s little salon. It was about six; the setting sun cast a ruddy light chequered by trees; twilight, beloved of women, spread through the room.“Here comes the future deputy of Brittany,” said Camille as Calyste raised the tapestry portière—“punctual as a king.”“You recognized his step just now,” said Claude to Félicité in a low voice.Calyste bowed low to the marquise, who inclined her head; he did not look at her but took the hand Claude Vignon held out and pressed it.“This is the celebrated man of whom we have talked so much, Gennaro Conti,” said Camille, not replying to Claude’s remark.She presented a man of medium height, thin, with chestnut hair, eyes almost red, and a freckled white skin, whose head so resembled Lord Byron’s that description seemed superfluous. Conti was proud of the resemblance.“I am fortunate,” he said, “to meet Monsieur du Guenic during the one day that I spend at Les Touches.”“It was for me to say that to you,” Calyste replied with a certain ease.“He is handsome as an angel,” said the marquise in an undertone to Félicité.Standing between the sofa and the two ladies, Calyste heard the words. He seated himself and looked furtively at the marquise. In the soft half-light he saw, reclining on a divan, a white serpentine shape that thrilled him. Félicité had done her friend a service; the marquise was much superior to Camille’s unflattering portrait. Had Béatrix wound blue-bells into her hair to set off her creped curls? The circles at her eyes, showing fatigue, were mother-of-pearl; her skin dazzled with fine blue veins. The delicacy of her features, diaphanous forehead, and long graceful neck lent many expressions. Her waist might be spanned by the hands; bare shoulders sparkled like a white camellia. A transparent fichu revealed the bosom's outline. A white muslin gown strewn with blue flowers, large sleeves, and shoes with crossed strings over stockings showed a charming knowledge of dress. Silver filigree ear-rings harmonized with the soft curls starred with blue-bells.Calyste’s eager eye took in these beauties at a glance and carved them on his soul. The fair Béatrix and the dark Félicité might have sat for contrasting portraits: force and feebleness of womanhood in full development. In a moment—at first sight—Calyste was seized with a love that crowned his hopes and fears. Mademoiselle des Touches had awakened his nature; Béatrix inflamed heart and thought. He felt a sudden power to conquer; he looked at Conti with an envious, gloomy rivalry he’d never felt for Claude Vignon. He strove to control himself; the inward tempest fell as soon as Béatrix’s eyes turned to him and her soft voice sounded. Dinner was announced.“Calyste, give your arm to the marquise,” said Camille, taking Conti with her right hand and Claude Vignon with her left, drawing back to let the marquise pass.The descent of that ancient staircase was to Calyste like going into battle. His heart failed him; he had nothing to say; a slight sweat pearled on his forehead and wet his back; his arm trembled so that as they reached the lowest step the marquise asked, “Is anything the matter?”“Oh!” he replied muffled, “I have never seen any woman so beautiful as you, except my mother, and I am not master of my emotions.”“But you have Camille Maupin before your eyes.”“Ah! what a difference!” said Calyste ingenuously.“Calyste,” whispered Félicité behind him, “did I not tell you that you would forget me as if I had never existed? Sit there,” she said aloud, “beside the marquise, on her right, and you, Claude, on her left. As for you, Gennaro, I retain you by me; we will keep a mutual eye on their coquetries.”Camille’s peculiar accent struck Claude Vignon, who observed her closely and never ceased to examine Mademoiselle des Touches during dinner.“Coquetries!” replied the marquise, taking off her gloves and showing her beautiful hands; “the opportunity is good, with a poet,” and she motioned to Claude, “on one side, and poesy the other.”By candlelight Béatrix seemed even more beautiful. The gleam made her forehead satiny, lit the spangles of her eyes, and turned her curls to gold. She threw back her thin gauze scarf, disclosing a beautiful nape and graceful neck—unlike Camille’s, the sign of another character.Calyste had trouble pretending to eat; nervous motions deprived him of appetite. The ardor of the heart, restrained by moral reserve, led to inward conflict—the long respectful hesitations and tender debatings of the pure-hearted. Studying furtively the marquise’s beauty, Calyste felt oppressed by her majesty; dwarfed by the hauteur of certain glances, the imposing expression of an aristocratic face, and the pride women express in slight motions. The false position she had assumed forced an imposing manner—an art of the great world fatal to vulgar women.Félicité’s eyes made Béatrix aware of the inward adoration she inspired in the youth beside her, and that it would be unworthy to encourage it. She now and then gave him repressive glances that fell upon his heart like an avalanche of snow. The unfortunate young fellow turned to Félicité with a look in which she could read the tears he was suppressing. She asked in a friendly tone why he ate nothing; the question piqued him and he forced himself to eat and take part in the conversation.But Madame de Rochefide paid little attention to him. Mademoiselle des Touches, speaking wittily of her journey to Italy, related many incidents that made Claude Vignon, Conti, and Félicité laugh.“How far such a woman is from me! Will she ever deign to notice me?” thought Calyste.Camille was struck with his expression and tried to console him with a sympathetic look. Claude intercepted and became sarcastically gay, telling Béatrix that love existed only by desire; that most women deceived themselves in loving; that they loved for reasons unknown to men and often to themselves; that the best among them were artful.“Keep to books, and don’t criticise our lives,” said Camille, glancing imperiously at him.The dinner ceased to be gay. Claude’s sarcasm made the two women pensive. Calyste felt pain amid the happiness of looking at Béatrix. Conti searched the marquise’s eyes to guess her thoughts. When dinner was over, Mademoiselle des Touches took Calyste’s arm, gave the other two men to the marquise, and let them pass so she could be alone with the young Breton a moment.“My dear Calyste,” she said, “you are acting in a manner that embarrasses the marquise; she may be delighted with your admiration, but she cannot accept it. Pray control yourself.”“She was hard to me, she will never care for me,” said Calyste, “and if she does not I shall die.”“Die! you! My dear Calyste, you are a child. Would you have died for me?”“You have made yourself my friend,” he answered.After coffee, Vignon asked Conti to sing. Camille sat at the piano. Together she and Gennaro sang the duet Dunque il mio bene tu mia sarai, the last duet of Zingarelli’s Romeo e Giulietta, a most pathetic modern page. The passage Di tanti palpiti expresses love in all its grandeur. Calyste, sitting in the same arm-chair in which Félicité had told him the story of the marquise, listened in rapt devotion. Béatrix and Vignon were on either side of the piano. Conti’s sublime voice blended with Félicité’s; both had often sung this piece and used all their gift. The music became a poem of divine melancholy, the farewell of two swans to life. When it was over, all present were under feelings that defy vulgar applause.“Ah! music is the first of arts!” exclaimed the marquise.“Camille thinks youth and beauty the first of poesies,” said Claude Vignon.Mademoiselle des Touches looked at Claude with vague uneasiness. Béatrix, not seeing Calyste, turned to see the music’s effect—more for Conti’s sake than Calyste’s; she saw a white face bathed in tears. At the sight, and as if seized by sudden pain, she turned back quickly and looked at Gennaro. Music had arisen before Calyste, touching him until he stood in presence of creation. He was dumbfounded by Conti’s genius. Despite Camille’s warnings about the musician, Calyste believed now in the beauty of the soul expressed by such love. How could he rival such an artist? What woman could cease to adore that genius? That voice entered the soul like another soul.The poor lad was overwhelmed by poesy and despair. He felt himself of no account; this ingenuous admission of his nothingness was readable on his face mingled with admiration. He did not observe the gesture with which Béatrix, moved by the contagion of a true feeling, called Félicité’s attention to him.“Oh! the adorable heart!” cried Camille. “Conti, you will never obtain applause of one-half the value of that child’s homage. Let us sing this trio. Béatrix, my dear, come.”When the marquise, Camille, and Conti arranged themselves at the piano, Calyste rose softly, flung himself on a sofa in the bedroom, whose door stood open, and sat with his head in his hands, plunged in meditation.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################X. DRAMA“What is it, my child?” Claude Vignon asked, slipping into the bedroom after Calyste and taking his hand. “You love; you think you are disdained; but it is not so. The field will be free to you in a few days and you will reign—beloved by more than one.”“Loved!” cried Calyste, springing up and beckoning Claude into the library. “Who loves me here?”“Camille,” Claude replied.“Camille loves me? And you—what of you?”“I?” Claude hesitated, sank wearily on a sofa, and after a pause confessed, “I am tired of life, but I have not the courage to quit it. I wish I were mistaken; yet for days a conviction has haunted me. I did not wander the marshes for pleasure; when I returned and found you with Camille my bitterness came from wounded feeling. I shall have an explanation with her soon. Two clear minds like ours can’t deceive each other long. I shall leave Les Touches—perhaps tomorrow with Conti. After we go strange things will happen here. I shall regret not witnessing conflicts of passion so rare in France. You are young to enter such dangerous lists; be careful.”Calyste, stunned to learn Camille loved him while he believed himself pledged to Beatrix, sat despairing and undecided. He could not reconcile Camille’s love with Beatrix’s hold on him. Silent, he overheard Claude confront Felicite:“You came to Paris last year desperately in love with Calyste,” Claude said. “Frightened by consequences, you took me as a screen, to mask your feelings and leave yourself free to follow your adoration. That plan was grand and ignoble; to execute it you should have chosen a common man or one so distracted as to be deceived. You thought me simple because I am a man of talent, not genius—and I have divined you. Yesterday, when I praised women of your age and said why Calyste loved you, did you suppose your glance was for me? It was for him. You have never been loved, Maupin; you will not be after rejecting that beautiful fruit.”“Why has love fled me?” she murmured.“Because you are not lovable,” he answered. “You do not yield to love; your strength repels those who might struggle with you. You are grand and sublime; bear the consequence: men are fatigued by you.”Camille protested, but Claude continued, explaining he would leave to avoid serving as her screen. He declared their superiority of mind condemned them to solitude: “Lord God, Thou hast made me powerful and solitary.”At that moment Calyste appeared. Camille, deeply moved and more beautiful than before, betrayed fear and a sudden flush. Claude told Calyste plainly that Felicite loved him, not for herself but in the peculiar way of a woman who delights in the sufferings born of longing—“in some sense a man,” he added sardonically. He advised Calyste to use Beatrix’s attraction as a shield for Camille, arguing the young man’s passion now endangered the older woman. Camille, humiliated and weeping, could not meet their eyes. Calyste, struck, fell at her feet and kissed her hands.“Do not abandon me, Claude,” Camille implored.Claude suggested there was no danger now: Calyste had fallen for the marquise, which would serve as a better barrier than Claude himself. Camille, rising with a dominating look, declared that she conceived a grandeur of love without hope: “Do not love me, Calyste; but I will love you as no woman will!” Claude knelt and kissed her hand.Calyste returned to Guerande in a daze, scarcely pitying Camille though later imagining her moans. At home gloom reigned; his mother, sewing by lamplight, noted his agitation. He embraced her passionately.“Camille loves me, but I love her no longer,” he told his mother.Next day Calyste ordered Gasselin to watch for Mademoiselle des Touches’ carriage and, learning it had passed, saddled horses. His family guessed mischief—some thought he rode to meet Charlotte de Pen-Hoel, a proposed match. Calyste denied that Felicite would be an obstacle to any marriage. His family fussed; the baron called out a tender farewell as Calyste rode away.He arrived at the port just as Camille and the marquise were waving to a departing steamer. Beatrix, poised in straw and muslin, gave Calyste an imperious look; Camille’s smile made the marquise aware of the vulgarity of such conduct. Camille took Calyste’s arm and left Beatrix to watch the vessel. Soon Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel, the Vicomtesse de Kergarouet, and Charlotte arrived; Camille offered them places in her carriage, and Calyste conveyed the message. Charlotte, who had cherished boyhood hopes of Calyste, was embarrassed to find him reserved.The party drove through Guerande where a crowd gathered. Calyste stayed near, giving his arm to the ladies and bidding Camille and Beatrix goodbye. He kissed Felicite’s hand; the marquise, with arms crossed, withheld her hand. At the du Guenics, dinner and cards followed; all presumed Calyste free and nearly engaged to Charlotte. He alone was silent, comparing his lifelong provincial ties with the witty, sophisticated Parisians. His mother saw his unease and urged that he accept Camille’s carriage offer. He ran to Les Touches.XI. FEMALE DIPLOMACYCalyste reached Les Touches as Camille and Beatrix left the grand salon after dinner. He offered his arm to Felicite, who pressed it and playfully chided his abandoning the viscountess. Conversation revealed social links; Beatrix remarked the Kergarouet girl would suit Calyste, but Camille quickly denied any marriage would occur.Mortified by Beatrix’s coolness toward Charlotte, Calyste was silent. Camille, seeing his despair, accused Beatrix of coldness and suggested she had helped drive him to the marquise. Beatrix’s manner—calm and slightly distrustful—wounded Calyste. Camille took him into her chamber and shut the door.“My child,” she said, “if you want to succeed with Beatrix, you must seem to love me still. You are too candid to play a part, but deception is the only way to win her. I will take it on. Come every day; spend mornings with me and study—four hours daily; in the evenings, we will be together. Obey me blindly. If you break our agreement and speak privately with the marquise, you will lose her forever.”Calyste, trusting her, kissed her hand; Camille did not restrain tears. He left believing that in a week Beatrix would love him. That evening at home he resumed his old gaiety; the card players praised him. He returned to Les Touches regularly, studying, bringing books, and attending the ruse.After Calyste left, Camille, exhausted, confessed to Beatrix, “I am forty and I love him!” She lamented lost youth and the shame of being loved from pity. She asked Beatrix, who loved Conti, to be noble and help retain Calyste. Beatrix, flattered and moved, resolved to behave generously, giving Calyste a cool shoulder while feeling the private delight of inspiring a man’s love.The next morning Calyste found Camille at the window, smoking and arranging small signals to draw the marquise’s attention. Camille urged him to stay to dinner but to refuse in a way that showed regret; Beatrix had dressed charmingly. These small staged scenes became a comedy of feminine diplomacy: Camille played the jealous, self-sacrificing woman; Beatrix, alternating between pride and curiosity, aimed to subdue her hurt by showing superiority. The duel between the two women was fought with snares, feints, false generosity, and crafty confidences—Camille’s words both exposed and hid her passion, poisoning Beatrix against her.For six days Camille concentrated every artifice toward her end. She spoke of Calyste’s purity and beauty to Beatrix, extolled his faith, and allowed the marquise to perceive both the nobility and the danger of the boy. Beatrix, between jealousy and generosity, affected gentle indifference while inwardly stirred. Calyste, who had promised to obey Camille, felt his love’s fury nearly undo her plan. He swore to see Beatrix and speak to her, craving to read her eyes and breathe her presence.One morning he slipped into the garden and surprised Beatrix; they conversed awkwardly. She answered that he had neither pleased nor displeased her, and when he declared he no longer loved Camille, she asked why he visited her every morning. Camille, appearing angrily, drew Calyste away, reproaching him. Beatrix, stupefied by the revelation and Camille’s dramatic abnegation, could not comprehend the part she was being asked to play—but the abnegation was exactly the extremity of feminine grandeur Camille was capable of.At breakfast the marquise resolved to stifle any rising love. Although she was not harsh, she remained gently indifferent—tormenting Calyste. Camille proposed an excursion, but Beatrix refused, fearing complications: Conti was coming back to fetch her. The thought of Conti’s return turned Calyste pale. Despite Camille’s urgings and his entreaties, Beatrix remained inflexible, showing the obstinacy Camille had warned about. Calyste left Les Touches plunged into the despair that often follows intense love, beginning to think of ways to force an explanation with Beatrix.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When Calyste reached home he shut himself in his room until dinner and returned there afterward. At ten his mother, uneasy at his absence, found him among blotted, half-torn sheets: he was composing a letter to Beatrix, tormented by jealousy about Camille after the marquise’s manner in the garden.No first love-letter is polished; it pours from the heart and repeats itself. This is the one Calyste finally wrote and read aloud to his astonished mother, who felt their old mansion glow with her son’s love.Calyste to Madame la Marquise de Rochefide.Madame,—I loved you like a dream; seeing you has made my love stronger. I need not praise your beauty; perhaps no other heart has felt such deep sentiment. I have studied and penetrated your delicacy. Have you ever been loved, understood, adored as you deserve? Not a trait of yours escapes me; your pride, your glance, your grace harmonize with the desires I divined in you, and therefore I feel worthy of your notice. This letter concerns you more than me.Beatrix, to write to you I have aged myself by this week of dreadful suffering you have, though innocently, caused. Do not think me like common lovers; loving a beautiful woman is no merit. I am a child drawn to light; you tread on the flowers of my soul. Absolute devotion, unbounded faith—these mean nothing if they win no return. Your cold, stern eye turns me to ice; your disdain governs between us. You cannot hate me as much as I love you; why should the weaker feeling rule? I once loved Felicite, yet forgot her at sight of you. You are my truth.You have unknowingly destroyed my happiness though you owe me nothing. I loved Camille without hope; I have none from you; yet you have taught me that to love is the greatest joy. Camille called stagnant love wretched and foresaw our end; she mingled tears with mine and rejected a love she knew must end. Therefore I am free to love you on earth and in heaven. If you loved me you would not use Camille’s arguments; we are young and could fly together.Believe in my submission, patience, mute adoration; I only ask you not to wound needlessly. I know you cannot love me without losing self-esteem; I ask no return. Camille spoke of a fatality in names; I felt it on the jetty of Guerande. You will pass through my life like Beatrice through Dante’s; my heart will be a pedestal for that white, distant statue—cold, jealous, oppressive.It is forbidden you love me; you will suffer betrayals and humiliations, bound by a devil’s pride. Blood gushes from my heart and darkens my intellect; I can only adore you whatever you do. Camille calls your resolution obstinacy; I call it virtue—you are more beautiful for it. I know my destiny; the pride of a Breton can rise to meet a proud woman.Be kind and consoling. Crown me with pity; you owe me the music of my sacrifice. Am I not proof of your grandeur? Ask Camille how I behaved when she told me she loved Claude Vignon; I suffered in silence. For you I will show greater strength—I will bury my feelings unless you drive me to despair. A single word of praise will make me bear martyrdom.But if you persist in cold silence you will make me think you fear me. Be what you are—charming, witty, gay, tender. Talk to me of Conti as Camille has talked of Claude. I have no genius but love; nothing in me should make you fear. I will be by you as if I loved you not.Can you refuse so humble a prayer? The child asks only that his Light shall lighten him, his Sun warm him. The man you love can be with you at all times, but I have so few days to see you; soon you will be free of me. May I return to Les Touches tomorrow? Will you not refuse my arm for an excursion to Croisic and Batz? If you do not go I shall take it for an answer—Calyste will understand.There were four more pages of similar impassioned repetition. Though unlikely to move Madame de Rochefide, it made his mother weep.“My child, you are not happy,” she said softly. The raging poem terrified the baroness; for the first time she read a love-letter. Calyste, perplexed how to send it, followed his mother into the salon with the letter burning in his pocket. The Chevalier du Halga was there; Charlotte de Kergarouet tried to use Calyste’s indifference for her own ends. Calyste wandered like a distracted butterfly. When the card game ended he asked the chevalier in a whisper how best to send a letter secretly to one’s mistress.“Slip it into the maid’s hand with a louis or two,” the chevalier suggested—though better, he said, to give the letter oneself. Calyste snatched his hat and ran to Les Touches, appearing like an apparition in the little salon where Camille and Beatrix sat together. He flung himself impulsively beside the marquise, took her hand, and so rapidly slipped the letter into it that Felicite did not perceive it. His heart tingled as Beatrix pressed his hand and, without interrupting her words, slipped the letter into her glove.“You fling yourself on a woman’s dress without mercy,” she laughed.“Calyste is wanting in common sense,” said Felicite, openly rebuking him.Calyste kissed Camille’s hand, then ran his fingernail over the piano keys. Camille, surprised by his exuberance, signed for him to come near.“What is the matter with you?” she whispered.“Nothing,” he said.“There is something between them,” Mademoiselle des Touches thought. The marquise remained impenetrable. Camille tried to draw speech from Calyste, hoping his artless mind would betray itself; he excused himself because his mother expected him, and left Les Touches at eleven, after facing Camille’s piercing glance.That wakeful night Calyste searched Guerande’s main street many times for an answer. At last the marquise’s waiting-woman brought a note, which he read in the garden grotto.Madame de Rochefide to Calyste.You are a noble child, but still only a child. You are bound to Camille, who adores you. You would not find in me the perfections that distinguish her or the happiness she can give. Whatever you may think, she is young and I am old; her heart is full of treasures, mine empty; she is unselfish and lives for you, while I am full of doubts and a slave.You forget I love and am beloved. My situation forbids accepting homage; a man saying he loves me is an insult. To yield would lower me to the level of the lowest of my sex. You who are young and pure are carried away by fancy; you cannot weigh the reflections evil suggests. Were I humiliated, betrayed, abandoned—no one in the world would see me more. I believe I should find courage to kill a man who, seeing me thus, spoke to me of love.You now know my mind. After receiving your letter and making this reply I could at ease be what you ask in Camille’s house; but the bitter ridicule that would follow if I softened is dreadful. A second robbery from Camille would prove her want of power which no woman could forgive. Even if I loved you, Camille’s love for you is a barrier too high to be overleaped; none but a devil would fail to recoil before such treachery. There are motives delicate women keep to themselves which you men cannot understand.Your mother is pure and spotless; she fulfils her destiny nobly. She is the woman your wife should be. I will never mock you by sending you back to little Charlotte; I commend you to some divine young girl worthy of your love.If I were yours your life would be blighted. I should have taken your existence and made you unhappy; I am jealous—trifles would make me wretched; inexorable thoughts from my heart would poison our existence. If after ten years a man were not as respectful and delicate, I should resent the change; such a lover could not believe in the Amadis and Cyrus of my dreams. True love today is but a dream; in yours I see merely the joy of desire whose end you do not yet perceive.I am not forty; I have not bent my pride beneath experience; I may be too young to be anything but odious. I will not answer for my temper; my grace and charm are external. Perhaps I have not suffered enough to be tender from cruel disappointments. Camille will always be your devoted slave; I should be an unreasonable tyrant.Felicite’s tenderness is inexhaustible; she will marry you to some young girl, no matter her own suffering; she will smooth difficulties for you. The sale of an acre in Paris would free your estate in Brittany; she will make you her heir—you are already her son by adoption.Alas! what could I do for your happiness? Nothing. Do not betray that infinite love which contents itself with the duties of motherhood. Camille is fortunate; she can forgive your fancy. I have studied her: mind and goodness, generous and simple—rare amalgamations. Dante’s line about eternal happiness, “Senza brama sicura ricchezza,” seems made for her. She has told me of her career and how love has eluded her; she seems an example of the difficulty, perhaps impossibility, of uniting two great glories.You, Calyste, are an angelic soul whose mate seems impossible to find; but Camille will obtain for you the hand of a young girl with whom you can make a happy home, even if she dies in doing so.For myself, I hold out a friendly hand; I count on your mind to make you a brother, as I shall be a sister; let this letter end a correspondence between Les Touches and Guerande that is rather absurd.Beatrix de Casteran.Stirred by her son’s strange exhibitions, the baroness could not sit quietly. After looking at him, she rose and came to him humbly yet boldly to demand a favor she felt entitled to ask. Calyste read Beatrix’s letter aloud. These two noble, guileless souls saw no malice in the marquise’s cautious epistle.“She is a noble, grand woman!” the baroness said with moist eyes. “I will pray for her. I did not know a woman could abandon her husband and child and yet keep a soul so virtuous. She is worthy of pardon.”“Have I not every reason to adore her?” cried Calyste.“But where will this love lead you?” said the baroness. “How dangerous are women with noble sentiments! There is less to fear from the bad. Marry Charlotte de Kergarouet and release two-thirds of the estate. By selling a few farms, Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel can secure that result in the marriage contract and help you manage your property. You could leave your children a great name and estate.”“Forget Beatrix!” said Calyste, muffled, eyes on the ground.He left to write an answer.Madame du Guenic, whose heart held every word of Beatrix’s letter, sought help to understand it and Calyste’s hope. She found the Chevalier du Halga on the mall and, certain to meet him, went out. Her walking in Guerande was an event; townspeople talked. Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel told her niece Charlotte, “Something very extraordinary is happening at the du Guenics.”“Calyste is madly in love with that beautiful Marquise de Rochefide,” Charlotte said. “I ought to leave Guerande.”The chevalier, surprised to be sought, unhooked his dog. “Chevalier,” began the baroness, “you used to practise gallantry?” He straightened with vain air as she, without naming Calyste or the marquise, recited the love-letter and asked his opinion of Beatrix’s reply. Du Halga listened, made grimaces, and finally—“When thoroughbred horses want to leap a barrier, they go up to reconnoitre it. Calyste is a lucky dog!” The baroness cried, “Oh, hush!” He rambled into old exploits, but escorted her to the alley, faithful to gallant forms, neglecting Thisbe.The baroness ran to Calyste’s room. He was absent; she saw an unsealed letter addressed to Madame de Rochefide on the table and, compelled by invincible curiosity, read it. The indiscretion punished her: the letter revealed how deep the gulf into which Calyste’s passion hurled him.Calyste to Madame la Marquise de Rochefide.What care I for the race of the du Guenics now, Beatrix? Their name is nothing to me. My name is Beatrix; your happiness is my happiness; your life is my life; all my fortune is in your heart. Our estates have been mortgaged for two hundred years; so they may remain; our farmers have them; no one can take them from us. To see you, to love you—that is my property, my object, my religion!You talk to me of marrying! The thought convulses me. Is there another Beatrix? I will marry no one but you; I will wait twenty years if need be. I am young, and you will be ever beautiful. My mother is a saint; she has never loved; you have taught me what she lost, and what sacrifices she made. You have taught me to love my mother better; she is in my heart with you—no other can be there; she is your only rival—you reign supreme.As for Camille, say the word and I will ask her to tell you she is not loved by me. She is the mother of my intellect, nothing more. From the moment I saw you she became a sister, a friend, a comrade; we have rights only of friendship. I took her for a woman until I saw you. You have proved that Camille is a man—she swims, hunts, smokes, drinks, rides, writes and analyzes hearts—she has no woman’s weaknesses; her movements lack your grace. In you are all the things of womanhood I love. From that first moment I felt you were mine.You will laugh, but the fancy has grown. It seems unnatural we should be apart. You are my soul; I cannot live where you are not!Let me love you! Let us fly to a country where you know no one, where only God and I can reach your heart! My mother might follow. Ireland is full of castles; my mother’s family will lend us one. Beatrix, let us go! A boat, a few sailors, and we are there before any one knows we have fled.You have never been loved. I see in your letter that if such reasons did not exist you might let yourself be loved by me. A sacred love wipes out the past. I love you so truly I could wish you doubly shamed if so my love might prove itself by making you a saint!You call my love an insult. Oh, Beatrix, you do not mean it! The love of noble youth—one you called noble—would honor a queen. Tomorrow let us walk as lovers by the sea; your step on my sands will bless them anew. Give me this day of happiness; that passing alms from you will be eternal riches to yourCalyste.The baroness let the letter fall before finishing. She knelt and prayed to God to save her son’s reason, to remove his madness and error. Calyste entered.“What are you doing, mother?” he asked.“I am praying to God for you,” she answered simply, turning tearful eyes on him. “I have committed the sin of reading that letter. My Calyste is mad!”“A sweet madness!” he said, kissing her.“I wish I could see that woman,” she sighed.“Mamma,” Calyste answered, “we shall take a boat to-morrow and cross to Croisic. If you are on the jetty you can see her.”So saying, he sealed his letter and departed for Les Touches. What especially terrified the baroness was that Calyste’s second letter showed instinctive feeling and a clear-eyed resolution that read as if the worldly chevalier might have dictated it.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Perhaps one of the greatest enjoyments for small or inferior minds is deceiving a great soul and setting snares for it. Beatrix knew herself far beneath Camille Maupin—not only in talent but in the deeper matter of the heart: passion.When Calyste rushed to Les Touches with the impetuosity of first love, the marquise delighted in being its object. She did not want to share the sentiment; she fancied herself heroic in repressing a caprice and persuaded herself she matched Camille’s devotion, imagining she sacrificed for her friend. Her vanities, that notable coquetry, were flattered by Calyste’s love. Assailed by such seduction, she resisted while inwardly praising her own virtues.The two women half-sat, half-reclined on the little salon divan; the room was calm, windows open to a southerly breeze over the salt lake. Their souls were as agitated as the scene was tranquil; the inward heat was ardent.Bruised by the machinery she had set in motion, Camille guarded herself against the clever friendly enemy she had admitted. To preserve secrets and remain aloof, she contemplated nature; she cheated her aching heart by seeking meaning in the world, finding God in that desert of heaven and earth. When an unbeliever perceives God, he flings himself unreservedly into Catholicism, seen as a complete system.That morning Camille wore the halo of thoughts born of these researches after a night of painful struggle. Calyste stood before her like a celestial image. The beautiful youth, to whom she had secretly devoted herself, had become a guardian angel. Did he not lift her into loftier regions where suffering ceased beneath the weight of infinity? Now a certain air of triumph about Beatrix disturbed her. No woman gains advantage without making it felt; the dumb moral struggle between these two—each hiding a secret and believing herself generous through hidden sacrifices—was strange.Calyste arrived, a letter between his hand and glove ready to slip to Beatrix. Camille, noting a subtle change in her friend’s manner, watched Beatrix in a mirror as Calyste entered. That sudden moment is crucial for women: the cleverest and the simplest, the frankest and the shrewdest, seldom keep secrets; they betray them in a look. Too much reserve or too little; a free luminous gaze; the mysterious lowering of eyelids—all give away what is hardest to hide. Women have a genius for nuance; they can read a rival from head to foot. Two women observing each other play one of the world's choicest comedies.“Calyste has committed some folly,” thought Camille, perceiving in each guest an indefinable air of mutual understanding.Beatrix no longer feigned indifference; she regarded Calyste as her own. Calyste was even more transparent; he colored like guilty or happy people. He announced he had come to arrange the excursion for the following day.“Then you really intend to go, my dear?” Camille asked.“Yes,” said Beatrix.“How did you know it, Calyste?” asked Mademoiselle des Touches.“I came here to find out,” replied Calyste, prompted by a look from Madame de Rochefide, who did not want Camille to learn of their correspondence.“They have an agreement together,” thought Camille, catching that look sweeping the room.A horrible discomposure spread over her face and frightened Beatrix.“What is the matter, my dear?” she cried.“Nothing. Well, then, Calyste, send my horses and yours across to Croisic, so we may drive home by way of Batz. We will breakfast at Croisic and be home in time for dinner. You must arrange the boat. Let us start by half-past eight. You will see some fine sights, Beatrix, and one very strange one; you will see Cambremer, a man who does penance on a rock for having wilfully killed his son. Oh! you are in a primitive land, among a primitive race of people, moved by other sentiments than ordinary mortals. Calyste shall tell you the tale; it is a drama of the seashore.”She went into her bedroom, stifling. Calyste gave his letter to Beatrix and followed Camille.“Calyste, you are loved, I think; but you are hiding something from me; you have done some foolish thing.”“Loved!” he exclaimed, sinking into a chair.Camille glanced into the next room; Beatrix had disappeared—odd, since women seldom leave a room with the man they admire unless sure of seeing him again or for some advantage. Mademoiselle des Touches wondered, “Can he have given her a letter?” but thought the innocent Breton incapable of such boldness.“If you have disobeyed me, all will be lost through your own fault,” she said gravely. “Go now and make your preparations for tomorrow.”She made a gesture Calyste did not resist.As he walked toward Croisic to hire boatmen, fears entered Calyste’s mind. Camille’s speech foreshadowed something fatal; he believed this maternal second sight. When he returned four hours later, tired and expecting to dine at Les Touches, he found Camille’s maid at the door to tell him that neither her mistress nor the marquise could receive him that evening. Calyste, surprised, wished to question her, but she bade him hastily good-night and closed the door.Six o’clock was striking in Guerande as Calyste entered his house; Mariotte gave him his belated dinner, after which he played mouche in gloomy meditation. Alternations of joy and gloom, the extinction of hopes following seeming certainties of being loved, bruised the young soul that had flown so high that the fall was dreadful.“Does anything trouble you, my Calyste?” his mother asked.“Nothing,” he replied, his eyes lacking their usual light.It is not hope but despair that measures our ambitions. The finest poems of hope are sung in secret; grief appears unveiled.“Calyste, you are not nice,” said Charlotte, after vain attempts at the provincial witcheries that turn to teasing.“I am tired,” he said, rising and bidding the company good-night.“Calyste is much changed,” remarked Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel.“We haven’t beautiful dresses trimmed with lace; we don’t shake our sleeves like this, or twist our bodies like that; we don’t know how to give sidelong glances and turn our eyes,” said Charlotte, mimicking the marquise’s air and attitudes. “We haven’t that head voice, nor the interesting little cough heu! heu! like a spook’s sigh; we are healthy and robust, and love our friends without coquetry; and when we look at them, we don’t pretend to stick a dart into them or watch them slyly; we can’t bend our heads like a weeping willow just to look more interesting—this way.”Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel laughed, but neither the chevalier nor the baron paid heed to this provincial satire against Paris.“But the Marquise de Rochefide is a very handsome woman,” the old maid said.“My dear,” said the baroness to her husband, “I know she is going to Croisic tomorrow. Let us walk on the jetty; I should like to see her.”While Calyste tried to guess what had closed Les Touches’ doors to him, a scene between Camille and Beatrix unfolded that would influence the morrow.Calyste’s last letter stirred in Madame de Rochefide’s heart emotions hitherto unknown to it. Women are seldom the object of love so young, guileless, sincere, and unconditional as his. Beatrix, who had loved more than she had been loved, suddenly desired to be a tyrant. Yet reading and rereading the letter, a cruel idea pierced her.What were Calyste and Camille doing together since Claude Vignon’s departure? If Calyste did not love Camille, and Camille knew it, how did they spend their mornings alone? Memory flashed certain speeches of Camille into Beatrix’s mind; a grinning devil seemed to show her, like a magic mirror, that heroic woman’s portrait with gestures and aspects that enlightened her. What! Instead of being Camille’s equal, was she crushed by Félicité? Instead of outwitting her, had she been outwitted? Was she merely a toy, a pleasure Camille gave to her child, whom she loved with an extraordinary, nonvulgar passion?To Beatrix such a thought was a thunderclap. She reviewed the past week minutely; the roles each had played unrolled before her; she felt horribly belittled. In her fury of jealous anger she fancied Camille’s conduct an intention of vengeance against Conti. Was hidden wrath from the past two years affecting the present?Once on that path of doubts, Beatrix did not pause. She paced, sat, tried to decide, unable to; she remained prey to indecision until dinner, when she rose hastily and went downstairs without dressing. As Camille saw her in a morning gown, with chilling air and taciturn manner, she felt a crisis had come. Beatrix’s look indicated the coming hostilities of an embittered heart.Camille instantly left the room and gave the order surprising Calyste; she feared he might arrive amid the quarrel and wished to fight alone, without witnesses. Beatrix alone would infallibly succumb. Camille knew the other’s soul was barren and pride petty and obstinate.The dinner was gloomy. Camille was gentle and kind; she felt superior. Beatrix was hard and cutting; she felt managed like a child. The battle began with glances, gestures, half-spoken sentences—insufficient to enlighten servants but enough to prepare an observer for the storm. When they rose, Camille offered her arm maliciously to Beatrix, who pretended not to see and sprang up the stair alone. After coffee, Mademoiselle des Touches dismissed the footman with “You may go”—a brief sentence serving as their signal.“The novels you make, my dear, are more dangerous than those you write,” said the marquise.“They have one advantage, however,” Camille replied, lighting a cigarette.“What is that?” asked Beatrix.“They are unpublished, my angel.”“Is the one in which you are putting me to be turned into a book?”“I’ve no fancy for the role of Oedipus; I know you have the wit and beauty of a sphinx, but don’t propound conundrums. Speak out plainly, my dear Beatrix.”“When, to make a man happy, amuse him, please him, and save him from ennui, we let the devil help us—”“That man would reproach us later for our efforts and would think them prompted by the genius of depravity,” interrupted Camille, taking the cigarette from her lips.“He forgets the love that carried us away; that’s our sole justification—but men are unjust and ungrateful,” Beatrix continued. “Women know each other; they know how proud and noble their minds are, and—frankly—how virtuous! But, Camille, I’ve just recognized some criticisms of your nature you’ve sometimes complained of. You have something of the man about you; you behave like a man; nothing restrains you; if you haven’t all a man’s advantages, you have his spirit and his contempt for women. I have no reason to be satisfied with you and am too frank to hide my dissatisfaction. No one has ever given or will give so cruel a wound as the one I now suffer. If you are not a woman in love, you are one in vengeance. It takes a woman of genius to find the most sensitive spot in another woman’s delicacy. I speak now of Calyste and the trickery—you have employed against me. To what depths have you descended, Camille Maupin! and why?”“More and more sphinx-like!” said Camille, smiling.“You want me to fling myself at Calyste’s head; but I am still too young for that. To me, love is sacred; love is love with all its emotions, jealousies, and despotisms. I am not an author; I cannot see ideas where the heart feels sentiments.”“You think yourself capable of loving foolishly!” said Camille. “Make yourself easy; you have plenty of sense. You calumniate yourself; your nature is cold enough to let your head judge your heart.”The marquise colored; she darted a venomous look and found the sharpest arrows in her quiver. Camille smoked composedly as Beatrix launched a furious tirade and even attacked Camille’s age.“Is that all?” said Félicité, when Beatrix paused, letting smoke exhale.“Do you love Calyste?”“No; of course not.”“So much the better,” Camille replied. “I do love him—far too much for my peace of mind. He may perhaps have had a passing fancy for you; for you are enchantingly fair, while I am as black as a crow; you are slim and willowy, while I have portly dignity; in short, you are young!—that’s the final word, and you have not spared it to me. You have abused your advantages as a woman against me. I have done my best to prevent what has now happened. However little woman you may think me, I am woman enough not to allow a rival to triumph unless I choose to help her.” This remark, apparently innocent, cut the marquise to the heart. “You take me for a very silly person if you believe all that Calyste tries to make you think of me. I am neither so great nor so small; I am a woman, and very much of a woman. Come, put off your grand airs, and give me your hand!” Camille took Madame de Rochefide’s hand. “You do not love Calyste, you say; that is true, is it not? Don’t be angry; be hard, cold, and stern to him tomorrow; he will end by submitting, especially after certain reproaches I mean to make. Still, Calyste is a Breton, and very persistent; if he continues to court you, tell me frankly, and I will lend you my little country house near Paris, where you will find comforts, and where Conti can come out and see you. You said Calyste calumniated me. Good heavens! what of that? The purest love lies twenty times a day; its deceptions prove its strength.”Camille’s superb disdain frightened Beatrix, who did not know how to answer. Camille dealt a last blow.“I am more confiding and less bitter than you,” she said. “I don’t suspect you of attempting to cover by a quarrel a secret injury that would compromise my life. You know me; I shall never survive the loss of Calyste, but I must lose him sooner or later. Still, Calyste loves me now; of that I am sure.”“Here is what he answered to my letter urging him to be true to you,” said Beatrix, holding out Calyste’s last letter.Camille read it; her eyes filled with tears; presently she wept as women weep in their bitterest sorrows.“My God!” she said, “how he loves her! I shall die without being understood—or loved,” she added.She sat with her head on Beatrix’s shoulder; her grief was genuine, the same terrible blow the Baronne du Guenic had felt reading that letter.“Do you love him?” she asked, looking fixedly at Beatrix. “Have you that infinite worship for him which triumphs over all pains, survives contempt, betrayal, the certainty that he will never love you? Do you love him for himself, and for the joy of loving him?”“Dear friend,” said the marquise tenderly, “be happy, be at peace; I will leave this place tomorrow.”“No, do not go; he loves you, I see that. Well, I love him so much I could not endure to see him wretched. I had formed plans, projects; but if he loves you, all is over.”“And I love him, Camille,” said the marquise, with some naïveté and coloring.“You love him, and yet you cast him off!” cried Camille. “Ah! that is not loving; you do not love him.”“I don’t know what fresh virtue he has roused in me, but certainly he has made me ashamed of myself,” said Beatrix. “I would I were virtuous and free, to give him something better than the dregs of a heart and the weight of my chains. I do not want a hampered destiny for him or myself.”“Cold brain!” exclaimed Camille, with horror. “To love and calculate!”“Call it what you like,” Beatrix replied, “but I will not spoil his life, or hang like a millstone round his neck to become an eternal regret. If I cannot be his wife, I shall not be his mistress. He has— you will laugh? No? Well, then, he has purified me.”Camille cast the most sullen, savage look female jealousy can cast.“On that ground, I believed I stood alone,” she said. “Beatrix, those words must separate us forever; we are no longer friends. Here begins a terrible conflict between us. I tell you now; you will either succumb or fly.”So saying, Camille bounded into her room, showing the maddened-lioness’s face to astonished Beatrix, then raised the portière and looked back in.“Do you intend to go to Croisic tomorrow?” she asked.“Certainly,” replied Beatrix proudly. “I shall not fly, and I shall not succumb.”“I play above board,” replied Camille; “I shall write to Conti.”Beatrix turned as white as her gauze scarf.“We are staking our lives on this game,” she murmured, not knowing what to say or do.The violent passions roused by this scene calmed during the night. Both argued with themselves and returned to the temporizing courses attractive to most women—an excellent system between men and women, but fatally unsafe among women alone. In the midst of this tumult Mademoiselle des Touches listened to that great Voice whose counsels subdue the strongest will; Beatrix heard only the promptings of worldly wisdom; she feared society’s contempt.Thus Félicité’s last deception succeeded; Calyste’s blunder was repaired, but a fresh indiscretion might yet be fatal to him.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################It was the end of August, the sky clear, the sea near the horizon molten-silver, and the shore rippling in tiny waves. A sunlight glow gave the sands an almost tropical air; salt gleamed white on the marsh. The paludiers, in white with long rakes, had been at their posts since morning—some leaning on mud-walls watching the harvest, others with family. The custom-house officers smoked calmly. The scene seemed exotic; a Parisian might not guess this was France.The baron and baroness, who had feigned interest in the harvest, stood on the jetty admiring the silent landscape where only the sea sounded and boats and a cultivated green belt lent a rare charm.“Well, my friends, I wanted to see the marshes of Guérande once more before I die,” said the baron.“Can a Guénic die?” one paludier replied.A party from Les Touches arrived by a narrow path. The marquise walked first alone; Calyste and Camille followed arm-in-arm, Gasselin bringing up the rear. The marquise caught sight of Madame du Guénic and felt strong repulsion for Béatrix, though Béatrix was well dressed in a Leghorn hat with bluebells, crimped hair, a gray gown, and a blue sash giving her the air of a princess disguised as a milkmaid. The baron admired Mademoiselle des Touches’s youthful appearance. Calyste introduced Camille to his parents; the paludier mentioned that Mademoiselle was the largest landowner and a good lady. They bowed and separated.A boat awaited at the jetty; the party embarked in silence. The marquise remained cold and dignified, having lectured Calyste on his disobedience; Calyste, in despair, cast glances at Béatrix where anger and love struggled. The passage from Guérande to Croisic was wordless. At Croisic, where salt is discharged and peasant-women carry it in earthen jars on their heads, barefoot carriers watched the little Danish vessel unload. To avoid remarks and to serve Calyste, Camille sprang toward the rocks, leaving him to follow with Béatrix. Gasselin kept some distance.The Croisic peninsula is flanked by granite rocks carved by the sea into arabesques and natural vaults. Under a vaulted roof is a hollow like a marble bathtub, floored with white sand and tepid water safe for bathing. Cool covered hollows, roughly carved but majestic, recall palaces copying nature. One rare tree stood on a granite promontory—a large box-bush whose roots suggested three hundred years’ life—above a cliff whose base formed a semicircle of rocks. It took nerve to climb this little Gibraltar; from the summit one sees clock towers and arid fields of Croisic and sandy dunes stretching to Batz.Calyste led Béatrix to this vantage point where the view was magnificent and the granite imposing. Camille, like a wounded wild animal craving solitude, threaded fissures and caves of the cliff in male attire—trousers with frilled edges, a short blouse, a peaked cap, carrying a riding-whip—courting danger to still her inward sufferings. She reached the rock by the box-bush and sat in its shade, lost in thought.Alone on that spot, Camille considered old age and what a woman like her should do after having drunk the cup of fame too eagerly. She drew forth a box of strawberry lozenges, ate several, and reflected that strawberries no longer existed but lived in their qualities—perhaps humans survive similarly in spiritual qualities. Facing the ocean’s infinite suggestion, Camille felt that only conviction of future holiness could give immortality meaning. A noble inward change came: the earthly woman died within her and a more spiritual being rose. She resolved to withdraw from worldly life, to turn the love she once pursued into a divine impulse that would lead her to renounce social ambitions forever.After walking in silence, Calyste compared the ocean’s depth and purity to his love. Béatrix remarked on the grandeur unlike that of the Mediterranean. When she laughed that “it is met by a rock,” he answered with a sublime look that he could summon the patience of the angels when he heard and saw her; yet when alone he suffered so much his mother wept for him. The marquise chose that barren plain dried by salt winds as an image of her inner desolation. She told Calyste she loved him but would never be his; she would remain in present bonds, the sandy plain without fruit. If abandoned, she would beg pardon of the man she had offended; she would never risk taking a happiness she believed would quickly end.Calyste replied passionately, offering to sacrifice everything—family, name, future—but she recoiled from the word “sacrifice,” seeing it as an insult. He cried that love cannot argue, it is itself; he would do the impossible for her. Moved, but firm, she said she had done wrong and asked him to tempt her no more. They walked on in tense silence, his inward fury visible only to those who love without hope.At the base of the rock by the box-bush, Calyste helped Béatrix ascend the steep slope; for him it was a precious privilege. He clasped her round the waist. When he asked whether she would never be his, she replied she could only be Béatrix—a dream—and that that sweet, tender dream could be without bitterness or repentance. When he, in frenzy, cried “You shall never belong to any man!” and pushed her, she slipped eight or ten feet into the cavity where the box-bush grew; her gown caught on a rock point and tore slowly, lowering her onto the bush.Mademoiselle des Touches, horrified, signaled to Gasselin.
