Calyste leaned forward, savage; he saw Béatrix’s precarious position and shuddered. Her lips moved as if praying; the bush began to give. With youth’s agility, Calyste slid down to the ledge below, grasped the marquise and held her though both risked sliding into the sea. She had fainted; in that aerial spot he fancied her wholly his, and his first emotion was pleasure. “Open your eyes,” he begged; she murmured the word “Die?” and when she opened her eyes he kissed her, feeling her tremble convulsively with passionate joy.Gasselin’s hob-nailed shoes sounded above them; he was followed by Camille. They sought a way to save the lovers. Gasselin declared he must slide down so they could climb on his shoulders and be pulled up; Camille was to fetch a ladder from Croisic. Béatrix asked to be laid down; Calyste placed her on a narrow ledge. She had fainted and when she recovered seemed weak. Camille insisted that whether Béatrix lived or died the incident must be seen as an accident. Calyste feared she would hate him; Camille reassured him she would adore him. Gasselin fetched a ladder, and with a contrivance involving Camille’s red shawl and the ladder, Béatrix reached the top; the Breton took her in his arms and carried her to the shore like an infant.She murmured to Félicité that she should not have wished for death, but suffering. Camille had her taken to a farmhouse from which the ladder had been borrowed and a doctor was summoned from Croisic. Calyste, Gasselin, and Camille made a litter from spare clothes upon the ladder and carried Béatrix; farmers gave her a bed while Gasselin rode for a doctor and told a boatman to row to the nearest landing.Calyste sat on a stool answering in monosyllables; Camille’s uneasiness grew at Calyste’s unnatural state. The physician bled Béatrix; she improved and consented to embark, and by five o’clock they reached the jetty at Guérande whence she was carried to Les Touches. News of the accident had spread.Calyste passed the night at Les Touches, at the foot of Béatrix’s bed with Camille. The doctor had assured them only slight stiffness would remain. Through despair there came a gleam of joy: he could watch her sleep or wake, study her pallid face and expressions. Camille recognized in him a passion one feels but once—a passion that consumes a man when nothing else distracts him. She saw he would never see the real woman in Béatrix.Calyste was guileless; when he saw Béatrix’s green eyes turned to him with love, confusion, and mischief, he blushed and looked away. Béatrix jested sweetly about men promising happiness and ending by flinging women down precipices. He knelt, took her moist hand, and kissed it humbly, telling her she had the right to reject his love. Camille, watching, saw that love had a wit of its own: it made Béatrix adored. Félicité soothed Calyste, asserting that unless a woman were a monster she must be flattered by such devotion.That night left marks on Calyste; he murmured Béatrix’s name as he slept. The hôtel du Guénic was calm; Mademoiselle des Touches had written to the baroness about the accident. Calyste returned to dinner at Les Touches and found Béatrix up and dressed though pale and feeble. The harshness and coldness had gone. Camille played music, leaving Calyste free to take Béatrix’s hands; no storms erupted; Félicité effaced herself.Cold, fragile, hard women like Madame de Rochefide—who have pale souls—need a thunderbolt to melt; to Béatrix, Calyste’s fury of love and his mad action were that thunderbolt. She felt humbled inwardly; a pure love bathed her heart. She breathed a genial atmosphere of feelings hitherto unknown; she felt raised to the ideal of Woman that Bretons honor. She relished the respectful adoration of youth, whose happiness cost her little; a gesture, a look, a word satisfied him. The value he placed on trifles touched and magnified her; to hold her gloved hand meant more to him than possession to another man.Few women resist such constant deification. Béatrix felt sure of being obeyed and could command Calyste to risk his life for a caprice. This gave her a noble and imposing air; she sought to remain the loftiest of women in his eyes. Her coquetries grew persistent because of inward weakness; she played the invalid for a week with charming hypocrisy, leaning on Calyste’s arm in languid dependence on walks between house and garden.Before the Croisic excursion the two women had talked of love, laughing at the ways men declare it; they agreed the cleverest men often go straight to the point. Madame de Rochefide had power to restrain Calyste by reminding him of his violence on the rocks; a look from her filled his eyes with tears and stilled his prayers and arguments. By infernal coquetry she brought him to seek Camille’s advice. Béatrix, armed with Calyste’s own letter, quoted his axiom that to love is the first happiness and to be loved comes later, using it to restrain his passion within respectful idolatry. She liked the sweet hymns of praise and adoration youth offers; she gave no definite answer. He asked only permission to love, and Calyste clung to love’s spiritual side.Driven by despair, Calyste complained to Camille. She told him she had intended to cure him by making him quickly understand Béatrix, but he had spoiled it; ten days ago he was her master, now her slave. She advised him to quarrel with her over her hardness—let her ill-treat him, then stay away until she recalled him. In extreme illness one accepts cruel remedies, and Calyste followed her advice, staying away two days. On the third he scratched at Béatrix’s door to say he and Camille awaited her for breakfast. Camille lamented he had lost another chance.During his absence Béatrix often looked through the window toward the road to Guérande. Camille found her watching the gorse’s golden blooms along the roadway and spoke of them, trying to distract her. The marquise kept them waiting long for breakfast; when she appeared her dress showed intention to fascinate and prevent another absence. After breakfast she walked with Calyste in the garden and expressed a wish to return to the rock where she had nearly perished.“Will you go with me alone?” Calyste asked.“If I refused, I should give you reason to think I found you dangerous. Alas! as I have told you, I belong to another, and I must be his only. I chose him knowing nothing of love. The fault was great, and bitter is my punishment,” she said with moist eyes. Calyste was filled with compassion that tempered his ardor; he adored her as if she were a Madonna. Béatrix was torn between herself and Calyste—between reentering the world and the young happiness he offered. She began to listen to his blind passion and allowed his pity to soothe her. Several times she was moved to tears as he commiserated her binding to an evil genius like Conti. Camille had told Calyste of Béatrix’s misery in Italy and given him lectures he profited from.“I will love you absolutely,” he said. “My only talent is to love you. My honor and pride are in your perfections. No other woman can have merit in my eyes; you are misconceived and wronged; for every wrong I will make you feel my comprehension day by day.” She listened with bowed head, letting him kiss her hands and admitting silently she was indeed an angel misunderstood. “I am too humiliated,” she said. “My past has robbed the future of all security.”It was a glorious morning for Calyste when he saw Béatrix at a window watching for him, wearing the same straw hat from their memorable first excursion. Small things of passion magnified the world for him. They left Les Touches by the garden gate onto the dunes. Béatrix loved the sands and gathered hardy little plants with rose-colored flowers to mix with Chartreux pansies and share with Calyste—an eternal relic for them. “We’ll add a bit of box,” she said smiling.They sat on the jetty awaiting the boat; Calyste told her of a juvenile escapade on the day of her arrival. “I knew of your little escapade,” she said, and that was the cause of her sternness the first night. During their walk her tone was lightly jesting with tenderness; Calyste had reason to think himself beloved. At a charming creek strewn with brilliant pebbles, when Calyste, at the summit of happiness, asked her plainly to fly with him to Ireland, she resumed her distant air, asked for his arm, and continued in silence to her Tarpeian rock.“My friend,” she said as she mounted the block of granite where she made herself a pedestal, “I have not the courage to conceal what you are to me. For ten years I have had no happiness comparable to searching for shells with you and exchanging pebbles for the necklace I will treasure more than diamonds. I have been a little girl again, such as I was at fourteen or sixteen—when I was worthy of you. The love I have inspired in you has raised me in my own eyes. Understand these words.”They reached the rock’s summit, seeing the vast ocean on one side and Brittany on the other. “But,” she continued, “I do not belong to myself; I am more bound by my own will than by law. You must be punished for my misdeed, but be satisfied to know we suffer together. Dante never saw his Beatrice again; Petrarch never had his Laura. Such disasters fall only on noble souls. But if I should be abandoned, if I fall lower into shame and ignominy and your Béatrix is cruelly misjudged, then, my adored child,” she said taking his hand, “to you she will still be first of all; you will see she rises to heaven as she leans on you; but then, if you wish to cast her down do not fail of your blow; after your love, death!”Calyste clasped her round the waist and pressed her to his heart. As if to confirm her words, Béatrix laid a tender, timid kiss on his brow. Walking back like those who perfectly understand each other—she thinking she had gained a truce, he not doubting his happiness—both were deceived. Calyste, believing from Camille’s information that Conti might be pleased to part from Béatrix, looked to chance to arrange the future; Béatrix yielded to the vagueness of her position. They reached Les Touches in bliss, entering by the garden gate whose key Calyste had taken. It was nearly six; the evening’s odors and sun harmonized with their feelings. Their steps were in unison, their silence full of mutual completeness.Suddenly, at a turn, Béatrix was seized with a horrible trembling—the contagious horror of seeing a snake—which Calyste felt before he saw the cause: on a bench beneath a weeping ash sat Conti, talking with Camille Maupin.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The marquise’s inward, convulsive trembling made the tragic drama visible to all present.“You did not expect me so soon, I fancy,” said Conti, offering his arm to Beatrix.The marquise could not avoid letting Calyste’s arm drop and taking Conti’s. This humiliating transfer overwhelmed Calyste, who flung himself on the bench beside Camille after exchanging the coldest salutations with his rival. At first he thought to attack Conti and reclaim Beatrix; but her violent trembling showed she suffered—she paid the penalty of her faults—and this so affected him that he was struck dumb, as if by an implacable necessity.Madame de Rochefide and Conti passed before Calyste’s seat. As Beatrix passed Camille, she shot Camille one of those terrible glances in which women say everything. She avoided Calyste’s eyes and turned to Conti, who seemed to jest.“What will they say to each other?” Calyste asked Camille.“Dear child, you don’t yet know the terrible rights an extinguished love gives a man over a woman. Beatrix could not refuse Conti’s arm. He’s surely jesting her about her new love; he may have guessed it from your manner and his own observation.”“Jesting her!” cried Calyste, starting up.“Be calm,” Camille counseled. “If he wounds her pride, she will crush him. But Conti is astute; he’ll act more cleverly. He’ll pretend not to suspect proud Madame de Rochefide of betrayal; he’ll paint you as an ambitious child wanting a marquise in love with him, setting himself up as arbiter. He’ll launch malicious insinuations; Beatrix will be forced to deny, and he’ll become her master again.”“He does not love her. I would leave her free. True love must be chosen anew each moment. Why did he not stay away longer? A few more days and he would not have found her. What brought him back?”“The jest of a journalist,” Camille replied. “His opera has failed. Some critic—likely Claude Vignon—said: ‘It is hard to lose fame and mistress at the same moment,’ and it cut him. Love based on petty sentiments is pitiless. He seemed weary of troubles, disgusted with life. He regrets his public alliance with the marquise, and spoke to me of past happiness in a too-clever melancholy poem. Perhaps he hoped to worm out your secret amid my flatteries.”Calyste watched Beatrix and Conti approaching but no longer listened to Camille’s words.Camille had prudently held the defensive in speaking with Conti; she revealed neither Calyste’s secret nor Beatrix’s. She knew Conti was capable of treachery, so she warned Calyste.“My dear friend,” she said, “this is the most critical moment for you. You need the caution and cleverness you do not possess; I fear you will be tricked by the most wily man I have ever known, and I can do nothing to help you.”The dinner bell rang. Conti offered his arm to Camille; Calyste gave his to Beatrix. Camille stepped back to let the marquise pass, but Beatrix found a moment to look at Calyste and, putting her finger to her lips, impressed upon him the absolute necessity of discretion.Conti was extremely gay at dinner; perhaps to probe Madame de Rochefide, who played her part ill. Had her conduct been mere coquetry she might have deceived Conti; but her new love was real and betrayed her embarrassedness. The wily musician, far from increasing her discomfort, pretended not to notice it. At dessert he steered talk to women and lauded the nobility of their sentiments: many women who abandon men in prosperity would sacrifice all in misfortune; women are constant and cling to their first lover as honor requires. His praises, meant to wound, fell like incense on the altar where one bleeding heart lay. Camille and Beatrix alone understood the bitterness beneath the eulogy. They flushed but forced themselves to control it. After dinner they took each other’s arms to return to Camille’s salon, and, as if by mutual consent, turned aside into the great salon to be alone for a moment in darkness.“It is dreadful to let Conti ride over me; yet I can’t defend myself,” Beatrix said in a low voice. “The galley-slave is always a slave to his mate. I am lost; I must go back to my galleys! And it is you, Camille, who have cast me there! Ah! you brought him back a day too soon, or a day too late. I recognize your infernal talent as author. Well, your revenge is complete, the finale perfect!”“I may have said I would write to Conti, but to do it was another matter,” cried Camille. “I am incapable of such baseness. But you are unhappy, and I forgive the suspicion.”“What will become of Calyste?” asked the marquise, with naive self-conceit.“Then Conti carries you off, does he?” Camille asked.“Ah! you think you triumph!” cried Beatrix.Anger distorted her face as she spoke to Camille, who tried to hide satisfaction under a false sympathy. The sparkle in Camille’s eyes belied the sadness of her face; Beatrix, schooled in such deceptions, saw through it. When a few minutes later they sat under a strong light on the divan where the first three weeks had hosted many comedies and where so many secret tragedies began, they examined each other for the last time and felt forever parted by undying hatred.“Calyste remains to you,” said Beatrix, looking into Camille’s eyes; “but I am fixed in his heart, and no woman can ever drive me out of it.”Camille replied, with an ironic tone that struck the marquise to the heart, quoting the famous words of Mazarin’s niece to Louis XIV: “You reign, you love, and you depart!”Neither Camille nor Beatrix noticed the absence of Conti and Calyste. The composer had remained at table with his rival, begging him to keep him company finishing a bottle of champagne.“We have something to say,” Conti added, to prevent refusal.Placed as he was, the young Breton could not refuse.“My dear friend,” the composer said in his most caressing voice as Calyste, having drunk a couple of glasses, listened, “we are both good fellows and can speak frankly. I have not come suspiciously. Beatrix loves me,”—with a gesture of self-conceit—“but the truth is, I have ceased to love her. I am not here to carry her off, but to break off our relations and leave her the honors of the rupture. You are young; you don’t yet know how useful it is to appear the victim when you are the executioner. Young men spit fire; they leave a woman in fury; they often despise her and make her hate them. But wise men do as I do; they get themselves dismissed, feigning mortification, which leaves regret in the woman’s heart and a sense of her superiority. You don’t know how hampered men are by rash promises women accept when gallantry makes us swear eternal faithfulness; let time cure what we promise. I have seen that you love Beatrix. I leave her in a position where she loses nothing of her majesty; she will certainly coquet with you, if only to tease that angel Camille Maupin. Well, take her, love her—you’ll do me a great service; I want her to turn against me. I fear her pride and virtue. Perhaps it will take time to effect this chase. On such occasions the wisest plan is to take no step at all. I did just now, walking on the lawn, attempt to let her see I knew all and was ready to congratulate her on her new happiness. Well, she was furious! At this moment I am desperately in love with a prima donna, Mademoiselle Falcon of the Grand Opera. I think of marrying her; yes, I have got as far as that. When you come to Paris you will see I have changed a marquise for a queen.”Calyste’s candid face revealed his satisfaction; he could not hide his love for Beatrix, which was all Conti wanted to discover. No man, however blasé, does not flame up again when his love is threatened by a rival. When lovers reach this extremity, both strive to act first, so deep is the wound to vanity. Questioned by the composer, Calyste related all that had happened during the last three weeks at Les Touches; Conti, concealing fury under charming good humor, received it all.“Come, let us go upstairs,” said Conti. “Women are so distrustful; those two will wonder how we can sit here together without tearing each other’s hair out; they might come down to listen. I’ll serve you faithfully, my dear boy. You’ll see me rough and jealous with the marquise; I shall seem to suspect her; there’s no better way to drive a woman to betray you. You will be happy, and I shall be free. Seem to pity that angel for belonging to a man without delicacy; show her a tear—for you can weep, you are young. I, alas! can weep no more; and that’s a great advantage lost.”Calyste and Conti went up to Camille’s salon. The composer, begged by his young rival to sing, gave them the greatest of musical masterpieces—the famous “Pria che spunti l’aurora,” which Rubini himself had performed with trembling, and which had often been Conti’s triumph. Never was his singing more extraordinary than on this occasion, when so many feelings contended in his breast. Calyste was in ecstasy. As Conti sang the first words of the cavatina, he looked intently at the marquise, giving the words a cruel signification fully understood. Camille, who accompanied him, guessed the order thus conveyed, which bowed Beatrix’s head. She looked at Calyste and felt sure he had fallen into some trap despite her advice. This conviction became certainty when the evidently happy Breton came up to bid Beatrix good-night, kissing her hand with a little air of confident triumph.By dawn, as the song foretold, Conti was carrying Beatrix away with Camille’s horses to the first relay. The morning twilight allowed Madame de Rochefide to see Guerande, its towers whitened by the dawn against the dark sky. Melancholy thoughts possessed her; she was leaving one of the sweetest flowers of her life—a pure love such as a young girl dreams of; the only true love she had known or was likely to know. The woman of the world obeyed the laws of the world; she sacrificed love to their demands just as many women sacrifice it to duty. Sometimes pride can appear as high as virtue. Read thus, this history is that of many women.The next day Calyste went to Les Touches about midday. From the window where, the day before, he had seen Beatrix watching, he saw Camille, who ran down and told the cruel truth in one word: “Gone!”“Beatrix?” asked Calyste, thunderstruck.“You have been duped by Conti; you told me nothing, and I could do nothing for you.”She led him to her little salon, where he flung himself on the divan and burst into tears. Felicite smoked her hookah and said nothing, knowing no words could arrest the first anguish of such pain. Calyste, unable even to think, sat all day in torpor. Just before dinner, Camille tried to speak after begging him to listen.“Friend,” she said, “you caused me the bitterest suffering, and I had not, like you, a beautiful young life in which to heal myself. For me, life has no spring, nor my soul a love. So, to console myself, I sought above. Here, in this room, the day before Beatrix came, I drew her portrait; I did not do her injustice, or you might have thought me jealous. I wanted you to know her as she is, for that would have kept you safe. Listen now to the full truth. Madame de Rochefide is unworthy of you. The scandal of her fall was unnecessary; she did it deliberately to play a part in society. She is one of those women who prefer the celebrity of scandal to tranquil happiness; they fly in the face of society to obtain the fatal alms of rebuke; they desire to be talked about at any cost. Beatrix was eaten up with vanity. Her fortune and wit had not given her the feminine royalty she craved; they had not enabled her to reign supreme over a salon. She then bethought herself of seeking the celebrity of the Duchesse de Langeais and the Vicomtesse de Beauseant. But the world gives its interest to real feelings only. Beatrix playing comedy was judged second-rate. There was no reason for her flight; the sword of Damocles was not suspended over her. She is neither sincere, loving, nor tender; if she were, would she have gone away with Conti this morning?”Camille talked long and eloquently; but this effort to open Calyste’s eyes was useless, and she said no more when he showed by gesture his absolute belief in Beatrix.She forced him to come down into the dining-room and sit while she dined; though he could not swallow. It is only in extreme youth that such bodily contractions occur; later organs harden. A man resists violent grief that might kill a youth because his organs are stronger.Mademoiselle des Touches was alarmed at Calyste’s calm resignation after his burst of tears. Before he left, he asked permission to go into Beatrix’s bedroom, where he had seen her on the night of her illness; there he laid his head on the pillow where hers had lain.“I am committing follies,” he said, grasping Camille’s hand, and bidding her good-night in deep dejection.He returned home to the usual company at mouche and spent the evening beside his mother. The rector, the Chevalier du Halga, and Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel all knew of Madame de Rochefide’s departure and rejoiced. Calyste would now return to them; they watched him cautiously. No one imagined the result of a first love in a heart so simple and true.For several days Calyste went regularly to Les Touches. He paced the lawn where he had walked with Beatrix. He often went to Croisic to stand on that fateful rock or lie for hours in the box-bush; by finding footholds he had means of climbing. These solitary trips, his silence, his gravity, made his mother anxious. After about two weeks of this caged-animal conduct, the poor lover ceased to cross the bay; he had scarcely strength to drag himself along the road from Guerande to the spot where he had seen Beatrix watching. The family, delighted at the departure of “those Parisians,” saw nothing fatal or diseased in the lad. The two old maids and the rector, pursuing their scheme, kept Charlotte de Kergarouet, who nightly played coquetries on Calyste, obtaining in return nothing better than advice in playing mouche. During long evenings Calyste sat between his mother and the little Breton girl, observed by the rector and Charlotte’s aunt, who discussed his mood as they walked home. Their simple minds mistook lethargic indifference for submission to their plans. One evening when Calyste, wearied, went off to bed, the players dropped their cards and looked at each other; they had heard his steps with anxiety.“Something is the matter with Calyste,” said the baroness, wiping her eyes.“Nothing is the matter,” replied Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel; “but you should marry him at once.”“Do you think marriage would divert his mind?” asked the chevalier.Charlotte, urged by the baron, arrived with unusual care. After a discourse on matrimony to which he could not answer, Calyste requested a few days’ grace. The old baron rubbed his hands with joy and whispered the “good news” to the baroness.Breakfast was gay; Charlotte sparkled. After the meal Calyste took Charlotte to the grotto. Their parents watched from the window.“They are watching us,” Charlotte said.“They cannot hear us,” he replied.“True; but they see us.”“Let us sit down, Charlotte,” he said, taking her hand.“Is it true your banner used to float from that twisted column?” she asked, already thinking of owning the house. “You will make changes, won’t you, Calyste?”“I shall not have time,” he said, taking her hands and kissing them. “I love someone you have seen and who loves me, and I cannot make any other woman happy; though from childhood you and I have been destined. But I shall wait.”“And I, too,” Charlotte said, tears in her eyes. “You cannot long love a married woman who, they say, has gone off with a singer—”“Marry, Charlotte,” Calyste interrupted. “With the fortune your aunt intends to give you, you could choose some better man than I. I have brought you here not to tell you what you know, but to entreat you, as a childhood friend, to take this rupture upon yourself and say you have rejected me. Say you do not wish to marry a man whose heart is not free; thus I shall be spared the sense that I have done you public wrong. You do not know how heavy life is now; I cannot bear the slightest struggle; I am like a man whose vital spark is gone. If it were not for the grief I should cause my mother, I would have flung myself into the sea. Do not speak of this to anyone. Good-bye, Charlotte.”He kissed her hair and left by the postern-gate to Les Touches, remaining near Camille till past midnight. Returning home at one, he found his mother waiting with her worsted-work. Softly he clasped her hand and asked, “Is Charlotte gone?”“She goes tomorrow, with her aunt, in despair,” answered the baroness. “Come to Ireland with me, my Calyste.”“Many a time I have thought of flying there—”“Ah!” cried the baroness.“With Beatrix,” he added.Some days after Charlotte’s departure, Calyste joined the Chevalier du Halga for his daily promenade. They sat on a bench with a view from Les Touches to the rocks of Croisic, against which waves dashed. Calyste was thin and pale; his strength diminished; he felt shudders denoting fever. His eyes, surrounded by dark circles, had the fixed-brightness of obsession. The chevalier was the only companion with whom he could exchange a few ideas; in him Calyste divined an apostle of his own religion and recognized vestiges of an eternal love.“Have you loved many women?” he asked.“Only one,” the chevalier replied.“Was she free?”“No,” exclaimed Du Halga. “Ah! how I suffered! She was the wife of my best friend—my protector—yet we loved each other so!”“Did she love you?” Calyste asked.“Passionately,” the chevalier replied fervently. “Until her death; she died at forty-nine during the emigration at St. Petersburg. I have thought of fetching her to Brittany and laying her near me! But she lies in my heart.”The chevalier brushed away his tears. Calyste took his hand and pressed it.“I care for this little dog more than for life,” the old man said, pointing to Thisbe. “The little darling is precisely like the one she held on her knees. I never look at Thisbe but I see Madame l’Amirale’s hands.”“Did you see Madame de Rochefide?” Calyste asked.“No,” replied the chevalier. “It is sixty-eight years since I have looked at any woman with attention—except your mother, who has some of Madame l’Amirale’s complexion.”Three days later the chevalier said, “My child, I have a hundred and forty louis laid by. When you know where Madame de Rochefide is, come and get them and follow her.”Calyste thanked him, envying his existence. But day by day he grew morose; he seemed to love no one; all things hurt him; he was gentle only to his mother. The baroness watched his decline with growing anxiety; by prayer and entreaty she made him swallow food. Toward the end of October the lad ceased to go to the mall; the chevalier came in vain to tempt him out.“We can talk of Madame de Rochefide,” the old man would say. “I’ll tell you my first adventure.”“Your son is ill,” he confided to the baroness when convinced efforts were useless.Calyste answered that he was well; but like all youths in melancholy, he took pleasure in thoughts of death. He no longer left the house but sat in the garden on a bench, warming himself in a pale sunshine, alone with his thought.Soon after Calyste ceased even to go to Les Touches, Felicite asked the rector to come. The assiduity with which Abbé Grimont called at Les Touches became the great topic of the town. The masters and servants were afflicted by Calyste’s increasing weakness though none imagined youth might die of love. Even the chevalier had no example of such a death among his memories. They attributed Calyste’s thinness to want of food. His mother implored him to eat. Calyste tried to conquer repugnance to comfort her; nourishment taken against his will only increased the slow fever consuming him.During the last days of October the cherished child could no longer mount the stairs; his bed was placed in the lower hall, surrounded by family. They finally sent for the Guerande physician, who broke the fever with quinine and reduced it in a few days, ordering Calyste to take exercise and find amusement. The baron, roused from apathy, recovered some strength; with Calyste, Gasselin, and two dogs he started for the forest, and for days they hunted. Calyste obeyed, visiting neighbors’ chateaus. But he had no spirit; nothing brought a smile. His livid, contracted features betrayed utter passivity. The baron, worn by exertion, returned home exhausted with Calyste almost as ill. For several days both were dangerously ill; the family sent, at the Guerande physician’s request, for two of the best doctors in Nantes.The baron had received a fatal shock on realizing Calyste’s change. With the lucidity of the dying he trembled that his line might perish. He prayed, and his face turned constantly toward his son’s bed. At the least motion Calyste made, a commotion stirred within him. The baroness no longer left the room where Zephirine sat knitting in horrible unease. Demands were made for wood and provisions; Zephirine, not nimble enough, gave her keys to Mariotte but insisted on knowing everything; she questioned Mariotte and her sister-in-law. One night, when father and son dozed, Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel told Zephirine she must resign herself to the baron’s death. The old woman dropped her knitting, groped, and drew from her pocket an old black chaplet, on which she prayed with a fervor that gave her withered face a new splendor; the other old woman imitated her, and all present, on a sign from the rector, joined in the spiritual uplift.“Alas! I prayed to God,” said the baroness, remembering her prayer after reading Calyste’s fatal letter, “and he did not hear me.”“Perhaps it would be well,” said the rector, “to beg Mademoiselle des Touches to come see Calyste.”“She!” cried Zephirine, “the author of all our misery! She turned him from his family, taught him impious books, an heretical language! Let her be accursed! She has destroyed the du Guenics!”“She may perhaps restore them,” the rector said gently. “Mademoiselle des Touches is a saintly woman; I am her surety. She has only good intentions toward Calyste.”“Let me know the day she sets foot in this house, that I may get out of it,” cried the old woman passionately. “She has killed both father and son. Do you not hear death in Calyste’s voice? He is so feeble he barely whispers.”At that moment the three doctors arrived. They plied Calyste with questions; their examination of the baron was short; they were surprised he still lived. The Guerande doctor calmly told the baroness that for Calyste it would probably be best to take him to Paris to consult the most experienced physicians; it would cost over a hundred louis.“People die of something, but not of love,” said Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel.“Alas! whatever be the cause, Calyste is dying,” said the baroness. “I see all the symptoms of consumption, that most horrible disease of my country, about him.”“Calyste dying!” said the baron, opening his eyes; two large tears rolled slowly down his wrinkled cheeks—the only tears he had probably ever shed. Suddenly he rose, walked to his son’s bedside, took his hand, and looked earnestly at him.“What is it you want, father?” said Calyste.“That you should live!” cried the baron.“I cannot live without Beatrix,” replied Calyste.The old man dropped into a chair.“Oh! where could we get a hundred louis to bring doctors from Paris? There is still time,” cried the baroness.“A hundred louis!” cried Zephirine; “will that save him?”Without waiting, the old maid ran her hands into her placket-holes, unfastened her petticoat, and let it fall, producing a heavy sound as coins tumbled. She knew where she had sewn her savings and ripped them out with the rapidity of magic. The gold pieces rang as they fell, one by one, into her lap. Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel gazed in amazement.“But they’ll see you!” she whispered.“Thirty-seven,” answered Zephirine, counting.“Every one will know how much you have.”“Forty-two.”“Double louis! all new! How did you get them, you who can’t see clearly?”“I felt them. Here’s one hundred and four louis,” cried Zephirine. “Is that enough?”“What is all this?” asked the Chevalier du Halga, coming in, puzzled by the old blind friend holding a petticoat full of gold.Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel explained.“I knew it,” said the chevalier. “And I have come to bring a hundred and forty louis I have been holding at Calyste’s disposal, as he knows.”The chevalier produced rouleaux from his pocket. Mariotte, seeing such wealth, sent Gasselin to lock the doors.“Gold will not give him health,” said the baroness, weeping.“But it can take him to Paris, where he can find her. Come, Calyste.”“Yes,” cried Calyste, springing up, “I will go.”“He will live,” said the baron, in a shaking voice; “and I can die—send for the rector!”The words cast terror on all present. Calyste, seeing the mortal paleness on his father’s face, came to his side. The rector, after hearing the doctors, had gone to Mademoiselle des Touches intending to bring her back; in proportion as he had formerly detested her, he now admired and protected her as a shepherd protects the most precious of his flock.When news of the baron’s approaching end spread in Guerande, a crowd gathered; peasants, paludiers, and servants knelt in the courtyard while the rector administered last sacraments to the old Breton warrior. The whole town was agitated by the probable extinction of this old Breton race; it was felt as a public calamity.The solemn ceremony affected Calyste deeply. His filial sorrow silenced the anguish of his love for a moment. During the baron’s last hour he knelt beside him, watching death come. The old man died in his chair in presence of the assembled family.“I die faithful to God and his religion,” he said. “My God! as the reward of my efforts grant that Calyste may live!”“I shall live, father, and I will obey you,” said the young man.“If you wish to make my death as happy as Fanny has made my life, swear to me to marry.”“I promise it, father.”It was touching to see Calyste, or rather his shadow, leaning on the chevalier’s arm—a spectre leading a shade—follow the baron’s coffin as chief mourner. The church and little square were crowded with country people from thirty miles round.But the baroness and Zephirine soon saw that despite his promise Calyste fell back into fatal stupor. On the day the family donned mourning the baroness took her son to a garden bench and questioned him closely. Calyste answered gently and submissively, but his replies proved only the despair of his soul.“Mother,” he said, “there is no life in me. What I eat does not feed me; the air does not refresh me; the sun feels cold; it seems to you to light that front of the house, but to me it is a blur. If Beatrix were here it would be dazzling. There is but one thing left that keeps its shape to my eyes—this flower,” and he drew from his breast the withered bunch the marquise had given him at Croisic.The baroness dared not say more. His answer seemed more indicative of madness than his silence. She saw no hope or light in the darkness.The baron’s last hours had prevented the rector from bringing Mademoiselle des Touches to Calyste. But that day, while mother and son sat on the bench, Calyste quivered at seeing Felicite through the opposite windows. She reminded him of Beatrix, and his life revived. It was to Camille the poor mother owed the first motion of joy that lightened her mourning.“Well, Calyste,” said Mademoiselle des Touches, “I want you to go to Paris with me. We will find Beatrix,” she added in a low voice.The pale, thin face flushed red and a smile brightened him.“Let us go,” he said.“We shall save him,” said Mademoiselle des Touches to the mother, who pressed her hands and wept for joy.A week after the baron’s funeral, Mademoiselle des Touches, the Baronne du Guenic, and Calyste started for Paris, leaving the household in charge of old Zephirine.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Félicité’s careful planning aimed to secure Calyste’s future. Allied with the ducal Grandlieu family—whose ducal line would end in five daughters—she had informed the Duchesse de Grandlieu about Calyste, his past, and her intentions. Having sold her rue du Mont-Blanc house for two million five hundred thousand francs, her agent bought a rue de Bourbon house for seven hundred thousand; she planned to use one million to recover the du Guenic estates and leave the remainder for Sabine de Grandlieu. Félicité knew the Grandlieus’ arrangements: the youngest daughter would marry the Vicomte de Grandlieu (the heir); Clotilde-Frédérique, the second daughter, would remain unmarried out of memory for Lucien de Rubempré though not become a nun; two sisters were already married; and Sabine, nearly twenty, was the eligible match. Félicité resolved that Sabine should cure Calyste of his passion for Béatrix.On the journey to Paris Mademoiselle des Touches revealed these plans to the baroness. The new rue de Bourbon house was being decorated to serve as Sabine and Calyste’s home if the scheme succeeded.At the hôtel de Grandlieu the baroness received the honors due her rank. Mademoiselle des Touches encouraged Calyste to see Paris, investigated the whereabouts of Béatrix (who had gone abroad), and occupied him with diversions. As the season of balls began and the duchess and her daughters presided, Calyste noticed a resemblance between Sabine and Madame de Rochefide, one of Paris’s most beautiful and charming women; this fancied likeness made him attentive to Sabine’s coquetries. Sabine liked Calyste, and the winter of 1837 passed with the young baron’s vitality restored. He listened without repugnance when his mother reminded him of his promise to his dying father and urged marriage with Sabine. Outwardly he agreed, though inwardly he remained indifferent—an indifference the baroness alone perceived and trusted time and a happy home would conquer.On the day the families gathered in the Grandlieu salon to sign the marriage contract, when the notary Léopold Hannequin began the preliminaries, Calyste, disquieted, suddenly and curtly refused Mademoiselle des Touches’s benefactions. Embarrassed, the company watched as Sabine entered and handed him a letter from Mademoiselle des Touches intended for this moment. Calyste read Camille Maupin’s letter.Camille’s letter was a renunciation and a last appeal. She wrote that before entering her convent cell she was allowed one last look at the world she abandoned, and that thought was of Calyste, who had been her whole world. As she would soon stand at another altar in a convent, betrothed to God, she implored him not to contest her chosen service. She acknowledged that if love is suffering she had indeed loved him; yet she wanted him to accept the service she could render now. Having been made happy and then miserable by him, she asked him to make her recompense by accepting a lasting joy: let her share in his material comfort, not as gift but as a trust. She had thought of his children and the old Breton house more than of him when planning to devote her Parisian profits. She begged him to accept a part of her fortune to restore the du Guenic name and sustain his lineage. Camille promised to pray for him and his family, describing her new innocence and serene devotion. Her conversion was attributed to the Abbé Grimont, who soon became vicar-general of the diocese.Reading the letter moved Calyste; he returned to the company and said, “Let us sign the contract.” The following week, after an early ceremony at Saint-Thomas d’Aquin, Calyste and Sabine left amid tears and congratulations. Men offered conventional compliments; the Duchesse de Grandlieu and Clotilde wept, fearing Sabine had been launched upon life with a husband who had not married entirely from his own free will. The baroness, sitting in a preceding carriage, had been asked by the duchess to take maternal care of Sabine. A chasseur rode as courier; servants, postilions, and the ritual accompaniments completed the convoy. The duchess hinted to Calyste that she understood him as they bade farewell.The narrative reflects on marriage’s nature: it is not mere pleasure but a game of temperamental compatibility, physical sympathies, and character harmonies. Women, especially mothers and marriageable daughters, know its risks; they weep at weddings because they grasp what is at stake, while men smile, thinking they risk little.Who can detail a honeymoon? Much belongs to the bride. Sabine, trained in the faubourg Saint-Germain’s manners and possessing virgin sincerity, proved as interesting as Mademoiselle de Chaulieu. Her letters to her mother during the honeymoon reveal her mind and temperament.In Guerande, Sabine wrote that Calyste had married with great sorrow; their difficulties were greater than her mother had anticipated. She confessed that she loved Calyste as if he were not her husband—so entirely and devotedly that she feared losing dignity but embraced devotion nevertheless. Alone in the carriage, their initial awkwardness led to polite words; she later pressed him to recount the events that had led to his despair and to the marriage he felt obliged to make. His confession about Madame de Rochefide produced an argument, but he ended with protestations of absolute forgetfulness and gestures of affection—kissing and clasping her hand—gestures that made her more devoted, even proud at having cured a wound he had thought incurable. She acknowledged that her devotion risked reducing her dignity but believed that loving him absolutely might be worth that danger.Days later she wrote that after a few days of intimacy they abandoned formalities. Her mother-in-law, trying to take her mother’s place, was charming and attentive; she sensed Sabine’s heroism and hid her anxiety in precaution. At the sight of Guerande’s towers, Sabine tested Calyste, asking if he had truly forgotten Béatrix; Calyste’s delight at such confidence led Sabine to devote herself entirely to excising Madame de Rochefide from his memory. She admitted her devotion had made her his slave, though she resolved not to give up her self-respect completely.By May 15, Sabine declared marriage delightful. She described playing the châtelaine—riding through forests, visiting farms, dining on oaken tables with centenary linen, attending local festivities, and receiving affection from tenants and neighbors. Calyste had assumed the seigneurial role with ease; peasants greeted him as a “beautiful seigneur” and likened him to former Guenics. Félicité’s repurchase of the du Guenic estates had drawn the tenants’ admiration, and they proposed fair leases. Calyste planned to quarter his arms with those of Des Touches and adopt a motto; the couple visited the convent with the Abbé Grimont, who affirmed that Félicité was indeed saintly. Mademoiselle des Touches, thinner and paler, would not receive Calyste but welcomed Sabine, speaking candidly.Mademoiselle des Touches advised Sabine in measured terms. She explained that she had entered the order of Saint-François de Sales to discipline the intellect rather than mortify the body; her “guilty” head had deceived her heart. She urged Sabine to preserve her happiness by exercising the “science of a woman’s life”: be capricious, coquettish—not by odious scheming but by artful economy between usury and prodigality—so as to bind Calyste to her, bear him children, and secure his respect for their mother. Above all, Mademoiselle des Touches counselled that Sabine should try to prevent Calyste from ever meeting Béatrix again.The name Béatrix stunned them both. When Sabine asked whether she should return to Guerande, Mademoiselle des Touches answered yes but warned, “Never go to Les Touches. I did wrong to give him that property.” She called Les Touches “Bluebeard’s chamber,” a place that could awaken a sleeping passion and thus be dangerously compromising.Sabine summarized their conversation and confessed she could not fully follow the advice to be capricious; her nature made her give herself over to love. She feared losing the happiness she had so willingly seized, yet clung to her devotion like a mother to an infant, trembling at any threat.Note: When Béatrix was first published in 1839, the volume ended with a paragraph stating that Calyste, rich and married to the most beautiful woman in Paris, retained a sadness which nothing dispelled—not even the birth of a son at Guerande in 1839—and that Béatrix still lived deep in his heart, leaving uncertain what disasters might follow should he meet Madame de Rochefide again. In 1842 that concluding paragraph was suppressed and the story continued as above.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Guerande, July, 1838.To Madame la Duchesse de Grandlieu:Ah, my dear mamma! After three months I know what jealousy is. My heart completes its experience; I now feel the deepest hatred and the deepest love. I am more than betrayed—I am not loved. How fortunate to have a mother, a heart on which to cry out as I will!Tell wives who are still half girls to avoid a certain keyhole on the map, to beware Les Touches, and you at once rouse every Eve’s curiosity. What a root of bitterness Mademoiselle des Touches planted in my love! Why forbid me Les Touches? What sort of happiness is mine if it depends on a visit to a paltry house in Brittany? Is there anything to fear? Add the woman’s desire to know whether her power is solid or precarious, and you’ll understand how I asked one day, carelessly:“What sort of place is Les Touches?”“Les Touches belongs to you,” said my mother-in-law.“If Calyste had never set foot in Les Touches!” cried my aunt Zephirine.“He would not be my husband,” I replied.“Then you know what happened there?” said my mother-in-law, slyly.“It is a place of perdition!” exclaimed Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel. “Mademoiselle des Touches sinned there.”“But they saved that noble woman’s soul, and made the fortune of a convent,” added the Chevalier du Halga.“Would you like to go to Les Touches?” asked my mother-in-law.“No, no!” I said hastily.Doesn’t that scene read like a page from some diabolical drama? It repeated until my mother-in-law finished by saying she understood my reluctance and approved it. An involuntary stab like that would have made you test whether your happiness rested on so frail a foundation. Calyste never proposed visiting that hermitage, now his property. But when we love we lack common sense; his silence piqued me. I asked one day, “What are you afraid of at Les Touches, that you never speak of it?”“Let us go there,” he replied.So I was caught—like other women who want to be caught, trusting chance to cut the Gordian knot. To Les Touches we went.It is enchanting, in a profoundly artistic style. I took delight in that place of horror where Mademoiselle des Touches had so earnestly forbidden me to go. Poisonous flowers are charming; Satan has his flowers too; we need only look within ourselves to see both at work. What delicious acridity in a situation where I played not with fire but with ashes! I studied Calyste; the point was to know if that passion was thoroughly extinct. I watched him as he moved from room to room like a child looking for something hidden. At first he seemed thoughtful; I thought I had vanquished the past. I felt strong enough to mention Madame de Rochefide—whom in my heart I called la Rocheperfide. At last we went to see the famous bush where Beatrix had been caught when he flung her into the sea that she might never belong to another man.“She must be light indeed to have stayed there,” I said laughing. Calyste kept silence, so I added, “We’ll respect the dead.”Still Calyste was silent.“Have I displeased you?” I asked.“No; but cease to galvanize that passion,” he answered.What a speech! Calyste, when he saw me cast down, redoubled his care and tenderness.August.I was, alas, at the edge of a precipice, amusing myself, like melodrama heroines, by gathering flowers. Suddenly a horrible thought rode through my happiness like the horse in the German ballad. I thought I saw Calyste’s love increasing through reminiscences; that he expended on me the stormy emotions I revived by reminding him of the coquetries of that hateful Beatrix—think of it! that cold, unhealthy nature, so persistent yet so flabby, dares to call itself Beatrix, Beatrice!I am forced to keep one eye open to suspicion when my heart is all Calyste’s; and isn’t it a catastrophe when the eye gets the better of the heart, and suspicion at last is justified? It came to pass thus:“This place is dear to me,” I said to Calyste one morning, “because I owe my happiness to it; and so I forgive you for taking me sometimes for another woman.”The loyal Breton blushed, and I threw my arms around his neck. But all the same I have left Les Touches, and never will I go back.The very strength of hatred which makes me long for Madame de Rochefide’s death—ah, heavens! a natural death, pleurisy, or some accident—makes me understand the power of my love for Calyste. That woman troubles my sleep; I see her in dreams; shall I ever meet her bodily? Ah! the postulant of the Visitation was right—Les Touches is a fatal spot; Calyste there recovered his past emotions, and they are, I see, more powerful than the joys of our love. Ascertain, my dear mamma, if Madame de Rochefide is in Paris, for if she is I shall stay in Brittany. Poor Mademoiselle des Touches might repent of her share in our marriage if she knew to what extent I am taken for our odious rival! I am ashamed. A frantic desire seizes me sometimes to fly from Guerande and those sands of Croisic.August 25th.I am determined to live in the ruins of the old château. Calyste, worried by my restlessness, agrees to take me. Either he knows life so little that he guesses nothing, or he does know the cause of my flight, in which case he cannot love me. I tremble with fear lest I find the certainty I seek—that, like a child, I put my hands before my eyes not to hear the explosion—Oh, mother! I am not loved with the love I feel. Calyste is charming to me, that’s true; but what man, unless a monster, would not be amiable and gracious when receiving the flowers of the soul of a young girl of twenty, brought up by you, pure, loving, and beautiful, as many have said I am.Guenic, September 18.Has he forgotten her? That’s the solitary thought echoing through my soul like remorse. Ah, dear mamma, have all women to struggle against memories as I do? None but innocent young men should be married to pure young girls. But that’s a deceptive Utopia; better have one’s rival in the past than in the future.Ah mother, pity me, though at this moment I am happy as a woman who fears to lose her happiness and clings to it—one way of killing it, says that wise Clotilde.I notice that for the last five months I think only of myself, that is, of Calyste. Tell sister Clotilde her melancholy bits of wisdom often recur to me. She is happy in being faithful to the dead; she fears no rival. A kiss to my dear Athénaïs, about whom I see Juste is beside himself. From what you told me it is evident he fears you will not give her to him. Cultivate that fear as a precious product. Athénaïs will be sovereign lady; I who fear I can never win Calyste back shall always be a servant.A thousand tendernesses, dear mamma. Ah! if my terrors are not delusions, Camille Maupin has sold me her fortune dearly. My affectionate respects to papa.These letters explain the secret relation between husband and wife. Sabine thought of a love marriage where Calyste saw only a marriage of expediency. The honeymoon’s joys had not entirely conformed to social expectations.During the couple’s stay in Brittany the restoration and furnishing of the hôtel du Guénic went on under the architect Grindot, with Clotilde and the Duc and Duchesse de Grandlieu superintending; arrangements were made for returning to Paris in December 1838. Sabine installed herself in the rue de Bourbon with pleasure—less from the desire to preside over a great household than to see what her family would think of her marriage.Calyste, with easy indifference, let Clotilde and the duchess guide him in society, and they were grateful for his obedience. He obtained his place through name, fortune, and alliance. Sabine’s success as one of the most charming women in Paris, the diversions of high society, duties to fulfill, and winter amusements gave a fresh life to the young household through excitements and interludes. Sabine, considered fortunate by her family, cast aside her gloomy notions; hearing her lot envied, she banished terrors into chimeras, until pregnancy gave guarantees to this neutral union. In October 1839 the young Baronne du Guénic had a son, and committed the common mistake of nursing it herself, thinking a mother must be wholly the child’s mother.Toward the end of the following summer, August 1840, Sabine had nearly finished nursing. Calyste, during two years in Paris, had shed the innocence of his first appearance in love. He was now intimate with the young Duc Georges de Maufrigneuse, newly married, and other habitués of his mother-in-law’s salon; he understood the differences between Parisian and provincial life. Wealth has idle hours which Paris knows how to entertain. Contact with husbands who deserted home for cigars, whist, clubs, or the turf undermined domestic virtues. A wife’s maternal solicitude often supports a husband’s dissipations. A wife is proud to see him return after liberty.One evening in October, to escape the crying of their newly weaned child, Calyste—on whose brow Sabine could not bear a frown—went, urged by her, to the Variétés. The footman had engaged a stall near the avant-scène. During the first interlude Calyste saw in a proscenium box Madame de Rochefide. Beatrix in Paris! The two thoughts pierced Calyste’s heart. To see her again after nearly three years convulsed him. He had sometimes substituted Beatrix for his wife so plainly that Sabine had perceived it. Beatrix was light and motion; Sabine was duty and expectation. One became pleasure; the other weariness. It was like a thunderbolt.From a sense of loyalty Calyste first thought to leave. As he left the orchestra stalls he saw the proscenium door half-open, and his feet took him there. He found Beatrix between two distinguished men, Canalis and Raoul Nathan. In three years she had changed, and the transformation made her more poetic to Calyste. Until thirty, pretty Parisian women need only clothing; after thirty they seek weapons and embellishments; they pass from nature to art.Beatrix, deserted by Conti, had become an artist in dress and coquetry. “Why is Conti not here?” Calyste asked Canalis, who warned him with a finger on his lip.“I am happy to see you,” Beatrix demurely said. “I said to myself when I recognized you that you at least would not disown me. Ah! my Calyste,” she whispered, “why did you marry—and with such a little fool!”A woman whispering to a newcomer and making him sit beside her naturally isolates the pair.“Come, Nathan,” said Canalis; “Madame la marquise will allow me to go say a word to d’Arthez.”The departure gave Calyste time to recover; but he nearly lost strength again as he inhaled the perfume, entrancing though venomous, of Beatrix. She had assumed a virgin air that evening—hair loosely draped, a gossamer scarf about her throat, clever corsetry—the whole a studied effect. A woman who can create herself beautiful seems to promise many resources for love. Deserted women usually love deeply and know the art of loving; Beatrix’s nature, though hollower, profited by artifice.“It is not a question of loving a man,” she had been saying; “we must tease and harass him if we want to keep him.”Calyste, wounded by a speech about his wife, hesitated between dignity as a husband and a defense of Sabine. The marquise had made that speech to know how far her empire yet extended. Seeing his weakness, she relieved his embarrassment.“Well, dear friend, you find me alone,” she said. “You forget me!”“You!” he replied; “but you are married. That was one of my griefs.”“Not only—I said to myself—I lose love, but I have lost a friendship I thought was Breton. Alas! we can make ourselves bear everything. Now I suffer less, but I am broken, exhausted! Obliged to seem proud before indifferent persons, and arrogant as if I had never fallen, and having lost my dear Félicité, there was no ear into which I could cry, I suffer! But to you I can tell the anguish I endured on seeing you so near me. Yes,” she said to a gesture of Calyste’s, “it is almost fidelity. That is how it is with misery; a look, a visit, a mere nothing is everything to us. Ah! you once loved me—you—as I deserved.”She played her eyes to effect. Calyste, speechless, let tears fall. Beatrix caught his hand and pressed it.“Thank you, Calyste, thank you, my poor child; that is how a true friend responds to grief. No, don’t add another word; leave me now; people are looking; it might cause trouble to your wife if someone said we were seen together—innocently enough, before a thousand people! There, you see I am strong; adieu—”She wiped her eyes, then laughed to remove the melancholy.“But,” said Calyste, “where can I go to see you?”“I am hidden in the rue de Chartres opposite the Parc Monceau, in a little house suitable to my means; I cram my head with literature—but only for myself to distract thoughts. Now go, leave me; what will the world say! Adieu—oh Calyste, my friend, if you stay another minute I shall burst into tears!”Calyste withdrew after holding out his hand and feeling that strange double pressure—full of seductive tingling.For the rest of the evening Beatrix did not look at Calyste directly, but sidelong glances tore him.At home the splendour of his apartments made him think of the mediocrity Beatrix had spoken of, and he hated his wealth because it could not belong to that fallen angel. He cursed the power of divination love had bestowed upon Sabine. When adored, a wife reads his face as in a book and asks if the cause is herself. Calyste feared he could not conceal the change within him.“How shall I manage tomorrow?” he said, dreading Sabine’s inspection. She might ask, “Do you still love me?” or “I don’t weary you, do I?”Next day Calyste, who loved his child, quivered with joy to learn Sabine watched the baby for fear of the croup. The baron made a pretext of business and went out, escaping like a prisoner, happy to go to a café where he had liked to breakfast as a bachelor.What is there in love? Does Nature rebel against social yoke? Calyste, brought up reverently, bounded toward a woman unworthy of him when fate had given him a young creature whose beauty was aristocratic, keen mind, piety, and loving attachment. Perhaps the noblest men retain some clay; the slough still pleases them. Madame de Rochefide, amid poetic pretensions and despite her fall, belonged to high nobility; she hid the courtesan beneath an aristocratic exterior. That does not fully account for Calyste’s passion. Vanity and the wish to triumph over a splendid young woman may have combined with his nature.Calyste lived on one sentence—“I shall see her again!”—and at two o’clock set out to the rue de Chartres. He recognized the house. The old footman asked his name; Calyste waited, thinking he ought to leave Beatrix freedom to receive him or not.Beatrix, like many great ladies who break their chain, had left her fortune to her husband when she fled; she could not beg from her tyrant. Conti and Mademoiselle des Touches had spared Beatrix petty worries; her mother often sent money. Finding herself on her own, she had taken a little house with a charming garden, rent eighteen hundred francs. Still served by an old footman, a maid, and a cook faithful through vicissitudes, her penury was opulence to many bourgeoises.Calyste climbed stairs with flowering landings. Silk and velvet furnished the rooms. On a mantel he saw two old celadons and a silver cup attributed to Benvenuto Cellini. On a table jewelled knick-knacks and a book with a dagger as paper-cutter—symbol of criticism—hung beside ten watercolors representing bedrooms where Beatrix had stayed, the measure of her impertinence.A silk rustle announced Beatrix in a studied toilette that told a roué her coming was awaited. The gown revealed a white bosom, sleeves of tulle trimmed with lace; hair escaped a cap of lace and flowers.“Already!” she said smiling. “A lover could not have shown more eagerness. You must have secrets to tell me, have you not?”She posed on a sofa. By chance—or choice—Beatrix wore the perfume she used at Les Touches. The scent, the dress, the glance in the semi-darkness turned Calyste’s brain. The luckless man was again impelled to violence that had once almost cost Beatrix her life; but she rose to ring the bell, laying a finger on his lips. Calyste controlled himself when he saw Beatrix had no inimical intent.“Antoine, I am not at home—for everyone,” she said. “Put some wood on the fire. I treat you as a friend,” she continued, “therefore do not treat me as a mistress. I have two remarks. First, I should not deny myself foolishly to any man I really loved; secondly, I am determined to belong to no other man on earth. I believed, Calyste, I was loved by a species of Rizzio, a man absolutely free, and see to what that fatal confidence led me. As for you, you are under the yoke of the most sacred of duties; you have a young, amiable, delightful wife; you are a father. We should be two fools—”“My dear Beatrix, all these reasons vanish before a single word—I have never loved but you on earth, and I was married against my will.”“Ah! a trick played upon us by Mademoiselle des Touches,” she said, smiling.Three hours passed as Beatrix spoke to Calyste about conjugal faith, pointing out the horrible alternative of renouncing Sabine. She affected to regard Sabine’s sacrifice as small; she knew her so well!“My dear child,” she said, “that’s a woman who fulfils the promises of girlhood. She is a Grandlieu, to be sure, but she’s as brown as her mother the Portuguese, not to say yellow, and as dry and stiff as her father. To tell the truth, your wife will never go wrong; she’s a big boy who can take care of herself. Poor Calyste! is that the sort of woman you needed? She has fine eyes, but such eyes are common in Italy and Spain and Portugal. Can any woman be tender with bones like hers? Eve was fair; brown women descend from Adam; blondes come from God.”About six o’clock Calyste, driven to desperation, took his hat.“Yes, go, my poor friend,” she said; “don’t give her the annoyance of dining without you.”Calyste stayed. At his age it was easy to snare him on his worst side.“What! you dare to dine with me?” said Beatrix, feigning surprise. “My poor food does not alarm you? Have you enough independence to crown me with joy by this proof of affection?”“Let me write a note to Sabine; otherwise she will wait dinner for me till nine.”“Here,” she said, “this is the table at which I write.”She lighted candles and took one to the table to look over what he wrote.“My dear Sabine—”“‘My dear’?—can you really say that your wife is still dear to you?” she asked, looking at him with a cold eye that froze the marrow. “Go—you had better go and dine with her.”“I dine at a restaurant with some friends.”“A lie. Oh, fy! you are not worthy to be loved either by her or me. Men are cowards in their treatment of women. Go, monsieur, go and dine with your dear Sabine.”Calyste flung back in his arm-chair and became pale as death. Presently he sat up, put his elbow on the table, his chin in his hand, and looked at the implacable Beatrix with a flashing eye. He was so superb that any woman might have fallen; but Beatrix, born on the borders of Normandy and Brittany, belonged to a race with ferocity; she wanted terrible notoriety as vengeance, and yielded to no weakness.“Dictate what I ought to write,” said the luckless man. “But, in that case—”“Well, yes!” she said, “you shall love me then as you loved me at Guerande. Write: I dine out; do not expect me.”“What next?” said Calyste.“Nothing; sign it. Good,” she said, darting on the note with restrained joy. “I will send it by a messenger.”Calyste returned about two in the morning. After waiting till half-past twelve, Sabine had gone to bed overwhelmed with fatigue. She slept, though keenly distressed by the laconic wording of his note. Still, she explained it. A woman’s true love invariably begins by explaining things to the man’s advantage. Calyste had been pressed for time, she said.The next morning the child was better; Sabine, smiling, came with little Calyste to present him to his father before breakfast with the pretty fooleries and senseless words gay young mothers use. The scene gave Calyste the chance to keep countenance. He was charming, thinking in his heart he was a monster, and he played like a child; but Sabine had not yet learned to read such distinctions.At breakfast she asked: “What did you do yesterday?”“Portenduere kept me to dinner,” he replied, “and after we went to the club to play whist.”“That’s a foolish life, my Calyste,” she said. She scolded him with a mother’s earnestness, then, with a mother’s glance, added: “Admit that the first note you ever wrote me was rather stiff.”“I did not think of sending you word till I got to the club.”“But you wrote on a woman’s note-paper; it had a perfume of feminine elegance.”“Those club directors are such dandies!”The Vicomte de Portenduere and his wife had become intimate with the du Guénics; the two young women were friends through exchanged counsels about infants.While Calyste, novice in falsehood, thought, “I must warn Savinien,” Sabine thought, “I am sure that paper bore a coronet.” This flashed through her mind; she scolded herself but resolved to find the paper, which she had flung into her letter-box.After breakfast Calyste went out saying he would return soon. He jumped into a small carriage and drove in minutes to the vicomte’s to beg him, with rights of return, to fib if Sabine questioned the vicomtesse. Thence he rushed to rue de Chartres to know how Beatrix had passed the night. He found her fresh from her bath, embellished, breakfasting with appetite. He admired her grace and the beauty of the gold service she used.Calyste listened entranced to her witty speeches until she wept when he rose to leave. He thought he had been there half an hour, but it was past three when he reached home. His English horse was so bathed in sweat it looked as if driven through the sea. By chance Sabine was at a window looking on the courtyard, impatient at his lateness; the condition of the horse surprised her.“Where can he have come from?”That question, whispered by the power that forebodes, showed the unseen and created belief.“Where do you come from, dear angel?” Sabine said to Calyste on the landing. “Abd-el-Kader is nearly foundered. You told me you’d be gone but a moment, and I have been waiting these three hours.”“Well, well,” thought Calyste, making progress in dissimulation, “I must get out of it by a present.” He took her round the waist with more cajolery than usual. “Dear little mother, they are finishing a dressing-table at which true artists have worked, and my mother and aunt Zephirine contributed.”Sabine clasped him and held him, faint with happiness at dispersing her dark doubt. It was one of those transports no love can prodigally spend. Then, as if bitten by a viper, she left him, threw herself on a sofa and fainted, the reaction of a chill to her glowing heart nearly killing her. As she had held Calyste, she had smelled the perfume of that note-paper—another woman’s head had lain there; she had kissed where the rival’s kisses were still warm.“What is the matter?” asked Calyste, who brought her back by smelling salts.“Fetch the doctor and my nurse! Yes, my milk has turned; they won’t come unless you fetch them—go!”Calyste rushed out. At the closing of the porte-cochère Sabine sprang up like a frightened doe, walked about the salon crying, “My God! my God! my God!”Those words took the place of thought. The crisis she had feared took place. Her hairs felt like red-hot needles; her blood seemed to mingle with her nerves. She was blind for moments, and cried, “I am dying!”Her maid ran in. Laid upon her bed and recovered, Sabine first sent the maid to Madame de Portenduere. She found her ideas whirling.She rang for the footman and, mastered by a mad desire for certainty, wrote:To Madame la Baronne du Guénic:Dear Mamma,—When you come to Paris, as you allow us to hope, I shall thank you in person for the beautiful present by which you and my aunt Zephirine and Calyste wish to reward me for doing my duty. I was already repaid by my happiness. I can never express the pleasure you have given me in that beautiful dressing-table, but when you are with me I shall try to do so. Believe me, when I array myself before that treasure I shall think, like the Roman matron, that my noblest jewel is our little angel, etc.She directed the letter to Guerande and gave it to the footman to post.When the Vicomtesse de Portenduere came, the chill of reaction had succeeded the first paroxysm. “Ursula, I think I am going to die,” she said.“What is the matter, dear?”“Where did Savinien and Calyste go after they dined with you yesterday?”“Savinien and I dined alone and went to the Opera without Calyste,” Ursula said, surprised.“Ursula, dearest, in the name of your love for Savinien, keep silence about what I will tell you. You alone shall know why I die—I am betrayed! at the end of three years, at twenty-two!”Her teeth chattered, her eyes dull, her face the greenish tinge of an old Venetian mirror.“You! so beautiful! For whom?”“I don’t know yet. But Calyste has told me two lies. Do not pity me; do not seem incensed; pretend ignorance and perhaps you can find who she is through Savinien. Oh! that letter of yesterday!”Trembling, she sprang to a piece of furniture and took the letter. “See,” she said, lying down, “the coronet of a marquise! Find if Madame de Rochefide has returned to Paris. Am I to have a heart in which to weep? Oh, dearest—to see one’s beliefs, poesy, idol, virtue, happiness, all in pieces! No God in the sky! no love on earth! no life in my heart! I scarcely feel the horrible sufferings in my body. Happily, the baby is weaned; my milk would have poisoned him.”Tears flowed.Pretty Madame de Portenduere, holding the fatal letter, was at first stupefied, shocked by this agony in spite of Sabine’s incoherent attempts to relate facts. Suddenly Ursula was illuminated by an idea only sincere friends have.“I must save her!” she thought. “Trust me, Sabine,” she cried. “Wait; I will find out the truth.”“Ah! in my grave I’ll love you,” exclaimed Sabine.Ursula went to the Duchesse de Grandlieu, pledged secrecy, and explained the situation.“Madame,” she said, “in order to avoid some fatal illness—we had better confide the truth to the doctor, and invent a tale to clear Calyste and make him seem innocent for the time being.”“My dear child,” said the duchess, chilled, “friendship has given you the experience of an older woman. I know how Sabine loves her husband; you are right—she might go mad.”“Or lose her beauty, which would be worse,” said the viscountess.“Let us go to her!” cried the duchess.They arrived before the accoucheur, Dommanget. “Ursula has told me everything,” said the duchess to her daughter, “and you are mistaken. In the first place, Madame de Rochefide is not in Paris. As for your husband, he lost a great deal of money at cards, so he does not even know how to pay for your dressing-table.”“But that?” said Sabine, holding the fatal letter.“That!” said the duchess, laughing; “why, that is written on Jockey Club paper; everybody writes on coroneted paper now; even our stewards will soon be titled.”The prudent mother threw the unlucky paper into the fire.When Calyste and Dommanget arrived, the duchess, who had arranged matters, was informed. She left Sabine to Madame de Portenduere and stopped Dommanget and Calyste in the salon.“Sabine’s life is at stake, monsieur,” she said to Calyste; “you have betrayed her for Madame de Rochefide.”Calyste blushed, detected in a fault.“And,” continued the duchess, “as you do not know how to deceive, you have behaved so clumsily that Sabine has guessed the truth. But I have for the present repaired your blunder. You do not wish my daughter’s death, I am sure. Monsieur Dommanget, this will put you on the track of her real illness and its cause. As for you, Calyste, an old woman like me understands your error though she does not pardon it. Such pardons come only by a lifetime of after happiness. If you wish me to esteem you, you must save my daughter; next, you must forget Madame de Rochefide; she is only worth having once. Learn to lie; have the courage of a criminal and his impudence. I have just told a lie myself, and I shall do penance for that mortal sin.”She told the two men the lies she had invented. The physician at the bedside studied the means of repair and ordered measures requiring rapid execution. Calyste sat at the foot of the bed striving to put tenderness into his glance.“So it was play that put those black circles round your eyes?” Sabine said feebly.The words made the doctor, mother, and viscountess tremble. Calyste turned red as a cherry.“That’s what comes of nursing a child,” said Dommanget roughly but cleverly. “Husbands are lonely when separated from their wives, and they go to the club and play. But you needn’t worry over the thirty thousand francs Monsieur le baron lost last night—”“Thirty thousand francs!” cried Ursula.“Yes,” replied Dommanget. “They told me this morning at the house of the young Duchesse Berthe de Maufrigneuse that Monsieur de Trailles won that money from you,” he added, turning to Calyste. “Why do you play with such men? Frankly, monsieur le baron, I can well believe you are ashamed.”Seeing his mother-in-law, a pious duchess, the viscountess, and the accoucheur all lying like dealers in bric-à-brac, the kind Calyste understood the danger; two heavy tears rolled from his eyes and completely deceived Sabine.“Monsieur,” she said, sitting up and looking angrily at Dommanget, “Monsieur du Guénic can lose thirty, fifty, a hundred thousand francs if it pleases him, without any one having a right to think it wrong. It is far better Monsieur de Trailles should win his money than that we should win Monsieur de Trailles’.”Calyste rose, took his wife round the neck, kissed her on both cheeks and whispered:—“Sabine, you are an angel!”Two days later the young wife was thought out of danger, and the next day Calyste was at Madame de Rochefide’s making merit of his infamy.“Beatrix,” he said, “you owe me happiness. I have sacrificed my poor little wife to you; she discovered all. That fatal paper bore your name and coronet, which I never noticed—I saw but you! Fortunately the ‘B’ was by chance effaced. But the perfume you left upon me and the lies I involved myself in have betrayed my happiness. Sabine nearly died; her milk went to her head; erysipelas set in, and she may bear marks for the rest of her days.”As Beatrix listened her face was due North, icy enough to freeze the Seine.“So much the better,” she said; “perhaps it will whiten her for you.”Beatrix, now hard as bone and sharp as her voice, continued a series of atrocious sarcasms. There is no greater blunder than for a man to talk of his virtuous wife to his mistress, unless to talk of his mistress to his wife. But Calyste had not received the Parisian education in the politeness of passions. He knew neither how to lie to his wife nor how to tell his mistress the truth—two apprenticeships necessary to manage women. He was therefore forced to use all passion’s power to obtain from Beatrix a pardon she made him solicit for two hours; a pardon refused by an injured angel who raised her eyes to the ceiling to avoid seeing the guilty man, and who mustered reasons sacred to marquises in a voice quivering with tears furtively wiped with lace.“To speak to me of your wife the very day after my fall!” she cried. “Why did you not tell me she is a pearl of virtue? I know she thinks you handsome; pure depravity! I, I love your soul! let me tell you, my friend, you are ugly compared to many shepherds on the Campagna,” etc.Such speeches were part of a system meditated by Beatrix in this her third incarnation—for at each passion a woman becomes another being. Clever women are never deceived about themselves; they count wrinkles, see crow’s-feet, and know themselves. Therefore to struggle successfully against a splendid young woman, Beatrix used the knowledge and science of courtesans.
