Without acknowledging her baseness, led by an instinctive passion for Calyste’s beauty, she resolved to make him think himself unpleasant, ugly, ill-made, and to behave as if she hated him. No system is more fruitful with conquering men. To such natures the presence of repugnance to be vanquished renews the triumph of the first day. It is flattery in disguise. Beatrix added contempt to constant comparison of her poetic, comfortable home with the hôtel du Guénic. Deserted wives abandon their surroundings; she counted on this and began an underhand attack on the faubourg Saint-Germain’s luxury, calling it stupid.The scene of reconciliation, where Beatrix made Calyste swear hatred to his wife—who she said played comedy—took place in a bower where she played off her graces amid ravishing flowers and rare plants. The science of nothings she carried to excess. Fallen by Conti’s desertion, Beatrix was determined to have the fame unprincipled conduct gives. The misfortune of the poor young wife, a rich and beautiful Grandlieu, should be her pedestal.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When a woman returns to ordinary life after nursing her first child she reappears in the world embellished and charming. This phase of maternity rejuvenates older women and gives young ones a splendor of freshness, a brisk activity, a brio of mere existence. In trying to return to the charming habits of the honeymoon, Sabine discovered that her husband was not the former Calyste. But Sabine, unhappy, observed him instead of resting in her happiness. She sought for the fatal perfume, and smelt it. This time she no longer confided in her friend, nor in the mother who had so charitably deceived her. She wanted certainty, and Certainty made no long tarrying. Certainty is never wanting, it is like the sun; and presently shades are asked for to keep it out. It is, in matters of the heart, a repetition of the fable of the woodman calling upon Death—we soon ask Certainty to leave us blind.One morning, about two weeks after the first crisis, Sabine received this terrible letter:Guerande.To Madame la Baronne du Guenic:My dear Daughter,—Your aunt Zephirine and I are lost in conjectures about the dressing-table of which you tell us in your letter. I have written to Calyste about it, and I beg you to excuse our ignorance. You can never doubt our hearts, I am sure. We are piling up riches for you here. Thanks to the advice of Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel on the management of your property, you will find yourself within a few years in possession of a considerable capital without losing any of your income.Your letter, dear child as dearly loved as if I had borne you in my bosom and fed you with my milk, surprised me by its brevity, and above all by your silence about my dearest little Calyste. You told me nothing of the great Calyste either, but then, I know that he is happy, etc.Sabine wrote across this letter these words, “Noble Brittany does not always lie.” She then laid the paper on Calyste’s desk.Calyste found the letter and read it. Seeing Sabine’s sentence and recognizing her handwriting he flung the letter into the fire, determined to pretend that he had never received it. Sabine spent a week in an agony known only to angelic or solitary souls never touched by the bad angel. Calyste’s silence terrified her.“I, who ought to be all gentleness, all pleasure to him, I have displeased him, wounded him! My virtue has made itself hateful. I have no doubt humiliated my idol,” she said to herself. These thoughts ploughed furrows in her heart. She wanted to ask pardon for her fault, but Certainty let loose upon her other proofs. Grown bold and insolent, Beatrix wrote to Calyste at his own home; Madame du Guenic received the letter, gave it to her husband unopened, and said to him, in a changed voice: “My friend, that letter is from the Jockey Club; I recognize both the paper and the perfume.”Calyste colored, and put the letter into his pocket.“Why don’t you read it?”“I know what it is about.”The young wife sat down. No longer did fever burn her; she wept no more; but a madness that in weak beings leads to crimes—even arsenic—possessed her. At this moment little Calyste was brought in, and she took him in her arms to dance him. The child, just awakened, sought the breast beneath the gown.“He remembers,—he, at any rate,” she said in a low voice.Calyste went to his own room to read his letter. When he left, the poor young woman burst into tears, weeping as women do when alone.Pain, like pleasure, has its initiation. The first crisis, like Sabine's, does not recur; it is the first wedge in the heart's torture; later shocks are expected, the nerves primed and forces already spent. So Sabine, sure of betrayal, spent three hours with her son beside the fire, surprising herself, when Gasselin, acting as footman, came to say:“Madame is served.”“Let monsieur know.”“Monsieur does not dine at home, Madame la baronne.”Who knows the torture for a twenty-three-year-old finding herself alone in the great dining-room of an old mansion, served by silent servants, under circumstances like these?“Order the carriage,” she said suddenly; “I shall go to the Opera.”She dressed superbly to show herself smiling. Amid remorse for the postscript she had added to Madame de Rochefide’s letter, she resolved to conquer, to win back Calyste by loving kindness, by the virtues of a wife, by the gentleness of the paschal lamb. She wished also to deceive all Paris. She loved—loved as courtesans and as angels love, with pride and humility. But the opera chanced to be Otello. When Rubini sang Il mio cor si divide, she rushed away. Music is sometimes mightier than actor or poet. Savinien de Portenduere accompanied Sabine to the peristyle and put her in the carriage without understanding this sudden flight.Madame du Guenic now entered a phase of suffering peculiar to the aristocracy. When you see on the arms of envious, miserable women golden serpents with diamond heads, necklaces clasped about their necks, remember those vipers sting and those bonds burn the flesh. Luxury is dearly bought. In situations like Sabine’s, women curse the pleasures of wealth; salon gilding is indifferent, silk divans seem coarse, exotic flowers are nettles, perfumes poison, choicest cookery scrapes the throat like barley-bread, and life becomes as bitter as the Dead Sea.Two or three examples may show this reaction of luxury upon happiness, so that all who have endured it may behold their own experience.Fully aware now of this terrible rivalry, Sabine watched her husband when he left the house to divine the future of his day. With what restrained fury does a woman fling herself upon the red-hot spikes of that savage martyrdom! What delirious joy if she could think he did not go to the rue de Chartres! Calyste returned, and then the study of his forehead, his hair, his eyes, his countenance, his demeanor gave a horrible interest to mere nothings, to observations pursued even to matters of toilet, in which a woman loses her self-respect and dignity. These fatal investigations, concealed in the depths of her heart, rot the delicate roots from which should spring the azure flowers of sacred confidence, the golden petals of the One only love, with all the perfumes of memory.One day Calyste looked about him discontentedly; he had stayed at home! Sabine made herself caressing and humble, gay and sparkling.“You are vexed with me, Calyste; am I not a good wife? What is there here that displeases you?” she asked.“These rooms are so cold and bare,” he replied; “you don’t understand arranging things.”“Tell me what is wanting.”“Flowers.”“Ah!” she thought to herself, “Madame de Rochefide likes flowers.”Two days later, the rooms of the hotel du Guenic had assumed another aspect. No one in Paris could flatter himself to have more exquisite flowers than those that now adorned them.Some time later Calyste, one evening after dinner, complained of the cold. He twisted about in his chair, declaring there was a draught, and seemed to be looking for something. Sabine could not at first imagine what this new fancy signified, she, whose house possessed a calorifere which heated the staircases, antechambers, and passages. At last, after three days’ reflection, she concluded that her rival probably sat surrounded by a screen to obtain the half-lights favorable to faded faces; so Sabine had a screen, but hers was of glass and of Israelitish splendor.“From what quarter will the next storm come?” she said to herself.These indirect comparisons with his mistress were not yet at an end. When Calyste dined at home he ate his dinner in a way to drive Sabine frantic; he would motion to the servants to take away his plates after pecking at two or three mouthfuls.“Wasn’t it good?” Sabine would ask, in despair at seeing all the pains she had taken in conference with her cook thrown away.“I don’t say that, my angel,” replied Calyste, without anger; “I am not hungry, that is all.”A woman consumed by a legitimate passion, who struggles thus, falls at last into a fury of desire to get the better of her rival, and often goes too far, even in the most secret regions of married life. So cruel, burning, and incessant a combat in the obvious and, as we may call them, exterior matters of a household must needs become more intense and desperate in the things of the heart. Sabine studied her attitudes, her toilets; she took heed about herself in all the infinitely little trifles of love.The cooking trouble lasted nearly a month. Sabine, with Mariotte and Gasselin, hatched little schemes to learn Madame de Rochefide’s dishes. Gasselin posed as the groom, consorted with her cook, and Sabine soon gave Calyste the same fare, only better; still he found fault.“What is wanting now?” she said.“Oh, nothing,” he answered, looking round the table for something he did not find.“Ah!” exclaimed Sabine, as she woke the next morning, “Calyste wanted some of those Indian sauces they serve in England in cruets. Madame de Rochefide accustoms him to all sorts of condiments.”She bought the English cruets and the spiced sauces; but it soon became impossible for her to discover all the preparations her rival invented.This period lasted months; not surprising given the attraction of such struggle. It is life, and that is preferable, with its wounds and its anguish, to the gloomy darkness of disgust, to the poison of contempt, to the void of abdication, to that death of the heart which is called indifference. But all Sabine’s courage abandoned her one evening when she appeared in a toilet such as women are inspired to wear in the hope of eclipsing a rival, and about which Calyste said, laughing:“In spite of all you can do, Sabine, you’ll never be anything but a handsome Andalusian.”“Alas!” she said, dropping on a sofa, “I may never make myself a blonde, but I know if this continues I shall soon be thirty-five years old.”She refused to go to the Opera as she intended, and chose to stay at home the whole evening. Once alone she pulled the flowers from her hair and stamped upon them; she tore off the gown and scarf and trampled them underfoot, like a goat in the tangle of its tether, which struggles till death comes. Then she went to bed.XXI. THE WICKEDNESS OF A GOOD WOMANPlaying for these terrible stakes Sabine grew thin; grief consumed her; but she never for a moment forsook the role she had imposed upon herself. Sustained by a sort of fever, her lips suppressed the bitter words that pain suggested; she repressed the flashing of her glorious dark eyes, and made them soft even to humility. But her failing health soon became noticeable. The duchess, an excellent mother, though her piety was growing more Portuguese, recognized a moral cause in the physical weakness in which Sabine took refuge. She knew the exact state of the relation between Beatrix and Calyste; and she took great pains to draw her daughter to her own house, partly to soothe the wounds of her heart, but more especially to drag her away from the scene of her martyrdom. Sabine, however, maintained the deepest silence for a long time about her sorrows, fearing lest some one might meddle between herself and Calyste. She declared herself happy! At the height of her misery she recovered her pride, and all her virtues.But at last, after some months during which her sister Clotilde and her mother had caressed and petted her, she acknowledged her grief, confided her sorrows, cursed life, and declared that she saw death coming with delirious joy. She begged Clotilde, who was resolved to remain unmarried, to be a mother to her little Calyste, the finest child that any royal race could desire for heir presumptive.One evening, as she sat with her young sister Athenais (whose marriage to the Vicomte de Grandlieu was to take place at the end of Lent), and with Clotilde and the duchess, Sabine gave utterance to the supreme cries of her heart’s anguish, excited by the pangs of a last humiliation.“Athenais,” she said, when the Vicomte Juste de Grandlieu departed at eleven o’clock, “you are going to marry; let my example be a warning to you. Consider it a crime to display your best qualities; resist the pleasure of adorning yourself to please Juste. Be calm, dignified, cold; measure the happiness you give by that which you receive. This is shameful, but it is necessary. Look at me. I perish through my best qualities. All I knew—my virtues—were rocks on which my happiness is wrecked. I have ceased to please because I am not thirty-six years old. In the eyes of some men youth is thought an inferiority. There is nothing to imagine on an innocent face. I laugh frankly, and that is wrong; to captivate I ought to play off the melancholy half-smile of the fallen angel, who wants to hide her yellowing teeth. A fresh complexion is monotonous; some men prefer their doll’s wax made of rouge and spermaceti and cold cream. I am straightforward; but duplicity is more pleasing. I am loyally passionate, as an honest woman may be, but I ought to be manoeuvring, tricky, hypocritical, and simulate a coldness I have not,—like any provincial actress. I am intoxicated with the happiness of having married one of the most charming men in France; I tell him, naively, how distinguished he is, how graceful his movements are, how handsome I think him; but to please him I ought to turn away my head with pretended horror, to love nothing with real love, and tell him his distinction is mere sickliness. I have the misfortune to admire all beautiful things without setting myself up for a wit by caustic and envious criticism of whatever shines from poesy and beauty. I don’t seek to make Canalis and Nathan say of me in verse and prose that my intellect is superior. I’m only a poor little artless child; I care only for Calyste. Ah! if I had scoured the world like her, if I had said as she has said, ‘I love,’ in every language of Europe, I should be consoled, I should be pitied, I should be adored for serving the regal Macedonian with cosmopolitan love! We are thanked for our tenderness if we set it in relief against our vice. And I, a noble woman, must teach myself impurity and all the tricks of prostitutes! And Calyste is the dupe of such grimaces! Oh, mother! oh, my dear Clotilde! I feel that I have got my death-blow. My pride is only a sham buckler; I am without defence against my misery; I love my husband madly, and yet to bring him back to me I must borrow the wisdom of indifference.”“Silly girl,” whispered Clotilde, “let him think you will avenge yourself—”“I wish to die irreproachable and without the mere semblance of doing wrong,” replied Sabine. “A woman’s vengeance should be worthy of her love.”“My child,” said the duchess to her daughter, “a mother must of course see life more coolly than you can see it. Love is not the end, but the means, of the Family. Do not imitate that poor Baronne de Macumer. Excessive passion is unfruitful and deadly. And remember, God sends us afflictions with knowledge of our needs. Now that Athenais’ marriage is arranged, I can give all my thoughts to you. In fact, I have already talked of this delicate crisis in your life with your father and the Duc de Chaulieu, and also with d’Ajuda; we shall certainly find means to bring Calyste back to you.”“There is always one resource with the Marquise de Rochefide,” remarked Clotilde, smiling, to her sister; “she never keeps her adorers long.”“D’Ajuda, my darling,” continued the duchess, “was Monsieur de Rochefide’s brother-in-law. If our dear confessor approves of certain little manoeuvres to which we must have recourse to carry out a plan which I have proposed to your father, I can guarantee to you the recovery of Calyste. My conscience is repugnant to the use of such means, and I must first submit them to the judgment of the Abbe Brossette. We shall not wait, my child, till you are in extremis before coming to your relief. Keep a good heart! Your grief to-night is so bitter that my secret escapes me; but it is impossible for me not to give you a little hope.”“Will it make Calyste unhappy?” asked Sabine, looking anxiously at the duchess.“Oh, heavens! shall I ever be as silly as that!” cried Athenais, naively.“Ah, little girl, you know nothing of the precipices down which our virtue flings us when led by love,” replied Sabine, making a sort of moral revelation, so distraught was she by her woe.The speech was uttered with such incisive bitterness that the duchess, enlightened by the tone and look of her daughter, felt certain there was some hidden trouble.“My dears, it is midnight; come, go to bed,” she said to Clotilde and Athenais, whose eyes were shining.“In spite of my thirty-five years I appear to be de trop,” said Clotilde, laughing. While Athenais kissed her mother, Clotilde leaned over Sabine and said in her ear: “You will tell what it is? I’ll dine with you to-morrow. If my mother’s conscience won’t let her act, I—I myself will get Calyste out of the hands of the infidels.”“Well, Sabine,” said the duchess, taking her daughter into her bedroom, “tell me, what new trouble is there, my child?”“Mamma, I am lost!”“But how?”“I wanted to get the better of that horrible woman—I conquered for a time—I am pregnant again—and Calyste loves her so that I foresee a total abandonment. When she hears of it she will be furious. Ah! I suffer such tortures that I cannot endure them long. I know when he is going to her, I know it by his joy; and his peevishness tells me as plainly when he leaves her. He no longer troubles himself to conceal his feelings; I have become intolerable to him. She has an influence over him as unhealthy as she is herself in soul and body. You’ll see! she will exact from him, as the price of forgiveness, my public desertion, a rupture like her own; she will take him away from me to Switzerland or Italy. He is beginning now to say it is ridiculous that he knows nothing of Europe. I can guess what those words mean, flung out in advance. If Calyste is not cured of her in three months I don’t know what he may become; but as for me, I will kill myself.”“But your soul, my unhappy child? Suicide is a mortal sin.”“Don’t you understand? She may give him a child. And if Calyste loved the child of that woman more than mine—Oh! that’s the end of my patience and all my resignation.”She fell into a chair. She had given vent to the deepest thought in her heart; she had no longer a hidden grief; and secret sorrow is like that iron rod that sculptors put within the structure of their clay—it supports, it is a force.“Come, go home, dear sufferer. In view of such misery the abbe will surely give me absolution for the venial sins which the deceits of the world compel us to commit. Leave me now, my daughter,” she said, going to her prie-Dieu. “I must pray to our Lord and the Blessed Virgin for you, with special supplication. Good-bye, my dear Sabine; above all things, do not neglect your religious duties if you wish us to succeed.”“And if we do triumph, mother, we shall only save the family. Calyste has killed within me the holy fervor of love—killed it by sickening me with all things. What a honey-moon was mine, in which I was made to feel on that first day the bitterness of a retrospective adultery!”The next day, about two in the afternoon, the Abbe Brossette, one of the faubourg Saint-Germain vicars, crossed the court-yard of the hotel de Grandlieu with that ecclesiastical step signifying caution, mystery, calmness, gravity, and dignity. He had been appointed to a vacant bishopric in 1840 (an office refused by him for the third time). He was a thin man of about fifty, his face white and hollow from austerities. Two black eyes, ardent with faith yet softened by a mysterious expression, animated that apostolic face. He smiled as he mounted the steps, little believing in the enormity of the cases for which he was summoned; but the duchess’s open hand made her confessions worth his time.When the vicar was announced the duchess rose, and made a few steps toward him in the salon,—a distinction she granted only to cardinals, bishops, simple priests, duchesses older than herself, and persons of royal blood.“My dear abbe,” she said, pointing to a chair and speaking in a low voice, “I need your authority before I enter a rather wicked intrigue which may do great good; will it hinder my salvation?”“Madame la duchesse,” replied the abbe, “do not mix up spiritual things with worldly things; they are usually irreconcilable. In the first place, what is this matter?”“You know that my daughter Sabine is dying of grief; Monsieur du Guenic has left her for Madame de Rochefide.”“It is very dreadful, very serious; but you know what our dear Saint Francois de Sales says on that subject. Remember too how Madame Guyon complained of the lack of mysticism in the proofs of conjugal love; she would have been very willing to see her husband with a Madame de Rochefide.”“Sabine is only too gentle; she is almost too completely a Christian wife; but she has not the slightest taste for mysticism.”“Poor young woman!” said the abbe, maliciously. “What method will you take to remedy the evil?”“I have committed the sin, my dear director, of thinking how to launch upon Madame de Rochefide a little man, very self-willed and full of the worst qualities, who will certainly induce her to dismiss my son-in-law.”“My daughter,” replied the abbe, stroking his chin, “we are not now in the confessional; I am not obliged to make myself your judge. From the world’s point of view, I admit that the result would be decisive—”“The means seem to me odious,” she said.“Why? No doubt the duty of a Christian woman is to withdraw a sinning woman from an evil path, rather than push her along it; but when a woman has advanced upon that path as far as Madame de Rochefide, it is not the hand of man, but that of God, which recalls such a sinner; she needs a thunderbolt.”“Father,” replied the duchess, “I thank you for your indulgence; but the thought has occurred to me that my son-in-law is brave and a Breton. He was heroic at the time of the rash affair of that poor MADAME. Now, if the young fellow who undertook to make Madame de Rochefide love him were to quarrel with Calyste, and a duel should ensue—”“You have thought wisely, Madame la duchesse; and it only proves that in crooked paths you will always find rocks of stumbling.”“I have discovered a means, my dear abbe, to do a great good; to withdraw Madame de Rochefide from the fatal path in which she now is; to restore Calyste to his wife, and possibly to save from hell a poor distracted creature.”“In that case, why consult me?” asked the vicar, smiling.“Ah!” replied the duchess, “Because I must permit myself some rather nasty actions—”“You don’t mean to rob anybody?”“On the contrary, I shall apparently have to spend a great deal of money.”“You will not calumniate, or—”“Oh! oh!”“—injure your neighbor?”“I don’t know about that.”“Come, tell me your plan,” said the abbe, now becoming curious.“Suppose, instead of driving out one nail by another,—this is what I thought at my prie-Dieu after imploring the Blessed Virgin to enlighten me,—I were to free Calyste by persuading Monsieur de Rochefide to take back his wife? Instead of lending a hand to evil for the sake of doing good to my daughter, I should do one great good by another almost as great—”The vicar looked at the Portuguese lady, and was pensive.“That is evidently an idea that came to you from afar,” he said, “so far that—”“I have thanked the Virgin for it,” replied the good and humble duchess; “and I have made a vow—not counting a novena—to give twelve hundred francs to some poor family if I succeed. But when I communicated my plan to Monsieur de Grandlieu he began to laugh, and said: ‘Upon my honor, at your time of life I think you women have a devil of your own.’”“Monsieur le duc made as a husband the same reply I was about to make when you interrupted me,” said the abbe, who could not restrain a smile.“Ah! Father, if you approve of the idea, will you also approve of the means of execution? It is necessary to do to a certain Madame Schontz (a Beatrix of the quartier Saint-Georges) what I proposed to do to Madame de Rochefide.”“I am certain that you will not do any real wrong,” said the vicar, cleverly, not wishing to hear any more, having found the result so desirable. “You can consult me later if you find your conscience muttering,” he added. “But why, instead of giving that person in the rue Saint-Georges a fresh occasion for scandal, don’t you give her a husband?”“Ah! my dear director, now you have rectified the only bad thing I had in my plan. You are worthy of being an archbishop, and I hope I shall not die till I have had the opportunity of calling you Your Eminence.”“I see only one difficulty in all this,” said the abbe.“What is that?”“Suppose Madame de Rochefide chooses to keep your son-in-law after she goes back to her husband?”“That’s my affair,” replied the duchess; “when one doesn’t often intrigue, one does so—”“Badly, very badly,” said the abbe. “Habit is necessary for everything. Try to employ some of those scamps who live by intrigue, and don’t show your own hand.”“Ah! monsieur l’abbe, if I make use of the means of hell, will Heaven help me?”“You are not at confession,” repeated the abbe. “Save your child.”The worthy duchess, delighted with her vicar, accompanied him to the door of the salon.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A storm was gathering over Monsieur de Rochefide, who enjoyed what a Parisian could most desire: being to Madame Schontz almost as much a husband as he had been to Béatrix. As the duke told his wife, such an agreeable existence seemed almost impossible to upset. The contrast between Rochefide’s happiness and the misery his wife would have suffered shows how laws and morals treat the sexes differently: what ruins a woman often brings contentment to a man. This contrast might encourage some young women, like Sabine du Guenic, to stay and practice the virtues they choose.Some days after Béatrix left him, Arthur de Rochefide, now an only child, became master of the hôtel de Rochefide and of two hundred thousand francs a year from his father; added to his marriage fortune, this yielded roughly a thousand francs a day. For the type Mademoiselle des Touches had described, such wealth was happiness enough. While his wife had tended maternal duties at home, Rochefide enjoyed his fortune; he neither spent it extravagantly nor sought intellectual growth. Vanity, attractive looks, repeated stage and journal wit, and a military joviality from his Royal Guard service made him seem witty to unintellectual women who did not contradict him.Arthur was essentially an imitator without originality: he adopted and dropped fashions, was accused only of lengthy toilette and wearing a corset, and pleased most by reflecting others’ ideas and follies, never aging in public estimation. As a husband he was pitied; people blamed Béatrix for deserting so good a fellow and taunted the woman. A member of every club and subscriber to every patriotic or partisan absurdity, this loyal, brave, very silly nobleman resembled many rich men. Seeking distinction, he adopted some fashionable mania: he glorified himself as the sultan of a four-footed harem, ruled by an old English groom, costing four to five thousand francs a month. His specialty was running horses; he supported a hippic magazine though he knew little of horses and depended entirely on his groom. Thus this semi-bachelor had nothing truly his own—neither mind, taste, position, nor eccentricity; even his fortune came from his fathers. After the displeasures of marriage he was so content to be again a bachelor that he told friends he had been born with a caul.Pleased to live without the obligations appearance demanded of husbands, his house—unchanged since his father’s death—resembled a traveling master’s: he lived there little, seldom dined, and rarely slept. The reason lay in his new liaison. Bored by fashionable women, after various adventures Rochefide had, in effect, married a certain Madame Schontz, celebrated in the circles of Fanny Beaupré, Susanne du Val-Noble, Florine, Mariette, Jenny Cadine. To portray that world faithfully one must proportion the number of such figures to their varied endings: poverty, early or self-inflicted death, lucky marriages, or opulence.Madame Schontz, at first called La Petite-Aurélie, belongs to the highest class of women whose social utility is undeniable: without the Aspasias of the Notre-Dame de Lorette quarter, far fewer houses would be built in Paris. They are pioneers on Montmartre’s heights, seeking speculation, pitched in carved free-stone solitudes that line European streets—architectural steppes where papers advertise apartments to let. Their position depends on where they camp in these apocryphal regions: near rue de Provence a woman fares well; approach the Boulevard Extérieur or Batignolles and she becomes destitute. When Rochefide first saw Madame Schontz, she lived on the third floor of the last house in rue de Berlin—on the border between misery and its reverse. Her real name was not Schontz or Aurélie; she concealed her father’s name, an old soldier of the Empire, often appearing at the dawn of such feminine existences as either father or seducer. Madame Schontz had had the Saint-Denis education—admirably accomplished yet offering no openings when girls left the school—an admirable creation of the Emperor, now lacking the Emperor himself.Aurélie was the daughter of the intrepid Colonel Schiltz, an Alsatian who nearly saved the Emperor in the 1814 campaign and died at Metz ruined. Napoleon placed little Josephine Schiltz, about nine, at Saint-Denis. Having lost both parents and with no resources, she remained after the Bourbons returned, serving as under-mistress until 1827, but her patience failed; her beauty seduced her. At majority Josephine—Empress’s goddaughter—was on the verge of a courtesan’s adventurous life, wooed by the fatal example of comrades without resources. She substituted Aurélie and took Saint-Aurélie for a patron saint.Lively, witty, and well-educated, she committed more faults than her duller companions, whose misdemeanors were base and self-interested. Having known various writers—clever but deeply in debt—tried rich but foolish men, and sacrificed solid interests to one true love, she went through all the schools of experience. One day of extreme misery, when at Valentino’s she danced in borrowed gown, hat, and mantle, she attracted Rochefide, who had come to see the galop. Her cleverness captivated a man who had not yet fixed his passion. Two years after Béatrix’s desertion, Rochefide was not blamed for marrying, so to speak, in the thirteenth arrondissement, a substitute for his wife.Let us sketch the four periods of this happiness; the strategy of passion in the thirteenth arrondissement is similar for all who come under its rule: marquis in his forties, sexagenary retired shopkeeper, quadruple millionaire or moderate man—the heart and the money-box always hold the same relation. This will show the difficulties the duchess would face in her plans about him.One expression, “tirer une carotte” (to dupe), had become popular; in the thirteenth arrondissement patois penetrates everything. Rochefide, like all little minds, feared being carotted, so from the start of his passion for Madame Schontz he guarded against being duped and was therefore very “rat”—niggardly. Madame Schontz had too much sense and knew men too well not to conceive great hopes. Rochefide allowed her five hundred francs a month, furnished shabby second-floor rooms in rue Coquenard costing 1,200 francs a year, and set himself to study Aurélie’s character while she, perceiving his intent, gave him a character to study. Rochefide was happy to find in her a woman of noble nature; he imagined her mother a Barnheim of Baden, and Aurélie’s upbringing confirmed the illusion. She spoke English, German, Italian, knew foreign literatures, could hold her own against second-class pianists, and behaved about her talents like a well-bred woman—she never mentioned them. She took up painting half in jest and produced a head that astonished. For amusement during her tenure at Saint-Denis she had advanced in the sciences; later life covered those seeds, yet she let Arthur take credit for their sprouting.Aurélie began by showing disinterestedness equal to her other charms, which allowed this weak corvette to fix its grapnels on the larger vessel. But by the end of the first year she made ignoble noises in the antechamber with her clogs, arriving when Rochefide awaited her in a draggled gown. By this time she had persuaded her gros papa that her ambition was to obtain honorably a comfortable bourgeois existence; about ten months after their meeting the second phase of happiness declared itself.She obtained a fine apartment in rue Neuve-Saint-Georges. Arthur, who could no longer conceal his fortune, gave her splendid furniture, a complete service of plate, 1,200 francs a month, a hired low carriage with one horse—and a tiger. Madame Schontz was not the least grateful; she knew the motive of his conduct and recognized the male rat’s calculations. Sick of restaurant life, Rochefide offered Aurélie forty francs a day to cover his dinner and that of a friend; she accepted.Having cashed in all her moral letters of credit, she was listened to favorably when she asked for 500 francs more a month for dress to avoid shaming her gros papa, whose friends belonged to the Jockey Club.“It would be a pretty thing,” she said, “if Rastignac, Maxime de Trailles, d’Esgrignon, La Roche-Hugon, Ronqueroles, Laginski, Lenoncourt, found you with a sort of Madame Everard. Besides, have confidence in me, papa, and you’ll be the gainer.”Aurélie contrived to display new virtues in this second phase. She assumed the housekeeping role for which she claimed credit: she said she balanced two thousand five hundred francs a month without debt—a rare achievement in the faubourg Saint-Germain of the thirteenth arrondissement—and served dinners superior to Nucingen’s, with wines at twelve francs a bottle. Rochefide, amazed and delighted to host friends economically, declared as he caught her round the waist, “She’s a treasure!”He soon hired a third of an Opera box for her and took her to first representations. He began consulting Aurélie and took credit for her clever sayings, which raised his reputation as an amusing man. He acquired certainty of being truly loved and for himself alone. Aurélie refused a Russian prince offering five thousand francs a month.“You are a lucky man, my dear marquis,” cried old Prince Galathionne at the club. “Yesterday I tried to get Madame Schontz away from you, but she said: ‘Prince, you are not handsomer, but you are a great deal older than Rochefide; you would beat me, but he is like a father to me; can you give me one-tenth of a reason why I should change? I’ve never had the grand passion for Arthur that I once had for little fools in varnished boots and whose debts I paid; but I love him as a wife loves her husband when she is an honest woman.’ And thereupon she showed me the door.”This speech, not exaggerated, increased the neglect and degradation in the hôtel de Rochefide. Arthur shifted his whole existence to Madame Schontz and prospered; after three years he had four hundred thousand francs to invest.The third phase began. Madame Schontz became the tenderest of mothers to Arthur’s son; she fetched him from school, showered him with presents and pocket-money. She took part in managing Arthur’s property: she made him buy into the Funds when low—just before the treaty of London that overturned the ministry of March 1st—and Arthur gained two hundred thousand francs by that transaction; Aurélie did not claim a penny. Rochefide invested six hundred thousand in Bank of France stock and put half in Josephine Schiltz’s name. A little house in rue de La Bruyère was hired and given to Grindot, that great decorator, to make it a bonbon-box.Henceforth Rochefide ceased managing his affairs. Madame Schontz received the revenues and paid the bills. Practically his wife and woman of business, she justified her position by making her gros papa more comfortable than ever; she had learned his fancies and gratified them as Madame de Pompadour gratified Louis XV. In short, Madame Schontz became absolute mistress. She began to patronize a few young artists and men of letters, newly famous and keen to make reputations with little to accomplish.Her conduct, a triumph of tactics, revealed her superiority. A dozen young fellows amused Arthur, supplying witty sayings and opinions without questioning the mistress’s fidelity; they proclaimed her eminently intelligent. These living advertisements soon set Madame Schontz up as the most agreeable woman on the borderland between the thirteenth arrondissement and the rest. Her rivals—Suzanne Gaillard, Fanny Beaupré, Mariette, Antonia—spread droll calumnies about the beauty of those young men and Rochefide’s complacent reception of them. Madame Schontz, who could out-blague those ladies by three turns, told them once at Nathan’s supper, after recounting her fortune and success, “Do as much yourselves!”—a remark they remembered.During this period she made Arthur sell his race-horses, through arguments perhaps derived from Claude Vignon, one of her habitués.“I can conceive,” she said after lashing the horses with wit, “that princes and rich men should set their hearts on horse-flesh, but only for the good of the country, not for betting. If you had a stud farm and could raise a thousand or twelve hundred horses, and all of France’s horses entered into one great competition, it would be fine; but you buy animals as managers of theatres trade in artists; you degrade an institution to gambling; you make a Bourse of legs, as of stocks. It is unworthy. Don’t you spend sixty thousand francs sometimes merely to read in the newspapers: ‘Lélia, belonging to Monsieur de Rochefide, beat by a length Fleur-de-Genet the property of Monsieur le Duc de Rhetore’? You had much better give that money to poets, who would carry you to immortality in prose and verse.’”By this prodding Rochefide saw the hollowness of the turf and economized sixty thousand francs; the next year Madame Schontz remarked, “I don’t cost you anything now, Arthur.”Many rich men envied Rochefide and tried to entice Madame Schontz away, but, like the Russian prince, they wasted their old age.“Listen to me,” she told Finot, now immensely rich. “I am certain Rochefide would forgive me a little passion if I fell in love, but one doesn’t leave a marquis with a kind heart like that for a parvenu like you. You couldn’t keep me in the position in which Arthur has placed me; he has made me half a wife and a lady, and that’s more than you could do even if you married me.”This last point clinched the fetters of that happy galley-slave: the speech, of course, reached the ears for which it was intended.The fourth phase had begun: habit, the final victory in these campaigns, which make the women of this class say of a man, “I hold him!” Rochefide, who had just bought the little hôtel in Mademoiselle Josephine Schiltz’s name (a trifle of eighty thousand francs), had reached, as the Duchesse de Grandlieu planned, the stage of finding vanity in his mistress—whom he now called Ninon II.—by boasting of her scrupulous honesty, excellent manners, education, and wit. He had merged his defects, merits, tastes, and pleasures into Madame Schontz, and was now, from lassitude, indifference, or philosophy, a man unable to change, clinging to wife or mistress.In five years Madame Schontz had so consolidated her position that introductions to her had to be proposed before being granted. She refused to receive dull rich people and smirched people, admitting only certain great aristocratic names.Ostensibly she possessed the three hundred thousand francs Rochefide had given her, invested and managed by a broker named Gobenheim—the only broker admitted to her house. Secretly she manipulated another little fortune of two hundred thousand francs, the result of savings over three years and constant movement of the known three hundred thousand—for she never admitted more than that known sum.“The more you make, the less you get rich,” Gobenheim told her.“Water is so dear,” she answered.This secret hoard was increased by jewels and diamonds, which Aurélie wore for a month and then sold. When anyone called her rich, Madame Schontz replied that at the Funds’ interest three hundred thousand francs produced only twelve thousand, and she had spent as much in the hardest days of her life.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Such conduct implied a plan, and Madame Schontz had one. Jealous for two years of Madame du Bruel, she was consumed with the ambition to be married by church and mayor. Every social position has a forbidden fruit—some small thing magnified into the weightiest concern. Her scheme required a second Arthur; Rochefide’s secret rival remained undiscovered. Bixiou fancied Leon de Lora the favorite; the painter suspected Bixiou, who, past forty, ought to be making a fate. Suspicions also fell on Victor de Vernisset, a Canalis-school poet desperately in love; he accused Stidmann, a young sculptor, of rivalry. Claude Vignon, the young Comte de la Palferine, Gobenheim, Vermanton—all frequented the salon and were suspected, then proved innocent. No one fathomed Madame Schontz, certainly not Rochefide, who thought she preferred the young, witty La Palferine; she was virtuous from self-interest and wholly bent on making a good marriage.Only one man of equivocal reputation was ever seen in Madame Schontz’s salon: Couture, a stock-gambler who had once scandalized his brothers. Couture had been one of Madame Schontz’s earliest friends, and she alone remained faithful to him. The false alarm of 1840 swept away the last vestige of this speculator’s credit; Aurelie, seeing his ill-luck, made Rochefide play in another direction. Glad to find a place at Aurelie’s table, Couture—who sometimes received a thousand-franc note from Finot, the cleverest or luckiest of parvenus—was shrewd enough to offer his hand and name to Madame Schontz. She studied whether this bold speculator had political power and sufficient gratitude not to desert his wife. Couture, about forty-three, half worn-out, did not redeem the unpleasant sonority of his name by birth; he said little of the authors of his days.Madame Schontz was bemoaning the scarcity of eligible men when Couture presented a provincial, supplied with the two handles by which women take hold of such pitchers when they wish to keep them. To sketch this person is to paint a portion of the youth of the day.In 1838, Fabien du Ronceret, son of a chief-justice of the Royal court at Caen (recently deceased), left Alençon, resigning his judgeship—an office his father had compelled him to hold—and came to Paris to make a noise. A Norman ambition, difficult to realize on an income scarcely scraping eight thousand francs a year; his mother still held a life-interest in a valuable estate in Alençon. In earlier visits he had tried his rope like an acrobat and had perceived the great vice of the social replastering of 1830. He meant to turn it to profit, following the example of the longest heads of the bourgeoisie. This requires a glance at one effect of the new order.Modern equality, unduly pushed, has produced in private life—parallel to politics—three great divisions of the social I: pride, conceit, and vanity. Fools wish to pass for wits; wits want to be thought men of talent; men of talent want to be treated as genius. By pulling down recognized superiorities, a nation opens the sluice through which a torrent of secondary ambitions rushes, each meaner than the last. Democrats say there was an evil in the aristocracy, but they substitute a dozen armed, contending aristocracies—the worst of all. Proclaiming equality, they promulgate a declaration of the rights of Envy. We inherit the Revolution’s saturnalia transferred to mind, industry, and politics; reputations won by toil, service, and talent are treated like privileges taken from the masses. Distinction is sought at any price—by ridicule, affectation, and petty public causes—creating fictitious dignities and a profusion of presidents, vice-presidents, and secretaries for societies whose numbers exceed the questions they pretend to solve. Society on a grand scale has been demolished to make a million little ones in the image of the defunct. These parasitic organizations reveal decomposition: are they not maggots in a dead body? All these societies are daughters of one mother, Vanity. Catholic charity or true beneficence proceed differently; they study and cure wounds rather than perorate in public meetings for the pleasure of perorating.Fabien du Ronceret, without being superior, had divined—by the greedy common-sense peculiar to a Norman—the gain he could derive from this public vice. Every epoch has a character clever men exploit. Fabien’s mind, though not clever, was wholly bent on making himself talked about.“My dear fellow, a man must make himself talked about, if he wants to be anything,” he said on parting from the king of Alençon, a certain du Bousquier, a friend of his father. “In six months I shall be better known than you are!”So Fabien obeyed the spirit of his age. He made his debut in Bohemia, where he was known as “The Heir” because of premeditated prodigalities. Du Ronceret profited by Couture’s follies for the pretty Madame Cadine: during an ephemeral opulence he had arranged a delightful ground-floor apartment with garden in the rue Blanche. The Norman, who wanted luxury ready-made, bought Couture’s furniture and the improvements he was forced to leave—kiosk, rustic gallery, India mattings, potteries—though he called the result his den and never acknowledged the architect Grindot, the carvings of Stidmann, or the paintings of Leon de Lora—Fabien’s crowning vanity lied for the sake of magnifying himself.He crowned the magnificence with a greenhouse built along a southern wall—not through any love of flowers but to attack public notice through horticulture. He had nearly reached his end. Elected vice-president of a floral society presided over by the Duc de Vissembourg, he wore the Legion of Honor ribbon at an exhibition, delivered an opening speech bought from Lousteau for five hundred francs and boldly pronounced it his own, and made a stir by presenting a flower given him by old Blondet of Alençon as the product of his greenhouse.But this was nothing. The Heir, who wanted to be accepted as a wit, planned to consort with clever celebrities and reflect their fame—hard to do on eight thousand francs a year. He repeatedly asked Bixiou, Stidmann, and Leon de Lora to present him to Madame Schontz and allow him into her menagerie of lions. Failing these, he applied to Couture, for whose dinners he had so often paid that the late speculator felt obliged to recommend him to Madame Schontz, if only as an elegant unpaid footman whom mistresses employ to run errands when servants are lacking.In three evenings Madame Schontz read Fabien like a book and said to herself, “If Couture does not suit me, I am certain of saddling that one. My future can go on two legs now.” This queer fellow whom everybody laughed at was really the chosen one—chosen, however, with an intention that made such preference insulting. The improbability of the choice kept suspicion away. Madame Schontz intoxicated Fabien with secret smiles and little scenes on the threshold when she bade him good-night, if Rochefide stayed. She often made Fabien a third with Arthur in her opera-box, excusing it by saying he had done her some service. Men have a natural conceit common as to women—the belief of being loved exclusively. Of all flattering passions none is more prized than that of a Madame Schontz: the man she makes the object of a love she calls “from the heart,” as distinct from another sort. A woman like her, who plays the great lady and whose intrinsic value is real, would be an object of pride to Fabien, who fell in love so that he never presented himself before her eyes except in full dress, varnished boots, lemon-kid gloves, embroidered shirt and frill, variegated waistcoat—in short, with all the external symptoms of profound worship.A month before the duchess’s conference with her confessor, Madame Schontz confided the secret of her birth and real name to Fabien, who did not at all understand the motive. A fortnight later she was surprised at his want of intelligence and exclaimed to herself, “Heavens! how stupid I am! he expects me to love him for himself.” Accordingly she took the Heir in her calèche to the Bois—she now had two little carriages—to discuss her future and declared she wished to marry.“I have seven hundred thousand francs,” she said, “and if I could find a man full of ambition who understands my character, I would change my position; for do you know the dream of my life? To become a true bourgeoise, enter an honorable family, and make my husband and children truly happy.”The Norman wished to be “distinguished” by Madame Schontz, but marrying her seemed a folly to a bachelor of thirty-eight whom the revolution of July had made a judge. Seeing his hesitation, Madame Schontz made him the butt of her wit and disdain and turned to Couture. Within a week Couture, whom she put upon the scent of her fortune, had offered his hand, heart, and future—three things of about the same value.The manoeuvres of Madame Schontz had reached this stage when the Duchesse de Grandlieu began inquiries into the life of the Béatrix of the Place Saint-Georges.Following the Abbe Brossette’s advice, the duchess asked the Marquis d’Ajuda to bring the celebrated Comte Maxime de Trailles—the archduke of Bohemia, political cut-throat and youngest of young men though fully fifty. D’Ajuda dined with Maxime at the club in the rue de Beuane and proposed afterward to go play dummy whist with the Duc de Grandlieu, who was feigning gout.Maxime did not deceive himself about the invitation’s meaning; he felt certain the duke or duchess needed him. Club life—men playing cards with others they do not receive at home—is a telling sign of the age.After several games the duke went to bed, leaving his wife tête-à-tête with Maxime and d’Ajuda. The duchess, seconded by the marquis, revealed her project and asked Maxime’s assistance while ostensibly seeking only his advice. Maxime listened without committing, waiting until she asked point-blank.“Madame, I fully understand you,” he said, casting that shrewd, penetrating look by which great scamps compromise their interlocutors. “D’Ajuda will tell you that if any one in Paris can conduct that difficult negotiation it is I—of course without mixing you up in it; without it being even known that I have come here this evening. Only, before anything is done, we must settle preliminaries. How much are you willing to sacrifice?”“All that is necessary.”“Very well, then, Madame la duchesse. As the price of my efforts you must do me the honor to receive in your house and seriously protect Madame la Comtesse de Trailles.”“What! are you married?” cried d’Ajuda.“I shall be married within a fortnight to the heiress of a rich but extremely bourgeois family—a sacrifice to opinion! I imbibe the very spirit of my government and start upon a new career. Consequently, Madame la duchesse will understand how important it is to have my wife adopted by her and her family. I am certain of being made deputy by my father-in-law’s resignation, and I am promised a diplomatic post in keeping with my new fortune. I do not see why my wife should not be as well received as Madame de Portenduere in that society of young women. My wife is pretty, and I will undertake to uncotton-night-cap her. Will this suit you, Madame la duchesse? You are religious, and if you say yes, your promise, which I know to be sacred, will greatly aid my change of life. It will be one more good action to your account. Alas! I have long been the king of mauvais sujets, and I want to make an end of it. After all, we bear, azure, a wyvern or, darting fire... and we have been counts since Catherine de’ Medici.”“I will receive and protect your wife,” said the duchess solemnly, “and my family will not turn its back upon her; I give you my word.”“Ah! Madame la duchesse,” cried Maxime, visibly touched, “if Monsieur le duc would also deign to treat me with some kindness, I promise you to make your plan succeed without its costing you very much. But,” he paused, “you must take upon yourself to follow my instructions. This is the last intrigue of my bachelor life; it must be all the better managed because it concerns a good action.”“Follow your instructions!” the duchess replied. “Then I must appear in all this.”“Ah! madame, I will not compromise you,” Maxime protested. “I esteem you too much to demand guarantees. I merely mean that you must follow my advice. For example, it will be necessary that du Guenic be taken away by his wife for at least two years; she must show him Switzerland, Italy, Germany—in short, all possible countries.”“You confirm a fear of my director,” said the duchess naively, recalling the Abbe Brossette’s prudence.Maxime and d’Ajuda smiled at the idea of an agreement between heaven and hell.“To prevent Madame de Rochefide from ever seeing Calyste again,” the duchess continued, “we will all travel—Juste and his wife, Calyste, Sabine, and I. I will leave Clotilde with her father—”“It is too soon to sing victory, madame,” said Maxime. “I foresee enormous difficulties; though I shall no doubt vanquish them. Your esteem and protection are rewards which would make me commit the vilest actions, but these will be—”“The vilest actions!” cried the duchess, whose piety disclosed selfishness.“And you would share them, madame, inasmuch as I am only your agent. But are you ignorant of the degree of blindness to which Madame de Rochefide has brought your son-in-law? I know it from Canalis and Nathan, between whom she was hesitating when Calyste threw himself into the lioness’s jaws. Béatrix has contrived to persuade that serious Breton that she has never loved any one but him; that she is virtuous; that Conti was merely a sentimental head-love—in short, a musical love. As for Rochefide, that was duty. So, you understand, she is virgin!—a fact she proves by forgetting her son, whom for more than a year she has not tried to see. The little count will soon be twelve, and he finds in Madame Schontz a mother who is all the more a mother because maternity is, as you know, a passion with women of that sort. Du Guenic would let himself be cut in pieces and would chop up his wife for Béatrix; and you think it easy to drag a man from such credulity! Ah! madame, Shakespeare’s Iago would lose all his handkerchiefs. People mistake theatrical passion for true love. Love, Madame la duchesse, is not loving a noble woman; it is to say to oneself: ‘She whom I love is infamous; she deceives me; she is abandoned; she is hellish,’ yet we rush to her and find there the blue of heaven. That is how Molière loved, and how we, scamps that we are, love. As for me, I weep at the great scene of Arnolphe. Now, that is how your son-in-law loves Béatrix. I shall have trouble separating Rochefide from Madame Schontz; but Madame Schontz will no doubt lend herself to the plot; I shall study her interior. But as for Calyste and Béatrix, they will need blows of an axe, far deeper treachery, and so base an infamy that your virtuous imagination could never descend to it—unless indeed your director gave you a hand. You have asked the impossible; you shall be obeyed. But in spite of my settled intention to war with fire and sword, I cannot absolutely promise you success. I have known lovers who did not recoil before the most awful disillusions. You are too virtuous to know the full power of women who are not virtuous.”“Do not enter upon those infamous actions until I consult the Abbe Brossette to know how far I may be your accomplice,” cried the duchess, with a naiveté that revealed the selfishness in her piety.“You shall be ignorant of everything, my dear mother,” interposed d’Ajuda.On the portico, as the marquis’s carriage drew up, d’Ajuda said to Maxime: “You frightened that good duchess.”“But she has no idea of the difficulty of what she asks. Let us go to the Jockey Club; Rochefide must invite me to dine with Madame Schontz tomorrow, for to-night my plan will be made, and I shall have chosen the pawns on my chessboard to carry it out. In the days of her splendor Béatrix refused to receive me; I intend to pay off that score, and I will avenge your sister-in-law so cruelly that perhaps she will find herself too well revenged.”The next day Rochefide told Madame Schontz that Maxime de Trailles was coming to dinner. That meant notifying her to display all her luxury and prepare the choicest food for this connoisseur emeritus, whom all women of Madame Schontz’s type revered. Madame Schontz considered her toilette and her house with the same care she would give to presenting to such a personage.In Paris there are as many royalties as varieties of art, mental and moral specialties, sciences, and professions; the strongest and most capable of their practitioners has a majesty all his own and is respected by peers who know their art’s difficulties. Maxime was, in the eyes of rats and courtesans, an extremely powerful and capable man who had known how to make himself excessively loved. He was also admired by men who appreciate the difficulty of living in Paris on good terms with creditors; in short, he had no rival in elegance, deportment, and wit except the illustrious de Marsay, who often employed him on political missions. All this explains his interview with the duchess, his prestige with Madame Schontz, and the authority of his words in a conference he planned to hold on the boulevard des Italiens with a young man already known, though newly arrived, in the Bohemia of Paris.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The next day Maxime de Trailles summoned Finot and arranged a Café Anglais breakfast with Couture and Lousteau to learn about Madame Schontz. Finot, who deferred to Maxime, complied. At the breakfast Couture, prompted by Finot and unwittingly by Lousteau, revealed what Maxime wished to know.Around one o’clock Maxime loitered on Tortoni’s portico with du Tillet, ostensibly waiting for the Comte de la Palferine. When the prince of Bohemia arrived, Maxime drew him aside and asked if La Palferine had debts—fifty or a hundred thousand? The count admitted only six thousand; Maxime shrugged that small sums can hamper as much as great ones, praised the young man’s spirit, and offered help: twenty thousand francs and a scheme to make a woman love him within ten days. La Palferine, flattered though feeling inferior, accepted. Maxime instructed him to meet that evening at Madame Schontz’s and to report nightly at one a.m. on the boulevard de la Madeleine.Maxime intended to exploit Madame d’Espard’s dislike of Beatrix; he called at the Hotel de Grandlieu and found the duchess, Madame du Guenic, and Clotilde upset: Calyste had not returned, and Beatrix was distraught. Maxime urged secrecy and proposed a plot requiring twenty thousand francs and perhaps offices—hinting at a receiver-generalship as inducement.Madame Schontz had invited Claude Vignon, Couture, Fabien du Ronceret (the Heir), Bixiou, Leon de Lora, La Palferine, and Nathan—nine guests of talent or ambition. Madame Schontz, anxious to seem young and beautiful, dressed with art: lace pelerine, blue velvet gown, opals, and smoothed hair. Though heavier, she still had a celebrated head. Maxime arrived, praised Rochefide’s good fortune through her, and bantered about art versus lorette; Aurelie insisted she saw herself as an artist and a progenitrix of honest men.Claude Vignon expressed literary opinions and Maxime teased him about Camille Maupin. The guests chatted; Maxime privately handed La Palferine the twenty thousand-franc notes, telling him not to economize and directing Nathan to present him to Madame la Marquise de Rochefide two nights hence. Maxime urged a drunken display and charm; La Palferine pledged to report nightly.Fabien du Ronceret, the Heir, appeared in waiter-like dress and Maxime scorned it but learned he had ties with d’Esgrignon. The dinner was lavish, and after the meal Maxime confided to Couture a scheme: if Couture wanted steadiness, Madame de Rochefide could obtain a receiver-generalship for him and Aurelie’s million would secure it, but Maxime wanted Couture’s vote in return. Couture pledged allegiance. Maxime then drove La Palferine home.That night Madame Schontz and Fabien flirted; when Arthur Rochefide accidentally surprised them, Aurelie played wounded virtue and threatened to marry Fabien, humiliating Arthur. Maxime advised Aurelie on how to force the issue—rouse jealousy, make Arthur see himself as unloved, demand a dramatic parting—promising Fabien a chief-justiceship and the Legion of Honor soon. The scene instigated domestic warfare: Arthur woke to Aurelie’s icy sarcasm. She accused him of scheming to reconcile with Madame de Rochefide, and he protested. She threatened to leave him and refused his offers, preferring to cultivate Fabien.Rochefide, in despair, sought Maxime’s counsel at the club. Maxime suggested Arthur show less obvious devotion and perhaps turn toward another woman, Madame Antonia, to recover self-respect and win his wife back. Though Arthur wished to remain faithful, Maxime urged a practical stratagem. Rochefide hurried home but found Aurelie had contrived a compromising scene: Fabien’s hat had been left, and the maid’s clumsy removal suggested intimacy. Arthur, devastated, submitted.Within a week La Palferine transformed: he paid debts, adopted a carriage, joined clubs, and published a successful novelette. Nathan, who promoted him to Madame de Rochefide, told Beatrix that La Palferine adored her. Ten days after Maxime’s arrangement, the count made his debut at Beatrix’s house while Calyste was at a party. Nathan primed her curiosity with artful reticence and lauded La Palferine’s prowess, implying his skill as a lover, which heightened Beatrix’s interest. The law of contraries played: courtesans sometimes yearn for virtue, and virtuous women secretly desire libertinism; Nathan exploited this, and Beatrix began to question whether she had been duped by her upbringing.The next day Calyste seemed to Beatrix a loyal but unimaginative gentleman; after the dazzling soirée his plainness failed to satisfy her appetite for brilliance. She feared a meeting between Calyste and La Palferine; Maxime’s maneuver soon forced a public humiliation. At the opera, where Beatrix sat in an obscure box, she exited and was recognized on the crowded staircase; whispers and stares left her isolated. La Palferine, graciously offering his arm, escorted her, sat with her in her carriage, and rode home beside her, to Calyste’s stupefaction. In the salon the count conversed brilliantly, ignoring Calyste, who seethed. La Palferine left on a witty note, ensuring the lovers’ quarrel.Maxime, watching from the Jockey Club, checked on Aurelie’s progress. Madame Schontz, pleased with her strategy, had managed to eject Arthur: she had led Fabien to believe she would reform and marry him, and Fabien, intoxicated and ambitious, embraced the project. Maxime promised Fabien the offices he’d been promised. Two days later Charles-Edouard told Maxime, “It is done.” Aurelie had turned Arthur out; Maxime consoled the marquis and suggested reconciliation could be bought with three hundred thousand francs and a new match arranged for him.Meanwhile the duchess learned Calyste planned to leave for Italy; Sabine wanted to follow and even bought pistols. Maxime counselled d’Ajuda to remain silent, promising that Madame de Rochefide would not leave Paris but would soon separate from Calyste. He arranged for his own marriage to be accepted publicly and urged that the duchess travel to Italy soon.Beatrix, now alternately consumed by passion and disdain, demanded that Calyste abandon Sabine to prove his devotion. Entrenched, she thought him unlikely to break with the Grandlieus because Sabine’s fortune mattered. Yet Calyste, driven by despair, obtained a passport and funds from his mother, and nine days after La Palferine’s opening success resolved to force a reconciliation. He arrived at Beatrix’s house intending to oust La Palferine and depart with her. Antoine, the old footman, sought to shield her by telling La Palferine she was out, but when La Palferine appeared Calyste watched him depart in a carriage; Beatrix, desperate, sent La Palferine a long letter of desperate devotion—an unprecedented signed confession.When La Palferine called next, Beatrix, ensnared and overjoyed, embraced him. He proposed a condition: she must be reconciled with Rochefide, restore her social honor, return to the Hôtel de Rochefide and play the grand lady in public; she must achieve this by political means and persistence. He had already arranged for the reconciliation; she had instructed La Palferine to insist on it. Calyste, repeatedly refused by Antoine and by La Palferine’s household, left letters and begged to be heard. Eventually La Palferine and Maxime confronted him and, with cold civility, showed him the six unopened letters he had sent: La Palferine had no wish to cause scandal, and he disclosed that Beatrix had taken him up in her carriage after leaving the opera and had insisted on bringing back Rochefide. Rochefide had been living with Madame Schontz, who had left Paris, and had returned with Aurelie to Nogent-sur-Marne while the Hôtel de Rochefide would be restored for the marquise’s triumphant return. La Palferine advised Calyste to resume his life with Sabine after three months and not to regret Beatrix: she was vain, incomplete both for vice and virtue, and would have abandoned him without remorse.Calyste left stunned but relieved to have his illusions pierced. Three days later the Duchesse de Grandlieu found Calyste content at home with Sabine planning the layette; Sabine whispered to the duchess, “I am loved, mother, and forever!”ADDENDUMThe following personages appear in other stories of the Human Comedy:- Ajuda-Pinto, Marquis Miguel d’ — Father Goriot; A Daughter of Eve; Honorine; Cousin Betty; The Unconscious Humorists############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ######################################################################### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ######################### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################If the French have as great an aversion to traveling as the English have a propensity for it, both may have reasons. Something better than England is to be found everywhere; the peculiar charm of France—the life of the brain, the talent for conversation, the subtle half-speech so familiar in Paris—is rarely elsewhere. A Frenchman whose raillery finds little comprehension abroad would wither like an uprooted tree; emigration opposes the French instinct. This prelude explains the joy French travellers feel in finding a little Paris abroad: not the exact cookery or the most fashionable pleasantry, but wines, a critical atmosphere, and that wit which makes Paris unique.In 1836, when the Sardinian Court was at Genoa, two Parisians could fancy themselves in Paris while in a palazzo on the last fold of the Apennines near the gate of San Tomaso and the lighthouse. This palazzo, one of Genoa’s magnificent villas, stood above the city; after a morning’s torrents of rain, a clear evening there—sea and sky matching, the quay and villa groves silent, marble heads spouting mysterious water, stars and Mediterranean waves—offered a Boccaccian hour known only on Mediterranean shores: perfumed air, voluptuousness in the chair, an ice or sorbet spoon, town at your feet and a fair woman opposite.Round the table sat the Marquis di Negro and the Marquis Damaso Pareto, two Frenchmen masquerading as Genoese, the Consul-General with his wife and two sleeping children, the French Ambassador and his wife, a secretary who fancied himself crushed and mischievous, and two Parisians come to bid farewell to the Consul’s wife at a splendid dinner. The predominant figure, upon whom eyes dwelt, was a celebrated woman—Camille Maupin, known in letters as Mademoiselle des Touches—the heroine of this improvised festival.The two Parisians were the landscape painter Leon de Lora and the critic Claude Vignon. Camille had been to Florence on business and had brought Leon to show him Italy, extending to Rome for the Campagna. Returning by the Cornice road to Marseilles, she stopped again at Genoa for Leon’s sake. The Consul-General, attentive to a woman of her position and talent, had wished to honor her; she, who knew Genoa intimately, had left her painter to the diplomat and the two marquises and had only a few minutes to spare. Though the Ambassador, a distinguished man of letters, had pressed his advances, Camille, fearing exhibition, initially refused; she consented to a farewell day at the Consul’s villa. Leon said her presence was the only return he could make to those hosting him, so she sacrificed one of the rare free days enjoyed by those watched by the world.Etiquette was banished and curiosity abounded among women eager to see whether Camille’s manly talents impaired her feminine grace. After dinner, conversation—gay and grave, enlivened by Leon’s sallies and by good taste—had scarcely touched literature. But talk turned, as it must among Frenchmen and women, to woman’s sin: when a woman falls, is the blame hers or the man’s? The three respectable women present—the Ambassadress, the Consul’s wife Onorina, and Camille—were merciless to the woman. The men argued that some virtues might survive a woman’s fall. Leon, impatient, broke the dispute, and the Consul rose to put his children to bed and to send a little black pocket-book by Gina—an action showing his wife’s confidence and their mutual tenderness.“I will tell you a story in which I played a part,” the Consul-General said, “and after that we can discuss it. Begin by dissecting a corpse.” All prepared to listen; it was then, after much talk, that he began his tale.He was a man of thirty-four, married about six years, and bore the living portrait of Lord Byron—poetical in look, but genuinely so. His handsome face and delightful nature had captivated a Genoese heiress, Onorina Pedrotti, the sole child of a banker without male heirs. Though daughters rarely inherit in Genoa, Onorina was the exception. The Consul had seemed unwilling to marry, but after two years and the Ambassador’s encouragement, the marriage was decided; the town attributed the Consul’s melancholy reserve to passion. Onorina gave her love as consolation; Signor Pedrotti had no reason to complain of the choice. Powerful protectors in Paris watched the young diplomat. The Ambassador secured for the Consul a title and honors; Signor Pedrotti’s daughter’s dowry was a million francs and the Casa Pedrotti’s fortune passed to the young couple when the old Count Pedrotti died in January 1831.Onorina Pedrotti was one of those Genoese beauties whose type inspired Michelangelo. A full, singularly placed bosom, long hair, dark complexion, dreamy eyes, and a long white dress embroidered with flowers: picture Michelangelo’s Night re-dressed for modern life, and you have Onorina, with a handsome boy of six and a little girl of four—childhood’s type sought by sculptors. This beautiful family was the object of Camille’s secret study. She noted that the Consul seemed too absent-minded for perfect happiness and wondered why a man of his intellect and a hundred thousand francs a year remained at Genoa. Yet she saw, in small details women perceive, that the husband was faithfully devoted. She alternately told herself, “What is wrong?—Nothing is wrong,” following the misleading symptoms of his demeanor; he had the calmness of consummate diplomats.The dinner’s talk had moved to the perennial subject—woman’s sin—when the Consul’s story began:At twenty-two, having taken his degree in law, the Consul’s uncle, the Abbe Loraux, seventy-two and Cure of the White Friars, sought a protector and position for his nephew. One morning in 1824 the Abbe told him to dress quickly: he was to be introduced to a man willing to engage him as secretary—Comte Octave (the Consul uses only the Christian name out of discretion). The Abbe explained that the Count, a high legal authority in the confidence of the Dauphiness and newly a State Minister, would offer the young man a salary and rooms, not admitting him to the Count’s table lest servants take charge, but promising that the secretary’s work would be summons to high posts later. The Abbe urged discretion.The narrator described himself: educated at the College Saint-Louis and under a virtuous headmaster, reared in his uncle’s house, pious and with modest means, he had a thirst for literature and the theatre. Entering Comte Octave’s service meant independent life: a hundred louis a year, society, and perhaps a protectress to rescue him from youthful danger. The Abbe, seeing the young man’s restlessness, would give him twenty francs and a smile; a maternal care that kept him from excess.At the Count’s house—an enormous place like the Hotel Carnavalet, with a hall like Versailles and a marble staircase wide enough for eight—he saw grandeur yet slight neglect: cobblestones hidden in grass, blackened walls, a vast library and study opening into a great garden. Labranche, the footman, announced them: “Monsieur le Cure of the White Friars, and his nephew, Monsieur de l’Hostal.” Comte Octave, dressed in long trousers and gray flannel, rose from his desk, took the Abbe’s hands, and made the young man feel welcome. The Count offered a room and explained the office: the young man would work hard and learn much.At first the narrator felt like an insect near an eagle, but he soon perceived Comte Octave’s extraordinary personality. The Count, though veiled in an austere, hermit-like life, showed depths: a harsh yet refined face, a large mouth suggesting both irony and kindness, a broad forehead, small turquoise-blue eyes as keen and expressionless as Talleyrand’s. His hair, streaked black and white, and his white, woman-like hands suggested delicate habit. The narrator withheld certain details of rank and office out of discretion, though the Count’s public life and fortune were as he described.Living with the Count, the narrator quickly found not plain peacefulness but an ice-buried youth, sorrow and fatigue under intense study—youth entombed under grief and suppressed passion. At moments, when the Count smiled for the Abbe, his age seemed forty, but otherwise the face had been transfigured by some blow. He was pious, regularly attending early mass at Saint-Paul’s for workmen and servants, yet his piety was private, almost a vice of secrecy. The narrator sensed subterranean caverns of sorrow; the Count evaded inquiry like a sharpshooter seeking cover. He often absented himself at odd hours and left the young man to carry on work entrusted to him.The Count loved flowers and owned a garden and study full of rare plants, often buying them half-withered—perhaps, the narrator thought, because they reflected his own faded state. He devoted himself to public interest with almost frantic zeal, as if to cheat some other passion. His struggle was not that of a cynic; he was religious, rigorous, a silent, concentrated man who pitied and admired without transient enthusiasm. The narrator detected long, harrowing internal battles and supposed at first that perhaps the Count loved a woman or lived with some unworthy mistress. He noticed oddities: the Count once conversed eagerly with an old woman, provoking suspect thoughts, and the Count never seemed to invest his considerable savings. Yet he was a masterful, serious statesman and judge—a man whose work and manner combined stoic duty with secret wounds.Over months, the Count and his secretary studied each other. The Count, whom the narrator later learned to be thirty-seven, had an exterior of quiet propriety born of duty, but beneath lay an almost volcanic passion, constrained by will. He attended to his duties and charities; his life was one of relentless labor, interrupted by moments when sorrow broke through and he became like a child—tears, excitement, long solitary stares at a goldfish basin, the senator’s mask dissolving. The young man felt affection like a son’s for this mysterious figure; curiosity, tempered by respect, drove him to watch for the cause of the Count’s unhappiness.The house had been mismanaged through neglect; the Count, despite an income of about 160,000 francs, spent recklessly on servants and was in need of reorganization. The narrator set about reforming the household: getting rid of worthless servants, securing honest ones, improving food and household management. Within two years the Count enjoyed modern comforts: good horses, competent cookery, and reasonable housekeeping at about 30,000 francs a year. The Count, grateful, joked about servants becoming rich restaurant owners. In time, their relationship deepened; the Count took the young man for a son, called him to confidence in practical matters, and introduced him into the highest circles, often sending his carriage and servants to represent him.Once, when the narrator, unlucky at play, lost two thousand francs and refused to touch his savings, he asked the Count for help. The Count, with simple kindness, gave him six thousand francs and said they would henceforth go halves: since the young man represented him publicly, his self-respect must not suffer.
