Fougeres had simply been inspired by the masterpiece of Gerard Douw; he had turned the group of the "Dropsical Woman" toward the window, instead of presenting it full front. The condemned man was substituted for the dying woman--same pallor, same glance, same appeal to God. Instead of the Dutch doctor, he had painted the cold, official figure of the sheriff's clerk attired in black; but he had added an old woman to the young one of Gerard Douw. The cruelly simple and good-humored face of the executioner completed and dominated the group. This plagiarism, very cleverly disguised, was not discovered. The catalogue contained the following:--     510. Grassou de Fougeres (Pierre), rue de Navarin, 2.     Death-toilet of a Chouan, condemned to execution in 1809.Though wholly second-rate, the picture had immense success, for it recalled the affair of the "chauffeurs," of Mortagne. A crowd collected every day before the now fashionable canvas; even Charles X. paused to look at it. "Madame," being told of the patient life of the poor Breton, became enthusiastic over him. The Duc d'Orleans asked the price of the picture. The clergy told Madame la Dauphine that the subject was suggestive of good thoughts; and there was, in truth, a most satisfying religious tone about it. Monseigneur the Dauphin admired the dust on the stone-floor,--a huge blunder, by the way, for Fougeres had painted greenish tones suggestive of mildew along the base of the walls. "Madame" finally bought the picture for a thousand francs, and the Dauphin ordered another like it. Charles X. gave the cross of the Legion of honor to this son of a peasant who had fought for the royal cause in 1799. (Joseph Bridau, the great painter, was not yet decorated.) The minister of the Interior ordered two church pictures of Fougeres.This Salon of 1829 was to Pierre Grassou his whole fortune, fame, future, and life. Be original, invent, and you die by inches; copy, imitate, and you'll live. After this discovery of a gold mine, Grassou de Fougeres obtained his benefit of the fatal principle to which society owes the wretched mediocrities to whom are intrusted in these days the election of leaders in all social classes; who proceed, naturally, to elect themselves and who wage a bitter war against all true talent. The principle of election applied indiscriminately is false, and France will some day abandon it.Nevertheless the modesty, simplicity, and genuine surprise of the good and gentle Fougeres silenced all envy and recriminations. Besides, he had on his side all of his clan who had succeeded, and all who expected to succeed. Some persons, touched by the persistent energy of a man whom nothing had discouraged, talked of Domenichino and said:--"Perseverance in the arts should be rewarded. Grassou hasn't stolen his successes; he has delved for ten years, the poor dear man!"That exclamation of "poor dear man!" counted for half in the support and the congratulations which the painter received. Pity sets up mediocrities as envy pulls down great talents, and in equal numbers. The newspapers, it is true, did not spare criticism, but the chevalier Fougeres digested them as he had digested the counsel of his friends, with angelic patience.Possessing, by this time, fifteen thousand francs, laboriously earned, he furnished an apartment and studio in the rue de Navarin, and painted the picture ordered by Monseigneur the Dauphin, also the two church pictures, and delivered them at the time agreed on, with a punctuality that was very discomforting to the exchequer of the ministry, accustomed to a different course of action. But--admire the good fortune of men who are methodical--if Grassou, belated with his work, had been caught by the revolution of July he would not have got his money.By the time he was thirty-seven Fougeres had manufactured for Elie Magus some two hundred pictures, all of them utterly unknown, by the help of which he had attained to that satisfying manner, that point of execution before which the true artist shrugs his shoulders and the bourgeoisie worships. Fougeres was dear to friends for rectitude of ideas, for steadiness of sentiment, absolute kindliness, and great loyalty; though they had no esteem for his palette, they loved the man who held it."What a misfortune it is that Fougeres has the vice of painting!" said his comrades.But for all this, Grassou gave excellent counsel, like those feuilletonists incapable of writing a book who know very well where a book is wanting. There was this difference, however, between literary critics and Fougeres; he was eminently sensitive to beauties; he felt them, he acknowledged them, and his advice was instinct with a spirit of justice that made the justness of his remarks acceptable. After the revolution of July, Fougeres sent about ten pictures a year to the Salon, of which the jury admitted four or five. He lived with the most rigid economy, his household being managed solely by an old charwoman. For all amusement he visited his friends, he went to see works of art, he allowed himself a few little trips about France, and he planned to go to Switzerland in search of inspiration. This detestable artist was an excellent citizen; he mounted guard duly, went to reviews, and paid his rent and provision-bills with bourgeois punctuality.Having lived all his life in toil and poverty, he had never had the time to love. Poor and a bachelor, until now he did not desire to complicate his simple life. Incapable of devising any means of increasing his little fortune, he carried, every three months, to his notary, Cardot, his quarterly earnings and economies. When the notary had received about three thousand francs he invested them in some first mortgage, the interest of which he drew himself and added to the quarterly payments made to him by Fougeres. The painter was awaiting the fortunate moment when his property thus laid by would give him the imposing income of two thousand francs, to allow himself the otium cum dignitate of the artist and paint pictures; but oh! what pictures! true pictures! each a finished picture! chouette, Koxnoff, chocnosoff! His future, his dreams of happiness, the superlative of his hopes--do you know what it was? To enter the Institute and obtain the grade of officer of the Legion of honor; to sit down beside Schinner and Leon de Lora, to reach the Academy before Bridau, to wear a rosette in his buttonhole! What a dream! It is only commonplace men who think of everything.Hearing the sound of several steps on the staircase, Fougeres rubbed up his hair, buttoned his jacket of bottle-green velveteen, and was not a little amazed to see, entering his doorway, a simpleton face vulgarly called in studio slang a "melon." This fruit surmounted a pumpkin, clothed in blue cloth adorned with a bunch of tintinnabulating baubles. The melon puffed like a walrus; the pumpkin advanced on turnips, improperly called legs. A true painter would have turned the little bottle-vendor off at once, assuring him that he didn't paint vegetables. This painter looked at his client without a smile, for Monsieur Vervelle wore a three-thousand-franc diamond in the bosom of his shirt.Fougeres glanced at Magus and said: "There's fat in it!" using a slang term then much in vogue in the studios.Hearing those words Monsieur Vervelle frowned. The worthy bourgeois drew after him another complication of vegetables in the persons of his wife and daughter. The wife had a fine veneer of mahogany on her face, and in figure she resembled a cocoa-nut, surmounted by a head and tied in around the waist. She pivoted on her legs, which were tap-rooted, and her gown was yellow with black stripes. She proudly exhibited unutterable mittens on a puffy pair of hands; the plumes of a first-class funeral floated on an over-flowing bonnet; laces adorned her shoulders, as round behind as they were before; consequently, the spherical form of the cocoa-nut was perfect. Her feet, of a kind that painters call abatis, rose above the varnished leather of the shoes in a swelling that was some inches high. How the feet were ever got into the shoes, no one knows.Following these vegetable parents was a young asparagus, who presented a tiny head with smoothly banded hair of the yellow-carroty tone that a Roman adores, long, stringy arms, a fairly white skin with reddish spots upon it, large innocent eyes, and white lashes, scarcely any brows, a leghorn bonnet bound with white satin and adorned with two honest bows of the same satin, hands virtuously red, and the feet of her mother. The faces of these three beings wore, as they looked round the studio, an air of happiness which bespoke in them a respectable enthusiasm for Art."So it is you, monsieur, who are going to take our likenesses?" said the father, assuming a jaunty air."Yes, monsieur," replied Grassou."Vervelle, he has the cross!" whispered the wife to the husband while the painter's back was turned."Should I be likely to have our portraits painted by an artist who wasn't decorated?" returned the former bottle-dealer.Elie Magus here bowed to the Vervelle family and went away. Grassou accompanied him to the landing."There's no one but you who would fish up such whales.""One hundred thousand francs of 'dot'!""Yes, but what a family!""Three hundred hundred thousand francs of expectations, a house in the rue Boucherat, and a country-house at Ville d'Avray!""Bottles and corks! bottles and corks!" said the painter; "they set my teeth on edge.""Safe from want for the rest of your days," said Elie Magus as he departed.That idea entered the head of Pierre Grassou as the daylight had burst into his garret that morning.While he posed the father of the young person, he thought the bottle-dealer had a good countenance, and he admired the face full of violent tones. The mother and daughter hovered about the easel, marvelling at all his preparations; they evidently thought him a demigod. This visible admiration pleased Fougeres. The golden calf threw upon the family its fantastic reflections."You must earn lots of money; but of course you don't spend it as you get it," said the mother."No, madame," replied the painter; "I don't spend it; I have not the means to amuse myself. My notary invests my money; he knows what I have; as soon as I have taken him the money I never think of it again.""I've always been told," cried old Vervelle, "that artists were baskets with holes in them.""Who is your notary--if it is not indiscreet to ask?" said Madame Vervelle."A good fellow, all round," replied Grassou. "His name is Cardot.""Well, well! if that isn't a joke!" exclaimed Vervelle. "Cardot is our notary too.""Take care! don't move," said the painter."Do pray hold still, Antenor," said the wife. "If you move about you'll make monsieur miss; you should just see him working, and then you'd understand.""Oh! why didn't you have me taught the arts?" said Mademoiselle Vervelle to her parents."Virginie," said her mother, "a young person ought not to learn certain things. When you are married--well, till then, keep quiet."During this first sitting the Vervelle family became almost intimate with the worthy artist. They were to come again two days later. As they went away the father told Virginie to walk in front; but in spite of this separation, she overheard the following words, which naturally awakened her curiosity."Decorated--thirty-seven years old--an artist who gets orders--puts his money with our notary. We'll consult Cardot. Hein! Madame de Fougeres! not a bad name--doesn't look like a bad man either! One might prefer a merchant; but before a merchant retires from business one can never know what one's daughter may come to; whereas an economical artist--and then you know we love Art--Well, we'll see!"While the Vervelle family discussed Pierre Grassou, Pierre Grassou discussed in his own mind the Vervelle family. He found it impossible to stay peacefully in his studio, so he took a walk on the boulevard, and looked at all the red-haired women who passed him. He made a series of the oddest reasonings to himself: gold was the handsomest of metals; a tawny yellow represented gold; the Romans were fond of red-haired women, and he turned Roman, etc. After two years of marriage what man would ever care about the color of his wife's hair? Beauty fades,--but ugliness remains! Money is one-half of all happiness. That night when he went to bed the painter had come to think Virginie Vervelle charming.When the three Vervelles arrived on the day of the second sitting the artist received them with smiles. The rascal had shaved and put on clean linen; he had also arranged his hair in a pleasing manner, and chosen a very becoming pair of trousers and red leather slippers with pointed toes. The family replied with smiles as flattering as those of the artist. Virginie became the color of her hair, lowered her eyes, and turned aside her head to look at the sketches. Pierre Grassou thought these little affectations charming, Virginie had such grace; happily she didn't look like her father or her mother; but whom did she look like?During this sitting there were little skirmishes between the family and the painter, who had the audacity to call pere Vervelle witty. This flattery brought the family on the double-quick to the heart of the artist; he gave a drawing to the daughter, and a sketch to the mother."What! for nothing?" they said.Pierre Grassou could not help smiling."You shouldn't give away your pictures in that way; they are money," said old Vervelle.At the third sitting pere Vervelle mentioned a fine gallery of pictures which he had in his country-house at Ville d'Avray--Rubens, Gerard Douw, Mieris, Terburg, Rembrandt, Titian, Paul Potter, etc."Monsieur Vervelle has been very extravagant," said Madame Vervelle, ostentatiously. "He has over one hundred thousand francs' worth of pictures.""I love Art," said the former bottle-dealer.When Madame Vervelle's portrait was begun that of her husband was nearly finished, and the enthusiasm of the family knew no bounds. The notary had spoken in the highest praise of the painter. Pierre Grassou was, he said, one of the most honest fellows on earth; he had laid by thirty-six thousand francs; his days of poverty were over; he now saved about ten thousand francs a year and capitalized the interest; in short, he was incapable of making a woman unhappy. This last remark had enormous weight in the scales. Vervelle's friends now heard of nothing but the celebrated painter Fougeres.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The day Fougeres began Mademoiselle Virginie's portrait he was already virtually son‑in‑law to the Vervelle family. The three Vervelles made his studio a sort of residence; they felt drawn to this clean, neat, artistic abode. Toward the end of the sitting the stairway shook; Joseph Bridau burst in like a whirlwind, hair flying, his haggard face casting quick glances. He paced the studio, returned to Grassou, pulled his coat over his stomach and fumbled at a missing button."Wood is dear," he said to Grassou."Ah!""The British are after me," he added—creditors. "Gracious! do you paint such things as that?""Hold your tongue!""Ah! to be sure, yes."The Vervelles flushed at the apparition."Brings in, hey?" continued Joseph. "Any shot in your locker?""How much do you want?""Five hundred. I've got a bull‑dog dealer after me; if he gets his teeth in he won't let go while there's a bit of me left. What a crew!""I'll write you a line for my notary.""Have you got a notary?""Yes.""That explains why you still paint cheeks like a perfumer's sign."Grassou colored; Virginie sat."Take Nature as you find her," resumed the great painter, lecturing. "Mademoiselle is red‑haired. Is that a sin? All things are magnificent in painting. Put some vermilion on your palette, warm those cheeks; touch the little brown spots; butter it in. Do you pretend to know more than Nature?""Look here," said Fougeres, "take my place while I go and write that note."Vervelle rolled to the table and whispered to Grassou: "Won't that country lout spoil it?""If he would only paint the portrait of your Virginie it would be worth a thousand times more than mine," replied Fougeres vehemently.Hearing this, the bourgeois withdrew to his wife, stupefied by the intruder and uneasy about his daughter's portrait."Here, follow these indications," said Bridau, returning the palette and taking the note. "I won't thank you. I can go back to d'Arthez' chateau, where I'm doing a dining‑room and Leon de Lora the door‑tops — masterpieces! Come and see us."He left without taking leave, having had enough of looking at Virginie."Who is that man?" asked Madame Vervelle."A great artist," Grassou answered."Are you sure," said Virginie, "that he has done no harm to my portrait? He frightened me.""He has only done it good," replied Grassou."Well, if he is a great artist, I prefer a great artist like you," said Madame Vervelle.The ways of genius had ruffled these orderly bourgeois.Saint‑Martin's summer was beginning. Timid as a neophyte before a man of genius, Vervelle invited Fougeres to his country house the following Sunday. "You artists want emotions, great scenes, witty talk; but you'll find good wines, and my picture collection will compensate an artist like you for dining with mere merchants," he said.This flattery stroked poor Pierre Grassou's innocent self‑love; he was unaccustomed to such compliments. The honest artist—an atrocious mediocrity with a heart of gold, loyal soul, poor draughtsman, worthy fellow—decorated by royalty with the Legion of Honor, prepared to go to Ville d'Avray to enjoy the last fine days of the year. He went modestly by public conveyance and admired the bottle‑dealer's villa: a five‑acre park atop Ville d'Avray, commanding a noble view. He imagined marrying Virginie and owning that villa.The Vervelles received him with enthusiastic, frank bourgeois absurdity that overwhelmed him. It was a day of triumph. The prospective son‑in‑law was marched about on raked nankeen paths; the trees looked brushed and the lawns freshly mown. The country air carried enticing cooking smells. Everything about the mansion proclaimed: "We have a great artist among us."Little Vervelle rolled like an apple through his park, the daughter meandered, and the mother followed. For seven hours they attended Pierre Grassou. After a magnificent dinner, Monsieur and Madame Vervelle staged their grand theatrical effect—the opening of the picture gallery, lit by carefully arranged lamps. Three neighbors (retired merchants), an old uncle (expected to inherit), an elderly Demoiselle Vervelle, and other guests joined Grassou, eager to hear his opinion of Pere Vervelle's famous collection, whose value he enjoyed inflating. The bottle‑merchant fancied himself rivaling King Louis‑Philippe and the galleries of Versailles.The pictures, magnificently framed, bore labels in black letters on gold:               Rubens     Dance of fauns and nymphs                         Rembrandt     Interior of a dissecting room. The physician van Tromp             instructing his pupils.There were one hundred and fifty pictures, varnished and dusted; some were veiled by green baize curtains not drawn in the presence of young ladies.Pierre stood with arms pendent and mouth agape as he recognized half his own pictures among them. He was Rubens, Rembrandt, Mieris, Metzu, Paul Potter, Gerard Douw—twenty great masters in one."What is the matter? You've turned pale!" someone cried."Daughter, a glass of water, quick!" Madame Vervelle exclaimed. The painter took Pere Vervelle by the button and led him aside, ostensibly to look at a Murillo—Spanish pictures being in vogue."You bought your pictures from Elie Magus?""Yes, all originals.""Between ourselves, tell me what he made you pay for those I shall point out to you."They walked the gallery together; the guests watched the gravity with which the artist and host examined each picture."Three thousand francs," whispered Vervelle as they reached the last, "but I tell everybody forty thousand.""Forty thousand for a Titian!" said the artist aloud. "Why, it is nothing at all!""Didn't I tell you," said Vervelle, "that I had three hundred thousand francs' worth of pictures?""I painted those pictures," said Pierre Grassou in Vervelle's ear, "and I sold them one by one to Elie Magus for less than ten thousand francs the whole lot.""Prove it to me," said the bottle‑dealer, "and I double my daughter's dot, for if it is so, you are Rubens, Rembrandt, Titian, Gerard Douw!""And Magus is a famous picture‑dealer!" said the painter, now understanding why his pictures had been aged in Elie's shop and why Magus had specified certain subjects.Far from losing esteem, Monsieur de Fougeres (as the family called him) gained so much that, when the portraits were finished, he presented them gratuitously to his father‑in‑law, mother‑in‑law, and wife.At the present day Pierre Grassou, who never misses exhibiting at the Salon, is considered in bourgeois circles a fine portrait‑painter. He earns some twenty thousand francs a year and spoils a thousand francs' worth of canvas. His wife brought six thousand francs' dowry, and he lives with his father‑in‑law. The Vervelles and the Grassous, who agree delightfully, keep a carriage, and are content. Pierre never leaves the bourgeois world, where he is esteemed; no family portrait between the barrier du Trone and the rue du Temple escapes him; none costs less than five hundred francs. The great reason bourgeois families employ him is: "Say what you will of him, he lays by twenty thousand francs a year with his notary."Because Grassou took a creditable part in the May 12th riots he was made an officer of the Legion of Honor and is a major in the National Guard. The Museum of Versailles commissioned a battle‑piece from such an excellent citizen, and he walked about Paris telling old comrades, "The King has given me an order for the Museum of Versailles."Madame de Fougeres adores her husband, who has presented her two children. Though a good father and husband, he cannot banish a fatal thought: artists laugh at his work, his name is a term of contempt in studios, and the feuilletons ignore his pictures. Yet he persists; he aims for the Academy, where he will doubtless enter. And — oh, the vengeance that fills him — he buys works by celebrated artists short of means and substitutes these true works for the daubs in the Ville d'Avray collection.Many mediocrities are more aggressive and mischievous than Pierre Grassou, who is anonymously benevolent and obliging.ADDENDUMThe following personages appear in other stories of the Human Comedy.Bridau, Joseph — The Purse; A Bachelor's Establishment; A Distinguished Provincial at Paris; A Start in Life; Modeste Mignon; Another Study of Woman; Letters of Two Brides; Cousin Betty; The Member for ArcisCardot (Parisian notary) — The Muse of the Department; A Man of Business; Jealousies of a Country Town; The Middle Classes; Cousin PonsGrassou, Pierre — A Bachelor's Establishment; Cousin Betty; The Middle Classes; Cousin PonsLora, Leon de — The Unconscious Humorists; A Bachelor's Establishment; A Start in Life; Honorine; Cousin Betty; BeatrixMagus, Elie — The Vendetta; A Marriage Settlement; A Bachelor's Establishment; Cousin PonsSchinner, Hippolyte — The Purse; A Bachelor's Establishment; A Start in Life; Albert Savarus; The Government Clerks; Modeste Mignon; The Imaginary Mistress; The Unconscious Humorists############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################MIDDLE OF THE STORY MIDDLE OF THE STORY MIDDLE OF THE STORY MIDDLE OF THE STORY MIDDLE OF THE STORY############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Produced by John Bickers, and DagnyEUGENIE GRANDETBy Honore De BalzacTranslated by Katharine Prescott WormeleyDEDICATIONTo Maria.May your name, like a noble portrait in this work, lie on its opening pages as a holy branch taken from an unknown tree, sanctified by religion and kept ever fresh to bless the house.De Balzac.EUGENIE GRANDETISome provincial houses wear a melancholy like cloisters or ruins; silence and barrenness prevail until a pale, motionless face appears at a window. Such was a dwelling in Saumur at the top of the steep street toward the chateau. This little-frequented, narrow, tortuous lane, echoing under its pebbles, is lined with three-century-old wooden houses, their carved ends blackened by time. Slate-covered beams trace bluish lines along frail walls; rotten shingles sag; blackened window-sills with faint sculpture hold clay pots. Doors studded with enormous nails bear domestic hieroglyphics—marks of Protestantism, Leaguer curses, or trades—the history of France written in wood.Next to a tottering artisan’s house rose a country gentleman’s mansion with battered armorial bearings. In this hilly street the ground-floors are neither shops nor warehouses but the simple ouvroirs of another age: low, deep, dark rooms without show-windows. Doors open in two parts; the upper half is fastened back while the lower, fitted with a spring-bell, swings continually. Light and air enter through the upper door or an open space between ceiling and low front wall, closed each evening by solid shutters and iron bars.This wall serves as a counter displaying mere samples—tubs of cod and salt, bundles of sail-cloth, cordage, copper wire, iron hoops, a few cloth pieces. Inside a neat girl in a white kerchief calls her father or mother, who sells what you want, phlegmatic, civil, or arrogant. A cooper may seem to own little beyond boat-ribs and laths, yet his wood-yard below supplies the cooperage of Anjou; a hot season makes him rich, a rainy one ruins him. Here, as in Touraine, weather controls commerce: wine-growers, proprietors, wood-merchants, coopers, inn-keepers, mariners—all watch the sun and dread frost, rain, drought. The barometer smooths, saddens, or brightens their faces; from end to end of this street men call out its signs: “Here’s golden weather,” or “It rains louis,” knowing what sun or rain will bring.On Saturdays these traders, with vineyards and provisions arranged, close shop and spend two days in the country. Purchases and profits settled, they have hours for pleasure, observation, criticism, and spying. Neighbors remark on every partridge or pot; a girl peeking at a window is seen by idling groups. Consciences live in sunlight; the dark, silent houses hide no mysteries. Life is mostly at door thresholds where households breakfast, dine, quarrel, and examine every passer-by. Once strangers were bantered from door to door—origin of many stories and the nickname copieux for Angers folk famed for such urban sarcasm.The ancient mansions of Saumur’s old town crown this street; the melancholy dwelling at its top is one of those venerable relics. Follow the thoroughfare to the dark recess hiding the door of Monsieur Grandet’s house. To explain why the town calls it by his name, we must relate Monsieur Grandet’s biography.Grandet’s provincial reputation is best understood locally. In 1789, still called le Père Grandet, he was a master-cooper who could read, write, and cipher. When the Republic sold church property in the arrondissement, this forty-year-old cooper had just married the daughter of a rich wood-merchant. With about two thousand louis-d’or—his and his wife’s dowry—and help from two hundred double louis given by his father-in-law to the republican presiding over sales, Grandet bought, cheaply, one of the finest vineyards, an old abbey, and several farms. The inhabitants, not very revolutionary, admired his boldness and patriotism; in truth his thought was open only to vineyards. Appointed to administrate Saumur, his influence proved pacific and commercial: he protected former nobles when possible and supplied Republican armies with puncheons of white wine, taking payment in splendid fields then reserved for sale.Under the Consulate Grandet became mayor, governed wisely, and reaped better pickings. Under the Empire he was called Monsieur Grandet; Napoleon supplanted him in office but he left without regret. He had built roads to his properties; his house and lands, assessed advantageously, paid moderate taxes; and his vineyards, through his care, became the “head of the country,” the local term for the finest yielders. He might well have asked for the Legion of Honor.By 1806 Grandet was fifty-seven, his wife thirty-six, and their only daughter ten. That year he inherited three fortunes—those of Madame de la Gaudiniere (née de la Bertelliere), old Monsieur de la Bertelliere, and Madame Gentillet—a hoard amassed by avarice. Old Monsieur de la Bertelliere prized his sight of gold above all. The inhabitants computed his savings by “the revenues of the sun’s wealth,” as they said.Grandet thus obtained a modern nobility. He farmed a hundred acres of vineyard yielding seven or eight hundred hogsheads in good years; he owned thirteen farms, an old abbey whose windows he had walled to save expense, one hundred and twenty-seven acres of meadow with three thousand poplars planted in 1793, and the house where he lived. This was his visible estate; as to other property, only two men could hazard a guess at its value: Monsieur Cruchot, a notary who managed Grandet’s usurious investments, and Monsieur des Grassins, Saumur’s richest banker, in whose profits Grandet had a secret share.Cruchot and des Grassins, discreet though obsequious, prompted townspeople to estimate Grandet’s wealth by their deference. No one doubted he kept a private treasure, a nightly hoard of louis. Avaricious observers found proof in his eyes, a yellow-metal gleam. The look of a man used to drawing enormous interest becomes furtive, eager, mysterious—an unspoken language of passion. Grandet inspired respect as one who owed no man: skilful cooper and experienced wine-grower, he could tell whether to make a thousand puncheons or five hundred; he never failed in speculation and always had casks when they were dear. He stored vintages and sold them slowly; his famous 1811 brought over two hundred and forty thousand francs.Financially, Grandet was part tiger, part boa-constrictor: lying in wait, springing on prey, swallowing masses of louis, then resting like a snake digesting—impassible, methodical, cold. Many felt the rending of those polished claws. For Cruchot he procured money at eleven per cent; for others des Grassins discounted bills at frightful deductions. Market talk daily mentioned Grandet. To some his fortune was a patriotic boast. Innkeepers and merchants told strangers: “We have two or three millionaire establishments; but as for Monsieur Grandet, he does not himself know how much he is worth.”In 1816 Saumur reckoned his landed property near four millions; having made about a hundred thousand francs a year from 1793 to 1817, it was fair to presume he held cash nearly equal to his estate. After a game of boston or vine-talk, townspeople said: “Le Père Grandet? Le Père Grandet must have at least five or six millions.”“You are cleverer than I; I have never been able to find out the amount,” Cruchot or des Grassins would answer when overheard.If Parisians spoke of Rothschild or Lafitte, Saumur folk compared them to Grandet; incredulous if anyone said a Parisian was richer. Such a fortune explained his actions. Early oddities once ridiculed had long ceased. His slightest movements, like a blink, became laws to the countryside where men studied him like a naturalist studies instinct.“It will be a hard winter,” said one; “Père Grandet has put on his fur gloves.”“Père Grandet is buying quantities of staves; there will be plenty of wine this year.”Grandet never bought bread or meat: his farmers supplied capons, chickens, eggs, butter, and his tithe of wheat. He owned a mill; the tenant took grain as rent and returned flour and bran. La Grande Nanon, his only servant, baked every Saturday. He arranged with gardeners and tenants for vegetables; he sold most fruits at market. Firewood came from hedgerows and half-rotten sheds whose planks farmers carted to town, cut, and stacked in his wood-house, receiving his thanks. His known expenses were consecrated bread, clothing for his wife and daughter, hire of their chairs in church, La Grande Nanon’s wages, tinning saucepans, lights, taxes, repairs, and industrial costs. He had six hundred acres of woodland recently bought, put under a neighbor’s care for a promised indemnity; after acquiring this, he ate game for the first time.Grandet’s manners were simple: he spoke little in short sententious phrases in a soft voice. Early he had stuttered when speaking at length; this incoherence, attributed to defects of education, was assumed and will be explained later. Four brief sentences sufficed him: “I don’t know; I cannot; I will not; I will see about it.” He never said yes or no nor committed himself to writing. If people talked, he listened coldly, chin in his right hand, right elbow on the left, forming opinions he never changed. He reflected long before any business agreement; when opponents confidently revealed plans, Grandet answered: “I can decide nothing without consulting my wife.” His wife, reduced to helpless slavery, was a useful screen in business. He went nowhere among friends; he neither gave nor accepted dinners; he made no stir, economizing even in motion. He never disturbed others’ things out of respect for property, yet at home coarser habits surfaced where self-control relaxed.Physically Grandet was five feet high, thick-set, square-built, with knotted knees and broad shoulders; his round face, pitted by small-pox, a straight chin, white teeth, and eyes with the calm, devouring expression of a basilisk. His forehead showed transverse wrinkles and prominent bumps; his yellow-gray hair was mockingly called silver and gold. His thick-ended nose bore a veined wen the common people said was full of malice. His countenance showed dangerous cunning, an integrity without warmth, and the egoism of a man used to concentrate every feeling on avarice’s enjoyments and upon the only human being who mattered to him—his daughter and sole heiress, Eugénie. Attitude, manners, bearing, all testified to the self-belief habit of success gives.Though outwardly unctuous, Grandet’s nature was iron. His dress had not changed since 1791: stout shoes tied with thongs, thick woollen stockings, short coarse maroon breeches with silver buckles, a yellow-and-puce velvet waistcoat, a large maroon coat, a black cravat, and a quaker’s hat. His gloves, thick as a gendarme’s, lasted twenty months; to preserve them he always laid them on his hat in one particular spot. Saumur knew no more.Only six persons had the right of entrance to Grandet’s house. The most important of the first three was a nephew of Monsieur Cruchot. Since his appointment president of the Civil courts of Saumur this young man had added Bonfons to Cruchot, signing himself C. de Bonfons. Any litigant who called him Monsieur Cruchot would soon feel the folly in court. The magistrate, thirty-three, owned Bonfons worth seven thousand francs a year; he expected to inherit his uncle the notary’s and another uncle Abbé Cruchot’s property. These three Cruchots, backed by cousins and allies, formed a party like the Medici.Madame des Grassins, mother of a son of twenty-three, came assiduously to play cards with Madame Grandet, hoping to marry her Adolphe to Mademoiselle Eugénie. Monsieur des Grassins furthered his wife’s schemes by services constantly rendered to the old miser. The des Grassins had adherents and cousins. On the Cruchot side the abbé—the family Talleyrand—backed by his brother the notary, contested every inch with his female adversary and tried to obtain the heiress for his nephew the president.This secret warfare for Eugénie’s hand kept Saumur agitated. Would Mademoiselle Eugénie marry Monsieur le président or Monsieur Adolphe des Grassins? Some said Monsieur Grandet would give his daughter to neither: eaten with ambition, he was said to seek a peer of France to whom an income of three hundred thousand francs would make the Grandet casks acceptable. Others replied the des Grassins were nobles and rich; Adolphe was personable; unless the old man had a papal nephew at his beck, such an alliance should satisfy a man who had come from nothing—whom Saumur remembered with an adze in his hand and the bonnet rouge. Certain wise heads noted Cruchot de Bonfons had the right of entry at all times whereas his rival was received only on Sundays; others trusted Madame des Grassins’ intimacy with the women. “It is diamond cut diamond,” said a Saumur wit.The oldest inhabitants declared the Grandets knew better than to let the property go out of the family, and that Mademoiselle Eugénie Grandet of Saumur would marry the son of Monsieur Grandet of Paris, a wealthy wholesale wine-merchant. To this the Cruchotines and Grassinists replied: the two brothers had seen each other only twice in thirty years; Monsieur Grandet of Paris, mayor and deputy and colonel of the National Guard, disowned the Saumur Grandets and meant to ally with some ducal family favored by Napoleon. In short, nothing was not said of the heiress, talked of for fifty miles and even on conveyances from Angers to Blois.At the beginning of 1811 the Cruchotines won an advantage. The estate of Froidfond, noted for its park, mansion, farms, streams, ponds, and forests and worth about three millions, was put up for sale by the young Marquis de Froidfond, who had to liquidate. Maître Cruchot, the president, and the abbé, aided by adherents, prevented sale in small lots. The notary concluded a bargain for the whole property, payable in gold, persuading the marquis that suits against purchasers of small lots would delay collection; it was better to sell the whole to Monsieur Grandet, solvent and able to pay in ready money. The marquisate of Froidfond was accordingly conveyed into Monsieur Grandet’s hands, who, to Saumur’s astonishment, paid for it under proper discount and formalities.This affair echoed from Nantes to Orléans. Monsieur Grandet took advantage of a cart returning by Froidfond to see his chateau. After casting a master’s eye over the property, he returned to Saumur, satisfied he had invested at five per cent, and seized by the stupendous thought of extending the marquisate by concentrating his property there. Then, to fill his coffers, now nearly empty, he resolved to thin his woods and sell the poplars in the meadows.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################One now understands the full meaning of “the house of Monsieur Grandet”—that cold, silent, pallid dwelling above the town, sheltered by the ruined ramparts. The two pillars and the arch of the porte-cochere were built, like the house, of tufa, a soft white Loire stone that hardly lasts two centuries. Irregular holes, bored or eaten by the weather, made the arch and side walls look like vermiculated stonework, like a jail gateway. Above the arch was a bas-relief in harder stone representing the four seasons, its faces crumbling and blackened. A projecting plinth above it had taken root in chance growths—pellitory, bindweed, nettles, plantain, and even a little cherry-tree.The archway door was solid oak, brown, shrunken, and split; though frail it was held by iron bolts in symmetrical patterns. A small square grating with close bars, red with rust, filled the middle panel and served as the knocker’s aperture; a ring struck upon the grinning head of a huge nail. Through the grating—originally for recognizing friends in civil war—one could perceive, at the far end of the dark slimy vault, a few broken steps leading to a garden shut in by thick damp walls nourishing sickly herbage. These walls were the ruined ramparts under which stretched the gardens of several neighboring houses.The principal room on the ground floor was a large hall entered directly from the porte-cochere vault. In small towns of Anjou, Touraine, and Berry the hall is antechamber, salon, office, boudoir, and dining-room all in one; it is the theatre of domestic life. There the barber came twice a year to cut Monsieur Grandet’s hair; farmers, the curé, the under-prefect, and the miller’s boy came on business. This room, with two windows onto the street, was entirely of wood: gray panels with ancient mouldings covered the walls; ceiling beams painted gray showed between whitewashed spaces yellowed with age. An old brass clock inlaid with arabesques adorned the mantel of the ill-cut white stone chimney-piece, above which hung a greenish mirror whose bevel reflected a thread of light along a gothic damascened steel frame. Two copper-gilt candelabra decorated the chimney-piece corners; by removing their side branches each main stem served as a candlestick. Antique chairs were covered with tapestry of La Fontaine’s fables, their colors faded and darned.At the four corners of the hall were closets or buffets, surmounted by dirty shelves. An old marquetry card-table, its top a chessboard, stood between the windows. Above it hung an oval barometer with a black border enlivened by gilt bands where flies had damaged the gilding. Opposite the chimney-piece were two pastel portraits, said to represent Madame Grandet’s grandfather, old Monsieur de la Bertelliere, as a lieutenant in the French guard, and the deceased Madame Gentillet in shepherdess guise. The windows were draped with red gros de Tours curtains held back by silken cords with ecclesiastical tassels—luxuries little in keeping with Monsieur Grandet’s habits but included in the purchase of the house along with the steel pier-glass, the tapestries, and the rosewood buffets.By the window nearest the door stood a straw chair on castors to lift Madame Grandet high enough to see passers-by. A stained cherry-wood work-table filled the embrasure, and Eugenie Grandet’s little armchair stood beside it. Here their lives had flowed peacefully for fifteen years in a round of work from April to November; on November 1 they took their winter station by the chimney. Grandet permitted a fire only from November 1 to March 31, regardless of early spring or wintry autumn chills. A foot-warmer filled with embers—the la Grande Nanon contrivance—saved mother and daughter from chilly mornings and evenings. Mother and daughter managed the family linen, spending their days in labor; if Eugenie wished to embroider a collar she had to take time from sleep and deceive her father to obtain light. For a long time the miser dispensed tallow candles to his daughter and la Grande Nanon just as he rationed bread and other necessities.La Grande Nanon was perhaps the only person capable of willingly accepting her master’s despotism. The whole town envied Monsieur and Madame Grandet for possessing her. So called for her height of five feet eight inches, she had lived with Monsieur Grandet thirty-five years. Though she received only sixty francs a year, she was reputed one of Saumur’s richest serving-women; those sixty francs, accumulated over thirty-five years, had enabled her to invest four thousand francs in an annuity with Maître Cruchot. This result of long, persistent economy seemed gigantic; every servant in town envied the old spinster, never considering the hard slavery through which it had been won.At twenty-two she had been unable to find a situation because her face repelled most, though some might have admired it on a grenadier’s shoulders. Forced to leave a farm where she kept cows because the dwelling burned down, she came to Saumur, robust and ready for labor. Le Père Grandet, then set on marriage and setting up his household, noticed the girl who had been rejected door to door. A good judge of strength from his trade as a cooper, he appreciated the work to be had from a woman shaped like a Hercules—firm on her feet, strong in the hips, square in the back, with hands like a cartman and honesty as sound as her unblemished virtue. Neither the warts on her martial visage, nor the brick tints of her skin, nor her ragged garments dismayed him. He fed, shod, and clothed her, paid wages, and set her to work without treating her too roughly. Seeing herself thus welcomed, Nanon wept and attached herself sincerely to her master, who from that day ruled and worked her with feudal authority. Nanon did everything: she cooked, made lye, washed linen in the Loire and carried it home on her shoulders; she rose early and slept late; she prepared vine-dressers’ food during harvest, watched market-people, protected her master’s property like a faithful dog, and obeyed his absurd exactions without murmur.In the famous year 1811, when the grapes were gathered with unheard-of difficulty, Grandet resolved to give Nanon his old watch—the first present in twenty years of service. Though he turned over his old shoes, it is impossible to call this a gift, for the shoes were always thoroughly worn out. Necessity had made the poor girl so niggardly that Grandet had grown to love her as one loves a dog, and Nanon allowed him to fasten a spiked collar round her throat whose spikes no longer pricked. If Grandet cut bread with parsimony, she made no complaint; she gladly shared the household’s severe regime in which no one was ever ill. Nanon was, in fact, one of the family; she laughed when Grandet laughed, felt gloomy or chilly, warmed herself, and toiled as he did. Never did the master need to find fault with her for pilfering grapes, plums, or nectarines. “Come, fall-to, Nanon!” he would say in generous years.To the poor peasant who had earned nothing but harsh treatment, Grandet’s ambiguous laugh was like a sunbeam. Nanon’s simple heart and narrow head could hold only one feeling and one idea. For thirty-five years she never ceased to see herself before Monsieur Grandet’s wood-yard, ragged and barefoot, and to hear him say, “What do you want, young one?” Her gratitude was ever new. Sometimes Grandet, reflecting that she had never heard a flattering word and might one day appear before God more chaste than the Virgin Mary, would say, looking at her, “Poor Nanon!” The exclamation was always followed by an undefinable look from the old servant. The words, uttered from time to time, formed a chain of friendship nothing ever parted, each exclamation adding a link. Such compassion, arising in the miser and accepted gratefully by the old spinster, had something inconceivably horrible about it. This cruel pity, recalling a thousand pleasures to the cooper, was to Nanon the sum total of happiness. Who does not likewise say, “Poor Nanon!” God will recognize his angels by the inflexions of their voices and by their secret sighs.Many households in Saumur treated servants better but received less satisfaction in return. People asked, “What have the Grandets ever done to make their Grande Nanon so attached? She would go through fire and water for them!” Her kitchen, whose barred windows looked into the court, was always clean, neat, and cold—a true miser’s kitchen where nothing went to waste. When Nanon had washed dishes, locked up dinner remains, and put out her fire, she left the kitchen, separated by a passage from the living-room, and went to spin hemp beside her masters. One tallow candle sufficed the family for the evening. The servant slept at the end of the passage in a closet lit only by a fan-light. Her robust health enabled her to live in this hole; she could hear the slightest noise through the deep silence that reigned day and night. Like a watchdog, she slept with one ear open and took her rest alert.A description of other parts of the dwelling will appear with the events of this history, though this sketch of the hall, where the household’s whole luxury appears, may enable the reader to surmise the nakedness of the upper floors.In 1819, at the beginning of an evening in mid-November, la Grande Nanon lighted the fire for the first time. The autumn had been fine. This day was a fête well known to the Cruchotines and the Grassinists: the six antagonists, armed at all points, prepared to meet at the Grandets and surpass each other in testimonials of friendship. That morning all Saumur had seen Madame and Mademoiselle Grandet, accompanied by Nanon, on their way to Mass; everyone remembered the day as Mademoiselle Eugenie’s birthday. Calculating when the family dinner would end, Maître Cruchot, the Abbé Cruchot, and Monsieur C. de Bonfons hastened to arrive before the des Grassins and be first to pay compliments. All three brought enormous bouquets gathered in their greenhouses. The president’s flower stalks were ingeniously wound with a white satin ribbon fringed with gold. In the morning Monsieur Grandet, following his custom on Eugenie’s birth and fête days, went to her bedside and solemnly presented his paternal gift—which for thirteen years had been a curious gold-piece. Madame Grandet gave her a winter or summer dress as needed. These two dresses and the gold pieces, supplemented by two more on New Year’s day and her father’s fête, gave Eugenie a little revenue of about a hundred crowns, which Grandet loved to see her amass. Was it not putting money from one strongbox to another, and training his heiress’s parsimony? He sometimes demanded an account of her treasure, formerly increased by the Bertelliere gifts, saying: “It is to be your marriage dozen.”The “marriage dozen” is an old custom still in force in parts of central France. In Berry and Anjou, when a young girl marries, her family, or that of the husband, gives her a purse containing, according to means, twelve pieces, or twelve dozen, or twelve hundred pieces of gold. The poorest shepherd-girl never marries without her dozen, even if only coppers. They still tell in Issoudun of a “dozen” presented to a rich heiress containing one hundred and forty-four portugaises d’or. Pope Clement VII., uncle of Catherine de’ Medici, gave her when he married her to Henri II. a dozen antique gold medals.During dinner, pleased to see Eugenie in a new gown, Grandet exclaimed, “As it is Eugenie’s birthday let us have a fire; it will be a good omen.”“Mademoiselle will be married this year, that’s certain,” said la Grande Nanon, carrying away the remains of the goose—the pheasant of tradesmen.“I don’t see anyone suitable for her in Saumur,” said Madame Grandet, glancing timidly at her husband and revealing the conjugal slavery under which she languished.Grandet looked at his daughter and exclaimed gaily, “She is twenty-three years old to-day; we must soon begin to think of it.”Eugenie and her mother silently exchanged a glance of intelligence.Madame Grandet was a dry, thin woman, yellow as a quince, awkward and slow—one born to be trodden upon. She had big bones, a big nose, a big forehead, big eyes, and bore the vague resemblance of mealy fruits without savor. Her teeth were black and few, her mouth wrinkled, her chin long and pointed. She was an excellent woman, a true la Bertelliere: angelic sweetness, resignation like that of an insect tortured by children, rare piety, a good heart, and unalterable equanimity made her universally pitied and respected. Her husband never gave her more than six francs at a time for personal expenses. Ridiculous as it seems, this woman, who by fortune and inheritances had brought Père Grandet more than three hundred thousand francs, felt so profoundly humiliated by dependence—and her gentleness prevented revolt—that she had never asked for a penny or remarked on the deeds Maître Cruchot brought for her signature. This foolish secret pride, this nobility of soul perpetually misunderstood and wounded by Grandet, ruled her conduct.Madame Grandet habitually wore a greenish levantine silk gown, making it last nearly a year; a large white cotton kerchief, a bonnet of plaited straw, and almost always a black-silk apron. As she seldom left the house she wore out few shoes and never asked anything for herself. Grandet, seized with occasional remorse when he remembered how long since he had given her the last six francs, always stipulated “the wife’s pin-money” when he sold his yearly vintage. The four or five louis presented by the Belgian or Dutch purchaser of the wine were the chief visible signs of Madame Grandet’s annual revenues. But after receiving these, her husband would often say, as though their purse were common, “Can you lend me a few sous?” and the poor woman, glad to help a man her confessor upheld as her lord and master, returned several crowns in winter out of the “pin-money.” When Grandet drew from his pocket the five-franc piece he allowed monthly for his daughter’s minor expenses—thread, needles, and toilet—he never failed to ask as he buttoned his breeches’ pocket, “And you, mother, do you want anything?”“My friend,” Madame Grandet would answer, moved by maternal dignity, “we will see about that later.”Wasted dignity! Grandet thought himself generous to his wife. Philosophers who meet the like of Nanon, Madame Grandet, and Eugenie have a right to say that irony lies at the bottom of Providence.After the dinner at which Eugenie’s marriage was first mentioned, Nanon fetched a bottle of black-currant ratafia from Monsieur Grandet’s bed-chamber and nearly fell coming down the stairs.“You great stupid!” said her master; “are you going to tumble about like other people, hey?”“Monsieur, it was that step on your staircase which has given way.”“She is right,” said Madame Grandet; “it ought to have been mended long ago. Yesterday Eugenie nearly twisted her ankle.”“Here,” said Grandet, seeing Nanon pale, “as it is Eugenie’s birthday, and you came near falling, take a little glass of ratafia to set you right.”“Faith! I’ve earned it,” said Nanon; “most people would have broken the bottle; but I’d sooner have broken my elbow holding it up.”“Poor Nanon!” said Grandet, filling a glass.“Did you hurt yourself?” asked Eugenie kindly.“No, I didn’t fall; I threw myself back on my haunches.”“Well! as it is Eugenie’s birthday,” said Grandet, “I’ll have the step mended. You people don’t know how to set your foot in the corner where the wood is still firm.”Grandet took the candle, leaving his wife, daughter, and servant with only the hearthlight, and went into the bakehouse to fetch planks, nails, and tools.“Can I help you?” cried Nanon, hearing him hammer.“No, no! I’m an old hand at it,” he answered.While Grandet mended the worm-eaten staircase and whistled the songs of his youth, the three Cruchots knocked.“Is it you, Monsieur Cruchot?” asked Nanon, peeping through the grating.“Yes,” answered the president.Nanon opened the door, and the hearth’s light enabled the Cruchots to find their way into the room.“Ha! you’ve come a-greeting,” said Nanon, smelling the flowers.“Excuse me, messieurs,” cried Grandet, recognizing their voices; “I’ll be with you in a moment. I’m not proud; I am patching up a step on my staircase.”“Go on, go on, Monsieur Grandet; a man’s house is his castle,” said the president sententiously.Madame and Mademoiselle Grandet rose. The president, profiting by the darkness, said to Eugenie, “Will you permit me, mademoiselle, to wish you, on this your birth-day, a series of happy years and the continuance of health?” He offered a huge bouquet and, taking the heiress by the elbows, kissed each side of her neck with complacency that made her blush. The president, who looked like a rusty iron nail, felt his courtship progressing.“Don’t stand on ceremony,” said Grandet, entering. “How well you do things on fête-days, Monsieur le président!”“When it concerns mademoiselle,” said the abbé, armed with his own bouquet, “every day is a fête-day for my nephew.” The abbé kissed Eugenie’s hand. Maître Cruchot boldly kissed her on both cheeks, remarking, “How we sprout up, to be sure! Every year is twelve months.”As he replaced the candlestick beside the clock, Grandet, who never forgot his jokes and repeated them when he fancied them funny, said, “As this is Eugenie’s birthday let us illuminate.” He carefully removed the candelabra branches, put a socket on each pedestal, took from Nanon a new tallow candle with paper twisted around its end, set it in the hollow, made it firm, lit it, and sat down beside his wife, looking alternately at friends, his daughter, and the two candles. The Abbé Cruchot, a plump puffy man with a red wig plastered down and a face like an old female gambler, stretched his feet well shod in stout shoes with silver buckles and asked, “The des Grassins have not come?”“Not yet,” said Grandet.“But are they coming?” asked the notary, twisting his face into a queer grimace.“I think so,” answered Madame Grandet.“Are your vintages all finished?” asked Monsieur de Bonfons.“Yes, all of them,” said the old man, rising to walk up and down with chest swelling with pride as he repeated the words, “all of them.” Through the passage door he saw la Grande Nanon sitting by her fire with a candle, preparing to spin so as not to intrude among guests.“Nanon,” he said, going into the passage, “put out that fire and that candle, and come and sit with us. Pardieu! the hall is big enough for all.”“But monsieur, you are to have the great people.”“Are not you as good as they? They are descended from Adam, and so are you.”Grandet returned to the president and said, “Have you sold your vintage?”“No, not I; I shall keep it. If the wine is good this year, it will be better two years hence. The proprietors, you know, have agreed to keep up the price; and this year the Belgians won’t get the better of us. Suppose they are sent off empty-handed for once, faith! they’ll come back.”“Yes, but let us mind what we are about,” said Grandet in a tone that made the president tremble.“Is he driving some bargain?” thought Cruchot.At that moment the knocker announced the des Grassins, interrupting a conversation begun between Madame Grandet and the abbé.Madame des Grassins was one of those lively, plump little women with pink-and-white skins who, thanks to provincial calm and virtuous habits, keep their youth past forty. She dressed well, took fashions from Paris, set the tone in Saumur, and gave parties. Her husband, formerly a quartermaster in the Imperial guard, wounded at Austerlitz and since retired, still retained the seeming frankness of an old soldier.“Good evening, Grandet,” he said, holding out his hand and affecting a superiority with which he always crushed the Cruchots. “Mademoiselle,” he added to Eugenie after bowing to Madame Grandet, “you are always beautiful and good; I do not know what to wish you.” So saying, he offered a little box from his servant containing a Cape heather—a recently imported, very rare flower.Madame des Grassins kissed Eugenie affectionately, pressed her hand, and said, “Adolphe wishes to make you my little offering.”A tall, blond young man, pale and slight, with tolerable manners and seeming shy though he had spent eight or ten thousand francs beyond his allowance in Paris while studying law, came forward and kissed Eugenie on both cheeks, offering her a workbox with silver-gilt utensils—mere show trumpery, despite the monogram E.G. in well-engraved gothic letters. As she opened it, Eugenie felt an unexpected delight that made her blush and tremble. She looked to her father for permission, and Monsieur Grandet replied, “Take it, my daughter,” in a tone that would have made an actor illustrious.The three Cruchots felt crushed as they saw the joyous look cast upon Adolphe des Grassins by the heiress, to whom such riches were unheard-of. Monsieur des Grassins offered Grandet snuff, took some himself, shook off grains that fell on the Legion of Honor ribbon in his buttonhole, then looked at the Cruchots with an air that seemed to say, “Parry that thrust if you can!” Madame des Grassins cast her eyes on the blue vases holding the Cruchot bouquets, regarding the enemy’s gifts with the pretended interest of a satirical woman. At this delicate juncture the Abbé Cruchot left the company seated in a circle round the fire and joined Grandet at the lower end of the hall. As they reached the far window embrasure the priest said in the miser’s ear, “Those people throw money out of the windows.”“What does that matter if it gets into my cellar?” retorted the old wine-grower.“If you want to give gilt scissors to your daughter, you have the means,” said the abbé.“I give her something better than scissors,” answered Grandet.“My nephew is a blockhead,” thought the abbé as he looked at the president, whose rumpled hair added to the ill grace of his brown countenance. “Couldn’t he have found some little trifle which cost money?”“We will join you at cards, Madame Grandet,” said Madame des Grassins.“We might have two tables, as we are all here.”“As it is Eugenie’s birthday you had better play loto all together,” said Père Grandet; “the two young ones can join,” and the old cooper, who never played, motioned to his daughter and Adolphe. “Come, Nanon, set the tables.”“We will help you, Mademoiselle Nanon,” said Madame des Grassins, quite joyous at the joy she had given Eugenie.“I have never in my life been so pleased,” the heiress said to her; “I have never seen anything so pretty.”“Adolphe brought it from Paris, and he chose it,” Madame des Grassins whispered.“Go on! go on! damned intriguing thing!” thought the president. “If you ever have a suit in court, you or your husband, it shall go hard with you.”The notary, sitting in his corner, looked calmly at the abbé, thinking, “The des Grassins may do what they like; my property and my brother’s and my nephew’s amount in all to eleven hundred thousand francs. The des Grassins, at most, have not half that; besides, they have a daughter. They may give presents; heiress and presents will be ours one of these days.”At half-past eight the two card-tables were set. Madame des Grassins succeeded in seating her son beside Eugenie. The actors in this scene, so full of interest though commonplace, were provided with bits of pasteboard striped in many colors and numbered, and counters of blue glass. They seemed to listen to the notary’s jokes, who never drew a number without a remark, while in fact all thought of Monsieur Grandet’s millions. The old cooper, with inward self-conceit, contemplated Madame des Grassins’ pink feathers and fresh toilette, the banker’s martial head, the faces of Adolphe, the president, the abbé, and the notary, saying to himself:—“They are all after my money. Hey! neither the one nor the other shall have my daughter; but they are useful—useful as harpoons to fish with.”This family gaiety in the old gray room dimly lighted by two tallow candles; this laughter, accompanied by the whirr of Nanon’s spinning-wheel, sincere only on Eugenie’s and her mother’s lips; this triviality mingled with important interests; this young girl, who, like certain birds made victims by the price put on them, was now lured by proofs of friendship of which she was the dupe—all contributed to make the scene a melancholy comedy. It is, moreover, a drama of all times and places, though here in simplest expression. The figure of Grandet, playing his own game with false friendship and reaping enormous profits, dominates the scene and throws light upon it. The modern god—the only god in whom faith is preserved—money, is here, in all its power, manifested in a single countenance. Tender sentiments hold a secondary place; only three pure simple hearts—Nanon’s, Eugenie’s, and her mother’s—were inspired by them. How much ignorance there was in their simplicity! Eugenie and her mother knew nothing of Grandet’s wealth; they estimated life’s things by the glimmer of their pale ideas, and neither valued nor despised money because they were used to do without it. Their feelings, bruised though they did not know it, ever-living, were the secret spring of their existence and made them curious exceptions among lives purely material. Frightful condition of the human race! There is no joy that does not come from some species of ignorance.At the moment Madame Grandet had won a loto of sixteen sous—the largest ever pooled in that house—and while la Grande Nanon laughed watching Madame pocket her riches, the knocker resounded on the house-door so loudly the women jumped in their chairs.“There is no man in Saumur who would knock like that,” said the notary.“How can they bang in that way!” exclaimed Nanon; “do they want to break in the door?”“Who the devil is it?” cried Grandet.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Nanon took a candle and opened the door; Grandet followed. Madame Grandet, startled, ran to the room’s door crying, “Grandet! Grandet!” The loto-players exchanged looks.“Suppose we all go?” said Monsieur des Grassins; “that knock strikes me as evil-intentioned.”A young man arrived, accompanied by a coach-office porter with two trunks and a carpet-bag. Grandet turned on his wife sharply: “Madame Grandet, go back to your loto; leave me to speak with monsieur.” He shut the door; the players paused their game.“Is he from Saumur?” asked Madame des Grassins.“No, a traveller.”“From Paris, no doubt,” said the notary, checking his watch. “It’s nine; the Grand Bureau diligence is never late.”“He looks young,” added the abbe.“Nanon does not come back,” observed Eugenie.“Perhaps your relation,” remarked the president.“Let us go on with our game,” Madame Grandet said gently, signalling that her husband might dislike talk.Adolphe whispered that the newcomer was probably M. Grandet’s son—Monsieur Charles—whom he’d met at a ball. When Grandet returned, followed by the traveller, the guests saw him like a peacock among poultry.“Sit near the fire,” Grandet said.The stranger saluted; the men inclined their heads, the women bowed. Madame Grandet tried to offer refreshment, but her husband brusquely cut her off. The stranger, surprised by the household’s bluntness, warmed himself, thanked Eugenie—“Thank you, my cousin, but I dined at Tours”—and told Grandet he needed nothing.“From Paris?” Madame des Grassins asked.The young man, Monsieur Charles Grandet of Paris, used an eye-glass to inspect the table and company and impertinently ogled Madame des Grassins. The loto-numbers were called languidly; the players noticed Charles and watched him and Eugenie with curiosity.Charles, handsome and twenty-two, contrasted sharply with the provincial regulars. He was the quintessential Paris dandy newly arrived to import refinements: a full hunting costume, the finest gun, multiple waistcoats, Buisson coats, fine linen, a gold toilet-set, and other trivial luxuries—“implements of a man of fashion,” from the little whip meant to provoke quarrels to chased pistols that might end them. Though his father had admonished modest travel, Charles had taken a private coupe, had his hair refreshed at Tours, put on a black satin cravat and a round collar, showed crossed cashmere waistcoats and a watch on a gold chain, gray trousers with black seams, gloves, and a cane with a gold knob. Only a Parisian could wear such foppery with a dashing air.The Cruchots and Grassins, shabby, snuff-taking provincials, stared as if at a giraffe. Monsieur des Grassins and his son, more versed in fashion, mocked him with satirical looks. Grandet, absorbed in a long letter and holding the only candle at the card-table, paid no heed. Eugenie, who had mended her father’s clothes and never seen such elegance, found Charles seraphic: she inhaled the scent of his curls, envied his soft gloves and refined features, and watched to see him use an embroidered handkerchief. To an ignorant young girl, he seemed an engraving come to life; his manners, eye-glass, and disdainful glance at the coffer enchanted her. For the first time she dreamed of a phoenix cousin.The game ended slowly. La Grande Nanon entered to ask for the sheets for monsieur’s bed. Madame Grandet followed her out; Madame des Grassins softly proposed they stop playing and keep their stakes. The guests gathered near the fire.Eugenie, moved by a young girl’s impulse, slipped away to prepare her cousin’s room. She arranged a warming-pan, covered a table, insisted on fresh linens and a good fire, and decorated the chimney-piece with a lacquer tray, a crystal goblet, a gilt spoon, and an antique flask. She even offered a five-franc piece meant for expenses to buy a wax candle and a little sugar. Madame Grandet protested—“What will your father say?”—but Eugenie coaxed Nanon on the plea of being the girl’s birthday. Nanon laughed and obeyed, though aware of how watchful her master was.Meanwhile Madame des Grassins made overtures to Charles, flattering him into conversation and advising that Saumur had amusements if he avoided being shut up with the miserly uncle, the pious aunt, and the uneducated cousin. Monsieur des Grassins laughed; the notary, the president, and the abbe made discreet remarks. The Abbe Cruchot slyly hinted that Madame des Grassins could best show Saumur’s honors to the visitor. Adolphe introduced himself—“I had the honor of dancing vis-à-vis with you at Baron de Nucingen’s ball”—which pleased Charles.Charles, unbuttoning his coat and striking a theatrical pose, felt he might succeed in Saumur. The des Grassins and the Cruchots traded witticisms and innuendo: Charles ogled the salon’s beauties, Madame des Grassins thought him promising marriage material, the abbe hinted at coquetries and social duties, and Adolphe might find a rival.All the while Père Grandet’s attention flickered over a single letter he read by candlelight. The notary and the president, sensing the master’s preoccupation, watched him for clues. The letter, from Victor-Ange-Guillaume Grandet in Paris to his brother, announced a catastrophe: Victor declared he could not survive the disgrace of bankruptcy. Failures of his broker and notary had consumed his resources, leaving debts near four million francs and assets no more than a quarter of that. The wine stock had fallen in value; in three days Paris would shout his dishonor. Unable to endure the infamy, he bewailed the ruin he left his adored son, and, foreseeing his end, begged his elder brother to be a father to Charles. He entreated that his son be treated gently and provided means to enter trade or the Indies. Victor confessed he had nothing to leave and begged his brother to obtain from Charles a renunciation of any claim on his estate; if the brother refused, remorse would follow; if he accepted, a prayer would bless him. The letter closed with a blessing and the dying man’s trust.Grandet carefully folded the letter and slipped it into his waistcoat, masking his feelings behind a humble air and hidden calculations. Then, recovering composure, he asked his nephew if he had warmed. “Thoroughly, my dear uncle,” Charles answered. Grandet, already thinking of practicalities, brusquely inquired about the women, then told Charles that Nanon would show him his room: “It isn’t a dandy’s room; but you will excuse a poor wine-grower who never has a penny to spare. Taxes swallow up everything.”The des Grassins rose to leave—“We do not wish to intrude, Grandet; you may want to talk to your nephew”—and offered parting bows. The notary fetched his lantern to accompany them. Madame des Grassins, who had not expected an early end to the evening because her servant was absent, accepted the abbe’s offered arm at first but then preferred her son; the abbe, indignant but playful, ended up walking with her husband’s party.“He is a good-looking young man,” the abbe whispered to Madame des Grassins, pressing her arm. “Good-by to the grapes—Eugenie will belong to the dandy. Unless this cousin is enamoured of some Parisian woman, your son Adolphe will find another rival—”“Not at all,” she answered coolly; she considered Eugenie plain and thought a cautious mother need not fear. “This young man cannot fail to see that Eugenie is a little fool—she was as yellow as a quince.”“Perhaps you made him notice?” the abbe suggested.“No, I did not take the trouble—”“Always place yourself beside Eugenie, madame, and you need never speak ill of her; the young man will make his own comparisons.”Madame des Grassins had already invited Charles to dine with her two days hence. The abbe urged her to cultivate the opportunity. The president loudly declared that Monsieur Grandet of Paris had evidently sent his son with matrimonial intentions. Monsieur des Grassins noted the old miser’s mysteries.As the Cruchots and Grassins dispersed, they analyzed the evening’s event with provincial acumen, sensing it altered alliances. The Grassins and the Cruchots, usually rivals, perceived the necessity of a temporary truce to oppose a common threat: they must hinder Eugenie from falling for her cousin, and must prevent the cousin from being taken in by opportunistic comparisons. They contemplated launching “treacherous insinuations, soft-spoken calumnies, slanders full of faint praise and artless denials” to circle about him and mislead him—an alliance of petty strategies intended to manipulate the newcomer’s impressions and preserve their positions in Saumur society.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When the four relatives were left alone, Monsieur Grandet told his nephew it was too late to talk business and that they would settle matters the next day. He outlined the household routine—breakfast at eight, a light midday repast, and dinner at five—and dismissed any suggestion of accompanying Charles about town, saying his work prevented it. He shrugged off talk of his wealth, declaring himself practically penniless and still working like an apprentice. Nanon brought a candle; Madame Grandet offered any help Charles might need. Charles, polite and amused, accepted a faded yellow Anjou candle from Nanon, and Monsieur Grandet led him through the passage beside the kitchen rather than the hall door, stopping at an inner stair-separating swing-door against the north wind. Nanon bolted the outer door and loosed the wolf-dog, whose muffled bark warned strangers.Ascending the worm-eaten stairs, Charles’s expectations sank as he took in smoke-stained walls and creaking steps. He glanced at his aunt and cousin, who smiled in a way he mistook for friendliness. At the landing he noticed three Etruscan-red doors; one above the kitchen was effectively walled off and only reachable through Grandet’s bedchamber. That room was his office and strong-room: iron-barred window, scales for weighing louis, hidden places for deeds and cash, and the solitary key in the miser’s hand. Grandet kept his accounts there like an alchemist guarding treasures, and none, not even his wife, might enter.Eugenie’s door faced the walled room. Madame Grandet’s room opened next to hers; the husband’s bedchamber and the strong-room were separated by thick walls. Grandet gave Charles lodging in the attic above his own room so he could overhear him if need be. After brief goodnights, Grandet showed Charles his attic. Nanon had made a warming-pan fire, which the miser deemed unnecessary and scolded her for. Charles, surprised by the shabby comforts, unpacked in a room with moth-eaten valances, a cold stone fireplace, yellow-wood chairs, a simple night-table, and a thin carpet. He chatted easily with Nanon—who was astonished by his silk dressing-gown and joked it would make a fine altar-cloth—promising perhaps to leave it as charity. Nanon, delighted, dreamed of embroidery and damask; Eugenie dreamed of love.That night Grandet brooded. He resentfully reflected that his brother had left him this nephew and that he had not even fifty francs to give the young man. He pictured the nephew’s finery and worried at the burden. Madame Grandet, practiced in reading her husband’s moods, feigned indifference and kept quiet.At dawn, Eugenie awoke early, her prayers unfinished, and began dressing with a new awareness. For the first time she wished to appear attractive; she brushed and arranged her hair, smoothed her stockings, and put on a new gown that made her feel pleasing. Sitting by the window, she studied the court and narrow garden: a well with a vine, neatly stacked wood, lichened paving, disjointed steps, a rotten trellis with creepers, two stunted apple-trees, gravel walks, box-edged beds, a terrace of old walls leading to lindens and raspberry-bushes, and a drooping walnut near the miser’s sanctum. Under the autumn sun the frost thawed, and the scene stirred a novel delight and tenderness in her. She watched the leaves fall and felt her heart quicken, measuring herself against an inner ideal: she feared she was too plain for Charles, yet her modest, plain beauty contained an unassuming nobility she did not fully appreciate.Flushed with new feeling, Eugenie went down to ask Nanon for cream and a galette for her cousin’s breakfast. Nanon laughed at the plan, worried about supplies and Grandet’s stinginess, and playfully scolded her. Grandet came down as Nanon fussed, and a discussion about provisions followed. He was parsimonious: if they were to use flour and butter, they must do all the cooking in one oven to save wood. Nanon, in a little rebellion, persuaded him to spare a bit of flour and butter for a galette by promising to manage the fires. She noted Charles’s fine boots and linen and admired them with wonder and envy. Grandet grumbled that some people put more money into boots than into the men who wear them.Grandet then took Eugenie for a morning walk to his fields by the Loire, followed by Maitre Cruchot, the notary, who knew Grandet always had an eye for profit. On the riverbank, where thirty men were clearing poplars, Grandet explained his calculations—how removing poplars reclaimed hay and profit—and delighted in showing where to plant cheaper poplars on poor land to save nourishment. Cruchot marveled at Grandet’s knack for turning trees into tens of thousands of francs.Cruchot then mentioned the talk in Saumur about the dandy nephew from Paris and anticipated drafting a marriage-contract. Grandet declared bluntly that he would rather fling his daughter into the Loire than give her to that cousin. The cold refusal struck Eugenie like a blow: her budding hopes, attached to Charles since the previous evening, withered. She returned trembling, the light drained from the street. At the house, Cruchot showed Grandet a newspaper: Monsieur Grandet of Paris had shot himself after being ruined by his broker and notary. The notary feared the Saumur Grandet had refused aid earlier; Grandet merely remarked he had known, and that the son—Charles—didn’t yet know. Cruchot departed, understanding now why Grandet had been so reserved.At breakfast, Madame Grandet and Eugenie sat knitting. Nanon reported Charles sleeping on, unaware. Grandet, cold and harsh, revealed the suicide: “His father has blown his brains out.” Eugenie wept at the thought of the poor young man, and Nanon sympathized, but Grandet scoffed, saying the young man wasn’t worth a sou.
