He ordered that no one speak to Charles of the news until he returned at noon; he intended to talk with the nephew about his affairs then and warned Eugenie that Charles would be sent off to the Indies and she would never see him again. He left, rigid and relentless.Alone, Eugenie flung herself upon her mother, gasping that she was suffocating. The two exchanged a brief, intuitive tenderness. Madame Grandet, aware of her daughter’s emotion, gently counseled restraint, asking whether Eugenie already loved him; Eugenie protested that liking and the desire to help were not wrong. The two silently conspired to make Charles’s breakfast special. Eugenie pleaded for cream and strong coffee; Nanon volunteered to run and buy coffee, fearful of being discovered by Grandet. Eugenie quietly arranged the table: grapes from the attic, a pyramid of pears, vine leaves for presentation. She listened at Charles’s door to his sleeping breaths and mused that sorrow watched over him in sleep. Nanon returned with two fresh eggs she had begged from a farmer; Eugenie embraced her in gratitude.Thus the household prepared in small, furtive joys and humilities—Nanon scheming for paltry luxuries, the mother’s silent understanding, Eugenie’s innocent coquetry—while Grandet’s economy and dominant will cast a shadow. The possible marriage, the news from Paris, and Grandet’s firm determination to keep his daughter’s fortune from Charles intertwined, kindling inner turmoil: Eugenie’s tender awakening and anguish, Grandet’s miserly calculations and contempt, and the private, locked world of the strong-room where gold and papers were jealously guarded. Each character’s habits and moods—Nanon’s practical devotion and small rebellions, Madame Grandet’s timid devotion, Grandet’s harsh thrift, Charles’s courteous ease—set the stage for the conflicts to come, with love and pity on one side and avarice and pride on the other.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################After two hours of nervous preparation—rising frequently to watch the coffee and listen for her cousin—Eugenie at last arranged a modest breakfast, a break from their usual midday standing meal of bread, fruit, a little butter, and wine. She feared her father’s reaction if he should return before Charles had eaten and checked the clock often.“Don’t be troubled, Eugenie; if your father comes in, I will take it all upon myself,” said Madame Grandet, and Eugenie wept with gratitude and sudden love for her mother.Charles arrived about eleven, dressed with Parisian dandyism, smiling and graceful; his presence made Eugenie’s heart beat. He joked about the ruin of his Anjou estate and sank into an easy-chair as the women sat near the fire. Habit-bound and provincial, they described their simple lives—church walks, haymakers—while Charles, used to luxuries, joked about partridges and admired small comforts like fresh eggs, butter, and a pretty watch. The women were tender with him; he returned kindness with caressing looks that stirred Eugenie, who was half-aware of her own blushes when she handled his ring.Charles teased gently, a mixture of vanity and good-nature. He laughed at their boiled coffee in a plain pot and suggested a Chaptal coffee-pot, a fuss Nanon scorned as impractical when there were cows to feed. Eugenie volunteered to make it, earning a reproachful glance from her father even in his absence. The mood shifted as the three women grew silent with apprehension—each expecting the old man’s return.At that moment the familiar knock sounded and Monsieur Grandet entered. His sharp eye took in the spread and the guest, and with dry sarcasm accused them of feasting.“Ha! ha! so you have been making a feast for your nephew; very good!” he said, and, taking a glass, cut a slice of bread, spread butter from his own knife, and ate standing. He noticed the sugar and demanded to know where it had come from; Nanon said it was bought from Fessard. Charles, tasting bitter coffee, sought the sugar, but Grandet had already pocketed it; he suggested adding milk instead. Eugenie, with quiet boldness, replaced the saucer her father had stowed away—a small act of defiance—and offered grapes and preserves to Charles. Grandet scorned the extravagance and warned that Charles must not “revolutionize” his household with such spendthrift habits.When Grandet curtly announced he had “something to tell” Charles in the garden, all three women read the seriousness in his tone. He led the young man out and, after pacing the garden and noting its box-borders and gnarled trees—images seared into Charles’s memory—revealed that his father was ill. The son guessed the truth and demanded horses and flight, but Grandet, blunt and unsparing, told him his father had shot himself and that the newspapers were full of it. He shoved an article into Charles’s hands. The young man, immature in feeling, burst into tears—a reaction Grandet found encouraging because it proved the fellow’s “sensibleness” might preserve his interest in more practical matters than grief.Grandet added the worst: the father had failed and ruined the family; Charles was penniless. He explained, in merciless, practical terms, that to “fail” was a dishonorable crime—worse, he said, than highway robbery—because the bankrupt leaves nothing and hides behind the law; thus Charles’s honor was compromised. The words crushed Eugenie, who, simple and upright, believed her father’s harsh categorization of failure as fraud.The young man’s sobs rang through the house. He fled to his room, grieving convulsively, while the women wept too. Back downstairs, Grandet admonished his wife and daughter not to indulge in “extravagance,” complaining at the breakfast served and insisting he would not pay for such stuffing of a pauper nephew. Eugenie began to see her father differently; his coldness toward mourning galled her.Grandet announced he would pay only as far as Nantes for Charles to go to the West Indies to try to make a fortune, which made Eugenie throw herself into his arms in grateful affection that briefly pierced the miser’s conscience. He explained wildly to her what a million and a napoleon were—fussing over figures and numbers, incapable of feeling beyond arithmetic. Madame Grandet suggested prayers and neuvaines for the dead, to which Grandet scoffed about spending and francs not growing on bushes.A muffled cry upstairs made Grandet order Nanon to see the young man was not suicidal; then he left to see the departing Dutchmen and talk with Cruchot. Once the door closed, Eugenie and her mother breathed easier. Eugenie, newly aware of her father’s nature and of the world’s complexities—millions, vineyard sales, purchases—tried to reconcile these facts with her image of him. She asked trivial questions about louis in a cask of wine and learned from her mother that sales sometimes fetched a hundred to two hundred francs, and that Cruchot had mentioned the purchase of Froidfond as a possible strain on the family.Nanon reported that Charles lay like a calf on his bed, crying—a sight she deemed a blessing. Eugenie, whose sympathy had already deepened into a dawning passion, wanted to go console him. Madame Grandet warned her to be careful—“you will love him!”—but Eugenie, already feeling the stirrings of womanhood, insisted. In Charles’s room she found him prostrate in grief, moaning that if his father had confided his troubles he might have helped repair them. The plain, sincere sorrow affected both women; Charles, speaking of honor, lamented that his father had “failed” and implored God’s forgiveness for him.Eugenie, delicate and instinctive, counseled, “Take courage! Your loss is irreparable; therefore think only of saving your honor,” trying to focus him inward toward what he could preserve—himself. Charles, in a burst of anguished pride, declared his honor the one thing left to protect. He tossed aside his hair, crossed his arms, and prayed forgiveness for his father, hiding his face in his hands. The women, comprehending but helpless before this raw grief, withdrew when he indicated he wanted solitude.Downstairs, they sat sewing in silence, each absorbed. Eugenie had noticed in his chamber the contrast of grief with signs of luxury—razors embossed with gold, a carefully kept dressing-case—which made him paradoxically more attractive. The serious event had, for the first time, stirred these secluded lives. Eugenie spoke of wearing mourning; Madame Grandet deferred to the father’s decision. Eugenie’s thoughts circled Charles: her first desire was to share his sorrow and the burden of his uncertain future.Thus the episode closed with the household shaken—Eugenie newly conscious of love and independent thought, Madame Grandet torn between piety and maternal concern, and Grandet unmoved by sentiment but pragmatic and parsimonious, already calculating what the crisis meant for his money and household order. The young man’s grief—sincere, virgin, and consuming—remained with them, while Grandet’s harsh judgment of bankruptcy as moral theft began to alter the family’s view of him.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################About four o’clock an abrupt knock at the door struck Madame Grandet’s heart.“What can have happened to your father?” she asked her daughter.Grandet entered joyous. After taking off his gloves he rubbed his hands hard; his tanned skin saved them from injury. Presently his secret escaped him.“Wife,” he said without stuttering, “I’ve trapped them all! Our wine is sold! The Dutch and the Belgians have gone. I walked about the market-place before their inn, pretending to do nothing. That Belgian fellow—you know who I mean—came up to me. The owners of the good vineyards have kept back their vintages, intending to wait; well, I didn’t hinder them. The Belgian was in despair; in a minute the bargain was made. He takes my vintage at two hundred francs the puncheon, half down. He paid me in gold; the notes are drawn. Here are six louis for you. In three months wines will have fallen.”These words, quietly uttered and bitterly sarcastic, would have chilled Saumur’s market-place had its inhabitants heard them: their panic would have destroyed prices.“Did you have a thousand puncheons this year, father?”“Yes, little one.”That term for his daughter was the superlative expression of the old miser’s joy.“Then that makes two hundred thousand pieces of twenty sous each?”“Yes, Mademoiselle Grandet.”“Then, father, you can easily help Charles.”Grandet’s rage, cold and concentrated, was fiercer than any spectacle of Babylon’s doom. He had forgotten his nephew until his daughter put him into the account.“What’s this? Ever since that dandy set foot in my house everything goes wrong! You behave as if you had a right to buy sugar-plums and make feasts and weddings. I won’t have that sort of thing. I hope I know my duty at my time of life! I certainly sha’n’t take lessons from my daughter, or from anybody else. I shall do for my nephew what is proper, and you have no need to poke your nose into it. As for you, Eugenie,” he added, facing her, “don’t speak of this again, or I’ll send you to the Abbaye des Noyers with Nanon, see if I don’t; and no later than to-morrow either, if you disobey me! Where is that fellow, has he come down yet?”“No, my friend,” answered Madame Grandet.“What is he doing then?”“He is weeping for his father,” said Eugenie.Grandet looked at his daughter without finding a word; after all, he was a father. He made a couple of turns up and down the room, then hurried to his secret den to think over an investment he was meditating in the public Funds. The thinning of his two thousand acres of forest had yielded six hundred thousand francs; adding sales of poplars and other gains he had nine hundred thousand francs, not counting the two hundred thousand from the sale just concluded. The twenty percent Cruchot promised in the Funds tempted him. He figured on the margin of the newspaper that gave his brother’s death account, all the while hearing his nephew’s moans but not listening. Nanon knocked on the wall to summon him to dinner. On the last step he was saying to himself—“I’ll do it; I shall get eight per cent interest. In two years I shall have fifteen hundred thousand francs, which I will then draw out in good gold,—Well, where’s my nephew?”“He says he doesn’t want anything to eat,” answered Nanon; “that’s not good for him.”“So much saved,” retorted her master.“That’s so,” she said.“Bah! he won’t cry long. Hunger drives the wolves out of the woods.”Dinner was eaten in silence.“My good friend,” said Madame Grandet when the cloth was removed, “we must put on mourning.”“Upon my word, Madame Grandet! what will you invent next to spend money on? Mourning is in the heart, and not in the clothes.”“But mourning for a brother is indispensable; and the Church commands us to—”“Buy your mourning out of your six louis. Give me a hat-band; that’s enough for me.”Eugenie raised her eyes to heaven without words. Her generous instincts, long repressed and now suddenly awakened, were galled. The evening passed as other monotonous evenings, yet it was certainly the most horrible. Eugenie sewed without lifting her head; Madame Grandet knitted; Grandet twirled his thumbs for four hours, absorbed in calculations whose results would astonish Saumur the next day. No one visited; the town buzzed with news of Grandet’s sale, his brother’s failure, and the arrival of his nephew. The upper and middle-class wine-growers gossiped at Monsieur des Grassins, fulminating imprecations against the ex-mayor. Nanon spun; the whirr of her wheel was the only sound beneath the gray rafters.“We don’t waste our tongues,” she said, showing her teeth like peeled almonds.“Nothing should be wasted,” answered Grandet, rousing from reverie. He saw a perspective of eight millions in three years and sailed along that sheet of gold. “Let us go to bed. I will bid my nephew good-night for the rest of you, and see if he will take anything.”Madame Grandet remained on the first landing to hear the coming conversation; Eugenie went up two stairs.“Well, nephew, you are in trouble. Yes, weep, that’s natural. A father is a father; but we must bear our troubles patiently. I am a good uncle to you, remember that. Come, take courage! Will you have a little glass of wine?” (Wine costs nothing in Saumur.) “Why!” added Grandet, “you have got no light! That’s bad, very bad; you ought to see what you are about,” and he walked to the chimney-piece. “What’s this?” he cried. “A wax candle! How the devil did they filch a wax candle? The spendthrifts would tear down the ceilings of my house to boil the fellow’s eggs.”Hearing this, mother and daughter slipped back into their rooms and burrowed in bed like frightened mice getting to their holes.“Madame Grandet, have you found a mine?” said the man, coming into his wife’s chamber.“My friend, wait; I am saying my prayers,” said the poor mother in a trembling voice.“The devil take your good God!” growled Grandet.Misers have little belief in a future life; the present is their all. This thought casts a terrible light upon an epoch in which money sways laws, politics, and morals. Institutions, books, and men conspire to undermine belief in a future life—a belief on which the social edifice rested for eighteen hundred years. The grave, once a feared transition, is now less so; the future has been imported into the present. To obtain, by any means, a terrestrial paradise of luxury and enjoyment, hardening the heart and macerating the body for fleeting possessions, is now a widespread thought—written even in laws that ask, “What do you pay?” instead of “What do you think?” When this doctrine passes from bourgeoisie to populace, where will the country be?“Madame Grandet, have you done?” asked the old man.“My friend, I am praying for you.”“Very good! Good-night; to-morrow morning we will have a talk.”The poor woman slept like a schoolboy who, unprepared, fears his master’s anger. As she drew the sheet over her to stifle sound, Eugenie, in her night-gown and with bare feet, ran to her and kissed her brow.“Oh! my good mother,” she said, “to-morrow I will tell him it was I.”“No; he would send you to Noyers. Leave me to manage it; he cannot eat me.”“Do you hear, mamma?”“What?”“He is weeping still.”“Go to bed, my daughter; you will take cold in your feet: the floor is damp.”Thus passed the solemn day which was to weigh upon the whole life of the rich and poor heiress, whose sleep was never again so calm or so pure. Certain actions seem improbable because we omit to turn psychological light upon the impulses that cause them. Perhaps Eugenie’s deep passion should be analyzed in its fibres; it became a malady that influenced her existence. Many prefer to deny results rather than estimate the force of hidden ties that link facts in the moral order. Here, Eugenie’s past life offers an explanation of her naive want of reflection and sudden emotions. The more tranquil her life, the more vivid her pity, the simpler the sentiments now developed.Restless from the day’s events, she woke at intervals to listen for her cousin, thinking she heard his sighs. Sometimes she saw him dying or fainting from hunger. Towards morning she was certain she heard a cry. She dressed and ran, in the dawning light, to his chamber, whose door he had left open. The candle had burned to the socket. Charles, overcome, slept dressed in an armchair beside the bed, his head on the bedpost; he dreamed as men dream on an empty stomach. Eugenie might weep at her ease; she could admire the handsome face blotted with grief, the eyes swollen as if with tears. Charles felt her presence; he opened his eyes and saw her pity.“Pardon me, my cousin,” he said, evidently not knowing the hour or place.“There are hearts who hear you, cousin, and we thought you might need something. You should go to bed; you tire yourself by sitting thus.”“That is true.”“Well, then, adieu!”She escaped, ashamed and happy. Innocence alone can be so bold. Once enlightened, virtue calculates like vice. Eugenie, who had not trembled beside her cousin, could scarcely stand when she regained her chamber. Her ignorant life had suddenly ended; she reproached herself.“What will he think of me? He will think that I love him!”That was what she most wished him to think. An honest love presages itself. What an event for this poor solitary girl to have entered a young man’s chamber! There are acts in love that to certain souls have the meaning of espousals. An hour later she dressed her mother as usual. They sat before the window waiting for Grandet with the cruel anxiety that freezes or warms the heart when a scene is feared. The goodman came down; he spoke to his wife absent-mindedly, kissed Eugenie, and sat to table without appearing to remember his threats.“What has become of my nephew? The lad gives no trouble.”“Monsieur, he is asleep,” answered Nanon.“So much the better; he won’t want a wax candle,” said Grandet with a jeer.This clemency and bitter gaiety struck Madame Grandet; she studied her husband. The goodman—who in regions like Touraine and Anjou is called “goodman” at a certain age whether kind or harsh—took his hat and gloves saying as he went out,—“I am going to loiter about the market-place and find Cruchot.”“Eugenie, your father certainly has something on his mind.”Grandet, a poor sleeper, spent half his nights in preliminary calculations, giving accuracy to his schemes and assuring their success. All human power is a compound of time and patience. The miser’s life is constant exercise of power for self: self-love and self-interest, compact and intelligent, form egotism. From this arises the persistent curiosity and the habit of trading on others. Where is the man without desire? and what social desire can be satisfied without money?Grandet had “something on his mind.” Like all misers, he craved to play a commercial game with men and win their money legally. To impose upon others was a sign of power, a proof he had the right to despise those who let themselves be preyed upon. Oh! who has ever truly understood the lamb lying peacefully at the feet of God?—the emblem of terrestrial victims. The miser fattens and slaughters the lamb, then despises it. His pasture is money and disdain. During the night his ideas had taken another course, explaining his sudden clemency. He had hatched a plot to trick the Parisians, to decoy and snare them, to make them sweat and hope—a plot to amuse himself, the old provincial cooper, sitting under his gloomy rafters. His nephew occupied his mind. He wished to save his dead brother’s honor without spending a penny on the son or himself. His own funds he would invest for three years; he needed only to manage his Saumur property. Finding nutriment for mischief in his brother’s failure, he resolved to crush the Parisians on Charles’s behalf and to play the good brother at the cheapest terms. The Cruchots were necessary; but he would not seek them—he resolved to make them come to him and to stage, that evening, a comedy whose plot he had conceived, which would make him the town’s admired man on the morrow without costing him a penny.In his absence Eugenie had the happiness of openly busying herself with her cousin, spending upon him fearlessly the treasures of her pity—woman’s sublime superiority, the sole she desires to have recognized, the sole she pardons man for letting her assume. Three or four times she went to listen to his breathing to see if he slept, then when he rose she turned to his breakfast—the cream, eggs, fruits, the plates and glasses—each item a special care. At length she ran up the old staircase to hear the noise he made. Was he dressing? Did he still weep? She reached the door.“My cousin!”“Yes, cousin.”“Will you breakfast downstairs, or in your room?”“Where you like.”“How do you feel?”“Dear cousin, I am ashamed of being hungry.”This conversation, held through the closed door, was like an episode in a poem to Eugenie.“Well, then, we will bring your breakfast to your own room, so as not to annoy my father.”She flew to the kitchen.“Nanon, go and do his room!”That staircase, often traversed and echoing to the slightest noise, lost its decaying aspect in Eugenie’s eyes. It grew luminous; it had a voice and spoke to her; it was young—young like the love it was now serving. Her kind indulgent mother lent herself to the caprices of the child’s love, and after the room was put in order both went to sit with the unhappy youth and keep him company. Does not Christian charity make consolation a duty? The two women drew little sophistries from religion to justify their conduct. Charles became the object of tender and loving care. His saddened heart felt the sweetness of the gentle friendship these two souls, crushed under restraint, displayed when, for an instant, they were unfettered in the regions of suffering.Claiming relationship, Eugenie began to fold linen and arrange the toilet articles Charles had brought; she could marvel at ease over each luxurious bauble and knick-knack of silver and gold, holding them under pretext of examination. Charles could not see without emotion the generous interest his aunt and cousin felt; he knew Paris society and, placed as he was, expected indifference. Eugenie thus appeared in splendor, and he admired the innocence of life and manners he had been inclined to ridicule. When Eugenie took from Nanon the bowl of coffee and cream and poured it with real feeling, giving him a kindly glance, the Parisian filled with tears; he took her hand and kissed it.“What troubles you?” she said.“Oh! these are tears of gratitude,” he answered.Eugenie abruptly turned to the chimney-piece to take the candlesticks.“Here, Nanon, carry them away!” she said.When she looked again she blushed, but her looks could at least deceive, not betray the excess of joy that flooded her heart; yet the eyes of both expressed the same sentiment—the future was theirs. This soft emotion was precious to Charles amid heavy grief because it was wholly unexpected. The knocker recalled the women to duty; happily they ran downstairs with speed to be seated at their work when Grandet entered; had he met them under the archway his suspicions might have been roused. After breakfast, which the goodman took standing, the keeper from Froidfond, whose promised indemnity had not yet been paid, appeared with a hare and partridges from the park, eels and two pike sent as tribute by the millers.“Ha, ha! poor Cornoiller; here he comes, like fish in Lent. Is all that fit to eat?”“Yes, my dear, generous master; it has been killed two days.”“Come, Nanon, bestir yourself,” said Grandet; “take these things, they’ll do for dinner. I have invited the two Cruchots.”Nanon opened her eyes, stupefied, and looked at everybody.“Well!” she said, “and how am I to get the lard and the spices?”“Wife,” said Grandet, “give Nanon six francs, and remind me to get some of the good wine out of the cellar.”“Well, then, Monsieur Grandet,” said the keeper, prepared to settle the indemnity, “Monsieur Grandet—”“Ta, ta, ta, ta!” said Grandet; “I know what you want to say. You are a good fellow; we will see about it to-morrow, I’m too busy to-day. Wife, give him five francs,” he added to Madame Grandet as he decamped.The poor woman was too happy to buy peace at eleven francs. She knew Grandet would let her alone for a fortnight after he had thus taken back, franc by franc, the money he had given her.“Here, Cornoiller,” she said, slipping ten francs into his hand, “some day we will reward your services.”Cornoiller could say nothing and went away.“Madame,” said Nanon, who had donned her black coif and taken her basket, “I want only three francs. You keep the rest; it’ll go fast enough somehow.”“Have a good dinner, Nanon; my cousin will come down,” said Eugenie.“Something very extraordinary is going on, I am certain,” said Madame Grandet. “This is only the third time since our marriage that your father has given a dinner.”About four o’clock, just as Eugenie and her mother had finished setting the table for six and the master had brought up a few bottles of the exquisite wine provincials cherish, Charles came down into the hall. He was pale; his gestures, expression, glance, and voice had a sadness full of grace. He truly suffered; the veil of pain gave him an air dear to women. Eugenie loved him the more for it. Sorrow had leveled the distance between them—Charles was no longer the rich, distinguished young man above her sphere, but a relation plunged into frightful misery. Misery begets equality. Women, like angels, claim suffering humanity. Charles and Eugenie spoke only with their eyes; the poor fallen dandy, orphaned and impoverished, sat apart, proud, calm, silent. From time to time the gentle caressing glance of the young girl shone upon him and drew him from sad thoughts into fields of hope, where she loved to hold him at her side.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Saumur was more excited about the dinner Grandet gave the Cruchots than it had been the night before at his vintage sale, though that sale had affronted the wine-growers. Had the politic old miser entertained from nobler motives he might have been praised; but believing himself superior to the townspeople, he cared little for their opinion.The des Grassins soon learned of Guillaume Grandet’s failure and violent death and resolved to call that evening to commiserate and to discover why Grandet had invited the Cruchots. At precisely five Monsieur C. de Bonfons and his uncle the notary arrived in their Sunday clothes. They dined with appetites; Grandet was grave, Charles silent, Eugenie mute, Madame Grandet reserved. When they rose, Charles said he must retire to undertake a long and painful correspondence.Once aunt and uncle were sure Charles could not overhear, Grandet dismissed his wife and daughter and, alone with the two Cruchots, put on more than ever the cunning that had earned him the nickname “the old dog.” He affected a stutter and some deafness—tricks he used to control conversation and avoid taking responsibility—so as to keep his real intentions in doubt.He began, stammering, to raise the subject of bankruptcy. The president, flattered by Grandet’s awkwardly respectful address, took the lead and explained how a threatened merchant might avoid dishonor by liquidating rather than being declared insolvent. He described how creditors might sell notes in the market at a reduction so that if one held the debts and paid them off by agreement, the estate would be honorably released. Grandet, feigning ignorance, professed that he knew nothing of such Paris tricks and that he lived in Saumur attending to vines and drains. He admitted he might be able to help his nephew, but he must consider, for he had no time or knowledge to engage personally.The president—smooth, sniffing snuff—went on about notes as merchandise, invoking Bentham’s abstract reasoning, and suggested that debts might be bought for twenty-five percent. The notary laughed; the president added practicalities: a friend could go to Paris, buy up the notes at a reduction, obtain extensions from creditors, and thus save the family’s honor. Grandet pretended distress at the complexity and expense, repeatedly claiming he was only a poor wine-grower; but his eye betrayed eagerness.The notary, uneasy, warned his nephew to let Monsieur Grandet explain his intentions. Before Cruchot could resume, the des Grassins arrived with expressions of condolence. The notary seized the opportunity to announce Grandet’s honorable intention to liquidate his brother’s affairs, and that the president had offered to go to Paris to negotiate with the creditors. Des Grassins, who had been gossiping on the way about the old man’s avarice—almost accusing him of fratricide—were astonished and delighted.“Grandet is honorable to the backbone,” cried the banker, embracing him. The scene allowed the des Grassins to present themselves as friends with business sense. The banker suggested that such a commercial matter required a consummate businessman and offered to take charge, since he was going to Paris and could attend to it while on his own affairs.Dropping some of his assumed hesitancy, Grandet confided privately to des Grassins that he trusted the banker more than the president and had other plans. He wanted to buy a few thousand francs in the Funds while the price was low; des Grassins knew about such things and could make the purchase by the end of the month. He signaled that none of this should be told to the Cruchots. The banker, pleased, promised to begin by the mail-post and to take final directions at five o’clock, just before dinner.The parties lingered a little; des Grassins struck Grandet on the shoulder, murmuring that it was fortunate to have such a relation. Grandet, rubbing his wen, declared his love for his brother and his intention to prove it—unless costs prevented him. Des Grassins had cut the Cruchots out of the commission; the president, disappointed at being sidelined, complained to his uncle that he had begun by being “de Bonfons” and ended as nothing but Cruchot. The notary advised modesty: let the des Grassins take this tack, and Eugenie would remain the president’s wife all the same.Soon the news of Grandet’s magnanimity spread through three houses and then the town. Everyone forgave his sale made in defiance of pledged good faith and lauded forsworn generosity; such sudden enthusiasm is French—quick to forget and quick to praise.When Père Grandet shut the door he called Nanon and instructed her not to let the dog loose and not to go to bed; they had work to do. At eleven Cornoiller would bring the chariot from Froidfond; Nanon must tell him to come softly, for there must be no nocturnal racket and no neighborly curiosity. Grandet returned to his private room, moving cautiously so as not to wake his wife and daughter and, above all, not to rouse his nephew, whom he had already begun to condemn for his pale, despairing looks.About the middle of the night Eugenie, anxious for Charles, fancied she heard a cry like that of a dying person. She wrapped herself in a pelisse and, thinking of the light under her cousin’s door, was about to go out when she saw Nanon’s heavy steps and heard horses. Peering into the corridor she met her father’s vague, unnoticing gaze and was terrified. The goodman and Nanon were yoked together by a stout stick; a thick rope over their shoulders carried a small keg like those Grandet made in the bakehouse—filled, as Nanon complained, with heavy copper sous. Grandet cautioned them to be careful of the candlestick; the scene was lit by a single candle on the staircase.Cornoiller had come with horses; Grandet asked if he had pistols—there was nothing to fear—and told Nanon to say to his wife that he had gone into the country and would be back to dinner. The chariot drove off with all possible secrecy; Nanon bolted the great door, let the dog loose, and went to bed with a bruised shoulder, the neighbors unaware of either the departure or its purpose. Grandet’s precautions and reticence were habitual; no one had ever seen a penny in that house, though it was filled with gold.In the morning, gossip at the port reported that exchange on gold had doubled because of military preparations at Nantes and that speculators had come to Angers to buy coin. The old wine-grower, having borrowed horses from his farmers, had seized the chance: he had gone to sell his gold and to bring back treasury notes, converting the sum he intended to put into the Funds and increasing it by advantage of the exchange.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“My father has gone,” thought Eugenie, having heard the news from the head of the stairs. Silence fell; the distant rumble of the carriage faded. A cry came from her cousin’s room; a narrow strip of light showed under the door and slanted across the rotten stair.“He suffers!” she cried, and rushed up. A second moan brought her to the landing. The door stood ajar; she pushed it open. Charles slept with his head drooped over the arm of an old chair, the pen fallen from his hand nearly touching the floor. His strained posture alarmed her; she entered quickly.“He must be very tired,” she thought, and glanced at the dozen sealed letters on the table—carriage-makers, a tailor—guessing he had been settling affairs to leave France. Her eyes fell on two open letters. The words “My dear Annette” at the head of one stunned her. Her heart beat fast; her feet felt nailed.“His dear Annette! He loves! He is loved! No hope! What does he say to her?” Thoughts rushed through her. “I ought to go away—What if I do read it?” She looked at Charles, then gently set his head against the chair back; he let her, like a sleeping child. She raised his drooping hand and kissed his chestnut hair. “Dear Annette!” a demon shrieked in her ear.“I am doing wrong; but I must read it,” she decided, torn. Passion and curiosity triumphed. As she read, the keen glow of first love made every feeling precious.My dear Annette,—Nothing could have separated us but the great misfortune that has fallen on me. My father has killed himself; his fortune and mine are irretrievably lost. I am an orphan at an age when, by my education, I am still a child; yet I must lift myself as a man out of the abyss. If I wish to leave France an honest man—I am without a hundred francs to try my luck in the Indies or America. Yes, Anna, I must seek my fortune in those deadly climates. I cannot remain in Paris. Neither my nature nor my face are made to bear the affronts, neglect, disdain shown to a ruined man, the son of a bankrupt! Good God! think of owing two millions! I should be killed in a duel the first week; therefore I shall not return. Your love—the most tender and devoted love—cannot draw me back. I have no money to go to you, to give and receive a last kiss that might strengthen me.“Poor Charles! I did well to read the letter. I have gold; I will give it to him,” thought Eugenie. She wiped her eyes and read on.I have never thought of the miseries of poverty. If I had the hundred louis for the journey, I have not a sou for an outfit. But no—I have not the hundred louis, not even one. I don’t know what will be left after I have paid my debts in Paris. If I have nothing, I shall go quietly to Nantes and ship as a common sailor; I will begin in the new world like other men who started with nothing and made fortunes. I have faced my future coolly. It seems more horrible to me because I was so petted by a mother who adored me, so indulged by a kindly father, so blessed to have known the love of an Anna. The flowers of life are all I have known. Such happiness could not last. Nevertheless I feel more courage than is supposed for a careless young man—above all, one used to caressing; oh, my father! Annette, he is dead!I have thought of my position and yours. I have grown old in twenty-four hours. Dear Anna, if to keep me with you in Paris you were to sacrifice your luxury, your dress, your opera-box, we should still not have enough for my extravagant ways. Besides, I would never accept such sacrifices. No, we must part now and forever—“He gives her up! Blessed Virgin! What happiness!” Eugenie quivered with joy. Charles moved; terror chilled her. He did not wake, and she resumed reading.When shall I return? I do not know. The climate of the West Indies ages a European who works hard. In ten years your daughter will be eighteen; she may be your companion, your spy. Society will be cruel, daughters sometimes cruel; remember four years of happiness and, if you can, be faithful to my memory. I cannot demand faithfulness, for I must adapt to my new life and take a commonplace view of it. Therefore marriage becomes a necessity; I have found here in Saumur, in my uncle’s house, a cousin whose face, manners, mind, and heart would please you, and who, besides, seems to me—The letter broke off; Charles had fallen asleep again. Eugenie excused him. How could an innocent girl suspect cold-heartedness in such a letter? Young, piously brought up girls see love’s light everywhere; they reflect warmth on their lover and attribute to him their highest thoughts. A woman’s errors often spring from belief in good and trust in truth. Eugenie’s simple heart heard “My dear Annette” like the sweetest words; the tears on the young man’s lashes seemed noble. How could she know that Charles, though he loved his father and mourned him, was moved more by paternal indulgence than by heroic resolve? Monsieur and Madame Guillaume Grandet had gratified every fancy of their son and lavished pleasures on him, keeping him from the hard reckonings other sons face.Yet Charles was a Parisian youth, schooled by society and by Annette to calculate; beneath youthful grace he had learned the prudence of social life—where to “see clear” one must believe in nothing and weigh a friend’s purse daily. After folly and adulation, Annette had made him think soberly; with a perfumed hand among his curls she spoke to him of his future, taught worldly prudence, effeminized and materialized him. Such outward sense often wins the trust of young women. Had Eugenie been more suspicious, she might still have been unable to distrust a cousin whose manners and words matched youthful hopes. Fate had laid before her Charles’s last effusions; she heard the last stirrings of his conscience. She laid down the letter—so full of love—and smiled, watching him sleep; the fresh illusions of life were still on his face; she vowed to love him always. Then she glanced at the other letter, reading it for new proofs of noble qualities she already believed him to possess.My dear Alphonse,—When you receive this letter I shall be without friends; but while I doubt the friendship of the world, I have never doubted yours. I beg you to settle my affairs and get as much as you can out of my possessions. You know my situation. I have nothing left, and I intend to go at once to the Indies. I have written to those I think I owe money, and enclose a list. My books, furniture, pictures, horses, etc., ought to pay my debts. I do not wish to keep anything, except perhaps a few baubles for my outfit. I will send a power of attorney so you can make these sales. Send me all my weapons. Keep Briton for yourself; nobody would pay his value, and I would rather give him to you—like a mourning-ring bequeathed by a dying man to his executor. Farry, Breilmann, & Co. built me a travelling-carriage not yet delivered; persuade them to keep it and not ask for payment. If they refuse, do what you can and avoid anything dishonorable. I owe the British Islander six louis lost at cards; don’t fail to pay him—“Dear cousin!” whispered Eugenie, throwing down the letter and running softly back to her room, carrying a lighted candle. A thrill passed as she opened the drawer of an old oak cabinet topped with the faint royal salamander. She took out a large red velvet purse edged with gold fringe—a relic from her grandmother. She weighed it proudly and began counting the forgotten hoard: portugaises, genovines, Spanish quadruples, Dutch ducats of 1756, Indian rupees, and a napoleon of forty francs received the day before. Her father loved these coins for rarity, but Eugenie thought only of Charles. After a few miscalculations she made out about five thousand eight hundred francs in intrinsic value—nearly six thousand for collectors. She clapped her hands like a child. Father and daughter had each counted their fortune—he, to sell his gold; she, to fling hers into the sea of affection. She put the coins back, took the purse, and ran upstairs without hesitation. The hidden misery of her cousin made her forget hour and propriety; she was strong in conscience, devotion, and happiness.At the threshold, holding the candle and purse, Charles woke and was speechless with surprise. Eugenie set the candle on the table.“My cousin, I must beg pardon for a wrong I have done you; but God will pardon me—if you—will help me to wipe it out.”“What is it?” asked Charles, rubbing his eyes.“I have read those letters.”Charles colored.“How did it happen?” she continued; “how came I here? Truly, I do not know. I am tempted not to regret too much that I have read them; they have made me know your heart, your soul, and—”“And what?” asked Charles.“Your plans, your need of a sum—”“My dear cousin—”“Hush, hush! not so loud; we must not wake others. See,” she said, opening her purse, “here are the savings of a poor girl who wants nothing. Charles, accept them! This morning I did not value money; you have taught me. It is but a means. A cousin is almost a brother; you can surely borrow your sister’s purse.”Eugenie, both woman and girl, never dreamed of refusal; but Charles remained silent.“Oh! you will not refuse?” cried Eugenie, whose heart could be heard beating in the silence.His hesitation mortified her, but the need of his position became clearer, and she knelt.“I will never rise till you have taken that gold!” she said. “My cousin, I implore you, answer me! let me know if you respect me, if you are generous, if—”Moved, Charles took her hands to keep her from kneeling; his tears fell on them. Eugenie sprang to the purse and poured its contents on the table.“Ah! yes, yes, you consent?” she sobbed. “Fear nothing, my cousin, you will be rich. This gold will bring you happiness; some day you shall bring it back to me—are we not partners? I will obey all conditions. But you should not attach such value to the gift.”Charles could finally speak.“Yes, Eugenie; my soul would be small indeed if I did not accept. And yet—gift for gift, confidence for confidence.”“What do you mean?” she said, frightened.“Listen, dear cousin; I have here—” He pointed to a square box covered by a leather case on the drawers. “There,” he continued, “is something as precious to me as life itself. This box was a present from my mother. All day I have thought that if she could rise from her grave, she would herself sell the gold which her love for me lavished on this dressing-case; but were I to do so, it would seem a sacrilege.” Eugenie pressed his hand.“No,” he added after a pause with a tender look, “no, I will neither sell it nor risk its safety on my journey. Dear Eugenie, you shall be its guardian. Never did friend commit anything more sacred to another. Let me show it to you.”He opened the case and revealed a richly wrought dressing-case with gold ornaments. “What you admire is nothing,” he said, pushing a spring that revealed a hidden drawer. He drew out two portraits, masterpieces by Madame Mirbel, richly set with pearls.“Oh, how beautiful! Is it the lady to whom you wrote that—”“No,” he smiled; “this is my mother, and here is my father, your aunt and uncle. Eugenie, I beg you on my knees, keep my treasure safely. If I die and your little fortune is lost, this gold and these pearls will repay you. To you alone could I leave these portraits; you are worthy to keep them. But destroy them at last, so they may pass into no other hands.” Eugenie was silent.“Ah, yes, say yes! You consent?” he added with winning grace.Hearing his words as if he addressed himself, she turned to him with a look of love—the first look of womanhood, half coquette, half depth. He kissed her hand.“Angel of purity! between us two money is nothing, never can be anything. Feeling and sentiment must be all henceforth.”“You are like your mother—was her voice as soft as yours?”“Oh! much softer—”“Yes, for you,” she said, dropping her eyelids. “Come, Charles, go to bed; I wish it; you must be tired. Good-night.” She disengaged her hand; he lighted her way to her room. At the threshold—“Ah!” he said, “why am I ruined?”“What matter?—my father is rich; I think so,” she answered.“Poor child!” said Charles, stepping in and leaning against the wall, “if that were so, he would never have let my father die; he would not let you live in this poor way; he would live otherwise himself.”“But he owns Froidfond.”“What is Froidfond worth?”“I don’t know; but he has Noyers.”“Nothing but a poor farm!”“He has vineyards and fields.”“Mere nothing,” said Charles disdainfully. “If your father had only twenty-four thousand francs a year do you suppose you would live in this cold, barren room?” he added, edging forward. “Ah! there you will keep my treasures,” he said, glancing at the old cabinet, as if to conceal his thoughts.“Go and sleep,” she said, hindering his entrance into the disordered room.They bade each other good-night with a mutual smile.Both fell asleep in the same dream; from that moment the youth began to wear roses with his mourning. The next morning, before breakfast, Madame Grandet found her daughter in the garden with Charles. The young man was still sad, fitting for one plunged in misfortune and measuring his future.“My father will not be home till dinner-time,” said Eugenie, seeing her mother’s anxious face.There was an evident unison of thought between her and her cousin. Charles spent the morning in the hall, his sadness respected. Everyone busied themselves with tasks. Grandet had left affairs unattended, and several people came on business—the plumber, mason, slater, carpenter, diggers, farmers—some to drive bargains about repairs, others to pay or be paid. Nanon put away produce brought as tribute, always waiting for her master’s orders before deciding to use or sell. It was the goodman’s custom, like many country gentry, to drink bad wine and eat spoiled fruit.Toward five in the afternoon Grandet returned from Angers, having made fourteen thousand francs by the exchange on his gold, bringing treasury-notes bearing interest until he should invest them in the Funds. He had left Cornoiller at Angers to look after the horses, nearly foundered, with orders to bring them home slowly after they were rested.“I have got back from Angers, wife,” he said; “I am hungry.”Nanon called from the kitchen: “Haven’t you eaten anything since yesterday?”“Nothing,” he answered.Nanon brought in the soup. Des Grassins arrived to take his client’s orders just as the family sat down. Grandet had not even observed his nephew.“Go on eating, Grandet,” said the banker; “we can talk. Do you know what gold is worth in Angers? They have come from Nantes after it? I shall send some of ours.”“Don’t send any,” said Grandet; “they have got enough. We are such old friends, I ought to save you from such a loss of time.”“But gold is worth thirteen francs fifty centimes.”“Say was worth—”“Where the devil have they got any?”“I went to Angers last night,” answered Grandet in a low voice.The banker started. A whispered conversation began between the two, during which they frequently looked at Charles. Presently des Grassins gave a start of astonishment; probably Grandet was then instructing him to invest the sum which was to give him a hundred thousand francs a year in the Funds.“Monsieur Grandet,” said the banker to Charles, “I am starting for Paris; if you have any commissions—”“None, monsieur, I thank you,” answered Charles.“Thank him better than that, nephew. Monsieur is going to settle the affairs of the house of Guillaume Grandet.”“Is there any hope?” said Charles eagerly.“What!” exclaimed his uncle, with well-acted pride, “are you not my nephew? Your honor is ours. Is not your name Grandet?”Charles rose, seized Pere Grandet, kissed him, turned pale, and left the room. Eugenie looked at her father with admiration.“Well, good-by, des Grassins; it is all in your hands. Decoy those people as best you can; lead ’em by the nose.”The two diplomats shook hands. The old cooper accompanied the banker to the front door. After closing it, he came back and plunged into his armchair, saying to Nanon,—“Get me some black-currant ratafia.”Too excited to sit long, he got up, looked at the portrait of Monsieur de la Bertelliere, and began to sing, performing what Nanon called his dancing steps—“Dans les gardes francaisesJ’avais un bon papa.”Nanon, Madame Grandet, and Eugenie looked at each other in silence. The master’s hilarity always frightened them when it reached its climax. The evening soon passed. Pere Grandet chose to go to bed early, and when he went to bed everybody else was expected to do the same. Nanon, Charles, and Eugenie were as tired as the master. Madame Grandet lived always according to her husband’s will. During the two hours consecrated to digestion the cooper, more facetious than ever, uttered a number of his apothegms—one of which gives the measure of his mind. When he had drunk his ratafia he looked at his glass and said,—“You have no sooner put your lips to a glass than it is empty! Such is life. You can’t have and hold. Gold won’t circulate and stay in your purse. If it were not for that, life would be too fine.”He was jovial and benevolent. When Nanon came with her spinning-wheel, “You must be tired,” he said; “put away your hemp.”“Ah, bah! then I shall get sleepy,” she answered.“Poor Nanon! Will you have some ratafia?”“I won’t refuse a good offer; madame makes it a deal better than the apothecaries. What they sell is all drugs.”“They put too much sugar,” said the master; “you can’t taste anything else.”############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The following day the family met at eight for the early breakfast, a picture of domestic intimacy. Grief had brought Madame Grandet, Eugenie, and Charles into close accord; even Nanon sympathized without understanding why. The four now made one family. The old man, whose avarice and certainty that he would soon be rid of the dandy without paying more than his journey to Nantes made him nearly indifferent, left the two children, as he called Charles and Eugenie, free to behave as they pleased under Madame Grandet’s watch, in whom he had implicit confidence for public and religious morality. He busied himself with straightening field boundaries, tending poplar plantations by the Loire, winter vineyard work, and matters at Froidfond. These things occupied him.For Eugenie the springtime of love had come. Since the night she gave her little treasure to her cousin, her heart had followed it. Confederates in the same secret, they exchanged looks and a mutual intelligence that deepened their communion, putting them beyond ordinary life. Their near relationship seemed to license gentleness and tender glances. Eugenie took delight in lulling her cousin’s pain with the childish joys of new love. There are sweet parallels between the birth of love and the birth of life: one rocks the babe with songs and soft glances, tells tales of a golden future, and Hope spreads her wings above it. Childhood and love were one to Eugenie and Charles; it was a first passion, childlike play—the more caressing because wrapped in sadness. Struggling at birth against the gloom of mourning, their love matched the plainness of that gray, ruined house. As they exchanged few words beside the well, lingered in the garden at sunset on a mossy seat saying the infinite nothings of love, or mused between the house and the ramparts as under church arches, Charles learned the sanctity of love; his great lady Annette had taught him only its stormy troubles. He left worldly passion, coquettish and vain, for true, pure love. He loved even the house, whose customs no longer seemed ridiculous. He rose early to speak with Eugenie before her father came to dole out provisions; when Grandet’s steps sounded he escaped to the garden. The small criminality of these morning tête-à-têtes, which Nanon pretended not to see, gave their innocent love the charm of forbidden joy.After breakfast, when Grandet had gone to his fields, Charles remained with the mother and daughter, finding pleasure in holding their skeins, watching them work, and listening to their quiet prattle. The simplicity of this half-monastic life, revealing the beauty of these souls, touched him. He had believed such morals impossible in France, admitting their existence only in Germany; even so, they seemed fabulous, only real in La Fontaine novels. Soon Eugenie became to him a Margaret of Goethe—before her fall. Day by day his words and looks enraptured the poor girl, who yielded with delicious non-resistance to the current of love; she caught her happiness like a swimmer seizes an overhanging branch to draw himself ashore. Did dread of coming absence sadden those fleeting days? Daily some small circumstance reminded them of the impending parting.Three days after des Grassins’ departure, Grandet took his nephew to the civil courts with the solemnity country people attach to legal acts, that he might sign a deed surrendering his rights in his father’s estate. Terrible renunciation! Charles also went before Maitre Cruchot to make two powers of attorney—one for des Grassins, the other for the friend charged with selling his belongings. He attended formalities to obtain a passport; when he received mourning clothes from Paris he sent for the Saumur tailor and sold his useless wardrobe. This last act pleased Grandet exceedingly.“Ah! now you look like a man prepared to embark and make your fortune,” he said when Charles appeared in a plain black surtout. “Good! very good!”“I hope you will believe, monsieur,” answered his nephew, “that I shall always try to conform to my situation.”“What’s that?” said his uncle, eyes lighting on a handful of gold Charles carried.“Monsieur, I have collected all my buttons and rings and other superfluities which may have some value; not knowing anyone in Saumur, I wanted to ask you to—”“To buy them?” said Grandet, interrupting.“No, uncle; only to tell me of an honest man who—”“Give me those things; I will estimate their value,” said Grandet, taking the mass of gold.“Cousin,” said Grandet, “may I offer you these two buttons? They can fasten ribbons round your wrists; that sort of bracelet is fashionable now.”“I accept,” she answered, giving him an understanding look.“Aunt, here is my mother’s thimble; I have always kept it carefully,” said Charles, presenting a pretty gold thimble to Madame Grandet, who had longed for one.“I cannot thank you; no words are possible, my nephew,” said the poor mother, tears in her eyes. “Night and morning in my prayers I shall add one for you, the most earnest of all—for those who travel. If I die, Eugenie will keep this treasure for you.”“They are worth nine hundred and eighty-nine francs, seventy-five centimes,” said Grandet, opening the door. “To save you the pain of selling them, I will advance the money—in livres.”The word livres on the Loire littoral signified that crown prices of six livres were to be accepted as six francs without deduction.“I dared not propose it,” answered Charles; “but it was repugnant to me to sell my jewels to some second-hand dealer in your town. People should wash their dirty linen at home, as Napoleon said. I thank you for your kindness.”Grandet scratched his ear; silence followed.“My dear uncle,” resumed Charles, uneasy as if fearing to wound him, “my aunt and cousin have accepted a trifling remembrance of me. Will you allow me to give you these sleeve-buttons, now useless? They will remind you of a poor fellow who, far away, will always think of those who are henceforth all his family.”“My lad, my lad, you mustn’t rob yourself! Let me see, wife, what have you got?” he added, turning eagerly to her. “Ah! a gold thimble. And you, little girl? Diamond buttons? Yes, I’ll accept your present, nephew,” he answered, shaking Charles’s hand. “But—you must let me—pay—your—yes, your passage to the Indies. I wish to pay your passage because, d’ye see, my boy, in valuing your jewels I estimated only the weight of the gold; very likely the workmanship is worth something. So let us settle that I give you fifteen hundred francs—in livres; Cruchot will lend them to me. I haven’t got a copper farthing here—unless Perrotet, behindhand with his rent, should pay up. By the bye, I’ll go and see him.”He took his hat, put on his gloves, and went out.“Then you are really going?” said Eugenie to her cousin, with a sad look mingled with admiration.“I must,” he said, bowing his head.For days Charles’s bearing, manners, and speech took on the dignity of a man who, despite profound affliction, feels obligations and gathers courage from misfortune. He no longer repined; he became a man. Eugenie never augured better of his character than when she saw him in plain black that suited his pale face. That day the two women put on mourning, and all three assisted at a Requiem in the parish church for the late Guillaume Grandet.At the second breakfast Charles received letters from Paris and began to read them.“Well, cousin, are you satisfied with the management of your affairs?” said Eugenie in a low voice.“Never ask such questions, my daughter,” said Grandet. “What the devil! do I tell you my affairs? Why do you poke your nose into your cousin’s? Let the lad alone!”“Oh! I haven’t any secrets,” said Charles.“Ta, ta, ta, ta, nephew; you’ll soon find out that you must hold your tongue in business.”When the two lovers were alone in the garden, Charles drew Eugenie down on the old bench beneath the walnut-tree and said,—“I did right to trust Alphonse; he has done famously. He has managed my affairs with prudence and good faith. I now owe nothing in Paris. All my things have been sold; he took advice of an old sea-captain and spent three thousand francs on a commercial outfit of European curiosities sure to be in demand in the Indies. He has sent my trunks to Nantes, where a ship is loading for San Domingo. In five days, Eugenie, we must bid each other farewell—perhaps forever, at least for years. My outfit and ten thousand francs, which two of my friends send me, are a very small beginning. I cannot look to return for many years. My dear cousin, do not weigh your life in the scales with mine; I may perish; some good marriage may be offered to you—”“Do you love me?” she said.“Oh, yes! indeed, yes!” he answered, with depth that revealed equal feeling.“I shall wait, Charles—Good heavens! there is my father at his window,” she said, repulsing him who leaned forward to kiss her.She ran under the archway. Charles followed. At the foot of the staircase she opened the swing-door and, scarcely knowing where she was going, reached the corner near Nanon’s den, in the darkest end of the passage. There Charles caught her hand and drew her to his heart. Passing his arm about her waist, he made her lean upon him. Eugenie no longer resisted; she received and gave the purest, sweetest, most unreserved of kisses.“Dear Eugenie, a cousin is better than a brother, for he can marry you,” said Charles.“So be it!” cried Nanon, opening the door of her lair.The two lovers, alarmed, fled into the hall, where Eugenie took up her work and Charles began to read the litanies of the Virgin in Madame Grandet’s prayer-book.“Mercy!” cried Nanon, “now they’re saying their prayers.”As soon as Charles announced his immediate departure, Grandet bestirred himself to show interest. He became liberal of what cost him nothing; took pains to find a packer; declared the man asked too much for his cases; insisted on making them himself out of old planks; got up early to plane and nail strips into strong cases in which he packed all Charles’s effects; he also sent them by boat down the Loire, insured them, and got them to Nantes in time.After the kiss in the passage, hours fled for Eugenie with frightful rapidity. Sometimes she thought of following him. Those who have known that most endearing of passions—the one shortened by time, age, mortal illness, and human chances—will understand her tortures. She wept as she walked in the garden, now so narrow; indeed the court, the house, the town seemed narrow. She launched in thought upon the wide expanse of ocean he was about to traverse. At last the eve of his departure came. That morning, in the absence of Grandet and Nanon, the case containing the two portraits was solemnly installed in the only drawer of the old cabinet that could be locked, where the now empty velvet purse lay. This deposit was not made without many tears and kisses. When Eugenie placed the key within her bosom she had no courage to forbid the kiss with which Charles sealed the act.“It shall never leave that place, my friend,” she said.“Then my heart will be always there.”“Ah! Charles, it is not right,” she said, as though blaming him.“Are we not married?” he said. “I have thy promise—then take mine.”“Thine; I am thine forever!” they each said, repeating the words twice.No promise made upon this earth was ever purer. The innocent sincerity of Eugenie sanctified for a moment the young man’s love.On the morrow the breakfast was sad. Nanon herself, despite the gold-embroidered robe and Jeannette cross bestowed by Charles, had tears in her eyes.“The poor dear monsieur who is going on the seas—oh, may God guide him!”At half-past ten the whole family started to escort Charles to the diligence for Nantes. Nanon let loose the dog, locked the door, and insisted on carrying the young man’s carpet-bag. All the tradesmen in the tortuous old street were on their sills to watch the procession, which was joined in the market-place by Maitre Cruchot.“Eugenie, be sure you don’t cry,” said her mother.“Nephew,” said Grandet in the doorway of the inn from which the coach started, kissing Charles on both cheeks, “depart poor, return rich; you will find the honor of your father safe. I answer for that myself, I—Grandet; for it will only depend on you to—”“Ah! my uncle, you soften the bitterness of my departure. Is it not the best gift that you could make me?”Not understanding his uncle’s words which he had interrupted, Charles shed tears of gratitude upon the tanned cheeks of the old miser, while Eugenie pressed her cousin’s and father’s hands with all her strength. The notary smiled, admiring the sly speech of the old man, which he alone had understood. The family stood about the coach until it started; then as it disappeared on the bridge and its rumble grew fainter, Grandet said:“Good-by to you!”Happily no one but Maitre Cruchot heard the exclamation. Eugenie and her mother had gone to a corner of the quay from which they could still see the diligence and wave their white handkerchiefs, to which Charles answered by displaying his.“Ah! mother, would that I had the power of God for a single moment,” said Eugenie, when she could no longer see his handkerchief.Not to interrupt the current of events about the Grandet family, it is necessary to glance at the operations the goodman carried on in Paris through Monsieur des Grassins. A month after the latter’s departure from Saumur, Grandet became possessed of a certificate of a hundred thousand francs a year from his investment in the Funds, bought at eighty francs net. The particulars revealed at his death by the inventory of his property threw no light on the methods his suspicious nature used to remit the price and receive the certificate. Maitre Cruchot thought that Nanon, unknown to herself, was the trusty instrument by which the money was transported; about this time she was absent five days under pretext of putting things to rights at Froidfond—as if the goodman were capable of leaving anything out of order!In all that concerned Guillaume Grandet’s house the old cooper’s intentions were fulfilled. The Bank of France affords exact information about large fortunes. The names des Grassins and Felix Grandet of Saumur were well known there; they enjoyed the esteem of financial celebrities whose wealth comes from unencumbered territorial possessions. The Saumur banker’s arrival to honorably liquidate Guillaume Grandet of Paris averted the shame of protested notes. The seals on the property were taken off in presence of the creditors, and the notary employed by Grandet began the inventory. Des Grassins called a meeting of the creditors, who unanimously elected him, conjointly with Francois Keller, head of a rich banking-house and a principal interested party, as liquidators, with full power to protect family honor and claimants’ interests. The credit of Grandet of Saumur and the hopes he diffused through des Grassins facilitated the transactions. Not a single creditor proved recalcitrant; each said confidently, “Grandet of Saumur will pay.”Six months went by. Parisians redeemed the notes as they fell due and kept them locked. First result sought by the old cooper! Nine months after the preliminary meeting, the two liquidators distributed forty-seven per cent to each creditor on his claim. This amount was obtained by selling securities and property of the late Guillaume Grandet and was paid with scrupulous fidelity. Unimpeachable integrity was shown. The creditors acknowledged the remarkable honor displayed by the Grandets. When praises circulated, the creditors asked for the rest of their money. It became necessary to write a collective letter to Grandet of Saumur.“Here it comes!” said the old man as he threw the letter into the fire. “Patience, my good friends!”In answer, Grandet demanded that all vouchers for claims against his brother’s estate be deposited with a notary, together with acquittances for the forty-seven per cent already paid; he made this demand under pretense of sifting accounts and finding the estate’s exact condition. It roused difficulties. Generally, the creditor is a species of maniac, ready to agree one day, breathe fire the next, later grow amicable and easy-going. To-day his wife is good-humored, his baby cuts its first tooth, and he is determined not to lose a sou; on the morrow it rains and he is gloomy; he agrees to anything that ends the affair; on the third day he demands guarantees; by the month's end he wants his debtor’s head and feels like an executioner. Grandet had studied creditors’ atmospheric variations, and his brother’s creditors justified his calculations. Some were angry and refused to give in their vouchers.“Very good; so much the better,” said Grandet, rubbing his hands over des Grassins’ letter.Others agreed only if their rights were fully guaranteed; they renounced none and even reserved the power to compel failure. This began long correspondence, ending with Grandet of Saumur agreeing to all conditions. By this concession placable creditors were able to bring the dissatisfied to reason. The deposit was then made, but not without complaints.“Your goodman,” they said to des Grassins, “is tricking us.”Twenty-three months after Guillaume Grandet’s death many creditors, swept away by more pressing Paris business, had forgotten their claims or only thought to say: “I begin to believe that forty-seven per cent is all I shall ever get.”The old cooper had calculated on the power of time, which, as he used to say, is a pretty good devil. By the end of the third year des Grassins wrote that he had brought creditors to accept ten per cent on the two million four hundred thousand francs still due by the house of Grandet. Grandet answered that the notary and broker whose failures caused his brother’s death were still living, that they might have recovered their credit, and should be sued to recover something toward the deficit.By the end of the fourth year liabilities were definitively estimated at twelve hundred thousand francs. Many negotiations took place between creditors and liquidators, and between liquidators and Grandet. To make a long story short, anxious to get out of the affair, about the ninth month of the fourth year Grandet told the liquidators his nephew had made a fortune in the Indies and was disposed to pay his father’s debts in full; therefore he could not settle without consulting him; he had written and awaited an answer. The creditors were held in check until the middle of the fifth year by the words “payment in full,” which the wily miser put out from time to time, smiling and cursing, “Those Parisians!”But the creditors were reserved for an unprecedented fate in commerce. When events bring them back into notice, they will be found still in the position Grandet had resolved from the first.As soon as the Funds reached a hundred and fifteen, Pere Grandet sold out and withdrew two million four hundred thousand francs in gold, to which he added the six hundred thousand francs compound interest from the capital. Des Grassins lived in Paris. First he had been made a deputy; then he became infatuated (father of a family though he was, and bored by provincial Saumur) with a pretty actress at the Theatre de Madame, known as Florine, and relapsed into old army habits. His conduct was considered profoundly immoral in Saumur. His wife was fortunate in having her property settled on herself and in possessing ability to keep up the Saumur banking-house, which was managed in her name and repaired the breach caused by her husband’s extravagance. The Cruchotines made much talk about the quasi-widow’s false position; she married her daughter badly and gave up hope of an alliance between Eugenie Grandet and her son. Adolphe joined his father in Paris and became, it was said, worthless. The Cruchots triumphed.“Your husband hasn’t common sense,” said Grandet as he lent Madame des Grassins money on a note securely endorsed. “I am very sorry for you, for you are a good little woman.”“Ah, monsieur,” said the poor lady, “who could have believed that when he left Saumur to go to Paris on your business he was going to his ruin?”“Heaven is my witness, madame, that up to the last moment I did all I could to prevent him from going. Monsieur le president was most anxious to take his place; but he was determined to go, and now we all see why.”In this way Grandet made it plain he was under no obligation to des Grassins.In all situations women have more cause for suffering than men, and they suffer more. Man has strength and the power of exercising it; he acts, moves, thinks, occupies himself; he looks ahead, and sees consolation in the future. It was thus with Charles. But the woman stays at home; she is always face to face with the grief from which nothing distracts her; she goes down to the abyss which yawns before her, measures it, and often fills it with her tears and prayers. Thus did Eugenie. To feel, to love, to suffer, to devote herself—is not this the sum of woman’s life? Eugenie was to be in all things a woman, except in the one thing that consoles for all. Her happiness, picked up like nails scattered on a wall—to use Bossuet’s simile—would never so much as fill the hollow of her hand. Sorrows are never long in coming; for her they came soon. The day after Charles’s departure the house resumed its ordinary aspect in the eyes of all except Eugenie, to whom it grew suddenly empty. She wished, if it could be done unknown to her father, that Charles’s room be kept as he had left it. Madame Grandet and Nanon were willing accomplices in this statu quo.“Who knows but he may come back sooner than we think?” she said.“Ah, don’t I wish I could see him back!” answered Nanon. “I took to him! He was such a dear, sweet young man—pretty too, with his curly hair.” Eugenie looked at Nanon. “Holy Virgin! don’t look at me that way, mademoiselle; your eyes are like those of a lost soul.”From that day the beauty of Mademoiselle Grandet took a new character. The solemn thoughts of love which filled her soul, and the dignity of the woman beloved, gave her features an illumination such as painters render by a halo. Before her cousin, Eugenie might be compared to the Virgin before the conception; after he had gone, she was like the Virgin Mother—she had given birth to love.Returning from Mass the morning after Charles’s departure—having vowed to hear it daily—Eugenie bought a map of the world which she nailed beside her looking-glass, that she might follow her cousin westward, put herself day by day into the ship that bore him, and ask a thousand questions—“Art thou well? Dost thou suffer? Dost thou think of me when the star, whose beauty and usefulness thou hast taught me to know, shines upon thee?” In the mornings she sat pensive beneath the walnut-tree on the worm-eaten bench covered with gray lichens where they had said so many precious things, where they had built pretty castles of a future home. She thought of the future as she looked upward to the bit of sky the high walls allowed; then she turned her eyes to the angle where the sun crept, and to the roof above the room in which he had slept. Hers was the solitary, persistent love that glides into every thought and becomes the tissue of life. When Pere Grandet’s would-be friends came in the evening for their game at cards, she was gay and dissembling; but all the morning she talked of Charles with her mother and Nanon. Nanon had brought herself to pity her young mistress without failing in duty to the old master, and she would say to Eugenie,—“If I had a man for myself I’d—I'd follow him to hell; yes, I’d exterminate myself for him; but I’ve none. I shall die and never know what life is. Would you believe, mamz’elle, that old Cornoiller (a good fellow all the same) is always round my petticoats for the sake of my money—just like the rats who come smelling after the master’s cheese and paying court to you? I see it all; I’ve got a shrewd eye, though I am as big as a steeple. Well, mamz’elle, it pleases me, but it isn’t love.”############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Two months passed. Domestic life, once monotonous, quickened with the intense interest of a secret that bound the women together. For them Charles lived beneath the grim gray rafters of the hall. Night and morning Eugenie opened the dressing-case and gazed at her aunt’s portrait. One Sunday morning her mother discovered her absorbed in searching Charles’s features in his mother’s face. Madame Grandet was then for the first time drawn into the terrible secret: Charles had been given Eugenie’s treasure.“You gave him all!” cried the poor mother, terrified. “What will you say to your father on New Year’s Day when he asks to see your gold?”Eugenie’s eyes fixed, and the two women lived through mortal terror for more than half the morning. So troubled they missed high Mass and only went to the military service. In three days 1819 would end. In three days a terrible drama would begin — a bourgeois tragedy without poison or dagger, but, regarding the actors, more cruel than any fabled horror in the house of Atreus.“What will become of us?” said Madame Grandet, letting her knitting fall. For two months she had been anxious; the woollen sleeves she needed were unfinished. For want of those sleeves, a chill seized her amid a sweat caused by a terrible explosion of anger from her husband.“I thought, my poor child, if you had confided in me we could have written to Monsieur des Grassins in Paris. He might have sent us gold pieces like yours; though Grandet knows them all, perhaps—”“Where could we have got the money?”“I would have pledged my property. Besides, Monsieur des Grassins would have—”“It is too late,” said Eugenie in a broken voice. “Tomorrow morning we must go and wish him a happy New Year in his chamber.”“But, my daughter, why not consult the Cruchots?”“No, no; it would be delivering me up to them and putting ourselves in their power. Besides, I have chosen my course. I have done right and repent nothing. God will protect me. His will be done! Ah! mother, if you had read his letter, you would have thought only of him.”January 1, 1820 dawned. The winter of 1819–1820 was one of the coldest of the epoch; snow lay thick on the roofs. The fear of Grandet’s inspection suggested a natural excuse to escape the formal entrance into his chamber.Madame Grandet called when she heard him stirring. “Grandet, will you let Nanon light a fire here for me? The cold is so sharp I am freezing. Eugenie shall come and dress here; the poor child might get ill dressing in her cold room. Then we will go and wish you a happy New Year by the hall fire.”“Ta, ta, ta, ta, what a tongue! a pretty way to begin the new year, Madame Grandet!” he answered. After a pause he consented, with a gruff jest about the Bertellieres, coughed, and entered his wife’s room fully dressed. “Yes, it is cold enough to freeze you. We shall have a fine breakfast, wife. Des Grassins has sent me a truffled pate-de-foie-gras! I am going now to get it at the coach-office. There’ll be a double napoleon for Eugenie in the package,” he whispered. “I have no gold left, wife. I had a few stray pieces—but I had to let them go in business.”He kissed her on the forehead and went out. The two women breathed a little.Nanon entered to light the fire. “First he said, ‘Good-morning; happy New Year, you big fool! Go and light my wife’s fire, she’s cold’; and then he gave me a six-franc piece, which isn’t worn one bit. Oh, the kind man! He is a good man, that’s a fact. He’s getting soft, improving with time, like your ratafia! He’s a good, good man—”Grandet’s joy sprang from the complete success of his speculation.
