Monsieur des Grassins, after deducting what Grandet owed for discounting a hundred and fifty thousand francs in Dutch notes and for the surplus he had advanced to make up the sum required for an investment yielding a hundred thousand francs a year, had sent him thirty thousand francs in silver, the remainder of his first half-year’s interest, and informed him the Funds had risen. They were then quoted at eighty-nine; the shrewdest bought later at ninety-three. Grandet had thus gained twelve percent on his capital in two months; he had simplified accounts and would henceforth receive fifty thousand francs interest every six months without taxes or repair costs. He understood investing in public securities; at the end of five years he found himself master of six million francs capital, which increased without much effort and, joined to his land, made him enormously wealthy. The six francs for Nanon were perhaps the reward for some small service rendered unknowingly.“Oh! oh! where’s Pere Grandet going? He has been scurrying about since sunrise,” said the tradespeople as they opened shops. When they saw him returning from the wharf followed by a porter with a barrow laden with sacks, they commented on his gold. “Silver sous,” whispered the porter. “If you want me to take care of you, keep your tongue between your teeth,” Grandet warned as they reached the door. He handed twenty sous to someone and bade them be off. The sacks were carried to his inner room, where he shut himself in. “When breakfast is ready, knock on the wall,” he said, then disappeared.The family breakfasted at ten. “Your father will not ask to see your gold downstairs,” Madame Grandet hoped after Mass. “You must pretend to be very chilly. We may have time to replace the treasure before your fête-day.”Grandet came down thinking of his speculation and wondering how to metamorphose his Parisian silver into solid gold; he resolved to invest everything until the Funds reached par. Fatal reverie for Eugenie. As soon as he came in, the two women wished him a happy New Year—his daughter by putting her arms round his neck and caressing him; Madame Grandet gravely.“Ha! ha! my child,” he said, kissing both her cheeks. “I work for you, don’t you see? I think of your happiness. Must have money to be happy. Here! there’s a new napoleon for you. I sent to Paris for it. On my word of honor, it’s all the gold I have; you are the only one that has any gold. I want to see your gold, little one.”“Oh! it is too cold; let us have breakfast,” Eugenie answered.“Well, after breakfast, then; it will help digestion. That fat des Grassins sent me the pate. Eat as much as you like, my children, it costs nothing. Des Grassins is getting along very well. I am satisfied with him. The old fish is doing Charles a good service, and gratis too. He is making a very good settlement of that poor deceased Grandet’s business. Hoo! hoo!” he muttered with his mouth full, “how good it is! Eat, wife; that will feed you for at least two days.”“I am not hungry. I am very poorly; you know that.”“Ah, bah! you can stuff yourself as full as you please without danger, you’re a Bertelliere; they are all hearty. You are a bit yellow, that’s true; but I like yellow, myself.”The expectation of ignominious and public scrutiny is perhaps less horrible to a condemned criminal than the anticipation of what was coming after breakfast to Madame Grandet and Eugenie. The more gleefully the old man talked and ate, the more their hearts shrank. Eugenie, however, had an inward prop—she drew strength through love.“For him! for him!” she cried within her, “I would die a thousand deaths.”At this thought she shot a glance at her mother that flamed with courage.“Clear away,” Grandet said to Nanon about eleven, “but leave the table. We can spread your little treasure upon it,” he said, looking at Eugenie. “Little? Faith! no; it isn’t little. You possess, in actual value, five thousand nine hundred and fifty-nine francs and the forty I gave you just now. That makes six thousand francs, less one. Well, now see here, little one! I’ll give you that one franc to make up the round number. Hey! what are you listening for, Nanon? Mind your own business; go and do your work.”Nanon disappeared.“Now listen, Eugenie; you must give me back your gold. You won’t refuse your father, my little girl, hein?”The two women were dumb.“I have no gold myself. I had some, but it is all gone. I’ll give you in return six thousand francs in livres, and you are to put them just where I tell you. You mustn’t think anything more about your ‘dozen.’ When I marry you (which will be soon) I shall get you a husband who can give you the finest ‘dozen’ ever seen in the provinces. Now attend to me, little girl. There’s a fine chance for you; you can put your six thousand francs into government funds, and you will receive every six months nearly two hundred francs interest, without taxes, or repairs, or frost, or hail, or floods, or anything else to swallow up the money. Perhaps you don’t like to part with your gold, hey, my girl? Never mind, bring it to me all the same. I’ll get you some more like it—like those Dutch coins and the portugaises, the rupees of Mogul, and the genovines—I’ll give you some on your fête-days, and in three years you’ll have got back half your little treasure. What’s that you say? Look up, now. Come, go and get it, the precious metal. You ought to kiss me on the eyelids for telling you the secrets and the mysteries of the life and death of money. Yes, silver and gold live and swarm like men; they come, and go, and sweat, and multiply—”Eugenie rose; but after a few steps toward the door she turned abruptly, looked her father in the face, and said,—“I have not got my gold.”“You have not got your gold!” cried Grandet, starting up like a horse that hears a cannon.“No, I have not got it.”“You are mistaken, Eugenie.”“No.”“By the shears of my father!”Whenever the old man swore that oath the rafters trembled.“Holy Virgin! Madame is turning pale,” cried Nanon.“Grandet, your anger will kill me,” said the poor mother.“Ta, ta, ta, ta! nonsense; you never die in your family! Eugenie, what have you done with your gold?” he cried, rushing upon her.“Monsieur,” said the daughter, falling at Madame Grandet’s knees, “my mother is ill. Look at her; do not kill her.”Grandet, frightened by his wife’s pallor, was momentarily softened. “Nanon, help me to bed,” she murmured; “I am dying—” Nanon gave her an arm, Eugenie another; with difficulty they got her upstairs; she fell with exhaustion at every step. Grandet remained. In a few moments he went up six or eight stairs and called,—“Eugenie, when your mother is in bed, come down.”“Yes, father.”She soon came after reassuring her mother.“My daughter,” Grandet said, “you will now tell me what you have done with your gold.”“My father, if you make me presents of which I am not the sole mistress, take them back,” she answered coldly, picking up the napoleon from the chimney-piece and offering it to him.Grandet seized the coin and slipped it into his breeches pocket. “I shall certainly never give you anything again. Not so much as that!” he said, clicking his thumb-nail against a front tooth. “Do you dare despise your father? have you no confidence in him? Don’t you know what a father is? If he is nothing for you, he is nothing at all. Where is your gold?”“Father, I love and respect you, in spite of your anger; but I humbly ask you to remember I am twenty-three. You have told me I have attained my majority, and I do not forget it. I have used my money as I chose, and you may be sure it was put to a good use—”“What use?”“That is an inviolable secret,” she answered. “Have you no secrets?”“I am the head of the family; I have my own affairs.”“And this is mine.”“It must be something bad if you can’t tell it to your father, Mademoiselle Grandet.”“It is good, and I cannot tell it to my father.”“At least you can tell me when you parted with your gold?”Eugenie shook her head.“You had it on your birthday, hein?”She repeated the negative sign, crafty through love as he was through avarice.“Was there ever such obstinacy! It’s a theft,” cried Grandet, his voice rising through the house. “What! here, in my own home, under my very eyes, somebody has taken your gold—the only gold we have—and I’m not to know who has got it! Gold is a precious thing. Virtuous girls go wrong sometimes, and give—I don’t know what; they do it among the great people, and even among the bourgeoisie. But give their gold—for you have given it to some one, hein?”Eugenie was silent and impassive.“Was there ever such a daughter? Is it possible that I am your father? If you have invested it anywhere, you must have a receipt—”“Was I free—yes or no—to do as I would with my own? Was it not mine?”“You are a child.”“Of age.”Dumbfounded by his daughter’s logic, Grandet turned pale and stamped. When at last he found words, he cried: “Serpent! Cursed girl! Ah, deceitful creature! You know I love you, and you take advantage of it. She’d cut her father’s throat! Good God! you’ve given our fortune to that ne’er-do-well—that dandy with morocco boots! By the shears of my father! I can’t disinherit you, but I curse you—you and your cousin and your children! Nothing good will come of it! Do you hear? If it was to Charles—but, no; it’s impossible. What! has that wretched fellow robbed me?—”He looked at his daughter, who continued cold and silent.“She won’t stir; she won’t flinch! She’s more Grandet than I’m Grandet! Ha! you have not given your gold for nothing? Come, speak the truth!”Eugenie looked at her father with a sarcastic expression that stung him.“Eugenie, you are here, in my house—in your father’s house. If you wish to stay here, you must submit to me. The priests tell you to obey me.” Eugenie bowed. “You affront me in all I hold most dear. I will not see you again until you submit. Go to your chamber. You will stay there till I give you permission to leave it. Nanon will bring you bread and water. You hear me—go!”Eugenie burst into tears and fled to her mother. Grandet, after marching two or three times round the garden in the snow without heeding the cold, suspected his daughter had gone to her mother; only too happy to find her disobedient, he climbed the stairs with the agility of a cat and appeared in Madame Grandet’s room just as she was stroking Eugenie’s hair, the girl’s face hidden on her motherly bosom.“Be comforted, my poor child,” she was saying; “your father will get over it.”“She has no father!” said the old man. “Can it be you and I, Madame Grandet, who have given birth to such a disobedient child? A fine education—religious, too! Well! why are you not in your chamber? Come, to prison, to prison, mademoiselle!”“Would you deprive me of my daughter, monsieur?” said Madame Grandet, now red with fever.“If you want to keep her, carry her off! Clear out—out of my house, both of you! Thunder! where is the gold? what’s become of the gold?”Eugenie rose, looked proudly at her father, and withdrew to her room. Grandet turned the key.“Nanon,” he cried, “put out the fire in the hall.”Then he sat down in an armchair beside his wife’s fire and said to her,—“Undoubtedly she has given the gold to that miserable seducer, Charles, who only wanted our money.”“I knew nothing about it,” she answered, turning away from his savage glances. “I suffer so much from your violence that I shall never leave this room, if I trust my presentiments, till I am carried out in my coffin. You ought to have spared me this suffering, monsieur—you, to whom I have caused no pain, I think. Your daughter loves you. I believe her to be as innocent as an unborn babe. Do not make her wretched. Revoke your sentence. The cold is very severe; you may give her some serious illness.”“I will not see her, neither will I speak to her. She shall stay in her room, on bread and water, until she submits to her father. What the devil! shouldn’t a father know where the gold in his house has gone to? She owned the only rupees in France, perhaps, and the Dutch ducats and the genovines—”“Monsieur, Eugenie is our only child; and even if she had thrown them into the water—”“Into the water!” cried her husband; “into the water! You are crazy, Madame Grandet! What I have said is said; you know that well enough. If you want peace in this household, make your daughter confess, pump it out of her. Women understand how to do that better than we do. Whatever she has done, I sha’n’t eat her. Is she afraid of me? Even if she has plastered Charles with gold from head to foot, he is on the high seas, and nobody can get at him, hein!”“But, monsieur—” Excited by the crisis and by her daughter’s fate, Madame Grandet suddenly observed a frightful movement of her husband’s wen, and, in replying, she changed her speech without changing tone—“But, monsieur, I have not more influence over her than you have. She has said nothing to me; she takes after you.”“Tut, tut! Your tongue is hung in the middle this morning. Ta, ta, ta, ta! You are setting me at defiance, I do believe. I daresay you are in league with her.”He looked fixedly at his wife.“Monsieur Grandet, if you wish to kill me, you have only to go on like this. I tell you, monsieur—and if it were to cost me my life, I would say it—you do wrong by your daughter; she is more in the right than you are. That money belonged to her; she is incapable of making any but a good use of it, and God alone has the right to know our good deeds. Monsieur, I implore you, take Eugenie back into favor; forgive her. If you do this you will lessen the injury your anger has done me; perhaps you will save my life. My daughter! oh, monsieur, give me back my daughter!”“I shall decamp,” he said; “the house is not habitable. A mother and daughter talking and arguing like that! Broooouh! Pouah! A fine New Year’s present you’ve made me, Eugenie.” He lectured on Charles’s worth, his morocco boots and airs, and the ruin that would come.When the street-door was shut, Eugenie came out of her room and went to her mother.“What courage you have had for your daughter’s sake!” she said.“Ah! my child, see where forbidden things may lead us. You forced me to tell a lie.”“I will ask God to punish only me.”“Is it true,” cried Nanon, rushing in alarm, “that mademoiselle is to be kept on bread and water for the rest of her life?”“What does that signify, Nanon?” said Eugenie tranquilly.“Goodness! do you suppose I’ll eat frippe when the daughter of the house is eating dry bread? No, no!”“Don’t say a word about all this, Nanon,” Eugenie begged.“I’ll be as mute as a fish; but you’ll see!”Grandet dined alone for the first time in twenty-four years.“So you’re a widower, monsieur,” said Nanon; “it must be disagreeable to be a widower with two women in the house.”“I did not speak to you. Hold your jaw, or I’ll turn you off! What is that I hear boiling in your saucepan on the stove?”“It is grease I’m trying out.”“There will be some company tonight. Light the fire.”The Cruchots, Madame des Grassins, and her son arrived at the usual hour of eight, surprised to see neither Madame Grandet nor her daughter.“My wife is not very well, and Eugenie is with her,” said the old wine-grower, whose face betrayed no emotion.After an hour of idle conversation, Madame des Grassins, who had gone up to see Madame Grandet, came down, and all asked, “How is Madame Grandet?”“Not at all well,” she answered; “her condition seems really alarming. At her age you ought to take every precaution, Papa Grandet.”“We’ll see about it,” said the old man absently.They all wished him good-night. When the Cruchots reached the street, Madame des Grassins said, “There is something going on at the Grandets. The mother is very ill without her knowing it. The girl’s eyes are red, as if she had been crying all day. Can they be trying to marry her against her will?”When Grandet had gone to bed Nanon came softly to Eugenie’s room in her stockinged feet and showed her a pate baked in a saucepan.“See, mademoiselle,” said the good soul, “Cornoiller gave me a hare. You eat so little that this pate will last you a full week; in such frosty weather it won’t spoil. You sha’n’t live on dry bread, I’m determined; it isn’t wholesome.”“Poor Nanon!” said Eugenie, pressing her hand.“I’ve made it downright good and dainty, and he never found it out. I bought the lard and the spices out of my six francs: I’m the mistress of my own money”; and she disappeared rapidly, fancying she heard Grandet.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For several months the old wine-grower visited his wife’s room constantly but never mentioned or saw his daughter. Madame Grandet stayed in her chamber and daily grew worse. Nothing softened the old man; he remained harsh and cold as granite. He continued his business but spoke less, grew more obdurate in transactions, and often made mistakes in his accounts.“Something is going on at the Grandets,” said the Grassinists and the Cruchotines. “What has happened in the Grandet family?” became a fixed question at Saumur’s little evening-parties. Eugenie went to Mass escorted by Nanon. If Madame des Grassins spoke to her after church she answered evasively. After two months it could no longer be concealed that Eugenie was confined to her room. Pretexts failed; at last, by an unknown betrayer, the town learned that since New Year’s Day Mademoiselle Grandet had been kept in her room without fire, on bread and water, by her father’s orders, and that Nanon cooked little dainties and took them to her secretly at night. It was even known that Eugenie could not see or care for her mother except when her father was out.Grandet’s conduct was severely condemned. Remembering his treachery and hardness, the town more or less outlawed him. Passersby pointed and muttered. When his daughter went to Mass or Vespers, inhabitants ran to windows to study the bearing of the rich heiress, whose face showed angelic gentleness and melancholy. Her imprisonment and her father’s condemnation seemed nothing to her; she had her little bench, the garden, the corner of the wall. Pious and pure before God, her conscience and love helped her bear her father’s wrath patiently.Her chief grief was her mother’s failing health. Madame Grandet, gentle and tender, beautified by a religious serenity, was perishing. Eugenie often reproached herself as the innocent cause of the slow malady wasting her away; this remorse bound her closer to her love. Every morning, as soon as her father left, she went to her mother’s bedside; Nanon brought her breakfast. The poor girl, sad and suffering over her mother’s pain, would turn her face to the old servant with a mute, weeping gesture, yet not daring to speak of her cousin. Madame Grandet first found courage to say, “Where is he? Why does he not write?”“Let us think about him, mother, but not speak of him. You are ill—you, before all.”“All” meant “him.”“My child,” said Madame Grandet, “I do not wish to live. God protects me and enables me to look with joy to the end of my misery.”Every utterance of this woman was unfalteringly pious and Christian. During the first months of the year, when her husband came to breakfast and paced the room, she would speak a few religious words with angelic sweetness and the firmness of one who faces death courageously.“Monsieur, I thank you for the interest you take in my health,” she would answer his commonplace inquiries; “but if you really desire to render my last moments less bitter and ease my grief, take back your daughter: be a Christian, a husband, and a father.”Grandet would sit by the bed with the air of a man who sees rain coming and shelters himself till it is over. Absolute forgetfulness of his daughter seemed graven on his stony brow and closed lips. He was unmoved by the tears that flowed down his unhappy wife’s cheeks as she listened to his meaningless answers.“May God pardon you,” she said, “even as I pardon you! You will some day stand in need of mercy.”Since Madame Grandet’s illness he had not dared to use his terrible “Ta, ta, ta, ta!” Yet his despotic nature was not disarmed by this angel of gentleness, whose homely features were slowly transfigured by inward light. If his language ceased to be contemptuous, an imperturbable silence, which saved his dignity as master, took its place.When Nanon appeared in the market, many quips about the master whistled in her ears; but however loudly public opinion condemned Monsieur Grandet, the old servant defended him for the honor of the family.“Well!” she would say, “don’t we all get hard as we grow old? Why shouldn’t he? Stop telling lies. Mademoiselle lives like a queen. She’s alone, that’s true; but she likes it. Besides, my masters have good reasons.”At last, toward the end of spring, Madame Grandet, worn out by grief and unsuccessful in reconciling father and daughter, confided her troubles to the Cruchots.“Keep a girl of twenty-three on bread and water!” cried Monsieur de Bonfons. “That constitutes wrongful cruelty; she can contest—”“Come, nephew, spare us your legal jargon,” said the notary. “Set your mind at ease, madame; I will put a stop to such treatment to-morrow.”Eugenie, hearing herself mentioned, came out of her room.“Gentlemen,” she said proudly, “I beg you not to interfere. My father is master in his own house. As long as I live under his roof I must obey him. His conduct is not subject to the world’s approbation or disapprobation; he is accountable to God only. I appeal to your friendship to keep total silence in this affair. To blame my father is to attack our family honor. I thank you for your interest; you will do me an additional service if you put a stop to the offensive rumors current in the town.”“She is right,” said Madame Grandet.“Mademoiselle, the best way to stop such rumors is to procure your liberty,” answered the notary respectfully, struck by the beauty which seclusion, melancholy, and love had stamped upon her face.“Well, my daughter, let Monsieur Cruchot manage the matter if he is so sure of success. If you wish to see me happy for my few remaining days, you must, at any cost, be reconciled to your father.”On the morrow Grandet, following his habit since Eugenie’s confinement, took walks in the little garden at the hour when Eugenie brushed and arranged her hair. He hid behind the walnut trunk sometimes to watch her movements, hesitating between obstinacy and his natural desire to embrace his child. Sometimes he sat on the rotten bench where Charles and Eugenie had vowed eternal love; she, too, looked at her father secretly in the mirror. If he rose and continued his walk, she sat obligingly at the window and looked at the angle of the wall where pale flowers hung and Venus-hair grew.Maitre Cruchot came early and found the old wine-grower sitting on the little bench, his back against the division wall, watching his daughter.“What may you want, Maitre Cruchot?” he said.“I came to speak on business.”“Ah! have you brought some gold in exchange for my silver?”“No, not about money; it is about your daughter Eugenie. All the town is talking of her and you.”“What does the town meddle for? A man’s house is his castle.”“Very true; but your wife is very ill. You ought to consult Monsieur Bergerin; she may die. If she dies without proper care you will not be at ease.”“Ta, ta, ta, ta! these doctors, if they once get their foot in—”“You have the right to choose your course. Besides, there is another thing which may have serious results for you. After your wife’s death, your daughter can claim a division of property; you enjoy your wife’s estate only during her lifetime. At her death your daughter can force a partition, perhaps force you to sell Froidfond. In short, she is her mother’s heir.”These words fell like a thunderbolt on Grandet, who had not thought of legal division.“Therefore I advise you to treat her kindly,” added Cruchot.“But do you know what she has done, Cruchot?”“What?”“She has given away her gold!”“Well, wasn’t it hers?” said the notary.“They all tell me that!” cried the old man, letting his arms fall in despair. “Are you going—for a mere nothing—to put obstacles in the way of concessions which you will be obliged to ask from your daughter as soon as her mother dies?”“Do you call six thousand francs a mere nothing?” said Grandet. His agitation was tragic.“Hey! my old friend, do you know what the inventory of your wife’s property will cost, if Eugenie demands division? Two, three, four thousand francs, perhaps! The property might have to be sold at auction. Instead of that, if you are on good terms—”“By the shears of my father!” cried Grandet, turning pale and sitting down. “We will see about it, Cruchot.”After a silence full of anguish he said, “Life is very hard! It has many griefs! Cruchot, you would not deceive me? Swear to me upon your honor that all you’ve told me is legally true. Show me the law; I must see the law!”“My poor friend,” said the notary, “don’t I know my own business?”“Then it is true! I am robbed, betrayed, killed, destroyed by my own daughter!”“It is true that your daughter is her mother’s heir.”“Why do we have children? Ah! my wife, I love her! Luckily she’s sound and healthy; she’s a Bertelliere.”“She has not a month to live.”Grandet struck his forehead, came back, cast a dreadful look at Cruchot, and said, “What can be done?”“Eugenie can relinquish her claim to her mother’s property. Should she do this you would not disinherit her, I presume; but if you want such a settlement, you must not treat her harshly. What I am telling you is against my own interests. What do I live by, if it isn’t liquidations, inventories, conveyances, divisions of property?—”“We’ll see! Don’t let’s talk any more about it; it wrings my vitals. Have you received any gold?”“No; but I have a few old louis, a dozen or so, which you may have. My good friend, make it up with Eugenie. Don’t you know all Saumur is pelting you with stones?”“The scoundrels!”“Come, the Funds are at ninety-nine. Do be satisfied for once in your life.”“At ninety-nine! Are they, Cruchot?”“Yes.”“Hey, hey! Ninety-nine!” cried the old man, and, agitated, he went up to his wife’s room and said, “Come, mother, you may have your daughter to spend the day with you. I’m going to Froidfond. Enjoy yourselves, both of you. This is our wedding-day, wife. See! here are sixty francs for your altar at the Fete-Dieu; you’ve wanted one for a long time. Come, cheer up, enjoy yourself, and get well! Hurrah for happiness!”He threw ten silver pieces upon the bed, kissed his wife’s forehead, and crooned coaxing words.“How can you think of receiving the God of mercy in your house when you refuse to forgive your daughter?” she said with feeling.“Ta, ta, ta, ta!” said Grandet in a coaxing voice. “We’ll see about that.”“Merciful heaven! Eugenie,” cried the mother, flushing with joy, “come and kiss your father; he forgives you!”But the old man had disappeared. He was hurrying toward his vineyards, trying to arrange the confused ideas that assailed him. Grandet had entered his seventy-sixth year. During the last two years his avarice had increased; the sight and possession of gold had become a monomania. His despotic spirit had grown with his avarice, and to part with any control at his wife’s death seemed to him “against nature.” To declare his fortune to his daughter, to inventory his property for division—“it would be cutting my throat!” he cried aloud in a field where he pretended to examine a vine.He returned to Saumur in time for dinner, resolved to unbend to Eugenie and coax her, that he might die regally, holding the reins of his millions so long as he had breath. As he entered the house stealthily, Eugenie had brought a beautiful dressing-case from the oak cabinet and placed it on her mother’s bed. Mother and daughter, in Grandet’s absence, allowed themselves the pleasure of looking for a likeness to Charles in the portrait of his mother.“It is exactly his forehead and his mouth,” Eugenie was saying as the old man opened the door. At the look his glance cast upon the gold, Madame Grandet cried, “O God, have pity upon us!”The old man sprang upon the box like a famished tiger. “What’s this?” he said, snatching the treasure and carrying it to the window. “Gold, good gold! All gold—it weighs two pounds! Ha, ha! Charles gave you that for your money, did he? Hein! Why didn’t you tell me so? It was a good bargain, little one! Yes, you are my daughter, I see that—” Eugenie trembled. “This came from Charles, of course, didn’t it?”“Yes, father; it is not mine. It is a sacred trust.”“Ta, ta, ta, ta! He took your fortune, and now you can get it back.”“Father!”Grandet took his knife to pry out some of the gold; he placed the dressing-case on a chair. Eugenie sprang forward to recover it; but her father, watching her and the treasure, pushed her back so violently that she fell upon her mother’s bed.“Monsieur, monsieur!” cried the mother, lifting herself.Grandet had opened his knife and was about to apply it to the gold.“Father!” cried Eugenie, falling on her knees and clasping his hands, “father, in the name of all the saints and the Virgin! in the name of Christ who died upon the cross! in the name of your eternal salvation, father! for my life’s sake, father—do not touch that! It is neither yours nor mine. It is a trust placed in my hands by an unhappy relation: I must give it back to him uninjured!”“If it is a trust, why were you looking at it? To look is as bad as touching it.”“Father, don’t destroy it, or you will disgrace me! Father, do you hear?”“Oh, have pity!” said the mother.“Father!” cried Eugenie in so startling a voice that Nanon ran upstairs terrified. Eugenie sprang upon a knife that was close at hand.“Well, what now?” said Grandet coldly, with a callous smile.“Oh, you are killing me!” said the mother faintly.“Father, if your knife so much as cuts a fragment of that gold, I will stab myself with this one! You have already driven my mother to her death; you will now kill your child! Do as you choose! Wound for wound!”Grandet held his knife over the dressing-case and hesitated as he looked at his daughter.“Are you capable of doing it, Eugenie?” he said.“Yes, yes!” said the mother.“She’ll do it if she says so!” cried Nanon. “Be reasonable, monsieur, for once in your life.”The old man looked at the gold and then at his daughter alternately for an instant. Madame Grandet fainted.“There! don’t you see, monsieur, that madame is dying?” cried Nanon.“Come, come, my daughter, we won’t quarrel for a box! Here, take it!” he cried hastily, flinging the case upon the bed. “Nanon, go and fetch Monsieur Bergerin! Come, mother,” he added, kissing his wife’s hand, “it’s all over! There! we’ve made up—haven’t we, little one? No more dry bread; you shall have all you want—Ah, she opens her eyes! Well, mother, little mother, come! See, I’m kissing Eugenie! She loves her cousin, and she may marry him if she wants; she may keep his case. But don’t die, mother; live a long time yet, my poor wife! Come, try to move! Listen! you shall have the finest altar that ever was made in Saumur.”“Oh, how can you treat your wife and daughter so!” said Madame Grandet weakly.“I won’t do so again, never again,” cried her husband; “you shall see, my poor wife!” He went to his inner room and returned with a handful of louis, which he scattered on the bed. “Here, Eugenie! see, wife! all these are for you,” he said, fingering the coins. “Come, be happy, wife! feel better, get well; you sha’n’t want for anything, nor Eugenie either. Here’s a hundred louis d’or for her. You won’t give these away, will you, Eugenie, hein?”Madame Grandet and her daughter looked at each other in astonishment.“Take back your money, father; we ask for nothing but your affection.”“Well, well, that’s right!” he said, pocketing the coins; “let’s be good friends! We will all go down to dinner to-day, and we’ll play loto every evening for two sous. You shall both be happy. Hey, wife?”“Alas! I wish I could, if it would give you pleasure,” said the dying woman; “but I cannot rise.”“Poor mother,” said Grandet, “you don’t know how I love you! and you too, my daughter!” He took her in his arms and kissed her. “Oh, how good it is to kiss a daughter when we have been angry with her! There, mother, don’t you see it’s all over now? Go and put that away, Eugenie. Go, don’t be afraid! I shall never speak of it again, never!”Monsieur Bergerin, the celebrated doctor of Saumur, presently arrived. After an examination he told Grandet that his wife was very ill; but that perfect peace of mind, a generous diet, and great care might prolong her life until the autumn.“Will all that cost much?” said the old man. “Will she need medicines?”“Not much medicine, but a great deal of care,” answered the doctor, scarcely able to hide a smile.“Now, Monsieur Bergerin,” said Grandet, “you are a man of honor, are you not? I trust to you! Come and see my wife how and when you think necessary. Save my good wife! I love her,—don’t you see?—though I never talk about it; I keep things to myself. I’m full of trouble. Troubles began when my brother died; I have to spend enormous sums on his affairs in Paris. Why, I’m paying through my nose; there’s no end to it. Adieu, monsieur! If you can save my wife, save her. I’ll spare no expense, not even if it costs me a hundred or two hundred francs.”In spite of Grandet’s vows, his show of consideration, and the tender care Eugenie lavished, Madame Grandet rapidly grew weaker. Every day she visibly wasted, fragile as autumn foliage; the radiance of heaven seemed to shine through her. It was a death worthy of her life. In October, 1822, after angelic patience and love for her daughter, she passed away without a murmur. Lamb without spot, she went to heaven, regretting only the sweet companion of her cold and dreary life, for whom her last glance seemed to prophesy sorrows. She shrank from leaving her ewe-lamb, white as herself, alone in a selfish world that sought to strip her of her fleece and grasp her treasures.“My child,” she said as she expired, “there is no happiness except in heaven; you will know it some day.”############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The day after her mother’s death, Eugenie felt an intensified attachment to her childhood home, where every object—window, chair—brought tears. She had thought her father’s heart hard, yet after the funeral he gave her tender care: offering his arm at breakfast, looking kindly, brooding over her as if precious. Observers like Nanon and the Cruchot family mistook Pere Grandet’s attentiveness and tremors for age; only Maitre Cruchot, who knew Grandet’s secret, perceived the real motive.After dinner, with doors closed, Grandet spoke to Eugenie about settling estate matters. Cruchot explained that Grandet did not want the property divided, sold, or taxed through an inventory. He proposed that she renounce her legal rights to her mother’s estate—leaving her father life’s use and disposition of the undivided property—while he would guarantee her capital. Eugenie, scarcely understanding the law, asked where to sign. Grandet, agitated, softened the proposal: instead of a costly formal deed, she could renounce informally and trust him to provide, for which he offered a monthly hundred francs. She agreed.The notary warned she was disinheriting herself without guarantee, but Eugenie brushed this aside; Grandet kissed and embraced her, called her virtuous, and insisted Cruchot prepare the relinquishment deed for the court records. The next morning she signed the papers that completed her spoliation. Yet in the first year Grandet did not pay the promised hundred francs; when reminded, he fetched jewels taken from his nephew and flung them into her apron, sarcastically asking whether she wanted them for her twelve hundred francs. He promised more the next year, rubbing his hands at the prospect of speculating on her feelings.Although still robust, Grandet began instructing Eugenie in thrift. For two years he made her order meals in his presence, collect rents, and learn vineyard and farm yields. By the third year his practices became her habits; he entrusted her with household keys and installed her as mistress of the house.Five years passed in monotonous routine. Eugenie’s deep sadness showed, but she never complained or spoke of the love at the heart of her sorrow. Her only society was the Cruchots and a few friends they introduced; they taught her whist and visited nightly. In 1827, as Grandet’s infirmities increased, he further instructed her in estate management and told her to consult Maitre Cruchot in doubt.Late that year the eighty-two-year-old patriarch was seized by a paralysis that progressed rapidly. Dr. Bergerin gave him up. With Charles absent, Eugenie clung to the last living link of affection; for her, love had been life. She devoted herself to nursing the father whose mind weakened while his instinctive avarice remained acute. The dying man positioned his chair facing the secret-room door and demanded explanation for every noise, waking at rent days or when accounts were due. He made Eugenie open the inner room, watched while she placed bags of money into his receptacle, and fingered the key in his waistcoat as if it were a talisman.Maitre Cruchot, hoping the heiress might marry his nephew the president if Charles did not return, redoubled efforts: he took Grandet’s orders, ran errands to Froidfond, sold vintages, and converted everything into coin to be carried in sacks to the hiding-place. The old man’s last struggle was rolling up his coverings and ordering Nanon to lock them away “for fear they should be stolen.” So long as he could open his eyes his whole being fixed on the door behind which his treasures lay, asking, in panic, “Are they there? are they there?”Eugenie spread coins on a table before him; hours passed with him staring at the money like a child seeing it. When the priest came for the last sacraments, the crucifix and silver vessels stirred him; he moved his lips and made a terrifying gesture as if to seize the crucifix—a final effort that cost him his life. He called for Eugenie though he did not see her and, as he died, told her, “Take care of it all. You will render me an account yonder!”—a grim confirmation that Christianity still sanctified the miser’s sense of duty.Left alone in the gray house with only Nanon for solace, Eugenie found in the old servant the one person who loved her truly and could hear her. Nanon was more than a servant: a humble friend and comfort. From Maitre Cruchot she learned the full extent of her inheritance: an income of three hundred thousand francs from landed and personal property in the Saumur arrondissement; six million francs invested at three percent in the Funds (bought at sixty, then worth seventy-six francs); two million francs in gold coin; one hundred thousand francs in silver crown-pieces; and unpaid interest—amounting in all to seventeen million francs. Her immediate thought was simply: “Where is my cousin?”—the absent Charles.When Cruchot gave her the clear schedule of the inheritance, Eugenie sat with Nanon by the vacant fireplace, where every object was memory—from her mother’s chair to the glass Charles had used. “We are alone,” she said; Nanon replied that if she knew where Charles was she would go on foot to find him. Eugenie could only think of the ocean between them. As she wept with Nanon, word of her seventeen-million fortune spread from Nantes to Orleans.Among her first acts after the funeral, Eugenie settled an annuity of twelve hundred francs on Nanon, making the old woman comfortably off since she already possessed six hundred francs. Less than a month later Nanon married Antoine Cornoiller, appointed keeper of Mademoiselle Grandet’s estates. Madame Cornoiller, though fifty-nine, looked not more than forty: her conventual habits gave her a rosy, healthy appearance, and she seemed to laugh at age. On her marriage day she presented a content, vigorous figure envied in the neighborhood. Townspeople joked about her “fast colors,” admired Cornoiller’s good fortune, and complimented her as she went to church. Eugenie gave her three dozen silver forks and spoons as a wedding present.Cornoiller, devoted to his mistress, spoke of her with tears; Madame Cornoiller, appointed housekeeper to Mademoiselle Grandet, delighted in her new duties as much as in her marriage. She took charge of weekly accounts, locked provisions, and issued them daily; she ruled two servants—a cook and a maid who mended linen and made dresses. Cornoiller combined keeper and bailiff duties. The new household ran strictly on Monsieur Grandet’s usages, and the farmers noticed no change.By thirty, Eugenie had known none of life’s joys. Her pale, sad childhood beside a mother always wounded by life left her with remorse and regrets. Her first and only love—brief, intense, and thwarted—had cost her mother’s life and brought Eugenie only sorrow and fragile hopes. She felt the heart needed mutual exchange to live; without it, it withers. Wealth offered no consolation: she could not live except through love, religion, and faith in the future. Love explained eternity to her; her heart and the Gospel opened two worlds. She retreated inward, loving and believing herself beloved. For seven years her passion pervaded her life. Her real treasures were not her millions but Charles’s dressing-case, the portraits above her bed, the jewels recovered from her father and kept in a drawer, and her aunt’s thimble that her mother had used—symbols more precious than gold.In mourning, a match seemed unlikely. Eugenie’s genuine piety was known. The Cruchots, guided by the old abbé, contented themselves with surrounding the heiress, paying affectionate attentions, and cultivating influence. Each evening the hall filled with devoted Cruchotines who praised their mistress. She had her doctor in ordinary, a grand almoner, a chamberlain, a first lady of honor, a prime minister—and a chancellor eager to have much to say. If she had wished for a train-bearer, one would have been produced. She was treated as a queen, flattered by little minds hoping to worm themselves into favor. The nightly gatherings, which called her Mademoiselle de Froidfond, outdid each other in admiration. At first the praise embarrassed her; gradually she grew used to homage and, perhaps, to enjoying the incense laid at her feet.Monsieur de Bonfons became the hero of this little court: his wit, person, education, and amiability were praised perpetually. Visitors spoke of the Cruchots’ rising fortunes, of offers for practices and prospects of official appointments. Rumors circulated that the Cruchots together had an income of forty thousand francs, or that one would sell his practice if appointed juge de paix. Bonfons affected youth: though forty and withered, he dressed in the latest fashions, toyed with a bamboo cane, and avoided snuff at Eugenie’s house to charm her. He addressed her familiarly as “Our dear Eugenie.” Apart from more visitors, the change in the house was only that loto had given way to whist and Monsieur and Madame Grandet were absent. The pack still pursued Eugenie and her millions; the scent lay surer and the pursuit more united. If Charles had returned from the Indies he would have found the same people and the same interests. Madame des Grassins still tormented the Cruchots; Eugenie remained the central figure; Charles would still have been sovereign in her heart. Small things had changed: flowers the president once presented on her birthdays had become a daily institution; every evening he brought a huge bouquet that Madame Cornoiller displayed and then secretly threw away after visitors left.Early in spring Madame des Grassins tried to stir the Cruchot circle by urging Eugenie to consider the Marquis de Froidfond, arguing that marriage to him would restore the ruined family and secure a marquise’s title. She lauded peerage and rank until, misreading Eugenie’s disdain, she went about proclaiming that marriage to Monsieur Cruchot was not as certain as people thought. “Though Monsieur de Froidfond is fifty,” she argued, “he does not look older than Monsieur Cruchot. He is a widower with children, and he is a marquis; he will be peer of France—where will you find a better match?” She added that Pere Grandet’s investment in Froidfond had aimed to graft himself onto that stock.One night, as she prepared for bed, Eugenie asked Nanon, “How is it that in seven years he has never once written to me?”############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################While these events happened in Saumur, Charles was making his fortune in the Indies. His commercial outfit sold well; he realized six thousand dollars. Crossing the line cleared many cobwebs from his brain; he perceived that the surest way to fortune in the tropics, as in Europe, was to buy and sell men. He went to the African coast and bought Negroes, combining this traffic with other profitable merchandise. He plunged into the business tirelessly, driven by the desire to return to Paris with the prestige of a large fortune and the hope of regaining an even more brilliant position than the one he had lost.Jostling with men, traveling through many lands, and observing conflicting customs modified his ideas and made him skeptical. He ceased to have fixed principles, since what one country called crime another praised as virtue. In the perpetual struggle of selfish interests his heart grew cold and contracted. The blood of the Grandets did its work: Charles became hard and eager for prey. He sold Chinamen, Negroes, bird-nests, children, artists; practised usury on a large scale; his habit of defrauding custom-houses made him less scrupulous about others’ rights. He went to St. Thomas and bought, for a song, goods captured by pirates and took them to ports where he could sell them well. If Eugenie’s pure face had accompanied him on his first voyage, like an image of the Virgin on a mast, and if he attributed his first success to her prayers, later women—blacks, mulattoes, whites, Indian dancing-girls—orgies and adventures effaced recollection of his cousin, Saumur, the house, the bench, the stolen kiss. He remembered only the little garden behind the crumbling wall, where he had learned his fate; but he rejected his family. His uncle was an old dog who had filched his jewels; Eugenie had no place in his heart, though she remained in his accounts as a creditor for six thousand francs.Such conduct explains Charles Grandet’s silence. In the Indies, at St. Thomas, on the African coast, at Lisbon, and in the United States, the adventurer used the name Shepherd so as not to compromise his family. As Charles Shepherd he could be indefatigable, bold, grasping, and greedy, like a man resolved to snatch his fortune quibuscumque viis and get the villainy over with to live honestly thereafter.With such methods prosperity was rapid; and in 1827 Charles returned to Bordeaux on the brig Marie Caroline, belonging to a royalist house. He brought nineteen hundred thousand francs worth of gold-dust, from which he expected another seven or eight per cent at the Paris mint. On the brig he met Monsieur d’Aubrion, a gentleman-in-ordinary to Charles X., a worthy old man who had married a fashionable woman with a fortune from the West Indies. To meet Madame d’Aubrion’s extravagance he had gone to the Indies to sell property and was now returning with his family.Monsieur and Madame d’Aubrion, of the old southern family d’Aubrion de Buch, were reduced to about twenty thousand francs income and had an ugly daughter whom the mother was resolved to marry off without a dot—the family fortune barely sufficing for her own expenses in Paris. The mother almost despaired of placing such a girl, but she made the best of her. Mademoiselle d’Aubrion was long, spare, with a disdainful mouth and a nose too long, thick at the end, sallow normally and very red after a meal—an unpleasant feature in a pale, dull face. Madame d’Aubrion made her daughter appear distinguished: hygienic treatment kept the nose manageable, she taught the art of dressing, charming manners, melancholy glances that interest a man, and the coquettish peep of the foot beneath a petticoat at the moment the nose reddened. By full sleeves, deceptive pads, puffed dresses, and tight corsets she had achieved strange feminine developments worth showing as an example to other mothers.Charles became intimate with Madame d’Aubrion precisely because she sought his intimacy. Persons on the brig said the handsome Madame neglected no means to secure so rich a son-in-law. Landing at Bordeaux in June 1827, Monsieur, Madame, Mademoiselle d’Aubrion, and Charles lodged at the same hotel and set out together for Paris. The hotel d’Aubrion was hampered with mortgages; Charles was to free it. The mother told him she would gladly give the ground-floor to a son-in-law. Not sharing Monsieur d’Aubrion’s prejudices about nobility, she promised Charles that she would obtain a royal ordinance from Charles X. allowing him to take the d’Aubrion name and arms and, by buying the entailed estate for thirty-six thousand francs a year, to succeed to the titles of Captal de Buch and Marquis d’Aubrion. By uniting fortunes, living together, and profiting by sinecures, the two families might occupy the hotel d’Aubrion with an income exceeding a hundred thousand francs.“And when a man has a hundred thousand francs a year, a name, a family, and a position at court—for I will get you appointed gentleman-of-the-bedchamber—you can do what you like,” she told Charles. “You can become master of the rolls, prefect, secretary to an embassy, ambassador if you like. Charles X. is fond of d’Aubrion; they have known each other from childhood.”Intoxicated with ambition, Charles toyed with the hopes thus presented in confidences poured from heart to heart. Believing his father’s affairs settled by his uncle, he imagined himself anchored in the Faubourg Saint-Germain—reappearing as the Comte d’Aubrion, like the Dreux reappearing in Bréze. Dazzled by the Restoration’s prosperity, which had been tottering when he left France, fascinated by aristocratic splendor, his intoxication, begun on the brig, increased after he reached Paris, and he resolved to take the course his would-be mother-in-law indicated. His cousin counted for no more than a speck in this brilliant prospect; but he went to see Annette. A woman of the world, Annette advised him to marry and promised her support in his ambitious projects. In her heart she was delighted to fasten an ugly, uninteresting girl on Charles, whose West Indies life had made him very attractive: bronzed complexion, bold, decisive manners like a man used to sharp decisions, to rule, and to succeed. Charles breathed easier in Paris, conscious of his part to play.Des Grassins, hearing of his return, his approaching marriage, and his fortune, came to see him and inquired about the three hundred thousand francs still required to settle his father’s debts. He found Grandet conferring with a goldsmith about jewels for Mademoiselle d’Aubrion’s corbeille. Charles had brought magnificent diamonds; the setting with plate and jewelry for the new establishment amounted to over two hundred thousand francs. When des Grassins, who had already called several times, presented himself, Charles treated him with the impertinence of a young man of fashion conscious of having killed four men in as many duels in the Indies. Coldly, and without fully grasping the plea, Charles replied:“My father’s affairs are not mine. I am much obliged, monsieur, for the trouble you have been good enough to take—by which, however, I really cannot profit. I have not earned two millions by the sweat of my brow to fling them at the head of my father’s creditors.”“But suppose that your father’s estate were within a few days to be declared bankrupt?”“Monsieur, in a few days I shall be called the Comte d’Aubrion; you will understand, therefore, that what you threaten is of no consequence to me. Besides, you know as well as I do that when a man has an income of a hundred thousand francs his father has never failed.” So saying, he edged Monsieur des Grassins to the door.At the beginning of August that year, Eugenie was sitting on the little wooden bench where her cousin had sworn to love her eternally, where she usually breakfasted when the weather was fine. The poor girl was happy for the moment in the fresh summer air, letting memory recall the great and little events of her love and the catastrophes that followed. The sun had just reached the angle of the ruined wall full of chinks that no one, by the mistress’s caprice, was allowed to touch, though Cornoiller often told his wife it would fall and crush somebody one day. At that moment the postman knocked and gave a letter to Madame Cornoiller, who ran into the garden, crying:“Mademoiselle, a letter!” She gave it to her mistress, adding, “Is it the one you expected?”The words rang as loudly in Eugenie’s heart as they echoed from wall to wall of the court and garden.“Paris—from him—he has returned!”Eugenie turned pale and held the letter for a moment. She trembled and could not break the seal. La Grande Nanon stood before her, hands on her hips, her joy showing in the lines of her face.“Read it, mademoiselle!”“Ah, Nanon, why did he return to Paris? He went from Saumur.”“Read it, and you’ll find out.”Eugenie opened the letter with trembling fingers. A cheque on the house of “Madame des Grassins and Coret, of Saumur,” fluttered out. Nanon picked it up.My dear Cousin,—“No longer ‘Eugenie,’” she thought, and her heart quailed.You—“He once said ‘thou.’” She folded her arms and dared not read further; great tears gathered.“Is he dead?” asked Nanon.“If he were, he could not write,” said Eugenie.She read the whole letter, which ran as follows:My dear Cousin,—You will, I am sure, hear with pleasure of the success of my enterprise. You brought me luck; I have come back rich, and I have followed my uncle’s advice, whose death, together with my aunt’s, I have just learned from Monsieur des Grassins. The death of parents is in the course of nature, and we must succeed them. I trust you are by this time consoled. Nothing can resist time, as I know. Yes, my dear cousin, the day of illusions is, unfortunately, gone for me. How could it be otherwise? Traveling through many lands, I have reflected on life. I was a child when I went away—I have come back a man. Today I think of many I did not dream of then. You are free, my dear cousin, and I am free still. Nothing apparently hinders the realization of our early hopes; but my nature is too loyal to hide from you the situation in which I find myself. I have not forgotten our relations; I have always remembered, throughout my long wanderings, the little wooden seat—Eugenie rose as if sitting on live coals and went to the stone steps of the court.—the little wooden seat where we vowed to love each other forever, the passage, the gray hall, my attic chamber, and the night when, by your delicate kindness, you made my future easier. Yes, these recollections sustained my courage; I said in my heart that you were thinking of me at the hour we had agreed upon. Have you always looked at the clouds at nine o’clock? Yes, I am sure of it. I cannot betray so true a friendship—no, I must not deceive you. An alliance has been proposed to me which satisfies all my ideas of matrimony. Love in marriage is a delusion. My experience warns me that in marrying we are bound to obey social laws and meet the world’s conventional demands. Between you and me there are differences which might affect your future more than mine. I will not speak here of your customs, inclinations, education, nor your habits, none of which fit Parisian life or the future I have marked out. My intention is to keep my household stately, to receive much company—in short, to live in the world; and I think I remember that you love a quiet and tranquil life. I will be frank and make you judge my situation; you have the right to understand and judge it.I possess now an income of eighty thousand francs. This fortune enables me to marry into the Aubrion family, whose heiress, nineteen years old, brings me a title, a place as gentleman-of-the-bedchamber, and a brilliant position. I will admit, my dear cousin, that I do not love Mademoiselle d’Aubrion; but in marrying her I secure to my children a social rank whose advantages will become incalculable: monarchical principles are gaining favor. Thus my son, when he becomes Marquis d’Aubrion, having an entailed estate with a rental of forty thousand francs a year, can obtain any State position he chooses. We owe ourselves to our children.You see, my cousin, with what good faith I lay my heart, hopes, and fortune before you. Possibly, after seven years’ separation, you have yourself forgotten our youthful loves; but I have never forgotten your kindness or my own words. I remember all, even lightly uttered words—words by which a less conscientious man, with a heart less upright, would scarcely feel bound. In telling you the marriage I propose is solely one of convenience, that I still remember our childish love, am I not putting myself entirely in your hands and making you the mistress of my fate? Am I not telling you that if I must renounce my social ambitions, I shall willingly content myself with the pure and simple happiness of which you have shown me so sweet an image?“Tan, ta, ta—tan, ta, ti,” sang Charles Grandet to the air of Non più andrai, as he signed himself,—Your devoted cousin, Charles.“Thunder! that’s doing it handsomely!” he said, looking for the cheque; having found it, he added:P.S.—I enclose a cheque on the des Grassins bank for eight thousand francs to your order, payable in gold, which includes the capital and interest of the sum you were kind enough to lend me. I am expecting a case from Bordeaux which contains a few things which you must allow me to offer you as a mark of my unceasing gratitude. You can send my dressing-case by the diligence to the hotel d’Aubrion, rue Hillerin-Bertin.“By the diligence!” said Eugenie. “A thing for which I would have laid down my life!”Terrible and utter disaster! The ship had gone down, leaving not a spar, not a plank, on a vast ocean of hope. Some women, abandoned, will try to tear a lover from a rival, commit crimes, or rush to the ends of the earth. Others bow their heads and suffer in silence; they go their way dying, resigned, weeping, forgiving, praying, and recollecting until the last breath. This was Eugenie’s love after she read that dreadful letter. She raised her eyes to heaven, thinking of her dying mother’s last words, who, with the prescience of death, had looked into the future: Eugenie, remembering that prophetic life, measured her destiny. Nothing was left for her; she could only unfold her wings, stretch upward, and live in prayer until deliverance.“My mother was right,” she said, weeping. “Suffer—and die!”############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Eugenie came slowly in from the garden, avoiding the corridor. The memory of her cousin lingered in the dim hall and on the chimney-piece where her morning saucer and Sèvres sugar-bowl stood.This day was to be solemn and eventful. Nanon announced the parish curate, allied with the Cruchots and Monsieur de Bonfons’s interests. The old abbé had urged him to speak to Mademoiselle Grandet about the duty of marriage for a woman of her station. Eugenie thought he had come for the thousand francs she gave monthly to the poor and told Nanon to fetch them; the curate only smiled.“To-day, mademoiselle,” he said, “I have come to speak of a poor girl in whom Saumur takes an interest, who, through lack of charity to herself, neglects her Christian duties.”“Monsieur le curé, you have come at a time when I cannot think of my neighbor; I am filled with thoughts of myself. I am very unhappy; my only refuge is in the Church; her bosom is large enough to hold all human woe.”“Mademoiselle, in speaking of this young girl we shall speak of you. If you wish to insure your salvation you have two paths—either leave the world or obey its laws. Obey either your earthly destiny or your heavenly destiny.”“Yes, God has sent you to me; I will bid farewell to the world and live for God alone, in silence and seclusion.”“My daughter, think long before you take so violent a step. Marriage is life, the veil is death.”“Yes, death—a quick death!” she cried with dreadful eagerness.“Death? but you have obligations to society, mademoiselle. Are you not the mother of the poor who receive clothes and wood and work in summer? Your fortune is a loan to be returned; you accepted it as such. To bury yourself in a convent would be selfishness; to remain an old maid is to fail your duty. Can you manage your vast property alone? You may lose it, be involved in lawsuits. Believe your pastor: a husband is useful; you are bound to preserve what God has bestowed upon you. You love God too truly not to find salvation among his world, of which you are a noble ornament and to which you owe example.”At that moment Madame des Grassins arrived, driven by vengeance and despair.“Mademoiselle—Ah! here is monsieur le curé; I am silent. I came to speak on business; but I see you are conferring with—”“Madame,” said the curé, “I leave the field to you.”“Oh! monsieur le curé,” said Eugenie, “come back later; your support is necessary.”“Ah, yes, indeed, my poor child!” said Madame des Grassins.“What do you mean?” asked Eugenie and the curé together.“Don’t I know about your cousin’s return and his marriage with Mademoiselle d’Aubrion? A woman doesn’t carry her wits in her pocket.”Eugenie blushed then assumed the impassive countenance her father had been known for.“Well, madame,” she said ironically, “no doubt I carry my wits in my pocket, for I do not understand you. Speak, say what you mean, before monsieur le curé; you know he is my director.”“Read this,” Madame des Grassins said, handing a letter from her husband.Eugenie read:My dear Wife,—Charles Grandet has returned from the Indies and has been in Paris about a month—“A month!” thought Eugenie; her hand fell. She read on.I had to dance attendance before I was allowed to see the future Vicomte d’Aubrion. Though all Paris is talking of his marriage and the banns are published—“He wrote to me after that!” thought Eugenie. She did not cry out but felt contempt.The marriage, however, will not come off. The Marquis d’Aubrion will never give his daughter to the son of a bankrupt. I went to tell Grandet of the steps his uncle and I took in his father’s business, and the manoeuvres by which we had kept the creditors quiet until now. The insolent fellow had the face to say to me—that his father’s affairs were not his! A solicitor might claim thirty or forty thousand francs in fees, one per cent on the debts. But patience! there are twelve hundred thousand francs legitimately owing to the creditors, and I shall at once declare his father a bankrupt.I went into this business on the word of that old crocodile Grandet, and I have made promises in the name of his family. If Monsieur le vicomte d’Aubrion does not care for his honor, I care for mine. I shall explain my position to the creditors. Still, I have too much respect for Mademoiselle Eugenie (to whom under happier circumstances we once hoped to be allied) to act before you have spoken to her about it—Eugenie paused and coldly returned the letter without finishing it.“I thank you,” she said to Madame des Grassins.“Ah! you have the voice and manner of your deceased father,” Madame des Grassins replied.“Madame, you have eight thousand francs to pay us,” said Nanon, producing Charles’s cheque.“That’s true; come with me now, Madame Cornoiller.”“Monsieur le curé,” said Eugenie, with composure inspired by what she was about to say, “would it be a sin to remain a virgin after marriage?”“That is a case of conscience. If you wish to know what Sanchez says in ‘De Matrimonio,’ I shall tell you to-morrow.”The curé left. Eugenie withdrew to her father’s secret room and spent the day alone, not coming to dinner despite Nanon’s entreaties. She appeared in the evening when the usual company arrived. The news of Charles’s return and treachery had spread through the town. Expecting scrutiny, Eugenie allowed none of the cruel emotions that wrung her; she met mournful looks and melancholy speeches with a smile, hiding misery behind courtesy. Around nine the games ended and players rose to leave. At the moment the assembly stood, an unexpected event resounded through Saumur.“Stay, monsieur le président,” said Eugenie to Monsieur de Bonfons as she saw him take his cane.No one in that assembly was unmoved. The president turned pale and was forced to sit down.“The president gets the millions,” said Mademoiselle de Gribeaucourt.“It is plain enough; the president marries Mademoiselle Grandet,” cried Madame d’Orsonval.“All the trumps in one hand,” said the abbé.“A love game,” said the notary.Provincial conventions are rigid; such an infraction seemed a solemn promise.“Monsieur le président,” said Eugenie with some emotion when they were left alone, “I know what pleases you. Swear to leave me free during my whole life, to claim none of the rights which marriage gives you over me, and my hand is yours. Oh!” she added, seeing him about to kneel, “I have more to say. I must not deceive you. In my heart I cherish one inextinguishable feeling. Friendship is the only sentiment I can give to a husband. I wish neither to affront him nor to violate the laws of my heart. But you can possess my hand and my fortune only at the cost of doing me an inestimable service.”“I am ready for all things,” said the president.“Here are fifteen hundred thousand francs,” she said, drawing a certificate of a hundred Bank of France shares. “Go to Paris—not tomorrow, but instantly. Find Monsieur des Grassins, learn the names of my uncle’s creditors, call them together, pay them in full with interest at five per cent from the day the debt was incurred to the present. Obtain full legal receipts before a notary. You are a magistrate, and I can trust this to you. You are a man of honor; I will put faith in your word and meet the dangers of life under your name. Let us have mutual indulgence. We have known each other so long we are almost related; you would not wish to render me unhappy.”The president fell at her feet, heart wrung with joy.“I will be your slave!” he said.“When you obtain the receipts, monsieur,” she resumed coldly, “you will take them with the other papers to my cousin Grandet, and you will give him this letter. On your return I will keep my word.”The president understood her bitterness and hurried to obey, lest time effect a reconciliation.When he left, Eugenie fell back in her chair and burst into tears. All was over.The president took the mail-post and reached Paris the next evening. The morning after his arrival he went to see des Grassins; together they summoned the creditors to meet at the notary’s office, where the vouchers had been deposited. Not a single creditor failed to appear. In an exercise of justice they were all punctual. Monsieur de Bonfons, in the name of Mademoiselle Grandet, paid the amount of their claims with interest. Paying interest was remarkable in Parisian commerce. When the receipts were legally registered and des Grassins had received fifty thousand francs for his services, the president went to the hôtel d’Aubrion and found Charles entering after a serious quarrel with his prospective father-in-law. The old marquis had told him plainly he should not marry his daughter until all Guillaume Grandet’s creditors were paid in full.The president gave Charles the following letter:My Cousin,—Monsieur le président de Bonfons has undertaken to place in your hands the acquittance for all claims upon my uncle, also a receipt by which I acknowledge having received from you the sum total of those claims. I have heard of a possible failure, and I think that the son of a bankrupt may not be able to marry Mademoiselle d’Aubrion. Yes, my cousin, you judged rightly of my mind and of my manners. I have, it is true, no part in the world; I understand neither its calculations nor its customs; and I could not give you the pleasures that you seek in it. Be happy, according to the social conventions to which you have sacrificed our love. To make your happiness complete I can only offer you your father’s honor. Adieu! You will always have a faithful friend in your cousinEugenie.The president smiled at the exclamation the ambitious young man could not restrain on receiving the documents.“We shall announce our marriages at the same time,” remarked Monsieur de Bonfons.“Ah! you marry Eugenie? Well, I am delighted; she is a good girl. But,” added Charles, struck by a thought, “she must be rich?”“She had,” said the president with a mischievous smile, “about nineteen millions four days ago; but she has only seventeen millions to-day.”Charles looked thunderstruck.“Seventeen mil—”“Seventeen millions; yes, monsieur. We shall muster, Mademoiselle Grandet and I, an income of seven hundred and fifty thousand francs when we marry.”“My dear cousin,” Charles said, recovering some assurance, “we can push each other’s fortunes.”“Agreed,” said the president. “Here is also a little case I am charged to give into your own hands,” and he placed a leather dressing-case on the table.“Well, my dear friend,” said Madame d’Aubrion, entering without noticing the president, “don’t pay any attention to what poor Monsieur d’Aubrion has just said; the Duchesse de Chaulieu has turned his head. I repeat, nothing shall interfere with the marriage—”“Very good, madame. The three millions which my father owed were paid yesterday.”“In money?” she asked.“Yes, in full, capital and interest; and I am about to do honor to his memory—”“What folly!” exclaimed his mother-in-law. “Who is this?” she whispered to Grandet, perceiving the president.“My man of business,” he answered low.The marquise bowed superciliously to Monsieur de Bonfons.“We are pushing each other’s fortunes already,” said the president, taking up his hat. “Good-by, cousin.”“He is laughing at me, the old cockatoo! I’d like to put six inches of iron into him!” muttered Charles.The president was out of hearing. Three days later he returned to Saumur and announced his marriage with Eugenie. Six months after the marriage he was appointed councillor in the Cour royale at Angers, later president of the superior courts, and he hoped for election to the Chamber of Deputies, yearning even for a peerage.“The king will be his cousin, won’t he?” said la Grande Nanon as she listened to her mistress recount the honors.Nevertheless, Monsieur de Bonfons (having finally dropped his patronymic Cruchot) did not realize all ambitions. He died eight days after his election as deputy of Saumur. Providence, which sees all, no doubt punished him for his sordid calculations and the legal cleverness with which, accurate Cruchot, he had drawn up his marriage contract, in which husband and wife gave to each other, “in case they should have no children, their entire property of every kind, landed or otherwise, without exception or reservation, dispensing even with the formality of an inventory; provided that said omission shall not injure their heirs and assigns…” This clause explains the profound respect the president always showed for Madame de Bonfons’s wishes and solitude. Women cited him as considerate and delicate, pitied him, and even blamed Eugenie’s passion and grief with cruel but discreet insinuations.Eugenie, endowed with the delicate perception of a solitary soul trained to divination by suffering and meditation, knew well that the president desired her death to step into possession of their immense fortune, augmented by property from his uncle the notary and his uncle the abbé, recently dead. The poor solitary pitied him. Providence avenged her for his calculations and indifference; he had respected her hopeless passion because it was his surest safeguard. To give life to a child would have ruined his hopes—those hopes of selfishness and ambition he cherished.God thus flung piles of gold upon this prisoner to whom gold was indifferent. She longed for heaven, lived pious and good, succored the unfortunate in secret, and never wearied of charity. Madame de Bonfons became a widow at thirty-six. She remained beautiful in the manner of a woman near forty: face white, placid and calm; voice gentle; manners simple. She possessed the saintliness of one who never soiled her soul by contact with the world, yet retained the rigid bearing of an old maid and the petty habits of provincial life. Despite vast wealth she lived as the poor Eugenie had once lived. The fire in her Saumur house was never lighted until the day her father had allowed it in the hall, and it was put out according to the rules that governed her youth. She dressed as her mother had dressed. Her house, without sun, always in shadow, mirrored her life. She carefully accumulated income and might seem parsimonious but disarmed criticism by noble uses of her wealth. Pious and charitable institutions, a hospital for old age, Christian schools, and a richly endowed public library testified against charges of avarice. The churches of Saumur owed much to her. Madame de Bonfons (sometimes ironically called mademoiselle) inspired reverential respect: yet that noble heart, beating only with tender emotions, had always been subjected to calculations of human selfishness; money had cast its frigid influence upon that hallowed life and taught distrust of feelings to a woman who was all feeling.“I have none but you to love me,” she said to Nanon.Her hand staunched secret wounds in many families. She went to heaven attended by a train of benefactions. The grandeur of her soul redeemed the narrowness of her education and the petty habits of her early life.Such is the history of Eugenie Grandet, who is in the world but not of it; created to be supremely a wife and mother, she had neither husband nor children nor family. Lately there was talk of her marrying again. Saumur people spoke of the Marquis de Froidfond and of the family laying siege to the rich widow as once the Cruchots had beset the heiress. It was said that Nanon and Cornoiller favored the marquis; nothing could be more false. Neither la Grande Nanon nor Cornoiller had sufficient mind to understand the corruptions of the world.ADDENDUMThe following personages appear in other stories of the Human Comedy.Chaulieu, Eleonore, Duchesse de — Letters of Two BridesGrandet, Victor-Ange-Guillaume — The Firm of NucingenGrandet, Charles — The Firm of NucingenKeller, Francois — Domestic Peace; Scenes from a Courtesan’s Life; Ursule MirouetNathan, Madame Raoul — The Muse of the Department; Lost Illusions; A Distinguished Provincial at Paris; Scenes from a Courtesan’s Life; The Government Clerks;
