The Member for ArcisRoguin — Cesar Birotteau; Eugenie Grandet; A Bachelor’s Establishment; The Vendetta############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################################ MIDDLE OF THE STORY — CONTINUED############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################For souls given to effusiveness there is a delightful hour between day and night; twilight softens lights and reflections, favoring a dreamy mood married to the play of light and shade. The prevailing silence charms artists, who grow contemplative, step back from pictures they can no longer work on, and pass judgment, rapt by meanings that flash on the inner eye of genius. He who has never stood pensive by a friend in such a poetic hour can hardly grasp its soothingness. Favored by the clear-obscure, material skill used to produce illusion disappears. If the work is a picture, the figures seem to speak and walk; shade is shadow, light is day; flesh breathes, eyes move, blood flows, and fabrics change sheen. Imagination heightens every realistic detail and sees only the work’s beauties. At that hour illusion reigns; it wakens a mind’s night, peopled by dreams, and bears the soul aloft to a world of fancies where the artist forgets the real world—yesterday, tomorrow, miseries and goods alike.At this magic hour a young, talented painter who saw in art nothing but Art itself was perched on a step-ladder working on a large, almost finished painting. Criticising and admiring himself, floating on the current of his thoughts, he lost himself in a meditative mood that elevated and soothed the soul. His reverie lasted long. Night fell. Whether he meant to come down or made some misjudged movement believing himself on the floor—the cause escaped him—he fell, struck his head on a footstool, lost consciousness, and lay motionless for an unknown time.A sweet voice roused him. When he opened his eyes a bright light made him close them again; through the mist he heard two women whispering and felt two timid hands supporting his head. He soon recovered; by the light of an old-fashioned Argand lamp he saw the most charming face he had seen, a head that for an artist realized the ideal beauty he had formed for himself. Her features recalled the refined delicacy of Prudhon and the poetic sentiment of Girodet: fresh temples, regular eyebrows, pure outline, virginal innocence stamped on every feature. Her figure was slight and graceful. Her dress, simple and neat, revealed neither wealth nor penury.As he came to, the painter expressed admiration and fumbling thanks. A handkerchief pressed to his forehead; amid the studio smell he recognized the strong odor of ether used to revive him. He saw an old woman, looking like a marquise of the old school, who held the lamp and advised the young girl.“Monsieur,” said the younger woman in answer to a few questions the painter asked while still vague from the shock, “my mother and I heard the noise of your fall and fancied we heard a groan. The silence after the crash alarmed us, and we hurried up. Finding the key in the latch, we entered and found you lying motionless. My mother fetched what was needed to bathe your head and revive you. You have cut your forehead—there. Do you feel it?”“Yes, I do now,” he replied.“Oh, it will be nothing,” said the old mother. “Happily your head rested against this lay-figure.”“I feel infinitely better,” replied the painter. “I need nothing further but a hackney cab to take me home. The porter’s wife will go for one.”He tried to repeat his thanks, but the elder lady interrupted at every sentence: “Tomorrow, monsieur, pray be careful to put on leeches, or to be bled, and drink a few cups of something healing. A fall may be dangerous.”The young girl stole glances at the painter and the pictures. Her expression revealed perfect propriety; her curiosity seemed rather an absence of mind, and her eyes spoke the natural interest women feel in suffering. The two strangers seemed to forget the painter’s works in his mishap. When he reassured them they left, looking at him with anxious kindness free from insistence or familiarity, without indiscreet questions or invitations. Their noble and simple manners made a quiet impression on the painter; later, recalling details of the incident, he was greatly struck by them.On the landing the old lady gently observed, “Adelaide, you left the door open.”“That was to hear me fall,” said the painter with a grateful smile.“You came down just now, mother,” replied the young girl with a blush.“Would you like us to accompany you downstairs? The stairs are dark.”“No, thank you, madame; I am much better.”“Hold tightly by the rail.”The two women remained to light him, listening to his steps.To appreciate the interest this scene aroused, the painter had only a few days earlier established his studio in the attics of this house in the darker, muddier part of the Rue de Suresnes, almost opposite the Church of the Madeleine and near his rooms in the Rue des Champs-Elysees. Fame had recently made him one of the artists most dear to his country; he was beginning to feel free from want, and, as he put it, was enjoying his last privations. Instead of working in cheaper studios near the city gates he had spared himself the long walk and the loss of time now more valuable than before.No man inspired more interest than Hippolyte Schinner, though he did not lightly reveal the secrets of his life. He was the idol of a necessitous mother who had raised him amid severe privations. Mademoiselle Schinner, daughter of an Alsatian farmer, had never married. Her gentle soul had been cruelly crushed long ago by a rich man who showed no delicacy in his love affairs. The day she lost her illusions was an age of reflection, religious thought, and resignation. She refused the betrayer’s alms, renounced the world, and made a glory of her shame. She devoted herself to maternal love, sought in it her joys instead of social pleasures, worked, and saved a treasure for her son. In after years one day amply repaid her for those sacrifices.At the last exhibition her son received the Cross of the Legion of Honor. Newspapers hailed an unknown genius, artists acknowledged Schinner as a master, and dealers covered his canvases with gold. At twenty-five Hippolyte, to whom his mother had transmitted her woman’s soul, understood his standing. Eager to restore to his mother the pleasures society had denied her, he lived for her, hoping that fame and fortune would one day make her happy, rich, respected, and surrounded by people of mark. Schinner chose friends among the honorable and distinguished; fastidious in selection, he desired to raise still further a position his talent had placed high. His solitary work from boyhood had left him with beautiful beliefs that grace early days. His adolescent soul was not closed to the shy emotions that make a young man an apart being, full of poetry and virginal hopes, puerile to the worldly but deep for their singleness.He had gentle, polite manners that spoke to the soul and fascinated even those who did not understand them. His voice, from the heart, stirred noble sentiments and testified to his modesty by ingenuous tones. Those who saw him felt an attraction of moral nature they could not analyze; they might call it galvanism or attempt to express it in formulas.These details help explain why, in the absence of the porter whom he had sent to the end of the Rue de la Madeleine for a coach, Hippolyte did not question the porter’s wife closely about the two women who had tended him. He replied to her natural inquiries about his accident and the fourth-floor tenants but could not stop her chatter, led by lodge policy and gossip.“Ah,” she said, “they were, no doubt, Mademoiselle Leseigneur and her mother, who have lived here four years. We do not know exactly what these ladies do; in the morning, till noon, an old woman who is half deaf comes in to help them; in the evening, two or three old gentlemen with ribbon loops, like you, monsieur, come to see them and often stay very late. One comes in a carriage with servants; they say he has sixty thousand francs a year. However, they are very quiet tenants, as you are, monsieur; and economical! they live on nothing, and pay as soon as a letter is brought. It is queer, monsieur, the mother’s name is not the same as the daughter’s. Ah, but when they go for a walk in the Tuileries, mademoiselle is very smart, and she never goes out without being followed by a lot of young men; but she shuts the door in their face, and she is quite right. The proprietor would never allow—”The coach came; Hippolyte heard no more and went home. His mother dressed his wound and would not allow him to return to the studio the next day. After advice and treatments, Hippolyte remained at home three days. During this enforced idleness his fancy recalled, bit by bit, the scene of his fainting fit. The young girl’s profile returned clearly against the darkness of his inward vision; he saw the mother’s faded features again, felt Adelaide’s touch. He remembered some gesture whose grace memory enhanced; then an attitude, a tone of voice rising like objects from deep waters to the surface.When he could resume work he went early to his studio; really he hurried to visit his neighbors. When passion wakes, inexplicable pleasures are felt; he mounted the fourth-floor stairs slowly, and his heart throbbed as he neared the humble brown door of Mademoiselle Leseigneur. He fancied similarity between their positions, imagined misfortunes of birth akin to his own. He lingered at the studio to think of them, dined there, then about seven o’clock went to call.No painter of manners has sufficiently told the curious privacy of certain Parisian existences: fresh elegant toilettes and brilliant women whose homes betray doubtful comfort. If my picture is sometimes bold, blame the truth; the appearance of his neighbors’ rooms greatly influenced Hippolyte Schinner’s feelings and hopes.The house belonged to one of those proprietors who abhor repairs and decoration, who regard being a Paris homeowner as business. Between miser and usurer, such men are allergic to moving a cupboard or opening an air-hole; when chimneys fall they deprive themselves of theatre nights “on account of repairs.” Hippolyte, who had seen a comical scene about decorative repairs, was not surprised by dark greasy paint, oily stains, and other disagreeable woodwork signs. These stigmata of poverty are not without a sort of poetry to an artist.Mademoiselle Leseigneur opened the door. Recognizing the young artist she bowed and, with Parisian tact and pride, turned to shut a glass partition through which Hippolyte might have seen linen hung over patent ironing stoves, an old camp-bed, charcoal and irons, a filter, household crockery—utensils of a small household. Muslin curtains screened this capharnaum, lighted by windows facing a neighboring yard.With an artist’s quick eye Hippolyte saw the uses, furniture, and general condition of this cut-in-half first room. The more honorable half, serving as ante-room and dining-room, was hung with old salmon-rose paper with a flock border patched with wafers. Prints of the battles of Alexander, in gilding-rubbed frames, were arranged on the walls. In the middle stood a massive, old-fashioned mahogany table worn at the edges. A small stove with a thin pipe stood before the chimney, whose hearth was blocked by a cupboard. Chairs showed remains of former splendor—carved mahogany, red morocco seats, gilt nails—scarred like a veteran.This room was a museum of objects that show both luxury and penury: a splendidly finished telescope hung over a discolored glass on the chimney. Between chimney and partition was a wretched painted sideboard pretending to be mahogany. Slippery red quarries, shabby rugs, and all the furniture shone with hard cleanliness that accentuated oldness. An indescribable odor mixed exhalations from the lumber-room, dining-room vapors, and stair smells, though the window was partly open. Street air fluttered dusty curtains, carefully drawn to hide a window bay where former tenants left ornamental additions—a sort of domestic fresco.Adelaide hastened to open the inner room door and announced the painter with obvious pleasure. Hippolyte, who had earlier seen similar poverty in his mother’s home, noted these signs with an impression vivid as earliest memories and entered with sympathy rather than scorn.“Well, monsieur, I hope you no longer feel the effects of your fall,” said the old lady, rising from an antique armchair and offering a seat.“No, madame. I have come to thank you for the kind care you gave me, and above all mademoiselle, who heard me fall.”As he spoke—words stamped with the exquisite awkwardness of first love—Hippolyte looked at the young girl. Adelaide lit the Argand lamp, replacing a tallow candle in a large copper candlestick, then placed the lamp on the chimney shelf and sat a little behind the painter to look at him at her ease while appearing interested in the lamp. The flame sputtered in the damp chimney. Seeing the large mirror on the chimney-piece, Hippolyte fixed his eyes on it to admire Adelaide; her stratagem only embarrassed them both.While talking with Madame Leseigneur—Hippolyte called her so—he examined the room unobtrusively. Egyptian figures on iron fire-dogs were scarcely visible under cinders; two brands struggled before a sham log. An old Aubusson carpet, faded and mended, left the tiled floor cold. Walls were hung with reddish paper imitating figured silk. Opposite the windows he saw a crack and the paper outline of double doors closing a recess where Madame Leseigneur likely slept, poorly disguised by a sofa. Above a mahogany chest of drawers hung a portrait of an officer of rank, so dim he could not judge it well; from what he saw it must have been painted in China. Window-curtains of red silk were as faded as the furniture in red-and-yellow worsted work. On the chest’s marble top was a costly malachite tray with a dozen magnificently painted coffee cups, perhaps Sevres. On the chimney shelf stood an Empire clock—a warrior driving four horses on a chariot whose wheel bore the hours. Tall candlesticks held smoke-yellowed tapers; porcelain vases crowned with dusty artificial flowers stood in the corners.In the middle of the room a card-table with new packs suggested a painful dilemma: either these two women were perfectly honest or they lived by intrigue and gambling. Yet in looking at Adelaide, so pure-minded, Schinner could not but believe in her innocence and attribute the furniture’s incoherence to honorable causes.“My dear,” said the old lady to the young one, “I am cold; make a little fire, and give me my shawl.”Adelaide fetched a cashmere shawl—once costly, now faded and darned—and wrapped her mother in it. She then brought a bundle of small wood from the lumber-room and gallantly threw it on the fire to revive it.It is hard to reproduce the ensuing conversation. Guided by the tact born of early hardship, Hippolyte did not remark on his neighbors’ poverty; any direct question would be indiscreet. The simplest pity can offend, so his thoughts and words diverged. The two ladies began by talking of painting; women easily guess the embarrassment of a first call and supply devices to end it. Questioning him about his art emboldened him to talk, and the chat’s light nothings led Hippolyte to reflections that revealed his habits and mind. Trouble had prematurely faded the old lady’s face; only prominent features remained, an outline suggesting shrewdness with grace in the eyes—an expression of the old Court that can hint either at artifice or refined delicacy.A woman’s face hides mystery: the line between frankness and duplicity, intrigue and heart, is inscrutable. A penetrating eye might discern differences; such appreciation lies in intuition alone. The old lady’s face, like her room, made it hard to tell whether the squalor covered vice or highest virtue. But at Schinner’s age the heart’s first impulse is to believe in goodness. As he studied Adelaide’s noble, almost haughty brow and soulful eyes, he breathed the modest fragrance of virtue. He seized an opportunity to discuss portraits and thus examine the faded pastel whose chalk had fallen away.“You are attached to that picture for the sake of the likeness, no doubt, mesdames, for the drawing is dreadful?” he said, looking at Adelaide.“It was done at Calcutta, in great haste,” replied the mother, her voice agitated.She gazed at the formless sketch with the absorption memories of happiness bring; in her face were traces of perennial regret. The painter interpreted her attitude as the mark of a life once happy. He then sat down beside her.“Madame,” he said, “in a very short time the colors of that pastel will disappear. The portrait will only survive in your memory. Will you allow me to reproduce the likeness on canvas? It will be more permanently recorded. Grant me, as a neighborly favor, the pleasure of doing you this service. There are times when an artist is glad of a respite from greater undertakings by doing work of less lofty pretension; it will be a recreation for me.”The old lady flushed at the offer, and Adelaide shot a glance of deep feeling. Hippolyte wanted some tie to his neighbors, to gain a right to mingle in their life. His offer appealed to the heart’s affections, gratified his pride as an artist, and could not wound the ladies. Madame Leseigneur accepted, without eagerness or reluctance, with the self-possession of a noble soul, aware of the character of bonds formed by such an obligation.“I fancy,” said the painter, “that the uniform is that of a naval officer.”“Yes,” she said, “that of a captain in command. Monsieur de Rouville—my husband—died at Batavia from a wound received in a fight with an English ship off the Asiatic coast. He commanded a frigate of fifty-six guns; the Revenge carried ninety-six. The struggle was unequal, but he defended his ship bravely, held out till nightfall and got away. When I returned to France Bonaparte was not yet in power, and I was refused a pension. When I applied again, quite lately, I was sternly informed that if the Baron de Rouville had emigrated I should not have lost him; that by this time he would have been a rear-admiral; finally, his Excellency quoted degrees of forfeiture. I took this step, urged by friends, only for Adelaide’s sake. I have always hated holding out my hand as a beggar in the name of a grief that deprives a woman of voice and strength. I do not like this money valuation for blood irreparably spilt—”“Dear mother, this subject always does you harm.”The Baronne Leseigneur bowed and was silent.“Monsieur,” said the young girl, “I had supposed a painter’s work was generally fairly quiet?”Schinner colored, remembering the noise he had made. Adelaide rose at the sound of a carriage stopping at the door. She went into her own room and returned with a pair of tall gilt candlesticks with partly burnt wax candles, which she quickly lit. Without waiting for the bell she opened the outer door where she set the lamp down. The sound of a kiss given and received touched Hippolyte’s heart. His impatience to see the man who treated Adelaide familiarly was not immediately gratified; the newcomers conversed in undertone with the young girl.At last Mademoiselle de Rouville returned, followed by two men whose costumes and countenances need a long tale. The first, about sixty, wore one of those coats fashioned for Louis XVIII.: neither civil nor military, it might pass for either. Fleurs-de-lis embroidered the lapels and gilt buttons bore the same. Shoulder straps begged for useless epaulettes; a buttonhole blossomed with many colored ribbons. He probably always carried his three-cornered hat with a gold cord, for his powdered hair showed no traces of pressure. He might be taken for fifty and seemed robust. His frank, loyal emigre expression hinted also at libertine habits, the reckless gallantry of the Musketeers. His gestures and manner showed no intention of renouncing royalism, religion, or love affairs.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A striking figure stood behind the portrait of “Louis XIV’s light infantry”—the Bonapartists’ nickname for these old Monarchy survivors. He ought to have been the picture’s principal subject, yet he was merely an accessory: a lean, dry man dressed like the first but seeming his reflection—an older coat, less white powder, dimmer fleurs-de-lis, more hopeless shoulder-straps; his intellect feebler, his life nearer its end. He was, as Rivarol quipped about Champcenetz, “the moonlight” of the other. The painter found this speechless Chevalier mysterious. He spoke to no one and was spoken to by none. Friend, relation, servant, protector—his role was obscure. At the Baronne de Rouville’s he provoked curiosity without satisfying it. Who, after the Restoration, could recall an attachment that before the Revolution had bound him to the dead woman who had been his friend’s wife?The leader of the group, least dilapidated, gallantly kissed Madame de Rouville’s hand and sat by her. The Chevalier bowed and stood a couple of chairs away. Adélaïde stood behind the old gentleman’s armchair, leaning her elbows on the back, unconsciously recalling Guérin’s Dido’s sister.Though the old gentleman’s familiarity was fatherly, it seemed to annoy the girl. “What, are you sulky with me?” he asked, then shot Schinner a shrewd, diplomatic glance that asked, “Is he one of us?”“This is our neighbor,” said the Baronne, indicating Hippolyte. “Monsieur is a celebrated painter; despite your indifference to the arts, you must have heard his name.”The old man saw the suppressed name and bowed. “Certainly. I heard much about his pictures at the last Salon. Talent has immense privileges,” he added, noting the artist’s red ribbon. “That distinction, which we must earn at the cost of blood and long service, you win in youth; but all glory is of the same kind,” he said, touching his Cross of Saint-Louis.Hippolyte murmured thanks and fell silent, content to admire Adélaïde. Her face gleamed in his mind; he forgot the extreme misery of the room. He answered briefly, inwardly divided between what he heard and what he felt—hope pouring out wild visions of happiness. He refused to analyze the evening’s small incidents.After a while, the Baroness and daughter began to play cards with the old gentleman. The Chevalier, faithful to his shadow role, stood behind, watching the game and responding with nods and repeated gestures. The gentleman complained of bad luck; Adélaïde sweetly offered advice. At last, the game ended. The old man threw two louis on the table in temper: “Forty francs—the exact sum. Deuce take it! It is eleven o’clock.”“It is eleven o’clock,” repeated the silent figure, looking at the painter.Hearing this, Hippolyte thought it time to go. He found a few commonplace remarks, took leave, and left wholly possessed by the first raptures of true love, without analyzing the evening.The next day he longed to see Adélaïde again. He might have come at six but waited until the afternoon. Blushing, he asked Mademoiselle Leseigneur for the Baron’s portrait. “But come in,” Adélaïde said; she had heard him. Tongue-tied, he felt happiness dull his wit. To see Adélaïde, to hear her skirt’s rustle after longing all morning, made trifles precious. The old lady was out. Adélaïde brought a chair to reach the picture; finding she could not reach without stepping on a chest, she blushed and asked, “Will you get it down?”He read her modesty, returned a look of mutual intelligence—love’s sweetest language. Adélaïde cast down her eyes. Hippolyte, almost timid, took down the picture, examined it, and left saying only, “I will return it soon.” The brief contact set long ripples in both hearts.Back in his studio he prepared a fresh canvas and, with ardor, worked until dinner. That evening he returned, stayed from nine till eleven, and, a little bolder, chatted with Adélaïde. A week passed and their feelings slowly deepened into mutual understanding. Each day Adélaïde’s greeting grew more intimate; they laughed and talked with the innocence of newly made friends. Schinner learned piquet poorly and lost often. Without a word of love they knew they were everything to each other: Hippolyte enjoyed his power over his gentle friend; Adélaïde, timid and devoted, permitted many concessions. Familiarity with the old Count waned: Adélaïde perceived the painter’s melancholy when the old man unceremoniously kissed her hand or throat.Mademoiselle Leseigneur began to expect accounts of the painter’s visits and scolded him for absences; he ceased other friendships and lived chiefly in the small apartment that enchanted him. Adélaïde’s jealousy flared when she heard he still visited Paris salons at eleven; she declared such life bad for his health and argued that a man who spent charms on several women could not love warmly. Driven by passion and Adélaïde’s demands, the painter lived almost exclusively with them.Never was love purer. Both were trusting and delicate; their passion grew without sacrifice. Two months after meeting, their lives were nearly one: Adélaïde could tell by his footsteps that he was home; he always looked in when he returned to his mother and flew back at evening. He thought the ideal she dreamed of was realized.The old gentleman came less often; Hippolyte, once jealous, had taken his place at the green table and shared his constant ill-luck. Yet sometimes, in happiness, Madame de Rouville’s desperate poverty haunted him. “Strange—twenty francs every evening?” he wondered, and dared not vocalize his odious suspicions.He spent two months on Adélaïde’s portrait; when finished, varnished, and framed, he considered it one of his best. Madame de Rouville had not mentioned it again. Was it indifference or pride? He would not analyze her silence. Joyfully plotting with Adélaïde, he planned to hang it while the Baroness was out. One day, while her mother walked in the Tuileries, Adélaïde came to his studio to see the portrait in its light. She stood speechless in rapt admiration. He leaned forward uneasily; she held out her hand, two tears fell, and he kissed it. They stood mute, longing to confess love but not daring. Overcome, they went downstairs and hung the picture. Hippolyte dined with the Baroness, who, in tears, embraced him.That evening the old émigré, the Baron de Rouville’s comrade, visited to announce a promotion to vice-admiral; his land travels through Germany and Russia had been counted as naval campaigns. Seeing the portrait, he shook Hippolyte’s hand and exclaimed he would give five hundred pistoles to see himself as well depicted. The Baroness, grateful, looked at Hippolyte. He suspected the admiral meant to buy both portraits; his pride and jealousy were offended, and he replied sharply that if he were a portrait-painter he would not have done this one. The admiral bit his lip and sat down to cards.Hippolyte stayed near Adélaïde, who proposed a dozen hands of piquet. As he played he noticed an unexpected eagerness in Madame de Rouville to win and finger the old man’s gold. Evil suspicions troubled him: could the Baroness live by gambling? Was she losing to pay debt or rent? Why would so rich a man frequent a fourth-story room where he lost some forty francs each evening? Why had he been familiar with Adélaïde and then given up any claim?These reflections led him to watch the old man and the Baroness more closely. Their sidelong glances at Adélaïde displeased him. “Am I being duped?” became his last, horrible thought. He resolved to stay after the two old men left to confirm or dispel his fears. He drew out his purse to pay and, carried away by thought, laid it on the table and fell into reverie; then, ashamed, he rose, answered a commonplace question, and went close to the Baroness to look at her as she talked.He stepped out, racked by doubts. A few steps down, he turned back to fetch the forgotten purse. “I left my purse here!” he cried to Adélaïde.“No,” she answered, reddening.“I thought it was there,” he said, pointing to the card-table. Not finding it, shamed for Adélaïde and the Baroness, he felt in his waistcoat and said, “I must have made a mistake.”The purse held fifteen louis d’or and change. The theft seemed flagrant and was denied; Hippolyte no longer doubted his neighbors’ morals. He stood on the stairs, legs shaking, dizzy, unable to walk, struggling with his destroyed hopes. He recalled trifling observations that now confirmed his suspicions. Had they waited until the portrait was given them? Perhaps the Baroness had used gambling to hide her need. He even remembered Adélaïde’s curiosity about a worn silk netting—had she been watching his purse? He briefly fantasized other motives—maybe the admiral hoped to marry Adélaïde—but rejected them: if she intended to marry him, they would not have robbed Hippolyte.Clinging to illusion, he told himself the purse must have fallen, then searched his memory and saw it distinctly on the green cloth. Yet he excused Adélaïde, thinking misfortune might explain a degrading action; perhaps some secret misfortune forced them. He refused to accept the proud face as a lie.The next morning he had not slept. The loss of the future they might have shared made deep inroads. To lose a dreamed future is keener than losing real happiness. Thus Hippolyte wandered under the Tuileries’ shade, absorbed and forgetful.There he met François Souchet, a schoolmate about to go to Italy. “Why, Hippolyte, what ails you?” the sculptor asked.“I am most unhappy,” Hippolyte admitted.“Only a love affair can make you so,” Souchet replied. Led into confidences, Hippolyte told him of the Rue de Suresnes and the young girl on the fourth floor. Souchet laughed and recalled what others knew: the mother’s appearance gave away her station. Jokes and mockery followed; Hippolyte’s reticence made him suffer as his secret was handled lightly. He pretended contradiction, but the jesting continued. “Come at four and study their walk,” Souchet advised.Torn, Hippolyte left his friends, convinced Adélaïde must be superior to such accusations and filled with remorse for suspecting her. His love was such that, despite the theft, he still worshiped her. He thought of playing the rescuer of a woman deserted by others—romantic and grand to his sensitive soul.He sat in his studio, looking at the portrait without working, tears in his eyes. Night came; roused, he went downstairs, met the admiral, looked darkly, and fled. He had planned to see the ladies, but the admiral’s presence froze him. For the hundredth time he wondered what interested this prodigal, who had eighty thousand francs a year, in a fourth-story room where he lost forty francs nightly; motives multiplied in his mind.In the next days Hippolyte threw himself into work to distract himself; study consoled him though memories of tender hours persisted. One evening, leaving his studio, he saw the ladies’ door half open and Adélaïde standing in the window recess. The door and staircase made it impossible not to be seen. He bowed coldly with a look of supreme indifference; yet thinking of the pain this coldness would cause, he shuddered. The week of disdain would have been terrible; perhaps the purse had been found and she had been seeking him. This hope filled him with remorse: perhaps Adélaïde deserved an inquiry and at least justification. Ashamed of resisting his heart, he called that evening on Madame de Rouville.All his suspicions vanished at the sight of Adélaïde, pale and thin. “Good heavens! what is the matter?” he asked.Adélaïde gave him a look of deep melancholy that pained him. “You must have thought us indifferent?” said the Baroness.“I was wrong,” he replied. “There are forms of pain we know not how to confide.”“The sincerity and strength of friendship are not to be measured by time,” the Baroness observed sadly.Hippolyte, blushing with shame, ascribed the loss to an accident. The evening was delicious; Adélaïde could read his remorse, and though he could not confess his doubts, he came back more in love and seeking tacit forgiveness. Yet when the Baroness suggested a game—“Let us have our little game; my old friend Kergarouet will not let me off”—his fears revived. He sat at the table; Adélaïde said he needed a partner.Throughout the game the Baroness and her daughter exchanged looks that alarmed Hippolyte more because he was winning. At last a final hand left the lovers in the old lady’s debt. Reaching for money, he saw a purse Adélaïde had slipped before him without his noticing; the poor child held the old one in her hand and, to keep her countenance, was looking into it for the money to pay her mother. The sight made blood rush to his heart; he was near fainting.The substituted purse contained his fifteen gold louis. It was worked with gilt beads; rings and tassels showed Adélaïde’s taste and the small hoard she had spent on it. It was an impossible, delicate way to show him that his gift could only be repaid by proof of affection.Overcome, Hippolyte clasped Adélaïde, pressed her to his heart, and kissed her; then, with an artist’s simple heartiness, he exclaimed, “I ask for her for my wife!” looking to the Baroness.Adélaïde looked half-wrothful; Madame de Rouville, somewhat astonished, considered her reply when a bell rang. The old vice-admiral entered, followed by his shadow and Madame Schinner. Alarmed by calumnies against Adélaïde, Hippolyte’s mother had inquired and reported them to the admiral, who swore to punish the scoundrels.Prompted by his wrath, the admiral explained his intentional losing at cards as his method to help the Baroness. After Madame Schinner paid her respects, the Baroness looked at Comte de Kergarouet, the Chevalier du Halga, Hippolyte, and Adélaïde, and said warmly, “So we are a family party this evening.”PARIS, May 1832ADDENDUMThe following personages appear in other stories of the Human Comedy. This is the central, repeated middle of the story, reiterated.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ##################################################################### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ##################################################################### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Produced by John Bickers, and DagnyPIERRETTEBy Honore De BalzacTranslated by Katharine Prescott WormeleyDEDICATIONTo Mademoiselle Anna Hanska:Dear Child,—You, the joy of the household, whose pink or white pelerine flutters in summer among the groves of Wierzschovnia, followed by the tender eyes of your father and mother,—how can I dedicate to you a melancholy story? And yet sorrows should be spoken of to a young girl idolized as you are, for the day may come when your sweet hands will be called to minister to them. It is difficult, Anna, to find in our manners and morals a subject worthy of your eyes; no choice has been left me; but perhaps you will feel how fortunate your fate is when you read the story sent to you by                                                    Your old friend,                                                         De Balzac.PIERRETTEI. THE LORRAINSAt dawn one October day in 1827 a sixteen-year-old youth in humble clothes stood in a small square of Lower Provins. The mills along the river already whirred; the wheels’ echo from the Upper Town made the place so still that even a distant diligence could be heard. Two long sides of the square, divided by an avenue of lindens, were built in a plain bourgeois style. No trade showed itself; porte-cochères were few, fronts were shaded by vines or roses climbing to second-story windows. One end opened toward the main street and river gardens; the other led to a quieter parallel street.At that end the youth recognized the house he sought: a white stone front grooved like courses, closed gray blinds, slender iron balconies with yellow-painted rosettes. Three dormer windows pierced a slate roof; a new weather-vane of a hunter shooting a hare topped the central one. Three stone steps led to the door. A leaden pipe discharged sink-water into a gutter at one side, indicating the kitchen; two shuttered windows with heart-shaped openings showed the dining-room. Vent-holes to the cellar had painted iron shutters cut in open-work. Everything looked recently restored, suggesting the small taste and self-satisfaction of a retired petty shopkeeper.The young man inspected these details with pleasure tinged with sadness; his eyes traveled from kitchen to roof. A calico curtain in one garret window caught the rosy light of the rising sun. He stepped back, leaned against a linden, and sang in the drawling tone of western France a Breton ditty often sung to newly married couples:“We’ve come to wish you happiness in marriage,  To m’sieur your husband  As well as to you:You have just been bound, madam’ la mariée,  With bonds of gold  That only death unbinds:You will go no more to balls or gay assemblies;  You must stay at home  While we shall go.Have you thought how you are pledged to be  True to your spouse,  And love him like yourself?Receive these flowers our hands do now present you;—  Alas! your fleeting honors  Will fade as they.”This tender, melancholy air would awaken a Breton maiden’s memories. As he finished the first couplet the singer, never taking his eyes from the attic curtain, saw no sign of life. While he sang the second couplet the curtain stirred. At “Receive these flowers,” a face appeared; a white hand cautiously opened the casement, and a girl made a sign with her head as he closed with the melancholy thought, “Alas! your fleeting honors will fade as they.”To her the young workman suddenly produced a yellow flower, common in Brittany—the furze, or broom.“Is it really you, Brigaut?” she said in a low voice.“Yes, Pierrette, yes. I am in Paris. I have started to make my way; but I’m ready to settle here, near you.”A first-floor window beneath Pierrette’s attic creaked. The girl showed terror and whispered: “Run away!”The lad leaped like a frightened frog to a bend in the street where a mill projected as the square opens into the main thoroughfare; but despite his agility his hob-nailed shoes echoed on the stones, easily distinguished from the mill’s music, and no doubt heard by the person who had opened the window.That person was an old maid. No man would have torn himself from a morning nap to listen to a minstrel in a jacket; only a maid wakes to songs of love. She opened her blinds with the furtive motion of a bat, looked about, saw nothing, and only heard the flying footfalls. In her neglected state—no false front or collarette, a little black silk bag covering a bony skull, pushed-aside night-cap—she had a harsh, angular face with deep wrinkles; the sight of her at a window was abrupt and forbidding. She owned the house with her brother, who slept so soundly that nothing disturbed him.The old maid stretched her neck from the window, raised cold pale-blue eyes toward the attic, tried to see Pierrette, and, finding it useless, drew back like a tortoise hiding its head. The blinds were then closed, and the street resumed its silence, broken only by peasants coming in or other early risers.When there is an old maid in a house, watch-dogs are unnecessary; not the slightest event escapes her observation or comment. This trifling incident was therefore destined to excite grave suppositions and to open the way for one of those obscure family dramas—none the less terrible because secret.Pierrette did not go back to bed. Brigaut’s arrival was an immense event to her. During the night—Eden of the wretched—she escaped the fault-findings that weighed on her by day. Like the hero of a ballad, her sleep seemed her only happy life; waking hours were a bad dream. She had her only pleasurable waking in three years. The memories of her childhood sang their melodious ditties in her soul. The first couplet had come in a dream; the second made her spring from bed; at the third she doubted her ears; but when the fourth was sung, standing in her night-gown with bare feet at the window, she recognized Brigaut, the companion of her childhood.Yes—there was the well-known square jacket with bobtails, the printed cotton waistcoat, the linen shirt fastened by a gold heart, the large collar, the earrings, stout shoes, and trousers of blue-gray drilling unevenly colored—the humble durable things of Breton peasantry. The big white-horn buttons that fastened the jacket made the girl’s heart beat. When she saw the bunch of broom her eyes filled with tears; then a dreadful fear drove back the budding happiness. She thought her cousin sleeping in the room below might have heard the noise she made in jumping to the window. The fear was just; the old maid had appeared, and Pierrette made Brigaut the terrified sign which he obeyed without understanding it. Such instinctive submission to a girl’s bidding reveals one of those innocent and absolute affections that blossom now and then in life. Whoever had seen the lad as he ran away would have loved the ingenuous chivalry of his feeling.Jacques Brigaut was worthy of Pierrette Lorrain, who was just fifteen. Two children! Pierrette could not help crying as she watched his flight in the terror her gesture had conveyed. Then she sat in a shabby armchair before a small table topped by a mirror. Resting her elbows, head in hands, she thought for an hour, calling to memory the Marais, the village of Pen-Hoel, perilous pond voyages in a boat untied from an old willow by little Jacques; the faces of her grandfather and grandmother, her mother’s sufferings, Major Brigaut’s handsome face—in short her careless childhood. It was a dream, a luminous joy on the gloomy present.Her beautiful chestnut hair escaped in disorder from her cap, rumpled in sleep—a cambric cap she had made. Little ringlets escaped from gray curl-papers at each side of her forehead. From the back hung a heavy braid half unplaited. Her whiteness betrayed the malady of girlhood, chlorosis, which deprives the body of color, destroys appetite, and disorders the organism. Waxy tones marked her flesh. The neck and shoulders betrayed wasted arms, flung forward and crossed upon the table. Her feet seemed enervated, the night-gown barely reached her knees, showing flaccid muscles, blue veins, and impoverished legs. The cold, which she heeded not, turned her lips violet; a sad smile drew up the corners of a sensitive mouth that showed small, ivory-white teeth. Her ears were dainty, and despite the roundness of her face she was lovely. All animation was in the eyes: the iris brown like Spanish tobacco with black flecks and golden reflections round brilliant pupils. Pierrette was made to be gay but was sad. Her lost gaiety still showed in the vivacious eye, the ingenuous grace of her brow, the smooth curve of her chin. The long eyelashes lay upon cheek-bones made prominent by suffering. Paleness rendered the lines and details purely distinct. The ear alone was a little masterpiece of modeling.Perhaps you would like to know her history, and this is it.Pierrette’s mother was a Demoiselle Auffray of Provins, half-sister by the father to Madame Rogron, mother of the present owners of the house.Monsieur Auffray had married at eighteen and again near sixty-nine. By his first marriage he had an only, plain daughter, who married at sixteen to innkeeper Rogron; by his second he had a charming daughter, so that Madame Rogron was fifty when the half-sister was born and already had two grown children.The youngest daughter married at eighteen a man of her choice, a Breton officer named Lorrain, captain in the Imperial Guard. The captain, anxious to rise to a colonelcy, exchanged into a line regiment. While he, then a major, and his wife enjoyed themselves in Paris on the allowance of Monsieur and Madame Auffray, or scoured Germany, old Auffray (formerly a grocer) died at eighty-eight without making a will. His property was administered by his daughter, Madame Rogron, and her husband so completely in their interests that nothing remained for the widow beyond the house she lived in on the little square and a few acres. This widow, the mother of Madame Lorrain, was thirty-eight at her husband’s death. Like many widows, she unwisely remarried. She sold the house and land to her step-daughter, Madame Rogron, and married a young physician named Neraud, who wasted her fortune. She died two years later of grief and misery.Thus the share of her father’s property that should have come to Madame Lorrain almost entirely disappeared, reduced to a small sum of eight thousand francs. Major Lorrain was killed at the battle of Montereau, leaving his wife, then twenty-one, with a little daughter of fourteen months and only a pension and an eventual inheritance from his parents, Monsieur and Madame Lorrain, retail shopkeepers at Pen-Hoel in the Marais. These Lorrains, Pierrette’s grandparents, sold wood, slates, tiles, pipes. Their business, from incapacity or ill-luck, did poorly and gave them scarcely enough. The failure of Collinet at Nantes, caused by 1814 events and a fall in colonial products, deprived them of twenty-four thousand francs they had deposited with that house.The arrival of their daughter-in-law was welcome. Her pension of eight hundred francs was a handsome income at Pen-Hoel. The eight thousand francs which the widow’s half-brother and sister Rogron sent her from her father’s estate were placed in the Lorrains’ business; they gave her a mortgage on a little house they owned in Nantes, let for three hundred francs and barely worth ten thousand.Madame Lorrain the younger, Pierrette’s mother, died in 1819. The child was small, delicate, and weakly; the damp climate of the Marais did not agree with her. But the husband’s family persuaded her, to keep her with them, that in no other place could she find a healthier region. She was so petted that her death brought nothing but honor to the old Lorrains.Some declared that Brigaut, an old Vendeen who had served under Charette and other leaders in the wars against the Republic, counted for much in the younger Madame Lorrain’s willingness to remain in the Marais. All Pen-Hoel saw him—called respectfully Major Brigaut—spending his days and evenings in the Lorrains’ parlor beside the window of the imperial major. Toward the last, the curate of Pen-Hoel urged old Madame Lorrain to have her daughter-in-law marry Brigaut, promising to have the major appointed justice of the peace through the Vicomte de Kergarouet’s influence. The young woman’s death ended the matter.Pierrette was left with her grandparents, who owed her four hundred francs a year, interest on the little property placed in their hands. This small sum supported her. Growing less fit, they soon faced an active competitor who ruined them. With his connivance, the Lorrains borrowed on notes they could not meet, driving them into bankruptcy. Pierrette’s claim on the house in Nantes was superseded by her grandmother’s legal rights, enforced to secure daily bread. The house sold for nine thousand five hundred francs, of which one thousand five hundred went for costs. The remaining eight thousand came to Madame Lorrain, who lived on their income in a humble place at Nantes called Saint-Jacques, where the two old people had bed and board for a small payment.As it was impossible to keep Pierrette with them, the old Lorrains wrote to her uncle and aunt Rogron in Provins. These Rogrons were dead. The letter might easily have been lost, but the post persistently traced it to Monsieur Rogron, son of the deceased, a mercer in the rue Saint-Denis in Paris; curiosity about a possible inheritance ensured its transmission, and the Treasury its fee.These Rogrons, toward whom the old Lorrains, though dreading to part with their dear granddaughter, stretched their supplicating hands, became unexpectedly the masters of Pierrette’s destiny. It is therefore indispensable to explain both their antecedents and their character.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Pere Rogron, the innkeeper of Provins who had married old Auffray’s daughter by his first wife, had an inflamed face, a veiny nose, and cheeks stained by Bacchic redness. Short, fat, pot-bellied, with stout legs and thick hands, he had the shrewdness of a Swiss innkeeper. Neither he nor his wife was handsome—never was a couple better matched. Rogron loved good living and to be waited on by pretty girls. He belonged to the class of egoists whose behavior is brutal; he gave way to his vices openly. Grasping, selfish, indecent, and always gratifying his whims, he spent his earnings until his teeth failed. Selfishness remained. In his old age he sold his inn, collected as much as he could of his late father-in-law’s property, and went to live in the little house in the square of Provins, bought for a trifle from the widow of old Auffray, Pierrette’s grandmother.Rogron and his wife had about two thousand francs a year from twenty-seven local lots, and twenty thousand from selling their inn. Old Auffray’s house, though dilapidated, was inhabited as it was by the Rogrons—old rats in wrack and ruin. Rogron took to horticulture and spent savings enlarging the garden; he carried it to the river’s edge between two walls and built a stone embankment at the end, where the aquatic flora showed itself.Early in their marriage the Rogrons had a son and a daughter, both hideous; for such human beings degenerate. Put out to nurse cheaply, these children returned after a coarse village upbringing—left to cry for hours while wet-nurses worked in the fields, shut up in damp, dark rooms. Treated thus, their features coarsened; voices grew harsh; they mortified their mother’s vanity, which she tried to correct sternly; their father’s severity made that sternness seem kindness by comparison. Generally left to run loose about stables, courtyards, or the streets, sometimes whipped, sometimes sent to their grandfather Auffray, who disliked them, the children suffered. The Rogrons justified taking the bulk of “the old scoundrel’s” property by accusing him of injustice to the children. Nevertheless Rogron sent his son to school and bought him a man, one of his cartmen, to save him from conscription. As soon as his daughter Sylvie was thirteen he sent her to Paris as an apprentice in a shop, with six hundred francs for her board; two years later he sent his son Jérôme-Denis the same way. Asked by his friends what he meant to do with his children, Pere Rogron was blunt.“When they are old enough to understand me I shall give ’em a kick and say: ‘Go and make your own way in the world!’” he replied, emptying his glass and wiping his lips. Then, with a wink: “My father gave me three kicks; I shall only give them one; he put one louis into my hand, I shall put ten in theirs, therefore they’ll be better off. After I’m gone, what’s left will be theirs. The notaries can find them and give it to them. What nonsense to bother about children. Mine owe me their life. I’ve fed them, and I don’t ask anything from them,—I call that quits. I began as a cartman, but that didn’t prevent me marrying the daughter of that old scoundrel Auffray.”Sylvie Rogron, apprenticed in the rue Saint-Denis to shopkeepers from Provins, was “at par” in two years; she earned her living and cost her parents nothing. By nineteen she had a salary of four hundred francs. At twenty she was second demoiselle in the Maison Julliard, wholesale silk dealers at the “Chinese Worm” rue Saint-Denis. Jérôme-Denis entered the Maison Guepin, a large wholesale mercer at the “Three Distaffs.” When Sylvie was twenty-one and forewoman at a thousand francs a year, Jérôme-Denis at eighteen was head-clerk with a salary of twelve hundred francs.Brother and sister met Sundays and feast-days for cheap amusements; they dined out and visited Saint-Cloud, Meudon, Belleville, or Vincennes. Around the end of 1815 they pooled about twenty thousand francs in savings and bought, from Madame Guenée, the property and good-will of the “Family Sister,” a large retail establishment in the quarter. Sylvie kept the books and did the writing; Jérôme-Denis was master and head-clerk. By 1821, after five years, competition was fierce and it cost them all they had to maintain the business and its reputation.Though Sylvie was scarcely forty, her natural ugliness, hard work, and a crabbed look made her seem fifty. At thirty-eight Jérôme presented the silliest face to customers: a retreating forehead with three folds, close-cut grizzled hair, and a dull, lifeless expression. His bluish eyes had neither flame nor thought. Short and fat like his father, his figure showed a ridiculous debility rather than brute obesity. His father’s high color had become livid flabbiness peculiar to those confined to back-shops or counting-rooms, forever tying bundles, making change, snarling at clerks, and repeating old phrases to customers.The small amount of brains the siblings possessed had been wholly absorbed in maintaining their business, getting and keeping money, and learning the market’s laws and usages. Thread, needles, ribbons, pins, buttons, and tailors’ furnishings had taken all their capacity. Outside business they knew nothing; they were ignorant of Paris beyond the Rue Saint-Denis. Their narrow natures saw no field except the shop. They were clever at nagging clerks and young women and proving them to blame. Their happiness lay in busy counters—the glib gabble of clerks using consecrated phrases made their day fine; on really fine days when Parisians strolled caring for no goods, the day was overcast to the Rogrons. “Bad weather for sales,” they said.Rogron’s skill at tying parcels made him admired by apprentices: he could fold, tie, and see all that happened in the street and the farthest recesses of the shop before handing a parcel back with “Here it is, madame; nothing else today?” But he would have been ruined without his sister. Sylvie had common sense and a genius for trade. She advised purchases and would send him to remote parts of France to save a trifle. Her shrewdness, not employed in the heart’s service, drifted into speculation. The necessity of a business to pay for—this thought kept the machine going and gave it infernal activity.Rogron was really only head-clerk; he understood nothing of the business as a whole. Self-interest—the great motor of the mind—had failed to instruct him. He was often aghast when his sister ordered some article to be sold below cost to clear out the end of its fashion; later he admired her idiotically. He reasoned neither ill nor well; he was simply incapable of reasoning; but he subordinated himself to his sister because “she is my elder.” Perhaps an existence always solitary, reduced to mere needs, deprived of money and pleasures in youth, explains to physiologists the clownish face, feebleness of mind, and vacant silliness. His sister had steadily prevented him from marrying—afraid to lose her power and seeing only expense and injury in a younger, likely prettier woman.Silliness behaves by talking or being silent. Silent silliness can be borne; Rogron’s was loquacious. He chattered to his clerks, explaining minutiae and ornamenting his talk with flat jokes—the “chaff” of shopkeeping—and had come to possess a phraseology of his own. This chatterer believed himself an orator. The necessity of explaining to customers, guessing their desires, and even giving them desires, exercises the tongues of retail shopkeepers. The petty dealer acquires the faculty of uttering words and sentences with no meaning but marked success. He explains matters of manufacture customers know nothing of; that alone gives a passing superiority; take him away from his thousand explanations and he is, relatively to thought, like a fish out of water.Rogron and Sylvie, two mechanisms baptized by mistake, lacked the feelings that give life to the heart. Their natures were shriveled and harsh, hardened by toil, privation, and the memory of suffering during a long apprenticeship to life. Neither complained. They were not implacable so much as impracticable toward others in misfortune. To them, virtue, honor, and loyalty consisted solely in the payment of bills. Irritable and irritating, sordid in economy, they bore a dreadful reputation among the merchants of the rue Saint-Denis. Had it not been for their connection with Provins, where they went three or four times a year and could close the shop for a day or two, they would have had no clerks or young women. But old Rogron sent them all the unfortunate young people of his neighborhood, whose parents hoped to start them in Paris. He obtained these apprentices by boasting of his son’s success. Parents, attracted by the prospect of close training and perhaps eventual succession to the business, sent whichever child was most in the way. But when clerks or young women found a way to escape, they fled with rejoicing that increased the Rogrons’ bad name. New victims were supplied yearly by the indefatigable old father.From fifteen Sylvie had two faces—the amiable seller and the sour spinster. Her acquired countenance was marvellous mimicry: all smiles and a soft, wheedling voice giving a commercial charm to business. Her real face, projecting from half-opened blinds, would have frightened the most resolute. When the Lorrains’ letter reached them they were in mourning for their father, from whom they inherited the house that had been as good as stolen from Pierrette’s grandmother, also certain lands bought by their father, and moneys acquired by usurious loans and mortgages to the peasantry. The yearly taking of stock was over. The price of the “Family Sister” had, at last, been paid in full. The Rogrons owned about sixty thousand francs’ worth of merchandise, forty thousand in bank or cash, and the value of their business. Sitting on a bench covered with striped-green Utrecht velvet behind their private counter, the siblings consulted what they should do.All retail shopkeepers aspire to become bourgeois. By selling the good-will of their business they would have over a hundred and fifty thousand francs, not counting their father’s inheritance. By placing their available property in the public Funds they would each obtain about four thousand francs a year, and by taking the proceeds of their business when sold they could repair and improve the house they inherited, making it a good investment. They could then live in Provins. Their forewoman was the daughter of a rich farmer at Donnemarie, burdened with nine children; in five years the farmer had lost seven children, which made the forewoman interesting. Rogron had tried, unsuccessfully, to make her marry him; she showed aversion which baffled him. Mademoiselle Sylvie opposed the match; she sought instead to make the shrewd young woman their successor.No passing observer can form an idea of the cryptic existence of a certain class of shopkeepers; one wonders on what and why they live. To discover the thin subsoil beneath their lives one must dig. The Parisian shopkeeper nurtures his soul on hope: one dreams of managing a theatre, honors of mayoralty, a country-house ten miles from Paris with a “park,” statues of tinted plaster, fountains that squirt threads of water—on which he will spend a mint; others dream of distinction in the National Guard. Provins filled the Rogrons with fanatical longings. Let it be said to La Champagne’s credit: this love is warranted. Provins rivals Frangistan and the valley of Cashmere; not only does it contain the poesy of Saadi, but it offers medicinal springs. The Provins roses are world-famous. Provins was the promised land that appeared to the two shopkeepers amid the muck of Saint-Denis.Crossing the gray plains between La Ferté-Gaucher and Provins—a productive desert of wheat—you reach a hill. Suddenly a town watered by two rivers appears; at the rock’s feet stretches a verdant valley of enchanting lines and fugitive horizons. From Paris you pass through Provins along the highroad encumbered with beggars and blind men who follow you with pitiful voices. From Troyes you approach on the valley side. The château, old town, and ramparts terrace the hillside; the new town lies below. Upper and Lower Provins differ: the upper is airy, steep, solemn, with imposing ruins; the lower is a town of mills watered by the Voulzie and the Durtain, a town of inns, shops, retired merchants, diligences, travelling-carriages, and waggons. Its historical memories, melancholy ruins, ravines, gardens, and rivers banked with gardens excite the love of all its children, who, like Auvergnats or Savoyards, leave to make fortunes and always return. “Die in one’s form,” the proverb for hares and faithful souls, seems Provins’ motto.Thus Jérôme and Sylvie thought constantly of their dear Provins. While Jérôme sold thread he saw the upper town; as he piled button cards he contemplated the valley; when he rolled ribbons he followed the shining rivers. Looking up at shelves he saw ravines where he had escaped his father’s anger and gathered blackberries. The little square in the lower town was his chief object: he imagined improving his house, dreaming of a new front, bedrooms, a salon, billiard-room, dining-room, and a kitchen garden turned into an English pleasure-ground with lawns, grottos, fountains, and statuary. The bedrooms they occupied in Paris were furnished with the merest necessaries, yet in fancy no one had finer furniture. Jérôme, walking, admired upholsterers’ windows and draperies he coveted for his house, and at home would tell his sister he’d found pieces “that will just do.” He bought and soon rejected each thing in favor of the next. The Budget itself could not have paid for his schemes. He wanted everything he saw, but abandoned each for the last. New balconies and ornaments he thought out of place in Paris: “Ah!” he said, “those fine things would look much better at Provins.” Leaning on his doorstep with a vacant eye, he visualized his gilded house, garden, fountain, billiard-table, and flowers.Sylvie thought deeply, pen in hand, receiving the bourgeoisie of Provins, looking at herself in salon mirrors, admiring a cap. The siblings began to find the rue Saint-Denis atmosphere unhealthy; the market mud made them long for the fragrance of Provins roses. They were victims of genuine nostalgia and monomania, frustrated for now by the need to sell tapes and bobbins before leaving Paris. The promised land of the valley attracted them because they had really suffered crossing the sandy wastes of mercer’s business.The Lorrains’ letter reached them amid meditations inspired by this future. They knew scarcely anything about their cousin Pierrette Lorrain. Their father had got possession of the Auffray property after they left home and said little of his affairs. They hardly remembered Aunt Lorrain. An hour of genealogical discussion was needed to make her out as the younger sister of their own mother by their grandfather Auffray’s second marriage. It struck them that this second marriage had harmed their interests by dividing the Auffray property between two daughters. In times past their father had sneered about it.The Rogrons considered the Lorrains’ application through these recollections, which were not favorable to Pierrette. To take charge of an orphan cousin who might become their legal heir if neither married was a matter for debate. They had never seen Pierrette; what trouble would a young girl be? Wouldn’t it commit them to obligations? Could they send her away if they disliked her? Wouldn’t they have to marry her? If Jérôme found a yoke-mate among Provins heiresses they ought to preserve property for his children. A yoke-mate, according to Sylvie, meant a stupid, rich, ugly girl who would let herself be governed. They decided to refuse the Lorrain request. Sylvie agreed to write the answer. Business being urgent, she delayed, and when the forewoman offered to buy the stock and good-will of the “Family Sister,” which the Rogrons accepted, the matter left the old maid’s mind.Sylvie Rogron and her brother departed for Provins four years before the arrival of Brigaut stirred Pierrette’s life. But the doings of the pair after reaching Provins are as necessary to relate as their life in Paris; for Provins was destined to be not less fatal to Pierrette than the commercial antecedents of her cousins.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When the petty shopkeeper returns from Paris to the provinces, he brings back echoes of the capital that soon fade, leaving only superficial marks where Paris briefly touched the town. The shift from constant bustle to provincial silence afflicts the returned shopkeeper. Those who have saved something often spend part of it on a long-nursed impulse: travel, municipal posts, hunting, usury, or speculation. The Rogrons, brother and sister, were seized by the desire to remodel their old house into something charming.Their fixed idea, carried out under constant consultation with the contractor, produced the façade and interior that Brigaut examined: elaborate plans, handsome furnishings, and a parade of choicer objects purporting to belong to Provins notables. The Rogrons wanted what the rich bourgeois possessed. “If Monsieur Garceland has it, put it in,” Mademoiselle Rogron declared. They fussed over architectural details (“ovolos”), balked at extra costs yet yielded to comparisons—“The Lesourds had theirs painted last year”—and aimed to equal the town’s leading families.For a year they watched workmen, answered townspeople’s questions, and attempted to insinuate themselves into Provins society. Having known no one in Paris, they thirsted for social life. In Provins their former masters had returned: Monsieur and Madame Julliard of the “Chinese Worm,” the Guepin clan now keeping the “Three Distaffs,” and Madame Guenee, from whom they had bought the “Family Sister.” These families had spread through the town—Garceland the mayor was a Guepin son-in-law; Abbé Peroux, the curate, was brother to Madame Julliard; Monsieur Tiphaine junior, a judge, was brother to Madame Guenee.The queen of Provins was the beautiful, clever, and ambitious Madame Mélanie Tiphaine junior, daughter of Madame Roguin. Taken from a convent to marry in the provinces to please her mother, she managed skilfully, hosting twice-weekly salons and cultivating every vanity to promote her husband’s career. Her aim was to have Monsieur Tiphaine elected deputy and then a judge in Paris; she gratified every elector and became the pride of Provins. Madame Guenee married her daughters into leading families and married Monsieur Garceland the collector herself; together with the Julliards they formed a court around Madame Tiphaine. Julliard junior, secretly enamored of her, became her Egeria and launched the “Bee-hive,” a journal filled with family contributions and local advertisements. Madame Tiphaine’s salon naturally became the first in town; the few aristocrats held a separate salon at the Comtesse de Breautey’s.The Rogrons were received, after study, first by Madame Julliard the elder and Madame Garceland, then—after difficulty—by Madame Tiphaine. Society winnows people by wealth, education, manners, accomplishments, and character; the Guepins, Guenees, and Julliards stood higher than the Rogrons, whose father had been held in contempt over the Auffray property—a fact known to notary Auffray.In Madame Tiphaine’s dignified salon everyone understood one another; the Rogrons fancied admission by the mayor would suffice. Sylvie practiced boston; Rogron, unable to play, twirled his thumbs and spoke only of his new house. He choked on words and made a comic show of embarrassment. Sylvie betrayed herself at cards—sharp, irritable, whining when she lost, insolent when she won; nagging and quarrelsome, she became a social scourge. Yet the pair possessed a silly, open ambition and, having spent thirty thousand francs on their restoration and possessing at least ten thousand francs a year, believed themselves wealthy and sought to impose themselves on Provins, which quickly measured their vulgarity, ignorance, and envy.At their presentation Madame Tiphaine had already noticed them and remarked to Julliard junior that they would not fit her select salon. “I wish my society to be select; and it certainly will not be if I admit those Rogrons,” she said. Monsieur Tiphaine sneered at their shopkeeper origins, and Julliard called them impudent, wanting to crush other houses though “they have hardly enough to live on.” They agreed the sister’s harsh voice and thick hands made her intolerable. Madame Tiphaine’s comment—“my salon is not an inn”—was much admired and circulated in town. A month passed before she returned Sylvie’s visit; provincial insolence and measured neglect were memorably felt.Sylvie had an awkward evening at Madame Tiphaine’s, provoking a scene with old Madame Julliard over boston. Afterwards the card-tables were set before Sylvie arrived so she would be shunted to an onlooker’s role; at Madame Julliard senior’s they played whist, which Sylvie did not know. Invitations stopped. Satirical townspeople teased the Rogrons about the eggs in their cornice and their wine cellar; the pair tried to parry by giving sumptuous dinners to return civilities and display luxury.Their first dinner, for town leaders, became a test. Madame Tiphaine, who had introduced the Parisian custom of leaving after coffee, was anxious to go; the Rogrons accompanied her to the door in the provincial way, but when she departed not staying for the full evening, the rest quickly imitated her, leaving the Rogrons disconcerted. They had hoped to surprise their guests with their “palace,” but Madame Tiphaine later described the house to the salon in terms that ridiculed their taste: a long corridor and portico, a painted statue, a black-marble painted staircase, white calico curtains with red borders fastened by brass lion-paws, lamps like restaurant lights—novelties and ornaments everywhere. The dining-room had five cheap engraved heroics in gilt frames; the salon was all red—curtains, furniture, mantelpiece—heavy common candelabra, a clock on Roman standards, a chandelier wrapped in green muslin, walls papered in red flock to imitate velvet, chromo-lithographs in stucco frames, uniform cashmere and elm furniture; an Aubusson carpet in a vulgar pattern completed a room that looked as if no one lived there: no books, knick-knacks, or flowers. Madame Tiphaine preferred Madame Julliard’s pastels and old provincial silver; “The provinces are provinces; they are only ridiculous when they mimic Paris,” she concluded. The sketch revealed the absurdities a clever builder had foisted on the Rogrons—expensive novelties that gratified bourgeois taste but exposed their vulgarity.Their social plans failed. Invitations were refused—“already engaged” to Madame Garceland and others. The Rogrons had supposed a few dinners would secure position; only young men who would mock them and habitués who dined everywhere accepted. Frightened by the loss of forty thousand francs on her “dear house,” Sylvie began economizing: she dismissed the cook, hired a country girl, and took to cooking “for pleasure.”Fourteen months after returning, they had fallen into solitude and idleness. Banished from society, Sylvie developed a strong hatred for the Tiphaines, Julliards, and the rest of the “clique” and sought a rival circle. But the lesser bourgeoisie comprised only small shopkeepers free only on Sundays and men like Vinet and the Bonapartist Baron Gouraud, whom Rogron carelessly befriended despite warnings.The siblings sat by the stove, recalling former customers and old resentments, weighed down by ennui. Days lengthened into lazy habits: lingering in bed, shaving as an event, quarreling about hot water, wandering to inspect garden shrubs or a sick hen, tapping the barometer like a friend. They read the “Bee-hive” and the Constitutionnel, the cost shared by Vinet and Gouraud; Rogron delivered the paper to Gouraud and enjoyed long yarns and puzzling warnings about the clique—warnings he foolishly repeated. His walks yielded scraps of gossip: archaeological projects, talks about Provins’ catacombs and springs. These bits became topics for evenings, but evenings at home were desert-like; they creaked at one another like rusty wheels. Rogron spoke of marrying, which frightened him; Sylvie sought a third person and remembered a distant cousin, Pierrette Lorrain, scarcely inquired about and possibly presumed dead.Sylvie, too careful to lose opportunity, pretended to have found a Lorrain letter to raise the prospect of Pierrette’s arrival. She answered Madame Lorrain’s letter half-affectionately, half commercially, blaming delay on the change of abode and hinting Pierrette might inherit twelve thousand francs a year if Jérôme did not marry. The Rogrons awaited the cousin like caged beasts. Sylvie even went to Madame Tiphaine to mention Pierrette, deploring the girl’s misfortune and posing as eager to assist—only to be met by sarcasm. During cards, Madame Garceland recalled the Rogrons’ shameful conduct over the Auffray property; the notary explained events. Monsieur Tiphaine asked where the little girl was.“In Brittany,” said Rogron.“Brittany is a large place,” Monsieur Lesourd remarked.Sylvie clumsily answered questions about when they had replied and where the girl might be sent. Rogron blundered, claiming to have written to the Institution of Saint-Jacques, only to be told that was an almshouse for the elderly. Desfondrilles recalled a Nantes house sold under court order swallowing up Mademoiselle Lorrain’s claim, leaving the girl unprovided for. The Rogrons retreated, blaming malicious gossip. Sylvie saw her benevolence had missed its effect; she had lost ground. Thereafter the Rogrons no longer concealed their hatred of that class.The brother recounted to his sister the scandals Colonel Gouraud and lawyer Vinet had hinted about the Tiphaines, Guenees, Garcelands, and Julliards—accusations that Madame Tiphaine’s family’s fortune was stolen. Once they began to declaim against “the clique,” they unwittingly set out to gather a society of their own: their house would become a rendezvous for the floating elements of the liberal party. Colonel Gouraud and the lawyer Vinet, ostracized and eager for consequence, had watched the Rogrons’ launch with curiosity. Both professed patriotism partly to become important. The Liberals in Provins were thinly scattered—an old soldier at a café, an innkeeper, Monsieur Cournant the notary, Doctor Neraud, and a few farmers. The colonel and the lawyer, delighted to find a foolish man with money to back schemes and a house to host meetings, exploited the Rogrons’ ill-will toward the Upper-town bourgeois aristocracy.Pierrette’s expected arrival hastened their plans. Gouraud, a veteran accustomed to horrors, felt little repelled by a poor cousin and began eyeing Sylvie’s fortune; he saw a house and the chance to live off someone else. The colonel was short, fat, decorated, with sparse whiskers, a red face disfigured by campaigns, a cavalry-built stomach and a sabre-cut on his nose; he wore the Legion of Honor and lived on about three thousand francs a year from emoluments and retired pay. Jolly, lawless, contemptuous of convention, he was ready to exploit opportunities.The lawyer Vinet, tall and thin, had liberal opinions in place of talent and earned little. Disfavored by the court for his opinions and resented for marrying a Chargeboeuf heiress, he had been repulsed by the influential family. His poverty and thwarted hopes made him eager for opposition triumphs to elevate him. He lived miserably in the Upper town; his wife rarely left. Vinet’s pale, jaundiced face, slit mouth, viperish head, quick, rasping speech, and shrewdness made him formidable. Accustomed to viewing everything for personal advantage and shrinking from nothing legal, he was well-fitted for politics; his strength lay in his willingness to do whatever the law permitted.Vinet had a terrible influence on Pierrette’s fate. He aimed to found a newspaper at Provins and, after studying the Rogrons from a distance with the colonel’s aid, concluded the brother could be made useful. He was right: Vinet’s days of poverty seemed about to end. When Gouraud told him the Rogrons had quarreled with the Upper-town bourgeois aristocracy, Vinet winked and proposed a plan.“One woman or another—handsome or ugly—you don’t care; marry Mademoiselle Rogron and we can organize something at once,” he said.Gouraud had thought of it, but they had sent for Colonel Lorrain’s daughter; she was the next of kin. Vinet suggested getting them to make a will in his favor. “You’d get a very comfortable house,” he said. As for the little girl—Gouraud leered, showing scant respect for any girl, and promised to see her, calculating how far he could profit.Thus the Rogrons, cast out of the elite they hoped to join, became the center of a new, unscrupulous circle: a veteran with a taste for plunder and a lawyer eager to exploit both politics and private advantage. Their hatred of the clique and their vanity, together with the colonel’s coarse designs and Vinet’s legal opportunism, set in motion schemes that would determine Pierrette’s fate and the Rogrons’ future.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################After her grandparents were taken to the hospital where they expected to die, young Pierrette, proud and unused to charity, felt relief when she learned she had well-to-do relations. Brigaut, the son of her mother’s friend and her childhood companion, apprenticed as a cabinet-maker in Nantes, offered the sixty francs of his apprentice tips to send her to Paris by diligence; she accepted with the indifference of true affection.Brigaut spent Sundays consoling Pierrette at Saint-Jacques, delighting in protecting the girl he loved. They had embroidered youthful plans together—he, with his plane, would scour the world to make their fortune while Pierrette waited. In October 1824, just after her eleventh birthday, the sorrowful grandparents and Brigaut entrusted her to the conductor of the Nantes–Paris diligence, begging him to see her safely onto the Provins coach. Brigaut ran after the coach until out of breath; both cried as she disappeared.The Lorrains and Brigaut knew little of city life, so Pierrette arrived in Paris penniless. The conductor, told about her rich cousins, paid hotel expenses and made the Provins conductor promise to look after the child and have the family pay.
