Four days out, about nine on a Monday night, a kind old conductor of the Messageries‑royales led Pierrette—whose only luggage was a small bundle with two dresses, two chemises, and two pairs of stockings—to Mademoiselle Rogron’s house, pointed out at the coach office.“I’ve brought you a cousin,” he announced to Sylvie and her brother, and demanded forty‑seven francs, sixty centimes, for expenses. The Rogrons, astonished, paid forty‑seven francs, twelve sous. The conductor refused the small gratitude Sylvie offered for himself and his comrade, saying they had cared for the child as if she were their own. Sylvie, Rogron, and their maid Adèle received Pierrette with stolid surprise; she was tossed to them like a parcel, with no transition between the wretched chamber at Saint‑Jacques and their dining room, which seemed a palace to her. Shy and speechless, she might have charmed others in her simple Breton clothes: coarse blue petticoat, cambric apron, thick shoes, blue stockings, white kerchief, red worsted mittens, and a washed Breton cap forming a halo around her rosy, artless face.“Are you not going to say anything? I am your cousin Sylvie, and that is your cousin Rogron,” they said. They fussed over her, inspected her bundle, and decided she must sleep; then, with a mixture of economy and officiousness, they tucked her into a modest little room with threadbare furnishings. Pierrette, amazed at the fineness of cotton sheets and the warmth of the house, quietly praised it. Meanwhile Sylvie and Rogron fretted, counting the cost of clothing and equipment: “You won’t get off under three hundred francs,” Rogron declared. Sylvie, shocked at the sum, lay awake thinking of the expense.Pierrette had been reared on affection and freedom at Pen‑Hoel, petted by everyone and wandering the fields with Brigaut. At Saint‑Jacques she had known hardship but lived on her grandparents’ love; now she missed the kindly looks and casual tenderness she had always had. The cold greeting of her cousins baffled her and wounded a heart delicate as a fawn’s. Still, tired, she slept.Next morning she rose early, explored the staircase and the garden, marvelled at the kiosk and the river, and was punished for noise and dirt at breakfast. “You must be very good and quiet,” Sylvie scolded; Rogron called her dirty and ignorant; Adèle sniffed at her coarse chemises. Their remarks hurt Pierrette though she scarcely understood why. The Rogrons, proud of their house and furniture, had turned care for possessions into worship. Sylvie kept a closet of dusting tools and took petty delight in maintaining and glorifying household goods; her ardor for cleanliness hardened into a principle and a pleasure.Sylvie and Rogron set about remaking Pierrette to fit their standards. Sewing and fittings filled the first week: chemises and petticoats, boots, a corset, a blue reps dress, a silk‑lined cape, and a drawn bonnet of blue velvet—fashionable items Sylvie hoped would stand up to the scrutiny of Provins society. She engaged Mademoiselle Borain, the town’s best seamstress, to make Pierrette’s outfit, scolding the child for ignorance when Pierrette confessed she had only played in Brittany and been loved by her grandparents.Pierrette preferred the warm, watching company of the sewing women to her relations’ pedantry. The seamstresses liked her, while Sylvie fretted the expense: “That child will make us pay through the nose!” she complained. Yet she pushed on, buying quality underclothes and fashion‑conscious bits to rival other mothers. After the transformation, Pierrette became the most enchanting child in Provins; Sunday after Sunday, the ladies of the town embraced her. Their admiration inflated Sylvie’s self‑regard—she believed the praise reflected her benevolence—yet she grew resentful when Pierrette was invited out and the guests did not visit her salon. The child’s success, which might have delighted a maternal heart, only enraged the Rogrons’ selfishness.Pierrette’s finery was the beginning of trouble. Used to running and rough play, she wore out shoes and tore delicate garments. A mother would have corrected gently, but the Rogrons calculated loss rather than showed pity. The constant admonitions about propriety, economy, and decency all flowed from one theme—“Pierrette will ruin us.” In fault‑finding lectures Sylvie and Rogron reverted to the petty, shopkeeping habits they'd ostensibly left behind; deprived of human subjects to rule, they exercised petty despotism on the helpless child. Narrow natures expand by persecuting; for them self‑interest fused with a thirst for control and vindication.Pierrette became central to their lives: first in the expense of her wardrobe, then as a presence to be governed and displayed. Sylvie’s initial terms of endearment hardened into the cold “Pierrette.” Reprimands sharpened, and the cousins descended into habitual severity they convinced themselves was beneficial. Pierrette, too sensitive and straightforward for such hardness, dreaded scoldings; tears rose at the slightest reproof. She learned to repress her natural sprightliness, showing it only in friends’ homes. Within a month she had learned passive compliance; when Rogron asked if she were ill, she ran to the garden and wept by the river.One day, despite the care taken with her wardrobe, she tore her best reps frock at Madame Tiphaine’s, where she’d been happy. Terrified of home punishment, she confided in friends; Madame Tiphaine happened to have matching material and discreetly repaired the frock. When Sylvie discovered this “trick,” she forbade Pierrette further visits to Tiphaine’s, cutting off one of the few joyful outlets she had. This refusal, and the beginning of formal education, marked the end of the first three months of Pierrette’s life in Provins—the only period that had held genuine pleasure.Meanwhile the colonel and Vinet watched the Rogrons with foxlike caution, making occasional calls and infiltrating Rogron’s confidence with polite reserve. On the night Sylvie forbade Tiphaine’s visits, the colonel and a lawyer friend—who used gossip and partisan spite to inflame Sylvie—suggested the Rogrons form an opposition salon against the “ministerialists” of Provins. Vinet exploited social slander to rouse Sylvie’s resentment; the lawyer painted Tiphaine’s speech as insult, provoking the Rogrons’ vanity and readying them for petty political posturing.Vinet soon introduced his wife to Sylvie—a gentle, timid, well‑bred woman who bore Sylvie’s airs with deference. Madame Vinet’s meekness pleased Sylvie, and the colonel, displaying affected courtliness, flattered her. These new allies encouraged the Rogrons’ snug social ambitions and fed Sylvie’s self‑importance; their influences would further isolate Pierrette.Under their watchful, acquisitive eyes the child’s life narrowed. Shut indoors and only allowed out under strict supervision, she lived beneath an incessant “Don’t touch that, child, let that alone!” She was drilled in carriage and behavior; Sylvie, rigid and prideful, ordered her to hold herself erect and sometimes enforced compliance with slaps to the back. The free, joyous Breton girl learned, little by little, to curb every liveliness and to become as best she could an automaton—quiet, obedient, and constrained to match the Rogrons’ house and their appetite for domination and display.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################One evening early in Pierrette’s second stay at her cousins’ house, the child, unseen all day, came in to kiss the three guests good-night. Sylvie turned her cheek coldly and Pierrette burst into tears. Vinet and Sylvie mocked her; Sylvie contrasted the grandmother who took Pierrette’s money with a cousin who would leave her hers. “I would rather be robbed and loved,” said Pierrette, and was told she would be sent back. Humiliated, she went to Rogron, who, with Sylvie, accused her of mischief—opening a watch, breaking a mainspring—and of being heedless. Ashamed, Pierrette left the room.Rogron vowed to subdue the child. Madame Vinet suggested school; the husband and the colonel, however, had other plans. Vinet proposed using the managing editor of an opposition journal as Pierrette’s teacher if Rogron would back the paper financially. Sylvie called Pierrette a “rough diamond” needing polishing, and the plan to employ a master and to launch the paper advanced while Pierrette dissolved in tears. She blamed her cousin and resolved to be faultless; unaware, she became the subject of further schemes in the salon.Pierrette did get a writing-master to teach reading, writing, and arithmetic—a change heralded at the Rogrons’ as bringing terrible disorder: ink-spots, scattered sand, torn books. She was told to earn her keep and not be a burden; to weep was treated as an affront to their kindness. Rogron adopted the tone he used with clerks: he scolded her, summoned her from play to drill lessons, and became almost savage. Sylvie, teaching what little she knew of women’s work, had no tenderness. Their narrow pleasure in worrying the child shifted from outward kindness into severity, justified by alleged “self-will.” Unable to adapt instruction to Pierrette’s slow learning, they called her stupid and clumsy for trifles. Constantly abused by words and looks, she took on a sheep-like submission, sheltering her mind in passive obedience. Her bright colors faded; when she complained of feeling ill “everywhere,” they dismissed the claim as nonsense or bad faith.Only Adele, the fat servant, softened toward the child—warming her bed secretly after Sylvie’s rebukes. But even this small protection was fragile. The Rogrons treated Pierrette’s affection as dissimulation; any show of gentle feeling was met with suspicion and accusations of ulterior motives. Colonel Gouraud, anxious to please Sylvie, approved of her actions; Vinet attributed the child’s behavior to Breton obstinacy and encouraged the Rogrons. Flattered, Rogron agreed to go security for the Courrier de Provins; Sylvie invested five thousand francs.The colonel and Vinet then organized a wider financial and political campaign. They sold shares in the paper to farmers, independents, and buyers of national-domain land, exploited local fears, and split judicial advertisements between the existing Bee-hive and the new Courrier. The paper’s first number contained a pompous eulogy of Rogron, likening him to Laffitte and arousing public expectations of contested elections. The new paper attacked local figures like the Tiphaine family; Madame Tiphaine feared losing her social position.As the Courrier spread, Vinet adopted the liberal dress and airs; his wife took a servant and comported herself as the wife of a prominent man. Vinet and his ally Cournant, a liberal notary, worked to increase the Rogrons’ rentals: they pursued suits over planting and trees, collected fines, and carried out ejectments of peasants who had defaulted on debts secured by the elder Rogron. Using the Rogrons’ savings—who had been setting aside a large sum annually—they invested in improved lands, dividing holdings into small farms rented to safe tenants. By 1826 these investments yielded about five thousand francs a year, with little need for repairs or insurance since tenants bore many costs.By the end of Pierrette’s second stay in Provins life under the Rogrons had hardened into cold indifference, petty fault-finding, and the absence of affection; she felt a grave chill closing in. She contemplated running back to Brittany to her grandparents, but two events prevented escape: old Lorrain died, and Rogron was appointed her guardian. Had the grandmother died first, Rogron—advised by Vinet—would have claimed Pierrette’s eight thousand francs and left the old man destitute. Prompted by Vinet’s comment that one never knew who might live or die, Rogron forced Madame Lorrain to secure for Pierrette the reversion of the eight thousand francs at her death.Pierrette, preparing for her first communion, abandoned thoughts of flight and began to change in another way. Sylvie insisted that the local vicar, Monsieur Habert—a zealous, austere priest thought to belong to a Congregation—should instruct Pierrette. Habert and his sister, a thin, unmarried schoolmistress, took an interest in the child. Rooted in Breton Catholic practice and naturally inclined toward devotion, Pierrette responded fully to the priest’s teaching. Suffering predisposed her to mysticism; the priest taught her to see Jesus as a heavenly bridegroom and to interpret her trials as part of divine providence. Faith gave meaning to her suffering; she abandoned escape and took refuge in prayer, hope, and charity. Surprised, Sylvie also adopted more piety under the priest’s influence, while Rogron—bound to Colonel Gouraud, Vinet, and Liberalism—remained unmoved.Mademoiselle Rogron befriended Mademoiselle Habert; the two spinsters became close. The sister offered to take Pierrette to her school to spare Sylvie the trouble, but the Rogrons decided the house would be too lonely without the child. Their attachment seemed excessive to the other camp.Gouraud and Vinet, however, feared the Haberts’ influence: they suspected the priest aimed to marry his sister advantageously, as the colonel hoped to marry Sylvie. They saw the priest and his sister as rivals as formidable as themselves. Both pairs—the liberals led by Vinet and the Haberts with their clerical circle—visited the Rogrons frequently, turning the salon into a battlefield where each faction sought to win the household’s allegiance.The liberals struck back. Vinet brought Mademoiselle de Chargeboeuf and her mother from Troyes—Bathilde de Chargeboeuf, noble yet impoverished—persuaded that by combining her means with Vinet’s she could live in Provins and marry the gullible Rogron. Their arrival strengthened the liberal party and scandalized Provins’ aristocrats. Madame de Breautey deplored that women of rank could be led into such alliances by money and social ambition. Bathilde, at twenty-five, was dazzling: fair, well-formed, with the carriage and features of a high-bred woman—yet, as Madame Tiphaine sniffed, perhaps tending to look too healthy for Rogron’s tastes. Her dress was simple but carefully managed to display charm; she lightly courted Rogron, who, overwhelmed, masked his excitement as indifference. Sylvie and Celeste Habert were deceived by his manner; Vinet was not.The colonel, courting Sylvie, took pains to look every evening like a party leader: clean shirt, velvet, a new overcoat, a rosette, foregoing smoking. Vinet and the colonel also spread insinuations that Monsieur Habert was sympathetic to liberal ideas; when the bishop heard, Habert was summoned and told to stop visiting the Rogrons—but his sister persisted. The Rogron salon, therefore, became firmly established as a liberal rendezvous.Politics and matrimonial schemes mingled: the Villele ministry fell in 1826; Vinet, who had borrowed to buy land to qualify as a candidate, nearly beat Tiphaine by two votes. The Rogrons’ salon served as Liberal headquarters, frequented by notary Cournant and Doctor Neraud. The Rogrons themselves scarcely understood the extent of their triumph.Bathilde, instructed by Vinet to view Pierrette as an enemy, treated the girl with disdain. Madame Vinet, constrained by her husband’s will and later estranged by the Chargeboeufs’ arrival, could do little for Pierrette. The child often clung to Madame Vinet, asking to be taught embroidery; gently treated, Pierrette could learn and shine. But with the Chargeboeufs installed and Sylvie contemplating marriage, Pierrette was increasingly seen as an obstacle.Nearly fourteen, pallidly lovely from neglected illness, Pierrette’s existence struck Sylvie as a burden. The salon habitués advised turning her into a servant: dismiss Adele and have Pierrette keep house and cook. Pierrette, generous and self-sacrificing, offered willingly to learn the bitter bread of service. Adele was sent away, depriving Pierrette of her only protector.Reduced to the status of a servant in their eyes, she was scolded for the smallest speck of dust. Objects she once admired became odious; no matter how she strove, her cousins found cause to reproach her. In two years she received no praise or kindness; the best she could hope for was simply not being scolded. She bore the morose ill-humor of two celibates—people unfamiliar with tenderness—with angelic patience, daily reminded of her dependence.Such pressure wore on her body as well as her spirit. Secret internal pains began to undermine her strength and halt her development. Slowly, through hidden sufferings and exhaustion, the poor child declined toward the state in which her childhood companion later found her—the fragile, silent figure beneath the morning song.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Before relating the domestic drama Jacques Brigaut’s coming provoked in the Rogron family, it is best to explain how the lad came to be in Provins; he is a relatively mute personage on the scene.When he fled the house Brigaut was not only frightened by Pierrette’s gesture; he was horrified by the change in his little friend. He could scarcely recognize the voice, the eyes, the gestures that had once been lively, gay, and tender. Once at a distance his legs trembled; hot flushes ran down his back. He had seen Pierrette’s shadow, but not herself. Climbing to the Upper town, he found a spot from which he could see the square and the house where Pierrette lived. He gazed at it mournfully, lost in thoughts that tore his heart and revealed to himself the depth of his love for his adopted sister.Passion between children of opposite sexes is rare. Yet Pierrette and he had often protected each other; he had blissfully given her money for her journey and had almost killed himself running after the diligence when she left. Pierrette had known nothing of this; for him the recollection warmed his hard life. For her sake he had struggled to improve himself; he had learned his trade for her and come to Paris to make his fortune. After a fortnight he could not resist seeing her, and walked from Saturday night to Monday morning. He had intended to return to Paris, but the sight of his little friend nailed him to Provins. An unconscious magnetism held him. Tears fell from his eyes when they rose in hers. If to her he was Brittany and childhood, to him she was life itself.At sixteen Brigaut had not yet learned to draw or model a cornice; ignorant in many ways, he had nevertheless earned by piece-work during his apprenticeship some four or five francs a day. On this he could live in Provins and be near Pierrette; he chose the best cabinet-maker in town and meant to learn the rest by working for him, thus keeping watch over his darling.Brigaut’s mind was made up. He went back to Paris, fetched his certificate, tools, and baggage, and three days later was a journeyman in the establishment of Monsieur Frappier, the best cabinet-maker in Provins. Active, steady workmen, not given to junketing, are rare, and masters hold to young men like Brigaut. By the end of the month he was made foreman and was fed and lodged by Frappier, who taught him arithmetic and line drawing. The house and shop were in the Grand’Rue, not a hundred feet from the little square where Pierrette lived.Brigaut buried his love and committed no imprudence. He made Madame Frappier tell him all she knew about the Rogrons, including how their father had laid hands on the property of old Auffray, Pierrette’s grandfather. He obtained other information about the brother and sister. He met Pierrette sometimes in the market with her cousin and shuddered to see the heavy basket she carried. On Sundays he went to church to look for her, dressed in his best clothes. There, for the first time, he became aware that Pierrette was Mademoiselle Lorrain. Pierrette saw him and made a hasty sign to keep out of sight. To him there was a world in that little gesture, as there had been earlier in the sign from her window telling him to run away. Ah! what a fortune he must make in the coming ten years to marry his little friend, to whom, he was told, the Rogrons were to leave their house, a hundred acres of land, and twelve thousand francs a year, not counting their savings!The persevering Breton set about thoroughly educating himself in his trade; for principles of the craft he could learn in Provins as well as Paris and thus remain near Pierrette, to whom he became anxious to explain his projects and the protection he could offer. He was determined to know why she looked pale and why debility was appearing in her eyes; he would also discover the cause of the sufferings that made her seem as if death might take her at any moment. The two signs—the gestures not denying friendship but imploring caution—alarmed him. Evidently Pierrette wished him to wait; otherwise there was danger. As she left the church she gave him one look, and he saw her eyes full of tears. But he could as soon have squared the circle as have guessed what had happened in the Rogrons’ house during the fortnight since his arrival.Pierrette herself had reason to be apprehensive the morning after Brigaut invaded her dreams. She thought Sylvie must have heard the song, or she would not have risen and opened her window; but Pierrette was ignorant of the powerful reasons that made the old maid so alert. For the last eight days strange events and bitter feelings had agitated the minds of the Rogron salon’s chief personages. These hidden matters, carefully concealed by all concerned, were destined to fall like an avalanche on Pierrette. Such secret schemes and calculations lie at the base of great revolutions, whether political, social, or domestic; their subtle significance cannot be rendered by a simple narrative, yet they must be traced to understand what followed.Mademoiselle Habert and Mademoiselle Sylvie were equally desirous of marrying, but Céleste Habert was ten years older and could hope her children would inherit all the Rogron property. Sylvie was forty-two, an age at which marriage is perilous. Céleste, instigated by her vindictive brother the priest, enlightened Sylvie as to the risks. Sylvie trembled; she was terribly afraid of death, an idea that haunts celibates. With the change of ministry the Liberals came to power and their party in Provins took heart: the colonel would be made mayor; the thought appealed to Sylvie; she determined to consult a doctor, though it would expose her.Consulting Monsieur Neraud, the Liberal physician and rival of Martener, would be a blunder. Céleste offered to hide Sylvie in her dressing-room while she herself consulted Monsieur Martener on this delicate matter. Whether Martener was Céleste’s accomplice need not be discovered; at any rate he told Céleste that even at thirty the danger, though slight, did exist. “But,” he added, “with your constitution, you need fear nothing.”“But how about a woman over forty?” asked Céleste.“A married woman who has had children has nothing to fear.”“But I mean an unmarried woman, like Mademoiselle Rogron, for instance?”“Oh, that’s another thing,” said Martener. “Successful childbirth is then one of those miracles God sometimes allows, but rarely.”Céleste pressed him, and the doctor gave a terrifying pathological account: the elasticity of youthful muscles and bones does not exist later, especially in sedentary women. “So you think an unmarried woman ought not to marry after forty?”“Not unless she waits some years,” replied the doctor. “But then, of course, it is not marriage; it is only an association of interests.”The interview’s clinical result was clear: an unmarried woman would make a great mistake marrying after forty. When the doctor departed Céleste found Sylvie in a frightful state, green and yellow, pupils dilated.“Then you really love the colonel?” asked Céleste.“I still hoped,” replied Sylvie.“Well, then, wait!” cried Mademoiselle Habert, Jesuitically, aware that time might rid her of the colonel.Sylvie’s new devotion to the church warned her that the morality of such a marriage might be doubtful. She sounded her conscience in the confessional. The stern priest explained Church opinions, which see marriage primarily as propagation and rebuke second marriages and all passion without social purpose. Sylvie’s perplexities were great. These internal struggles gave extraordinary force to her passion, investing it with the attraction that forbidden desire often holds. Mademoiselle Rogron’s perturbation did not escape the lynx-eyed lawyer.One evening, after the game had ended, Vinet approached Sylvie, took her hand, and led her to a sofa.“Something troubles you,” he said.She nodded sadly. He let the others depart; Rogron walked home with the Chargeboeufs, and when Vinet was alone with the old maid he wormed the truth from her.“Cleverly played, abbé!” he thought. “But you’ve played into my hands.”The foxy lawyer, more decisive even than the doctor, advised marriage in ten years. Inwardly he vowed the whole Rogron fortune should go to Bathilde. He rubbed his hands and hurried home. The influence exercised by Monsieur Habert, physician of the soul, and by Vinet, doctor of the purse, balanced each other. Rogron had little piety; the black-robed pair were fairly matched.On discovering Céleste’s victory in her anxiety to marry Rogron over Sylvie, the lawyer saw his chance to drive the colonel from the field. He knew Rogron well enough to be certain he could marry him to Bathilde; Jérôme had already inwardly succumbed to her charms, and Vinet knew the first time the pair were alone the marriage would be settled. Rogron had reached the point of keeping his eyes fixed on Céleste to avoid looking at Bathilde. Vinet had learned Sylvie’s secret and saw the force of her love for the colonel. He fully understood the struggle of such passion in an old maid gripped by religion and saw a way to rid himself of Pierrette and the colonel alike by making each the cause of the other’s overthrow.The next day, after court, Vinet met the colonel and Rogron walking their daily walk. Whenever the three men were seen in company the town talked. This triumvirate was a pride to the Liberals. Vinet, sole editor of the “Courrier,” was head of the party; the colonel, its arm; Rogron, by his purse, its nerves. When Rogron turned to go home, Vinet stopped the colonel by taking Gouraud’s arm.“Well, colonel,” he said, “I am going to take a fearful load off your shoulders; you can do better than marry Sylvie; if you play your cards properly you can marry that little Pierrette in two years’ time.”He then related the Jesuit’s manoeuvre and its effect on Sylvie.“What a skulking trick!” cried the colonel; “and spreading over years, too!”“Colonel,” said Vinet gravely, “Pierrette is a charming creature; with her you can be happy for the rest of your life; your health is so sound that the difference in ages won’t seem disproportionate. But you mustn’t think it easy to change a dreadful fate to a pleasant one. To turn a woman who loves you into a friend and confidant is as perilous as crossing a river under fire. You must study the position and manoeuvre your forces with the same wisdom you have displayed and which has won us our present standing. If I get to be attorney-general you shall command the department. Oh! if you had been an elector we should be further advanced; I would have bought votes by threatening men with the loss of their places, and we should have had a majority.”The colonel had long thought about Pierrette but concealed his feelings with utmost dissimulation. His roughness to the child was only a mask. She could not understand why the man who claimed to be her father’s old comrade usually treated her so ill when sometimes, if alone with her, he would chuck her under the chin and give a friendly kiss. After Vinet’s conversation about Sylvie’s fears, Gouraud began to seek opportunities to find Pierrette alone; the rough colonel made himself as soft as a cat. He told her how brave her father was and what a misfortune it had been that she lost him.A few days before Brigaut’s arrival Sylvie had suddenly come upon Gouraud and Pierrette talking together. Jealousy rushed into her heart with monastic violence. Jealousy, credulous and suspicious, gives fancy freedom but not intelligence; it filled her with fantastic ideas. When she heard Brigaut’s ditty a few mornings later she jumped to the conclusion that the man who had used the words “Madam’ le mariée,” addressing them to Pierrette, must be the colonel. She was certain, for she had noticed a change in his manners for a week. He was the only man who, in her solitary life, had ever paid her attention. Consequently she watched him with all her eyes and mind; by hope and despair she brought the matter to enormous proportions and saw all things in a mental mirage. By dint of staring she saw nothing. Alternately she repelled, admitted, and conquered the idea of rivalry. She compared herself with Pierrette: forty-two with gray hair, versus Pierrette delicately fair with soft eyes. She had heard men of fifty apt to love young girls of that kind. Before the colonel came to the house Sylvie had heard in the Tiphaines’ salon strange stories of his life and morals. Old maids preserve exaggerated Platonic sentiments; they hold to fixed doctrines like those with little experience of life. The mere thought of being jilted by the colonel was torture to Sylvie. She lay in bed going over her desires, Pierrette’s conduct, and the song that had awakened her with the word “marriage.” Foolishly, instead of looking through the blinds to see the lover, she opened her window without reflecting that Pierrette would hear her. If she had had the instinct of a spy she would have seen Brigaut, and the fatal drama begun would never have taken place.It was Pierrette’s duty, weak as she was, to unbar and open the wooden shutters of the kitchen, then the glass door from corridor to garden. She fetched various brooms and utensils with a care and particularity no servant, not even a Dutchwoman, gives her work. She hated reproof. Happiness for her was seeing her cousin’s cold blue eyes—not satisfied but calm—after glancing about with the look of an owner. Pierrette’s skin was moist with labor when she returned to put the kitchen in order and light the stove so she might carry up hot water to her two cousins (a luxury she never had), and to prepare the dining-room fire. For these she fetched wood from the cellar, leaving warm rooms for damp air; such sudden transitions, made quickly to escape harsh words, aggravated her condition. She did not know she was ill, yet she suffered. She began to have strange cravings; she liked raw vegetables and salads and ate them secretly. The innocent child was unaware her condition was serious and required utmost care. If Neraud, the Rogrons’ doctor, had told this to Pierrette before Brigaut’s coming she would have only smiled; life was so bitter she could smile at death. But now her feelings changed; the child, whose physical suffering was increased by Breton homesickness, was suddenly content to be in Provins. The sight of that yellow flower, the song, the presence of her friend revived her like rain on a parched plant. Unconsciously she wanted to live and even thought she did not suffer.Pierrette slipped timidly into her cousin’s bedroom, made the fire, left the hot water, said a few words, and went to wake Rogron and do the same for him. Then she went down to take in the milk, bread, and other provisions. She stood some time on the door-sill hoping Brigaut would come; by then he was already on his way to Paris.She had finished arranging the dining-room and was busy in the kitchen when she heard Sylvie coming down. Mademoiselle Rogron appeared in a brown silk dressing-gown with a cap, false front awry, night-gown showing above the silk, slippers down at heel. She cast an eye about and came straight to Pierrette, who awaited orders.“Ha! here you are, lovesick young lady!” said Sylvie mockingly.“What is it, cousin?”“You came into my room like a sly cat and crept out the same way, though you knew I had something to say to you.”“To me?”“You had a serenade this morning, as if you were a princess.”“A serenade!” exclaimed Pierrette.“A serenade!” mimicked Sylvie. “And you’ve a lover, too.”“What is a lover, cousin?”Sylvie avoided answering. “Do you dare tell me a man did not come under your window and talk to you of marriage?”Persecution had taught Pierrette the wariness of slaves; she answered bravely: “I don’t know what you mean—”“Who means?—your dog?” said Sylvie sharply.“I should have said ‘cousin,’” replied the girl humbly.“And didn’t you get up and go in your bare feet to the window?—which will give you an illness; and perhaps you didn’t talk to your lover, either?”“No, cousin.”“You have many faults, but I did not think you told lies. You will have to explain this affair to your cousin and to me, or I shall take severe measures.”The old maid, exasperated with jealousy and curiosity, meant to frighten the girl. Pierrette, who endured more than she had strength for, kept silence. Silence is the weapon by which such victims sometimes conquer; it baffles envy’s charges and suspicion’s skirmishings and is absolute. Sylvie watched Pierrette narrowly. The girl colored, but the color came out in patches on her cheekbones—burning and significant spots. A mother would have changed tone at once, taken the child on her lap, and questioned her; she would have seen the signs of weakness and known that digestive disturbance and other infirmities threatened the lungs. But an old maid who never awakened family instincts or knew the precautions required for adolescence had neither indulgence nor the compassionate intelligence of a mother; Pierrette’s sufferings only hardened her.“She blushes, she is guilty!” thought Sylvie.Pierrette’s silence thus was turned to her injury.“Pierrette,” Sylvie continued, “before your cousin comes down we must have some talk together. Come,” she said softer, “shut the street door; if any one comes they will ring and we shall hear them.”In spite of the damp mist rising from the river, Sylvie took Pierrette along the winding gravel path across the lawn to the rock terrace—an ornamental quay covered with iris and aquatic plants. She changed tactics, thinking firmness might catch Pierrette tripping; the hyena became a cat.“Pierrette,” she said, “you are no longer a child; you are nearly fifteen; it is not surprising you should have a lover.”“But, cousin,” said Pierrette, raising her eyes with angelic sweetness to Sylvie’s cold face, “what is a lover?”It would have been impossible for Sylvie to define a lover truthfully and decently. Instead of seeing adorable innocence, she considered the question insincere.“A lover, Pierrette, is a man who loves us and wishes to marry us.”“Ah,” said Pierrette, “when that happens in Brittany we call the young man a suitor.”“Well, remember that in owning your feelings for a man you do no wrong, my dear. The wrong is in hiding them. Have you pleased some of the men who visit here?”“No.”“Do you love any of them?”“No.”“Certain?”“Quite certain.”“Look at me, Pierrette.”Pierrette looked.“A man called to you this morning in the square.”Pierrette lowered her eyes.“You went to your window, you opened it, and you spoke to him.”“No cousin, I went to look out and I saw a peasant.”“Pierrette, you have much improved since your first communion; you have become pious and obedient, you love God and your relations; I am satisfied with you. I don’t say this to puff you up with pride.”The horrible creature had mistaken submission and the silence of wretchedness for virtues.Pierrette raised grateful eyes to her cousin, feeling she could almost forgive the suffering she had caused.“But if it is all hypocrisy, if I find you a serpent that I have warmed in my bosom, you will be a wicked girl, an infamous creature!”“I think I have nothing to reproach myself with,” said Pierrette, painfully shocked by the sudden change from praise to hyena tones.“You know that to lie is a mortal sin?”“Yes, cousin.”“Well, you are now under the eye of God,” said the old maid, with a solemn gesture to the sky; “swear to me that you did not know that peasant.”“I will not swear,” said Pierrette.“Ha! he was no peasant, you little viper.”Pierrette rushed away like a frightened fawn. Sylvie called her in a dreadful voice.“The bell is ringing,” she answered.“Artful wretch!” thought Sylvie. “She is depraved in mind; now I am certain the little adder has wound herself round the colonel. She has heard us say he was a baron. To be a baroness! little fool! Ah! I’ll get rid of her; I’ll apprentice her out, and soon too!”Sylvie was so lost in thought she did not notice her brother coming down the path lamenting the frost’s injury to his dahlias.“Sylvie! what are you thinking about? I thought you were looking at the fish; sometimes they jump out of the water.”“No,” said Sylvie.“How did you sleep?” he began to tell his dreams. “Don’t you think my skin is getting tabid?”—a Rogron word.Ever since Rogron had desired to marry Mademoiselle de Chargeboeuf he had been uneasy about himself and his health. At this moment Pierrette came down and called that breakfast was ready. At sight of her, Sylvie’s skin turned green and yellow; her bile was in commotion. She looked at the corridor floor and declared Pierrette ought to rub it.“I will rub it now if you wish,” said the little angel, unaware of the injury such work may do a girl.The dining-room was irreproachably in order. Sylvie pretended through breakfast to want this and that—which she would never have thought to ask in a quieter mood—only to make Pierrette rise again and again just as she began to eat. But mere teasing was not enough; she wanted fault and was angry with herself for not finding one. She scarcely answered her brother’s remarks yet kept her eyes on him and avoided Pierrette. Pierrette, deeply conscious, brought milk mixed with cream in a large silver goblet after heating it in a bain-marie. The cousins poured coffee. When Sylvie carefully prepared hers she saw an atom of coffee-grounds floating on the surface. On this the storm broke.“What is the matter?” asked Rogron.“The matter is that mademoiselle has put dust in my milk. Do you suppose I am going to drink coffee with ashes in it? Well, I am not surprised; no one can do two things at once. She wasn’t thinking of the milk! a blackbird might have flown through the kitchen today and she wouldn’t have seen it! and then it was my coffee, ha! that didn’t signify!”As she spoke she laid aside the coffee-grounds that had run through the filter.“But, cousin, that is coffee,” said Pierrette.“Oh! then it is I who tell lies, is it?” cried Sylvie, blasting her with a fearful flash from her eyes.Organizations not exhausted by emotion often have much vital fluid at their service. This extreme clearness of eye in anger was marked in Mademoiselle Rogron because she often exercised it in her shop to inspire dependents with fear.“You had better dare to give me the lie!” continued Sylvie; “you deserve to be sent from the table to eat by yourself in the kitchen.”“What’s the matter with you two?” cried Rogron, “you are as cross as bears this morning.”“Mademoiselle knows what I have against her,” said Sylvie. “I leave her to make up her mind before speaking to you; for I mean to show her more kindness than she deserves.”Pierrette looked out the window to avoid her cousin’s eyes, which frightened her. “Look at her! she pays no attention to what I am saying than if I were that sugar-basin! And yet mademoiselle has a sharp ear; she can hear and answer from the top of the house when some one talks to her from below. She is perversity itself,—perversity, I say; and you needn’t expect any good of her; do you hear me, Jérôme?”“What has she done wrong?” asked Rogron.“At her age, too! to begin so young!” screamed Sylvie.Pierrette rose to clear the table to give herself something to do; she could hardly bear the scene. Though such language was not new, she had never grown used to it. Her cousin’s rage seemed to accuse her of a crime. She imagined what fury would be if they knew about Brigaut; perhaps they would send him away and she would lose him. All the rapid thoughts of a slave came to her, and she resolved to keep absolute silence about a circumstance her conscience told her was innocent. But the cruel words and wounding suspicion so shocked her that as she reached the kitchen she was taken with a convulsion of the stomach and turned deadly sick. She dared not complain; she was not sure anyone would help her. When she returned to the dining-room she was white as a sheet and, saying she was not well, went to bed, dragging herself up step by step by the baluster and thinking she was going to die. “Poor Brigaut!” she thought.“The girl is ill,” said Rogron.“She ill! That’s only shamming,” replied Sylvie loudly that Pierrette might hear. “She was well enough this morning, I can tell you.”This last blow struck Pierrette to the earth; she went to bed weeping and praying to God to take her out of this world.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For a month Rogron had stopped bringing the “Constitutionnel” to Gouraud; the colonel came punctually to fetch his paper, to gossip, and, weather permitting, to take Rogron for a walk. Sylvie dressed with as much coquetry as she could—hopeful of seeing the colonel and able to question him. She fancied herself handsome in a green gown, yellow shawl with red border, and a white bonnet with gray feathers. Around the hour the colonel usually came, she stationed herself in the salon with her brother, keeping him at home in dressing-gown and slippers.“It is a fine day, colonel,” Rogron said when Gouraud entered. “But I’m not dressed; my sister wished to go out; wait for me—I'll be ready soon.” Rogron left Sylvie alone with the colonel.“You are dressed divinely,” observed Gouraud, noting a certain solemnity on the pock-marked face of the old maid.“I wanted to go out, but my little cousin is ill and had to go to bed.”Gouraud’s distrust of Vinet was constant. Vinet controlled the paper, took the revenues, and reigned as master while the colonel, the responsible editor, earned little. Vinet and Cournant had done the Rogrons many services; Gouraud was a powerless half-pay colonel. Vinet would be the deputy, the leader consulted by the liberals. Moreover, the colonel knew of Rogron’s ardent passion for Bathilde de Chargeboeuf; Rogron’s hopes were intense though hidden. When Gouraud had hinted he might propose to Bathilde, Rogron had turned pale and since then shown him coldness and even hatred.Thus Vinet reigned in the Rogron household; the colonel had only the precarious tie of a mendacious affection for Sylvie. When the lawyer told him of the priest’s machinations and advised breaking with Sylvie to marry Pierrette, it flattered Gouraud’s vanity—but the colonel suspected Vinet intended to separate him from Sylvie and, by exploiting her fears, throw the Rogron property into Mademoiselle de Chargeboeuf’s hands.Left alone with Sylvie, the colonel detected her uneasiness; she had planned to see him alone. Suspecting Vinet, he went on guard. He never believed a woman’s word; when Sylvie said Pierrette had gone to bed before midday, he concluded jealousy and secrecy. He tried to sound casual.“She is getting to be quite pretty, that little thing,” he said.“She will be pretty,” Sylvie replied.“You ought to send her to Paris and put her in a shop,” the colonel suggested. “She would make her fortune. Milliners all want pretty girls.”“Is that your advice?” Sylvie asked, uneasy.“Good!” thought the colonel—Vinet had set him to propose Pierrette to spoil his chances with Sylvie. He spoke louder: Bathilde was noble and well-connected but poor; Pierrette had nothing and would never marry. Beauty was common and silly to him; at forty-eight he preferred comfort and a woman to care for him—he did not want children. Sylvie’s face betrayed her, and when she asked, “Then you don’t love Pierrette?” he laughed, denying it.Sylvie, reassured, feigned shrewdness by attributing the marriage plan to her brother. The colonel, seeing an opening, declared his real wish: he had long chosen Sylvie for her good qualities and desired no wife but her. He took her hand and kissed it.“But if I want you to marry Pierrette? If I leave her my fortune?” Sylvie asked.“No,” he said, fearing a mismatch in age and future humiliations from younger men courting a young wife; he preferred not to marry someone who would cause him misery.Sylvie appeared to smile, though in truth grim-faced. Rogron arrived; the colonel bowed to the old maid in a lover-like way. Gouraud resolved to press his suit, to make himself master of the house and, through influence over Sylvie during the honeymoon, to rid the household of Bathilde and Céleste Habert. On their walk he told Rogron he had been joking before and had no real intention toward Bathilde; he confessed his wishes, declaring he longed to be Rogron’s brother-in-law.“If nothing is wanting but my consent you have it,” cried Rogron, delighted to be rid of Bathilde as a rival.Sylvie spent the morning imagining how to arrange the household—an upper floor for her brother and the rest furnished for herself and her husband. True to old-maid suspicion, she resolved to test the colonel’s heart and morals and ensure Pierrette had no private intercourse with him.Pierrette came down before dinner to lay the table. Sylvie, forced to cook, swore at the “cursed Pierrette” for a grease spot on her gown—proof, she thought, that Pierrette hadn’t done her work herself.“Oh, here you are, peeking? You are like the dog of the marshal who woke up as soon as the saucepans rattled. You want us to think you are ill, you little liar!” Sylvie snapped, using the earlier incident in the square as proof that Pierrette lied in everything.To Pierrette’s astonishment Sylvie sent her to dress in her best clothes after dinner; suspicion made Sylvie invent reasons to keep Pierrette close to judge the colonel’s truthfulness.That evening the Chargeboeuf ladies arrived first. On Vinet’s advice Bathilde dressed elaborately—blue velveteen, transparent fichu, garnet pendants, ringlets—displaying queenly manners and girlish coquetry aimed at Rogron. Her mother shared an aristocratic insolence. Bathilde had intelligence; Vinet, during their two-month stay at his house, had discovered and fed her mind, inflaming her contempt for a world that prized money over beauty. Vinet told her she could use marriage to gain position and influence: marry Rogron, make him deputy, and, through political maneuvering, secure promotion—then, if fortunes changed, she could marry up later. He spoke of women as instruments who advanced men like post-horses.Vinet, who visited nightly with Madame and Mademoiselle de Chargeboeuf, had transformed his appearance into that of a man of consequence—gold spectacles, silk waistcoat, white cravat, a watch—shedding his former bitterness for a sly, agreeable perfidy.“Good-evening, my dear; how are you?” Madame de Chargeboeuf greeted Sylvie.Bathilde went to the fireplace, took off her bonnet, looked in the glass, and placed her foot on the fender for Rogron’s admiration.“What is the matter with you?” she said to him. “You have not bowed to me. Pray why should we put on our best velvet gowns to please you?” She pushed past Pierrette, who took her hat as if she were a servant. The disdain of a young woman who feels superior was intolerable to Pierrette. Bathilde’s “Thank you, mademoiselle,” to Pierrette was a small performance of superiority: Bathilde was tall, wealthy, and proud; Pierrette small, weak, living on charity.“Good-evening, little girl,” Madame de Chargeboeuf said with condescending grandeur.Vinet added insult by fixing his gaze on Pierrette and exclaiming in mock admiration how fine they were that night.“Fine!” the poor child replied. “You should say that to Mademoiselle de Chargeboeuf, not to me.”Vinet turned to Rogron, grasping his hand. “Isn’t she always beautifully dressed?” he asked.“Yes,” Rogron said.Bathilde teased Rogron, standing before him. He looked at her and closed his eyes like a cat half-pleased.“You are too beautiful,” he murmured. “Too dangerous.”“Why?” she asked. Rogron grew silent and looked at the fire. At that moment Mademoiselle Habert entered, followed by the colonel.Céleste Habert, now the enemy of the Rogrons, could count only on Sylvie; yet everyone present showed her civility because each was undermining her in their own way. Her brother, though no longer in action, knew what was happening and, seeing his sister’s hopes thwarted, became an implacable adversary of the Rogrons.Mademoiselle Habert resembled a schoolmistress: caps, stern eye, grim mouth, strings of her cap limp and faded; two brown moles on her chin bore wiry hairs she let grow; she took snuff ungracefully. She had that posture of women who sit on their hips rather than chairs; she would turn her whole bust when spoken to. Her presence completed the company.They set about a game of boston. Mademoiselle Habert sat opposite Sylvie; the colonel sat beside her, opposite Madame de Chargeboeuf. Bathilde was near her mother and Rogron. Sylvie placed Pierrette between herself and the colonel. Rogron had set a second card-table in case more guests came. Two lamps, candelabra, and candles of cheap price lit the tables.“Come, Pierrette, take your work, my dear,” said Sylvie with treacherous softness, noticing the girl watching the colonel’s play. Pierrette took her embroidery but kept her eyes on Gouraud. Gouraud behaved as if unaware. Sylvie noticed his apparent indifference and found it suspicious. Presently she undertook a grande misere in hearts, the pool full of counters and twenty-seven sous. More company arrived, including deputy-judge Desfondrilles, who had lately shifted political alliances toward the Vinets. He stood before the chimney with his coat tails over his arms, watching Mademoiselle de Chargeboeuf shine in the salon’s reds meant to enhance her.“Play that,” Pierrette said to the colonel, pointing to a heart. The colonel began a sequence in hearts; he won Sylvie’s ace, though protected by five small hearts.“That’s not fair!” Sylvie cried. “Pierrette saw my hand and the colonel took her advice.”“But, mademoiselle,” Céleste said, “the colonel’s game was to play hearts after you began them.”The scene amused Desfondrilles. “Yes, it was the colonel’s game,” said Cournant the notary, though he didn’t grasp the quarrel.Sylvie shot a feline, cruel look at Mademoiselle Habert. “Pierrette, you did see my hand,” she accused, fixing on the girl.“No, cousin,” Pierrette answered.“I was looking at you,” said the deputy-judge, “and I can swear Pierrette saw no one’s hand but the colonel’s.”“Pooh!” Gouraud said, alarmed. “Little girls know how to slide their eyes into everything.”“Ah!” Sylvie exclaimed.“Yes,” Gouraud persisted. “I dare say she looked into your hand to play you a trick. Didn’t you, little one?”“No,” the truthful Breton said. “I wouldn’t do such a thing; if I had, it would have been for my cousin’s interests.”“You are a story-teller and a little fool,” Sylvie cried. “After what happened this morning do you suppose I can believe a word you say? You are a—” She did not finish her insult; Pierrette, fearing a torrent, fled without a light and ran to her room. Sylvie, hearing her head strike the open passage door, cried, “Good! I’m glad of it.”“She must be hurt,” said Desfondrilles.“She deserves it,” Sylvie replied.“It was a bad blow,” said Mademoiselle Habert.Sylvie thought she might escape paying her misere if she went to check on Pierrette, but Madame de Chargeboeuf stopped her. “Pay us first,” she laughed; “you will forget it when you come back.” The remark alluded to Sylvie’s notorious bad faith at cards and was approved by the others. Sylvie sat and forgot Pierrette—an indifference that surprised no one. When the game ended around half past nine, she flung herself into an easy chair by the fire and did not even rise as guests departed. The colonel’s behavior tormented her; she did not know what to think.“Men are so false!” she cried as she went to bed.Pierrette had struck a blow on the head just above the ear, at the spot where girls part their hair; the next day there was a swelling.“God has punished you,” Sylvie said at breakfast. “You disobeyed me; you treated me with disrespect in leaving before I had finished; you got what you deserved.”“Nevertheless,” Rogron suggested, “she ought to put on a compress of salt and water.”“Oh, it is nothing at all, cousin,” Pierrette said. The poor child had reached the point where even such a remark seemed to her a proof of kindness.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The week ended as it had begun: continual torment. Sylvie grew more inventive in her cruelty. Pierrette, too proud and stubborn to complain, bore vague and acute pains alone, thinking always of Brigaut — fearing for him if discovered, comforted that he was nearby in Provins. The single glimpse she had caught of him lingered like the exile’s look at his country or the martyr’s look to heaven.Brigaut, moved by the same hunger to communicate, contrived a simple plan: Pierrette should lower letters on a string from her window at a set hour. In the market one morning he managed, with coolness belying his excitement, to slip a note to her while pretending to buy fruit. Pierrette hid it in her apron, cheeks aflame. These two children, scarcely aware of the depth of their feelings, carried more emotion between them than many adult lovers. Sylvie, who did not notice Brigaut’s Breton accent, paid him no heed.That night Pierrette read Brigaut’s letter, in which he begged her to let down a string at midnight so they could exchange notes, called her the victim of “monsters,” invoked their Breton past, vowed to earn enough to take her back home, said he had 150 francs and could send money by the string, and begged her to pray with him in church. The letter filled Pierrette with hope; it was a balm in her misery.After much courage she went to the dining-room to steal pen, paper, and ink, and wrote to him in return. Her letter was a simple outpouring: she declared him and her grandmother the only people she loved, described the brutal treatment she suffered — made to scrub pots, to do work that marred her hands, scolded and treated like a dog — and begged him to rescue her and return her to Pen-Hoël, to the only happy memories of sliding on the pond and the “big pong.” She confessed bad pains in her head and body, hid her tears, and asked if he would love her always. She weighted her note with a crust of bread and lowered it at midnight. Brigaut broke the string to take her letter and then lingered beneath her window; they signaled to one another in the candlelight for over an hour before parting.Next day Pierrette rose transformed — light, joyful — a change Sylvie observed with the sharp jealousy of an old maid. At dinner she accused the girl of feigning illness or having a lover, ordered her to sit in the dining-room with a candle, and watched her closely. Pierrette endured; Brigaut’s promised nightly visits kept her alive. One midnight Brigaut left a new note explaining a signal — an owl-cry he had learned from his father — and said he would return in a few days with good news, even hinting at measures to liberate her. Brigaut also wrote secretly to Madame Lorrain in Nantes, begging her to come for her granddaughter and to justify herself against the rumor that she had taken Pierrette’s money; Brigaut feared the grandmother might be dead.Pierrette clung to Brigaut’s assurances as to a desert traveler seeing a palm-tree at last. But Sylvie had grown suspicious. She had seen a figure under the window and then, alarmed, discovered Pierrette’s cord and began spying as relentlessly as any customs officer. Pierrette, foreseeing danger, sewed the letters into her corset and covered them. That seemed to end the lovers’ nightly meetings.Sylvie spent three nights awake, then sent Pierrette to confession and searched the garret, finding nothing; her resentment hardened into a cruel watchfulness. Pierrette’s pains grew worse; she developed a regular fever. One Sunday evening, amid a salon game, Pierrette fainted. The colonel caught and carried her to a sofa. Sylvie sneered that it was a trick, and in front of guests forced Pierrette to unfasten her gown; when Sylvie felt papers in the corset she pretended outrage, calling the girl a hypocrite. Afterwards she detained Vinet, a lawyer and an ally, and revealed through him her determination never to marry the colonel. Vinet, pragmatic and selfish, advised Sylvie to secure power and property by other means: find a rich wife for Rogron, or force bargains that would leave Pierrette with nothing. He hinted that the colonel was a gambler whose recklessness would ruin him unless he married a fortune. This conversation planted schemes of revenge and deprivation in Sylvie’s mind.That night, three owl-like cries echoed in the square. Pierrette, feverish, opened her window at the signal and lowered a ball of silk. Sylvie, awake and frantic, heard the noise, raced upstairs, and surprised Pierrette unfastening the cord. A violent struggle followed. Pierrette clenched the letter in her hand; Sylvie grabbed and twisted the child’s arm, dug her nails in, and — in a frenzy — even bit the wrist, trying to force the hand open. Pierrette screamed, “Help! help! they are murdering me!” The scene was a grotesque contest between stoic innocence and savage jealousy; Pierrette’s firm resistance was like a martyr’s. Exhausted and bleeding, she cried aloud.At that instant loud knocks sounded at the front door. Rogron, alarmed, opened; Brigaut rushed in, followed by a tall gray woman — Pierrette’s grandmother — whose sudden appearance changed everything. Seeing the corset Sylvie had already torn, and the bloody hand, the old woman carried Pierrette away in her arms to Frappier’s cabinet-maker’s house nearby, where Brigaut and the Frappiers improvised a bed. Pierrette fainted; the letter fell from her loosened fingers.The grandmother’s presence cowed Sylvie. She had come from Pen-Hoël, having received Brigaut’s urgent letter. The old Breton woman — solemn, with a spare, parchment face and glittering old eyes — felt in an instant what Pierrette had endured. She swore, knelt upon the bricks in a vow, and demanded that Brigaut fetch the best doctor in Paris. Producing banknotes — forty-two thousand francs recently given her by a repentant creditor, Monsieur Collinet, to reimburse past losses — she offered funds for immediate care. Brigaut rode off by diligence for Bianchon in Paris, as Martener, the local doctor, suggested.Madame Lorrain’s story must be briefly told. Living in Saint-Jacques in Nantes after heavy family losses, she had lately received a sudden reparation from Monsieur Collinet, who, after making his fortune in America, restored both principal and interest totalling about forty-two thousand francs to her late husband’s creditors and added to pay some remaining debts. She planned to fetch Pierrette and marry her to Brigaut. It was upon reading Pierrette’s letter enclosed with Brigaut’s that she set out at once.Back at Frappier’s, Martener examined Pierrette and found an abscess above the left ear; the bruised hand could not have been self-inflicted. He urged full disclosure of the maltreatment and awaited the Paris physician’s arrival while prescribing a quieting draught: Pierrette needed sleep. Left to her grandmother, the child told everything — the endless scolding, the heavy labor, the insults, the scalded and injured hands, the hunger for the Breton home — all the details that would later serve in a judicial inquiry. The grandmother’s knowledge of Pierrette’s suffering and her promises of security soothed the girl.Meanwhile Sylvie, stunned and perplexed by the grandmother’s sudden might and by Brigaut’s letters which she had read after ripping open the corset, slept in uneasy bewilderment, not foreseeing where her spite would lead. The colonel, meanwhile, intercepted by Vinet’s schemes and threatened by the prospect of political and marital entanglements, found himself at odds with those around him. Vinet and Sylvie plotted to secure property and influence; the colonel, warned and enraged, demanded a rich wife before elections or threatened consequences.At Frappier’s Pierrette lay half-dead, her fingers unbent and bloodied, while those who loved her — Brigaut, Madame Lorrain, the Frappiers — stood silent. The grandmother, who had fetched the money that would make rescue possible, clasped her hands and resolved to complain to the authorities and to secure proper care. Monsieur Martener, convinced this was no mere youthful pretence, prepared to consult with the best surgeon available. Brigaut had already left for Paris to fetch Bianchon.Thus the little drama — love, spying, cruelty, and rescue — reached a decisive moment. Pierrette, exhausted by the physical consequences of her courage, sank into a sleep made safe by her grandmother’s presence and a soothing draught. The old woman watched her with a reverent tenderness, kissing the forehead, hair, and hands of the child she had come to reclaim, while the others, stunned and ashamed, anticipated the legal and moral reckonings to come.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################At nine that morning Monsieur Martener told Monsieur Tiphaine of Pierrette’s plight: the scene between Pierrette and Sylvie, the physical and moral torments inflicted by the Rogrons, and the two serious ailments their cruelty had produced. Tiphaine summoned Auffray the notary, a maternal relative of Pierrette’s.The affair inflamed an existing feud between the Vinet and Tiphaine parties. Rogron supporters had been spreading scandalous rumors about Madame Tiphaine’s mother and du Tillet’s involvement with the family, and private grievances in Provins quickly turned into partisan controversies. Tiphaine saw Pierrette’s case as a chance to humiliate the Rogrons, who hosted the salon where anti-monarchist plans and an opposition journal had been born. The public prosecutor was informed; in a private conference Auffray was chosen as guardian so he could call a Family Council and, with the testimony of three doctors, demand Pierrette’s release from the Rogrons. Lesourd, the procureur, would steer the matter toward a criminal inquiry.By midday the town buzzed. Pierrette’s faint cries from the previous night became horror as a crowd gathered at Frappier’s shop; Frappier recounted her arrival with broken fingers and a bloody hand. Doctor Bianchon’s post-chaise arrived about one; Martener and the hospital surgeon were summoned. The Rogrons were accused of deliberate ill-treatment that had nearly killed their cousin.At the law courts Vinet hurried to the Rogrons. Sylvie, evasive about the night’s events, admitted nothing until pressed; under questioning she confessed jealousy and, painfully, that her suspicions were baseless. Vinet exploited their fear, insisting on a plausible public motive for his defense. They contrived Rogron’s immediate marriage to Mademoiselle de Chargeboeuf—announcing it that day and posting banns Sunday. Sylvie agreed to appear in the marriage contract as assigning her capital to her brother while retaining income; Vinet urged antedating the contract to bind the Chargeboeufs publicly and preserve their visits. He promised to defend them if they signed.By noon Vinet had full authority to draw the contract and represent the Rogrons. At twelve a petition was filed against Brigaut and the widow Lorrain for abducting the minor Pierrette from her guardian’s house—turning the defenders into complainants. The judge postponed the hearing until four; Tiphaine wanted Auffray present with the doctors’ report.The marriage announcement and Sylvie’s sacrifices alienated two supporters, Mademoiselle Habert and Colonel Gouraud, who publicly stayed with the Rogrons but intended to hurt them. When Martener mentioned Pierrette’s head injury, Céleste and the colonel recounted a blow Pierrette had inflicted on herself the evening she was forced to leave the salon and quoted Mademoiselle Rogron’s heartless remarks. Vinet had expected such opposition, but having secured the Rogrons’ fortune for Mademoiselle de Chargeboeuf he planned to use that alliance to dominate Provins socially and politically.From midday to four the Tiphaine circle watched over Pierrette. Unaware of the tumult, she found consolation in being with her grandmother and Brigaut. Bianchon protested vehemently at such barbarity and insisted all town physicians examine the case; Dr. Neraud, a Rogron friend, attended. The unanimous report explained Pierrette’s malady—a sadly common but horrifying condition—and attributed it to ill-treatment.At four President Tiphaine took his seat. Madame Lorrain, Auffray, Brigaut and others entered the court; Vinet alone represented the Rogrons. He claimed two strangers had forced entry into the Rogron house and abducted the minor. Auffray, as surrogate guardian, presented the physicians’ report—signed by a Paris authority and all Provins doctors—asserting that Pierrette’s near-fatal condition resulted from ill-treatment by Rogron and his sister. Auffray asked that Pierrette not be returned to the guardian’s household but remain with a family member designated by the judge and proposed convening a Family Council to consider deposing the guardian.Vinet objected that he had not seen the report and could not rebut it. The judge, noting the report might have been submitted to the procureur du roi, heard the case anyway. Tiphaine forbade returning Pierrette to her guardian until the Family Council met, commissioned the attending physician and the hospital surgeon to inspect her, and stipulated that if her injuries were alarming the criminal courts would intervene, without prejudice to Auffray’s civil suit.Vinet scoffed, calling it fuss over a girl supposedly involved with an apprentice, then left to rally his faction. He argued the Tiphaines were making an unjust spectacle of Rogron, portrayed Sylvie’s intervention as natural for a jealous sister seizing letters, and framed the dispute as partisan.Frappier’s house was later judged unsuitable for Pierrette; she was moved to Auffray’s with conspicuous care. Laid on a stretcher, accompanied by a Gray Sister and her grandmother, she passed under many windows, her pallor strengthening Auffray’s case. At Auffray’s she was installed on the second floor with Martener and the hospital surgeon attending; she was tenderly cared for.That evening exaggerations grew on both sides. The Rogron salon filled as Vinet rallied Liberals; the Chargeboeuf ladies dined there to sign the contract. Vinet downplayed the Pierrette episode, confident royal courts would vindicate them; some accepted the Chargeboeuf alliance as wiping the Rogrons’ slate clean and depicting Pierrette as depraved. In Madame Tiphaine’s salon, vengeance for past slanders was taken out on the Rogrons, now cast as monsters. Journalists offered opposing accounts: one article claimed no case existed; the Rogron paper hesitated, advising patience for the law.The Family Council, chosen by the juge de paix, included Rogron and the two Auffrays as nearest relatives, Monsieur Ciprey (a maternal nephew), Mademoiselle Habert, Pierrette’s confessor, and Colonel Gouraud. The judge’s choice seemed impartial—Habert and Gouraud were considered Rogron friends. Rogron requested Vinet for the council, postponing it to late December when Tiphaine would be in Paris—a delay tactic by Vinet.Vinet spoke for three hours before the council, attempting to prove an intrigue between Pierrette and Brigaut to justify Sylvie’s severity, and portraying the guardian’s delegation of duties as natural. Despite his efforts, the council unanimously removed Rogron’s guardianship; Auffray became guardian and Ciprey surrogate. Testimony included Adele the servant against her former masters, Mademoiselle Habert recounting Mademoiselle Rogron’s cruel remarks, and Brigaut’s letter from Pierrette describing her ill-treatment. The council concluded Pierrette’s illness resulted from neglect by the guardian, and the case would proceed publicly. Rogron, advised by Vinet, opposed the council’s report being accepted by the court. Authorities intervened as Pierrette’s condition worsened; the trial, placed on the docket, was postponed until March 1828.Meanwhile Rogron married Mademoiselle de Chargeboeuf; Sylvie moved to the second floor to share with her. Madame Rogron supplanted Madame Tiphaine socially. Tiphaine, supported by Nucingen and du Tillet, advanced to civil judge and moved to Paris; his son inherited and sold the Provins house—evidence, in Vinet’s telling, of Tiphaine’s contempt for the town. The promotion yielded little for the Vinet faction, but Vinet exploited the events politically. Thus Pierrette’s brutal martyrdom—culminating in the trepanning performed by Martener under Bianchon’s advice—was mired in legal delay by an unscrupulous lawyer while she languished in agonizing pain.Martener and the Auffrays grew fond of Pierrette and her grandmother, whose Roman steadiness impressed them. Martener, who had begun his career in Paris but returned to provincial life, devoted himself to curing her, treating her with reverent care. The Auffrays’ household grew quiet in her presence; even the children kept silence for her sake. The Auffray house, on a terrace beneath chateau ruins overlooking the valley, became her refuge; her room allowed her to see a landscape she had rarely known.Brigaut, unable to work, visited thrice daily, sorrowful and faithful. The grandmother, concealing her despair to comfort Pierrette, made her a Breton cap recalling happier times. Pierrette’s delicate, pale features and serious expression made her an embodiment of melancholy; all served her devotedly. Madame Martener sent a piano to amuse her; music transfixed her, and her calm resignation impressed the clergy and lay visitors alike.Martener corresponded with Bianchon and sought to drain the abscess; periodic improvements—better appetite, some color—cheered them, but the cranial disease remained grave. Bianchon came from Paris with the celebrated Desplein, who warned that only a miracle could save the child: caries of the bone had begun and, at her age, bones were fragile. The operation was performed in early March 1828 by Desplein, but he cautioned the odds. Martener had even proposed a daring procedure akin to lithotrity—passing a hollow instrument into the head—that Desplein refused. Returning from Paris, Martener announced to the Auffrays, Madame Lorrain, the priests and Brigaut that science could do no more; recovery was left to God. They responded with masses and vows.While Pierrette lay dying, Vinet calumniated her in court. The judge accepted the Family Council’s report; Vinet appealed. The procureur du roi required further evidence, including testimony from Pierrette. When Desfondrilles came to the Auffrays to receive it, Pierrette was dying with her confessor near for extreme unction. She begged forgiveness for her cousins, saying judgment belonged to God; she told her grandmother to leave all to Brigaut and to give Adele a thousand francs, insisting that if Adele had stayed with the Rogrons she would not be dying.On a bright Tuesday of Easter week at three in the afternoon, Pierrette died. Her grandmother kept vigil through the night and sewed her shroud with trembling hands. Brigaut went to Frappier’s to make the coffin. Frappier watched as Brigaut planed, his plane cutting ribbons of wood wet with tears; he fashioned and lined the coffin with lead. By chance the lead cost exactly the sum Pierrette had given Brigaut for her journey—the coincidence overwhelmed him. He burned his tools, settled accounts, and bid farewell, his grief making him part of the awful finale of the Rogrons’ tyranny.At the Auffray house the room where Pierrette lay was crowded. Rogron’s emissaries stood near the body, and a surgeon in a dissecting apron prepared instruments. Madame Lorrain, on her knees, cried out, believing they intended to open the body as they had while she lived. Brigaut, who had just brought the coffin, seized a chisel, threatened the men, and swore he would kill anyone who touched her. Auffray and the curate tried to restrain him; the lawyers and doctors left. The plumber soldered the lead to make the coffin harder to open. With Pierrette dead, the guardianship suit collapsed; Rogron did not dare attend her funeral.Brigaut left Provins, went to Paris, petitioned the Dauphiness to join the Royal guard and was admitted. When the Algerian expedition opened he sought service and rose: he became sub-lieutenant, then advanced, distinguishing himself and rising to major; later he received a commission as an officer—taciturn, solitary, trusted, and never wounded. He inherited forty-six thousand francs from old Madame Lorrain, who died in Paris in 1829.In public life Vinet prospered: elected deputy in 1830 and later procureur-general; his influence remained secure. Rogron became receiver-general in the same town where Vinet held legal functions; Tiphaine, after aligning with the July dynasty, became president of the Royal court there. The once-beautiful Madame Tiphaine and Madame Rogron lived amicably; Vinet remained close with Tiphaine. Rogron, declining in health and wit, made absurd remarks; Sylvie managed his property, lent money at high interest, and lived frugally.General Baron Gouraud married Mademoiselle Matifat, daughter of a druggist, as Vinet had arranged; he became a departmental commander and was later made peer of France for his conduct during riots under Casimir Perier. None of those involved in Pierrette’s death showed remorse. Desfondrilles, to advance his prospects, was installed as president of the Provins court through Vinet’s influence. Sylvie kept a small circle.Over time locals would downplay the scandal. Travelers returning from Paris who had visited Mademoiselle Rogron heard softened versions blaming weak constitutions or imprudent liaisons. Frappier was mocked as a drunk; Habert’s motives were dismissed as merely personal.Only two men remembered Pierrette daily: Doctor Martener and Major Brigaut; they alone knew the hideous truth. To grasp the full scale of that truth one thinks of Beatrice Cenci—another young woman ruined by intrigues like those that destroyed Pierrette. As in those cases, legality often serves scoundrelism unless one believes in a higher justice.ADDENDUM: Many of these personages recur in Balzac’s Human Comedy: Bianchon, Brigaut, Desplein, General Gouraud, Keller, Matifat, Montriveau, Nucingen, Roguin and Madame Roguin, Tillet, Madame Tiphaine, and Vinet appear in other works such as Father Goriot, Lost Illusions, Cesar Birotteau, The Chouans, Cousin Pons, The Middle Classes, The Vendetta, The Member for Arcis, and The Firm of Nucingen.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################################### MIDDLE OF THE STORY.############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Early in the autumn of 1826 the Abbé Birotteau, the tale’s central figure, was caught in a downpour returning from an evening at a friend’s. He hurried, as his corpulence and gout allowed, across the deserted Cloister behind Saint-Gatien at Tours. Small, apoplectic and about sixty, he dreaded wet shoes because damp brought gout. Yet he bore the rain resignedly: he had won three francs ten sous at rubber with Madame de Listomère, wrapped himself in a canon’s fur cape, and was elated by talk that he might be named canon — a post he had long coveted.On the Grand’Rue side of the Cloister a cluster of cathedral houses formed a Close that had become the narrow Rue de la Psalette. Opposite, a single house, its walls overshadowed by Saint-Gatien’s buttresses, had long been occupied by abbés and belonged to an old maid, Mademoiselle Gamard. Time had dimmed the cathedral and thrown the house into shadow; the dwelling, suitable only for those of absolute nullity or uncommon fortitude, had been bought by her father during the Terror and accepted without objection by the restored Church. Devout and careful, Mademoiselle Gamard boarded priests and kept the house with punctilious economy.Birotteau had lived two years in that house and longed for its rooms as much as for a canonry. His two great desires — being Mademoiselle Gamard’s boarder and becoming a canon — reflected the ambitions of a provincial priest who sought decent lodging, good food, clean garments and the dignity of a title. The apartment he inhabited had been the object of a passion that began when he first admired the rooms of his friend, the Abbé Chapeloud, and which grew into an obsession.For years Chapeloud, who became canon when worship was restored under Napoleon, had occupied the south-facing apartment, furnishing and renovating it with modest legacies and gifts: an oak bookcase from a demolished château, waxed floors, painted woodwork, a long ebony table, Boulle cabinets, folios and Fathers of the Church. The shabby rooms were transformed into a dignified oratory and a salon hung with red damask and mahogany. Birotteau, poor and the son of peasants like Chapeloud, admired these changes and secretly yearned to possess them. His desire increased until, when Chapeloud fell ill, his thoughts ran, “If Chapeloud dies I can have this apartment.” Yet he never contemplated any means to influence his friend’s will.Chapeloud, indulgent and aware of his friend’s weakness, forgave his covetousness and, on his deathbed as Birotteau read the Quotidienne, said, “This time you will certainly get the apartment.” He bequeathed his library and furniture to Birotteau, who, though saddened, found consolation in the inheritance and in Mademoiselle Gamard’s cheerful agreement to take him as lodger. He moved into the apartment and enjoyed comforts he had long imagined: meticulous linen, fires, well-chosen wood, fragrant orris-root, and the small attentions for which Chapeloud had praised the spinster. Mademoiselle Gamard’s household had been celebrated, Chapeloud had often declared, for its perfect order and attentive service.Yet having obtained his long-desired comforts, Birotteau soon replaced the old obsession with a new one: the canonry. Encouraged by Madame de Listomère’s conversations, he believed promotion was near. Absorbed in these hopes, he did not at once notice, on returning one evening, that he had left his umbrella and that some domestic attentions were missing.
