He found the outer door locked, Marianne the servant answering awkwardly that Mademoiselle had thought he was in; his candlestick had been moved to an ante-chamber; the fire in his room had not been burning; his slippers were not placed where they usually were. These trivial changes struck him as deliberate signs of displeasure. By a sort of clerical logic he concluded that Mademoiselle Gamard intended to chill and humiliate him. The small humiliations mortified him deeply.Birotteau, kindly but simple-minded and ignorant of worldly calculations, lacked frank self-knowledge about what he had done to provoke dislike. He was a good man whose goodness verged on stupidity; his learning was shallow, and his life centered on mass and the confessional. He could not perceive how unconscious selfishness and provincial narrowness, common to many, had made him difficult. He was, in short, a clumsy egoist, unlike Chapeloud, who was a shrewd one.Chapeloud had understood Mademoiselle Gamard: an old maid of about thirty-eight whose pretensions amounted to strong self-esteem. The canon judged she needed constant, precise attentions if he would live comfortably under her roof. So he kept limited contact with her, avoided breakfast and supper, and came to dinner a few minutes early to pay polite compliments about the food and service. By these attentions, and by never creating points of contact beyond politeness, Chapeloud secured a peaceful coexistence and won praise for being easy to live with.Troubert, the other lodger, was her creature: obedient, patient and entirely under her rule, often suspected of coveting her property because of their intimate domestic life. When Chapeloud died the landlady at first considered offering the rooms to Troubert, but seeing Birotteau’s attachment to the apartment she accepted him as lodger for her own advantage and even improved the rooms: replacing brick with wooden floors and rebuilding the chimney.But Birotteau, dazzled by his new condition, did not adopt Chapeloud’s circumspection. He fell into Mademoiselle Gamard’s life with the embarrassment and timidity of a man who fears seeming rude and stays too long in conversation. He began to spend his evenings there, and the spinster, whose real desire had long been to make her salon a weekly center of company, welcomed the chance to display more prominent visitors. Taking pride in entertaining, she boasted that thanks to Birotteau she could gather respectable company.The vicar soon deserted her salon for Madame de Listomère’s and Mademoiselle Salomon’s, preferring aristocratic circles to which the landlady did not belong. His withdrawal gravely wounded Mademoiselle Gamard; she had hoped to use him to attract society. Her disappointment, joined to an old-maid’s craving for return on social investment and wounded pride, turned into resentment. The vicar, however, never understood this. He perceived, belatedly and clumsily, signs of slights—small omissions and unkindnesses—and concluded that ill will was directed at him though he could not see the reasons.Mademoiselle Gamard, like many who never adapted their habits to changing lives, indulged a petty despotism over small things—the basket of counters, the arrangement of cards, the order of household routine. Such trifles she magnified into tests of obedience. The vicar, unlike Chapeloud, could not accept being persistently cinched by such crochets without protest; he withdrew rather than submitting, irritating her more by his absence than by any direct challenge.Her friends, defending her, called Chapeloud amiable and blamed Birotteau for turning away from the old maid; society at large often sided, covertly, with those who escaped a nightly round in the Cloister. Mademoiselle Gamard enjoyed the condolence she received, and Troubert explained the departure by saying the abbe sought fashion, luxury and witty talk—thus flattering the spinster by implying he had higher tastes.In truth, the vicar’s abandonment was a clumsy desertion, and his naïveté and lack of social tact made him gradually intolerable to Mademoiselle Gamard. Eighteen months after she had taken him in, when he mistook her silence for contentment, she had in fact begun a deliberate, underhand persecution. The locked door, the forgotten slippers, the unlit fire and the moved candlestick were the first signs. Birotteau, too innocent to see his own faults toward his landlady, could not understand why she had turned against him.This prologue, recounting the small domestic developments that precede the greater drama, needs particulars. The little meannesses, jealousies and petty ambitions of bourgeois life inflict injuries as keen as those produced by grander interests, and their slow, minute growths require this account to explain the coming catastrophe.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The next morning Birotteau woke thinking so much of his prospective canonry that he forgot the four ominous signs of misfortune he had seen the night before. He did not get up without a fire; he rang for Marianne, then lay half-asleep in his usual musings. When Marianne did not appear after twenty minutes, he was about to ring again when he heard footsteps on the stairs. After a discreet knock, the Abbe Troubert, who visited him about once a month, came in. Birotteau at once called for Marianne sharply when he saw there was no fire, opened the window, and told her to come at once; then he turned to Troubert and, quite naively, related the steps he had taken and even the names of those Madame de Listomere was using in his favor—unaware that Troubert bore a grudge against that lady for having snubbed him, the same man twice proposed by the bishop as vicar-general.Troubert and Birotteau presented many contrasts. Troubert, tall, lean, yellow-faced and bilious, had long, deeply wrinkled features that sometimes expressed sarcasm or contempt; he seldom spoke or laughed, often seemed grave and was suspected of ambition. Birotteau was plump, ruddy, kindly, talkative and simple—qualities that marked him permanently as vicar and nothing higher. Troubert’s imposing presence and cautious conduct, combined with a saintly life and failing health, had removed fears about his alleged ability; his chronic malady made it likely he would soon become vicar-general of the archbishopric. Birotteau’s robust appearance, his triple chin and even his gout, seemed to others guarantees of long life and offered no hope of elevation.The Abbe Chapeloud, a sensible, amiable man popular in Tours, had quietly opposed Troubert’s rise by keeping him out of the best salons, though Troubert treated Chapeloud with great deference while Chapeloud remained unconvinced: “Distrust that lean stick of a Troubert,—Sixtus the Fifth reduced to the limits of a bishopric!” he had said to Birotteau.Troubert had come that morning, the day after Mademoiselle Gamard—Birotteau’s landlady and the old maid who had seemingly declared war on the vicar—had behaved harshly toward him, simply to ask to borrow Birotteau’s Register of Bishops. Birotteau, pleased, sent him to the library while he dressed. Presently the breakfast bell rang; Birotteau, reflecting that without Troubert he would have had no fire, thought, “He’s a kind man.”The two priests went downstairs carrying huge folios to lay on a dining-room table. Mademoiselle Gamard greeted them sharply. “I hope you are not going to litter up my dining-room with your old books!” she snapped at Birotteau. Troubert explained he had borrowed them from the vicar. Mademoiselle Gamard remarked contemptuously that Birotteau did not often read such large books.Birotteau answered her with his usual mellifluous politeness. She replied that he had woken her the night before out of her first sleep and had kept her awake. Sitting down, she added, “Gentlemen, the milk is getting cold.” Stupefied at the ill-natured reception and alarmed at the prospect of a dispute, the timid vicar fell silent. He struggled inwardly between feeling he should not submit to discourtesy and his instinct to avoid quarrel.To distract himself he examined the green lines painted on the oilcloth she habitually left on the breakfast table. They sat opposite each other; she dominated the head of the table from a high, cushioned chair near the stove. When Mademoiselle Gamard handed him his coffee, the vicar felt chilled by the silence and dared not meet Troubert’s dried features or the landlady’s threatening face. He turned, to keep up appearances, to the fat pug on a cushion by the stove, and addressed it, as he often did, to keep up conversation on the theory—his own—that speech favored digestion. The dog, once important in the household, now merely closed his eyes peevishly.Mademoiselle Gamard believed in health maxims and in talking at meals; Birotteau had for some mornings strained to find topics to loosen her tongue. Their conversations, if recorded, would have illustrated provincial ignorance and credulous gossip—absurdities about Napoleon’s death, the Dauphin, taxes, and other political and medical superstitions. But that morning Birotteau felt tongue-tied; after a long pause he risked, “This coffee is excellent,” and later, “It will be finer to-day than yesterday.” At the second remark she cast her most gracious look toward Troubert, then turned on Birotteau with terrible severity.Mademoiselle Sophie Gamard was the quintessential old maid. To understand her, the narrative pauses to outline the nature of such women. Society condemns the unproductive individual who consumes without producing; old maids fall under a similar stigma because they decline or fail to fulfill what society deems their role. Their celibacy—whether from ugliness, lack of charm, deliberate independence, or other causes—isolates them and corrupts their social standing. Deprived of the activity through which womanly qualities develop and are exercised, they are starved of purpose. Bitterness, jealousy and a wounded pride follow; they blame others for their condition, then themselves, and often turn to revenge. Their social failure shows in an oblique, ashamed glance, a hardness of manner, and a certain selfishness: used to being useless, they prefer what is convenient for themselves to what would please others. Habits of self-centeredness produce peevishness and lack of refinement; deprived of love’s practice, their faculties wither.All these truths could be read in Mademoiselle Gamard’s dull gray eyes, the dark circles under them, the straight lines of her wrinkles. Her scattered hairs on the chin, thin lips barely covering long teeth, dark complexion, and the gaps where she poorly adjusted a false front revealed a carelessness born of inward conflict. Her thin, tight brown gowns, limp collar, and a neck thick and ribbed with age matched the hard, angular movements of a woman who had risen from rural origins—the daughter of a wood-merchant—and who carried in her face and gait the despotism of character and narrowness of mind. Her abrupt gestures, loud nasal blows on her handkerchief, and erect, blocklike stride betrayed a lack of grace. When in good humor she would recount missed chances of marriage and men’s lack of means, proof, to her, that worldly prudence had prevailed over the heart.Mademoiselle Gamard’s dining-room showed her taste: varnished paper with Turkish landscapes, two pier tables, a barometer, little faded worsted cushions before each abbé’s chair. Her salon, “the yellow salon,” was yellow in curtains, furniture and walls; the mantelpiece with a gilt mirror, crystal candlesticks and clock seemed sharp and precise. Her private apartment was never shown; conjecture thought it full of odds, worn-out furniture, and things dear to an old maid.Unable to expend womanly activity naturally, she had converted it into petty intrigues, provincial cabals and self-seeking schemes—the occupation of many old maids. Birotteau had developed in her the only strong feeling she could muster: hatred. This hatred, previously latent beneath provincial calm, became intense because expended on petty things within a narrow sphere. Birotteau’s timidity and incapacity to see the world’s malice made him especially helpless before her. He was the sort of man doomed to suffer because he could not avoid the harms that beset him.After breakfast, Birotteau, unnerved by the silence, left the room with the coffee heavy in his stomach and strolled among the narrow, box-edged paths of the little garden, laid out in a star. Turning after a first round, he saw Mademoiselle Gamard and Troubert standing motionless on the threshold; he felt as if they were watching and counting his steps. Nothing is so distressing to a timid person as being closely examined, more so when those eyes express hatred. Embarrassed, he fancied he was in their way and, moved by fear and kindness, left the garden for Saint-Gatien, where he found much to occupy him—funerals, a marriage, baptisms—and so forgot his pain. When his watch told him dinner should be ready he hurried back, mindful of Mademoiselle Gamard’s punctuality.Passing the kitchen, he saw the first course had been removed. In the dining-room she said sourly and with evident pleasure at finding ground for reproach, “It is half-past four, Monsieur Birotteau. You know we are not to wait for you.” The vicar looked at the clock and noticed by the moved gauze that the hands had been set ahead of the cathedral clock. He said nothing; any remark would have led to one of her fierce expositions, in which women of her class could be eloquently censorious. Mademoiselle Gamard instinctively divined a thousand petty annoyances that might provoke or discomfort a timid man like Birotteau, and she delighted in plotting against his domestic comfort—always in a way that left her apparently never in the wrong. Her methods showed a profoundly malignant ingenuity: she could contrive to torment him while keeping the appearance of justice and propriety.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Eight days after the events that opened this history, changes in the household and in the relations between the Abbé Birotteau and Mademoiselle Gamard made the former perceive a plot that had been growing for six months.At first the old maid’s vengeance had been covert and the vicar could ignore it; the harm was slight. But after the candlestick and the altered clock, Birotteau no longer doubted that hatred watched him. From then on he fell into despair, seeing Mademoiselle Gamard’s thin, clawlike fingers everywhere. She, delighting in revenge, hovered above him like a bird of prey preparing to pounce. She had long devised a plan which the simple priest could not imagine, and now unfolded it with the petty skill often shown by solitary people who, barred from large feelings, expend themselves on the details of outward devotion.These small torments kept Birotteau, effusive and craving pity, from confiding in friends—another sting. His timidity made him dread seeming ridiculous over such pettiness. Yet these trifles composed his life—busy about nothing, finding happiness in the absence of passion. His paradise altered to hell; the fear of confronting Mademoiselle Gamard grew, and the secret distress harmed his health. One morning, while putting on mottled blue stockings, he noticed his calves had shrunk. Terrified, he resolved to ask Abbé Troubert to intervene officially between him and the landlady.In Troubert’s bare, cheerless room the vicar, ashamed to bother so grave a man, after many hesitations, explained his case. The canon listened coldly, occasionally suppressing a smile. He blamed Birotteau’s failure to see the trouble on his preoccupation with elevated thoughts, remarked that former recluses thought little of food and lodging, and suggested modern priests can retire into the solitude of their hearts. He offered to act as arbitrator because his friendship with Mademoiselle Gamard had never exceeded Church limits, but justice required hearing both sides. Troubert professed to see no change in Gamard, attributing slight temper fluctuations to pulmonary suffering and recommending patience; if Birotteau stayed longer he would come to esteem her better.Birotteau left confounded. Unable to consult anyone, he judged Mademoiselle Gamard alone and fancied that by leaving her house for a few days he might extinguish her dislike. He resolved to spend a week at Madame de Listomère’s country house, a practice he had often followed. Ironically, this was precisely what Gamard wished; only patient endurance could have foiled her plan. The vicar, incapable of divining this, was doomed to fall.Madame de Listomère’s country-place, the Alouette, near Tours, combined town and country charms. About ten days after his arrival there, while breakfasting, Birotteau was told Monsieur Caron wished to speak with him. Caron was Mademoiselle Gamard’s lawyer. Agitated and surprised, Birotteau learned Caron had been sent to ask how long he intended to remain in the country. The original agreement had not foreseen a long absence and might lead to contest. Gamard, anxious to avoid dispute, had sent Caron to learn his plans. The abbé asked for time to take advice.Back at table, Birotteau described his trouble. The company, bored with country-house monotony, eagerly sided against the old maid. Madame de Listomère surmised Troubert wanted Birotteau’s apartment; Monsieur de Listomère urged resistance. An old landowner, Monsieur de Bourbonne, suggested something more serious: that Gamard intended to turn Birotteau out, and those like her risked nothing in such games. De Bourbonne, thin, shrewd, practised in weighing words and measuring actions, had a reputation for cunning and decency that made him feared in local affairs.Mademoiselle Salomon de Villenoix arrived from Tours with news that changed everything. She reported the vicar-general was very ill and the archbishop had provisionally delegated his powers to Abbé Troubert, so the canonry would depend on him. At a recent gathering the Abbé Poirel had spoken of Birotteau’s alleged annoyances to Mademoiselle Gamard in a way that cast blame on the vicar. Madame de Listomère asked Birotteau whether he preferred to be made canon or to continue living with Gamard; in chorus they said canon. She suggested that by leaving Gamard’s house he might secure the canonry—“one good turn deserves another.” All applauded, except her nephew, the Baron, who joked about wishing to see a fight.Despite their support, forces were unequal: the city’s best society had less power than Gamard backed by Troubert. By Madame de Listomère’s advice, a servant fetched Caron back. De Bourbonne counselled caution and delay, but passion prevailed. In a short conference Caron urged Birotteau to sign a paper declaring his relinquishment of domicile. Confused but overwhelmed, Birotteau read and mechanically signed it: he declared he left Gamard’s house of his own wish, that he had been properly fed and lodged. Caron asked where to send the abbé’s belongings; Birotteau said Madame de Listomère. De Bourbonne, suspicious, requested to see the paper and noted there must be prior documents between Birotteau and Gamard. The fatal deed, the vicar said, was in his library.The helplessness of the poor man, innocent of worldly craft, moved Madame de Listomère and Mademoiselle Salomon, who soothed him like mothers consoling a child. They promised lodging and counsel, urged play to distract him, and spoke of asking Troubert for help. Birotteau, dazzled by the prospect of living with Madame de Listomère, temporarily forgot his ruined happiness. But that night the reality returned: the thought of moving, of his books and furniture scattered, of the end of long habits, overwhelmed him. He could not reconcile one sweet first year in Gamard’s house with the cruel second; the canonry seemed poor compensation. He clung to Mademoiselle Salomon’s friendship but dared not fully trust new attachments.A digression notes that among spinsters some are heroic—those who sacrifice themselves nobly. Mademoiselle Salomon belonged to this class: once beloved and young, she had tended a lover who went mad, refusing to call him insane; she had devoted herself for five years to his mechanical well-being, and after his death settled in Tours to do good. Simple, frank, and of regular features, she attached herself to the feeble, which made her warmly interested in Birotteau.Mademoiselle de Villenoix returned Birotteau to Tours the next morning and left him at the quay of the cathedral to go to the Cloister, bent on preserving the canonry and overseeing removal of his furniture. At the house he found his old apartment occupied: Marianne told him Troubert was in his old rooms. The sight of Troubert, comfortably seated in Chapeloud’s library and armchair, confirmed the depth of the conspiracy. Birotteau grasped, with a sinking vision, that Troubert intended to possess Chapeloud’s spoils. Facing Troubert’s sardonic glance, the abbé felt struck dumb.Troubert coolly told him that Mademoiselle Gamard had informed him of Birotteau’s departure and that he had been installed out of necessity; if the furniture belonged to Birotteau, Gamard’s scrupulous honesty guaranteed its return; if Birotteau wished to return, Troubert would cede the apartment. The words were terrible. Birotteau ran to find Gamard and met her on the staircase. Bowing despite her derisive smile, he protested she should have waited to let him pack his books and furniture. She interrupted, asking if he had not read his deed, and then, with the triumphant clarity of hatred, reminded him of the clause: by agreement, if he left her house his furniture would indemnify her for the difference in board between what he paid and what the late Abbé Chapeloud had paid. With Poirel’s appointment as canon just made, Gamard exulted in the arrangement. At these words Birotteau faintly bowed and fled, fearing a complete breakdown if he stayed.He found at Madame de Listomère’s house his linen, clothing, and papers packed in a trunk, and sank, hiding his face in his hands. Poirel had been made canon; Birotteau had no home, means, or furniture. Mademoiselle Salomon drove by; the porter noticed the priest’s despair and persuaded her to take him in her carriage. She, ignorant of the deed’s terms, believed his derangement due to the shock of Poirel’s nomination. Back at the Alouette, friends cared for him until evening, when he recounted the morning’s events.De Bourbonne asked to see the deed. Birotteau produced it. De Bourbonne read the clause providing that, because of an eight-hundred-franc yearly difference between Chapeloud’s board and what Gamard charged, and because Birotteau was unable to pay full price, he engaged in consideration of sums lent by Gamard to leave her, as indemnity, all household property he might possess at death, or to transfer the same should he voluntarily give up the apartment. De Bourbonne cursed the agreement and declared Gamard armed with claws.Birotteau had never imagined anything would separate him from the house where he hoped to live and die. He had not remembered the clause, which he had not discussed; in his eagerness to enter the house he would have signed any document. Madame de Listomère, in hot indignation, exclaimed she had made him sign and must restore his lost happiness. De Bourbonne advised law: the deed might constitute fraud. The Baron and others urged litigation. De Bourbonne privately advised a different path: that most now friendly would desert him soon, and if Birotteau wished peace he should resign the vicariate and leave Tours for a distant parish where Troubert could not pursue him. This prospect horrified the vicar—leaving Tours for him was like death; celibates replace feelings with habits, and Birotteau needed his routine: his walks, salons, and games.Word quickly spread that Madame la Baronne de Listomère had invited Birotteau to stay. The baroness’s reception of him implicitly denied Gamard’s assertions and censured her conduct, so Gamard was deeply wounded. To understand how Gamard had resources, one must see the network that served Troubert. Accompanied by Troubert, Gamard made evening rounds to several houses where circles of old maids, a few men swayed by servants’ gossip, and other busybodies met. These women, ever sifting words and scrutinizing neighbors, kept exact accounts of fortunes and actions, retailing scandal and controlling local opinion. Spread through town like capillary fibres, they absorbed household secrets and transmitted them mechanically to Troubert. They formed a kind of police—dumb but perpetually talking—whose gossip could harm when it touched important interests. The quarrel between Birotteau and Gamard became the absorbing passion of these salons; a class sentiment underlay the conflict—a people-vs.-senate antipathy reduced to a provincial molehill.Madame de Listomère returned to Tours and took Birotteau to her lawyer. He did not think the case easy. Friends who had believed justice certain had delayed bringing the suit until returning to town, and Gamard’s allies had taken the initiative, spreading the affair to Birotteau’s damage. The lawyer—whose practice lay among the devout—cautioned against litigation. According to the deed, Gamard had the law on her side; in equity, judges and respectable laymen might view Birotteau as having departed from his formerly peaceable, mild character. Gamard had lent him money to pay Chapeloud’s legacy duties without a receipt; Birotteau, not of age or character to sign without understanding, left Gamard’s house after two years while Chapeloud and Troubert had been there twelve and fifteen years respectively; the suit would appear like ingratitude. The lawyer declined to undertake it and besought Madame de Listomère to avoid embroiling herself.That evening Birotteau could not refrain from telling friends the lawyer’s refusal. De Bourbonne advised caution and mentioned only a radical lawyer might take the case. The Baron, heatedly, insisted: Troubert’s involvement made it a public matter; valuable pictures and furniture had been involved, and Birotteau could not have intended to give away such wealth for two years of board. He proposed getting Monsieur Salmon, an expert from the Museum, to appraise the paintings and furniture.Two days later the suit began. Choosing the radical lawyer harmed Birotteau’s cause: opponents of government and critics of religion seized on the case and the town buzzed. The Museum expert appraised the Virgin of Valentin and the Christ of Lebrun at eleven thousand francs; the bookshelves and Gothic furniture, increasingly fashionable in Paris, had immediate value of at least twelve thousand. The full appraisal exceeded thirty-six thousand francs. Clearly, Birotteau could not have intended to give Gamard such a sum under the deed; legally, he had equitable grounds to amend the agreement; if refused, Gamard’s conduct might constitute intentional fraud. The radical lawyer served a harsh writ on Gamard, a document of skillful legal argument supported by precedent and code citations; it so clearly condemned the old maid that many copies were maliciously distributed through the town.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A few days after hostilities began between Birotteau and the old maid, the Baron de Listomere, who expected promotion to corvette captain, received a letter warning he might be put on the retired list. Astonished, he went to Paris and to the minister, who laughed at his fears; but the next day, despite the minister’s assurances, the baron made inquiries. By an indiscretion a secretary showed him a document confirming the news, awaiting only the director’s signature.The baron hurried to an uncle, a deputy who could see the minister at the Chamber, and begged him to learn the minister’s intentions in a matter that threatened his future. Waiting anxiously in his uncle’s carriage, he was told on the way home: the minister had first accused him of leading Radicals in Tours and of objectionable politics; but the real trouble, his uncle learned, was ecclesiastical. The baron had spoken slightingly of Abbe Troubert, the vicar-general, an influential man representing the Jesuits in the province. His uncle warned him to make peace with Troubert at once, saying such priests were men with whom one must live in harmony; Troubert was likely to be raised to a bishopric and could even harm the uncle’s prospects.These words explained to the baron Troubert’s secret activities that Birotteau had often wondered about: “I can’t think what he does with himself—sitting up all night.” Troubert’s position, skill, and female network in Tours had made him the Congregation’s proconsul there; archbishop, general, prefect—all were under his covert influence. The Baron de Listomere decided on his course. “I shall take care not to get another round shot below my water-line,” he told his uncle.Three days later the baron, returning hurriedly, told his aunt the family must abandon support of “that fool of a Birotteau.” He detained Monsieur de Bourbonne as the old man was leaving a rubber of whist; Bourbonne’s clear-sightedness seemed essential to navigate the reefs the Listomeres faced. The baron’s return and disquiet hinted he had met a check in his campaign against Gamard and Troubert. He revealed Troubert’s hidden power.“I knew that,” said Bourbonne.“Then why did you not warn us?” the baroness cried.“Forget that I knew it, and I will forget that you did,” Bourbonne replied. “If we do not keep this secret now we shall be thought his accomplices. Pretend to be duped; but watch your steps. I warned you, but you would not understand me.”Abandoning Birotteau was taken for granted by the three. “To beat a retreat with the honors of war has always been the triumph of the ablest generals,” said Bourbonne. “Bow to Troubert; if his hatred is less than his vanity you may win him; if you bow too low he will walk over you. Make believe you intend to leave the service, and you’ll escape him. Send away Birotteau, madame, and you will set things right with Mademoiselle Gamard. Ask Troubert, when you meet him at the archbishop’s, if he plays whist; he will say yes. Invite him to your salon; you are a woman and can win a priest. When the baron is promoted and his uncle made a peer, and Troubert a bishop, you can make Birotteau a canon if you choose. Meantime yield—but yield gracefully, with a slight menace. Your family can give him as much as he can give you. As for you, sailor, keep your deep-sea line about you.”“Poor Birotteau?” said the baroness.“Get rid of him at once,” Bourbonne replied. “If some clever Radical gets that empty head, he may cause trouble. The court might give a verdict in his favor, and Troubert must fear that. I have said my say.”The next morning the baroness took Birotteau aside and, not without embarrassment, asked him to withdraw his lawsuit against Mademoiselle Gamard and to leave her house. As she spoke he turned pale. She explained, briefly and without disguising the gravity, the wide ramifications of the affair, making him see the net woven by revenge and the capacity and power of Troubert, whose hatred of Chapeloud now vented itself in seizing Chapeloud’s property and persecuting his friend. Birotteau clasped his hands and wept, listening as one at the edge of a precipice. The baroness ended: family duties must be considered before friendship; yield, and she would ensure he lacked nothing, arranging matters through Monsieur de Bourbonne to save appearances. “Grant me the right to abandon you,” she begged.The bewildered abbe cried, “Chapeloud was right when he said that if Troubert could drag him by the feet out of his grave he would do it!” Yet he yielded. “I trust myself to you—I am but the stubble of the streets,” he said, using a local word for a pathetic bit of straw. He asked only for Chapeloud’s portrait, painted for him, and the baroness promised to see Mademoiselle Gamard herself and to consult the archbishop.Birotteau left dismayed; Troubert now seemed to him a pyramid whose hands were in Paris and elbows in the cloister. The news of Birotteau’s removal from the Listomere house seemed mysterious: the baroness told people the nephew meant to marry and leave the navy, and wanted the vicar’s apartment. The Listomeres’ apparent neutrality implied recognition of the Congregation’s occult power. But the lawsuit still awaited judgment; their opponents yielded while threatening.Then Mademoiselle Gamard took cold leaving the cathedral and was soon dangerously ill. The town burst into commiseration: “Mademoiselle Gamard’s sensitive nature could not bear the scandal; in spite of the justice of her cause she was likely to die of grief—Birotteau has killed his benefactress.” Such talk spread through the female conclave and the town.Madame de Listomere went to pay her promised visit but the old maid was too ill to see her. She asked to speak to the vicar-general. Troubert, gratified to receive in Chapeloud’s library the woman who had hitherto ignored him, kept her waiting before admitting her. No courtier could have put more dissimulation into negotiating personal interests than the baroness and the priest did now.Bourbonne had coached the baroness: “Don’t forget your cue. You are a mediator, not an interested party. Troubert is a mediator. Weigh your words; if he strokes his chin you have him.” Their conversation was a duel of implied meanings. Madame de Listomere began by expressing regret at Birotteau’s lawsuit and her desire to settle it.“The harm is done, madame,” Troubert said gravely. “The pious Mademoiselle Gamard is dying.” (He intended to place the guilt on the baroness.)“On hearing of her illness I entreated Monsieur Birotteau to relinquish his claims; I have brought the document intending to give it to that excellent woman,” she replied. (She meant to avoid compromising herself.) After a tense interchange of civilities and veiled thrusts about religion, politics, and the paintings, she laid the deed of relinquishment on the table and said she had come to reconcile two Christians.“Your nephew has been to Paris, I believe,” Troubert said. “He returns tonight; the minister does not want him to leave the service.” “He has done us the service of getting a proper estimate on the value of those paintings,” Troubert added, eyeing the pictures. Madame de Listomere offered frames for Saint-Gatien if they were to be given to the chapel. He replied they did not belong to him. She handed him the relinquishment, saying it ended all discussion and that she had some compassion for Birotteau; he had asked, as the price of renunciation, for Chapeloud’s portrait. Troubert looked fixedly at her and went down to ask Mademoiselle Gamard.He soon returned. “Madame,” he said, “I bring you the words of a dying woman. ‘The Abbe Chapeloud was so true a friend...that I cannot consent to part with his picture.’ As for me, if it were mine I would not yield it. My feelings to my late friend were so faithful that I should feel my right above others’.”“That’s no need to quarrel over a bad picture,” the baroness said, offering to have a copy made and courteously inviting him to play whist at her house. Troubert, stroked his chin—caught by vanity—and promised he would oblige her for her sake.Madame de Listomere went home hoping the archbishop would complete the peace. But Mademoiselle Gamard died the next day. Her will left everything to Abbe Troubert; the fortune was appraised at three hundred thousand francs. He sent the Listomeres invitations to the services and funeral; the baroness insisted they go. “We must go,” she said. Bourbonne added that the baron must go to the cemetery; the lieutenant, unluckily, had not left Tours.The services were performed with unusual ecclesiastical magnificence. Only Birotteau wept, kneeling in a side chapel; he believed himself guilty and sincerely prayed for the deceased, lamenting that he had not obtained her forgiveness. Troubert followed the body to the grave and delivered a discourse that, by eloquence, enlarged the old maid’s life to monumental proportions. In the peroration he spoke of her life devoted to God and of Providence withdrawing her from trials; his tone suggested she rested innocent. After the sermon, in a gesture that betrayed more joy than grief, he sprinkled the coffin with holy water.The next morning Mademoiselle Salomon came to tell Madame de Listomere that “our poor Abbe Birotteau has just received a frightful blow: he is appointed curate of Saint-Symphorien.” Saint-Symphorien lay beyond the bridge, a suburb three miles away—an exile in plain sight of the town. The parsonage was cold and damp, the parish too poor, and the removal seemed an infamous plot.To end the history: five months later Troubert was made Bishop of Troyes; Madame de Listomere died, leaving an annuity of fifteen hundred francs to Abbe Birotteau. The day her will’s dispositions were announced, Monseigneur Hyacinthe, Bishop of Troyes, about to leave Tours for his diocese, delayed when he received the news. Furious at being foiled by the baroness—whom he had lately received while she had secretly aided a man he counted as an enemy—Troubert again threatened the baron’s career and jeopardized his uncle’s peerage. He made one of those soft, vengeful priestly speeches in the archbishop’s salon. The Baron de Listomere went to him the next day and must have submitted to hard conditions, for his later conduct showed entire submission to the Jesuit’s will.The new bishop deeded Mademoiselle Gamard’s house to the cathedral chapter, gave Chapeloud’s books and bookcases to the seminary, and presented the two disputed pictures to the Chapel of the Virgin—but he kept Chapeloud’s portrait. No one could explain this near-total renunciation of Gamard’s bequest; Bourbonne supposed the bishop had secretly kept invested monies to support his rank in Paris. Only the night before Troubert’s departure did Bourbonne unearth the hidden reason: the baron had contested Madame de Listomere’s legacy to Birotteau on grounds of undue influence. The deathblow to the feeblest victim was complete.A few days after the case was brought the baron was promoted to captain. As ecclesiastical discipline, the curate of Saint-Symphorien—Birotteau—was suspended; his superiors judged him guilty. The murderer of Sophie Gamard was also a swindler; had Troubert kept the property openly, it would have been harder to censure Birotteau.When Monseigneur Hyacinthe drove along the quay Saint-Symphorien in a post-chaise on his way to Paris, poor Birotteau was placed in an armchair in the sun on a terrace above the road. Pale and haggard, smitten by the archbishop, he was the skeleton of the man who less than a year earlier had rolled contentedly along the cloister. The bishop cast one look of pity and contempt, then went his way.There is no doubt Troubert might once have been a Hildebrand or an Alexander VI. In our era the Church is no longer the political power it once was; celibacy concentrates a man’s faculties on a single passion—egotism—which makes celibates either useless or mischievous. We live when governments tend to make Man for Society rather than Society for Man. There is a perpetual struggle between the Individual and a social system that uses him while he tries to use it. In earlier times man was first father, then lived for clan or city; later, for caste or religion, and his range of interests widened. Today his life attaches to a vast country; perhaps someday his family may be the whole universe.Will such moral cosmopolitanism—Christian Rome’s hope of Brotherhood—prove only a sublime error? It is natural to believe in such a noble vision, but human nature lacks such divine proportions. Souls vast enough to hold a world of feeling belong to great exceptions. Physiologists who say as the brain enlarges the heart narrows are mistaken. The apparent egotism of those who bear a science, a nation, or a code is a highest passion; it is the maternity of the masses. To create peoples and ideas they must unite in their mighty brains the tenderness of woman and the force of God. The histories of Innocent III, Peter the Great, and other great leaders show the same vast thought of which Troubert was a provincial representative.ADDENDUMThe following personages appear in other stories of the Human Comedy.     Birotteau, Abbe Francois       The Lily of the Valley       Cesar Birotteau     Bourbonne, De       Madame Firmiani     Listomere, Baronne de       Cesar Birotteau       The Muse of the Department     Troubert, Abbe Hyacinthe       The Member for Arcis     Villenoix, Pauline Salomon de       Louis Lambert       A Seaside Tragedy############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################################ MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################DEDICATIONTo Monsieur Charles Nodier, member of the French Academy.My dear Nodier, here is a book of deeds concealed from legal action behind the closed doors of domestic life; in these matters the finger of God, often called chance, stands in for human justice, and the moral, though sometimes ridiculed, remains striking and instructive.These deeds teach much about Family and Maternity. We may yet understand, perhaps too late, the effects of the diminution of paternal authority. That authority, which formerly ceased only at the father’s death, was once the sole human tribunal before which domestic crimes could be arraigned; kings sometimes aided its execution. However good and tender a mother may be, she cannot perform the patriarchal function any more than a woman can take a king’s place on the throne. Here I have perhaps more plainly shown how essential to European society is the indissoluble marriage bond, how fatal feminine weakness can be, and how dangerous selfish interests are when indulged without restraint. May a society that worships wealth shudder to see the impotence of the law in dealing with a system that deifies success and forgives every means of attaining it. May it return to the Catholic religion for purification of its masses and an education other than that of a lay university.In the “Scenes from Military Life” many noble self-devotions will be set forth; allow me to point out the depraving effect of war’s necessities on minds who then act at home as if on the battlefield.You have cast a sagacious glance over our time; your philosophy shines in many bitter reflections. You have better than most perceived the havoc wrought in our minds by the existence of four distinct political systems. I cannot more fitly dedicate this history than under your protection. Your name may defend my work against the criticisms sure to follow it—for where is the patient who keeps silence when the surgeon lifts the dressing from his wound?To the pleasure of dedicating this Scene to you I add the pride of making known your friendship for one who here subscribes himselfYour sincere admirer,De BalzacParis, November, 1842.THE TWO BROTHERSCHAPTER IIn 1792 the people of Issoudun had a physician named Rouget, whom they regarded as consummately malicious. Rumor said he made his wife miserable, though she was the neighborhood beauty. Whether she was foolish or not, friends and enemies never laid bare that household. The doctor was of the “not pleasant to deal with” sort, and townsmen treated him civilly. His wife, a demoiselle Descoings, weak in health as a girl (said to be why the doctor married her), bore a son and, ten years later, a daughter, Agathe, whose birth surprised even the doctor.These facts must be told, for without them a man like Rouget would appear a monster, when in fact he followed the evil tendencies many shelter under the axiom that “men should have strength of character,” a phrase that has caused much female misery.The elder Descoings, father-in-law and mother-in-law of the doctor, were commission merchants in the wool trade, buying for manufacturers and selling for producers, pocketing commission on both sides, and growing rich and miserly. Their son Descoings disliked Issoudun, went to Paris, set up as a grocer in the rue Saint-Honoré, and there, by love and patience and a little money from his parents, married the widow of his predecessor, Monsieur Bixiou.In 1792 Descoings seemed to prosper. The old Descoings had retired and invested in buying forfeited estates. Doctor Rouget, foreseeing his wife’s death, sent Agathe to Paris to his brother-in-law both to let her see the capital and to carry out his scheme: he judged that his brother-in-law and sister, childless and older, might grow fond of the child. Agathe, the handsomest maiden of Issoudun, did not resemble either parent. Her birth had caused a breach between Doctor Rouget and his friend Monsieur Lousteau, a former sub-delegate who left town when sub-delegates were suppressed. Madame Rouget spent much time with Madame Hochon, Lousteau’s sister and Agathe’s godmother, to whom she confided her griefs; Madame Hochon later told most of Issoudun what it knew of Madame Rouget.When Rouget told his wife he would send Agathe to Paris her first words were, “I shall never see my daughter again.” Madame Hochon thought she was right. The poor mother grew yellow with grief; tongues said the doctor was killing her by inches. The behavior of her foolish son Jean-Jacques, who owed her little attention or respect, added to her misery. Jean-Jacques resembled his father in the worst ways; the doctor himself was unsatisfactory at best.Agathe’s arrival did not bring happiness to her uncle. In the same period Descoings was imprisoned on a hint from Robespierre. He had expressed doubts of the famine to customers in his shop, and citoyenne Duplay, Robespierre’s housekeeper, took offense and denounced him for “accaparation.” His wife’s ill-judged efforts to obtain his release only made matters worse. She knew Bridau, secretary to Roland, and put him on the war-path. That incorruptible official sought to enlighten the arbiters, but they only replied, “Why do you meddle?” Bridau advised patience. Madame Descoings fretted and even appealed to a member of the Convention, who promised to speak to Robespierre and then forgot. A little sugar or liqueur to citoyenne Duplay would likely have saved Descoings. The lesson of those days is that it is as dangerous to employ honest men as scoundrels; one must rely on oneself. Descoings perished on the scaffold with André Chénier. His death produced more sensation then than Chénier’s; it has taken thirty years to prove France lost more by Chénier’s death than by Descoings’s.That neighborhood shop, so near Robespierre’s lodging, seemed cursed: César Birotteau later failed there as well. During the visits Bridau paid the widow, he was struck by Agathe’s cold, calm, innocent beauty and married her with the consent of her father. Doctor Rouget, eager to secure his design, hurried to Paris to draw the marriage contract to his advantage. Bridau’s ardent and disinterested love gave carte blanche to the perfidious doctor, who made full use of his son-in-law’s blindness, as this history will show.Madame Rouget, or rather the doctor, inherited the elder Descoings’ property; within two years both had died, and Rouget, after his wife’s death in 1799, acquired vineyards, farms, and iron-works. Jean-Jacques proved incapable of doing anything; the father destined him for the life of a proprietor and allowed him to grow up in wealth and silliness. After 1799 the cipherers of Issoudun put at least thirty thousand francs’ income to the doctor’s credit. From his wife’s death he led a debauched life, kept within his house; this man of “strength of character” died in 1805. Jean-Jacques remained a bachelor for certain reasons partly his father’s fault, as will appear.It is well to ask the result of the secret vengeance the doctor took on a daughter he did not recognize as his own, though legitimately his. Agathe bore a strong likeness to the doctor’s mother; resemblances sometimes skip generations, as vexing to science as any other mystery of heredity. In turn, Agathe’s eldest child, who resembled her physically, possessed the moral qualities of his grandfather, Doctor Rouget.Agathe’s beauty was pure and placid—an oval face, clear complexion, golden hair, a composed mouth, long lashes, and tender dark-blue eyes. Bridau’s portrait of her hangs in his atelier. She was the ideal provincial housekeeper, pious though not sanctimonious, educated by the Church. In ordinary terms she was an accomplished wife, yet her ignorance of life opened the way to misfortunes; the Roman epitaph “She did needlework and kept the house” fitted her.Under the Consulate Bridau adored Napoleon and in 1804 became head of a department at the ministry of the interior with a salary of twelve thousand francs and handsome emoluments. From 1804 to 1808 the Bridaus lived in a handsome suite on the Quai Voltaire, near the ministry and the Tuileries. A cook and footman were their only servants. Agathe, early afoot, went to market with her cook and prepared breakfast; Bridau never went to the ministry before eleven. When together, she prepared his breakfast, watched him off at the window, cleared the table, dressed, played with the children, and later sat silent, knitting by his writing table when he worked late. Sometimes they attended the theatre or used a ministerial box; Agathe enjoyed the occasional restaurant as any provincial new to Paris. Bridau received friends Thursdays and gave an annual Shrove-Tuesday ball.Their conjugal life had but three events: the birth of two children three years apart and Bridau’s death in 1808, killed by overwork just as Napoleon was about to appoint him director-general, count, and councillor of state. Napoleon, impressed by Bridau’s devotion, was about to reward him when the effort to finish a work before the Emperor’s departure caused an inflammatory fever that killed him. Touched by a devotion that received none of the brilliant recognitions given to soldiers, Napoleon planned an order to requite civil services, the Order of the Reunion, worn with a blue ribbon, though Providence thwarted that profanation. After Bridau’s death the Emperor inquired into his widow’s circumstances: both sons received scholarships at the Imperial Lyceum paid from the Emperor’s privy purse, and Madame Bridau was given a pension of four thousand francs.From her marriage to her husband’s death Agathe had had no communication with Issoudun. Her mother died as she was about to bear her youngest son, and when her father died, the coronation of the Emperor kept Bridau busy and Agathe unwilling to leave him. Her brother Jean-Jacques had not written. Once a year she received a letter from her godmother Madame Hochon, to which she replied with commonplaces and paid little heed to the pious advice therein.Some time before Doctor Rouget’s death Madame Hochon warned Agathe she would get nothing from her father’s estate unless she gave a power of attorney to Monsieur Hochon. Agathe was reluctant to harass her brother. Whether because Bridau thought the spoliation legal or because he shared his wife’s magnanimity, he would not listen to Roguin, his notary, who urged him to use his ministerial position to contest the deeds by which the father had deprived his daughter. Thus husband and wife tacitly sanctioned the transfer. Roguin, however, forced Bridau to reflect that if he died first Agathe would be left without property. Reviewing their finances, Bridau found that between 1793 and 1805 they had used nearly thirty thousand of the fifty thousand francs that old Rouget had given to Agathe at her marriage. He invested the remaining twenty thousand in the public funds then quoted at forty, from which Agathe received about two thousand francs a year. As a widow, she could live suitably on an income of six thousand francs. With provincial good sense she planned to change residence, dismiss the footman, and keep only a cook; but her intimate friend Madame Descoings, insisting on being considered her aunt, sold her own establishment and came to live with Agathe, taking Bridau’s study as her bedroom.The two widows pooled their revenues, possessing jointly about twelve thousand francs a year. Such simple, “natural” measures often deserve attention, for fools excuse themselves by calling wrongs natural. Behold the serpent amid flowers.In 1809 Madame Descoings, who never told her age, was sixty-five. Once called a beauty, she had kept her looks by constitution; medium height, plump, fresh, with fine shoulders and a rather rosy complexion, her blond hair showed no gray despite her husband’s fate. She loved good cheer, the theatre, and a secret vice—lottery shares. Like women lucky enough to stay young, she spent on dress and was the pleasantest of companions, never opposing anyone and giving and taking a jest. If she did not marry again, it was no doubt the times: during the Empire marrying men found young women too easily.Madame Descoings often cheered Madame Bridau with theatre and drives, prepared excellent dinners, and even tried to marry her to her own son by her first husband, Bixiou. To do this she revealed a family secret carefully kept by her, her late husband, and their notary: her son Bixiou, thirty-five and a widowed major who later perished at Lützen, had a son—a grandson she secretly saw and claimed as the child of her first wife. That boy, educated with Agathe’s sons at the Imperial Lyceum on a half-scholarship, proved clever, witty, and gifted as a draughtsman and designer.Agathe, who lived only for her children, declined to remarry out of fidelity to her husband and good sense. But being a good mother is harder than being a good wife; a widow must both love and command her children. Few women are firm enough to perform that double duty. Thus Agathe, despite many virtues, was the innocent cause of grave unhappiness. Through her lack of intelligence and blind confidence, she fell victim to Madame Descoings, who nursed a combination of three numbers in a lottery and used the joint household money to pay stakes, hoping at last to win and enrich her grandson, Agathe, and the little Bridaus.When the debts reached ten thousand francs she increased her stakes; failing to win, the debt rolled up. At twenty thousand francs she lost her head, still hoping for a trey. She attempted to mortgage her property to help, but Roguin, the notary, showed that the late Doctor Rouget had secured her a life-interest only by disinheriting Madame Descoings in favor of his son Jean-Jacques. No money-lender would advance twenty thousand francs to a sixty-six-year-old on an annuity of about four thousand when ten percent could be obtained elsewhere. One morning Madame Descoings fell at Agathe’s feet and, sobbing, confessed the state of things. Agathe reproached her not; she dismissed the footman and cook, sold all but the bare necessities of her furniture, sold three-fourths of her government funds, paid the debts, and bade farewell to her appartement.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################One of the worst corners in Paris was the section of the rue Mazarin between the rue Guenegard and the rue de Seine, behind the Institute. The high gray walls of the college and Cardinal Mazarin’s library cast chill shadows; the sun seldom shone and the north wind blew. The poor ruined widow, Madame Bridau, took a third-floor lodging in a shallow house at that damp, dark corner. Opposite were the Institute buildings with the studios of young artists—rapins—still there. From Madame Bridau’s windows one could peer into those melancholy barred cages. Looking north the view closed on the dome of the Institute; up the street the only movement was a file of hackney-coaches. For a while she planted boxes of earth at the windows, cultivating illicit aerial gardens whose vegetables improved the air. The staircase wound round upon itself; the third floor was the last. Three windows opened to three rooms—dining-room, a small salon, and a chamber on one side of the landing; on the other a little kitchen and two single rooms; above, an immense garret without partitions. She chose this lodging because it cost only four hundred francs a year on a nine-year lease, because it was near her sons’ school, and because she was used to the quarter.The interior matched the outside. The dining-room, hung with yellow paper patterned with small green flowers and floored with unglazed tiles, contained only a table, two sideboards, and six chairs brought from the other apartment. The salon had an Aubusson carpet given to Bridau when the ministry was refurnished, and a common mahogany sofa with Egyptian heads covered in green silk. Over it hung an amateur pastel portrait of Bridau that at once drew the eye: the noble, obscure brow, serene yet proud eyes, prudent lips, and frank smile suggested a man of duty—“Justum et tenacem,” as the Emperor had said. On the opposite wall, over a card-table, flashed a picture of the Emperor in brilliant colors by Vernet, Napoleon riding rapidly, attended by his escort.Agathe had given herself two large birdcages—canaries and Java sparrows—and adopted this childish fancy since her husband’s loss. After three months the widowed chamber had become a litter: cats domiciled on the sofa; canaries, occasionally let loose, left droppings on the furniture. She scattered millet and chickweed and put tidbits for the cats in broken saucers. Garments lay everywhere. Everything of Bridau’s was scrupulously preserved: his desk implements were kept with the care one might give a knight’s armor; she had wrapped and sealed the last pen he used and marked it so. The cup from which he drank his last draught stood on the fireplace. After his death no trace of coquetry or ordinary self-care remained in the young widow of thirty-five. Parted from the only man she had known and loved—one who had never caused her unhappiness—she seemed to have renounced her womanhood. Some people, through love, transfer the self to another; when that other dies, no life of their own remains.Agathe now lived only for her children and was infinitely sad at the privations their ruin would bring. From the move to the rue Mazarin a melancholy took her face. She hoped a little in the Emperor; he could do no more than he already was doing—giving three hundred francs a year to each child from his privy purse, besides scholarships.The brilliant Descoings occupied an apartment on the second floor, similar to her niece’s above. She had assigned Madame Bridau three thousand francs out of her annuity; Roguin, the notary, arranged it, but it would take seven years of slow repayment to make good the loss. The Descoingses, reduced to an income of twelve hundred francs, lived modestly with her niece. They employed a woman-of-all-work for mornings only; Madame Descoings liked to cook. In evenings a few old friends—persons at the ministry who owed their places to Bridau—came for cards with the two widows. Madame Descoings still cherished her trey, declaring it obstinate about turning up; she hoped by one stroke to repay the loan she’d made upon her niece. She was dear to the Bridaus partly from a feeling of wrong she had done them and partly because her niece never reproached her under privation. So Philippe and Joseph were cosseted; the old gambler of the Imperial Lottery cooked them nice dinners with sweets. Later, they could extract small sums from her—one for pencils and paper, the elder for tennis-shoes and marbles. Madame Descoings’s passion forced her to be content with fifty francs a month for domestic expenses so she could gamble with the rest.Madame Bridau, by motherly love, kept her expenses to the same sum. As penance for former overconfidence she cut off little enjoyments. Timid and distrusting, she exaggerated risk into virtue. She feared the Emperor might die or forget them; she shuddered at the risk her children ran of being left without means. Unable to understand Roguin’s assurance that in seven years Descoings’s assignment would restore the sold sum, she trusted neither notary, aunt, nor government; she trusted only herself and practiced privation. By laying aside three thousand francs every year from her pension she fancied she would have thirty thousand in ten years, yielding fifteen hundred a year to her children. At thirty-six she might live twenty years more; if she kept to the plan she could leave each child enough for bare necessities.Thus the two widows passed from hollow opulence to voluntary poverty—one through a vice, the other through the purest virtue. These petty details matter: the view into student dens below, the tumult of the rapins, the need to look up at the sky to escape the damp street, the presence of that portrait full of soul, the sight of rich old colors in the calm home, the mother’s preference for her elder child and opposition to the younger’s tastes—these facts prefaced this history and perhaps explain Joseph Bridau, one of the greatest painters of the modern French school.Philippe, the elder, strikingly resembled his mother. Blond and blue-eyed, he had a daring look taken for intrepidity and courage. Old Claparon, who had entered the ministry with Bridau and was one of the whist players, tapped Philippe’s cheek and prophesied he’d “stand to his guns.” Stimulated, the boy assumed a resolute manner, became adroit at bodily exercises; fights at the Lyceum taught endurance and contempt for pain, laying the foundation of military valor. He acquired a distaste for study; public education failed to develop mind and body together.Agathe believed physical resemblance implied moral likeness; she confidently expected delicacy of feeling combined with manly vigor. Philippe was fifteen when the family moved to rue Mazarin; his winning ways confirmed maternal belief. Joseph, three years younger, resembled his father but only in defective ways. His thick black hair was always disorderly; Philippe’s was neat. By some fatality Joseph could not keep his clothes clean; dress him in new clothes and he made them look old. The elder took care of his things out of vanity. Unconsciously Agathe scolded Joseph and held up his brother as example; she never treated them alike. Fetching them from school she worried especially about Joseph. Such trifles drove her into maternal preference.No one among the ordinary circle—Du Bruel, Claparon, Desroches, or even the Abbé Loraux—noticed Joseph’s faculty for observation. Absorbed in his own tastes, the future colorist paid no attention to himself; during childhood this torpor alarmed his father. His face, which later gained originality, wore a sullen expression. The features were pinched, contracted by the close attention Joseph paid to his surroundings. Philippe flattered his mother with clever sayings and lively answers, prompting hope. Joseph was taciturn, a dreamer. The mother hoped great things of Philippe and expected nothing of Joseph.Joseph’s leaning to art began simply. During Easter holidays of 1812, returning from a walk in the Tuileries with his brother and Madame Descoings, he saw a pupil drawing a caricature of a professor on the wall of the Institute and stopped in admiration. The next day he stood at the window watching pupils enter by the rue Mazarin door; then slipped into the long courtyard with statues, busts, half-finished marbles, plasters, and baked clays; he gazed feverishly—his instinct awakened, his vocation stirred. He entered a ground-floor room with a half-open door where a dozen young men drew from a statue; they began to make fun of him.“Hi! little one,” cried the first, scattering crumbs.They mocked his ugliness. For a quarter hour Joseph bore the teasing in Chaudet’s atelier. But his persistency and expression struck the pupils; when asked what he wanted, Joseph said he wished to learn to draw. They encouraged him. Won by friendliness, he said he was Madame Bridau’s son.“Oh! if you are Madame Bridau’s son,” they cried, “you will certainly be a great man!” They teased and tested him—one pupil set up a “telegraph trial,” stretching Joseph’s arms and daring him to hold them so. Joseph, at thirteen, stood motionless for five minutes amid shouts; at ten minutes sweat stood on his brow. Then a bald-headed, pale, sickly man entered—Chaudet. He lowered Joseph’s arms, patted him, and asked what brought him. Hearing Joseph wanted to be an artist and was Madame Bridau’s son, Chaudet, struck by recollection of Bridau’s service, welcomed him. He gave him a board, paper, and chalks, sent a pupil for cakes and sugar-plums, and petted the child. The scene—rough, warm-hearted, and real—impressed Joseph. Chaudet, for whom the Emperor’s protection earlier opened prospects later cut short by premature death, appeared like a vision. Joseph told his mother nothing, but he spent two hours every Sunday and Thursday in Chaudet’s atelier. Madame Descoings, who humored the boy’s fancies, supplied pencils, red chalks, prints, and paper. At school he sketched masters, drew comrades, and showed surprising assiduity in drawing-class. Lemire, his drawing-master, reported Joseph’s faculty to Madame Bridau. She, like a true provincial with little knowledge of art, was terrified. When Lemire left, she wept.“What is to become of me!” she cried to Madame Descoings. “Joseph, whom I meant to make a government clerk—protected by his father’s memory at the ministry—wants to be a painter, a vagabond! I always knew that child would give me nothing but trouble.”Madame Descoings confessed she had encouraged Joseph’s passion, taking him to the Salon where his interest in pictures was, she declared, miraculous.“If he understands painting at thirteen,” she said, “your Joseph will be a man of genius.”“Yes; and see what genius did for his father—killed him with overwork at forty!” Agathe answered.As Joseph entered his fourteenth year, Agathe, against Madame Descoings’s entreaties, went to Chaudet and requested he cease to “debauch” her son. She found the sculptor in a blue smock modeling his last statue, working with spasmodic jerks born of passion. He received the widow roughly at first but, moved by Bridau’s memory and by devotion to craftsmanship, he burst into holy wrath at her plea. He declared that a real vocation was a call, that an artist was like a king in his realm, happier and independent, and that to force Joseph into a clerk’s life would be to murder him. He dismissed the work for the day and refused to give up the boy’s training.Agathe, alarmed by the sight of a nude woman in a corner, left horrified. Chaudet told his pupils she was not to bring the little Bridau any more; they jeered as she closed the door. When the students learned she opposed the Institute, their happiness turned to the project of getting Joseph among them. He often slipped into Regnauld the painter’s studio where he was encouraged to paint. When the widow complained the bargain was not kept, Chaudet’s pupils insisted they were not Chaudet and composed a long mocking song about her.That evening Agathe refused cards and sat drowned in tears.“What is the matter, Madame Bridau?” asked old Claparon.“She thinks her boy will have to beg his bread because he has the bump of painting,” Madame Descoings said. The men offered their usual advice: let the boy follow his bent; if not a genius, he could later be placed in an office. Desroches spoke of keeping a son hard at work to make a man of him; Du Bruel warned against letting a boy choose a profession at his age. Claparon and others counselled resignation to Joseph’s tastes. Their unanimity only increased Agathe’s anxieties. She feared the atelier’s naked women; Madame Descoings joked about “good fires.”Soon after, the disasters of the retreat from Moscow became known; Napoleon returned to Paris to organize troops. Philippe, tired of school, wanted to serve the Emperor; he saw a review at the Tuileries—the last Napoleon ever held—and became infatuated with soldier’s life. Military splendor and uniforms seduced him. Without his mother’s knowledge he wrote a petition to the Emperor asking to enter the army. Within twenty-four hours the Emperor sent him to the Imperial Lyceum at Saint-Cyr; six months later, in November 1813, he was appointed sub-lieutenant in a cavalry regiment. He spent that winter in cantonments, learned to ride, and was dispatched to the front. During the campaign in France he was made lieutenant after an outpost affair where his bravery saved his colonel’s life. The Emperor named him captain at La Fere-Champenoise and took him on his staff. Inspired, Philippe won the cross at Montereau, witnessed Napoleon’s farewell at Fontainebleau, refused to serve the Bourbons, and returned in July 1814 to find his mother ruined.Joseph’s scholarship was withdrawn after the holidays; Madame Bridau vainly entreated that it be inscribed on the ministry rolls. Joseph, more devoted than ever to painting, begged to go to Regnauld and promised to earn his living, saying he was advanced enough in the second class to do without rhetoric. Philippe, a captain at nineteen and decorated, who had served the Emperor as aide-de-camp in two battles, flattered the mother immensely. Coarse and blustering, without merit beyond cavalry bravery, he was to her a man of genius; whereas Joseph, puny, sickly, unkempt, and dreaming an artist’s glory, would only worry her.The winter of 1814–1815 favored Joseph. Secretly encouraged by Madame Descoings and Bixiou, a pupil of Gros, he worked in that celebrated atelier, forming an intimacy with Schinner. The return from Elba came; Captain Bridau joined the Emperor at Lyons, accompanied him to the Tuileries, and commanded a squadron in the Guard’s dragoons. After Waterloo—where he was slightly wounded and took the officer’s Legion of Honor—he was near Marshal Davoust at Saint-Denis and, through Davoust’s intercession, retained his cross and rank though placed on half-pay.Joseph, anxious about his future, studied with ardor through these turbulent times, sometimes to the point of illness.“It is the smell of the paints,” Agathe said to Madame Descoings. “He ought to give up a business so injurious to his health.”Agathe’s anxieties remained for her lieutenant-colonel son. When he returned in 1816 reduced from a nine-thousand-franc commander’s salary to half-pay, she fitted attic rooms for him and spent her savings. Philippe belonged to the faithful Bonapartes of café Lemblin; he acquired the habits and airs of a half-pay officer, exaggerated them, spurned the Bourbons, and refused infantry opportunities. To his mother he seemed noble. “The father himself could have done no more,” she said.Philippe’s half-pay sufficed; he cost little at home, whereas Joseph’s expenses were paid by the widows. From then Agathe’s preference for Philippe was openly shown. Up to that time it had been secret; but the colonel’s persecution for the Emperor and his wound drew forth maternal tenderness. “He is unfortunate” explained everything to her. Joseph, simple in the artist’s way and brought up to admire his big brother, was not hurt by maternal preference and shared his worship of the hero who had carried Napoleon’s orders. How could he doubt the superiority of the brother he had seen in green and gold, commanding on the Champ de Mars?Agathe, notwithstanding preference, was an excellent mother. She loved Joseph, though she could not understand him. Joseph adored his mother; Philippe let her adore him. Toward her the dragoon softened his military roughness yet never concealed contempt for Joseph—expressed in a patronizing, almost friendly way. He nicknamed the weak, shrunken seventeen-year-old “Cub.” Philippe’s patronizing manners would have wounded anyone less indifferent than Joseph, who believed soldiers hid goodness beneath brutal exteriors. Joseph did not yet know that soldiers of genius can be gentle and courteous; all genius is alike.“Poor boy!” said Philippe to his mother, “we mustn’t plague him; let him do as he likes.” To Agathe, the colonel’s contempt seemed fraternal affection. “Philippe will always love and protect his brother,” she thought.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In 1816 Joseph obtained his mother’s permission to convert the garret next to his attic into an atelier; Madame Descoings contributed some money. The two widows treated painting as a trade, but Joseph arranged the humble studio himself: Madame Descoings persuaded the owner to add a skylight; Joseph painted the hall chocolate and hung sketches. Agathe reluctantly installed an iron stove so her son could work at home without abandoning Gros’s or Schinner’s studios.The constitutional party, backed by half-pay officers and Bonapartists, stirred emeutes around the Chamber of Deputies; conspiracies were rumored. Philippe, flirting with these affairs, was arrested and released for lack of proof; the minister of war restored him to the active list, ending his half-pay. France seemed unsafe; Agathe feared provocateurs. Philippe spent time in disaffected cafés, drinking and acquiring bad habits, while Agathe lived in mortal dread. The Grecian sages still visited Madame Bridau nightly; Claparon, retained at the ministry though cautious, whispered Moniteur news. Desroches, retired after old Du Bruel, still fought for his pension. Their friends urged Agathe to send Philippe abroad.“They talk of conspiracies, and your son will be certain to fall a victim,” they warned.“Philippe is cut from the wood the Emperor made into marshals,” Du Bruel replied; “he mustn’t give up his profession. Let him serve in the East, in India—”“Think of his health,” Agathe answered.“Why doesn’t he get some place, some business?” Desroches asked. “There are private offices to be had.” Agathe insisted Philippe was a soldier and wouldn’t want anything else. Du Bruel suggested royal favor could make him lieutenant-colonel, though Madame Descoings doubted his prospects without noble birth.To please his mother, Philippe took up the notion of joining General Lallemand in the United States and helping found the Champ d’Asile, one of the most disastrous swindles carried off under the name of a national subscription. Agathe gave ten thousand francs and saw him off at Havre. By the end of 1817 she lived on the six hundred francs left from her funds and, by prudent investment, secured seven percent on some savings. Joseph emulated her frugality: dressed plainly, denied himself comforts, and lived on bread, milk, and Brie; only Madame Descoings and Bixiou showed sympathy.Joseph later recalled how the coming summer saved him the cost of charcoal. Now a colorist as capable as Gros, he consulted his master only occasionally. He was already planning a revolt against classical traditions, aiming to free art to represent Nature — a struggle that began with his first Salon exhibition and never ceased.1818 was terrible for the family. Roguin, Madame Descoings’s notary, absconded with seven years’ withheld annuity funds. Three days later a bill of exchange for a thousand francs arrived from New York: Philippe had lost everything in the Champ d’Asile. The letter told of debts contracted and comrades who had indorsed for him. Agathe, blaming herself, wept. The two women faced selling silver, linen, and furniture. Joseph, alarmed, told Gérard, who secured an order for two royal copies of Louis XVIII’s portrait at five hundred francs each and supplied Joseph with materials. Joseph painted four panels in ten days, sold them, and brought his mother the thousand francs to meet Philippe’s draft. Then came a letter announcing Philippe’s imminent return on a packet whose captain had trusted him for passage; he would need a thousand francs at Havre.Joseph promised his mother the money after finishing the copies. Agathe, ecstatically grateful, kissed him and called him “his father all over.” Madame Bridau rushed to Havre and watched beyond the round tower daily for the packet. The sight of home softened Philippe, but his misfortunes in Texas and New York had developed his worst traits: he had grown brutal, selfish, rude, drank and smoked excessively, and imagined perpetual persecution. Such men either live by honest virtues or by treating every want as a necessity; Philippe had drifted into the latter, becoming cunning and self-centered while keeping an appearance of frank soldierly simplicity. His stature had grown somewhat rotund, his face bronzed, his speech terse and decisive; exposed to hardship, he had become the kind of man who makes himself feared. To his mother he remained a hero; in truth he had become, bluntly, a blackguard.At Havre Agathe bought him clothes and could not, at first, restrain his indulgence. The scheme of conquering Texas with imperial remnants had failed less for lack of idea than for treachery and poor management; the Liberals had used the enterprise selfishly, abandoning the devoted heroes. Agathe, a household manager with shrewd common sense, saw through the political chicane and recognized the subscription’s failure; she could not make her son see he had been duped rather than persecuted. During the costly week at Havre she could not persuade him to make terms with the royal government. She brought him back to Paris before her funds gave out.On arrival, those awaiting them noted how much Agathe had aged. Philippe greeted Madame Descoings coolly. Joseph, now twenty-one and confident, was hurt by his brother’s contempt. At a family dinner the friends — Du Bruel, Claparon, Desroches, and the Abbe Loraux — observed the exile’s roughened manners and coarse voice. While Joseph prepared the card tables, the intimate friends asked Agathe what she intended for Philippe. She could not get him to serve the Bourbons; Du Bruel suggested other roles like writing plays, as his son had once done. Philippe, Bixiou, and young Desroches played écarté. Madame Descoings, eager to celebrate the prodigal’s return, provided funds for play; Joseph secretly slipped twenty francs to Philippe.The evening grew lively. Philippe, a poor player, ended owing fifty francs. He showed bad temper and quarreled with young Desroches and then with Desroches senior, who forbade further play though his son was right. Madame Descoings silenced her grandson’s barbed witticisms for fear of provoking the colonel. Philippe's temper and gambling continued into the night.The next morning the two widows, counting resources, resolved to economize: Madame Descoings proposed selling furniture and giving up apartments to save seven hundred francs a year and pay Philippe fifty francs monthly until he found something. They pooled incomes — about five thousand three hundred francs, four thousand from Madame Descoings as a life annuity — and had consumed their savings. Philippe promised to find a situation and asked only for board and lodging.
