The château, once almost a town with walls and moats, remains a distinct quarter entered by ancient gateways via three bridges across two river arms, resembling an old city. Ramparts show formidable foundations on which houses have sprung. Above the château stands the famous tower, once the citadel. Conquering the city, which lay around these two fortified points, still left the tower and castle to be won.The Saint-Paterne suburb, a palette-shaped extension beyond the tower encroaching on meadows, was likely part of the ancient city. In 1822 this seemed certain because of the then-existence of the charming church of Saint-Paterne, recently pulled down by the heir of the buyer from the nation. This Romanesque church, one of France’s finest, perished without a single drawing of its preserved portal. The lone voice raised to save it found no echo in town or department. Though the château appears like an old town with narrow streets and mansions, the main city, repeatedly captured and burned—especially during the Fronde—has a modern air. Spacious streets and well-built houses contrast with the citadel’s aspect, earning Issoudun the epithet “pretty” in some geographies.In such a town, lacking activity, taste for art, or learned occupations, many young men after the Restoration in 1816 had no career until marriage or inheritance. Bored at home and lacking distraction, they “shed their cuticle” by sowing wild oats at the town’s expense. Avoiding daytime escapades to prevent recognition and arraignment before police courts, they chose the night. Thus, amid traces of lost civilizations, a last flare of drollery burst forth.These young men amused themselves as courts and soldiers once did, banding together for mutual help and tricks. They became an organized conspiracy—the “Knights of Idleness.” By day they played saints, sleeping late after nights of mischief. The Knights began with commonplace pranks—unhooking and changing signs, ringing bells, flinging casks into neighbors’ cellars, rousing inhabitants with crashes suggesting explosions. In Issoudun, cellars are entered by openings near the door, covered by a wooden scuttle with iron hinges and padlock.In 1816 such pranks persisted. But in 1817 the Order acquired a Grand Master and, until 1823, Maxence Gilet, called Max, led mischief that spread a sort of terror in Issoudun and kept artisans and bourgeois perpetually uneasy.Maxence Gilet’s background, energy, and bearing suited him for the role. Town rumor supposed him the natural son of the sub-delegate Lousteau—the brother of Madame Hochon whose gallantries were remembered—and some said he might be Doctor Rouget’s son; in truth his father was a dragoon officer garrisoned at Bourges. Because Rouget and Lousteau had been close friends, both claimed paternity, leaving the matter undecided. Max’s mother, wife of a poor sabot-maker in the Rome suburb, was strikingly beautiful—a Trasteverine beauty she transmitted to her son. Madame Gilet, pregnant with Max in 1788, enjoyed the town’s attributions of his paternity, perhaps to set reputed fathers against each other; she colluded with old Gilet to keep protectors ignorant. Madame Hochon paid sixty francs a year for the lad’s schooling—a liberality attributed to her brother at Sancerre.Doctor Rouget, unlucky in sons, seeing Max’s beauty, paid the youngster’s seminary board until 1805. Lousteau died in 1800, and the doctor continued payments, leaving paternity unresolved. Max, the butt of jests, was soon forgotten when in 1806, a year after Rouget’s death, he began a venturesome life. Gifted with strength and agility, he plotted with Monsieur Hochon’s grandsons to vex grocers, gathered fruit before owners could pick it, scaled walls, excelled at physical exercises, and loved hunting. He spent the doctor’s money on powder and shot for a pistol old Gilet had given him. In autumn 1806, at seventeen, Max committed an involuntary homicide: startling a pregnant woman in dusk while stealing fruit, her death was blamed on him. Threatened with the guillotine by Gilet, Max fled to Bourges, enlisted with a regiment bound for Egypt, and nothing came of the woman’s death.In three campaigns Max distinguished himself and rose to captain; his lack of formal education did not impede him. In Portugal, 1809, he was left for dead in an English battery. Taken prisoner, he was sent to the Spanish hulks at Cabrera, the most horrible prison station. Friends petitioned for the Legion of Honor and major’s rank, but the Emperor, campaigning in Austria and displeased with Portuguese events, withheld favors from those taken prisoner. Max was at Cabrera from 1810 to 1814. There he was demoralized: the hulks were like galleys, full of crime and infamy. To maintain will and protect against corruption, he killed in duels seven bullies among his fellow-prisoners, ridding the island of tyrants. He then reigned in his hulk by skill with weapons, strength, and cunning.Though deprived of good example, he returned innocent when peace came in April 1814. He found his parents dead in the almshouse and destitute. At Napoleon’s landing at Cannes he went to Paris to ask for rank and decoration. A marshal then minister of war, recalling Max’s conduct in Portugal, put him in the Guard as captain—giving him the grade of major in infantry—but could not secure the cross. “The Emperor says that you will know how to win it at the first chance,” said the marshal. Indeed, Napoleon put Max on the decoration list the evening after Fleurus, where he distinguished himself.After Waterloo Max retreated to the Loire. At disbandment Marshal Feltre refused to recognize his grade or cross. Enraged, Max declared he would not serve without rank or decoration. The war-office deemed his demands presumptuous; he resigned, losing half-pay. Issoudun was roused at seeing the brave young man left with only twenty napoleons; the mayor gave him a six-hundred-franc post, which Max held a few months before giving it up. Captain Carpentier, a faithful Napoleonic officer, replaced him.By now Max was grand master of the Knights of Idleness and lived in ways that lost him the bourgeoisie’s goodwill, though none openly told him, for he was feared, even by officers who, like him, had refused to serve the Bourbons and returned to Berry. Issoudun, small and unimportant, contained more Bonapartists than any town in France; these men became, as is known, nearly all Liberals.In Issoudun and vicinity a dozen officers shared Max’s position. They admired and followed him, except Captain Carpentier and Major Mignonnet. Carpentier, a cavalry officer risen from the ranks, had married into the town’s best family; Mignonnet, from the École Polytechnique, served in a corps that held itself superior. In the Imperial armies two types existed: a majority who despised civilians, and others—particularly artillerymen—more republican and not adopting the military-civil dichotomy. Thus Major Potel and Captain Renard befriended Max, while Mignonnet and Carpentier sided with the bourgeoisie, judging his conduct dishonorable.Major Mignonnet, lean and dignified, busied himself with steam-engine problems and lived modestly with Monsieur and Madame Carpentier. His gentle manners and scientific pursuits won town respect; he and Carpentier were often said to be “quite another thing” from Potel, Renard, Max, and the café Militaire habitués, who retained soldierly manners and the Empire’s defective morals.When Madame Bridau returned to Issoudun, Max was excluded from society. He showed self-respect by not appearing at the club or complaining, though he was the town’s handsomest, best-dressed man, spent much money, and kept a horse—a rarity at Issoudun as Byron’s horse in Venice. We shall see how Max, poor and apparently without means, became the town’s dandy. His disgrace, earning bourgeois and religious contempt, connected with interests that brought Agathe and Joseph to Issoudun.Judging by his audacious bearing and face, Max cared little for public opinion; he expected revenge and to lord it over those who condemned him. Although the bourgeoisie thought ill of him, the common people admired his courage, daring, and decision—his degradations were mostly unknown, even to them. Max played a role like the blacksmith in The Fair Maid of Perth: champion of Bonapartism and Opposition, counted on in great occasions.An incident illustrates his heroism. In 1819 a battalion commanded by royalist officers, fresh from the Maison Rouge, passed through Issoudun to Bourges. They lingered at the café Militaire—rendezvous of Bonapartists—where they asked for royalist papers. The fat landlady refused, offering the “Commerce” (the “Constitutionnel”). A captain tore it up, spat on it, and cried, “Bring dominos!” Word spread: “Let us tell Max!” Max entered the café with Major Potel and Captain Renard, followed by thirty young men. After a little comedy about the newspaper, Max, enraged, slapped the nearest officer and fought. They fought in the allée de Frapesle, three against three; Max killed his man. Major Potel wounded another who died in hospital; the third escaped. The battalion left that night. The affair spread through Berry and firmly established Max as a hero.The Knights of Idleness, all young—the eldest not more than twenty-five—admired Max. Some envied his position. Under such a leader they accomplished great mischief. After May 1817, rarely a week passed without uproar. Max imposed conditions; statutes were drawn. These youths trained like pupils of Amoros—bold, agile, clever and strong as criminals. They practised roof-climbing, scaling houses, noiseless walking, mixing mortar, walling up doors, and collected ropes, ladders, tools, and disguises. They perfected invention and execution of pranks, achieving a Panurge-like genius for evil that provoked laughter while covering victims with ridicule and silencing complaints. Being sons of good families, they had household information facilitating outrages.Sometimes they lay in ambush along the Grand’rue or the Basse rue, towns’ arteries. Crouched in wall angles at the hour households slept, they called along the town in tones of terror: “What’s the matter?” “What is it?” repeated cries woke citizens who hurried out in shirts and caps with lights, asking questions and showing queer faces.A poor old bookbinder who believed in hobgoblins worked in a small basement shop. The Knights, disguised as devils, invaded at night, put him in his cutting-press, and left him shrieking as if among hell's souls. He roused the neighbors with tales of Lucifer and was nearly driven insane.In a severe winter the Knights took down the collector of taxes’ chimney and rebuilt it overnight so noiselessly that they left no trace, but arranged the flue to send all smoke into the house. The collector suffered two months before discovering the cause and had to rebuild a new chimney.Another time they put three trusses of hay dusted with brimstone and oiled paper down the chimney of a pious old woman friend of Madame Hochon. In the morning, lighting her fire, she thought she had started a volcano. Fire-engines came; the population rushed to help; Knights among the firemen deluged her house, frightening her as much with water as with the “fire.” She grew ill with fear.To keep someone on guard and in terror, they wrote anonymous letters warning of an impending robbery, then stole softly around his house or under his windows, whistling as if to call each other.A famous performance was sending letters to all heirs of a miserly old woman likely to leave large property, announcing her death and requesting they be present when the seals were affixed. Eighty persons arrived from neighboring towns in deep mourning—widows, sons, children, some in carrioles or carts. Imagine the scene between servants and arrivals and the notaries’ consultations; it created a sort of riot.At last the sub-prefect waked to the intolerable state, made worse by the difficulty of discovering perpetrators. Suspicion fell on several youths; but the National Guard was a mere name, there was no garrison, and the lieutenant of police had only eight gendarmes—no patrols—so proof was impossible. The sub-prefect was made an object of mockery. He breakfasted on two fresh eggs, kept chickens, boiled eggs himself with watch in hand, boasting his egg-boiling skill. For two years he had boiled eggs successfully, earning many witticisms. Then, for a month, each night his eggs were taken and replaced by hard-boiled ones, ruining his reputation as the “sous-préfet à l’oeuf.” He lost his egg-boiling laurels and never suspected the Knights, who had planned instead. Max greased the sub-prefect’s stoves nightly with fetid oil, making the house unbearable. His wife, going to mass one morning, found her shawl glued inside by a tenacious substance and had to go without it. The sub-prefect finally requested transfer. His cowardly submissiveness helped establish the Knights’ weird and comic authority.Beyond the rue des Minimes and the place Misère lay a quarter enclosed between an arm of the “Rivière forcée” and the ramparts, from the place d’Armes to the pottery market. This square was filled with poor houses crowded together, divided by streets so narrow two abreast could not pass. This quarter, a sort of cour des Miracles, housed poor people and lowly trades—hovels and “blind houses.” It has been, from earliest ages, an accursed quarter, haunt of evil-doers; one thoroughfare is called “the street of the Executioner.” For five centuries it’s been customary for the executioner to have a red door. The executioner of Châteauroux’s assistant is said to live there, seen rarely; vine-dressers alone keep intercourse with him, for he inherits the craft of curing wounds and fractures. In Issoudun’s heyday women wandered this quarter. It was home to second-hand sellers and old-clothes dealers whose wares infected the air; in short, the rendezvous of that apocryphal population found in such city parts, where two or three Jews sometimes gain ascendancy.At a corner in the livelier half of the quarter existed, from 1815 to 1823 and perhaps later, a public-house kept by a woman called Mère Cognette. The house was well built of white stone with ashlar and cement infill, one storey with an attic; over the door hung a huge pine branch like a Florentine bronze. A blue poster showed, above the words “Good Beer of Mars,” a soldier pouring foam in an arched jet toward a very décolletée woman—an image to stir the imagination.The ground floor was an immense hall serving as kitchen and dining-room, from whose beams provisions hung on huge nails. A winding staircase led to the upper storey; at the foot a door opened into a low, long room lit from a narrow, sunken provincial court like a chimney flue. Hidden by a shed and walls, this low room was where the Bad Boys held their plenary court. Ostensibly Pere Cognet boarded country folk on market-days; secretly he was landlord to the Knights. Pere Cognet, formerly a groom in a rich household, had married La Cognette, a cook in a good family. In the Rome suburb the town follows the Latin custom of placing a feminine termination on the husband’s name and giving it to the wife.By uniting savings Pere Cognet and his spouse bought their house. La Cognette, about forty, tall and plump, with a Roxelane nose, swarthy skin, jet-black hair, lively brown eyes and a mirthful intelligence, was chosen by Max as the Order’s Leonarde for her character and cookery. Pere Cognet, about fifty-six, thick-set and under his wife’s rule—“he only sees things with a good eye,” she joked, being blind of the other—kept absolute silence about nightly gatherings. From 1816 to 1823 neither revealed what went on. Their devotion was absolute, but self-interest secured their affection and silence. Whenever the Knights knocked a certain way Pere Cognet would get up, light the fire and candles, open the door, and fetch a particular wine laid in for the Order; La Cognette prepared an excellent supper, eaten before or after expeditions planned the previous evening or day.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################While Joseph and Madame Bridau were traveling from Orléans to Issoudun, the Knights of Idleness played one of their best tricks. An old Spaniard, a former prisoner of war who lived near Issoudun and sold grain, left his empty cart at the foot of the tower early one morning. Maxence, who had come for a rendezvous of the Knights there, was asked in a whisper, “What are we to do tonight?”“Here’s Père Fario’s cart,” he answered. “I nearly cracked my shins over it. Let us get it up on the embankment of the tower first, and we’ll decide afterwards.”When Richard Cœur-de-Lion built the Issoudun tower he raised it on the ruins of a basilica, itself above Roman and Celtic remains; these ruins make a cone of monuments of three ages. The tower stands on the apex of this cone with steep descents, reached only by steps; toward the town its pedestal was eighty feet high. In an hour the Knights took the cart off its wheels and hoisted it, piece by piece, to the top of the embankment at the tower’s base—a feat like soldiers carrying artillery over a mountain pass. The cart was remounted; then the Knights, hungry and thirsty, returned to Mère Cognette’s to laugh at how Père Fario would fume when he came for his barrow in the morning.The Knights did not pull pranks every night; three clever fellows could not invent 365 mischiefs a year. Sometimes the moon shone, or a prank had angered their betters; sometimes a relation was involved and a member refused to join. But if they did not meet nightly, they gathered by day for hunting, the vintages, and winter skating. Among these twenty youths, some were closer to Maxence and made him their idol. Two grandsons of Madame Hochon—François Hochon and Baruch Borniche—were his henchmen. Accepting family gossip about Lousteau’s left-handed parentage, they regarded Max as kin. Max lent them money, took them hunting, and exercised more influence than their grandfather did. Both were orphans kept under Monsieur Hochon’s guardianship for reasons to be explained later.That night François and Baruch sat one on each side of Max at the middle of a table lit by four tallow candles. A dozen or fifteen bottles had just been drunk; only eleven Knights were present. Baruch—his name showing the Calvinist memory in Issoudun—said to Max as the wine loosened tongues, “You are threatened in your stronghold.”“What do you mean by that?” asked Max.“My grandmother has had a letter from Madame Bridau, her goddaughter, saying she and her son are coming here. My grandmother’s getting two rooms ready.”“What’s that to me?” Max asked, gulping his glass with a comic gesture.Max then was thirty-four. A candle lit his soldierly face—brow, clear skin, ardent eyes, and black hair that bristled in those five black tongues called the “five points.” Despite this contrast his face was sweet, Raphael-like in outline, with a well-cut, gracious mouth; when he laughed he showed thirty-two good teeth. About five feet six, well proportioned, broad-shouldered and active, he had the bearing of an officer and the charm of someone born to advantage. He possessed both grace and the noble blood of his real father.“Don’t you know, Max,” cried young Goddet—son of a former surgeon-major and now the best doctor in town—“that Madame Hochon’s goddaughter is the sister of Rouget? If she’s coming with her son, she might secure the property when he dies, and then—good-by to your harvest!”Max frowned, watched the effect of the news around the table, and again asked, “What’s that to me?”“But,” said François, “I should think that if old Rouget revoked his will—in case he made one in favor of the Rabouilleuse—”Max cut him short. “I’ve stopped the mouths of people who dared meddle with you, my dear François,” he said; “and this is how you pay your debts? You use a contemptuous nickname for a woman to whom I am known to be attached.”Max had never before spoken so distinctly of his relation with Mademoiselle Flore Brazier, called the “Rabouilleuse” by respectable Madame Hochon. Everyone knew it was a ticklish subject; none joked about Flore unless Max began; to risk his anger was dangerous. In other company, Major Potel would say, “If he is the natural brother of Jean-Jacques Rouget where else would you have him live?” Captain Renard would add, “The girl is worthless, and if Max lives with her where’s the harm?” After this snub François could not catch his thread; he was subdued when Max, gently, said, “Go on.”“Faith, no!” cried François.“You needn’t get angry, Max,” said Goddet; “didn’t we agree to talk freely at Mère Cognette’s? Outside, we’d be mortal enemies if we remembered what’s said here. All the town calls Flore Brazier the Rabouilleuse; and if François let the nickname slip, is that a crime against the Order of Idleness?”“No,” said Max, “but against our personal friendship. However, I thought better of it; I recollected we were in session, and that’s why I said ‘Go on.’”Silence followed, so uncomfortable Max broke it. “I’ll go on for him—for all of you—and tell you what you are thinking. You think that Flore, the Rabouilleuse, La Brazier, the housekeeper of Père Rouget—you think that woman supplies all my wants since I returned to Issoudun. If I am able to throw three hundred francs a month to the dogs, treat you to suppers—as tonight—and lend money to all of you, you think I get the gold out of Mademoiselle Flore Brazier’s purse? Well, yes. Yes, Mademoiselle Brazier is aiming straight for the old man’s property.”“She gets it from father to son,” observed Goddet.“You think I intend to marry Flore when Père Rouget dies, and so this sister and her son, of whom I hear to-night for the first time, will endanger my future?”“That’s just it,” cried François.“That is what everyone thinks,” said Baruch.“Don’t be uneasy, friends,” answered Max. “Forewarned is forearmed! Now then, I address the Knights of Idleness. If, to get rid of these Parisians I need the help of the Order, will you lend me a hand? Oh! within the limits we have marked out for our fooleries,” he added, seeing hesitation. “Do you suppose I want to kill them—poison them? Thank God I’m not an idiot. Besides, if the Bridaus succeed, and Flore has nothing but what she stands in, I should be satisfied; do you understand that? I love her enough to prefer her to Mademoiselle Fichet—if Mademoiselle Fichet would have me.”Mademoiselle Fichet was the richest heiress in Issoudun; her name figured in rumor about the younger Goddet’s passion for a mother. Frankness prized, all the Knights rose as one: “You are a fine fellow, Max!” “We’ll stand by you!” “A fig for the Bridaus!” “Hurrah for Max!” Eleven acclamations, toasts, and relaxed morality reflected their mood. It is easy to see why Max became their grand master: by leading youths of the best families and doing them services, he built a support to recover position later. He rose, waved his glass, and addressed them:“As a mark of the ill-will I bear you, I wish you all a mistress equal to the beautiful Flore! As to this irruption of relations, I don’t feel present uneasiness; and as to the future, we’ll see what comes—”“Don’t forget Fario’s cart!”“Hang it! that’s safe enough!” said Goddet.“Oh! I’ll engage to settle that business,” cried Max. “Be in the market-place early, all of you, and let me know when the old fellow goes for his cart.”At half-past three in the morning the Knights slipped out silently to their homes, gliding close to the walls in list shoes. Max returned slowly to the place Saint-Jean—the quarter of the rich bourgeoisie. He had concealed his fears, but the news struck home. His time on the hulks at Cabrera had taught him deep dissimulation. Above all, the forty thousand francs a year from Rouget’s landed property was the core of Max’s passion for Flore Brazier. By his bearing one saw how much confidence she had given him in the financial future she expected via the old bachelor. Still, the arrival of legitimate heirs shook Max’s faith in Flore’s influence. Rouget’s savings, accumulated over seventeen years, still stood in his own name; even if the will Flore declared had been made in her favor were revoked, these savings could at least be secured by putting them into Mademoiselle Brazier’s name.“That fool of a girl never told me, in all these seven years, a word about the sister and nephews!” cried Max, turning from the rue de la Marmouse into the rue l’Avenier. “Seven hundred and fifty thousand francs placed with different notaries at Bourges, Vierzon, and Châteauroux can’t be turned into money and put into the Funds in a week without everybody knowing in this gossiping place! The important thing is to get rid of these relations; once they’re driven away we must hurry to secure the property. I must think it over.”Tired, Max let himself into Père Rouget’s house with a pass-key and went to bed without noise, saying, “To-morrow my thoughts will be clear.”Now it is necessary to relate why the sultana of place Saint-Jean picked up the nickname “Rabouilleuse,” and how she became quasi-mistress of Jean-Jacques Rouget’s home.As old Doctor Rouget—father of Jean-Jacques and Madame Bridau—aged he perceived his son’s nonentity and treated him harshly, preparing him to submit to first tyranny. Coming home one day from his rounds, the malignant and vicious old man encountered a bewitching little girl at the edge of some fields along the avenue de Tivoli. Hearing the horse, the child sprang up from the brook, half naked, wearing a forlorn petticoat of coarse strips, a rough paper hat, and tattered garments; yet beneath these rags was the loveliest head and figure. One companion wading in the water—her uncle—raised his head and said, “What are you about, Flore? While you are talking instead of catching, the creatures will get away.”“Why have you come here from Vatan?” said the doctor.“I am catching crabs for my uncle Brazier here.”“Rabouiller” is a Berrichon word meaning troubling the water to bring crabs to the surface; Flore Brazier held her rabouilloir with childlike grace.“Has your uncle got permission to hunt crabs?”“Hey! aren’t we all under a Republic that is one and indivisible?” cried the uncle.“We are under a Directory,” said the doctor, “and I know of no law that allows a man to come from Vatan and fish in the territory of Issoudun. Have you got a mother, little one?”“No, monsieur; my father’s in the asylum at Bourges. He went mad from a sunstroke in the fields.”“How much do you earn?”“Five sous a day while the season lasts; I catch them as far as the Braisne. In harvest I glean; in winter I spin.”“You are about twelve years old?”“Yes, monsieur.”“Do you want to come with me? You shall be well fed and well dressed, and have some pretty shoes.”“No, my niece will stay with me; I am responsible to God and man for her,” said Uncle Brazier, who had come up. He wore a sun-rotted peasant hat, a dark, sunken face, patchwork jacket and crash towelling trousers.“I am Doctor Rouget,” said that man; “as you are the guardian, bring her to my house in place Saint-Jean. It will not be a bad day’s work for you nor for her.”The doctor spurred his horse and returned to Issoudun. He had hardly sat to dinner before his cook announced the citoyen and citoyenne Brazier.“Sit down,” said the doctor to uncle and niece. Flore and her guardian, still barefoot, looked round the doctor’s dining-room with wonder; never had they seen such a place.Rouget’s house, inherited from the Descoings family, stands in the middle of place Saint-Jean, a long narrow square planted with a few sickly lindens. The Descoings house has three windows on the first storey and a porte-cochère leading to a courtyard and garden. Under the archway is a large hall lighted by two street windows, beyond which lie the kitchen and stair to upper floors, a wood-shed, wash-house, stables and coach-house.The hall that amazed the little peasant was decorated with wooden carvings and a handsome marble chimney-piece with a large gilded mirror. On the panelled walls hung pictures rescued from religious houses—old masters and sacred subjects that declared the family’s taste and wealth. The furniture showed a luxury little valued in Issoudun: a Boulle clock between silver candlesticks, carved oak armchairs covered with tapestry, and a rich console. But Rouget, his son, and the cook took little care: they spat upon a delicate hearth, the chandelier and ceiling bore black speckles from flies; brocatelle curtains were torn; a chest worth many thousand francs served as a sideboard.“Here, Fanchette,” cried Rouget to his cook, “bring two glasses; give us some of the old wine.”Fanchette, a big Berrichon countrywoman considered a better cook than even Mère Cognette, ran in with alacrity, showing Rouget’s despotism and her own curiosity.“What is an acre of vineyard worth in your parts?” asked the doctor, pouring a glass for Brazier.“Three hundred francs in silver.”“Well, then! leave your niece here as my servant; she shall have three hundred francs in wages, and, as you are her guardian, you can take them.”“Every year?” exclaimed Brazier, eyes wide.“I leave that to your conscience,” said the doctor. “She is an orphan; up to eighteen she has no right to what she earns.”“Twelve to eighteen—that’s six acres of vineyard!” said the uncle. “Ay, she’s a pretty one, gentle as a lamb, well made and active, and obedient as a kitten. She were the light o’ my poor brother’s eyes.”“I will pay a year in advance,” observed the doctor.“Bless me! say two years, and I’ll leave her with you; she’ll be better off with you than with us; my wife beats her; there’s none but I to stand up for her, and the little saint of a creature is as innocent as a new-born babe.”When he heard “innocent,” the doctor, struck, made a sign to the uncle and took him into the courtyard and garden, leaving the Rabouilleuse at the table with Fanchette and Jean-Jacques, who questioned her and to whom she naively related her meeting with the doctor.“There now, my little darling, good-by,” said Uncle Brazier, kissing Flore; “you can say I’ve made your happiness leaving you with this kind and worthy father of the poor; you must obey him as you would me. Be a good girl and behave nicely and do everything he tells you.”“Get the room over mine ready,” said the doctor to Fanchette. “Little Flore—I am sure she’s worthy of the name—will sleep there. To-morrow we’ll send for a shoemaker and dressmaker. Put another plate on the table; she shall keep us company.”That evening all Issoudun talked of the sudden appearance of the little rabouilleuse in Doctor Rouget’s house; in that satirical region the nickname stuck to Mademoiselle Brazier before, during, and after her good fortune.The doctor likely intended something for Flore similar in miniature to Louis XV’s conduct with Mademoiselle de Romans, but he was too late and too old. From twelve to fourteen the charming Rabouilleuse lived a life of unmixed happiness. Well dressed and often finer than Issoudun’s richest girls, she sported a gold watch and jewels given by the doctor to encourage studies, and had a master to teach her to read, write, and cipher. But the peasant life had instilled in Flore a deep repugnance to the bitter cup of knowledge, so the doctor stopped her education at that point. His intentions toward the child, whom he cleansed, clothed, and taught, were interpreted variously by the town’s gossip—fatal in such communities. Provincial minds insist on full explanations and cling to preferred versions; to recognize truth requires impartiality.“What do you suppose that old gorilla wants at his age with a little girl only fifteen years old?” society still said two years after Flore’s arrival.“Ah! that’s true,” they answered. “His days of merry-making are long past.”“My dear fellow, the doctor is disgusted at his son’s stupidity, and he persists in hating his daughter Agathe; he may intend to marry little Flore; suppose she gave him a fine, active boy like Max?” said one.“Bah! don’t talk nonsense! After such a life as Rouget and Lousteau led from 1770 to 1787, is it likely either would have children at sixty-five? The old villain has read the Scriptures and does as David did in his old age; that’s all.”“They say Brazier, when drunk, boasts in Vatan that he cheated him,” cried one who always believed the worst.“Good heavens! neighbor; what won’t they say at Issoudun?”From 1800 to 1805 the doctor enjoyed educating Flore sans the annoyances that accompany some protégées. The Rabouilleuse was envied by peasant girls within ten miles, although her conduct might be religiously reprehensible. Flore, born in 1787, grew up amid the saturnalias of 1793 and 1798, whose effects reached these country regions then deprived of priests and faith; marriage had become more a legal coupling, and revolutionary maxims left a deep impression. By 1802 Catholic worship was scarcely re-established; the Emperor found it difficult to obtain priests. In 1806 many parishes still lacked clergy, so slowly had the dispersed clergy returned after the Revolution.In 1802 therefore nothing was likely to reproach Flore Brazier except conscience; and conscience was sure to be weaker than self-interest in Uncle Brazier’s ward. If, as everyone supposed, the cynical doctor was compelled by age to respect a child of fifteen, the Rabouilleuse was still considered very “wide awake,” a local phrase. Some thought she could claim a certificate of innocence from the cessation of the doctor’s care in his last two years, during which he showed her less than coldness.Old Rouget had killed too many people not to know when his end was near; and his notary, finding him on his death-bed, pressed him to make provision in favor of the young girl, then seventeen.“So I do,” he said cynically; “my death sets her at liberty.”This phrase revealed the old man’s nature. Cloaking his evil deeds with witty sayings, he obtained indulgence in a land that applauds wit—especially when tied to self-interest. The notary read in those words a concentrated hatred of a man whose calculations had been balked by Nature, revenging himself upon the innocent object of impotent love. The doctor’s obstinate resolution to leave nothing to the Rabouilleuse—saying with a bitter smile when urged again, “Her beauty will make her rich enough!”—confirmed this opinion.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Jean-Jacques Rouget did not mourn his father, though Flore Brazier did. The old doctor had made his son unhappy since Jean-Jacques came of age in 1791, but he had given the little peasant girl the comforts loved by country folk. After the funeral, when Fanchette asked Flore, “Well, what is to become of you, now that monsieur is dead?” Jean-Jacques’s eyes lighted; for the first time his dull face showed feeling.“Leave the room,” he said to Fanchette, who was clearing the table.At seventeen Flore kept delicate features and the distinction of the beauty that had attracted the doctor, though the embonpoint of a life without toil was beginning to appear. Her bust and shoulders had developed; the face’s outline remained faultless and the chin delicate.“Flore,” Jean-Jacques said in a trembling voice, “you feel at home in this house?”“Yes, Monsieur Jean.”As he prepared to declare himself he fell silent, troubled by recollections of the dead man and uncertain how far his father’s indiscretions might have gone. Flore, unable to suspect his simplicity, waited; Jean-Jacques left her without finishing. It would be long before she understood his character.At his father’s death Jacques—then thirty-seven—was as timid and submissive to paternal discipline as a child of twelve. That timidity explains his life. There are kinds of timidity—of mind and of nerves; when both unite a man becomes a cipher. Jean-Jacques’s timidity sprang from a torpor of his faculties, which a strong teacher might have roused. In him, the sense of love had more force than intellect, though he had enough sense for ordinary affairs. Passion, without the romantic ideal youths typically invest in it, only increased his fear. He had never courted any woman in Issoudun: mean stature, awkwardness, a vulgar flattened face, pallid skin and large, prominent light-green eyes made him seem old before his time. A woman’s presence stultified him; desire petrified his speech. Paralyzed between passion and lack of ideas, he sought solitude.The doctor, too late, saw the havoc of this temperament. He wanted his son married but hesitated because a wife would manage the property; to avoid surrendering control he tried to instill avarice in Jean-Jacques, training him in mechanical habits and fixed ideas about investing revenues. He kept estates in good order and let them long to spare his son management difficulties. But one fact escaped notice: timidity hid Jean-Jacques’s passionate love for the Rabouilleuse. Flore was the only woman in the bachelor’s presence, the light of his paternal home; he secretly contemplated her at all hours and rejoiced in his father’s education of her as making her easier to win. Passion that can reflect gives even ninnies a kind of intelligence; animal instinct persists with the force of thought.The day after the funeral Flore, expecting a declaration, waited; Jean-Jacques kept near her and glanced with passion but could not speak. At last, during dessert, he managed awkwardly to ask if she liked life there and whether she was honest. Flore, astonished by his timidity yet touched, answered readily. He asked if she was still the barefoot girl her uncle had brought; she blushed. Encouraged, Rouget stumbled, turned red, and could only say, “Are you the same?” She answered on her honor; he took her hand and, unable to say more, declared that she could stay, suggesting she be mistress of the house.This strange situation lasted three weeks. One night Flore found Jean-Jacques crouched like a dog on the landing; she thought, “He loves me,” and, indulgent, did not mind. At last, during another dessert, Flore told him the truth about his father: the old man had been a worthy man and a joker, but had had some spite and might have meant harm to Jean-Jacques. Rouget, taking her hand, said, “If your father was nothing to you you can stay here with me, can’t you?” She admitted she would if he wished it. He confessed he had always loved her since she came barefoot. Flore made no direct answer at first; when silence became embarrassing, Jean-Jacques used a practical argument: “Wouldn’t it be better than returning to the fields?” She assented.Yet he got no further. Men of his nature want certainty; the effort to avow love costs too much and they quail. Ten months after his father’s death Jean-Jacques changed: his leaden face cleared and he breathed happiness. Flore insisted he take better care of himself and watched him go out. The town noticed the transformation.“Have you heard?” people said. “Jean-Jacques inherits everything, even the Rabouilleuse. She’s sly! She’s very handsome and will make him marry her.” Some thought he ought to marry a woman of family; others praised Flore for devotion to the poor fellow. This is how Flore obtained management of the Rouget household—from father to son, as young Goddet had said. It is necessary to sketch that management for the edification of old bachelors.Fanchette, the cook, alone thought it wrong that Flore should be queen over Jean-Jacques. She protested with injured virtue but was humiliated by a child mistress. Fanchette owned three hundred francs a year in the Funds and had an annuity from the doctor; she could live without working and left the house nine months after the funeral, April 15, 1806. That date may indicate when Flore ceased to be an honest girl.Foreseeing Fanchette’s departure, Flore resolved to do without a servant. For six months she had studied Fanchette’s culinary operations. The doctor, trained under Rouelle in Paris, had applied chemistry to cooking and improved provincial techniques. He taught subtleties—how to treat omelettes, the use of a “cagnard,” methods to prevent browned butter’s tartness—secrets honed in his kitchen.Flore, a born fryer and roaster, soon surpassed Fanchette and became a cordon-bleu with care for Jean-Jacques’s comfort. She rubbed up furniture, kept everything clean, managed the wash and mended linen with thrift. She learned family business, consulted the notary Monsieur Heron, and advised Jean-Jacques well. Two months before the doctor’s death Flore’s uncle Brazier had died after a fall, leaving a wine-shop where he had spent most of his time. Flore had also lost her father; thus she served her master with an orphan’s gratitude.This period was paradise for poor Jean-Jacques. He acquired gentle habits of an animal, trained into monastic regularity. Flore woke him with breakfast ready; after a morning walk he read papers at three and spent much time at dinner, then went to bed early to save fire and candles—provincial economies that contribute to empty-mindedness.For nine years their life was regular. Jean-Jacques lent at five percent on first mortgages, with release of the wife’s rights if married, never more than a third of a property’s value, and required notes for an extra two and a half percent—his father’s rules. Usury, that clog on peasant ambition, destroyed rural regions; Rouget’s seven and a half percent levy seemed reasonable and gave him investment choice; notaries reaped commissions.During these nine years Flore obtained absolute control. At first she treated him familiarly; then, surpassing him in mind and force, she became in fact his superior and he the servant of his servant. He met this mastery halfway by letting her care for him as a son clings to a mother’s protection. Flore kept the house and managed all business; Jean-Jacques left everything to her so completely that life without her seemed impossible. She indulged all his fancies and understood him thoroughly. Her brightness was his chief pleasure; when it clouded his state betrayed her power, which she sometimes exercised. Secure in her power, she did not think to make him marry her.Towards the close of 1815 Flore, then twenty-seven, had reached full beauty: plump, fresh, white-skinned, the ideal “buxom housewife,” likened to a handsome barmaid of a higher type. In 1816 she saw Maxence Gilet and fell in love at first sight. Maxence, a penniless officer, saw more than a passing fancy in Flore once he learned of her and Jean-Jacques’s positions. He gladly took up abode at the Rougets’, recognizing the old bachelor’s weakness. Flore’s passion affected household affairs: she became sullen and gave Jean-Jacques flashes of temper like a wife suspecting infidelity. He asked why; her eyes were full of hatred and her voice contemptuous.“You have neither heart nor soul!” she cried. “Sixteen years I have spent my youth in this house, and I have only just found out you have a stone there” (striking her breast). “For two months you have seen that brave captain, a victim of the Bourbons, cut out for a general and down in poverty, hunted into a hole where there’s no way to make a penny! He’s forced to sit on a stool all day in the mayor’s office to earn—what? Six hundred miserable francs—and here are you, with six hundred and fifty-nine thousand well invested, and sixty thousand francs’ income—thanks to me, who never spend more than three thousand a year—and you never think of offering him a home here, though the second floor is empty! You’d rather rats and mice run riot than put a human being there—and he’s a lad your father always allowed to be his own son! Do you want to know what you are? A fratricide! And I know why. You see I take an interest in him, and that provokes you. Well, yes! I do take an interest in him.”But Jean-Jacques stammered and could only plead ignorance of any such wish. She answered bitterly that there ought to be a man in the house for safety and reputation, and that Max could swallow three burglars in a flash. People might gossip; the proper story would be that his father commanded care of the poor Max. For nine years she had eaten his bread and accepted many offers of court; she had always replied she would not abandon the “poor innocent.” Jean-Jacques, terrified at the thought of losing her, promised to invite Maxence.He did so politely, before witnesses. A reconciliation took place; but Jean-Jacques noticed signs that Flore’s affections had wholly shifted. For weeks she complained to tradespeople about Rouget’s alleged tyranny in inviting his natural brother. No one was deceived: they saw Flore as clever and artful. Old Rouget, however, found himself comfortable after Max became master of his house; he gained a companion who paid him attention without servility. Gilet talked, discussed politics, and sometimes walked with Rouget. After Max’s arrival Flore stopped cooking, saying it spoiled her hands. Mere Cognette, grand master of idleness, produced a relative, an old maid who had lost a curate master and promised devotion: an excellent cook whose fidelity would be rewarded with an annuity of three hundred francs after ten years. This Vedie, pitted by smallpox and ugly, took over the kitchen.Flore now adopted the title Madame Brazier. She wore corsets, silk or fine woollen and cotton dresses, expensive neckerchiefs, embroidered caps, collars, lace ruffles and boots. She renewed her youth through richness and elegance, trimming a rough diamond into worth. She wished to honor Max. In 1817 she brought a horse, called English, from Bourges for the weary cavalry captain. Max had found in Issoudun an old lancer of the Imperial Guard, a Pole named Kouski, who, now poor, asked to quarter himself at Rouget’s as the captain’s servant. Max idolized Kouski, especially after the duel with three royalists. Thus from 1817 the household numbered five persons, three of whom were masters, and expenses rose to about eight thousand francs a year.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################At the time Madame Bridau returned to Issoudun to claim—Maitre Desroches said—an inheritance in jeopardy, Jean-Jacques Rouget had become almost semi‑vegetative. After Max’s instalment, Flore set the household on a strict footing. Exposed to better food and Vedie’s excellent cooking, Rouget ate more but did not fatten; he weakened, fatigued perhaps by digestion, his eyes ringed by dark circles. When neighbors questioned him he always said he had never been better. Thought mentally deficient, people failed to notice his failing faculties. His love for Flore kept him alive; he existed only for her, obeying her slightest look, watching her like a dog. As Madame Hochon remarked, at fifty‑seven he seemed older than Monsieur Hochon, an octogenarian.Everyone supposed, rightly, that Max’s apartment matched his charm. In six years the captain had improved the comfort and adorned it for himself and Flore. But it was only Issoudun luxury—colored tiles, somewhat elegant wallpapers, mahogany furniture, gilt mirrors, muslin curtains with red borders, a canopy bed, provincial draperies—luxury in Issoudun though common in Paris. One small oddity caused talk: rush‑matting on the stairs to muffle footsteps. Max came in at daybreak without waking anyone; Rouget did not suspect his guest was an accomplice in the Knights of Idleness’ nocturnal performances.About eight the next morning Flore, in a cotton dressing‑gown with narrow pink stripes, a lace cap, and furred slippers, softly opened Max’s door; seeing him asleep she stood beside the bed.“He came in so late,” she thought. “Half‑past three. He must have a good constitution. I wonder what they did last night.”“Oh, there you are, my little Flore!” said Max, waking like a soldier trained to be instantly ready.“You are sleepy; I’ll go away.”“No, stay; there’s something serious going on.”“Were you up to mischief last night?”“Ah, bah! It concerns you and me and that old fool. You never told me he had a family! Well, his family are coming—coming here—no doubt to turn us out.”“Ah! I’ll shake him well,” said Flore.“Mademoiselle Brazier,” said Max gravely, “things are too serious for giddiness. Send me my coffee; I’ll take it in bed and think over what we had better do. Come back at nine and we’ll talk. Meanwhile, act as if you’ve heard nothing.”Frightened, Flore went to make his coffee; a quarter‑hour later Baruch burst into Max’s room, crying to the grand master,—“Fario is hunting for his barrow!”In five minutes Max was dressed and in the street; though he sauntered, he soon reached the tower embankment, where a crowd had gathered.“What is it?” asked Max, pushing through and reaching the Spaniard.Fario was a withered little man, ugly as though blue‑blooded grandee. His fiery, close‑set eyes were piercing; calm, slow in movement, people called him “goodman Fario.” His gingerbread skin and gentle manner hid a half‑Moorish peasant nature from Granada, long unroused from phlegmatic indolence.“Are you sure,” Max asked after hearing his grievance, “that you left your cart here? There are no thieves in Issoudun.”“I left it just there—”“If the horse was harnessed, hasn’t he drawn it somewhere?”“Here’s the horse,” said Fario, pointing to the animal harnessed thirty feet away.Max gravely went to where the horse stood, from which one could see the bottom of the tower at the top of the embankment; the crowd followed, as the scoundrel wanted.“Has anybody thoughtlessly put a cart in his pocket?” cried Francois.“Turn out your pockets, all of you!” said Baruch.Laughter rose. Fario swore; with a Spaniard oaths mark high anger.“Was your cart light?” asked Max.“Light!” cried Fario. “If those who laugh had it on their feet, their corns would never hurt them again.”“Well, it must be devilishly light,” answered Max, “for look there!” pointing to the foot of the tower; “it has flown up the embankment.”All eyes lifted, and for a moment there was uproar in the market‑place. Each pointed at the bewitched barrow and talked.“The devil makes common cause with the inn‑keepers,” said Goddet to the astonished Spaniard. “He means to teach you not to leave your cart about in the streets, but to put it in the tavern stables.”The crowd hooted; Fario was thought a miser.“Come, my good fellow,” said Max, “don’t lose heart. We’ll go up and see how your barrow got there. Thunder and cannon! we’ll lend you a hand! Come along, Baruch.”“As for you,” he whispered to Francois, “get the people to stand back, and make sure there is nobody at the foot of the embankment when you see us at the top.”Fario, Max, Baruch, and three other knights climbed. During the perilous ascent Max and Fario noticed no trace of the barrow’s passage; Fario imagined witchcraft and lost his head. At the top it seemed impossible the cart had got there.“How shall I ever get it down?” said the Spaniard, whose small eyes for the first time showed fear; his yellow face whitened.“How?” said Max. “Why, that’s not difficult.”Taking advantage of Fario’s stupefaction, he raised the barrow by the shafts with his robust arms and prepared to fling it down, calling as it left his grasp, “Look out there, below!”No accident happened; the crowd, persuaded by Francois and curious, had retired to a distance. The cart dashed to pieces in a very picturesque manner.“There! you have got it down,” said Baruch.“Ah, brigands! ah, scoundrels!” cried Fario; “perhaps it was you who brought it up here!”Max, Baruch, and their comrades laughed at the Spaniard’s rage.“I wanted to do you a service,” said Max coolly, “and in handling the damned thing I came very near flinging myself after it; and this is how you thank me, is it? What country do you come from?”“I come from a country where they never forgive,” replied Fario, trembling. “My cart will be the cab in which you shall drive to the devil!—unless,” he suddenly grew meek, “you give me a new one.”“We will talk about that,” said Max, descending.At the bottom Max took Fario by the jacket and said,—“Yes, my good Fario, I’ll give you a magnificent cart, if you will give me two hundred and fifty francs; but I won’t warrant it to go, like this one, up a tower.”At this jest Fario cooled as though making a bargain.“Damn it!” he said, “give me the wherewithal to replace my barrow, and it will be the best use you ever made of old Rouget’s money.”Max turned livid; he raised his fist to strike, but Baruch, knowing the blow would be misdirected, plucked Fario away and whispered,—“Don’t commit such a folly!”Called to order, the grand master laughed and said to Fario,—“If I, by accident, broke your barrow, and you try to slander me, we are quits.”“Not yet,” muttered Fario. “But I am glad to know what my barrow was worth.”“Ah, Max, you’ve found your match!” said a spectator not of the Order.“Adieu, Monsieur Gilet. I haven’t thanked you yet for lending me a hand,” cried the Spaniard, as he kicked his horse and vanished amid hurrahs.“We will keep the tires of the wheels for you,” shouted a wheelwright who had come to inspect the damage.One shaft stuck upright in the ground as straight as a tree. Max stood by, pale and thoughtful, annoyed by Fario’s speech. For five days the Spaniard’s barrow was all Issoudun talked of; Goddet said it would travel abroad—indeed the tale reached Berry, and in a week the affair, to Fario’s satisfaction, was the talk of three departments. Because of the Spaniard’s vindictive speech, Max and the Rabouilleuse were whispered about in Issoudun and openly discussed in Bourges, Vatan, Vierzon, and Chateauroux. Maxence Gilet knew how envenomed such comments would become.“We can’t stop their tongues,” he said. “Ah! I did a foolish thing!”“Max!” said Francois, taking his arm. “They are coming to‑night.”“They! Who!”“The Bridaus. My grandmother has had a letter from her goddaughter.”“Listen, my boy,” said Max low. “I have been thinking deeply. Neither Flore nor I ought to seem opposed to the Bridaus. If these heirs are to be got rid of, it is for you Hochons to drive them out. Find out what sort of people they are. Tomorrow at Mere Cognette’s, after I’ve taken their measure, we can decide what to do, and how to set your grandfather against them.”“The Spaniard found the flaw in Max’s armor,” said Baruch to his cousin Francois as they turned into Monsieur Hochon’s and watched their comrade entering his own door.While Max was thus occupied, Flore, despite advice, could not restrain her wrath and burst upon the poor bachelor. When Jean‑Jacques incurred her anger, his little attentions and vulgar fondlings—his joy—were suppressed. Flore sent him, as children say, into disgrace. No more tender glances or caressing words—“my kitten,” “my old darling,” “my bibi,” “my rat”—were heard. A cold, sharp, ironic “you” cut through the heart of the miserable old bachelor; the “you” was a declaration of war. Instead of helping him with his toilet, handing what he wanted, forestalling his wishes, or admiring him—“You look as fresh as a rose!”—she left him to dress alone. If he called, she answered from the foot of the stairs, “I can’t do everything at once; how can I look after your breakfast and wait upon you? Are not you big enough to dress yourself?”“Oh, dear! what have I done to displease her?” he asked that morning, after a rebuff when calling for shaving‑water.“Vedie, take up the hot water,” cried Flore.“Vedie!” exclaimed the poor man, stupefied. “Vedie, what is the matter with Madame this morning?”Flore Brazier required her master, Vedie, Kouski, and Max to call her Madame.“She seems to have heard something about you which isn’t to your credit,” Vedie said, pretending great concern. “I’m only a poor servant and have no right to poke my nose; but I say you may search through all the women and you won’t find the equal of Madame. You ought to kiss the ground she steps on. Goodness! if you make her unhappy, you’ll only spoil your own life. There she is, poor thing, with her eyes full of tears.”Vedie left the poor man cast down; he dropped into an armchair and stared into vacancy like the melancholy imbecile he was, and forgot to shave. These alternations of tenderness and severity had on this feeble creature, whose only life was through his amorous fibre, the same morbid effect that sudden climatic changes have on the body. It was a moral pleurisy wearing him out like disease. Flore alone could thus affect him; to her and her alone he was as good as foolish.“Well, haven’t you shaved yet?” she said, appearing at his door.Her sudden presence made him start; from pale and cast down he grew red, yet dared not complain.“Your breakfast is waiting,” she added. “You can come down as you are, in dressing‑gown and slippers; for you’ll breakfast alone, I can tell you.”Without waiting, she disappeared. To breakfast alone was his worst punishment; he loved to talk to her as he ate. At the foot of the stairs he was seized with a coughing fit; emotion excited his catarrh.“Cough away!” said Flore in the kitchen, without caring if he heard. “Confound the old wretch! he is able enough to get over it without bothering others. If he coughs up his soul, it will only be after—”Such were the amenities the Rabouilleuse addressed to Rouget when angry. The poor man sat in deep distress at a corner of the table and looked at his old furniture and pictures with a disconsolate air.“You might at least have put on a cravat,” said Flore. “Do you think it pleasant for people to see such a neck as yours, redder and more wrinkled than a turkey’s?”“But what have I done?” he asked, lifting his big light‑green eyes, full of tears, to her and trying to meet her hard countenance.“What have you done?” she exclaimed. “As if you didn’t know! Your sister Agathe—who is as much your sister as I am sister of the tower of Issoudun, if one’s to believe your father, and who has no claim at all upon you—is coming from Paris with her son, a miserable two‑penny painter, to see you.”“My sister and my nephews coming to Issoudun!” he said, bewildered.“Oh, yes! play surprised; try to make me believe you didn’t send for them! Don’t fash yourself; we won’t trouble your Parisians—before they set their feet in this house we shall have shaken the dust off ours. Max and I will be gone, never to return. As for your will, I’ll tear it in quarters under your nose and to your very beard—do you hear? Leave your property to your family if you don’t think we are your family; and then see if you’ll be loved for yourself by a lot of people who have not seen you for thirty years—who in fact have never seen you! Is that the sort of sister who can take my place? A pinchbeck saint!”“If that’s all, my little Flore,” said the old man, “I won’t receive my sister, or my nephews. I swear this is the first I heard of their coming. I never wrote to my sister; my father made me promise not to leave her any property; to leave it to the Church sooner than to her. Well, I won’t receive my sister Agathe or her sons—”“Your father was wrong, my dear Jean‑Jacques, and Madame Brazier is still more wrong,” answered Max. “Your father no doubt had his reasons, but he is dead, and his hatred should die with him. Your sister is your sister, and your nephews your nephews. You owe it to yourself to welcome them, and you owe it to us as well. What would people say in Issoudun? Thunder! I’ve enough on my shoulders, without hearing people say we shut you up and don’t allow you a will of your own, or that we influence you against your relations and are trying to get hold of your property. The devil take me if I don’t pull up stakes if calumny is flung at me! the other is bad enough! Let’s eat our breakfast.”Flore, now mild as a weasel, helped Vedie set the table. Old Rouget, full of admiration for Max, took him by both hands and led him to a window recess, saying low,—“Ah! Max, if I had a son, I couldn’t love him better than I love you. Flore is right: you two are my real family. You are a man of honor, Max, and what you have just said is true.”“You ought to receive and entertain your sister and her son, but not change your arrangements about property,” Max said. “In that way you will do right in the world’s eyes and yet keep your promise to your father.”“Well! my dear loves!” cried Flore gayly, “the salmi is getting cold. Come, my old rat, here’s a wing for you,” she said, smiling on Jean‑Jacques.At the words the long‑drawn face lost its cadaverous tints, a smile flickered on his lips; but he had another fit of coughing; joy excited as violent an emotion as punishment. Flore rose, pulled a little cashmere shawl from her shoulders, and tied it round his throat, exclaiming: “How silly to put yourself in such a way about nothing. There, you old goose, that will do you good; it has been next my heart—”“What a good creature!” said Rouget to Max, while Flore fetched a black velvet cap for the nearly bald head.“As good as she is beautiful,” answered Max, “but she is quick‑tempered, like all people who carry their hearts in their hands.”This scene, played again and again with variations, is in its coarse way the type of scenes women enact on whatever social rung when interest draws them from obedience and induces them to grasp power. Between Flore Brazier and a duchess, between a duchess and the richest bourgeoise, between a bourgeoise and the luxuriously kept mistress, differences are those of education and surroundings. The pouting of a fine lady is the same as the violence of a Rabouilleuse. At all levels, bitter sayings, ironical jests, cold contempt, hypocritical complaints, false quarrels win as much success as low outbursts of this Madame Everard of Issoudun.Max began to relate, with humor, Fario and his barrow, making the old man laugh. Vedie and Kouski in the kitchen exploded, and Flore laughed convulsively. After breakfast, while Jean‑Jacques read the newspapers (they subscribed to the Constitutionnel and the Pandore), Max carried Flore to his quarters.“Are you sure he has not made any other will since the one leaving property to you?”“He hasn’t anything to write with,” she answered.“He might have dictated it to some notary,” said Max; “we must look out for that. Therefore be cordial to the Bridaus, and at the same time turn those mortgages into money. The notaries will be glad to make the transfers; it is grist to their mill. The Funds are rising; we shall conquer Spain, deliver Ferdinand VII and the Cortez, and they will be above par. You and I could make a good thing by putting the old fellow’s seven hundred and fifty thousand francs into the Funds at eighty‑nine. Only you must try to get it done in your name; it will be so much secured anyhow.”“A capital idea!” said Flore.“And as there will be an income of fifty thousand francs from eight hundred and ninety thousand, we must make him borrow one hundred and forty thousand francs for two years, to be paid back in two instalments. In two years, we shall get one hundred thousand francs in Paris, and ninety thousand here, and risk nothing.”“If it were not for you, my handsome Max, what would become of me now?” she said.“Oh! tomorrow night at Mere Cognette’s, after I have seen the Parisians, I shall find a way to make the Hochons themselves get rid of them.”“Ah! what a head you’ve got, my angel! You are a love of a man.”The place Saint‑Jean is at the centre of a long street called at the upper end rue Grand Narette, and at the lower rue Petite Narette. “Narette” in Berry expresses a steep street. The Grand Narette rises rapidly from place Saint‑Jean to the port Vilatte. The house of old Monsieur Hochon is exactly opposite Jean‑Jacques Rouget’s. From Madame Hochon’s usual sitting‑room it was easy to see what went on at the Rouget household, and vice versa, when curtains were drawn back or doors left open. The Hochon house resembled the Rouget house; doubtless the same architect built both. Monsieur Hochon, formerly tax‑collector at Selles in Berry, born at Issoudun, had returned home and married the sister of the sub‑delegate, the gay Lousteau, exchanging his office at Selles for one at Issoudun. Having retired before 1787, he escaped the Revolution’s dangers, to whose principles, however, he adhered like other “honest men” who howl with the winners. Monsieur Hochon had the reputation of miser; a single episode will show him.At his daughter’s wedding, necessary to give a dinner to the Borniche family, the bridegroom, heir to a large fortune, had mismanaged his property and been mortified by his parents’ refusal to help. The old people were delighted to see Monsieur Hochon step in as guardian to secure his daughter’s dowry. While the marriage contract was solemnly read by young Heron, the notary, the cook came in and asked Monsieur Hochon for some twine to truss the turkey—an essential of the repast. The old man dove into his coat and gave an end of string already used to tie a parcel, and before she left the room called out, “Gritte, mind you give it back to me!” (Gritte is Berry for Marguerite.)Year by year Hochon grew more petty and penurious; at eighty‑five he belonged to men who stop mid‑conversation to pick up a pin, remarking as they stick it in their coat sleeve, “There’s the wife’s stipend.” He complained of cloth quality and how his coat had lasted ten years. Tall, gaunt, thin, sallow; saying little, reading little, doing nothing to fatigue himself; observant of forms, he enforced at home strict abstemious discipline, weighing and measuring food and drink for his family: his wife née Lousteau, his grandson Borniche with a sister Adolphine, heirs of old Borniche, and his other grandson Francois Hochon.Hochon’s eldest son, taken by the 1813 draft which drew sons of well‑to‑do families into the “guards of honor,” was killed at Hanau. He had married early a rich woman to avoid conscription; after enrolment he wasted his substance, foreseeing his end. His wife, who followed the army at a distance, died at Strasburg in 1814, leaving debts Hochon refused to pay—answering creditors with an axiom: “Women are minors.”The house, though large, was scantily furnished; on the second floor two rooms suited Madame Bridau and Joseph. Old Hochon repented having kept them with two beds, each with an old armchair of natural wood covered with needlework, and a walnut table with a wide‑mouthed water‑pitcher in a basin. He kept winter stores of apples, pears, medlars, and quinces on heaps of straw in these rooms where rats and mice ran riot, exhaling a mixed odor of fruit and vermin. Madame Hochon directed everything be cleaned; wallpaper fastened with wafers; she decorated windows with curtains pieced from old hoards. Denied a strip of drugget, she laid her bedside carpet for little Agathe—“Poor little thing!” she said—who was now over forty‑seven. Madame Hochon borrowed two night‑tables from a neighbor and boldly hired two chests of drawers with brass handles from a second‑hand dealer next to Mere Cognette. She had preserved two candlesticks carved in choice woods by her father, who had the “turning” mania; from 1770 to 1780 it was fashionable for rich people to learn a trade, and Monsieur Lousteau had been a turner. These candlesticks were ornamented with circlets from roots of rose, peach, and apricot trees. Madame Hochon actually risked using her relics. These preparations and sacrifices increased old Hochon’s anxiety; until now he had not believed the Bridaus’ arrival.The morning of the Fario trick Madame Hochon said after breakfast,—“I hope, Hochon, you will receive my goddaughter, Madame Bridau, properly.” Then, making sure her grandchildren were out of hearing, she added, “I am mistress of my own property; don’t oblige me to make up to Agathe in my will for any incivility on your part.”“Do you think, madame,” answered Hochon mildly, “that at my age I don’t know forms of decent civility?”“You know very well what I mean, you crafty old thing! Be friendly to our guests, and remember I love Agathe.”“And you love Maxence Gilet also, who is getting the property away from your dear Agathe! Ah! you’ve warmed a viper in your bosom; but after all, the Rouget money is bound to go to a Lousteau.”Having said this about Max and Agathe, Hochon turned to leave; but Madame Hochon, still erect and spare, wearing a round cap with ribbon knots, powdered hair, a taffeta petticoat of changeable colors like a pigeon’s breast, tight sleeves, and high‑heeled slippers, placed her snuff‑box on a little table and said,—“Really, Monsieur Hochon, how can a man of your sense repeat absurdities which cost my poor friend her peace and Agathe the property she ought to have had from her father. Max Gilet is not the son of my brother, whom I often advised to save the money he paid for him. You know as well as I do that Madame Rouget was virtue itself—”“And the daughter takes after her; for she strikes me as uncommonly stupid. After losing all her fortune, she brings her sons up so well that here is one in prison and likely to be indicted before the Court of Peers for a conspiracy worthy of Berton. As for the other, he is worse—a painter. If your protégés stay here till they have extricated that fool Rouget from Gilet and the Rabouilleuse, we shall eat a good deal more than half a measure of salt with them.”“That’s enough, Monsieur Hochon; you had better wish they may not have two strings to their bow.”Monsieur Hochon took his hat and his cane with an ivory knob and went away petrified by that speech; he had no idea his wife could show such resolution. Madame Hochon took her prayer‑book to read the service; her age prevented daily church attendance; she got there with difficulty on Sundays. Since receiving her goddaughter’s letter she had added a petition to her prayers, supplicating God to open Jean‑Jacques Rouget’s eyes, bless Agathe, and prosper the expedition she had drawn her into. Concealing the fact from her grandchildren, whom she accused of being “parpaillots,” she had asked the curate to say a mass for Agathe’s success during a neuvaine held by her granddaughter Adolphine Borniche, who thus made her prayers in church by proxy.Adolphine, then eighteen, who for seven years had sewn at her grandmother’s side in that cold household of monotony and method, undertook the neuvaine hoping to inspire some feeling in Joseph Bridau, in whom she took an interest because of the monstrosities her grandfather attributed to the young Parisian.All the old and sensible people of the town and fathers of families approved Madame Hochon’s conduct in receiving her goddaughter; their good wishes for her success matched the secret contempt for Maxence Gilet’s conduct. Thus news of Rouget’s sister and nephew raised two parties in Issoudun—the higher and older bourgeoisie, who offered good wishes and watched without assisting, and the Knights of Idleness and Max’s partisans, who were capable of high‑handed outrages against the Parisians.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Agathe and Joseph arrived at the Messageries-Royales office in the Place Misère at three o’clock. Madame Bridau, tired yet cheered by the sight of her native town, felt each step recall girlhood memories. Word of the Parisians spread at once through Issoudun; within ten minutes everyone knew they had come. Madame Hochon met her godchild at the door and kissed her like a daughter. After seventy-two barren years marked by the graves of three unhappy children, she cherished this friendship and at once took an interest in Bridau. Agathe was led into the salon where Monsieur Hochon sat, cool as a tepid oven.“Here is Monsieur Hochon; how does he seem to you?” his wife asked.“Exactly as when I last saw him,” Agathe answered.“Ah! it is easy to see you come from Paris; you are so complimentary,” the old man sneered.Introductions followed: Baruch Borniche, twenty-two; François Hochon, twenty-four; and little Adolphine, blushing and awkward, anxious not to look at Joseph Bridau, who in turn was closely observed from different angles by the two young men and old Hochon. The miser thought, “He’s just out of the hospital; he’ll be as hungry as a convalescent.” The young men muttered about his brigand-like face and wild expression.“This is my son, the painter; my good Joseph,” Agathe said, the emphasis betraying a mother’s heart and the memory of her son in the Luxembourg prison.“He looks ill,” said Madame Hochon. “He is not at all like you.”“No, madame,” Joseph answered bluntly; “I am like my father, and very ugly at that.”Madame Hochon squeezed Agathe’s hand as if to say, “Ah! my child; I understand now why you prefer your good-for-nothing Philippe.” She added aloud that she had heard of Joseph’s talent from the late Madame Descoings. “Talent!” Joseph replied. “Not yet; but with time and patience I may win fame and fortune.”“By painting?” Monsieur Hochon sneered.The household bustled: introductions completed, trunks carried to attic chambers, and the town’s curiosity flaring. Baruch rushed into town to announce the Bridaus’ arrival and to describe Joseph’s haggard, brigand-like face; that evening Joseph was the talk of every household. The town’s gossip painted him monstrous, dangerous, and capable of anything; some proposed having their portraits painted to make use of his presence.After inspecting the bare, damp house—plain walls, a shabby staircase and furniture—Joseph felt the sharp fall from poetic Paris to the arid province. Coming down to find Monsieur Hochon slicing bread with Molière’s Harpagon-like parsimony, he thought they should have stayed at an inn. Dinner confirmed his fears: a watery soup, ceremoniously garnished bouilli cut paper-thin, separate vegetables counted as distinct parts of the meal, hard-boiled eggs on sorrel, a salad dressed with nut-oil, cups of custard flavored with burnt oats in place of vanilla, butter, radishes, pickled gherkins and horseradish. Madame Hochon nodded contentedly; her husband shrugged as if to say, “See the extravagances you force me to commit!”After Monsieur Hochon sliced the bouilli into soles-of-dancing-shoe thickness he replaced it with three pigeons. The wine was the 1811 country vintage. Adolphine had decorated the table with flowers. “At Rome as the Romans do,” thought Joseph, and began to eat—like a man who had breakfasted on execrable coffee. When he asked for more bread, Monsieur Hochon unlocked a cupboard and broke off a section of a twelve-pound loaf with the resigned sanctity of a man performing a ritual. Joseph took his half-slice and understood asking for more was forbidden. Conversation went on; Agathe learned her birthhouse had been bought by the Borniches and wished to see it.Gritte, the sixty-year-old servant, brought dessert—the famous ripe cheese of Touraine and Berry, mold like vine-leaves—nuts and time-honored biscuits. When asked for fruit she insisted there was none; Joseph laughed, seeing that parsimony had grown into habit here. “Bah! we can eat them all the same,” he exclaimed, and Monsieur Hochon fetched peaches, pears and Saint Catherine plums. Adolphine was ordered to gather grapes. Joseph looked at the two young men as if to ask how they owed their healthy faces to such fare; Baruch smiled—the cousins were moderate and frequenters of Mére Cognette’s for feasts.“We will take the liqueur in the salon,” Madame Hochon said, and whispered to Agathe as they left: “This dinner won’t give you indigestion; it is always Lent here; you will get enough just to keep life in you, and no more. So you must bear it patiently.” The old woman’s kindness pleased Joseph. She produced a flask of ratafia she made from a nuns’ recipe and they drank. Agathe remarked the immutable provincial customs made her feel as if her father’s turn had come round.“Hochon will go to his club presently to read the papers, and we shall have a little time to ourselves,” the old lady said. Ten minutes later three women and Joseph were alone in the salon, which looked exactly as when Agathe left; Monarchy, Revolution, Empire and Restoration had left no trace here. They spoke of life’s trials; Madame Hochon counselled resignation and turned the conversation to the Rouget windows across the street where, she said, the enemy sat.Adolphine, destined for a cloister, kept looking out the window, eager for more about Maxence Gilet, the Rabouilleuse and Jean-Jacques. Madame Hochon promised many curious visitors that night. She recounted Maxence’s influence over Rouget and the Rabouilleuse’s doings with all local embroidery. The Borniches, Beaussier, Lousteau-Prangin, Fichet, Goddet-Herau families—fourteen persons in all—were expected.“It will not be easy to get this property out of the wolf’s jaws,” the old lady said.“It seems so difficult,” Joseph replied. “A scoundrel such as you represent him, and a daring woman like that crab-girl—defeating them would take a year in Issoudun and mean stooping to meannesses. My mother has only two weeks’ leave; her place is permanent. As for me, in October I have an important commission; my future is in my brushes.”Madame Hochon, superior to the town but not a believer in painting, glanced at her goddaughter and pressed her hand. Joseph muttered that Maxence was like a second volume of Philippe from Paris—only cleverer and better behaved. Madame Hochon counseled a couple of weeks, judiciously used, could produce results, urging them to heed her advice. Joseph admitted an incapacity for domestic statesmanship.Soon the invited company—Mesdames Borniche, Goddet-Herau, Beaussier, Lousteau-Prangin and Fichet, with their husbands—arrived. When the fourteen were seated, Madame Hochon presented Agathe and Joseph. Joseph sat in his armchair all evening, slyly studying the faces which, from five until half-past nine, posed for him gratis. His sarcastic glances and sinister visage left the company ruffled and uneasy.After ten, when the household slept, Madame Hochon kept Agathe in her chamber until midnight. The two women, secure from interruption, exchanged sorrows. Agathe perceived the immensity of the desert in which this noble unrecognized soul had been wasted and knew that despite Paris’s small joys she was not the most unhappy. “You who are so pious,” she said, “explain my shortcomings; tell me what God punishes in me.” “He is preparing us,” the old woman answered, “for the striking of the last hour.”At midnight the Knights of Idleness assembled under the boulevard Baron trees, whispering. François thought Maxence meant merely to give them supper. Baruch feared his grandfather would be alarmed at two extra mouths to feed. Maxence appeared and suggested they go to Mére Cognette’s. The cry “To Mére Cognette’s!” rang through the town like troops rushing. A supper worthy of La Cognette brightened the faces of twenty-two guests; the whole Order was present. At two, after the feast, Max rose to speak.“My dear fellows! the honor of your grand master was grossly attacked this morning after our memorable joke with Fario’s cart—attacked by a vile peddler, and what is more, a Spaniard (oh, Cabrera!); and I have resolved to make the scoundrel feel my vengeance—always, of course, within our rules. After reflecting, I have a trick worth execution—a famous trick that will drive him crazy. While avenging the insult, we shall feed the sacred animals of the Egyptians—little beasts unjustly persecuted. Thus good is the child of evil.
