Each was to stand at his line and advance only on the seconds’ word.“Do we take off our coats?” Philippe asked coldly; Maxence adopted a bully’s manner: “Of course.” They stripped to shirts; their muscles hardly trembled. Goddet, the seconds, and two soldiers admired their composure.As the signal was given Max caught sight of Fario’s sinister face peering through a hole in the church roof; those eyes, full of hatred and revenge, dazzled him. Philippe adopted an inside guard that marked a duellist of the first rank. Max sensed inferiority.He tried a moulinet to bewilder Philippe, but felt the colonel’s wrist was iron and spring-like. Philippe’s parries were cool and precise; between two men of such calibre victory depended on a flash of calculation or a single false movement. At a bad parry by Max the colonel sent his sabre spinning from his hand.“Pick it up,” Philippe said; “I am not the man to kill a disarmed enemy.” The grandeur of the words, their conscious superiority, stunned onlookers. When Max resumed position, he had lost coolness. He resolved to redeem his shame by a bold stroke; taking his sabre in both hands he rushed furiously. Philippe received a cut slashing his forehead and part of his face, but with a terrible oblique moulinet he cleft Max’s head. These two savage blows ended the combat in the ninth minute. Fario came down to gloat over his enemy’s convulsions; Philippe was carried back to his uncle’s house.Thus perished a man who might have achieved great deeds in other circumstances; nature had given him courage, self-possession, and political sagacity, but not the nobility of conduct needed for greatness. He was not much regretted, given the perfidy by which his adversary—worse in character—had brought him into disrepute. His death ended the exploits of the Order of Idleness, to Issoudun’s relief. Philippe had little to fear as the duel’s circumstances were spread with praise for the bravery of both combatants.“But they had better both have been killed,” Monsieur Mouilleron remarked; “it would have been a good riddance for the Government.”Flore Brazier’s situation, which might have been embarrassing for Philippe, was saved by her collapse after Max’s death. A brain fever set in, combined with dangerous inflammation from her escapade to Vatan. She hovered between life and death for three months, attended by Goddet, who also cared for Philippe.As soon as Philippe could write he sent letters. To Monsieur Desroches he reported he had “killed the most venomous of the two reptiles,” though his own head had been split by a sabre. He noted the other “viper” remained and he must come to terms with her because his uncle clung to her. He said illness had kept Flore in bed, which he hoped Providence would use to call her soul if she repented. He claimed Goddet and others had been won to his side; Goddet preached resignation to Flore and had promised to keep her in bed for three months. He had bought over the cook, who spread tales suggesting Max would have ill-treated Flore. “All goes well so far,” he wrote; “my uncle, by old Hochon’s advice, has destroyed his will.”To Monsieur Giroudeau, care of Mademoiselle Florentine, he requested a girl—Cesarine, if available—or some ballet-girl who could appear as the respectable daughter of a brave soldier and be ready at his call; if she answered his purpose he would give her fifty thousand francs on his uncle’s death. He described the duel and hinted at lively times ahead.Agathe, informed by Madame Hochon, rushed to Issoudun and was given Philippe’s former room. Her maternal tenderness revived as she listened to town praises for her son.“After all, my child,” Madame Hochon said, “youth must have its fling. The dissipations of a soldier under the Empire must, of course, be greater than those of young men looked after by their fathers. Thanks to your son, Issoudun now breathes and sleeps in peace. If he can be kept away from the temptations of Paris, he will end by being a comfort to you.”Philippe played the saint to his mother because he needed her. He did not wish to resort to Cesarine unless he remained an object of horror to Mademoiselle Brazier. He recognized Flore was essential to his uncle’s life and preferred to use her rather than bring in a dancer who might marry the old man. He conceived the idea of making his uncle marry Flore, legitimizing her position and keeping her—and the property—within reach. He asked his mother to visit Flore and treat her sisterly.“I must confess, my dear mother,” he said in an affected tone before Monsieur and Madame Hochon, “that my uncle’s way of life is not becoming; he could, however, make Mademoiselle Brazier respected by the community if he chose. Wouldn’t it be far better for her to be Madame Rouget than the servant-mistress of an old bachelor? She had better obtain a definite right to his property by a marriage contract. If you, or Monsieur Hochon, or some good priest would speak to both parties, you might put a stop to the scandal which offends decent people.”The next day Agathe and Madame Hochon repeated Philippe’s words at Flore’s bedside. In Issoudun the colonel was praised for his supposed nobility toward Flore. For a month Flore heard Goddet, Madame Hochon, Agathe, and Rouget urging a marriage. When the idea of becoming Madame Rouget enticed her, it was made clear she would be unwelcome in the Rouget family if she intended to turn Philippe out.“Besides,” the doctor whispered, “you really owe him this good fortune. Max would never have allowed you to marry old Rouget. And, if you have children, you can revenge Max, for that will disinherit the Bridaus.”Two months after the duel, in February 1823, the sick woman, urged by those around her and implored by Rouget, consented to receive Philippe; the sight of his scars moved her to tears, and his softened manner calmed her. By Philippe’s wish they were left alone.“My dear child,” he said, “it is I who, from the start, have advised your marriage with my uncle; if you consent, it will take place as soon as you are recovered.”“So they tell me,” she replied.“Circumstances have compelled me to give you pain; it is natural therefore that I should wish to do you all the good I can. Wealth, respect, and a family position are worth more than what you have lost. You wouldn’t have been that fellow’s wife long after my uncle’s death, for I happen to know, through friends of his, that he intended to get rid of you. Come, my dear, let us understand each other and live happily. You shall be my aunt and nothing more. You will take care that my uncle does not forget me in his will; on my side, you shall see how well I will have you treated in the marriage contract. Keep calm, think it over, and we will talk later. All sensible people, indeed the whole town, urge you to put an end to your illegal position; no one will blame you for receiving me. It is well understood in the world that interests go before feelings. By the day of your marriage you will be handsomer than ever. The pallor of illness has given you an air of distinction, and on my honor, if my uncle did not love you so madly, you should be the wife of Colonel Bridau.”Philippe left after planting the idea of revenge through children—a hint that stirred her. In this scene his manipulative phrasing masked calculation as sentiment; self-interest did the work of persuasion.At the beginning of April 1823 Rouget’s hall hosted a splendid dinner to celebrate signing the marriage contract between Mademoiselle Flore Brazier and the old bachelor. Guests included Monsieur Heron, the witnesses, Mignonnet, Carpentier, Hochon, Goddet, the mayor, the curate, Agathe Bridau, Madame Hochon and Madame Borniche. The bride, well protected by Philippe and the two ladies, shone; the curate had instructed her for a fortnight and she was to take first communion the next day. Local journals praised the marriage as a revival of religion and moral correction; the “Journal du Cher” and the “Journal de l’Indre” lauded Philippe’s supposed disinterestedness.By the contract Rouget secured to Flore a dower of one hundred thousand francs and a life annuity of thirty thousand.After the wedding Agathe returned to Paris triumphant and told Joseph and Desroches the “good news.”“Your son Philippe is too wily a man not to keep his paw on that inheritance,” said Desroches.“You, and Joseph too, will always be unjust to that poor boy,” replied Madame Bridau, defending her son as statesmanlike.“Why do you suppose that he will not succeed by honest means?” she demanded.“You will see!” exclaimed Desroches. “Fortunate or unfortunate, Philippe will remain the man of the Rue Mazarin, the murderer of Madame Descoings, the domestic thief. But don’t worry yourself; he will manage to appear honest to the world.”The morning after the marriage Philippe took Madame Rouget aside.“My dear aunt,” he said, “you now belong to the family. Thanks to me, the law has tied the knot. Now, no nonsense. I intend that you and I should play above board. I know the tricks you will try against me; and I shall watch you like a duenna. You will never go out of this house except on my arm; and you will never leave me. As to what passes within the house, damn it, you’ll find me like a spider in the middle of his web.” He showed her a letter from Mariette and friends describing a plan to provide a trained actress to entrap Rouget and secure the property for Philippe; the letter detailed the girl’s arts and the social circle ready to exploit any fortune. The letter terrified Madame Rouget and exposed the calculated danger she had escaped.“I trust you,” he said, fixing her with terrible meaning, “as you see; but I expect some return. I have made you my aunt intending to marry you some day. You are worth more to me than Esther in managing my uncle. In a year from now we must be in Paris; you will amuse yourself much better there than here; it is a perpetual carnival. I shall return to the army and become a general, and you will be a great lady. There’s our future; now work for it. But I must have a pledge to bind this agreement. You are to give me, within a month from now, a power of attorney from my uncle, which you must obtain under pretence of relieving him of the fatigues of business. Also, a month later, I must have a special power to transfer the income in the Funds. When that stands in my name, you and I have an equal interest in marrying each other. There it all is, my beautiful aunt, as plain as day. Between you and me there must be no ambiguity. I can marry my aunt at the end of a year’s widowhood; but I could not marry a disgraced girl.”He left without waiting for an answer. Vedie, clearing the table, found her mistress pale and sweating. Flore felt as if she had fallen to the bottom of a precipice; the future seemed black. The damp chill of vaults, an instinctive fear of the man, oppressed her; yet a voice told her she deserved to have him for her master. She was helpless.Flore had once had a room in Rouget’s house; now Madame Rouget belonged to her husband and was deprived of freedom. She briefly held out hope of bearing a child, but Philippe’s constant watch made that impossible. Benjamin served as Philippe’s innocent spy; Vedie trembled under his scrutiny. Flore felt deserted; without knowing how, she was certain that if he suspected pregnancy her doom would be sealed. The sound of his voice, the gambler’s eye, the slightest movement of the soldier—politeness cloaking brutality—made her shudder. The power of attorney he demanded he obtained as soon as he wanted it; Flore fell under his dominion as France had under Napoleon.Like a butterfly caught in wax, Rouget rapidly dissipated into decay; in his decline the nephew remained cold and impassible as diplomats in convulsed times.Philippe, now skeptical of Napoleon II., wrote to the minister of war through intermediaries to offer his services to the King. He explained Napoleon was gone; having been faithful, he was now free to serve the monarch. He asked for consideration and reinstatement at his rank, professing submission and assuring the minister of his fidelity. Accompanying the letter were requests for permission to go to Paris on urgent family business and endorsements from the mayor, the sub-prefect, and the commissary of police of Issoudun, praising Philippe’s conduct and citing newspaper articles about Rouget’s marriage.Two weeks later Philippe received permission and a letter informing him that by order of the King he was, as a preliminary favor, reinstated lieutenant-colonel in the royal army.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Lieutenant-Colonel Bridau returned to Paris with his aunt and the helpless Rouget, and three days after their arrival escorted them to the Treasury, where Jean-Jacques assigned his income to Philippe. The exhausted old man and the Rabouilleuse were plunged by their nephew into the dissipations of actresses, journalists, artists, and equivocal women among whom Philippe had already wasted his youth; old Rouget found excitements that soon killed him. Instigated by Giroudeau, Lolotte, one of the handsomest of the Opera ballet-girls, was the amiable assassin of the old man. Rouget died after a splendid supper at Florentine’s, and Lolotte blamed a slice of pâté de foie gras; as the Strasburg masterpiece could make no defence, it was considered settled that the old man died of indigestion.Madame Rouget thrived amid this décolleté society; but Philippe put her in charge of Mariette, and that monitress prevented the widow—whose mourning was diversified with a few amusements—from committing any actual follies.In October, 1823, Philippe returned to Issoudun with a power of attorney from his aunt to liquidate his uncle’s estate; the business was soon over, and he returned to Paris in March, 1824, with sixteen hundred thousand francs—the net proceeds of old Rouget’s property, not counting the pictures, which had never left Monsieur Hochon’s hands. Philippe put the whole into Mongenod and Sons, where young Baruch Borniche was employed, on Hochon’s assurances of solvency. This house took his funds at six per cent per annum, on condition of three months’ notice for withdrawal.One day Philippe visited his mother and invited her to his marriage, witnessed by Giroudeau, Finot, Nathan, and Bixiou. By the contract, the widow Rouget, whose portion was a million francs, secured to her future husband her whole fortune should she die childless. No invitations were sent; Philippe had designs. He lodged his wife in an appartement in the rue Saint-Georges, bought ready-furnished from Lolotte. Madame Bridau the younger thought it delightful; her husband rarely set foot in it. Without her knowledge, Philippe purchased in the rue de Clichy a magnificent hotel for two hundred and fifty thousand francs, paying one hundred and fifty thousand down and taking two years for the rest. He spent large sums altering the interior and furnishing it; in fact, he put his income for two years into this outlay. The restored pictures, then estimated at three hundred thousand francs, shone there.The accession of Charles X. brought greater court favor to the family of the Duc de Chaulieu, whose eldest son, the Duc de Rhetore, was in the habit of seeing Philippe at Tullia’s. Under Charles X., the elder branch, believing itself secure, followed advice to encourage adherence of former Imperial soldiers. Philippe, who had likely made invaluable revelations about the conspiracies of 1820 and 1822, was appointed lieutenant-colonel in the regiment of the Duc de Maufrigneuse. That nobleman felt bound to protect the man from whom he had taken Mariette. The corps-de-ballet went for something, therefore, in the appointment. Moreover, it was decided in private councils to give a tinge of liberalism to the Dauphin’s surroundings. Philippe, now a sort of equerry to the Duc de Maufrigneuse, was presented to the Dauphin and Dauphine, who liked brusque soldierly characters noted for past fidelity. Philippe understood the part the Dauphin had to play and turned this spurious liberalism to his profit by getting appointed aide-de-camp to a marshal who stood well at court.In January, 1827, Philippe, promoted in the Royal Guard as lieutenant-colonel in a regiment commanded by the Duc de Maufrigneuse, solicited ennoblement. Under the Restoration, nobility became a perquisite to “roturiers” serving in the Guard. Colonel Bridau had bought the estate of Brambourg and asked to entail it under the title of count. The favor was granted through his intimacies in high society, where he now appeared with the luxury of horses, carriages, and liveries; in short, the surroundings of a great lord. As soon as he saw himself gazetted as Comte de Brambourg, he began to frequent the house of a lieutenant-general of artillery, the Comte de Soulanges.Insatiable and backed by mistresses of influential men, Philippe solicited the honor of being one of the Dauphin’s aides-de-camp. He had the audacity to tell the Dauphin that “an old soldier, wounded on many a battle-field and who knew real warfare, might, on occasion, be serviceable to Monseigneur.” Philippe, who could assume all tones of sycophancy, became in the highest social regions exactly what the position required; just as at Issoudun, he had copied Mignonnet’s respectability. He had a fine establishment and gave fêtes and dinners, admitting none of his old friends whose position might compromise him. He was pitiless to companions of former debauches and curtly refused Bixiou when that lively satirist asked him to say a word for Giroudeau, who wanted re-entry into the army after Florentine’s desertion.“The man has neither manners nor morals,” said Philippe.“Ha! did he say that of me?” cried Giroudeau. “Of me, who helped him get rid of his uncle!”“We’ll pay him off yet,” said Bixiou.Philippe intended to marry Mademoiselle Amelie de Soulanges and become a general commanding a Guard regiment. He asked so many favors that, to keep him quiet, they made him a Commander of the Legion of Honor and of Saint Louis. One rainy evening, as Agathe and Joseph returned home along the muddy streets, they met Philippe in full uniform, bedizened with orders, leaning back in a coupe lined with yellow silk, whose armorial bearings were surmounted with a count’s coronet. He was on his way to a fête at the Élysée-Bourbon; the wheels splashed his mother and brother as he waved a patronizing greeting.“He’s going it, that fellow!” said Joseph. “Nevertheless, he might send us something better than mud in our faces.”“He has such a fine position, in such high society, that we ought not to blame him for forgetting us,” said Madame Bridau. “When a man rises to so great a height, he has obligations to repay, sacrifices to make; it is natural he should not come to see us, though he may think of us all the same.”“My dear fellow,” said the Duc de Maufrigneuse one evening to the new Comte de Brambourg, “I am sure your addresses will be favorably received; but to marry Amelie de Soulanges you must be free. What have you done with your wife?”“My wife?” said Philippe, with a gesture, look, and accent worthy of Frederick Lemaitre. “Alas! I have the melancholy certainty of losing her. She has not a week to live. My dear duke, you don’t know what it is to marry beneath you. A woman who was a cook, who has the tastes of a cook! Who dishonors me—ah! I am much to be pitied. I explained my position to Madame la Dauphine. At the time of the marriage, it was a question of saving the family a million which my uncle left to that person. Happily, my wife took to drinking; at her death, I come into possession of that million, which is now in Mongenod and Sons. I have thirty thousand francs a year in the five per cents, and my entailed landed property brings forty thousand more. If Monsieur de Soulanges gets a marshal’s baton, I am on the high-road, with my title, to becoming general and peer of France. That will be the proper end of an aide-de-camp of the Dauphin.”After the Salon of 1823, one of the leading painters obtained the management of a lottery-office near the Markets for Joseph Bridau’s mother. Agathe was soon able to exchange it for an office in the rue de Seine, in a house where Joseph had his atelier. The widow now hired an agent herself and was no longer an expense to her son. And yet, as late as 1828, though she directed an office owed to Joseph’s fame, Madame Bridau still doubted that fame, which was hotly contested as all true glory is. The great painter, struggling with genius, had enormous wants; he did not earn enough to pay for the luxuries that his relations and position in the young School of Art demanded. Though sustained by his Cénacle friends and by Mademoiselle des Touches, he did not please the bourgeoisie. The bourgeoisie, from whom money comes, never unties its purse for genius that is in question; unfortunately, Joseph had the classics and the Institute and their critics against him. Though backed by Gros and Géricault (Gérard), by whose influence he was decorated after the Salon of 1827, he obtained few orders. Moreover, Joseph gave way too much to imaginative fancies, producing inequality in his work which enemies used to deny his talent.“High art is at a low ebb,” said his friend Pierre Grassou, who made daubs for bourgeois tastes.“You ought to have a whole cathedral to decorate; that’s what you want,” declared Schinner; “then you would silence criticism with a master-stroke.”Such speeches alarmed Agathe and confirmed her long-held judgment about Philippe and Joseph. Facts sustained that judgment in a provincial woman’s mind. Philippe, her favorite, was now a great man; his early errors were only youth’s ebullitions. Joseph, whose productions she could not appreciate—having seen them in progress too often—seemed no more advanced in 1828 than in 1816. Poor Joseph owed money and was bowed by debt; she felt he had chosen a worthless career that gave no return. She could not conceive why they had given him the Legion cross. Philippe, rich enough to cease gambling, a guest at Madame’s fêtes, the brilliant colonel appearing at reviews and processions in splendid uniforms with two crosses, realized all her maternal dreams. One day of public ceremony effaced from Agathe’s mind the sight of Philippe’s earlier misery; on that day he passed her at the same spot, in attendance on the Dauphin, with plumes in his shako and a pelisse gorgeous with gold and fur. Agathe, who to her artist son was now a devoted sister-figure, felt herself the mother of none but the dashing aide-de-camp. Proud of Philippe, she felt he made her life easy and happy—forgetting that the lottery-office enabling her to live came through Joseph.One day Agathe noticed Joseph more worried than usual by his color-man’s bill and resolved, though cursing his profession in her heart, to free him from debts. She kept the house with the office’s proceeds and never asked Joseph for a farthing; she had no money of her own but relied on Philippe’s good heart and purse. For three years she had expected his visits; she imagined that if she appealed he would bring an enormous sum, and pictured the happiness of giving it to Joseph, whose judgment of his brother she found unfair.Without telling Joseph, she wrote the following letter to Philippe:  To Monsieur le comte de Brambourg:  My dear Philippe,—You have not given the least little word of remembrance to your mother for five years. That is not right. You should remember the past, if only for the sake of your excellent brother. Joseph is now in need of money, and you are floating in wealth; he works, while you are flying from fête to fête. You now possess, all to yourself, the property of my brother. Little Borniche tells me you cannot have less than two hundred thousand francs a year. Well, then, come and see Joseph. During your visit, slip into the skull a few thousand-franc notes. Philippe, you owe them to us; nevertheless, your brother will feel grateful to you, not to speak of the happiness you will give  Your mother,  Agathe Bridau, née RougetTwo days later the concierge brought to the atelier the terrible reply:  My dear Mother,—A man does not marry a Mademoiselle Amelie de Soulanges without the purse of Fortunatus, if under the name of Comte de Brambourg he hides that of  Your son,  Philippe BridauAgathe fell half-fainting on the sofa; the letter dropped. The slight noise and her smothered exclamation startled Joseph, who had been rubbing in a sketch. He leaned his head round the canvas, saw his mother stretched out, and rushed to lift what seemed lifeless. He carried Agathe to her bed and sent for Horace Bianchon. When he could question her she told him of her letter and its reply. Joseph went to the atelier, picked up Philippe’s concise brutality, which had broken his mother’s heart and shattered the edifice of trust. When Joseph returned to her bedside he was silent; he did not speak of his brother during the three weeks of the poor woman’s death agony. Bianchon, who watched with devotion, told Joseph the truth on the first day of her seizure.“At her age,” he said, “and under the circumstances, all we can hope is to make her death as little painful as possible.”She herself felt so surely called that she asked for Abbé Loraux, her confessor for over twenty-two years. Alone with him, having poured out her griefs, she said—as she had said to Madame Hochon and repeated to herself—“What have I done to displease God? Have I not loved Him with all my soul? Have I wandered from the path of grace? What is my sin? Can I be guilty of wrong when I know not what it is? Have I time to repair it?”“No,” said the old man gently. “Alas! your life seems pure and your soul spotless; but the eye of God is keener than that of his ministers. I see the truth too late; for you have misled even me.”Hearing such words from lips always peaceful, Agathe rose suddenly and opened wide her eyes in terror.“Tell me! tell me!” she cried.“Be comforted,” said the priest. “Your punishment is a proof that you will receive pardon. God chastens his elect. Woe to those whose misdeeds meet with fortunate success; they will be kneaded again in humanity until sorely punished and brought to maturity. Your life, my daughter, has been one long error. You gave your heart to an unnatural son, in whom you made your glory, and you have misunderstood the child who is your true glory. You have been so unjust that you never saw the striking contrast between the brothers. You owe the comfort of your life to Joseph, while your other son has pillaged you repeatedly. The poor son, who loves you without equal return, gives you all the comfort your life has had; the rich son, who never thinks of you, despises you and desires your death—”“Oh no,” she cried.“Yes,” resumed the priest. “Your humble position stands in the way of his proud hopes. Mother, these are your sins! You have sinned by ignorance. Love Joseph now; give him your heart and pray for him, as I shall for you.”The mother’s eyes opened to her life’s course. Illuminated, she wept. The old priest, moved by her repentance, left the room hastily.Joseph returned about two hours after the confessor had left. He had been to borrow the money needed to pay pressing debts and sat quietly, thinking his mother asleep. He sprang up with a chill as he heard her say, brokenly, “Will he forgive me?”“What is it, mother?” he exclaimed, shocked at her expression.“Ah, Joseph! can you pardon me, my child?” she cried.“For what?” he said.“I have never loved you as you deserved to be loved.”“Oh, what an accusation!” he cried. “Not loved me? For seven years have we not lived alone together? Have you not taken care of me? Are you not my gentle and indulgent companion? I was saying to Grassou only yesterday: ‘What comforts me is that I have such a good mother. She sees to everything; she takes care of my material wants without troubling me.’”“No, Joseph, no; you have loved me, but I have not returned love for love. Ah! would I could live a little longer—Give me your hand.”Agathe took his hand, kissed it, held it to her heart, and looked long at him, letting him see tenderness she had hitherto bestowed only on Philippe. The painter, apt at judging expression, saw how his mother’s heart opened and took her to his breast, crying, “My mother! oh, my mother!”“Ah! I feel that I am forgiven!” she said. “God will confirm the child’s pardon of its mother.”“You must be calm: don’t torment yourself; hear me. I feel loved enough in this one moment for all the past,” he said, laying her back on pillows.During the two weeks’ struggle between life and death, each effusion of feeling seemed an expression of a lifetime. The mother thought only of her son; sustained by love, she was unaware of her sufferings. D’Arthez, Michel Chrestien, Fulgence Ridal, Pierre Grassou, and Bianchon often kept Joseph company, the friends serving as a band of brothers in thought and action. Joseph, inspired by feeling and genius, divined in his mother’s occasional expressions a desire hidden in her heart and said to d’Arthez, “She has loved that brigand Philippe too well not to want to see him before she dies.”Joseph begged Bixiou, who frequented Bohemian regions where Philippe was still occasionally found, to persuade that shameless son to play, out of pity, a little comedy that might fold the mother in an illusory peace. Bixiou, an observing misanthropic scoffer, undertook the mission. When he told the Comte de Brambourg—who received him in a bedroom hung with yellow damask—Philippe laughed.“What the devil do you want me to do there?” he cried. “The only service the poor woman can render me is to die as soon as she can; she would be a sorry figure at my marriage with Mademoiselle de Soulanges. The less my family is seen, the better my position. You can easily understand that I should like to bury the name of Bridau under all the monuments in Père-Lachaise. My brother irritates me by bringing the name into publicity. Look at it as if it were your own: if you were a deputy, with a tongue like yours, you would be as much feared as Chauvelin; you would be made Comte Bixiou, and director of the Beaux-Arts. Once there, how should you like it if your grandmother Descoings were to turn up? Would you want that worthy woman hanging on you? Damn it, you’d wish her six feet under ground. Come, breakfast with me, and let us talk of something else. I am a parvenu, my dear fellow, and I know it. I don’t choose that my swaddling-clothes shall be seen. My son will be more fortunate than I. The scamp will wish me dead; I expect it—or he won’t be my son.”He rang for breakfast. “The fashionable world wouldn’t see you in your mother’s bedroom,” said Bixiou. “What would it cost you to seem to love that poor woman for a few hours?”“Whew!” cried Philippe, winking. “So you come from them, do you? I’m an old camel, who knows all about genuflections. My mother makes the excuse of her last illness to get something out of me for Joseph. No, thank you!”When Bixiou related this to Joseph, the poor painter was chilled to the soul.“Does Philippe know I am ill?” asked Agathe the day after Bixiou’s errand.Joseph left, suffocating with emotion. The Abbé Loraux, sitting by the bed, took her hand and said, “Alas! my child, you have never had but one son.” The words, which Agathe understood, conveyed a shock that began the end. She died twenty hours later.In the delirium preceding death, she murmured, “Whom does Philippe take after?”Joseph followed his mother to the grave alone. Philippe had gone on business to Orléans; in reality he was driven from Paris by the letter Joseph wrote a moment after Agathe’s last sigh:  Monster! my poor mother has died of the shock your letter caused her. Wear mourning, but pretend illness; I will not suffer her assassin to stand at my side before her coffin.  Joseph B.Joseph, who no longer had the heart to paint though grief needed the distraction of labor, was surrounded by friends who vowed never to leave him alone. Thus, two weeks after Agathe’s funeral, Bixiou sat in the atelier with a group when the servant brought a letter from an old woman below.  Monsieur,—To you, whom I scarcely dare to call my brother, I am forced to address myself, if only on account of the name I bear.--Joseph turned the page and read the signature: “Comtesse Flore de Brambourg.” He shuddered, foreseeing new atrocity from his brother.“That brigand,” he cried, “is the devil’s own. And he calls himself a man of honor! And he wears crosses on his breast! And he struts at court instead of being bastinadoed! And the scoundrel is called Monsieur le Comte!”“There are many like him,” said Bixiou.“After all,” said Joseph, “the Rabouilleuse deserves her fate. She’s not worth pitying; she’d have had my neck wrung without saying, ‘He’s innocent.’”Joseph flung the letter; Bixiou caught and read it aloud:  Is it decent that the Comtesse Bridau de Brambourg should die in a hospital, no matter what her faults? If so, will you, who are the friend of Doctor Bianchon, ask him for a permit to let me enter a hospital?  The person who carries this letter has been eleven consecutive days to the hotel de Brambourg, rue de Clichy, without getting any help from my husband. Poverty prevents me from employing a lawyer to demand what is due me, that I may die with decency. Nothing can save me, I know that. If you are unwilling to see your unhappy sister-in-law, send me, at least, the money to end my days. Your brother desires my death; he has always desired it. He warned me that he knew three ways of killing a woman, but I had not the sense to foresee the one he has employed.  In case you will relieve me and judge for yourself my misery, I live in the rue du Houssay, corner of the rue Chantereine, on the fifth floor. If I cannot pay my rent to-morrow I shall be put out--and then, where can I go?  Your sister-in-law,  Comtesse Flore de Brambourg.“What a pit of infamy!” cried Joseph; “there is something under it all.”“Let us send for the woman who brought the letter; we may get the preface,” said Bixiou.The woman appeared, a mass of old gowns fringed with mud, mounted on thick legs with ragged stockings and leaking shoes. Above the mound rose a head like Charlet’s scavenger-women, caparisoned with a filthy bandanna.“What is your name?” said Joseph as Bixiou sketched her, leaning on an umbrella dated year II.“Madame Gruget, at your service. I’ve seen better days,” she said. “If my poor girl hadn’t loved some one too much, you wouldn’t see me what I am. She drowned herself in the river, my poor Ida,—saving your presence! I’ve had the folly to nurse up a quaterne, and that’s why, at seventy-seven, I’m obliged to take care of sick folks for ten sous a day, and go—”“—without clothes?” said Bixiou. “My grandmother nursed up a trey, but she dressed properly.”“Out of my ten sous I pay for lodging—”“What’s wrong with the lady you nurse?”“In the first place, she hasn’t any money; and she has a disease that scares doctors. She owes me for sixty days’ nursing; that’s why I keep on. The husband, who is a count—she is really a countess—will no doubt pay me when she is dead; so I’ve lent her all I had. I haven’t anything left; all I did have has gone to the pawn-brokers. She owes me forty-seven francs and twelve sous, besides thirty francs for nursing. She wants to kill herself with charcoal. I tell her it ain’t right; I’ve had to get the concierge to look after her while I’m gone, or she’ll jump out the window.”“But what’s the matter with her?” said Joseph.“Ah! monsieur, the doctor from the Sisters’ hospital came; but as to the disease,” said Madame Gruget, modestly, “he told me she must go to the hospital. The case is hopeless.”“Let us go and see her,” said Bixiou.“Here,” said Joseph, “take these ten francs.”Plunging his hand into his skull and taking out all his remaining money, Joseph called a coach and went for Bianchon, who was fortunately home. Bixiou ran to the rue de Bussy for Desroches. The four friends reached Flore’s retreat in the rue du Houssay an hour later.“That Mephistopheles on horseback, named Philippe Bridau,” said Bixiou as they climbed the steep stair, “has sailed his boat cleverly to get rid of his wife. You know Lousteau? well, Philippe paid him a thousand francs a month to keep Madame Bridau in the society of Florine, Mariette, Tullia, and the Val-Noble. When Philippe saw his crab-girl so used to pleasure and dress that she couldn’t do without them, he stopped paying and left her to get it as she could—it is easy to know how. By the end of eighteen months the brute forced his wife lower and lower; then, by the help of a young officer, he gave her a taste for drinking. As he rose, his wife sank; the countess is now in the mud. I don’t know what means Philippe has lately taken to get rid of her. I am anxious to study this drama, for I am determined to avenge Joseph. Alas, friends,” he added, leaving them in doubt whether jesting, “give a man over to a vice and you’ll get rid of him. Didn’t Hugo say: ‘She loved a ball, and died of it’? So it is. Vice! vice! my dear friends, do you want to know what vice is? It is the Bonneau of death.”“Then you’ll die of a joke,” said Desroches, laughing.Above the fourth floor, they climbed a ladder-like attic stair. Though Joseph remembered Flore’s beauty, he was unprepared for the hideous spectacle. In a room with bare walls under the attic roof, on a cot with a scanty mattress, a woman lay green as a body drowned, thin as a consumptive. Her head, nearly hairless, was bound with a checked handkerchief; circles round the hollow eyes were red; eyelids like the pellicle of an egg. Nothing remained of the captivating body but an ignoble bony structure. As Flore saw Joseph, two great tears rolled down her cheeks.“She can still weep!” whispered Bixiou. “A strange sight—tears from dominos! It’s like the miracle of Moses.”“How burnt up!” cried Joseph.“In the fires of repentance,” said Flore. “I cannot get a priest; I have nothing, not even a crucifix, to help me see God. Ah, monsieur!” she cried, raising arms like carved wood, “I am a guilty woman; but God never punished any one as he has punished me! Philippe killed Max, who advised dreadful things, and now he has killed me. God uses him as a scourge!”“Leave me alone with her,” said Bianchon, “and let me see if the disease is curable.”“If you cure her, Philippe Bridau will die of rage,” said Desroches. “I will draw up a statement of her condition. He has not brought her before the courts as an adulteress, and thus her conjugal rights stand: he shall have the shame of a suit. But first we must remove the Comtesse to Doctor Dubois’s private hospital in the rue du Faubourg-Saint-Denis. She will be cared for there. Then I will summon the count for restoration of the conjugal home.”“Bravo, Desroches!” cried Bixiou. “What a pleasure to do so much good that will make some people feel so badly!”Ten minutes later Bianchon joined them. “I am going straight to Despleins,” he said. “He can save her by an operation. Ah! he will take care of the case, for her abuse of liquor has developed a disease thought lost.”“Wag of a mangler! Isn’t there but one disease in life?” cried Bixiou.Bianchon was already gone. Two hours later Flore was removed to Dubois’s hospital. Three weeks later the “Hospital Gazette” published an account of one of modern surgery’s boldest operations on a case designated by the initials “F. B.” The patient died—more from exhaustion produced by misery and starvation than from treatment.No sooner did she die than the Comte de Brambourg went, in deep mourning, to call on the Comte de Soulanges and inform him of his sad loss. Soon after, fashion whispered that the Comte de Soulanges would marry his daughter to a parvenu of merit about to be appointed brigadier-general and given command of a Royal Guard regiment. De Marsay told Eugène de Rastignac this news at the Rocher de Cancale, where Bixiou happened to be.“It shall not take place!” said the witty artist to himself.Among the many old friends Philippe refused to recognize were some unable to revenge themselves; but he had wounded Bixiou, who, thanks to brilliant qualities, was everywhere received and never forgave an insult. One day at the Rocher de Cancale, before many well-bred people, Philippe had replied to Bixiou—who spoke of visiting him at the hotel de Brambourg—“You can come and see me when you are made a minister.”“Am I to turn Protestant before I can visit you?” said Bixiou, feigning misunderstanding; but he thought, “You may be Goliath, but I have my sling.”The next day he went to an actor friend, metamorphosed himself by dress into a secularized priest with green spectacles, drove to the hotel de Soulanges, and, received by the count on a message that he had serious business, related in a feigned voice the whole story of the dead countess and the secret particulars confided to him by Bianchon; the histories of Agathe’s death, of old Rouget’s death (which the Comte had boasted of), of Madame Descoings’s death, of the theft from the newspaper, and of Philippe’s early private morals.“Monsieur le comte, don’t give him your daughter until you have made every inquiry; interrogate his former comrades,—Bixiou, Giroudeau, and others.”Three months later the Comte de Brambourg gave a supper to du Tillet, Nucingen, Eugène de Rastignac, Maxime de Trailles, and Henri de Marsay. The host accepted their half-consolatory condolences about his rupture with Soulanges with nonchalance.“You can do better,” said Maxime de Trailles.“How much must a man have to marry a demoiselle de Grandlieu?” asked Philippe of de Marsay.“You? They wouldn’t give you the ugliest of the six for less than ten millions,” answered de Marsay insolently.“Bah!” said Rastignac. “With an income of two hundred thousand francs you can have Mademoiselle de Langeais; she is thirty, ugly, and penniless; that ought to suit you.”“I shall have ten millions two years from now,” said Philippe Bridau.“It is now the 16th of January, 1829,” cried du Tillet, laughing. “I have been hard at work for ten years and have not made as much as that yet.”“We’ll take counsel of each other,” said Bridau; “you shall see how well I understand finance.”“How much do you really own?” asked Nucingen.“Three millions, excluding my house and estate, which I shall not sell; in fact, I cannot, for the property is now entailed and goes with the title.”Nucingen and du Tillet exchanged a sly glance; du Tillet said to Philippe, “My dear count, I shall be delighted to do business with you.”De Marsay intercepted the look, which meant, “We will have those millions.” The two bank magnates were central in political affairs and could, at a given time, manipulate matters at the Bourse to play a sure game against Philippe when probabilities seemed for him but were secretly against him.The occasion came. In July, 1830, du Tillet and Nucingen had helped the Comte de Brambourg make fifteen hundred thousand francs; he trusted them. Philippe, owing his rise to the Restoration, was misled by profound contempt for “civilians”; he believed in the triumph of the Ordonnances and was bent on playing for a rise; du Tillet and Nucingen, sure of a revolution, played against him for a fall. The crafty pair confirmed his judgment and seemed to share his convictions; they encouraged his hopes of doubling his millions and apparently took steps to help. Philippe fought like a man with four millions depending on the struggle. His devotion was so noticeable that he received orders to go to Saint-Cloud with the Duc de Maufrigneuse and attend a council. This mark probably saved his life; for when the order came on July 25th he was intending to charge and sweep the boulevards, where he would undoubtedly have been shot by his friend Giroudeau, who commanded a division of attackers.A month later nothing was left of Colonel Bridau’s immense fortune but his house and furniture, his estates, and the pictures from Issoudun. He committed the folly of believing in the Restoration of the elder branch, to which he remained faithful until 1834. Jealous of Giroudeau’s colonelcy, he re-entered the service. Unluckily, in 1835 he obtained command of a regiment in Algeria, where he remained three dangerous years, always hoping for a general’s epaulets. But some malignant influence—General Giroudeau—continually balked him. Grown hard and brutal, Philippe exceeded ordinary severity and was hated, despite bravery à la Murat.At the beginning of 1839, while making a dash upon Arabs during a retreat before superior forces, he flung himself against the enemy with only a single company and fell in with the main body. The battle was bloody and terrible, man to man, and few horsemen escaped. Seeing their colonel surrounded, these men, at some distance, were unwilling to perish attempting to rescue him. They heard his cry: “Your colonel! to me! a colonel of the Empire!” but they rejoined the regiment. Philippe met a horrible death: the Arabs hacked him to pieces with scimitars and cut off his head.Joseph, who was married about this time through Comte de Serizy to the daughter of a millionaire farmer, inherited his brother’s Paris house and the estate of Brambourg by the entail, which Philippe, had he foreseen this, would have broken. The chief pleasure Joseph derived from his inheritance was the fine collection of paintings from Issoudun. He now possesses an income of sixty thousand francs, and his father-in-law continues to pile up five-franc pieces. Though Joseph Bridau paints magnificent pictures and renders important services to artists, he is not yet a member of the Institute. As result of a clause in the deed of entail, he is now Comte de Brambourg, a fact which often makes him roar with laughter among his atelier friends.ADDENDUMThe following personages appear in other stories of the Human Comedy.- Bianchon, Horace: Father Goriot; The Atheist’s Mass; Cesar Birotteau; The Commission in Lunacy; Lost Illusions; A Distinguished Provincial at Paris; The Secrets of a Princess; The Government Clerks; Pierrette; A Study of Woman; Scenes from a Courtesan’s Life; Honorine; The Seamy Side of History; The Magic Skin; A Second Home; A Prince of Bohemia; Letters of Two Brides; The Muse of the Department; The Imaginary Mistress; The Middle Classes; Cousin Betty. He may mark the transition from an epoch of individual creative power to one of universal, levelling influence—equalizing products, spreading them among the masses, and itself governed by the principle of unity, the final expression of social organization. Do we not see the dead level of barbarism succeed the saturnalia of popular thought and the final struggles of civilizations that concentrated the world’s treasures in a single direction?The commercial traveller is to ideas what our stage-coaches are to men and things: their vehicle. He takes from the luminous centre a handful of light and scatters it among the drowsy populations of duller regions. This human pyrotechnic is a scholar without learning, a juggler deceived by his own tricks, an unbelieving priest who expounds mysteries and dogmas all the better for his want of faith. Curious being! He has seen everything, known everything, and lays claim to all the ways of the world. Soaked in the vices of Paris, he affects provincial familiarity. He links village and capital—neither Parisian nor provincial, but a traveller. He sees nothing to the core: men and places he knows by name; things he surveys at the surface, measures with a little tape-line. His glance skims all and penetrates none. He keeps busy with much, yet nothing truly occupies him.Jester and bon vivant, he courts every political opinion and is patriotic to the marrow. A capital mimic, he can turn on smiles of persuasion, satisfaction, and good-humour, and drop them to reveal his ordinary face. In his trade he must be a certain kind of observer: probing men with a glance to guess habits, wants, and above all solvency. To economize time he comes to quick decisions about his chances—practice that makes him more or less a man of judgment; on this basis he sets up as a critic of theatres, discoursing about Parisian and provincial stages alike.He knows all the good and bad haunts in France “de actu et visu.” He can pilot you equally to vice or virtue. With an eloquence like a hot-water spigot, he can open or check a stream of phrases kept on tap, producing upon his victims a moral shower-bath. Loquacious as a cricket, he smokes, drinks, wears trinkets, overawes the common people, passes for a lord in villages, and never allows himself to be “stumped”—a bit of his own slang. He knows how to slap his pockets at the right moment and make his money jingle if he thinks suspicious servants might take him for a thief. Activity is among his most astonishing qualities. No hawk, stag, or hound can rival his rapid dart for a commission, his agility to trip up rivals and get ahead, or his keenness in scenting a customer and finding where his wares will sell.What great qualities such a man must possess! In all countries one finds these low diplomats: consummate negotiators selling calico, jewels, frippery, wines, often showing truer diplomacy than ambassadors, who mostly know only its forms. No one in France doubts the powers of the commercial traveller: that intrepid soul who boldly brings the genius of civilization and Parisian inventions to reckon with the plain common sense of remote villages and their stolid routines. Who can forget the manoeuvres by which he worms himself into the minds of the populace, bringing a volume of words to bear upon the refractory, like the slow file eating into porphyry? Would you know the utmost force of language or the strongest pressure a phrase can exert against rebellious lucre, against the miserly proprietor in his country lair—listen to one of these great ambassadors of Parisian industry as he revolves, works, and sucks like an intelligent piston of the steam-engine called Speculation.“Monsieur,” said a shrewd political economist, the director-cashier-manager and secretary-general of a celebrated fire-insurance company, “out of every five hundred thousand francs of policies to be renewed in the provinces, not more than fifty thousand are paid up voluntarily. The other four hundred and fifty thousand are got in by the activity of our agents, who go among those in arrears and worry them with stories of horrible incendiaries until they are driven to sign the new policies. Thus you see that eloquence, the labial flux, is nine tenths of the ways and means of our business.”To talk and make people listen—that is seduction itself. A nation with two Chambers, a woman who lends both ears, are soon lost. Eve and her serpent are the everlasting myth of an hourly fact which began, and may end, with the world itself.“A conversation of two hours ought to capture your man,” said a retired lawyer.Let us walk round the commercial traveller and look at him well. Note his olive-green overcoat, his cloak with morocco collar, his striped blue cotton shirt. In this original figure—so distinct that it cannot be rubbed out—how many personalities we meet! What an acrobat, circus, battery all at once! Intrepid mariner, he plunges armed with a few phrases to catch five or six thousand francs in the frozen seas of France’s interior, among the red Indians of the countryside. The provincial fish will not rise to harpoons; it can only be taken with seines, nets, and gentle persuasions. The traveller’s business is to extract gold from country caches by a purely intellectual operation, pleasantly and without pain. Think of the flood of phrases which, renewed each dawn, leaps over the length and breadth of sunny France.You know the species; now take a look at the individual.There lives in Paris an incomparable commercial traveller, the paragon of his race, a man who possesses the qualifications necessary to his success in the highest degree. His speech is both vitriol and glue—glue to catch and entangle his victim, vitriol to dissolve hard heads, close fists, and close calculations. His line was once the hat; his skill at snaring the wariest provincial made him a celebrity, so that all vendors of “article Paris” sought him and begged him to take their commissions.When he returned to Paris between triumphs in the provinces, he lived in perpetual festivity: weddings and suppers. In the smaller towns the correspondents made much of him; in Paris, the great houses feted him. Welcomed, flattered, and fed wherever he went, to breakfast or dine alone became novelty. He lived like a sovereign, or better, like a journalist; in fact he was the perambulating “feuilleton” of Parisian commerce.His name was Gaudissart; his renown and the flatteries heaped on him won him the surname Illustrious. Wherever he went—behind a counter or before a bar, into a salon or to the top of a stage-coach, up to a garret or dining with a banker—everyone said, on seeing him, “Ah! here comes the illustrious Gaudissart!” No name suited him better. All things smiled upon our traveller, and he smiled back. “Similia similibus”—he believed in homoeopathy. Puns, horse-laugh, monkish face, friar’s skin, true Rabelaisian exterior—clothing, body, mind, and features—combined to give devil-may-care jollity to every inch of him. Free-handed and easy-going, he might be recognized at once as the grisettes’ favourite: he jumped lightly to the top of stage-coaches, helped timid ladies down, joked with postillions and sold them caps, smiled at maids and caught them by the waist or heart; gurgled at dinner like a bottle of wine, pretended to draw a cork by a brisk blow on his cheek, played tunes with his knife on champagne glasses without breaking them, dared the company to copy him; chaffed the timid traveller, contradicted the knowing one, lorded it at table and managed to get the titbits. Strong as he was, he could throw aside nonsense and mean business when he flung away a cigar stump and said, with a glance at some town, “I’ll go and see what those people have got in their stomachs.”Buckled down to work he became the slyest and cleverest diplomat. All things to all men, he could accost a banker like a capitalist, a magistrate like a functionary, a royalist with pious sentiments, a bourgeois as one of themselves. Where he went he was what he ought to be; he left Gaudissart at the door when he entered, and picked him up when he came out.Until 1830 the illustrious Gaudissart was faithful to the article Paris. Close contact with humanity’s caprices taught him the windings of the human heart. He learned persuasive eloquence, the knack of loosening the tightest purse-strings, the art of rousing desire in husbands, wives, children, and servants; and how to satisfy it. No one better inveigled a merchant by the charms of a bargain and vanished at the crisis of desire. Grateful to the hat trade, he always declared that capping heads had enabled him to understand interiors; he had “capped and crowned” so many people that he felt at liberty to fling himself at their heads. His jokes about hats and heads were irrepressible, if not dazzling.Yet after August and October 1830 he abandoned the hat trade and the article Paris, tearing himself from visible wares to mount into the higher spheres of Parisian speculation. “He forsook,” in his words, “matter for mind; manufactured products for the infinitely purer elaborations of human intelligence.” This needs explanation.The general upheaval of 1830 brought forth many old ideas recomposed by clever speculators. After 1830 ideas became property. A writer, too wise to publish, once said, “more ideas are stolen than pocket-handkerchiefs.” Perhaps we may one day have an Exchange for thought; even now ideas, good or bad, have their consols, are bought, imported, exported, sold, and quoted like stocks. If ideas are not on hand, speculators pass off words in their stead and live on them as a bird lives on millet seeds. Do not laugh: a word is worth as much as an idea in a land where the ticket on a sack is more important than its contents. Have we not seen libraries working off the word “picturesque” when literature would have slain the word “fantastic”? Fiscal genius has guessed the proper tax on intellect; it has estimated the profits of advertising and registered a prospectus of thought’s quantity and worth, weighing intellectual property at the Stamp Office in the Rue de la Paix.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Having become an article of commerce, intellect and its products must obey the laws that bind other manufactured interests. Thus ideas conceived by idle Parisians—who nevertheless fight many a moral battle over champagne and pheasants—are handed at birth to commercial travellers employed to spread them “urbi et orbi,” seasoned with advertisement and prospectus, by which they catch in their rat-trap the departmental rodent called subscriber, sometimes stockholder, occasionally corresponding member or patron, but invariably fool.“I am a fool!” many a poor country proprietor has said when, caught by the prospect of being the first to launch a new idea, he finds that he has launched his thousand or twelve hundred francs into a gulf.“Subscribers are fools who never can be brought to understand that to go ahead in the intellectual world they must start with more money than they need for the tour of Europe,” say the speculators.Consequently there is endless warfare between the recalcitrant public, which refuses to pay Parisian imposts, and the tax-gatherer who, living by his receipt of custom, lards the public with new ideas, turns it on the spit of lively projects, roasts it with prospectuses, basting with flattery, and finally gobbles it up with some toothsome sauce. Moreover, since 1830 what honors and emoluments have been scattered throughout France to stimulate the zeal and self-love of the “progressive and intelligent masses”! Titles, medals, diplomas, a sort of legion of honor for martyrs, have followed each other rapidly. Speculators in the manufactured products of the intellect have developed a spice, a ginger, all their own. From this have come premiums, forestalled dividends, and that conscription of noted names levied without the knowledge of the unfortunate writers who bear them, who thus find themselves associated with more enterprises than there are days in the year; the law takes no account of the theft of a patronymic. Worse is the rape of ideas which these caterers for the public mind, like slave-merchants, tear from the parental brain before they are matured, and drag half-clothed before the eyes of their sultan—the terrible public—which, if they don’t amuse it, will cut off their heads by curtailing the ingots and emptying their pockets.This madness of our epoch reacted upon the illustrious Gaudissart, and here follows how it happened. A life-insurance company, having been told of his irresistible eloquence, offered him an unheard-of commission, which he accepted. The bargain concluded, our traveller was put in training by the secretary-general, who schooled him in the business: its dark holes, its dialect, the particular public to be gullible, impromptu replies, and unanswerable arguments—sharpening the file of the tongue which was about to operate upon the life of France.The puppet amply rewarded the pains bestowed upon him. The heads of the company boasted of the illustrious Gaudissart so loudly that the financial managers of two then-prominent newspapers—since defunct—were seized with the idea of employing him to get subscribers. The proprietors of the “Globe,” an organ of Saint-Simonism, and the “Movement,” a republican journal, each invited him to a conference and proposed paying ten francs a head for every subscriber if he brought in a thousand, but only five francs if he got five hundred. The cause of political journalism did not interfere with his pre-accepted life-insurance work; the bargain was struck. Gaudissart even demanded indemnity from the Saint-Simonians for eight days spent studying their doctrines, asserting a prodigious effort of memory and intellect was necessary to get to the bottom of that “article,” though he asked nothing from the republicans. He inclined to republican ideas and had already dipped into conspiracies; he had been arrested and released for want of proof; and, as he told the newspaper proprietors, he had lately grown a mustache and needed only a hat of certain shape and a pair of spurs to represent the Republic suitably.CHAPTER IIFor a week this commanding genius went every morning to be Saint-Simonized at the “Globe,” and every afternoon to the life-insurance company, where he learned financial diplomacy. His aptitude and memory were prodigious; by April 15, the usual opening of the spring campaign, he could set out. Two large commercial houses, alarmed at declining business, begged him not to desert the article Paris and seduced him with large offers. The king of travellers yielded; enormous premiums and old friendships swayed him.“Listen, my little Jenny,” he said in a hackney-coach to a pretty florist.All truly great men delight in allowing themselves to be tyrannized over by a feeble being, and Gaudissart had found his tyrant in Jenny. He was bringing her home at eleven from the Gymnase, where he had taken her in full dress to a first-tier box.“On my return, Jenny, I shall refurnish your room in superior style. That big Matilda, who pesters you with comparisons and her India shawls, won’t have a word to say then. I consecrate to the adornment of your room all the ‘Children’ I shall get in the provinces.”“That’s a pretty thing to say! Monster of a man! Do you dare to talk to me of your children? Do you suppose I am going to stand that sort of thing?”“Oh, what a goose you are, my Jenny! That’s only a figure of speech in our business.”“A fine business, then!”“Well, but listen; if you talk all the time you’ll always be in the right.”“I mean to be. You take things easy!”“You don’t let me finish. I have taken under my protection a superlative idea—a newspaper written for children. In our profession, when travellers have caught, say, ten subscribers to the ‘Children’s Journal,’ they say, ‘I’ve got ten Children,’ just as I say when I get ten subscriptions to the ‘Movement,’ ‘I’ve got ten Movements.’ Now don’t you see?”“That’s all right. Are you going into politics? If you do you’ll get into Saint-Pelagie, and I shall have to trot down there after you. Oh! if one only knew what one puts one’s foot into when we love a man, by my word of honor we would let you alone to take care of yourselves, you men! However, if you are going away to-morrow we won’t talk of disagreeable things—that would be silly.”The coach stopped before a pretty fourth-story house in the Rue d’Artois, the abode of Mademoiselle Jenny Courand, reputed privately married to the illustrious Gaudissart, a rumor he did not deny. To maintain her supremacy, Jenny required innumerable attentions and threatened to dismiss him for the slightest omission. She ordered him to write from every town and render a minute account of his proceedings.“How many ‘Children’ will it take to furnish my chamber?” she asked, throwing off her shawl by a good fire.“I get five sous for each subscriber.”“Delightful! And is it with five sous that you expect to make me rich? Perhaps you are like the Wandering Jew with your pockets full of money.”“But, Jenny, I shall get a thousand ‘Children.’ Think that children have never had a newspaper to themselves before. But what a fool I am to try to explain matters to you—you can’t understand such things.”“Can’t I? Then tell me—tell me, Gaudissart, if I’m such a goose why do you love me?”“Just because you are a goose—a sublime goose! Listen, Jenny. I am going to undertake the ‘Globe,’ the ‘Movement,’ the ‘Children,’ the insurance business, and some of my old article Paris; instead of earning a miserable eight thousand a year, I’ll bring back twenty thousand at least from each trip.”“Unlace me, Gaudissart, and do it right; don’t tighten me.”“Yes, truly,” said the traveller complacently. “I shall become a shareholder in the newspapers, like Finot, one of my friends, the son of a hatter, who now has thirty thousand francs income, and is going to make himself a peer of France. When one thinks of that little Popinot—ah, mon Dieu! I forgot to tell you that Monsieur Popinot was named minister of commerce yesterday. Why shouldn’t I be ambitious too? Ha! ha! I could easily pick up the jargon of those fellows who talk in the chamber, and bluster with the rest of them. Now, listen to me:—“Gentlemen,” he said, standing behind a chair, “the Press is neither a tool nor an article of barter: it is, under its political aspects, an institution. We are bound, as legislators, to consider all things politically, and therefore we must examine the Press and ask if it is useful or noxious, if it should be encouraged or taxed or free. These are serious questions. I feel that I do not waste the time of this Chamber by examining this article—the Press—and explaining to you its qualities. We are on the verge of an abyss. Undoubtedly the laws have not the nap they ought to have—Hein?” he said, looking at Jenny. “All orators put France on the verge of an abyss. Don’t I know their dodges? I’m up to all the tricks of all the trades. Do you know why? Because I was born with a caul; my mother has got it, but I’ll give it to you. You’ll see! I shall soon be in the government.”“You!”“Why shouldn’t I be the Baron Gaudissart, peer of France? Haven’t they twice elected Monsieur Popinot as deputy? He dines with Louis-Philippe. There’s Finot; he is going to be, they say, a member of the Council. Suppose they send me as ambassador to London? I tell you I’d nonplus those English! No man ever got the better of Gaudissart, and nobody ever will. Yes, I say it! No one ever outwitted me, and no one can—in any walk of life, politics or impolitics. But, for the time being, I must give myself wholly to the capitalists; to the ‘Globe,’ the ‘Movement,’ the ‘Children,’ and my article Paris.”“You will be brought up with a round turn, you and your newspapers. I’ll bet you won’t get further than Poitiers before the police will nab you.”“What will you bet?”“A shawl.”“Done! If I lose that shawl I’ll go back to the article Paris and the hat business. But as for getting the better of Gaudissart—never! never!”And the illustrious traveller threw himself into a Napoleonic attitude. Gaudissart was thirty-eight, of medium height, stout and fat like men who roll about in stage-coaches, with a round face, ruddy cheeks, and regular features of the type sculptors adopt as models for Abundance, Law, Force, Commerce. His stomach swelled in a pear shape; he caught Jenny up and kissed her.“Hold your tongue, young woman!” he said. “What do you know about Saint-Simonism, Fourierism, heroic enterprise, or woman’s freedom? I’ll tell you what they are—ten francs for each subscription, Madame Gaudissart.”“On my word of honor, you are going crazy, Gaudissart.”“More and more crazy about you,” he replied, flinging his hat upon the sofa.The next morning, after a glorious breakfast with Jenny, Gaudissart departed on horseback to work the chief towns of his district, assigned by his various enterprises. After forty-five days beating the country between Paris and Blois, he remained two weeks at Blois to write correspondence and make short visits. The night before leaving Blois for Tours he indited a letter to Mademoiselle Jenny Courand which proves their tie and his triumphs: he boasted of lodging two millions between Paris and Blois, selling many Ternaux shawls at Orléans, lamented that the “Movement” did not move the republicans, recounted making a hundred “Globes,” and rejoiced at getting two thousand “Children” subscriptions. He described cajoling a dynastic diner into subscribing to the “Children’s Journal” rather than quarrel, and promised to do Amboise in two days and “topple” Tours.CHAPTER IIIFive days later Gaudissart started from the Hotel des Faisans in Tours and went to Vouvray, a rich district where the public mind seemed susceptible. Trotting along the embankment, he admired the landscape, little dreaming his commercial infallibility was about to perish in Vouvray.A note on the Tourangian mind: stamped by Rabelais’ spirit, Touraine’s people are polished, witty, poetic, voluptuous, yet their impulses subside quickly. The softness of the atmosphere, ease of life, and jovial manners smother sentiment of art and enervate will. At home the Tourangian sits like an Indian on his mat or a Turk on his divan, employing wit in laughing at neighbors and making merry all his days. Touraine is like Rabelais’ Abbaye of Theleme—good cheer reigns.The illustrious Gaudissart encountered in Vouvray one of those indigenous jesters whose wit is perfection. The Tourangians especially hated and villified Saint-Simonian doctrines, their hatred taking the form of superb disdain and witty maliciousness. For his sins, after arriving and consulting the landlord Mitouflet, Gaudissart betook himself to the town’s merry-maker, a former dyer named Monsieur Vernier, influential among the small proprietors, a leader of gossip and squabbles.“Monsieur Vernier,” Gaudissart began, “I come to solicit your aid. I am sent into the provinces on an enterprise of the utmost importance, undertaken by bankers who—”“Who mean to win our tricks,” said Vernier, used to travellers.“Precisely,” replied Gaudissart. “But with your fine tact, Monsieur, you must be aware we can’t win tricks unless it is their interest to play at cards. I am no longer a common traveller. The most distinguished bankers in Paris take part in this affair; the most respectable houses are concerned, and their interests guarantee—” and Gaudissart drew forth his string of phrases.Vernier let him go the length of his tether, listening with apparent interest which completely deceived him. But after “guarantee” Vernier ceased attention and plotted to play him a malicious trick: to fetch the town’s supposed banker—Margaritis, a half-insane man—and have Gaudissart deal with him.At the head of the Valley Coquette lived Margaritis, of Italian origin, half-insane, married, childless, cared for by a devoted wife. He carried two long-bladed knives, sometimes threatened his wife, and insisted on keeping a sack of flour and two puncheons of wine in the cellar. He made incessant demands for newspapers and, for seven years, read the same old “Indre-et-Loire” without perceiving the repetition. When company came he often sat silent, then at ten o’clock on a certain clock he would rise with mechanical precision and say sternly, “Go away!” He had lucid intervals when he advised on wine sales; at times he became annoying, ransacking closets and stealing delicacies. Once in five years his being told “the good-man does not hear anything” drove him to frenzy, knife flourishing.The evening before Gaudissart reached Vouvray, Madame Margaritis had found it hard to deceive her husband, who insisted on watching his puncheons; she had begged neighbor Pierre Champlain to roll two puncheons into their cellar to quiet him. Vernier told his wife he would take Benoîte Fontanieu as witness to the scene, to spread the joke.Vernier escorted Gaudissart, who believed he had made a conquest of both ladies with wit and humor, to La Fuye, the modest house of Margaritis. The salon opened to the field; Margaritis, seated in a yellow Utrecht-velvet arm-chair, did not stir. Madame Vernier introduced the traveller and bade him “put away your mathematics and talk.” Margaritis rose and invited Gaudissart to sit.“Monsieur has doubtless been in business?” Gaudissart began.“Public business,” answered Margaritis. “I pacificated Calabria under Murat.”“In that case we shall quickly understand each other,” said Gaudissart.Margaritis’s peculiarities—pear-shaped bald head, sunken eyes, thin nose, long chin—gave him a look between a retired professor and a rag-picker. Gaudissart, turning his watch-key with a rotatory click that kept the lunatic quiet, launched into his patter about life insurance. Time, he said, is the most precious social wealth; to economize time is to become wealthy. The bankers and capitalists who had seen the waste of rising men's time had “capitalized” future prospects, cashing talents by discounting time, representing in money the products developed by time—the moral qualities and talents as living forces. He explained with literal examples: suppose a painter has a glorious future; the company will recognize and insure his intellectual capital, paying his family in case of death; the painter pays a small annual premium, three percent, to secure his family and thus free himself of anxieties distracting him from success. Insurance, Gaudissart called it, is the exchequer of beneficence, a mutual insurance against poverty, the cashing of talent.“That is usury!” cried Margaritis.Gaudissart switched tack and offered wine; Margaritis poured Vouvray of 1819 and claimed it as Head of Vouvray, a wine for kings. The lunatic delighted in pointing out the etymology: capital, caput—head—the Head of Vouvray. He even offered to sell his puncheons.Gaudissart returned to his argument: the insured man, once recognized and holding company paper as security, could borrow on it at the Bourse and among bankers; civilization denies bread to the men who give it luxury, he declared, and insurance bridges the gulf. The company also offered annuities on a sliding scale superior to tontines. Margaritis urged another glass; Gaudissart praised the wine.When Gaudissart turned to the “Globe” and Saint-Simonian doctrine, Margaritis fell into position and professed comprehension, though answering with local touches: “Twice; on foot.” Gaudissart praised the paper as full of life, science, and as something to make time pass agreeably in the country. It cost eighty francs; Margaritis would not pay but offered seven francs for the “Children’s Journal” and consented to push the “Globe”’s interests if Gaudissart took his wine, bargaining humorously over the price. The two men strolled among the vines; the three ladies and Vernier watched, laughing at the spectacle of the urbane traveller and the lunatic discussing, gesticulating, and walking with vehemence.Finally Gaudissart sat at a card-table and wrote an order for delivery of the two casks. Margaritis read it, counted out seven francs for the “Children’s Journal,” and gave them to the traveller. Gaudissart, a Norman who required reciprocal agreements, demanded Margaritis sign a bond to deliver the two puncheons. Margaritis did. Gaudissart left humming triumphantly.CHAPTER VAt the Soleil d’Or Gaudissart conversed with landlord Mitouflet, an old soldier, about Margaritis. The host, who never laughed at a jest but took it with a soldier’s gravity, hinted that Margaritis was a lunatic.“May be; the good-man knows a deal. He knows too much for other folks,” said Mitouflet.Gaudissart, enraged when Vernier’s prank was revealed, marched to the dyer’s salon where Vernier was laughing with friends. “Monsieur,” he began, “you are a scoundrel and a blackguard; under pain of being thought a turn-key—you will give me satisfaction for the insult you dared to offer me in sending me to a lunatic! Do you hear me, Monsieur Vernier, dyer?”Vernier, delighted at the audience, replied that it was right to make fun of a man who wanted them to feather his nest by subscribing to a paper preaching a new religion that denied inheritance. “Père Margaritis said things a great deal more sensible,” he added.Gaudissart flew at Vernier to slap him but the crowd interposed; his wig fell on Mademoiselle Clara Vernier’s head. He declared he would be at the Soleil d’Or until morning, ready to show what giving satisfaction meant; he had “fought in July.” Vernier answered he would “fight in Vouvray,” and make the traveller stay longer than he imagined.For the first time the prince of travellers did not dine jovially. The town was in a ferment over the duel. Madame Fontanieu and the assistant-mayor implored Mitouflet to arrange things to avoid a violent death. He took it upon himself. In the evening he brought pens and paper to Gaudissart.“If you are going to fight to-morrow,” Mitouflet said, “you had better make some settlement of your affairs; perhaps you have letters to write. Are you a good swordsman? I have foils.” They put on foils and masks; Mitouflet’s experience won him sixty-two passes. He advised pistols, suggesting they be loaded to fire wide so both could retire with honor. Gaudissart grumbled about possibly killing the man; Mitouflet calmed him.Next morning the adversaries met by the bridge of La Cise. Vernier nearly shot a cow; they embraced after firing in the air. “Your joke was rough, but it was a good one,” said Gaudissart; Vernier announced twenty subscriptions to the “Children’s Journal.” “Why shouldn’t we all breakfast together?” said Gaudissart. Men who fight are often those who come to good understanding.Back at the inn Gaudissart wanted to send a summons to Margaritis for delivery of the two casks. “But he has not got them,” said Vernier. Gaudissart demanded indemnity and considered himself outwitted if he failed to pursue the claim. Madame Margaritis, alarmed, brought thirty francs to the traveller; Gaudissart accepted it and left Vouvray content with the settlement, though recognizing the region’s resistance to new ideas.On returning from his trip through the southern departments, Gaudissart rode in a diligence and explained the enigmas of life to a young man as if to an infant. Passing Vouvray the young man exclaimed at the fine site; Gaudissart, patronizing, replied, “Yes, Monsieur, but not habitable on account of the people. You get into duels every day. Why, it is not three months since I fought one just there,” pointing to the bridge, “with a damned dyer; but I made an end of him—he bit the dust!”ADDENDUMThe following personages appear in other stories of the Human Comedy. The Armorial of the Etudes, devised by Ferdinand de Gramont, is a complete manual of French Heraldry, forgetting nothing, even the arms of the Empire, and I shall preserve it as a monument of friendship and patience. What knowledge of the feudal spirit is shown in the Beauseants’ Pulchre sedens, melius agens; the Espards’ Des partem leonis; the Vandenesses’ Ne se vend. Your poetic touch brings elegance and accuracy to countless learned details.Your old friend,DE BALZAC.THE MUSE OF THE DEPARTMENTOn the skirts of Le Berry stands a town on the Loire that attracts the traveler’s eye. Sancerre crowns the highest hill of a chain ending the Nivernais range. The Loire floods the flats at the slopes’ feet, leaving fertile yellow alluvium except where sand from violent risings has spoiled the land.Because the town’s hill lies well above the river, the little port Saint-Thibault handles Sancerre’s traffic: wine shipments and oak staves, produce from the upper and lower Loire. By the time this story begins, suspension bridges at Cosne and Saint-Thibault had been built, so travelers from Paris by the southern road no longer needed ferries.
