This marks that the great cross-shuffle of 1830 was past; the House of Orleans had long favored substantial improvements, though somewhat like a husband spending his wife’s marriage portion.Except on a small plateau, the streets are steep; the town is girdled by slopes called the Great Ramparts, the place’s highroads. Outside them lies a belt of vineyards. Wine is the chief industry, producing aromatic vintages close to Burgundy’s in character, deceiving the common palate. Sancerre finds in Paris wine-shops the quick market necessary for wines that will not keep more than seven or eight years. Below the town are a few villages—Fontenoy and Saint-Satur—almost suburbs, recalling the smiling vineyards about Neuchâtel in Switzerland.The town retains much of its ancient look: narrow pebble-paved streets and some old houses remain. The citadel, a relic of feudal power, endured one of the most terrible sieges of our religious wars, when French Calvinists surpassed even the ferocity of Walter Scott’s Cameronians.Richer in a greater past but divested of military importance, Sancerre is destined for a less glorious future, since trade favors the Loire’s right bank. The sketch suggests it will grow lonelier despite two bridges to Cosne.Sancerre, the pride of the left bank, numbers at most three thousand five hundred inhabitants, while Cosne now exceeds six thousand. Within half a century the roles of these opposite towns have reversed. The advantage of situation still belongs to the historic town: the view is enchanting, the air pure, vegetation splendid, and the inhabitants—friendly and in harmony with nature—are not Puritans, though two-thirds are Calvinists. Under these conditions, despite small-town inconveniences and an officious gaze that makes private life almost public, a local spirit—a kind of patriotism, though not a substitute for love of home—flourishes.Sancerre prides itself on producing Horace Bianchon, a glory of modern medicine, and Etienne Lousteau, an author of secondary rank and one of our most successful journalists. The district, however, suffers from domination by seven or eight large landowners—the wire-pullers of elections. A small conspiracy to shake off the electoral yoke imposed by a creed that had reduced it to a rotten borough was attempted by a handful whose vanity was provoked, but it failed: jealousy of one man’s inevitable elevation roused the others. This revealed the scheme’s fatal defect; the proposed remedy was to rally round a champion at the next election—one of the two men who had so gloriously represented Sancerre in Paris.This idea was advanced for provincial politics; since 1830 the nomination of parochial dignitaries has so increased that genuine statesmen have become rare in the lower chamber. In fact, the plan was hatched by the Superior Woman of the borough, dux feminae fasti, from personal interest. It was so rooted in her past and crucial to her future prospects that it can scarcely be explained without a sketch of her antecedent career.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Sancerre at that time could boast a Superior Woman, long misprized but now, about 1836, enjoying an extensive local reputation. This was also the period when two Sancerrois in Paris were attaining distinction and fashion: Etienne Lousteau wrote reviews for a paper with eight thousand subscribers; and Bianchon, already chief physician to a hospital, Officer of the Legion of Honor, and member of the Academy of Sciences, had just been made a professor.If the word did not to many readers imply blame, one might say George Sand created “Sandism,” so true is it that morally every good has a reverse. This leprosy of sentimentality might have been charming. Still, Sandism has a good side: the woman it attacks bases her superiority on scorned feelings; she is a blue-stocking of sentiment and somewhat less of a bore, love partly neutralizing literature. The chief result of George Sand’s celebrity was to reveal that France has an enormous number of superior women who, until now, generously left the field to the Maréchal de Saxe’s granddaughter.The Superior Woman of Sancerre lived at La Baudraye, a town- and country-house within ten minutes of the town, in the village or suburb of Saint-Satur. The present La Baudrayes, as often happens, have thrust themselves in and substituted for those La Baudrayes whose name, glorious in the Crusades, figured in Le Berry’s history.The story must be told.In the time of Louis XIV a certain sheriff named Milaud, whose forefathers had been fervent Calvinists, was converted at the Revocation of the Edict of Nantes. To encourage conversions in a Calvinist stronghold, the King gave Milaud an appointment in the “Waters and Forests,” granted him arms and the title Sire de la Baudraye, with the fief of the old La Baudrayes. The descendants of the famous Captain la Baudraye later fell into one of the snares for heretics in the new decrees and were hanged—an unworthy deed of the great King.Under Louis XV Milaud de la Baudraye rose from squire to Chevalier and had influence enough to obtain for his son a cornet’s commission in the Musketeers. This officer perished at Fontenoy, leaving a child to whom Louis XVI subsequently granted, by patent, the privileges of a farmer-general in remembrance of his father’s death.This financier, a fashionable wit great at charades, capping verses and posies to Chloris, lived in society, clung to the Duc de Nivernais, and fancied himself obliged to follow the nobility into exile; but he took care to carry his money with him. Thus the rich émigré was able to assist more than one family of high rank.In 1800, tired of hoping and perhaps of lending, he returned to Sancerre, bought back La Baudraye out of vanity and imaginary pride—quite intelligible in a sheriff’s grandson—though under the Consulate his prospects were slender; all the more so because the ex-farmer-general had small hopes that his heir would perpetuate the new race of La Baudraye.Jean Athanase Polydore Milaud de la Baudraye, his only son, delicate from birth, was plainly the child of a man whose constitution had been exhausted by excesses and late marriage, bringing degeneracy into high society. During the emigration Madame de la Baudraye, a girl of no fortune chosen for her birth, patiently reared this sallow, sickly boy with the devoted love mothers show such changelings. Her death—she was a Casteran de la Tour—contributed to Monsieur de la Baudraye’s return to France.This Lucullus of the Milauds, when he died, left his son the fief, stripped indeed of its fines and dues, but graced with weathercocks bearing his coat-of-arms, a thousand louis-d’or (in 1802 a considerable sum), and certain receipts for claims on very distinguished émigrés enclosed in a pocketbook full of verses, the wrapper inscribed Vanitas vanitatum et omnia vanitas.Young La Baudraye did not die; he owed his life to habits of monastic strictness, to the economy of action Fontenelle preached as the religion of the invalid, and, above all, to the air of Sancerre and the influence of its fine elevation, whence a panorama over the Loire valley extends for forty leagues.From 1802 to 1815 young La Baudraye added several plots to his vineyards and devoted himself to the culture of the vine. The Restoration at first seemed so insecure that he dared not go to Paris to claim his debts; but after Napoleon’s death he tried to turn his father’s collection of autographs into money, though he did not understand the philosophy that mixed IOUs and copies of verses. But the winegrower wasted time trying to impress his identity on the Duke of Nivernais “and others,” and returned to Sancerre to his vintage without having obtained anything but offers of service.The Restoration raised the nobility to such lustre that La Baudraye wished to justify his ambitions by having an heir. This result of matrimony he considered doubtful, or he would not have so long postponed the step; however, finding himself still above ground in 1823 at forty-three—a length of years which no doctor, astrologer, or midwife would have dared promise—he hoped to earn the reward of his sober life. Yet his choice showed such lack of prudence regarding his frail constitution that the malicious wit of a country town suspected some deep calculation.At that time Monseigneur the Archbishop of Bourges had converted to Catholicism a young woman, the daughter of one of the citizen families that had upheld Calvinism and, thanks to obscurity or compromise, escaped persecution under Louis XIV. The Piedefers—whose name suggested the champions of the Reformation—had become respectable cloth merchants. In Louis XVI’s reign Abraham Piedefer fell into difficulties and at his death in 1786 left his two children in extreme poverty. One, Tobie Piedefer, went to the Indies, leaving the meagre inheritance to his elder brother. During the Revolution Moise Piedefer bought nationalized land, pulled down abbeys and churches with ancestral zeal, and married a Catholic, the only daughter of a member of the Convention who had perished on the scaffold. This ambitious Piedefer died in 1819, leaving a little girl of remarkable beauty. Brought up in the Calvinist faith, she was named Dinah, following the sect’s custom of choosing Biblical rather than Roman Catholic saint-names.Mademoiselle Dinah Piedefer was placed by her mother in the Demoiselles Chamarolles’ school in Bourges and was soon distinguished for mind as well as beauty; but she was snubbed by girls of birth and fortune destined to play greater parts in the world than a plebeian’s daughter dependent on the settlement of the Piedefer estate. Having raised herself above her companions, Dinah aimed to remain on a level with them for life. She determined, therefore, to renounce Calvinism in the hope that the Cardinal would favor his proselyte and interest himself in her prospects. You may judge of Mademoiselle Dinah’s superiority, since at seventeen she converted solely from ambition.The Archbishop, convinced Dinah would adorn society, was anxious to see her married. But every family to whom he made advances recoiled from a damsel with the looks of a princess, reputed the cleverest of Mademoiselle Chamarolles’ pupils and who always shone at the prize-giving. A thousand crowns a year, the share she might hope from La Hautoy when divided between mother and daughter, would be trifling compared to the expenses a husband would incur by the personal advantages of so brilliant a creature.As soon as these facts reached little Polydore de la Baudraye—they were the talk of every circle in the Cher—he went to Bourges just when Madame Piedefer, a devotee at high services, had almost made up her mind and her daughter’s to take the first comer with well-lined pockets—the first chien coiffé, as they say in Le Berry. If the Cardinal was delighted to receive Monsieur de la Baudraye, Monsieur de la Baudraye was even more pleased to receive a wife from the Cardinal’s hands. The little gentleman only demanded a formal promise that His Eminence would support his claims with the President of the Council to enable him to recover his debts from the Duc de Navarreins “and others” by a lien on their indemnities. This method, however, seemed to the Minister then occupying the Pavillon Marsan too sharp, and he gave the vine-owner to understand his business would be attended to in good time.It is easy to imagine the excitement produced in the Sancerre district by news of Monsieur de la Baudraye’s imprudent marriage.“It is quite intelligible,” said President Boirouge; “the little man was very much startled, I am told, at hearing handsome young Milaud, the Attorney-General’s deputy at Nevers, say to Monsieur de Clagny as they looked at La Baudraye’s turrets, ‘That will be mine some day.’—‘But,’ says Clagny, ‘he may marry and have children.’—‘Impossible!’—So you may imagine how such a changeling as little La Baudraye must hate that colossal Milaud.”At Nevers there was a plebeian branch of the Milauds, grown rich in the cutlery trade, that had brought its representative up to the civil service under Marchangy, now dead.It will be useful to set aside the baser material interests that occupied Monsieur de la Baudraye, by briefly relating his negotiations in Paris. This will also shed light on certain mysterious contemporary political phenomena that hampered the Ministry during the Restoration.The Ministers’ promises were so illusory that Monsieur de la Baudraye decided to go to Paris when the Cardinal’s presence was required there by the Chambers’ sitting.This is how the Duc de Navarreins, the principal debtor threatened by Monsieur de la Baudraye, got out of the scrape.The country gentleman, lodging at the Hotel de Mayence, Rue Saint-Honoré near the Place Vendôme, one morning received a visit from a confidential agent of the Ministry, an expert in “winding up” business. This elegant person, stepping from an elegant cab and neatly dressed, was shown to the third floor, to a small room where he found his provincial concocting coffee over a bedroom fire.“Is it to Monsieur Milaud de la Baudraye that I have the honor—”“Yes,” said the little man, draping himself in his dressing-gown.After examining the garment—an illicit offspring of an old china wrapper of Madame Piedefer’s and a gown of the late Madame de la Baudraye—the emissary considered the man, the dressing-gown, and the tin saucepan on the little stove so characteristic that he deemed it needless to beat about the bush.“I will lay a wager, monsieur,” said he audaciously, “that you dine for forty sous at Hurbain’s in the Palais-Royal.”“Pray, why?”“Oh, I know you, having seen you there,” replied the Parisian gravely. “All the princes’ creditors dine there. You know you recover scarcely ten per cent on debts from these fine gentlemen. I would not give you five per cent on a debt to be recovered from the estate of the late Duc d’Orléans—nor even,” he added in a low voice, “from MONSIEUR.”“So you have come to buy up the bills?” said La Baudraye, thinking himself clever.“Buy them!” said his visitor. “Why, what do you take me for? I am Monsieur des Lupeaulx, Master of Appeals, Secretary-General to the Ministry, and I have come to propose an arrangement.”“What is that?”“Of course, monsieur, you know the position of your debtor—”“Of my debtors—”“Well, monsieur, you understand their position; they stand high in the King’s good graces but have no money and must make a good show. Again, you know the political difficulties. The aristocracy must be rehabilitated against a strong third estate. The King’s idea—of which France does him scant justice—is to create a peerage as a national institution analogous to the English peerage. To realize this we need years—and millions. Noblesse oblige. The Duc de Navarreins, who is, as you know, first gentleman of the Bedchamber to the King, does not repudiate his debt, but he cannot—Now, be reasonable—consider politics. We are emerging from the pit of the Revolution—and you yourself are noble—He simply cannot pay—”“Monsieur—”“You are hasty,” said des Lupeaulx. “Listen. He cannot pay in money. Well then; you, a clever man, can take payment in favors—royal or ministerial.”“What! When in 1793 my father put down one hundred thousand—”“My dear sir, recrimination is useless. Listen to a simple statement in political arithmetic: the collectorship at Sancerre is vacant; a certain paymaster-general of the forces has a claim on it, but he has no chance of getting it; you have the chance—and no claim. You will get the place. You will hold it for three months, then resign, and Monsieur Gravier will give twenty thousand francs for it. In addition, the Order of the Legion of Honor will be conferred on you.”“Well, that is something,” said the wine-grower, tempted more by the money than the red ribbon.“But then,” said des Lupeaulx, “you must show your gratitude to His Excellency by restoring to Monseigneur the Duc de Navarreins all your claims on him.”La Baudraye returned to Sancerre as Collector of Taxes. Six months later he was superseded by Monsieur Gravier, regarded as one of the most agreeable financiers who had served under the Empire, and whom Monsieur de la Baudraye introduced to his wife.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When released from his functions, Monsieur de la Baudraye returned to Paris to settle accounts with his debtors. He became Referendary under the Great Seal, Baron, and Officer of the Legion of Honor. After selling the referendary appointment and collecting from remaining debtors, he reappeared at Sancerre as Master of Appeals and was named Royal Commissioner to a commercial association in the Nivernais with a salary of six thousand francs—a sinecure. Thus the man thought to have blundered financially had in fact made a shrewd match in marriage.By thrift and an indemnity from his father’s estate, nationalized and sold in 1793, he realized his dream by 1827. Paying four hundred thousand francs down and committing to further instalments—after six years of strict economy—he bought the estate of Anzy on the Loire, two leagues above Sancerre, and its celebrated castle by Philibert de l’Orme, long the Uxelles family seat. He became a major provincial landowner. An entail created by letters patent in December 1820 included Anzy, La Baudraye, and La Hautoy—though this did not erase Dinah’s long years of unacknowledged parsimony until 1835.This account of the first Baron de la Baudraye’s finances explains his character. Those familiar with rural manias will recognize in him the land-hunger that becomes a consuming passion—an outward avarice that often brings ruin when mortgage interest outstrips agricultural yield. From 1802 to 1827, those who had laughed at the little man trotting to Saint-Thibault and tending his vineyards saw their error when the ant-lion seized his prey after the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse’s extravagance led to the sale of her splendid property.Madame Piedefer came to live with her daughter. The combined fortunes of Monsieur de la Baudraye and his mother-in-law—who accepted an annuity of twelve hundred francs on La Hautoy and handed it over to him—yielded an acknowledged income of about fifteen thousand francs.Early in her married life Dinah made La Baudraye agreeable. She converted a spacious forecourt into a formal garden, removed wine-stores and shabby outhouses, and laid out a second garden behind the small turreted Manor with shrubs, flower-beds, and lawns, screened from the vineyards by a creeper-clad wall. She made the interiors as comfortable as circumstances allowed.To prevent being ruined by so superior a young woman, Monsieur de la Baudraye prudently kept silent about his Parisian recoveries. That secrecy lent him mystery and gave dignity in his wife’s eyes during their early years—so majestic is silence.The changes at La Baudraye made everyone eager to see the mistress, especially since Dinah would receive no company until she’d settled at home and studied the neighborhood and her taciturn husband. When, one spring morning in 1825, she was first seen walking on the Mall in a blue velvet dress with her mother in black, Sancerre was excited. Visitors, constrained in her presence, admired fine furnishings and curios—shawl-pattern carpets, a carved gilt writing-table, brocade curtains, a Japanese bowl of flowers among recent books. Hearing Dinah play at sight on the piano without demur confirmed her reputation. To avoid carelessness or bad taste, she kept abreast of fashions and luxury through active correspondence with Anna Grossetete, her schoolfriend at Mademoiselle Chamarolles’. Anna, with a fine fortune, had married the Comte de Fontaine’s third son, so Dinah’s callers were constantly piqued that she led fashion; however they tried, they were always, as turfmen say, distanced.These trifles stirred malignant envy among Sancerre’s ladies, but Dinah’s conversation and wit provoked absolute aversion. In her ambition to keep her mind at Parisian levels, she tolerated no vacuous small talk, no old-fashioned compliments, no petty backstairs slander. She would not endure tittle-tattle; she liked to hear of discoveries in science and art, the latest theatre pieces, the newest poems, and by airing current cant words she gave the impression of thinking deeply.The Abbé Duret, cure of Sancerre, an old worldly cleric fond of cards, welcomed this taste and enjoyed her company. The sous-préfet, Vicomte de Chargeboeuf, found in her drawing-room an oasis from provincial life. Monsieur de Clagny, the public prosecutor, so admired Dinah that he refused all promotion and became a devoted worshipper. He was a tall, lean man with a minatory countenance and terrible eyes under enormous eyebrows; his eloquence could be incisive, though his love-making was not.Monsieur Gravier was a little, round man who had been a charming ballad-singer in the Empire days, an accomplishment that helped him become Paymaster-General of the forces. Having been mixed up in Spanish matters with generals then in opposition, he leveraged connections to secure the Receivership at Sancerre by payment. The easy frivolity of the Empire had turned ponderous in Gravier; he failed, or refused, to grasp the difference in manners under the Restoration. He fancied himself superior to Monsieur de Clagny and prided himself on better taste—buff waistcoat, gray trousers, snug coats, a fashionable silk tie slipped through a diamond ring—while the lawyer wore plain black.These four men were the first to extol Dinah’s cultivation, taste, and refinement, calling her a woman of superior mind. The women, however, whispered that “Madame de la Baudraye must laugh at us behind our back.”That belief, more or less correct, kept them from visiting. Dinah, branded a pedant for speaking grammatically, was nicknamed the Sappho of Saint-Satur. At last the neighborhood made insolent sport of the great qualities that had roused their enmity. They denied her relative superiority—an act that only emphasized their own ignorance and could not forgive it. In a community of hunched backs, a straight figure is monstrous; Dinah was deemed dangerous and found herself in a desert.Astonished by the infrequent, perfunctory calls of the neighborhood women, Dinah asked Monsieur de Clagny the reason. “You are too superior a woman to be liked by other women,” he said. Monsieur Gravier, when pressed by the forlorn Dinah, admitted that she was not content to be merely charming: she was clever, well educated, read every new book, loved poetry, played music, and spoke delightfully—too much for jealous women to forgive.Men told Monsieur de la Baudraye, “You who have such a Superior Woman for a wife are very fortunate.” He began to echo the sentiment proudly. Madame Piedefer, flattered by her daughter, boasted similarly in society—“My daughter, who is a very Superior Woman, was writing yesterday to Madame de Fontaine...”Those who know the world, especially Paris, know how such reputations are made.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Two years later, by the end of 1825, Dinah de la Baudraye was accused of preferring only men as visitors; soon it was said she did not care for women—and that became a crime. Not a trifling action escaped criticism or misrepresentation. After making every sacrifice a well-bred woman could make and placing herself entirely in the right, Madame de la Baudraye was so rash as to say to a false friend who condoled with her isolation:“I would rather have my bowl empty than with anything in it!”The remark produced a terrible effect in Sancerre and was cruelly retorted on the Sappho of Saint-Satur when, being childless after five years of marriage, little de la Baudraye became a byword for laughter. To explain the provincial witticism, recall the Bailli de Ferrette—some may have known him—of whom it was said he was the bravest man in Europe for daring to walk on his legs and who was accused of putting lead in his shoes to save himself from being blown away. Monsieur de la Baudraye, a sallow, almost diaphanous creature, would have been engaged by the Bailli de Ferrette as first gentleman-in-waiting if that diplomatist had been the Grand Duke of Baden rather than merely his envoy.Monsieur de la Baudraye’s legs were so thin that, for decency, he wore false calves; his thighs were like the arms of an average man, his body not unlike that of a cockchafer, and he would have made an advantageous foil to the Bailli de Ferrette. As he walked his leg-pads often twisted onto his shins; he thanked anyone who warned him. He wore knee-breeches, black silk stockings, and a white waistcoat until 1824. After the marriage he adopted blue trousers and boots with heels, which made Sancerre declare that he had added two inches to his stature so as to come up to his wife’s chin. For ten years he was always seen in the same little bottle-green coat with large white-metal buttons and a black stock that accentuated his cold, stingy face, lighted by gray-blue eyes as keen and passionless as a cat’s. Gentle, as men with fixed habits are, he seemed to please his wife by never contradicting her; he allowed her to do the talking and moved with the deliberate tenacity of an insect.Dinah, adored for her unrivaled beauty and admired for her cleverness by the most gentlemanly men of the place, encouraged their admiration by conversations it was later said she prepared beforehand. Finding her listeners rapt, she began to listen to herself, enjoyed haranguing them, and at last regarded her friends as the chorus in a tragedy, there only to give her cues. In fact, she had a fine collection of phrases and ideas, derived either from books or by assimilating the opinions of her companions, and thus became a sort of mechanical instrument, going off on a round of phrases as soon as some chance remark released the spring. To her credit, Dinah was full of knowledge and read everything—medical books, statistics, science, jurisprudence—because she did not know how to spend her days once she had reviewed her flower-beds and given orders to the gardener. Gifted with an excellent memory and the knack some women have for hitting the right word, she could talk on any subject with the lucidity of a studied style. Men came from Cosne, La Charité, Nevers, and from Léré, Vailly, Argent, Blancafort, and Aubigny to be introduced to Madame de la Baudraye, as in Switzerland they were introduced to Madame de Staël. Those who heard the tunes emitted by this musical snuff-box went away amazed and told such wonders of Dinah that all the women ten leagues round grew jealous.There is an indescribable headiness in the admiration we inspire, or in the effect of playing a part, which keeps criticism from reaching the idol. An atmosphere, perhaps from unceasing nervous tension, forms a halo through which the world below is seen. How otherwise account for the perennial good faith that leads to repeated presentments of the same effects, and the constant ignoring of warnings given by children, so dreaded by parents, or by husbands familiar with the peacock airs of their wives? Monsieur de la Baudraye had the frankness of a man who opens an umbrella at the first drop of rain. When his wife started on Negro emancipation or prison reform, he would take up his little blue cap and vanish without a sound, certain he could get to Saint-Thibault to see off a cargo of puncheons and return an hour later to find the discussion near its close. Or, if he had no business, he would walk on the Mall, which commanded the Loire valley panorama, and take a draught of fresh air while his wife performed a sonata in words or a dialectical duet.Once established as a Superior Woman, Dinah was eager to prove her devotion to remarkable creations of art. She threw herself into propaganda for the romantic school, extending Art to poetry, painting, literature, sculpture, furniture, and opera. She became a medievalist and prized Renaissance treasures, employing her allies as commission agents. Soon after marriage she acquired the Rougets’ furniture, sold at Issoudun in early 1824. She purchased good things in Nivernais and the Haute-Loire. At New Year and on her birthday her friends never failed to give curiosities. These fancies pleased Monsieur de la Baudraye; they made him seem to sacrifice a few crowns to his wife’s taste. In fact his land mania allowed him to think of nothing but the estate of Anzy.These “antiquities” then cost much less than modern furniture. In five or six years the anteroom, dining-room, two drawing-rooms, and boudoir Dinah arranged on La Baudraye’s ground floor—every spot, even the staircase—were crammed with masterpieces collected in the four adjacent departments. Called “queer” by neighbors, they suited Dinah. All these marvels, soon to be the rage, struck strangers introduced to her; they came expecting something unusual and found their expectations surpassed when, behind a bower of flowers, they saw catacombs full of old things, piled as Sommerard used to pile them—that “Old Mortality” of furniture. These finds served as springs which, turned by a question, played off essays on Jean Goujon, Michel Colombe, Germain Pilon, Boulle, Van Huysum, and Boucher, the great native painter of Le Berry; on Clodion, the carver of wood; on Venetian mirrors; on Brustolone, an Italian tenor who was the Michael-Angelo of boxwood and holm oak; on the thirteenth through seventeenth centuries, on Bernard de Palissy’s glazes, Petitot’s enamels, Dürer’s engravings—whom she called Dur—on illuminations on vellum, on Gothic architecture, early decorated, flamboyant and pure—enough to rouse both old and young.Possessed with the idea of waking up Sancerre, Madame de la Baudraye tried to form a so-called literary circle. The Presiding Judge, Monsieur Boirouge, who had a house and garden as part of the Popinot-Chandier property, favored the notion. The wily Judge discussed rules with Madame de la Baudraye; he proposed to figure as one of the founders and to let the house for fifteen years to the literary club. Within a year the members were playing dominoes, billiards, and bouillotte, and drinking mulled wine, punch, and liqueurs. A few elegant suppers were given and some masked balls during Carnival. As to literature—there were the newspapers. Politics and business were discussed. Monsieur de la Baudraye was constantly there—on his wife’s account, as she joked.This result deeply grieved the Superior Woman, who despaired of Sancerre, and therefore collected the wit of the neighborhood in her own drawing-room. Nevertheless, despite the efforts of Messieurs de Chargeboeuf, Gravier, and de Clagny, of the Abbé Duret and the two chief magistrates, of a young doctor, and a young Assistant Judge—all blind admirers of Dinah—there were occasions when, weary of discussion, they allowed excursions into agreeable frivolity, the common basis of worldly conversation. Monsieur Gravier called this “from grave to gay.” The Abbé Duret’s rubber made another pleasing variety on the oracle’s monologues. The three rivals, tired of keeping their minds at the “high range of discussion,” but not daring to confess it, would sometimes turn with ingratiating hints to the old priest.“Monsieur le Curé is dying for his game,” they would say.The wily priest lent himself readily to the little trick. He protested.“We should lose too much by ceasing to listen to our inspired hostess!” and so he would incite Dinah’s magnanimity to take pity at last on her dear Abbé.This bold manoeuvre, a device of the Sous-préfet’s, was repeated with such skill that Dinah never suspected her slaves of escaping to the prison yard, so to speak, of the cardtable; they would leave her one of the younger functionaries to harry.One young landowner, the dandy of Sancerre, fell from Dinah’s favor after some rash demonstrations. Having solicited admission to this circle, where he flattered himself he could snatch the blossom from the constituted authorities who guarded it, he was unfortunate enough to yawn in the middle of an explanation Dinah was favoring him with—for the fourth time, it is true—on the philosophy of Kant. Monsieur de la Thaumassière, grandson of the historian of Le Berry, was thenceforth regarded as a man entirely bereft of soul and brains.The three devotees en titre each submitted to these exorbitant demands on their mind and attention in hope of a crowning triumph when Dinah should become human; for none dared imagine she would give up her innocence as a wife till she had lost all illusions. In 1826, when she was surrounded by adorers, Dinah completed her twentieth year, and the Abbé Duret kept her in a sort of fervid Catholicism; so her worshipers had to be content to overwhelm her with little attentions and small services, only too happy to be taken for the carpet-knights of this sovereign lady by strangers admitted to spend an evening or two at La Baudraye.“Madame de la Baudraye is a fruit that must be left to ripen.” This was Monsieur Gravier’s opinion, who was waiting.As to the lawyer, he wrote four-page letters, to which Dinah replied in soothing words as she walked, leaning on his arm, round and round the lawn after dinner.Madame de la Baudraye, thus guarded by three passions and always under the eye of her pious mother, escaped the malignity of slander. It was evident to all Sancerre that no two of these three men would ever leave the third alone with Madame de la Baudraye; their jealousy was a comedy to lookers-on.To reach Saint-Thibault from Caesar’s Gate there is a way much shorter than that by the ramparts, down what in mountainous districts is called a coursière, at Sancerre le Casse-cou, or Break-neck Alley. The name fits a steep, stony path shut in by high banks of vineyards. By the Break-neck the distance from Sancerre to La Baudraye is much abridged. The ladies of the place, jealous of the Sappho of Saint-Satur, were wont to walk on the Mall, looking down this Longchamp of the bigwigs, whom they would stop and engage in conversation—sometimes the Sous-préfet and sometimes the Public Prosecutor—who would listen with every sign of impatience or uncivil absence of mind. As the turrets of La Baudraye are visible from the Mall, many a younger man came to contemplate Dinah’s abode while envying the ten or twelve privileged persons who might spend their afternoons with the Queen of the neighborhood.Monsieur de la Baudraye was not slow to discover the advantage he, as Dinah’s husband, held over his wife’s adorers, and he used them openly, obtaining remission of taxes and winning two lawsuits. In every litigation he used the Public Prosecutor’s name so effectively that matters were carried no further; like many undersized men, he was contentious and litigious in business, though in the gentlest manner.At the same time, the more plainly guiltless she appeared, the less conceivable Madame de la Baudraye’s position seemed to prying women. Frequently, at the Présidente de Boirouge’s house, ladies of a certain age would spend whole evenings discussing the La Baudraye household among themselves. They all suspected a mystery, a secret such as always interests women experienced in life. And, in fact, at La Baudraye one of those slow, monotonous conjugal tragedies was being played out which would have remained forever unknown if the merciless scalpel of the nineteenth century, driven by the demand for novelty, had not dissected the darkest corners of the heart, or at least those which the decency of past centuries left unopened. That domestic drama sufficiently accounts for Dinah’s immaculate virtue during her early married life.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A young woman whose school triumphs sprang from pride, and whose first scheme in life had succeeded, was unlikely to rest on her laurels. Frail as Monsieur de la Baudraye seemed, he was an unexpectedly good match for Mademoiselle Dinah Piedefer. What motive had this country landowner, at forty-four, in marrying a girl of seventeen—and what could his wife make of the bargain? These were Dinah’s first thoughts.The little man never behaved as his wife expected. He let her take the five precious acres around La Baudraye for pleasure grounds, and paid, almost generously, the seven or eight thousand francs Dinah required for house improvements, enabling her to buy furniture at the Rougets’ sale and redecorate in various styles—Mediaeval, Louis XIV, Pompadour. For a while Dinah believed either he was not so miserly as reputed or that she had great influence over him. The illusion lasted a year and a half.After his second journey to Paris Dinah discovered in him the coldness of a provincial miser where money was concerned. The first time she asked for supplies and played sweetness, the little man bluntly set strict sums: two hundred francs a month for her personal expenses, twelve hundred francs a year to Madame Piedefer as a charge on La Hautoy—two hundred more than the marriage settlement—and nothing explicitly for housekeeping. He reminded her that as a bachelor he had lived on fifteen hundred francs a year and now spent six thousand including rates and repairs; that a winegrower’s returns could be ruined by weather, by bankrupt clients, or bad speculation; and that small owners must count on the minimum, keep a year’s income in hand, and reckon on no more than two-thirds of returns.Any resistance is enough to make a woman vow to subdue it; Dinah flung herself against a will of iron masked with gentleness. She tried to fill him with jealousy and alarms, but his confidence was insolent. He left her, when he went to Paris, with Medor’s conviction in Angelique’s fidelity. When she affected cold disdain to nettle him, Monsieur de la Baudraye gazed at her with fixed eyes, like a cat that waits when a blow is threatened. His mute indifference unnerved the young wife of twenty; she could not understand the selfish quiescence of a man who regulated his life with clocklike regularity. So the little man always evaded, while she always struck out—ten feet above his head.Dinah’s fits of fury at being condemned to La Baudraye and Sancerre can be imagined—she who had dreamed of handling a fortune and managing the dwarf whom she at first humored to command. Hoping to appear on Paris’s greater stage, she accepted the vulgar incense of her admirers with a view to seeing Monsieur de la Baudraye’s name drawn from the electoral urn; she supposed him ambitious after seeing him return thrice from Paris, each time a step higher in social rank. But when she struck at his heart, it was like tapping on marble. The man who had been Receiver-General, Referendary, Master of Appeals, Officer of the Legion of Honor, and Royal Commissioner was but a mole throwing up little hills round a vineyard. Lamentations to the Public Prosecutor, the Sous-préfet, even Monsieur Gravier, increased their devotion to this supposed victim; like many women she never mentioned her speculative schemes, and, finding such speculation vain, she ceased to speculate.Dinah, tossed by mental storms, was undecided when, in the autumn of 1827, news came of the Baron de la Baudraye’s purchase of the estate of Anzy. Then the little man showed a pride and glee that for months altered Dinah’s ideas; she fancied some hidden greatness when she found him applying for a patent of entail. In his triumph he cried, “Dinah, you shall be a countess yet!” There followed a patched-up reunion between husband and wife, the kind that can never endure and only humiliates a woman whose apparent superiority is unreal while her unseen superiority is genuine. Dinah, ridiculous from the perversity of her cleverness, had real qualities of soul which circumstances did not bring to light; provincial life debased her small change of wit daily. Monsieur de la Baudraye, devoid of soul, strength, and wit, seemed a man of character simply by pursuing a fixed plan he was too feeble to change.Their lives entered a first phase of six years during which Dinah became utterly provincial. Paris has many kinds of women—the duchess, the financier’s wife, the ambassadress, the minister’s wife, and ladies on both banks of the Seine—but in the country there is but one kind: the provincial woman. This remark points to a sore of modern society. France in the nineteenth century divides into two broad zones—Paris and the provinces. The provinces are jealous of Paris; Paris barely thinks of the provinces but to demand money. Once Paris was the capital of the provinces and the court ruled the capital; now all Paris is the Court, and all the country is the town.However lofty or clever a girl from any department may be, if she marries in the country and remains there she inevitably becomes the provincial woman. The commonplace of second-rate ideas, indifference to dress, and the culture of vulgar people swamp the sublimer essence hidden in youth; everything decays. How should it be otherwise? Country girls see none but provincials; their choice lies among mediocrities; provincial fathers marry daughters to provincial sons; crossing races isn’t thought of, and thus mind degenerates. Mankind becomes dwarfed in mind and body, for the fatal principle of conformity of fortune governs matrimonial alliances. Men of talent, artists, superior brains—every bird of brilliant plumage—flies to Paris. The provincial woman, inferior in herself, is also inferior through her husband. How can she be happy under this twofold consciousness?A third element deepens the aridity. One flattering balm for a woman is the assurance of being something in the life of a superior man chosen by herself. But in the country husbands are often inferior beings, and bachelors are no better. When a provincial wife commits her “little sin,” she falls for some local dandy who is at best merely well dressed; she knows her fancy is chasing the commonplace. Dinah was preserved from this danger by her pride and the conviction of her own superiority. Even if not guarded by her mother’s presence, her pride and sense of her destiny would have protected her. Flattered by admirers, she saw no lover among them. No man realized the poetical ideal she and Anna Grossetete had sketched. Stirred by homage, she asked herself who she would choose; she preferred Monsieur de Chargeboeuf, a gentleman of good family, but his cold nature, selfishness, and narrow ambitions repelled her. He had deserted a woman he had loved when his family feared scandal. Monsieur de Clagny, whose mind appealed to hers and whose ambition was founded on love, Dinah thought perfectly odious. When condemned to six years at Sancerre she nearly accepted Chargeboeuf’s devotion, but he was appointed to a prefecture and left. Clagny’s great satisfaction was that the new Sous-préfet was a married man whose wife befriended Dinah. The lawyer’s only rival now was Monsieur Gravier—the typical man of forty used by women while they laugh at him, whose hopes they remorselessly encourage. In six years among men introduced from twenty leagues round, none stirred Dinah’s anticipation of love or superior soul.Thus none of Dinah’s choicest faculties developed; she swallowed many insults to her pride, constantly bruised by a husband who calmly played supernumerary in the drama of her life. Compelled to bury her wealth of love, she showed only surface to the world. Now and then she tried to rouse herself, to form some manly resolution; but she was kept in leading strings by the need for money. Slowly, despite ambitious protests and grievous recriminations of her mind, she underwent the provincial metamorphosis. Each day took a fragment of her spirited determination. She had rules for her person which she gradually abandoned. Though at first she kept up with fashions and novelties, she had to limit purchases to her allowance: instead of six hats, caps, or gowns she resigned herself to one gown each season; an admired bonnet served two seasons. Often her artistic sense led her to sacrifice personal requirements to obtain some bit of Gothic furniture. By the seventh year she had her morning dresses made at home by the best local needlewoman; her mother, husband, and friends pronounced her charming in these inexpensive costumes which credited her taste. Her ideas were imitated; with no standard of comparison she fell into provincial snares. If a Parisian has a defect she makes it an adornment; the provincial woman accepts the worst. Dinah, extremely slim, showed it to excess; reduced by dull weariness she began to look like a skeleton in clothes; those who saw her daily did not notice the gradual change.This is a natural result of provincial life. In spite of marriage a young woman preserves her beauty for some time; the town is proud of her, but everyone sees her every day and perception dulled. If she loses color it is scarcely noticed; a little neglect is thought charming. When Dinah ceased to have a new dress each season she seemed to have yielded to the place’s philosophy.Matters of speech, choice of words and ideas, rust as the body if not rubbed up in Paris; but provincialism most stamps gesture, gait, and movement. The provincial walks as if there were nothing to avoid; being known and always in her place, everyone makes way for her, and she loses the charm of the unforeseen. Life in common induces simian mimicry: gestures, tone, manner, attitudes, and countenance are copied. In six years Dinah sank to the pitch of her society. As she took on Monsieur de Clagny’s ideas she assumed his tone and unconsciously fell into masculine manners from seeing none but men; she thought laughing at their ridiculous traits kept her safe, but some hue of what she laughed at remained. A Parisian woman, seeing many examples of good taste, learns to seize the moment to appear advantageously; Madame de la Baudraye, accustomed to the stage, acquired an indefinable theatrical and domineering manner—the air of a prima donna—ironical smiles would soon have cured her in the capital.After she had acquired these absurdities, deceived by worshipers and imagining them graces, a moment of terrible awakening came like an avalanche. In one day she was crushed by a frightful comparison.In 1829, after Chargeboeuf’s departure, she expected the Baronne de Fontaine. Anna’s husband, now Director-General under the Minister of Finance, took advantage of leave on his father’s death to take his wife to Italy. Anna came to Sancerre; the meeting was disastrous. Anna, less handsome than Dinah at school, was now a thousand times handsomer despite fatigue and traveling dress. She stepped from an elegant chaise loaded with Paris milliners’ boxes and had a lady’s maid whose airs frightened Dinah. All the difference between a Paris woman and a provincial was evident: Anna spent six thousand francs a year on herself—the same sum that kept the household at La Baudraye.In twenty-four hours the friends exchanged confidences; the Parisian, seeing herself so far superior to the phoenix of Mademoiselle Chamarolles’ school, showed kindness and attentions that stabbed Dinah. She understood Anna’s advantages lay on the surface while her own were buried. When Anna left, Madame de la Baudraye, by then twenty-two, fell into despair.“What ails you?” asked Monsieur de Clagny, seeing her dejected.“Anna,” she said, “has learned to live, while I have been learning to endure.”A tragi-comedy was enacting in the house, in harmony with money quarrels and successive transformations—a drama the Abbe Duret and Monsieur de Clagny alone partly understood—when Dinah, in idleness or vanity, revealed the secret of her anonymous fame.Though mixing verse and prose is odd in French literature, to give a fair idea of her unconfessed struggle it is necessary to analyze a poem born of her despair. Her patience broken by Chargeboeuf’s departure, Dinah took the Abbe’s advice to exhale her thoughts in verse—“You will find relief as those who write epitaphs or elegies,” he said. The strange production stirred the Allier, the Nievre, and the Cher, proud of a poet to rival Paris. Paquita la Sevillane, by Jan Diaz, was published in the Echo du Morvan, a review that for eighteen months survived provincial indifference. Some at Nevers thought Jan Diaz mocked the new school of eccentric verse imitating foreign mannerisms.The poem began with a ballad praising Spain’s fragrant plains and Sevillian dancers and contrasting them with Northern squalor. Dinah then wrote a magnificent if affected description of Rouen—where she had never been—contrasting a manufacturing town’s life with Spain’s careless life, poetry versus sordid money-making. Jan Diaz made Paquita a Seville native sovereign of her town at thirteen, loved by three toreadors, whose soldier-lover carried her away to Normandy only to leave for Russia. The parting and vows were painted with Byron-like feeling. Much of the poem describes Paquita’s sufferings waiting alone in Rouen; she lives on narcotics and dreams, almost dies, but remains faithful; the soldier returns pale and frozen by Russia, and she, true, is still beautiful. The poem then takes a bitter turn: Paquita, failing to find suitable luxury in Rouen, falls into vice, ending in a house of ill-fame described with stark realism. Finally she is faded and old, but it was she who once sang of Spain.The poem, some six hundred lines, with seguidillas at beginning and end, had gloomy vigor that alarmed Dinah, who found herself admired in three departments under the cloak of anonymity. While she enjoyed success, she feared provincial malignity: if the secret leaked, other women would trace likenesses between writer and Paquita. Reflection came too late; she shuddered at having made “copy” of some of her woes. “Write no more,” said the Abbe. “You will cease to be a woman; you will be a poet.” Moulins, Nevers, Bourges were searched for Jan Diaz; Dinah remained impenetrable.To remove suspicion, she wrote a charming two-canto poem on the Mass-Oak, a Nivernais legend: foes from Nevers and Saint-Saulge, about to battle in the forest of Faye, are stayed by a priest who, by saying Mass under an oak, makes them friends. This poem, far superior to Paquita, was less admired.After these attempts Madame de la Baudraye, feeling herself a poet, had a light on her brow and a flash in her eyes that made her handsomer than ever. She cast longing looks at Paris, aspiring to fame—and fell back into her den at La Baudraye, daily squabbles with her husband, and a little circle whose characters had become a bore. Though literature gave relief—poetry echoed in her empty life and provided an outlet—it increased her hatred of the gray, ponderous provincial atmosphere.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When, after the Revolution of 1830, George Sand’s fame revived interest in Le Berry, towns envied La Châtre for producing this rival of Madame de Staël and Camille Maupin, and were ready to acclaim any minor feminine talent. Thus in France arose a host of would-be Muses—young girls or wives drawn from silent lives by the lure of glory. Strange doctrines were proclaimed about women’s role: French common sense was not wholly perverted, but women were allowed to express ideas and opinions they would not have owned a few years earlier.Monsieur de Clagny took advantage of this outbreak of freedom to collect the works of Jan Diaz in a small volume printed by Desroziers at Moulins. He wrote a short notice amusing to those in the secret, though not novel. Practical jokes are capital while the author is unknown; they fail when the poet’s identity is known.From this angle, the memoir of Jan Diaz, said to be born at Bourges in 1807, son of a Spanish prisoner, might deceive a future compiler of a Universal Biography. Nothing is overlooked: names of professors at Bourges College, deceased schoolfellows such as Lousteau and Bianchon, who supposedly knew the dreamy boy and his bent toward poetry. An elegy called Tristesse written at school; the two poems Paquita la Sevillane and Le Chene de la Messe; three sonnets, a description of the Cathedral and the House of Jacques Coeur at Bourges, and a tale called Carola, published as his work in hand at death—these remains, and the poet’s last hours of misery near Château-Chinon, wrung the hearts of the feeling public in the Nievre, the Bourbonnais, the Cher, and the Morvan, where he died unknown even to the woman he had loved.Two hundred copies of the little yellow volume were printed; one hundred and fifty sold—about fifty in each department. This average revives enthusiasm for provincial passion for letters, now more apt to expend itself in business than in books.After Monsieur de Clagny had given away some copies, Dinah still had seven or eight wrapped in newspapers that published notices. Twenty copies sent to Paris papers were swamped in editors’ piles. Nathan and several fellow-countrymen of Le Berry were taken in; Nathan wrote an article crediting the great man with all the fine qualities we ascribe to the dead.Lousteau, warned by schoolfellows who could not remember Jan Diaz, sought information from Sancerre and learned Jan Diaz was a pseudonym assumed by a woman.Then, in and around Sancerre, Madame de la Baudraye became the rage; she was hailed as the future rival of George Sand. From Sancerre to Bourges a poem was praised that at any other time would have been hooted. The provincial public does not love the middle course: it lifts you to the skies or drags you in the mud.By this time the good Abbé who had counselled Madame de la Baudraye was dead; he would certainly have prevented her rushing into public life. But three years’ work without recognition weighed on Dinah’s soul, and she accepted the clatter of fame as a substitute for disappointed ambitions. Poetry and dreams of celebrity, which had lulled her grief since her meeting with Anna Grossetête, no longer sufficed to exhaust her morbid heart. The Abbé Duret—who had spoken of the world when religion’s voice was impotent and had promised a happy future by assuring her God would compensate brave sufferings—could no longer stand between her and the opening to sin.The wise old priest had tried more than once to enlighten Dinah about her husband’s character, telling her the man could hate; but women are not ready to believe in such force in weak natures. Dinah, finding her husband incapable of love, denied him the power to hate.“Do not confound hatred and vengeance,” said the Abbé. “They are different. Hatred is a vice of narrow souls; they feed it with meanness and make it a pretext for sordid tyranny. So beware of offending Monsieur de la Baudraye; he would forgive an infidelity, because he could make capital of it, but he would be doubly implacable if you touched that spot so cruelly wounded by Monsieur Milaud of Nevers, and would make your life unendurable.”Now, when the whole countryside—Nevers and Sancerre, Le Morvan and Le Berry—prided itself on Madame de la Baudraye and lauded her as Jan Diaz, “little La Baudraye” felt his glory a mortal blow. He alone knew the secret source of Paquita la Sevillane. When this terrible work was spoken of, everybody said of Dinah, “Poor woman! Poor soul!” The women rejoiced to pity her who had so long oppressed them; never had Dinah seemed to stand higher in the neighborhood.The shriveled old man, more wrinkled, yellower, feebler than ever, gave no sign; but Dinah sometimes detected in his gaze an icy venom that belied his increased politeness. She understood at last that this was not a mere domestic squabble; when she forced an explanation with her “insect,” as Monsieur Gravier called him, she found cold, hard impassibility. She flew into a passion, reproached him for her life over the past eleven years, and made, intentionally, what women call a scene. “Little La Baudraye” sat in an armchair with his eyes shut and listened phlegmatically. As usual, the dwarf got the better of his wife. Dinah saw she had done wrong in writing; she vowed never to write another line, and she kept her vow.Then there was desolation in the Sancerrois.“Why did not Madame de la Baudraye compose any more verses?” was the universal cry.At this time Madame de la Baudraye had no enemies; everyone rushed to see her, not a week passed without fresh introductions. The wife of the presiding judge, a proud bourgeoise née Popinot-Chandier, desired her son Gatien to pay his respects to La Baudraye, flattering herself that he might win favor with this Superior Woman—the epithet replacing the absurd nickname of “The Sappho of Saint-Satur.” Nine years leading the opposition, she praised the Muse of Sancerre lavishly.After scrambling through so many brambles and setting off on many roads, after dreaming of love in splendor and smelling the darkest dramas, weary of her dreary existence, one day Dinah fell into the pit she had sworn to avoid. Seeing Monsieur de Clagny always sacrificing himself and at last refusing a high appointment in Paris where his family wanted him, she said to herself, “He loves me!” She vanquished her repulsion and seemed willing to reward so much constancy.This impulse formed a coalition in Sancerre to secure Monsieur de Clagny’s return at the next elections. Madame de la Baudraye dreamed of going to Paris in the wake of the new deputy.But despite solemn promises, the hundred and fifty votes hoped for this adorer of Dinah—who hoped to see him Keeper of the Seals—figured as an imposing minority of fifty votes. The jealousy of the President de Boirouge and Monsieur Gravier’s hatred, for he believed in the candidate’s supremacy in Dinah’s heart, were worked on by a young sous-préfet; for this deed the allies got the young man made a préfet elsewhere.“I shall never cease to regret,” he said as he left Sancerre, “that I did not succeed in pleasing Madame de la Baudraye; that would have made my triumph complete!”The household thus racked by domestic troubles was calm on the surface; two ill-assorted but resigned beings upheld the propriety society insists on, a yoke to Dinah. Why did she long to throw off the mask she had worn for twelve years? Whence this increasing weariness and hope of finding herself a widow?These illusions deceive many women. After attempting to master Monsieur de la Baudraye, she had hoped to become a mother. Between those miserable disputes and the melancholy conviction as to her fate, time passed. Then, when she looked for consolation, Monsieur de Chargeboeuf left her. Thus the temptation that commonly causes women to sin had hitherto been absent. For while some women make straight for unfaithfulness, many cling to hope and do not fall till long wandering in a labyrinth of secret woes.Such was Dinah. She had so little impulse to fail in duty that she did not care enough for Monsieur de Clagny to forgive him his defeat.The move to the Château d’Anzy, the rearrangement of her treasures and curiosities—now set off by Philibert de Lorme’s splendid setting—occupied her for months and gave her leisure to meditate one of those decisive steps that startle the public, ignorant of motives sometimes revealed by gossip.Madame de la Baudraye had been struck by Lousteau’s reputation as a ladies’ man and wished to know him. She read his books and was fired with enthusiasm, less for his talents than for his successes with women; to attract him to the country, she broached the notion that Sancerre must return one of its great men at the elections. She made Gatien Boirouge write to the great physician Bianchon, whom he claimed as cousin via the Popinots, and persuaded an old friend of the departed Madame Lousteau to stir the journalist’s ambitions by saying certain persons in Sancerre were determined to elect a deputy from among Paris’s distinguished men.Tired of commonplace neighbors, Madame de la Baudraye would at last meet really illustrious men and might give her fall the lustre of fame.Neither Lousteau nor Bianchon replied at first; Bianchon, who had won his professorial chair the year before, could not leave his lectures. In September, when the vintage was at its height, the two Parisians arrived in their native province and found it absorbed in the wine-crop of 1836; there could be no public demonstration in their favor. “We have fallen flat,” said Lousteau.In 1836 Lousteau, worn by sixteen years of struggle in the Capital and aged by pleasure, penury, and disappointments, looked forty-eight though he was thirty-seven. Bald and assuming a Byronic air to match his decay, he blamed the Press for consuming superior talents and lent a grace to his exhaustion. He affected contempt of life and spurious misanthropy, though his eyes could still flash with fire, and he dressed fashionably to make up for lost youth.Horace Bianchon, wearing the ribbon of the Legion of Honor, was fat and burly, with a patriarchal air, thick hair, a prominent brow, the frame of a hard worker, and the calm expression of a philosopher. This prosaic personality set off his more frivolous companion.The two great men remained unrecognized at the inn the whole morning, and only by chance Monsieur de Clagny heard of their arrival. Madame de la Baudraye, in despair, despatched Gatien Boirouge—who had no vineyards—to beg them to spend a few days at the Château d’Anzy. For the last year Dinah had played the chatelaine and spent the winter only at La Baudraye. Monsieur Gravier, the public prosecutor, the presiding judge, and Gatien combined to give a banquet for the great men to meet the town’s literary personages.On hearing that the beautiful Madame de la Baudraye was Jan Diaz, the Parisians went to Anzy, fetched in a wagonette driven by Gatien. The young man, genuinely believing in his heroine, spoke of her as the handsomest woman in those parts, a Superior Woman who might produce a sensation in Paris. Hence the suppressed astonishment of Bianchon and the waggish journalist when they beheld, on the garden steps of Anzy, a lady in thin black cashmere with a deep tucker, a short riding-habit effect, and a black velvet cap; below it her hair fell in thick curls. The attire showed a pretty figure, fine eyes, and lids somewhat faded by her weariful life. In Le Berry the singularity of this artistic costume served as cloak for the romantic affectations of the Superior Woman.On seeing these affectations—indeed, affectations of soul and mind—the friends put on a serious face to listen to Madame de la Baudraye, who made them a set speech of thanks for coming to cheer her monotony. Dinah walked her guests round the lawn, ornamented with large vases of flowers in front of the château.“How is it,” said Lousteau, “that so handsome a woman as you, and apparently so superior, should have remained buried in the country? What do you do to make life endurable?”“That is the crux,” said the lady. “It is unendurable. Utter despair or dull resignation—there is no third alternative; that is the arid soil in which our existence is rooted, and on which a thousand stagnant ideas fall; they cannot fertilize the ground but supply food for the etiolated flowers of our desert souls. Never believe in indifference! Indifference is either despair or resignation. Then each woman takes up the pursuit which, according to her character, seems to promise some amusement. Some rush into jam-making and washing, household management, the vintage or harvest, bottling fruit, embroidering handkerchiefs, the cares of motherhood, the intrigues of a country town. Others torment a much-enduring piano until it sounds like an old kettle. Some pious dames argue the Word of God’s brands; they play cards in the evening, dance with the same partners for twelve years, make solemn walks on the Mall, visits of politeness, and ask each other where they bought their gowns.”“I admire such courage, madame,” said Bianchon. “When we endure such misfortunes, it is well to have the wit to make a virtue of necessity.”Amazed at Dinah’s laying provincial life bare in anticipation of sarcasm, Gatien nudged Lousteau: “Well! did I say too much?”“But, madame,” said Lousteau, “you prove we are still in Paris. I shall steal this gem of description; it will be worth ten thousand francs to me in an article.”“Oh, monsieur,” she retorted, “never trust provincial women.”“And why not?” said Lousteau.Madame de la Baudraye was wily enough—an innocent form of cunning—to show the two Parisians, one of whom she hoped to conquer, the snare into which he would fall, reflecting she would have the upper hand when he ceased to see it.“When you first come,” she said, “you laugh at us. Then, forgetting Paris’s brilliancy, you pay court to us, if only as a pastime. You who are famous for past passions will be flattered. Then take care!” cried Dinah, with a coquettish gesture. “When a poor little country-bred woman has an eccentric passion for some superior man who has wandered into the provinces, it is to her something more than a sentiment; she makes it her occupation and part of her life. There is nothing more dangerous than such attachment; she compares, studies, reflects, dreams, and will not give up her dream; she thinks of the man when he has ceased to think of her.“One catastrophe that weighs on a woman in the provinces is that abrupt termination of a passion often seen in England. A life under minute observation compels a woman either to keep on the rails or to start aside like a steam engine wrecked by an obstacle. Parisian strategies of love, the coquetting forming half the composition of a Parisian woman, are unknown here.”“That is true,” said Lousteau. “There is in a country-bred woman’s heart a store of surprises, as in some toys.”“A woman will have spoken to you three times in a winter and without your knowing it you will be lodged in her heart. Then comes a picnic or an excursion and all is said—or done! A poet or philosopher, instead of vilifying the provincial woman and believing her depraved, would guess the unrevealed poetry and the sweet romance whose last phrase falls to some happy sub-lieutenant or provincial bigwig.”“The provincial women I have met in Paris,” said Lousteau, “were rapid in their proceedings—”“My word, they are strange,” said the lady, with a shrug.“They are like playgoers who book the second performance, sure the piece will not fail,” replied the journalist.“And what is the cause of all these woes?” asked Bianchon.“Paris is the monster that brings us grief,” replied the Superior Woman. “The evil radiates seven leagues round and devastates the land. Provincial life is not self-existent. Only when a nation is divided into many minor states can each have a physiognomy, and a woman reflect the glory of her sphere. In France, as in any country with one capital, centralization produces a dead level of manners.”“Then you would say manners could recover individuality by a federation of French states into an empire?” said Lousteau.“That is hardly to be wished; France would have to conquer too many countries,” said Bianchon.“This misfortune is unknown in England,” exclaimed Dinah. “London does not exert such tyranny; it has a worse disease—vile hypocrisy—which is a far greater evil!”“The English aristocracy,” said Lousteau, “assimilates every form of superiority; it lives in magnificent parks and is in London for no more than the season. It lives in the country, flourishing there.”“Yes,” said Madame de la Baudraye, “London is the capital of trade and government. The aristocracy hold a ‘mote’ there for sixty days only, give and take the passwords of the day, look in on legislative cookery, review the girls to marry and the carriages to be sold, exchange greetings, and are away again.”“Hence,” said Lousteau, in epigram, “in Perfidious Albion you may meet a charming woman anywhere.”“But charming English women!” replied Madame de la Baudraye with a smile. “Here is my mother,” she added, seeing Madame Piedefer coming.Having introduced the two Paris lions to the ambitious skeleton that called itself woman under Madame Piedefer’s name—a tall, lean person with a red face—Dinah left her visitors for a few minutes.“Well,” said Gatien to Lousteau, “what do you think of her?”“I think the clever woman of Sancerre is simply the greatest chatterbox,” replied the journalist.“A woman who wants to see you deputy!” cried Gatien. “An angel!”“Forgive me, I forgot you were in love with her,” said Lousteau. “Forgive the cynicism of an old scamp. Ask Bianchon; I have no illusions left. I see things as they are. The woman has evidently dried up her mother like a partridge roasted at too fierce a fire.”Gatien de Boirouge contrived to let Madame de la Baudraye know what the journalist had said of her during the dinner, which was copious and splendid, and the lady took care not to talk too much while it proceeded. This lack of conversation betrayed Gatien’s indiscretion. Etienne tried to regain his footing, but Dinah’s advances were directed to Bianchon.However, half-way through the evening, the Baroness was gracious to Lousteau again. Have you never observed what great meanness may be committed for small ends? Thus the haughty Dinah, who would not sacrifice herself for a fool, who in the country led a wretched life of struggles, suppressed rebellion, and unuttered poetry, suddenly stepped off her peak when she recollected her album.Madame de la Baudraye had caught the mania for autographs; she possessed an oblong volume truly an album, two-thirds blank. The Baronne de Fontaine, who had kept it three months, had with difficulty obtained a line from Rossini, six bars by Meyerbeer, the four lines Victor Hugo writes in every album, a verse from Lamartine, a few words from Béranger, the first words of Télémaque by George Sand, Scribe’s famous lines on the umbrella, some notes from Berlioz. Monsieur de Clagny had added a song by Lacenaire, two lines from Fieschi, and a short note from Napoleon. Then Monsieur Gravier had persuaded Mademoiselle Mars and other distinguished actors and singers to write their names. This beginning was precious to Dinah because she was the only person for ten leagues round who owned an album.Within the last two years, several young ladies had acquired such books filled with more or less absurd quotations. You who spend your lives collecting autographs, simple souls like Dutch tulip-fanciers, will excuse Dinah when, fearing to keep her guests only two days, she begged Bianchon to enrich the volume she handed him.The doctor made Lousteau smile by showing him this sentence on the first page:“What makes the populace dangerous is that it has in its pocket an absolution for every crime.“J. B. DE CLAGNY.”“We will second the man brave enough to plead for the Monarchy,” Desplein’s great pupil whispered to Lousteau, and he wrote below:“The distinction between Napoleon and a water-carrier is evident only to Society; Nature takes no account of it. Thus Democracy, resisting inequality, constantly appeals to Nature.“H. BIANCHON.”“Ah!” cried Dinah, amazed, “you rich men take a gold piece from your purse as poor men bring out a farthing.... I do not know,” she went on, turning to Lousteau, “whether it is taking an unfair advantage of a guest to hope for a few lines—”“Nay, madame, you flatter me. Bianchon is a great man, but I am too insignificant! Twenty years hence my name will be more difficult to identify than that of the Public Prosecutor whose axiom, written in your album, will designate him as an obscurer Montesquieu. And I should want twenty-four hours to improvise some sufficiently bitter reflections, for I could only describe what I feel.”“I wish you needed a fortnight,” said Madame de la Baudraye graciously, as she handed him the book.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################At five the next morning everyone at the Château d’Anzy was astir. Little La Baudraye had arranged a day’s shooting for his Parisians—less for their pleasure than to show the twelve hundred acres he intended to reclaim, a project costing about a hundred thousand francs and promising an annual increase of thirty to sixty thousand francs to the estate.“Do you know why the Public Prosecutor has not come out with us?” Gatien Boirouge asked Monsieur Gravier.“He told us he must sit to‑day; the minor cases are before the Court,” Gravier answered.“And did you believe that?” cried Gatien. “My papa said to me, ‘Monsieur Lebas will not join you early, for Monsieur de Clagny has begged him as his deputy!’”Gravier’s face changed. “Indeed! And Monsieur de la Baudraye is gone to La Charité!”“Why meddle?” Bianchon reproved Gatien.“Horace is right,” Lousteau added. “Why trouble about each other? You squander time in frivolities.”At a copse Gravier went off with the two great men and Gatien under a keeper’s guidance through a ravine. When they reached a clearing Bianchon suggested waiting for Gravier.“You may be a great physician,” said Gatien, “but ignorant of provincial life. Gravier will be back at the château within twenty minutes.” He checked his watch. “I couldn’t rest if Madame de la Baudraye were alone with Monsieur de Clagny. There are two of them now; Dinah will be guarded.”“Ah ha! Then Dinah hasn’t decided?” Lousteau teased.“So mamma thinks. I fear Monsieur de Clagny may have enchanted her. If she sees him as Attorney‑General he may appear handsome; eloquence has privileges. Besides, Madame de la Baudraye is ambitious; she dislikes Sancerre and dreams of Paris.”“You yourself hope to succeed the Public Prosecutor?” Lousteau smiled.“Paris offers many women,” Gatien pleaded. “But at Sancerre, where women set up for sanctity, the handsomest keeps others at a distance. A young man of twenty‑two may guess at her secrets if she treats him with some consideration.”Bianchon dismissed Gatien’s alarm. Lousteau proposed a plot: after dinner they would tell lurid stories of murderous wives to watch the faces of Madame de la Baudraye and de Clagny.That evening Lousteau returned an album to Dinah with an elegy—“Spleen”—that painted himself as a blighted poet. Dinah looked pitying; Clagny, suspicious, scowled at the sham melancholy. He sat down to play backgammon with the cure; Gravier placed a lamp to cast light on Dinah, who embroidered a wicker‑paper basket. The three conspirators sat near.“For whom the basket?” asked Lousteau.“For Monsieur de la Baudraye,” she replied.Lousteau mocked Parisians’ disbelief in women’s virtue; de Clagny complained of their literature’s focus on adultery. Etienne retorted with an extravagant defense: if you exclude all books mentioning illicit love you must burn vast swathes of literature—Homer, Milton, Dante, Rousseau.“When you take the political attitude,” he went on, “a judge of Tiberius’s day might have called Joseph’s wife an adulteress; Pilate would not recognize miracles. Judges always represent established interests—Pilate, Anytus, modern prosecutors. In their view Socrates and Jesus were criminals. So to call religions or revolutions impostures is to think like a public prosecutor.”“Your argument is blasphemy,” said de Clagny.“I grant it,” Etienne answered, “but not maliciously. If there were many men like me, government would be impossible.”Madame Piedefer made arch faces. Bianchon warned Lousteau not to upset the company with talk of religion. Gravier, if a romancer, said, would favor wronged husbands; he had seen dramatic, even heroic avengers. Bianchon and Lousteau agreed that deceived husbands could be admirable, and Lousteau promised a chilling story of revenge—one told by Charles Nodier about a Chevalier de Beauvoir.He recounted the tale: after the 18th Brumaire, a Maille envoy, Chevalier de Beauvoir, sent by Chouans, was arrested on landing. Though his papers were in order and he bore himself well, sticking to minute orders meant he was detained and sent to the Castle of l’Escarpe, a fortress on precipitous rocks.The commandant, a Corsican, treated him well; his pretty wife took a fancy to Beauvoir. Whether imprudence occurred the Chevalier never explained, but the jealous commandant imprisoned him in a dungeon, fettered and fed on black bread. In despair Beauvoir planned escape when a turnkey, secretly paid, offered him a file and a knotted rope and explained how to cut a bar and lower himself by knots. Beauvoir filed the bars, disguised the breach, hid his rope, and one murky night let himself down. At the last knot he found no footing: the distance to the rocks was a hundred feet. Suspecting a trap, he climbed back, feigned an attempted escape by leaving his rope, and lay in wait. When the jailer returned and opened the door, Beauvoir struck him dead with the iron bar he’d filed out, stripped him, donned his clothes, mimicked his gait, and walked out with the unsuspecting warders’ confidence.The tale did not seem to affect de Clagny or Dinah; the conspirators watched them for signs of guilt but found coolness. Bianchon then told “La Grande Bretèche”—the Spanish grandee walled in a cupboard—ending to dramatic effect. Madame de la Baudraye marvelled at a woman’s courage to risk such things for love; Gravier offered a Spanish anecdote of a party in Madrid when the surgeon‑major told of being kidnapped to deliver a woman’s child in secret.He described being forced, blindfolded, into a house where the husband snored nearby, the woman in labor veiled and biting a handkerchief, the lover and waiting‑woman attending. Though the infant proved dead, the surgeon cut and saved the mother. The Spaniard lover, distraught, carried off the body, while the surgeon later, haunted by fear, saw the Spaniard’s burning eyes in the garden; the surgeon was later murdered—stabbed by the lady’s husband—after being shown the amputated arm of someone bearing a telltale mole.Gravier added that during the restoration of Ferdinand VII, he met a Spanish grandee with a motionless right arm and a lovely wife whose past horrors might explain its loss. The company agreed Spain bred strange passions.De Clagny then related a French crime: a Tours woman, whose husband disappeared during the Loire army’s disbandment and who had been devout, confessed to bleeding him to death, salting the body, packing it into casks, and throwing pieces into the Loire; the head remained pickled, discovered by the examining judge. She was condemned and executed, her earlier repentance having softened public feeling. Bianchon and the others reflected on the hidden tragedies behind private life and the difficulty of judging crimes between spouses.At bedtime the bachelors gathered in a passage, candle in hand, to discuss their plan. Gravier revealed the scheme: they would seal the doors of Madame de la Baudraye and the Public Prosecutor to catch any nocturnal tryst. Robbing barefoot like thieves, Bianchon, Lousteau, Gravier, and Gatien stuck hairs across the two doors with wax—a trick common in country houses—and agreed to inspect them at five. To their amazement the hairs were still intact on both doors in the morning.“This alters the matter!” cried Lousteau. If Dinah were virtuous, she was worth the effort. He resolved to press his suit.In the garden Lousteau found Madame de la Baudraye and engaged her. She invited him to La Baudraye to learn more of provincial life and declared provincials simple but true.
