Etienne told her he had only teased Gatien into confessing his adoration; her silence at dinner had betrayed an indiscretion, he said, but he assured her her innocence was proven. “If you fancied the magistrate,” he added, “you’d have lost my respect. I love perfection; I cannot believe the Public Prosecutor matches you.”Madame de la Baudraye’s manner warmed. Lousteau spoke frankly of his life: he had sought pleasure but never loved; perhaps he had defects that made him less lovable. Dinah heard Paris through him—its daring axioms and decadence—and was intrigued. Etienne, acting the Parisian wit, charmed her with tales of the capital; she spent one of her happiest days with him and Bianchon.Lousteau flattered her by disparaging a great female celebrity of Le Berry, implying Dinah could rival her; this intoxicated Dinah. Clagny, Gravier, and Gatien thought she was warmer to Etienne than before. Their exaggerated praise spread through Sancerre; sixteen people came to Anzy that evening, some in family coaches, others in wagonettes and on hired horses. Dinah, expecting triumph, lit up the huge drawing‑room and unveiled the furniture; the Parisians studied the arrivals’ attitudes and speeches.Visitors displayed ancestral laces and faded honors; the Présidente Boirouge complained of chest pains to Bianchon, who prescribed “tea after dinner” as an English remedy. Conversation turned to Bianchon’s possible candidacy for elections; Lousteau, recalling the doctor’s fame, proposed him as a candidate, but Bianchon demurred—science and law differ. Madame Chandier and the Mayor’s wife compared incomes and boots; Gatien boasted Lousteau made twenty thousand francs a year.The footman brought letters and proofs; Lousteau left a packet for Bianchon. Dinah picked up a printers’ wrapper, surprised to find literature pursuing them. Bianchon noticed a used proof folded round the journal proofs: a serial romance, “Olympia, or Roman Revenge.” He handed it to Lousteau, who read a page aloud. From the fragment—brigands in a cavern, a vanished Rinaldo, a Duchess who forgets her glove—the Parisians sat amused while Lousteau mocked the author’s lack of “local color,” and Bianchon defended censored writers. The provincial ladies and men stared at Lousteau, half scandalized, half entertained.Thus the evening passed—stories, parodies, and flirtations weaving the lives of Parisians and provincials at the Château d’Anzy.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The Public Prosecutor’s wife, who, in Monsieur Gravier’s phrase, might have put a Cossack to flight in 1814, sat up like a rider in his stirrups and made a face at her neighbor as if to say, “They are looking at us; we must smile as if we understood.”“Charming!” said the Mayoress to Gatien. “Pray go on, Monsieur Lousteau.”Lousteau glanced at the two women, forced a smile, called “Attention!” and resumed: a rustle of dress, Cardinal Borborigano confronting the Duchess—gloomy, bitter—saying she was under suspicion and should flee whether guilty or not. The Duchess thanked him, saying the Duke of Bracciano would reappear when she wanted to prove he was alive.“Cardinal Borborigano!” cried Bianchon, enchanted by the name. Dinah, pitying the eighteen faces watching Lousteau, said she could see the whole plot: Rome, a murdered husband, a wife bold and wicked sleeping over a volcano of crime, always fearing discovery. Lousteau elaborated: a French Adolphe embraced by the Duchess, Rinaldo lurking like a melodramatic conspirator; Victor Hugo and Pixerecourt were invoked.The company gaped. Madame de la Baudraye and others urged Lousteau to continue. He read fragments and stage directions, stopping to explain the printers’ proofs: a waste sheet printed on one side only, a made-up sheet following it, numbers indicating volume and sheet, tailed with a block engraving. From these clues Lousteau inferred the romance’s form—four duodecimo volumes, second edition—and that the narrative was advancing toward a conclusion.He translated a jumble of quoted lines and directions: the Duchess’s key, Rinaldo’s substitution of a similar key, the terror of a lost key leading to death, and a tailpiece by Normand cut by Duplat. Teasing the audience with a missing page, he jumped to a later complete sheet labeled IV.; J, which moved the action ahead: Rinaldo steals into the Baths of Vespasian, finds a concealed door, then realizes he is imprisoned. The made-up sheet skipped pages—212 to 217—showing how the story progressed.He read on: Rinaldo fled to what he thought was the cellar where the Bracciano family’s treasures lay; he found a door and vanished, then sensed a terrible fact—he was imprisoned. The narrator described Rinaldo’s panic and his hollow roar “like that of a hyaena,” provoking Lousteau to comment on such blunt expressions in Empire literature—sketchy rather than ornate, terse where modern work elaborates character. Bianchon defended modern criticism: readers now demand invention, style, thought, learning, and feeling; critics, though often envious, chase defects because they cannot create.Ignoring protests to remain on point, Lousteau continued. A deep groan answered Rinaldo’s cry; a voice called “Santa Maria!” A dialogue ensued. Rinaldo mocked, then heard a voice claim to be the Duke of Bracciano. The Duke, speaking from darkness, guided Rinaldo through the cell until Rinaldo felt iron bars and realized the voice was in a cage. The Duke had been confined thirty months, unable to sit, raised nightly by a counterpoise under the Duchess’s bedroom so she could taunt him when she embraced her lover. Tricked in a duel of wits with his wife, he had been trapped, left to live on a piece of bread; she showed him the poison he had prepared, withheld it, and displayed her lover in his arms.The Duke begged for alliance; Rinaldo, the brigand, revealed he had files and pincers and had filed through one bar. The Duke pleaded for a dagger, and Rinaldo lent it; the spring clattered, the Duke shot up, and Rinaldo, astonished at the Duke’s haste to leave, muttered that he had sworn never to injure a woman. Lousteau broke in with printerly observations: another tailpiece, a Cupid on a snail, a blank page 230, then “Conclusion.”The fragment’s conclusion was brief and passionate: the Duchess, more lovely than ever, freshly bathed and goddess-like, finds Adolphe voluptuously reclining; they exchange professions of love, and she presses the springs that open the concealed passage. Lousteau lamented that the foreman had torn off the rest of the proof in wrapping, but declared the author full of promise.Reactions varied. Gatien Boirouge and Monsieur Gravier confessed confusion; Lousteau insisted the piece was typical of Empire-era romance. Monsieur Gravier suggested the brigand’s graceful talk betrayed an author ignorant of Italy; Madame Gorju disparaged the fiction and praised Bianchon’s prescriptions. Bianchon, looking at Mademoiselle Euphemie Gorju, defended leniency: they had only twenty pages of a thousand.Lousteau and Bianchon speculated about the author: perhaps not a Councillor of State but a woman—Madame Hadot—citing women’s greater extravagance of invention: Frankenstein, Leone Leoni, Ann Radcliffe. Dinah glared at Monsieur de Clagny when he applied such examples, feeling the slight in his choice.Little La Baudraye, commenting on revenge, noted that society’s penalties often suffice: a cuckolded woman might be reduced to penury and ostracism rather than killed. Practical as ever, he said a woman’s loss of finery and respect was the sum of misery; Madame de la Baudraye added that she might still have happiness because she had a lover. Monsieur de la Baudraye, blowing out his candle, returned that the woman had a lover, and Bianchon and Lousteau exchanged wry asides.Dinah’s small laugh and knowledge of Louise’s remarks betrayed to Bianchon the inner life of Madame de la Baudraye; he saw, from her premature wrinkles and knowing smile, something of her past. But Dinah did not catch the full innuendo of her husband’s quip; little La Baudraye had seen a swift flash of tenderness in her eyes when she looked at the journalist. Despite his virtues, Bianchon observed, a man like Lousteau—poet, journalist, libertine—had that tinsel of intelligence and freedom that attracted women who wanted to feel chosen, desired, and remembered.Lousteau noticed Dinah’s superiority to the provincial visitors—her dress, grace, and complexion shining by candlelight against a background of shy, ill-dressed faces. As the party dwindled and the Sancerre visitors prepared for their hour’s drive, only Monsieur de Clagny, Monsieur Lebas, Gatien, and Monsieur Gravier remained to sleep at Anzy. Lousteau, his view of Dinah changing since their meeting, accepted the provincial setting but found himself drawn to her personality. Madame de la Baudraye’s audacious prophecy—that Lousteau would be the happy man of Dinah’s choice—seemed in play.The rest of the evening passed pleasantly in small-party epigrams at the expense of the Sancerre visitors. Bianchon, going to bed with Lousteau in a large room, told him frankly: “You will be the happy man of this woman’s choice.” Lousteau demurred, yet the doctor observed how appealing a man reputed to have had many mistresses can be; a woman likes to feel she will be longer remembered than others, to appeal to experience, to have her lover’s reputation confirm his value. Bianchon predicted Dinah's beauty would be enhanced by love, and, pragmatically, that a child would secure her fortune. Lousteau declared making love to her might be an act of virtue and, with Bianchon’s help, hinted at plans for the next day.Next morning, Madame de la Baudraye decided to take Bianchon as far as Cosne, dropping her mother at La Baudraye, continuing to Cosne with the Parisians, and returning alone with Etienne. She was elegantly dressed; Lousteau had put on his “war-paint” clothes, the studied Parisian look. Monsieur de Clagny and Monsieur Gravier felt out of place as they watched the Parisians climb into the carriage. Monsieur de la Baudraye waved from above, his little hand, and the men joked about escorting them.Gatien returned from the stables and joined the chaise, a prudent escort. Dinah sighed that in thirteen years of marriage she had never had three hours’ liberty. Lousteau quipped an anecdote about Michaud setting out for the Holy Land, retorting to those who cited his marriage that he was “so little married.” Even the rigid Madame Piedefer allowed a smile.Dinah teased that Monsieur de Clagny might mount her pony to complete the escort. Bianchon, to free her from Gatien, suggested a simple plan: ask Gatien to go back to Anzy for some lecture notes Bianchon had left on his table. That little plot delighted Madame de la Baudraye; from the road between Anzy and Sancerre the Loire could be seen like a noble lake, and the drive was agreeable. Dinah, content at being understood, found herself discussing love in theory with the journalist. Lousteau spoke with a tone of refined corruption: love obeys no law; it is varied by character and circumstance; any woman, however firm, might be carried away in a few hours by an internal whirlwind known only to God. He declared this the key to all the adventures they had discussed over the last three days.Throughout the reading and the subsequent talk, Lousteau’s literary commentaries punctuated the narrative. He mocked the slovenly printers of the Quay and their lack of correctors, concluding they did not care for the refined art of making up a book. He contrasted the terse, outline-based style of Empire romances—where Lubin loves Toinette, Toinette refuses, Lubin kills and is guillotined—with modern writers who reveal inner life. Bianchon agreed, noting criticism’s evolution: readers demand the five literary senses and punish works that lack them; critics, lacking creation, denounce the incomplete. This controversy, Lousteau teased, was the history of contemporary literary quarrels: masters like Walter Scott combined the senses, leaving lesser writers to lament.As the Sancerre guests took offense at what they deemed a Parisian joke—feeling mocked for not following the proofs—Madame Piedefer urged her daughter to pacify the ladies. Provincial folks are quick to anger when they suspect they have been made to look ridiculous. Dinah’s distinct appeal set her apart in the drawing-room, making Lousteau’s interest natural. He accepted the beauty of the château and the antique splendor, but it was Dinah’s personality that completed the picture. Thus, by the time the visitors left, his mind had shifted from amusement toward attraction.Bianchon’s assessment of Madame de la Baudraye’s case was succinct: he had seen in a few signs what might otherwise take years to discover. Dinah’s glance at Lousteau—little, luminous, signifying a readiness to be moved—had told him enough. In private he told Lousteau as much, who, though amused, seemed to plan on acting with Bianchon’s assistance.Their evening ended with gossip, literary theory, and the implicit movement of attraction. The reading had unveiled a tragic, melodramatic tale of entrapment and revenge—the Duke in his marble cage, nightly mocked by his wife, the brigand Rinaldo’s reluctant help—while the company displayed small-town temper, critical pretension, and the social mechanics of flirtation. As they prepared to travel the next day, the conversation about love’s unpredictability and power closed the circle: Lousteau’s claim that love is the key to all such adventures left the women and men reflecting on the thin line between plotting in fiction and plotting in life.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For three days Dinah’s imagination brimmed with perilous possibilities fed by the Parisians’ talk; Lousteau watched, ready to seize any moment her hidden readiness overcame irresolution.Dinah planned to show La Baudraye to her two visitors. Gatien rode off on his master’s errands; Madame Piedefer went shopping; Dinah went to Cosne alone with Lousteau and Bianchon. Lousteau sat beside her while Bianchon rode behind, both men pitying a noble nature trapped in vulgar surroundings. Bianchon mocked the Public Prosecutor and Gatien, strengthening Lousteau’s case with scorn for the provincials.Crossing the Loire, Bianchon told Dinah she had been drawn to Etienne; he loved her, but was frivolous, poor, and tied to Paris while she was rooted in Sancerre. He counseled prudence: make Lousteau a friend, accept limited visits, and secure beauty, happiness, and fortune. He warned that without love she would grow old and hideous—a blunt, paternal argument that unhinged her defences. Though she protested she would not be a mere passerby’s mistress, Etienne’s arrival interrupted her dissembling; he seemed to study Cosne as he drew near.Bianchon gathered his luggage to leave, offering Dinah a final almost paternal blessing and allowing Lousteau to take her hand. The touch thrilled Dinah; Lousteau felt triumphant. He read small gestures—managing her frock, fretting over muslin—as signs of choice. Bianchon quietly departed, advising Dinah to go home, and Lousteau, audacious, shook his hand with indifference.Returning to La Baudraye, Lousteau abruptly declared his love. Dinah trembled and protested she would not entrust the best of herself to a man who would see her only as transient pleasure. Gatien’s arrival forced the moment: Lousteau insisted Gatien go back to the inn, then deliberately messed Dinah’s muslin so she would not be presentable—an outrageous “trick” he called a way to spare her reputation. Baffled and hurt, Gatien was sent away; humiliated, Dinah fled in tears. Lousteau followed, threw himself at her feet, and vowed devotion.Madame Piedefer arrived; Lousteau suggested the mother find another dress. Hearing Gatien’s return, Dinah and her mother withdrew to change into something warmer, as Bianchon had advised. Gatien, bewildered by conflicting explanations—that the muslin was torn on a nail or trampled by Lousteau—felt mocked. Dinah reappeared in velvet, forced to hide coldness behind Lousteau’s presence, and was escorted to the carriage with him. Lousteau professed to renounce Paris and linger in Sancerre for her—rhetoric that, while insincere to Gatien, struck Dinah and made her laugh—a contrite surrender.That evening gentlemen whispered: she had gone out in muslin and returned in velvet. The muslin story became scandal; the town joked, gossiped, and condemned. The Presidente Boirouge thundered that Dinah’s poetry proved she must live what she wrote; others muttered that virtue is fine until happiness arrives, then Calumny follows. Monsieur de Clagny defended her, claiming she and Lousteau worked on some great project, but was mocked.October’s beauty aided Dinah: as Bianchon predicted, genuine passion bloomed. Within a month she changed—alive, discovering dormant qualities, feeling Lousteau as an angel and delighting in being loved for herself. The vast château and grounds favored the romance. Lousteau, who prized her artlessness and originality, played the consummate seducer, supplying every practiced flourish of passion. Even seeing the affair as temporary, he strove to imprint himself on her memory with eloquent, elaborate tenderness. Their long walks and evenings deepened mutual feeling; when two lovers exhaust every step of their duet yet remain enamored, their love becomes real.Lousteau’s time was limited—he planned to leave Anzy in early November. The day before his departure the master showed them the château’s front bearing the arms of Uxelles; he wanted to replace them with his own new coat of arms, a sign of his newly established entail and pride. Lousteau teased, and the old man’s remark that Dinah was still young—there was time—made Lousteau smile without fully grasping its meaning.They postponed farewells; Dinah accompanied Lousteau visibly as far as Cosne and in the meantime extracted many solemn promises and pledges. Back at La Baudraye she later told Monsieur de Clagny, Gatien, and Gravier—each in turn—that Lousteau had taught her she had not been loved for herself, and she delivered a noble speech on man and base passions. She accepted Clagny’s steadfast devotion as confidant and lavished on him the marks of friendship a woman can give to a suitor willing to be an adoring, submissive companion.Thus Dinah’s brief, passionate conversion—engineered and complicated by Lousteau’s artful audacity and Bianchon’s counsel—transformed her social standing and inner life, provoking scandal in Sancerre even as it led her to embrace a new identity shaped by affection and theatrical devotion.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In Paris again, Lousteau soon lost the glow of his happy time at the Chateau d’Anzy. The reason was simple: he lived by his pen.In this century, particularly since the bourgeois triumph—the thrifty citizen who avoids imitating Francis I or Louis XIV—to live by the pen is a kind of penal servitude. To live by the pen means to create daily—or to seem to create; imitation costs as dearly as reality. Besides his weekly newspaper piece, which came every Monday like Sisyphus’s stone on his pen, Etienne wrote for three or four literary magazines. Do not be alarmed; he put no artistic conscientiousness into his work. This man of Sancerre had a facility—carelessness, if you will—that made him a mere scrivener, a literary hack. In Paris, hack-work cuts a man off from pretensions to a literary position; when he can do no more or no longer cares for advancement, the man who can write becomes a journalist and a hack.The life he led was not without pleasure. Blue-stockings, beginners in every walk of life, actresses, publishers and authors all made much of his ready pen. Lousteau, a thorough man about town, lived at little expense beyond paying his rent. He had boxes at all the theatres; the sale of the books he reviewed or left unreviewed paid for his gloves; and he would say to authors who published at their own expense, “I have your book always in my hands!” He took toll from vanity in drawings or pictures. Every day had engagements to dinner, every night its theatre, every morning callers, visits, and lounging. His newspaper serial, two novels a year for weeklies, and miscellaneous articles were the tax for this easy life. Yet to reach this position Etienne had struggled for ten years.Known in the literary world, liked for mischief and easy good humor, he let himself float with the stream, never planning for the future. He ruled a small circle of newcomers and kept fifteen-year habits with those he supped and dined with, indulging his wit alongside them. He earned seven to eight hundred francs a month, a sum he found insufficient for the prodigality peculiar to the impecunious. Indeed, Lousteau was now as hard up as when, on first appearing in Paris, he had told himself, “If I had but five hundred francs a month, I should be rich!”The cause was this: Lousteau lived in the Rue des Martyrs in handsome ground-floor rooms with a garden, splendidly furnished. When he settled there in 1833 he had struck a deal with an upholsterer that kept his pocket money low for a long time. These rooms were let for twelve hundred francs. January, April, July, and October were, as he phrased it, his indigent months; the rent and the porter’s account cleaned him out. Lousteau took no fewer hackney cabs, spent a hundred francs on breakfasts, smoked thirty francs’ worth of cigars, and could never refuse the mistress of a day a dinner or a new dress. He dipped so deeply into the fluctuating earnings of following months that he could no more find a hundred francs on his chimney-piece now, making seven or eight hundred francs a month, than he could in 1822, when he was hardly getting two hundred.Sometimes tired by the incessant vicissitudes of a literary life, and as much bored by amusement as a courtesan, Lousteau would get out of the tideway and sit on the bank, and say to one or another of his intimates—Nathan or Bixiou—who sat smoking in his small garden, looking out on an evergreen lawn as big as a dinner-table:“What will be the end of us? White hairs are giving us respectful hints!”“Lord! we shall marry when we choose to give as much thought to the matter as we give to a drama or a novel,” said Nathan.“And Florine?” retorted Bixiou.“Oh, we all have a Florine,” said Etienne, flinging away his cigar and thinking of Madame Schontz.Madame Schontz was pretty enough to put a high price on the interest of her beauty while reserving an absolute ownership for Lousteau, the man of her heart. Like all the women called Lorettes, from the Church of Notre-Dame de Lorette around which they dwell, she lived in the Rue Flechier, a stone’s throw from Lousteau. This lady took pride in teasing friends by boasting of having a Wit for her lover.These details of Lousteau’s life and fortune are indispensable, for this penury and bohemian existence—where Parisian luxury had become a necessity—was fated to have a cruel influence on Dinah’s life. Those familiar with Parisian bohemia will understand how it was that, by the end of a fortnight, the journalist, deep in his literary environment, could laugh about his Baroness with his friends and even with Madame Schontz. To those who regard such things as mean, it is almost useless to make excuses they will not accept.“What did you do at Sancerre?” asked Bixiou the first time he met Lousteau.“I did good service to three worthy provincials—a Receiver-General of Taxes, a little cousin of his, and a Public Prosecutor, who for ten years had been dancing round one of the hundred ‘Tenth Muses’ who adorn the Departments,” said he. “But they had no more dared to touch her than we touch a decorated cream at dessert till some strong-minded person has made a hole in it.”“Poor boy!” said Bixiou. “I said you had gone to Sancerre to turn Pegasus out to grass.”“Your joke is as stupid as my Muse is handsome,” retorted Lousteau. “Ask Bianchon, my dear fellow.”“A Muse and a Poet! A homeopathic cure then!” said Bixiou.On the tenth day Lousteau received a letter with the Sancerre postmark.“Good! very good!” said Lousteau.“‘Beloved friend, idol of my heart and soul----’ twenty pages of it! all at one sitting, and dated midnight! She writes when she finds herself alone. Poor woman! Ah, ha! And a postscript—“‘I dare not ask you to write to me as I write, every day; still, I hope to have a few lines from my dear one every week, to relieve my mind.’—What a pity to burn it all! it is really well written,” said Lousteau to himself, as he threw the ten sheets of paper into the fire after reading them. “That woman was born to reel off copy!”Lousteau was not much afraid of Madame Schontz, who really loved him for himself, but he had supplanted a friend in the heart of a Marquise. This Marquise, not coy, sometimes dropped in unexpectedly at his rooms in the evening, veiled in a hackney coach; and she, as a literary woman, allowed herself to hunt through all his drawers.A week later, Lousteau, who hardly remembered Dinah, was startled by another budget from Sancerre—eight leaves, sixteen pages! He heard a woman’s step; he thought it announced a search from the Marquise, and tossed these rapturous proofs of affection into the fire—unread!“A woman’s letter!” exclaimed Madame Schontz, as she came in. “The paper, the wax, are scented—”“Here you are, sir,” said a porter from the coach office, setting down two huge hampers in the ante-room. “Carriage paid. Please to sign my book.”“Carriage paid!” cried Madame Schontz. “It must have come from Sancerre.”“Yes, madame,” said the porter.“Your Tenth Muse is a remarkably intelligent woman,” said the courtesan, opening one hamper while Lousteau signed. “I like a Muse who understands housekeeping, and who can make game pies as well as blots. And, oh! what beautiful flowers!” she went on, opening the second hamper. “Why, you could get none finer in Paris!—And here, and here! A hare, partridges, half a roebuck!—We will ask your friends and have a famous dinner, for Athalie has a special talent for dressing venison.”Lousteau wrote to Dinah; but instead of writing from the heart, he was clever. The letter was insidious; it was like one of Mirabeau’s letters to Sophie. The style of a true lover is transparent. It is a clear stream that shows the bottom of the heart between two banks, bright with trifles of existence, and covered with the flowers of the soul that blossom afresh every day, full of intoxicating beauty—but only for two. As soon as a love letter has any charm for a third reader, it is beyond doubt the product of the head, not of the heart. But a woman will always be beguiled; she always believes herself to be the determining cause of this flow of wit.By the end of December Lousteau had ceased to read Dinah’s letters; they lay in a heap in a drawer of his chest that was never locked, under his shirts, from which they scented.Then one of those chances came to Lousteau which such bohemians ought to clutch by every hair. In the middle of December, Madame Schontz, who took a real interest in Etienne, sent to beg him to call one morning on business.“My dear fellow, you have a chance of marrying.”“I can marry very often, happily, my dear.”“When I say marrying, I mean marrying well. You have no prejudices: I need not mince matters. This is the position: A young lady has got into trouble; her mother knows nothing of even a kiss. Her father is an honest notary, a man of honor; he has been wise enough to keep it dark. He wants to get his daughter married within a fortnight, and he will give her a fortune of a hundred and fifty thousand francs—for he has three other children; but—and—it is not a bad idea—he will add a hundred thousand francs, under the rose, hand to hand, to cover the damages. They are an old family of Paris citizens, Rue des Lombards—”“Well, then, why does not the lover marry her?”“Dead.”“What a romance! Such things are nowhere to be heard of but in the Rue des Lombards.”“But do not take it into your head that a jealous brother murdered the seducer. The young man died in the most commonplace way of a pleurisy caught as he came out of the theatre. A head-clerk and penniless, the man entrapped the daughter to marry into the business—A judgment from heaven, I call it!”“Where did you hear the story?”“From Malaga; the notary is her milord.”“What, Cardot, the son of that little old man in hair-powder, Florentine’s first friend?”“Just so. Malaga, whose ‘fancy’ is a little tomtit of a fiddler of eighteen, cannot in conscience make such a boy marry the girl. Besides, she has no cause to do him an ill turn.—Indeed, Monsieur Cardot wants a man of thirty at least. Our notary, I feel sure, will be proud to have a famous man for his son-in-law. So just feel yourself all over.—You will pay your debts, you will have twelve thousand francs a year, and be a father without any trouble on your part; what do you say to that? And, after all, you only marry a very consolable widow. There is an income of fifty thousand francs in the house, and the value of the connection, so in due time you may look forward to not less than fifteen thousand francs a year more for your share, and you will enter a family holding a fine political position; Cardot is the brother-in-law of old Camusot, the depute who lived so long with Fanny Beaupre.”“Yes,” said Lousteau, “old Camusot married little Daddy Cardot’s eldest daughter, and they had high times together!”“Well!” Madame Schontz went on, “and Madame Cardot, the notary’s wife, was a Chiffreville—manufacturers of chemical products, the aristocracy of these days! Potash, I tell you! Still, this is the unpleasant side. You will have a terrible mother-in-law, a woman capable of killing her daughter if she knew! This Cardot woman is a bigot; she has lips like two faded narrow pink ribbons.“A man of the town like you would never pass muster with that woman, who, in her well-meaning way, will spy out your bachelor life and know every fact of the past. However, Cardot says he means to exert his paternal authority. The poor man will be obliged to do the civil to his wife for some days; a woman made of wood, my dear fellow; Malaga, who has seen her, calls her a penitential scrubber. Cardot is a man of forty; he will be mayor of his district, and perhaps be elected deputy. He is prepared to give, in lieu of the hundred thousand francs, a nice little house in the Rue Saint-Lazare, with a forecourt and a garden, which cost him no more than sixty thousand at the time of the July overthrow; he would sell, and that would be an opportunity for you to go and come at the house, to see the daughter, and be civil to the mother.—And it would give you a look of property in Madame Cardot’s eyes. You would be housed like a prince in that little mansion. Then, by Camusot’s interest, you may get an appointment as librarian to some public office where there is no library.—Well, and then if you invest your money in backing up a newspaper, you will get ten thousand francs a year on it, you can earn six, your librarianship will bring in four.—Can you do better for yourself?“If you were to marry a lamb without spot, it might be a light woman by the end of two years. What is the damage?—an anticipated dividend! It is quite the fashion.“Take my word for it, you can do no better than come to dine with Malaga to-morrow. You will meet your father-in-law; he will know the secret has been let out—by Malaga, with whom he cannot be angry—and then you are master of the situation. As to your wife!—her misconduct leaves you as free as a bachelor—”“Your language is as blunt as a cannon ball.”“I love you for your own sake, that is all—and I can reason. Well! why do you stand there like a wax image of Abd-el-Kader? There is nothing to meditate over. Marriage is heads or tails—well, you have tossed heads up.”“You shall have my reply to-morrow,” said Lousteau.“I would sooner have it at once; Malaga will write you up to-night.”“Well, then, yes.”Lousteau spent the evening writing a long letter to the Marquise, giving the reasons that compelled him to marry: his constant poverty, the torpor of his imagination, his white hairs, his moral and physical exhaustion—in short, four pages of arguments. “As to Dinah, I will send her a circular announcing the marriage,” said he to himself. “As Bixiou says, I have not my match for knowing how to dock the tail of a passion.”Lousteau, who at first had been on some ceremony with himself, by next day dreaded lest the marriage should not come off. He was pressingly civil to the notary.“I knew monsieur your father,” said he, “at Florentine’s, so I may well know you here, at Mademoiselle Turquet’s. Like father, like son. A very good fellow and a philosopher was little Daddy Cardot—excuse me, we always called him so. At that time, Florine, Florentine, Tullia, Coralie, and Mariette were the five fingers of your hand, so to speak—it is fifteen years ago. My follies, as you may suppose, are a thing of the past.—In those days it was pleasure that ran away with me; now I am ambitious; but, in our day, to get on a man must be free from debt, have a good income, a wife, and a family. If I pay taxes enough to qualify me, I may be a deputy yet, like any other man.”Maître Cardot appreciated this profession of faith. Lousteau had laid himself out to please and the notary liked him, feeling himself more at ease, as may be easily imagined, with a man who had known his father’s secrets than he would with another. On the following day Lousteau was introduced to the Cardot family as the purchaser of the house in the Rue Saint-Lazare, and three days later he dined there.Cardot lived in an old house near the Place du Chatelet. In this house everything was “good.” Economy covered every scrap of gilding with green gauze; all the furniture wore holland covers. Though it was impossible to feel uneasy as to the family’s wealth, at the end of half an hour no one could suppress a yawn. Boredom perched in every nook; the curtains hung dolefully; the dining-room was like Harpagon’s. Even had Lousteau not known Malaga, he could have guessed the notary’s real life was spent elsewhere.The journalist saw a tall, fair girl with blue eyes, at once shy and languishing. The elder brother took a fancy to him; he was the fourth clerk in the office, but strongly attracted by literary snares, though destined to succeed his father. The younger sister was twelve. Lousteau, assuming a little Jesuitical air, played the Monarchist and Churchman for the mother’s benefit: smooth, deliberate, and complimentary.Within three weeks of their introduction, at his fourth dinner there, Félicie Cardot, who had been watching Lousteau out of the corner of her eye, carried him a cup of coffee where he stood in the window recess, and said in a low voice, with tears in her eyes:“I will devote my whole life, monsieur, to thanking you for your sacrifice in favor of a poor girl—”Lousteau was touched; there was so much expression in her look, her accent, her attitude. “She would make a good man happy,” thought he, pressing her hand in reply.Madame Cardot looked upon her son-in-law as a man with a future; but, above all, she was delighted by his high tone of morals. Etienne, prompted by the wily notary, had pledged his word that he had no natural children, no tie that could endanger the happiness of her dear Félicie.“You may perhaps think I go rather too far,” said the bigot to the journalist; “but in giving such a jewel as my Félicie to any man, one must think of the future. I am not one of those mothers who want to be rid of their daughters. Monsieur Cardot hurries matters on, urges forward his daughter’s marriage; he wishes it over. This is the only point on which we differ.—Though with a man like you, monsieur, a literary man whose youth has been preserved by hard work from the moral shipwreck now so prevalent, we may feel quite safe; still, you would be the first to laugh at me if I looked for a husband for my daughter with my eyes shut. I know you are not an innocent, and I should be very sorry for my Félicie if you were” (this in a whisper); “but if you had any liaison—For instance, monsieur, you have heard of Madame Roguin, the wife of a notary who, unhappily for our faculty, was sadly notorious. Madame Roguin has, ever since 1820, been kept by a banker—”“Yes, du Tillet,” replied Etienne; but he bit his tongue as he recollected how rash it was to confess acquaintance with du Tillet.“Yes.—Well, monsieur, if you were a mother, would you not quake at the thought that Madame du Tillet’s fate might be your child’s? At her age, and née de Granville! To have as a rival a woman of fifty and more. Sooner would I see my daughter dead than give her to a man who had such a connection with a married woman. A grisette, an actress, you take her and leave her.—There is no danger, in my opinion, from women of that stamp; love is their trade, they care for no one; one down and another to come on!—But a woman who has sinned against duty must hug her sin; her only excuse is constancy, if such a crime can have an excuse. At least, that is the view I hold of a respectable woman’s fall, and that is what makes it so terrible—”Instead of seeking the meaning of these speeches, Etienne made a jest of them at Malaga’s, whither he went with his father-in-law elect; for the notary and the journalist were the best of friends.Lousteau had already given himself the airs of importance; his life at last was to have a purpose; he was in luck’s way, and in a few days would be the owner of a delightful little house in the Rue Saint-Lazare; he was going to be married to a charming woman, he would have about twenty thousand francs a year, and could give the reins to his ambition; the young lady loved him, and he would be connected with several respectable families. In short, he was in full sail on the blue waters of hope.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Madame Cardot had asked to see the prints for Gil Blas, an illustrated volume the publishers were issuing, and Lousteau had taken the first numbers for her inspection. But she had an intention: she had borrowed the book only as an excuse to call unexpectedly on her future son-in-law. The sight of those bachelor rooms, which her husband had described as charming, would tell her more about Lousteau’s habits than any report. Madame Camusot, ignorant of the fateful secret, was terrified at such a marriage for her niece. Monsieur Camusot, a Councillor of the Supreme Court and old Camusot’s son by his first marriage, had given his stepmother, Cardot’s sister, an unflattering account of the journalist.Lousteau, clever, did not find it strange that the wife of a rich notary should wish to examine a volume costing fifteen francs before buying. Such clever men seldom study the middle class; while he made game of them, they were ready to throttle him.One day in January 1837 Madame Cardot and her daughter took a hackney coach to the Rue des Martyrs to return parts of Gil Blas to Félicie’s betrothed, eager to see Lousteau’s rooms. Such domiciliary visits are common among the old bourgeoisie. The porter was out, but his daughter, on being told they had come, handed over the key—especially after Madame Cardot slipped her a gold piece.It was about noon, the hour the journalist would return from breakfast at the Café Anglais. Crossing the open space between Notre-Dame de Lorette and the Rue des Martyrs, Lousteau fancied he saw Dinah in a hired coach going up the Rue du Faubourg-Montmartre. He stood rooted; at his door he saw her at the coach.“What has brought you here?” he asked, in the familiar tu. Formal vous was out of the question to a woman he meant to get rid of.“Why, my love,” she cried, “have you not read my letters?”“Certainly I have,” he said.“Well, then?”“Well, then?”“You are a father,” said the country lady.“Faugh!” he ejaculated. He thought she must be prepared for the blow.He told the coachman to wait, took Madame de la Baudraye’s hand, and, leaving the chaise full of trunks, vowed he would send her and her luggage back where she had come from.“Monsieur, monsieur,” called little Pamela. The child had sense; she thought three women must not be allowed to meet in a bachelor’s rooms.“Well, well!” said Lousteau, dragging Dinah along. Pamela, thinking the lady some relation, added, “The key is in the door; your mother-in-law is there.”In his agitation Lousteau heard only “mother is there” and went in.Félicie and her mother, already in the bedroom, crept into a corner on seeing Etienne enter with a woman.“At last, Etienne, my dearest, I am yours for life!” cried Dinah, flinging her arms around him and clasping him while he turned the key. “Life is perpetual anguish to me in that house at Anzy. I could not bear it longer; and when it came for me to proclaim my happiness—well, I had not courage. Here I am, your wife with your child! And you have not written to me; you have left me two months without a line.”“But, Dinah, you put me in the greatest difficulty—”“Do you love me?”“How can I do otherwise than love you? But would you not have been wiser to remain at Sancerre? I am in the most abject poverty, and I fear to drag you into it—”“Your misery will be paradise to me. I only ask to live here, never to go out—”“Good God! that is all very fine in words, but—” Dinah burst into tears at his roughness.Lousteau could not resist her distress. He clasped the Baroness and kissed her.“Do not cry, Didine!” he said; and seeing Madame Cardot’s figure in the mirror, he murmured in her ear, “Come, Didine, go with Pamela and get your trunks unloaded. Go; do not cry; we will be happy!” He led her to the door and returned to face the storm.“Monsieur,” said Madame Cardot, “I congratulate myself on having resolved to see for myself the home of the man who was to be my son-in-law. If my daughter were to die of it, she should never be the wife of such a man as you. You must devote yourself to making your Didine happy, monsieur.” The virtuous lady then left, followed by Félicie, who wept for the loss of Etienne. Madame Cardot stared insolently at Dinah as she drove off; Dinah’s heart smarted at “that is all very fine in words,” yet, like every woman in love, she believed the murmured “Do not cry, Didine!”Lousteau, practical from a stormy life, reflected: “Didine is high-minded; when she knows of my proposed marriage, she will sacrifice herself for my prospects, and I know how to let her know.” Delighted at this trick’s apparent certainty, he began humming to himself; and, to Dinah’s surprise, she found him dancing.“Your arrival and our happiness have turned my head with joy,” he said, to explain his mood.“And I fancied you had ceased to love me!” she cried, dropping her handbag and weeping with joy.“Make yourself at home, my darling,” said Etienne laughing inwardly; “I must write two lines to excuse myself from a bachelor party, for I mean to devote myself to you. Give your orders; you are at home.”He wrote to Bixiou:MY DEAR BOY,—My Baroness has dropped into my arms, and will be fatal to my marriage unless we perform one of the most familiar stratagems of the thousand and one comedies. I rely on you to come here, like one of Moliere’s old men, to scold your nephew Leandre for his folly, while the Tenth Muse lies hidden in my bedroom; you must work on her feelings; strike hard, be brutal, offensive. I, you understand, shall express my blind devotion, and be deaf, so you may have to shout at me.Come if you can at seven o’clock.Yours,E. LOUSTEAU.Having sent this letter by a commissionaire to the man who delighted in such practical jokes—the artist’s “charge”—Lousteau made a show of settling the Muse of Sancerre in his rooms. He busied himself arranging her luggage and told her about the house with overflowing glee, kind words and caresses, so Dinah believed herself supremely beloved. The fashionable rooms pleased her far more than her old château.Pamela Migeon, the intelligent fourteen-year-old, was asked if she would like to be waiting-maid to the Baroness. Pamela, delighted, ordered dinner from a boulevard restaurant. Dinah, taking possession of closets, found none of the household necessities—extreme poverty lurked under the superficial elegance of the bachelor home. She indulged in tender dreams: she would alter Étienne’s habits, make him domestic, and fill his cup of happiness.The novelty hid the disastrous side; Dinah saw reciprocated love as absolution of her sin and did not yet look beyond the walls. Pamela, whose wits matched a lorette’s, went to Madame Schontz to borrow plate, telling her what had happened. Madame Schontz welcomed her and hurried to Malaga to warn Cardot of the catastrophe.The journalist, not uneasy in respect to his marriage prospects, grew ever more charming to the provincial lady. Dinner was the child’s play of lovers set free. After coffee, Lousteau sat by the fire, Dinah on his knee, when Pamela ran in, scared.“Here is Monsieur Bixiou!” she cried.“Go into the bedroom,” said Lousteau to his mistress; “I’ll soon get rid of him. He’s one of my most intimate friends, and I must explain my new start in life.”“Oh, ho! dinner for two, and a blue velvet bonnet!” cried Bixiou. “I am off.—Ah! that is what comes of marrying—one must go through some partings. How rich one feels when one begins to move one’s sticks, heh?”“Who talks of marrying?” said Lousteau.“What! are you not going to be married, then?” cried Bixiou.“No!”“No? My word, what next? You, who have come across twenty thousand francs a year, and a house, and a wife connected with all the first families—a wife, in short, out of the Rue des Lombards—”“That will do, Bixiou, enough. It is at an end. Be off!”“Be off? I have a friend’s privileges, and I shall take every advantage.—What has come over you?”“What has come over me is my lady from Sancerre. She is a mother, and we are going to live together happily to the end of our days.—You would have heard it tomorrow, so you may as well be told it now.”“Many chimney-pots are falling on my head, as Arnal says. But if this woman really loves you, she will go back to the place she came from. Did any provincial woman find her sea-legs in Paris? She will wound all your vanities. Have you forgotten what a provincial is? She will bore you as much when she is happy as when she is sad; she will have a talent for escaping grace as a Parisian has in inventing it.“Lousteau, listen. A passion may make you forget the times, but I have no mythological bandage over my eyes. For fifteen years you have been tossing in the literary world; you are no longer young, you have padded the hoof till your soles are worn through! You hide your rags like a street urchin with turned socks. In short, the joke is stale. Your excuses are more familiar than a patent medicine—”“I may say to you, like the Regent to Cardinal Dubois, ‘That is kicking enough!’” said Lousteau.“Oh, venerable young man,” said Bixiou, “the iron has touched the sore. You are worn out, aren’t you? Well, then; in the heyday of youth, under penury, what have you done? You are not in the front rank, and you have not a thousand francs of your own. Can you, in the decline of your powers, support a family by your pen, when your wife, if honest, won’t have the resources of a woman of the streets, who can extract her thousand-franc note from the depths where milord keeps it safe? You are rushing into the lowest depths of the social theatre.“And this is only the financial side. Consider the political position. We live in a bourgeois age, where honor and genius are summed up in paying your way, owing nobody, and conducting your affairs with judgment. Be steady, respectable, have a wife and children, pay rent and taxes, serve in the National Guard, and you may aspire to the Ministry! You were preparing to fulfill the conditions for a political personage; you are capable of the mean tricks necessary in office. And just for a woman, who will abandon you—the end of every passion—you throw away the sacred Hearth, the Rue des Lombards, a political career, thirty thousand francs per annum, respectability and respect! Ought that be the end of a man who has done with illusions?“If you had kept a pot boiling for some actress who gave you fun for it—well; that is a cabinet matter. But to live with another man’s wife is a draft at sight on disaster; it is bolting the bitter pills of vice with none of the gilding.”“That will do. One word answers it all; I love Madame de la Baudraye, and prefer her to every fortune and every position the world can offer. I may have been carried away by ambition, but everything must give way to the joy of being a father.”“Ah, ha! you have a fancy for paternity? But, wretched man, we are fathers only of our legitimate children. What is a brat that does not bear your name? The last chapter of the romance.—Your child will be taken from you! We have seen that story in twenty plays these ten years past.“Society, my dear boy, will drop upon you sooner or later. Read Adolphe.—I fancy I can see you, after you and she are used to each other: dejected, hang-dog, bereft of position and fortune, fighting like shareholders tricked by a director. Your director is happiness.”“Say no more, Bixiou.”“But I have only just begun,” continued Bixiou. “Listen. Marriage has been out of favor; but apart from certifying heredity and offering a chance for a penniless young man to make his fortune, it survives. There is not the man living who would not repent, sooner or later, of having lost the chance of marrying thirty thousand francs a year.”“You won’t understand me,” cried Lousteau, exasperated. “Go away—she is there—”“I beg your pardon; why did you not tell me sooner?—You are of age, and so is she,” he added, lowering his voice but loud enough for Dinah to hear. “She will make you repent bitterly of your happiness!—”“If it is a folly, I intend to commit it.—Good-bye.”“A man gone overboard!” cried Bixiou.“Devil take those friends who think they have a right to preach,” said Lousteau, opening the bedroom door. He found Madame de la Baudraye sunk in an armchair, dabbing her eyes with an embroidered handkerchief.“Oh, why did I come here?” she sobbed. “Good Heavens, why indeed? Etienne, I am not so provincial as you think me. You are making a fool of me.”“Darling angel,” answered Lousteau, taking her in his arms and lifting her half-lifeless into the drawing-room, “we have both pledged our future; it is sacrifice for sacrifice. While loving you at Sancerre, they were engaging me to be married here, but I refused.—Oh! I was extremely distressed—”“I am going,” cried Dinah, starting up.“You will stay, my Didine. All is at an end. Is this fortune so lightly earned? Must I marry a gawky, tow-haired creature, with a red nose, the daughter of a notary, and saddle myself with a stepmother who could give Madame de Piedefer points for bigotry—”Pamela flew in and whispered, “Madame Schontz!”Lousteau rose, leaving Dinah on the sofa, and went out.“It is all over with you, my dear,” Madame Schontz said. “Cardot does not mean to quarrel with his wife for the sake of a son-in-law. The lady made a scene—something like a scene! So, to conclude, the head-clerk, who had been the late head-clerk’s deputy for two years, agrees to take the girl with the business.”“Mean wretch!” exclaimed Lousteau. “What! in two hours he has decided?”“Bless me, that is simple enough. The rascal, knowing all the dead man’s little secrets, guessed your master’s fix from overhearing a few words of the squabble with Madame Cardot. The notary relies on your honor and good feeling, for the affair is settled. The clerk, whose conduct has been admirable, even went so far as to attend mass! A finished hypocrite—just suits the mamma. You and Cardot will still be friends. He is to be a director in an immense financial concern, and he may be useful to you.—So you have been waked from a sweet dream.”“I have lost a fortune, a wife, and—”“And a mistress,” said Madame Schontz, smiling. “Here you are, more than married; you will be insufferable, always wanting to get home, nothing loose about you, neither your clothes nor your habits. And, after all, my Arthur does things in style. I will be faithful to him and cut Malaga’s acquaintance.“Let me peep at her through the door—your Sancerre Muse,” she went on. “Is there no finer bird than that to be found in the desert? You are cheated! She is dignified, lean, lachrymose; she only needs Lady Dudley’s turban!”What is it now?” asked Madame de la Baudraye, hearing the rustle of silk and a woman’s murmur.“It is, my darling, that we are now indissolubly united.—I have had an answer to the letter you saw me write, which was to break off my marriage—”“So that was the party you gave up?”“Yes.”“Oh, I will be more than your wife—I am your slave, I give you my life,” said the deluded creature. “I did not believe I could love you more than I did! Now I shall not be a mere incident, but your whole life.”“Yes, my beautiful, my generous Didine.”“Swear to me,” she begged, “that only death shall divide us.”Lousteau was ready with the most fascinating vows. Between the apartment door where he had taken the lorette’s farewell kiss and the drawing-room where the Muse lay bewildered by shocks, he had remembered little De la Baudraye’s precarious health, his fine fortune, and Bianchon’s remark about Dinah—“She will be a rich widow!”—and he said to himself, “I would a hundred times rather have Madame de la Baudraye for a wife than Félicie!”His plan was quickly fixed: he determined to play the farce of passion again, and to perfection. His mean self-interest and false vehemence had disastrous effects. Madame de la Baudraye had intended, on coming to Paris, to live in rooms of her own near Lousteau; but the proofs of his devotion—his giving up brilliant prospects—and the perfection of the first days of their illicit union kept her from suggesting a parting. The second day was to be, and was, a high festival; such a proposal then would have been a discord.Lousteau, anxious to make Dinah feel dependent, kept her in a state of intoxication with incessant amusement. These circumstances prevented two clever people from avoiding the slough they fell into—that of a life in common, a folly common in Parisian literary circles.Thus the whole programme of a provincial amour, satirically described by Lousteau to Madame de la Baudraye, was enacted—a fact neither he nor she now remembered. Passion is born deaf and mute.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################This winter in Paris was to Madame de la Baudraye what October had been at Sancerre. Etienne, to initiate “his wife” into Paris life, mixed their honeymoon with theatre evenings, where Dinah occupied the stage box. At first she retained traces of provincial modesty; afraid of being seen, she hid her happiness and timidly remarked, “Monsieur de Clagny or Monsieur Gravier may have followed me to Paris.” Even in the capital she felt fearful, as if still in Sancerre.Lousteau, excessively vain, educated Dinah in fashion and society. Her provincial look soon vanished, and his friends congratulated him on his conquest. During that season Etienne wrote little and ran into debt, though Dinah, proud, bought her clothes from her savings, fancying she had cost him nothing. After three months she was acclimatized to Paris: she revelled in the Italian opera, knew the plays, actors, and jests of the day, and adapted to the quick, witty atmosphere where clever people thrive.One morning, reading the papers collected by Lousteau, two lines took her back to Sancerre: “Monsieur le Baron de Clagny... has been appointed Deputy Public Prosecutor to the Supreme Court in Paris.” A journalist sneered that the lawyer loved her; she replied simply, “Poor man! What did I tell you? He is following me.”Etienne and Dinah were in that dazzling stage of passion where each is comfortably accustomed to the other yet freshness remains. They had not explored every secret; enchantment reigned with no collisions. Dinah spoke love’s hyperbole—“When you cease to love me, kill me”—and Lousteau joked about testing constancy; neither ever acted on such proposals. Her consent to their union was a proud, self-respecting act. In intimacy she never surrendered modesty or ambition; Lousteau involuntarily held her in high esteem. As a Parisian she could outshine a courtesan by information, habits of mind, and reading. “There is in Dinah,” Etienne told Bixiou, “the stuff to make both a Ninon and a De Staël.”When Dinah’s pregnancy began to show, she prepared to shut herself away but insisted on attending the first night of a new play by Nathan—a literary ceremonial. She dressed perfectly, but misfortune awaited: their box was next to Anna Grossetete’s; Lousteau left his seat at the end of the first act, abandoning Dinah to scrutiny. Between acts Monsieur de Clagny entered her box.“I am happy to see you, to tell you how pleased I am by your promotion,” she said.“Oh! Madame, for whom should I come to Paris?” he answered, confessing his unease about her position. He warned that living openly with a man of letters exposes a woman to public curiosity and loss of respectability, and advised she might be happier at home with her mother. Dinah replied she had renounced society for love and was content to live freely and confide in him.Clagny, moved, worried whether Lousteau would appreciate her sacrifice. Dinah invited him to the Rue des Martyrs to see for himself. The lawyer left to avoid scandal when Lousteau returned, who boasted, “You are a greater hit than the piece.” That brief triumph pleased Dinah, but three days later she learned they were penniless, with two quarters’ rent owing and distraint imminent. The reality pierced her; she repented tempting Etienne into extravagance. Dinah, used to never owing, learned through Migeon that she could pawn jewels; her “uncle” advanced nine hundred francs. She kept three hundred for baby needs and gave Lousteau the rest so he could wrestle through a novel for a periodical. “Finish without sacrificing to necessity,” she urged. “Polish the style; work up the subject. I will be housewife and attend to business.”For months Etienne had taken Dinah daily to the Café Riche; she was dismayed to find five hundred francs owing for the last fortnight. “Have we been drinking wine at six francs a bottle?” she cried. He shrugged: “It makes little difference whether we are robbed at a restaurant or by a cook.” She secured a kitchen and two servants’ rooms and wrote to her mother for linen and a loan. Her mother sent two trunks of linen, some plate, and two thousand francs by a pious cook.Ten days after the theatre evening Clagny called and found Dinah making a little cap; the sight of the proud woman at needlework moved him. He saw she meant to be Lousteau’s guardian spirit, to lead him to a nobler road. Dinah recognized that Lousteau’s troubles sprang from indolence and lack of will; she strove to make him happy by economy and method. “His happiness will be my absolution,” she told Clagny.Etienne had flaunted their triumph at Nathan’s first night, revealing Dinah as “a fine feather in his cap.” The judge, at first deceived by Lousteau’s ingratiating manner, gradually saw the truth. Between April and July, when Dinah expected confinement, she discovered why Lousteau had not triumphed over poverty: he was idle and weak of will. Genius requires a strong will; talent without it is unproductive. Men who lead their age deny themselves and keep their faculties ready. Lousteau’s cigarette habit lulled him; criticism as a profession also stultified him. The newspaper critic’s acrobatics—praising or tearing down for convenience—fell short of real criticism’s principles; Lousteau could not bridge the gulf between dexterity and art.Dinah, her intellect quickened, measured her idol. She saw him scamping work, writing at the last minute. She excused him—“He is a poet!”—and tried to help. Loving him, she sought through her mother to negotiate an allowance from her husband without Etienne’s knowledge. A few days before July’s end she crumpled a letter from her mother conveying Monsieur de la Baudraye’s ultimatum: “Madame cannot need an allowance in Paris when she can live in perfect luxury at her Chateau of Anzy: she may return.” Lousteau read the letter. “I will avenge you!” he said, in an ominous tone flattering to a woman whose antipathies were flattered.Five days later Bianchon and Duriau were engaged at Lousteau’s; he made a display over the infant’s arrival. Monsieur de Clagny and Madame Piedefer were to be godparents. Dinah gave birth to a boy that might have filled a queen with envy. Bianchon and Clagny registered the child at the Mayor’s office as the son of Monsieur and Madame de la Baudraye, unknown to Etienne. Lousteau, rushing to a printer, had fifty circulars set up:“Madame la Baronne de la Baudraye is happily delivered of a son.Monsieur Etienne Lousteau has the pleasure of informing you of the fact.The mother and child are doing well.”Clagny found a list of Sancerre names to which Lousteau planned to send the notice, confiscated the list and remaining circulars, showed proofs to Madame Piedefer, and dashed to another printer for a corrected announcement:“Madame la Baronne de la Baudraye is happily delivered of a son.Monsieur le Baron de la Baudraye has the honor of informing you of the fact.Mother and child are doing well.”Clagny destroyed proofs and intercepted sent copies, recovering thirty and finally leaving only one of the original notes—sent to Nathan. He called on Nathan five times; Nathan at first laughed, calling the autograph a weapon he wouldn’t give up. Clagny argued it was not for Lousteau but for a woman and child ignorant of the damage to their fortune and honour. At last Nathan yielded and gave up the circular.When the jest was counteracted, Clagny rated Lousteau in Madame Piedefer’s presence and found Etienne fuming. “I did it with a purpose!” Etienne protested. “Monsieur de la Baudraye has sixty thousand francs a year and refuses an allowance; I wished to make him feel the child is in my power.” “For that reason I agreed to be godfather,” Clagny replied. “If the child is registered as the son of the Baron and Baronne, the child is heir to a great entailed estate.” Lousteau retorted, “And is the mother to die of hunger?” “Be at ease. I will transact with Monsieur de la Baudraye.” Clagny left chilled; Dinah, his idol, seemed loved for her money; he pitied her.The infant’s care and Madame Piedefer’s presence were adverse to writing; Lousteau moved to rooms on the first floor. Obliged to attend first nights without Dinah, living apart, he found freedom alluring. He was dragged to jollifications and to friends’ mistresses’ houses, seeing brilliantly dressed young women in whom economy seemed treason. Dinah, after nursing for three months, could not compare with these perishable blossoms.Yet home life had its charm. In three months the mother, Didine, the Sancerre cook, and little Pamela transformed the apartment. Lousteau found breakfasts and dinners served with a domestic luxury; Dinah, handsome and careful, anticipated his wishes; he felt king at home. Didine’s affection showed in every trifle; the deceptions of his unreal passion continued.Dinah knew Lousteau’s life threatened ruin to love and household. After ten months she weaned her baby, installed her mother upstairs, and restored family intimacy. Daily domestic intercourse gives a woman power; men left too often to themselves become susceptible to release. Dinah intended to be indispensable and to infuse energy into Lousteau, whose weakness she mistook for security. She suggested subjects, sketched treatments, sometimes wrote whole chapters. Her collaboration revived dying talent; she produced two successful books while keeping her role secret from Madame Piedefer.This galvanism improved pay, and they lived comfortably through 1838. Lousteau grew accustomed to seeing Dinah do his work and paid her nothing—“monkey money.” The more she gave, the more she loved him; she felt she could never give him up.But another child came, and that year proved a trial. Despite precautions, Etienne contracted debts; he worked himself nearly to death to pay them while Dinah was ill, and she thought him heroic. Appalled at supporting two women, children, and two maids, he could not continue sustaining them by his pen after failing even to maintain himself. So he let things take their chance and exaggerated home-made love to secure liberty abroad.Dinah endured stoically without adequate support. Belief in his love gave her strength. She worked hard, sacrificing dress—dying gowns black—and risking beauty. Malaga mocked the stench of black; the thrift irked Lousteau, who reproached her for dowdiness while secretly benefiting from her sacrifices.Dinah then tried to hold the reins by which a clever woman steers a weak man, but in doing so she lost some moral lustre; suspicion and reproach led to quarrels and bred disrespect. She made concessions; Lousteau entertained friends—Nathan, Bixiou, Blondet, Finot—whose manners and talk were depraving. They mocked her principles as provincial prudishness and preached woman’s superiority.During Carnival 1840 she disguised herself to spy on Etienne at balls and suppers. The morning after Mid-Lent she returned from a ball in fancy dress to go to bed; she had gone to watch Lousteau, who, believing her ill, had engaged that evening to Fanny Beaupré. Warned by a friend, he behaved so as to deceive her, and she, eager to be deceived, was duped.Stepping from the hired cab she met Monsieur de la Baudraye; the porter pointed her out. The old man took his wife by the arm in an icy tone: “So this is you, madame!” The conjugal authority froze her. To escape Etienne’s eye she had chosen a dress he would not detect. Using her mask she slipped away, changed, and went to her mother’s rooms, where she found her husband waiting. Despite her assumed dignity she blushed.“What do you want of me, monsieur? Are we not separated forever?” she asked.“Actually, yes. Legally, no,” he answered. Madame Piedefer signalled; Dinah understood his visit was prompted by interest. “Nothing could have brought you here but your own interests,” she said bitterly. “Our interests,” he replied coldly, “for we have two children. Your Uncle Silas Piedefer is dead in New York; after making and losing fortunes, he left some seven or eight hundred thousand francs—some say a million. I am chief in our common interests and act for you.”“In everything that relates to business I trust Monsieur de Clagny,” she retorted. “He knows the law; come to terms with him.”“I have no occasion for Monsieur de Clagny to take my children from you,” Monsieur de la Baudraye said.“Your children!” Dinah laughed. “Your children to whom you have sent no sou!” Her outburst froze under his coolness. “I do not intend to part from them. I shall take them to Anzy, if only to save them from seeing their mother disguised like a—”“Silence!” Dinah cried. “What do you want of me?”“A power of attorney to receive our Uncle Silas’ property.” Dinah wrote two lines to Clagny and asked her husband to return in the afternoon.At five o’clock Clagny—now Attorney-General—explained her position and undertook to bargain with the old man. Monsieur de la Baudraye, needing her power of attorney to deal as he wished, bought it by concessions: he agreed to allow her ten thousand francs a year so long as she resided in Paris; the children were to be placed in his custody at six; and Clagny extracted payment in advance.Little La Baudraye came jaunty to say good-bye in a white india-rubber overcoat. He looked so like the La Baudraye of 1836 that Dinah despaired. From the garden, where he smoked, Lousteau watched him cross the forecourt. This short scene changed the writer’s private calculation. Their life together had cost both sides; in moral accounting Lousteau considered himself the creditor. It was a favorable moment to desert her. Tired after three years of a comedy that could not be habit, he hid his weariness and forced smiles like a debtor. The advantages he had expected in the future had sustained him; when Monsieur de la Baudraye embarked for America, Lousteau ceased to believe in that future.Entering the drawing-room where Dinah had just left her husband, he found her grave. “Etienne,” she said, “do you know my lord proposed that if I wish to return to Anzy during his absence, he hopes my mother’s advice will weigh with me and that I shall go back with my children.” “It is very good advice,” Lousteau replied dryly. His tone and look struck Dinah, who lived only in love; two tears slipped down unnoticed as she wiped them. He asked tenderly, “What is it, Didine?” She confessed she felt she had sold her children by selling the chance to remain, that he would have them at six and she could only see them in Sancerre—torture.Lousteau knelt and kissed her hands, coaxing. “You do not understand me,” he said. “I blame myself; I am not worth such sacrifices. I am second-rate in literature. If I can no longer cut a figure at the bottom of the newspaper, the editors will throw me aside. We tight-rope dancers have no retiring pension! I am forty-two, idle as a marmot. My love can only be fatal to you. At twenty-two I lived on Florine; what was excusable in youth is a disgrace to a man of forty. Out of devotion you wear black and that does me no credit.” Dinah shrugged magnanimously. His prudence, measured like Clagny’s, gratified and staunched her tears. “He loves me for myself alone!” she thought, smiling.After four years their intimacy combined every shade modern society creates: heart and head, passion, caprice, and taste. Lousteau could magnetize Dinah—one look or touch reduced her to obedience. A kind word wreathed her soul with flowers; a cold look depressed her. Walking at his arm she lost sense of herself. Fascinated by his wit and magnetized by his airs, his vices seemed trivial; she even loved the cigar-smoke drifting into her room. She hated editors who refused Lousteau money and deluded herself that he was writing a remunerative novel rather than merely spinning out debts.This is true love: she argued that if she had placed herself outside the pale, why should he not sacrifice some of a man’s honor for her? “We live outside social convention,” she said. “Nathan can accept homage from Florine; why not from me? We will square accounts when we part; only death can part us. We shall be married some day—time enough to part when little La Baudraye is safe back again.” “I never thought of that,” Lousteau said simply, adding to himself, “Time enough to part when little La Baudraye is safe back again.”############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################From that day forth Etienne lived in luxury; and Dinah, on first nights, could hold her own with the best dressed women in Paris. Lousteau affected among his friends the posture of a man overborne, bored to extinction, ruined by Madame de la Baudraye.“Oh, what would I not give to the friend who would deliver me from Dinah! But no one ever can!” he said. “She loves me enough to throw herself out of the window if I told her.”He was pitied; he took precautions against Dinah’s jealousy when accepting invitations. Yet he was shamelessly unfaithful. Monsieur de Clagny, really in despair at seeing Dinah in such disgraceful circumstances when she might have been rich and in the position she once sought, came to warn her—“You are betrayed”—and she only replied, “I know it.”The lawyer was silenced; still he found his tongue to say one thing.Madame de la Baudraye interrupted him when he had scarcely spoken.“Do you still love me?” she asked.“I would lose my soul for you!” he exclaimed, starting to his feet.The hapless man’s eyes flashed, he trembled like a leaf; he believed himself blessed to be accepted as his idol’s avenger, and this poor joy filled him with rapture.“Why are you so startled?” said she, making him sit again. “That is how I love him.”The lawyer understood this ad hominem. Tears shone in the Judge’s eyes, who had just condemned a man to death.Lousteau’s satiety, that odious end of illicit relations, betrayed itself in a thousand little things—grains of sand against the panes of the little magical hut where lovers dwell and dream. These grains, growing to pebbles, had never been discerned by Dinah till they were as big as rocks. Madame de la Baudraye had at last thoroughly understood Lousteau’s character.“He is,” she said to her mother, “a poet, defenseless against disaster, mean from laziness, not for want of heart, rather too prone to pleasure; in short, a great cat, whom it is impossible to hate. What would become of him without me? I hindered his marriage; he has no prospects. His talent would perish in privations.”“Oh, my Dinah!” Madame Piedefer exclaimed, “what a hell you live in! What is the feeling that gives you strength enough to persist?”“I will be a mother to him!” she replied.There are horrible situations in which we decide nothing until friends disclose our dishonor. We accept compromises with ourselves so long as we escape the prosecutor’s scrutiny. Monsieur de Clagny, clumsy as a tortured man, had been tormenting Dinah.“To preserve my love I will be all that Madame de Pompadour was to preserve her power,” she thought when Clagny left. This phrase showed that her love was becoming a burden and would become toil rather than pleasure.The part she assumed was horribly painful, and Lousteau made it no easier. When he wanted to go out after dinner he played tender farces of affection, addressing Dinah in words full of devotion; he would take her by the chain, and when he had bruised her would say, “Did I wound you?”These false caresses degraded Dinah, who believed in a revival of his love. The mother yielded to the mistress with shameful readiness. She felt herself a mere plaything, and at last confessed to herself: “Well, then, I will be his plaything!”—finding joy in it—the rapture of damnation.A woman of really manly spirit, picturing solitude, felt courage fail. She preferred the miseries of fierce intimacy to the absence of joys that are more exquisite because they spring from remorse, from a terrible struggle of a No persuaded to be Yes. At every moment she seemed to meet the bitter pool one finds in a desert, and drank with more relish than the traveler sips the finest wines at a prince’s table.When Dinah wondered at midnight, “Will he come home, or will he not?” she was not alive again till she heard Lousteau’s boots and ring at the bell.She often tried to restrain him by giving him pleasure; she hoped to match her rivals, and leave them no hold on that agitated heart. How many times a day she rehearsed the tragedy of Le Dernier Jour d’un condamne, telling herself, “To-morrow we part.” And how often would a word, a look, a kiss apparently artless bring her back to the depths of her love!It was terrible. More than once had she meditated suicide as she paced the little town garden where a few pale flowers bloomed. In fact, she had not yet exhausted the vast treasure of devotion a woman bears in her heart.The romance of Adolphe was her Bible; above all, she would not be an Ellenore. She allowed herself no tears, avoided the bitterness so cleverly described by the critic to whom we owe an analysis of that striking work; his comments seemed to Dinah almost superior to the book. She read again this fine essay in the Revue des Deux Mondes.“No,” she said repeating the author’s words, “no, I will not ‘give my requests the form of an order,’ I will not ‘fly to tears as a means of revenge,’ I will not ‘condemn the things I once approved without reservation,’ I will not ‘dog his footsteps with a prying eye’; if he plays truant, he shall not on his return ‘see a scornful lip, whose kiss is an unanswerable command.’ No, ‘my silence shall not be a reproach nor my first word a quarrel.’—I will not be like every other woman!” she went on, laying on her table the little yellow paper volume which had already drawn Lousteau’s remark, “What! are you studying Adolphe?”—“If for one day only he should recognize my merits and say, ‘That victim never uttered a cry!’—it will be all I ask. And besides, the others have him for an hour; I have him for life!”Thinking himself justified by his private tribunal in punishing his wife, Monsieur de la Baudraye robbed her to pursue his cherished enterprise of reclaiming three thousand acres of moorland, to which he had devoted himself since 1836, living like a mouse. He manipulated Monsieur Silas Piedefer’s property so ingeniously that he contrived to reduce the proved value to eight hundred thousand francs while pocketing twelve hundred thousand. He did not announce his return; while his wife endured unspeakable woes, he was building farms, digging trenches, and ploughing rough ground with courage that ranked him among the most remarkable provincial agriculturists.The four hundred thousand francs he had filched from his wife were spent in three years on this undertaking, and Anzy was expected to return seventy-two thousand francs a year net after taxes. The eight hundred thousand he invested at four and a half per cent, buying at eighty francs during the financial crisis of the First of March. By securing his wife an income of forty-eight thousand francs he considered himself no longer in her debt. Could he not restore the odd twelve hundred thousand as soon as the four and a half per cents rose above a hundred? He fancied himself nearly the greatest man in Sancerre besides one—the richest proprietor in France—whose rival he considered himself. He saw himself with an income of a hundred and forty thousand francs, of which ninety thousand came from entailed lands. Calculating his payments—ten thousand in taxes, three thousand in working expenses, ten thousand to his wife, and twelve hundred to his mother-in-law—he would say in Sancerre’s literary circles:“I am reputed miserly, and said to spend nothing; but my outlay amounts to twenty-six thousand five hundred francs a year. And I have still to pay for the education of my two children! I daresay it is not a pleasing fact to the Milauds of Nevers, but the second house of La Baudraye may yet have as noble a center as the first.—I shall most likely go to Paris and petition the King of the French to grant me the title of Count—Monsieur Roy is a Count—and my wife would be pleased to be Madame la Comtesse.”And this was said with such coolness that no one dared laugh. Only Monsieur Boirouge, the Presiding Judge, remarked: “In your place, I should not be happy unless I had a daughter.”“Well, I shall go to Paris before long—” said the Baron.In early 1842 Madame de la Baudraye, feeling she was to Lousteau no more than a reserve, had again sacrificed herself absolutely to secure his comfort; she had resumed black raiment, but now as mourning, for her pleasure was turning to remorse.
