She was too often shamed not to feel the chain; her mother found her sunk in moods of meditation into which visions of the future cast unhappy souls in a torpor.Madame Piedefer, by her spiritual director’s advice, watched for exhaustion, which the priest said would inevitably come, and then she pleaded for the children. She urged Dinah and Lousteau to live apart, not asking her to give him up. In real life violent situations do not close as in books—by death or contrived catastrophes; they end far less poetically—in disgust, the blighting of every flower of the soul, the commonplace of habit, or in another passion which robs a wife of interest. When common sense, social propriety, family interest, a sense of insults too offensive, the fatigue of constant self-sacrifice near exhaustion, and a too cruel blow—one of those mean acts a man never lets a woman know unless he believes himself master—put the crowning touch to her revulsion, the moment comes for intervention by the friend who undertakes the cure. Madame Piedefer had no difficulty now in removing the film from her daughter’s eyes.She sent for Monsieur de Clagny, who completed the work by assuring Madame de la Baudraye that if she gave up Etienne, her husband would allow her to keep the children, live in Paris, and restore her to the command of her fortune.“And what a life you are leading!” he said. “With care and judgment, the support of pious persons, you may have a salon and conquer a position. Paris is not Sancerre.”Dinah left it to Monsieur de Clagny to negotiate reconciliation with the old man.Monsieur de la Baudraye had sold his wine, wool, felled timber, and without telling his wife had come to Paris to invest two hundred thousand francs in a delightful residence in the Rue de l’Arcade being sold in liquidation. He had been a member of the Departmental Council since 1826, and now, paying ten thousand francs in taxes, was doubly qualified for a peerage under the new legislation.Some time before the elections of 1842 he had put himself forward as candidate unless called to the Upper House as Peer of France. He asked for the title of Count and promotion in the Legion of Honor. In the event of his being won over to the Government, Sancerre would be more of a royalist rotten borough. Monsieur de Clagny, whose talents and modesty were appreciated by the authorities, supported him, pointing out that raising this enterprising agriculturist to the peerage would guarantee such undertakings.Monsieur de la Baudraye, Count, Peer of France, and Commander of the Legion of Honor, was vain enough to wish to cut a figure with a wife and handsomely appointed house.—“He wanted to enjoy life,” he said.He therefore addressed a letter to his wife, dictated by Monsieur de Clagny, begging her to live under his roof and to furnish the house, giving play to the taste of which the evidences had charmed him at the Chateau d’Anzy. The newly made Count told his wife that while their property’s interests forbade his leaving Sancerre, the education of their boys required her presence in Paris. He asked Monsieur de Clagny to place sixty thousand francs at Madame la Comtesse’s disposal for interior decoration, requesting a marble tablet over the gateway with the inscription: Hotel de la Baudraye.He then accounted for Silas Piedefer’s money, told her of the investment at four and a half per cent of the eight hundred thousand francs he had brought from New York, and allowed her that income for expenses, including the children’s education. As he would be compelled to stay in Paris during the House of Peers sessions, he requested his wife reserve for him a suite of rooms in an entresol over the kitchens.“Bless me! why, he is growing young again—a gentleman—a magnifico—What will he become next? It is quite alarming,” said Madame de la Baudraye.“He now fulfils all your wishes at the age of twenty,” replied the lawyer.Comparing future prospects with present position was unendurable to Dinah. Only the day before, Anna de Fontaine had turned away to avoid seeing her at Chamarolles’ school.“I am a countess,” said Dinah to herself. “I shall have the peer’s blue hammer-cloth on my carriage, and the leaders of the literary world in my drawing-room—and I will look at her!”—This little triumph weighed heavily at the moment of her rehabilitation, as the world’s contempt had once weighed on her happiness.One fine day in May 1842 Madame de la Baudraye paid all small household debts and left a thousand crowns atop the packet of receipted bills. After sending her mother and the children to the Hotel de la Baudraye, she awaited Lousteau, dressed to leave. When Etienne came to dinner, she said:“I have upset the pot, my dear. Madame de la Baudraye requests the pleasure of your company at the Rocher de Cancale.”She carried off Lousteau, bewildering him with the light and easy manners assumed by the woman who till that morning had been his slave; she too had been acting a farce for two months.“Madame de la Baudraye is figged out as if for a first night,” said he.“Do not forget the respect you owe to Madame de la Baudraye,” said Dinah gravely. “I do not mean to understand such a word as figged out.”“Didine a rebel!” said he, putting his arm round her waist.“There is no such person as Didine; you have killed her, my dear,” she replied, releasing herself. “I am taking you to the first performance of Madame la Comtesse de la Baudraye.”“It is true, then, that our insect is a peer of France?”“The nomination is to be gazetted in this evening’s Moniteur, as Monsieur de Clagny tells me, who is promoted to the Court of Appeal.”“Well, it is right,” said the journalist. “The entomology of society ought to be represented in the Upper House.”“My friend, we are parting for ever,” said Madame de la Baudraye, trying to control trembling of voice. “I have dismissed the two servants. When you go in, you will find the house in order, and no debts. I shall always feel a mother’s affection for you, but in secret. Let us part calmly, without a fuss, like decent people.“Have you had a fault to find with my conduct during the past six years?”“None, but that you have spoiled my life, and wrecked my prospects,” he answered hard. “You have read Benjamin Constant very diligently; you have even studied the last critique; but you have read with a woman’s eyes. Though you have one of those superior intellects which would make a fortune of a poet, you have never dared to take the man’s point of view.“That book, my dear, is of both sexes.—We agreed that books were male or female, dark or fair. In Adolphe women see nothing but Ellenore; young men see only Adolphe; men of experience see Ellenore and Adolphe; political men see the whole of social existence. You did not think it necessary to read the soul of Adolphe—any more than your critic, who saw only Ellenore. What kills that poor fellow, my dear, is that he has sacrificed his future for a woman; that he never can be what he might have been—an ambassador, a minister, a chamberlain, a poet—and rich. He gives up six years of his energy when a man is ready to submit to apprenticeship—to a petticoat, which he outstrips in ingratitude, for the woman who has thrown over her first lover is certain sooner or later to desert the second. Adolphe is, in fact, a tow-haired German, who has not spirit enough to be false to Ellenore. There are Adolphes who spare their Ellenores all ignominious quarreling and reproaches, who say to themselves, ‘I will not talk of what I have sacrificed; I will not for ever be showing the stump of my wrist to let that incarnate selfishness I have made my queen,’ as Ramorny does in The Fair Maid of Perth. But men like that get cast aside.“Adolphe is a man of birth, an aristocratic nature, who wants to get back into the highroad to honors and recover his social birthright.—You, at this moment, are playing both parts. You are suffering from losing your position, and think yourself justified in throwing over a hapless lover whose misfortune it has been that he fancied you so far superior as to understand that, though a man’s heart ought to be true, his sex may be allowed to indulge its caprices.”“And do you suppose that I shall not make it my business to restore to you all you have lost by me? Be quite easy,” said Madame de la Baudraye, astounded by this attack. “Your Ellenore is not dying; and if God gives her life, if you amend your ways, if you give up courtesans and actresses, we will find you a better match than a Felicie Cardot.”The two lovers were sullen. Lousteau affected dejection; Dinah, really distressed, listened to her heart’s reproaches.“Why,” said Lousteau presently, “why not end as we ought to have begun—hide our love from all eyes, and see each other in secret?”“Never!” cried the new-made Countess, with an icy look. “Do you not comprehend that we are, after all, finite creatures? Our feelings seem infinite by reason of our anticipation of heaven, but here on earth they are limited by our physical strength. There are some feeble, mean natures which may receive an endless number of wounds and live on; but some highly-tempered souls snap at last under repeated blows. You have—”“Oh! enough!” cried he. “No more copy! Your dissertation is unnecessary, since you can justify yourself by merely saying—‘I have ceased to love!’”“What!” she exclaimed. “Is it I who have ceased to love?”“Certainly. You have calculated that I gave you more trouble, more vexation than pleasure, and you desert your partner—”“I desert!” cried she, clasping her hands.“Have not you yourself just said ‘Never’?”“Well, then, yes! Never,” she repeated vehemently.This final Never, spoken in fear of falling under Lousteau’s influence, he took as the death-warrant of his power, since Dinah remained insensible to his sarcastic scorn.The journalist could not suppress a tear. He was losing sincere affection. He had found in Dinah the gentlest La Valliere, the most delightful Pompadour that any egoist short of a king could hope for; and, like a boy who has killed a cockchafer by tormenting it, Lousteau shed a tear.Madame de la Baudraye rushed out of the dining room, paid the bill, and fled home to the Rue de l’Arcade, scolding herself and thinking herself a brute.Dinah, who had made her house a model of comfort, now metamorphosed herself. This cost thirty thousand francs more than her husband had anticipated.The fatal accident depriving the House of Orleans of the heir-presumptive in 1842 required the Chambers to meet in August; La Baudraye presented his titles to the Upper House sooner than expected, and then saw what his wife had done. He was so delighted that he paid the thirty thousand francs without a word, as he had formerly paid eight thousand for decorating La Baudraye.On his return from the Luxembourg, where he had been presented by two peers—the Baron de Nucingen and the Marquis de Montriveau—the new Count met the old Duc de Chaulieu walking along; the contrast filled his heart with a draught of the balm on which the middle class had been getting drunk since 1840.Madame de la Baudraye was shocked to see her husband improved and looking better than on the day of their marriage. The little dwarf, full of rapturous delight at sixty-four, triumphed in the life long denied him; in the family his handsome cousin Milaud of Nevers had said he would never have; and in his wife—who had asked Monsieur and Madame de Clagny to dinner to meet the parish cure and his two sponsors to the Chamber of Peers. He petted the children with fatuous delight.The handsome display on the table met his approval.“These are the fleeces of the Berry sheep,” he said, showing Monsieur de Nucingen the dish-covers surmounted by his coronet. “They are of silver, you see!”Though consumed by melancholy, which she concealed with the determination of a superior woman, Dinah was charming, witty, and young again in her court mourning.“You might declare,” cried La Baudraye to Monsieur de Nucingen with a wave to his wife, “that the Countess was not yet thirty.”“Ah, ha! Matame is a voman of dirty!” replied the baron, prone to time-honored remarks.“In every sense,” replied the Countess. “I am, in fact, five-and-thirty, and mean to set up a little passion—”“Oh, yes, my wife ruins me in curiosities and china images—”“She started that mania,” said the Marquis with a smile.“Yes,” said La Baudraye, cold at the Marquis, “you know that in ‘25, ‘26, and ‘27, she picked a million francs’ worth of treasures. Anzy is a perfect museum.”“What a cool hand!” thought Monsieur de Clagny, seeing the country miser on the level of his position.But misers have savings for use.On the day after the Regency vote passed, the little Count went back to Sancerre for the vintage and resumed old habits.That winter the Comtesse, supported by the Attorney-General to the Court of Appeals, tried to form a little circle. She had an “at home” day, selected men of mark, receiving those of serious purpose and ripe years. She went to the Opera twice a week with her mother and Madame de Clagny, who visited Dinah at her husband’s insistence. Still, despite cleverness, charming manners, and stylishness, she was never really happy but with her children, on whom she lavished disappointed affection.Worthy Monsieur de Clagny tried to recruit women for the Countess’ circle, and he succeeded among advocates of piety more than women of fashion.“And they bore her!” said he to himself with horror, seeing his idol matured by grief, pale from remorse, then restored by luxury and care for her boys. This devoted friend, encouraged by her mother and the cure, was full of expedients. Every Wednesday he introduced some celebrity from Germany, England, Italy, or Prussia to the dear Countess; he spoke of her as an exceptional woman to people she hardly addressed two words; but she listened with such deep attention that they went away convinced of her superiority. In Paris, Dinah conquered by silence as at Sancerre she had conquered by loquacity. Now and then a smart saying or a sarcasm betrayed a woman used to deal with ideas—the one who, four years since, had given new life to Lousteau’s articles.This phase was to the hapless lawyer like the Indian summer after a sunless year. He affected to be older than he was, to have the right to befriend Dinah without injury, and kept at a distance as though he were young, handsome, and compromising, like a man who has happiness to conceal. He tried to keep attentions secret, the trifling gifts Dinah showed to everyone; he suggested a dangerous meaning for his little services.“He plays at passion,” said the Countess, laughing. She made fun of Monsieur de Clagny to his face, and he thought, “She notices me.”One evening Monsieur de Clagny and his wife were taking Dinah home from the theatre; she was pensive. They had seen Leon Gozlan’s La Main Droite et la Main Gauche.“What are you thinking about?” asked the lawyer, alarmed at her dejection.This deep melancholy, though disguised, was a perilous malady for which Clagny knew no remedy; for true love is often clumsy, especially when not reciprocated. True love takes expression from character. Now, this good man loved like Alceste, when Madame de la Baudraye wanted love like Philinte. The meaner side of love can never suit the Misanthrope’s loyalty. Thus Dinah had taken care never to open her heart to him. How could she confess that sometimes she regretted the slough she had left?She felt a void in fashionable life; she had no one for whom to dress, or whom to tell of her successes. Sometimes memory of wretchedness came, mingled with memories of consuming joys. She would hate Lousteau for not following her; she wished for tender or furious letters from him.Dinah made no reply, so Clagny repeated the question, taking her hand and pressing it with devout respect.“Will you have the right hand or the left?” said she, smiling.“The left,” said he, “for I suppose you mean the truth or a fib.”“Well, then, I saw him,” she said into the lawyer’s ear. “And as I saw him looking so sad, so out of heart, I said to myself, Has he a cigar? Has he any money?”“If you wish the truth, I can tell it,” said he. “He is living as a husband with Fanny Beaupre. You have forced me to tell you this secret; I should never have told you, for you might have suspected me perhaps of an ungenerous motive.”Madame de la Baudraye grasped his hand.“Your husband,” she said to her chaperon, “is one of the rarest souls!—Ah! Why—”She shrank into her corner, looking out, but did not finish the sentence Clagny could guess: “Why had not Lousteau a little of your husband’s generosity?”This information cured Dinah of melancholy; she threw herself into the whirl of fashion. She wished for success, and she achieved it; still she did not make much way with women and found introductions difficult.In March, Madame Piedefer’s friends—the priests and Monsieur de Clagny—got Madame de la Baudraye appointed receiver of subscriptions for Madame de Carcado’s charitable work. She was commissioned to collect Royal Family donations for Guadeloupe earthquake sufferers. The Marquise d’Espard, hearing of the Countess’s commission, said: “I have lived a long time in the world, and I can remember nothing finer than the manoeuvres undertaken for the rehabilitation of Madame de la Baudraye.”In early spring, by whim of our planets, Paris smiled in March 1843, making the Champs-Elysees green before Longchamp. Fanny Beaupre’s attache had seen Madame de la Baudraye several times without being seen by her. Stung to the heart by prompts of jealousy and envy familiar to provincials, he beheld his former mistress in a handsome carriage, well dressed, with dreamy eyes, and his two little boys, one at each window. He accused himself the more because he was warring with sharp poverty—poverty unconfessed. Like light, frivolous natures, he cherished a point of honor: never to derogate in the eyes of one’s little public, which drives some men on the Bourse to crime to avoid expulsion from Per-cent’s temple, and has made some criminals perform acts of virtue.Lousteau dined and smoked as if rich. Not for an inheritance would he buy anything but the dearest cigars; he took walks in patent-leather boots; but he feared an execution on goods that had already received the last sacrament. Fanny had nothing left to pawn; her salary was pledged. After exhausting advances from newspapers and publishers, Etienne knew not what ink could churn gold. Gambling houses, suppressed, no longer cashed IOUs. The journalist was reduced to such extremity that he had borrowed a hundred francs of poorest friend Bixiou, whom he’d never asked for a franc. What distressed him was not owing five thousand francs but seeing himself bereft of elegance, of furniture bought by privation and added to by Madame de la Baudraye.On April 3 a yellow poster announced the sale of a handsome suite of furniture the following Saturday. Lousteau was walking, smoking, seeking ideas—for in Paris ideas are in the air—yet he found nothing but friends who carried him to dinner and intoxicating champagne.“Beware,” said the virulent Bixiou one night—the man who would give a hundred francs and stab a comrade with sarcasm—“if you go to sleep drunk every night, one day you will wake up mad.”On the Friday the unhappy wretch felt condemned. He might once have said, “The furniture is old! I will buy new,” but he was incapable now of literary legerdemain. Publishers paid poorly; newspapers bargained hard; Opera managers did with tenors as they pleased.He walked with a cigar and affected smile. Then he saw Madame de la Baudraye pass in a carriage; she was going to drive in the Bois.“There is nothing else left!” he said to himself, and went home to smarten.That evening, at seven, he arrived in a hackney at Madame de la Baudraye’s door, and begged the porter to send a note up—a few lines:“Would Madame la Comtesse do Monsieur Lousteau the favor of receiving him for a moment, and at once?”This note bore the seal they both had used. The Countess had the word Parce que engraved on a carnelian—a potent, accounting word.She had just finished dressing for the Opera; at the sight of the seal she turned pale.“I will come,” she said, tucking the note into her dress.Firm to conceal agitation, she begged her mother to put the children to bed and sent for Lousteau, receiving him in a boudoir with open doors. She wore brocaded brocade in pale blue stripes; gloves trimmed with tassels set off white arms; hair a la Sevigne; pearls lay on her bosom like bubbles.“What is the matter, monsieur?” she asked, putting out a foot to rest on a velvet cushion. “I thought, I hoped, I was quite forgotten.”“If I should reply Never, you would refuse to believe me,” said Lousteau, standing, chewing flowers he plucked.For a moment silence reigned. Dinah saw he was dressed as a fastidious dandy.“You are the only person in the world who can help me, or hold out a plank—I am drowning and have swallowed more than one mouthful,” he said. “Since you see me here, it is because my affairs are going to the devil.”“That is enough,” said she; “I understand.”He turned away, took out his handkerchief, seemed to wipe a tear.“How much do you want, Etienne?” she went on in motherly tones. “We are at this moment old comrades; speak to me as you would to—Bixiou.”“To save my furniture from vanishing to-morrow at the auction, eighteen hundred francs! To repay my friends, as much again! Three quarters’ rent—My ‘uncle’ wants five hundred francs—”“And you—to live on?”“Oh! I have my pen—”“It is heavier to lift than anyone who reads your articles believes,” she said with a subtle smile.—“I have not such a sum, but come to-morrow at eight; the bailiff will surely wait till nine, especially if you bring him away to pay him.”She must dismiss Lousteau, who affected inability to look; she felt pity that might cut every social Gordian knot.“Thank you,” she added, rising and offering her hand. “Your confidence has done me good! It is long since my heart has known such joy—”Lousteau took her hand and pressed it to his heart.“A drop of water in the desert—and sent by the hand of an angel! God always does things handsomely!”He spoke half jest, half pathos; as acting it was as fine as Talma’s Leicester. Dinah felt his heart beat through his coat; it throbbed with satisfaction and fire at seeing her rejuvenescent restored by wealth.Stealing a glance, Dinah saw that his expression matched flowers of love which had blossomed in his heart; she tried to look into his eyes, but blood rushed to her face. Their eyes met with the same fiery glow as when Lousteau had encouraged himself on the Quay to crumple Dinah’s muslin. The Bohemian put his arm round her waist; she yielded; their cheeks touched.“Here comes my mother, hide!” cried Dinah and hurried to intercept Madame Piedefer.“Mamma,” said she—this coaxing word always enthralled the stern old lady—“will you do me a great favor? Take the carriage and go to my banker, Monsieur Mongenod, with a note I will give you, and bring back six thousand francs. Come, come—it is an act of charity; come into my room.”She dragged away her mother, anxious to see who was in the boudoir.Two days afterwards Madame Piedefer consulted the parish cure. After hearing the old mother’s lamentations, the priest said gravely:“Any moral regeneration not based on strong religious sentiment and carried out in the Church is built on sand.—The many means of grace enjoined by Catholicism are so many dams necessary to restrain evil promptings. Persuade your daughter to perform all her duties, and we shall save her yet.”Within ten days the Hotel de la Baudraye was shut. The Countess, the children, her mother, and household, including a tutor, had gone to Sancerre, where Dinah intended to spend the summer. She was everything that was nice to the Count, people said.And so the Muse of Sancerre had simply come back to family and married life; but evil tongues declared she had been compelled to come back, for the little peer’s wishes would no doubt be fulfilled—he hoped for a little girl.Gatien and Monsieur Gravier lavished care and servile attention on the handsome Countess. Gatien, who had been to Paris to learn lionnerie, was supposed to have a good chance of favor. Others bet on the tutor; Madame Piedefer urged religion.In June 1844, as the Comte took a walk on the Mall at Sancerre with the two fine little boys, he met Monsieur Milaud the Public Prosecutor and said, “These are my children, cousin.”“Ah, ha! so these are our children!” replied the lawyer with a twinkle.PARIS, June 1843–August 1844.ADDENDUMThe following personages appear in other stories of the Human Comedy.Beaupre, Fanny — A Start in Life; Modeste Mignon; Scenes from a Courtesan’s LifeBerthier, Madame (Felicie Cardot) — Cousin PonsBianchon, Horace — Father Goriot; The Atheist’s Mass; Cesar Birotteau; The Commission in Lunacy; Lost Illusions; A Distinguished Provincial at Paris; A Bachelor’s Establishment; The Secrets of a Princess; The Government Clerks; Pierrette; A Study of Woman; Scenes from a Courtesan’s Life; Honorine; The Seamy Side of History; The Magic Skin; A Second Home; A Prince of Bohemia; Letters of Two Brides; The Imaginary Mistress; The Middle Classes; Cousin Betty; The Country Parson. ONE OF MANY CHEVALIERS DE VALOISMany provinces in France could boast a Chevalier de Valois. One lived in Normandy, another at Bourges, and a third—the present one—flourished in Alencon. They were little known to one another. If any Valois remained they came from Charles de Valois, Duc d’Angouleme; the male line, until proved otherwise, had ended in the Abbe de Rothelin. The Valois-Saint-Remy, descending from Henri II, ended in the Lamothe-Valois implicated in the Diamond Necklace affair.Like his fellows, the Chevalier of Alencon was an old gentleman—tall, thin, withered and nearly penniless. He had spent his youth in Paris, reached thirty amid conquests and gallantries when the Revolution came, and was accepted by the provincial aristocracy as a true Valois. He distinguished himself by manners: he dined out daily, played cards each evening, and told anecdotes of Louis XV’s reign and the Revolution’s beginnings that seemed witty at first hearing. He never repeated his bons mots, never spoke of his love affairs, and, while lax about mass, bore a royalist devotion that secured indulgence for his irreligion.His manner—perhaps borrowed from Mole—included taking snuff from a gold box adorned with the portrait of the Princess Goritza, a Hungarian for whom he had once fought a duel. At fifty-eight he owned to only fifty; his thin, fair figure helped preserve a youthful air. A single enormous nose divided a pale face: the left side reddened by digestion, the right remained white. He ate like an ogre and blamed a “hot liver,” perhaps to excuse his appetite; in a region of long meals his stomach seemed providential. Physicians said heat on the left denotes a prodigal heart; his gallantries agreed.His constitution remained vigorous; his face bore the furrows of pleasure—crow’s-feet and marches-du-palais—signs of passion. He was fastidious in dress and ablutions: fine white linen, a clean though half-worn coat, a maroon coat with gilt buttons, half-tight breeches, a plain white waistcoat, a tight cravat hiding the shirt-collar, square-buckled shoes, and two watch-chains. His hair and brows were combed to preserve youth; part of his skull shone like ivory. His hands were delicate. Were it not for his magisterial nose he might seem too dainty.He had oddities: cotton in his ears and two little diamond ear-rings shaped as negroes' heads, which he claimed cured headaches. These were forgiven because of his graces. He labored to conceal his age and give pleasure, comporting himself with an old-marquis ease now rare since Mole’s disappearance.His private life seemed open yet remained mysterious. He lodged modestly on the second floor of Madame Lardot’s house in the rue du Cours, paying a hundred francs a year for two rooms. He dined out daily and returned only to sleep; at home he breakfasted on chocolate, bread and butter, and seasonal fruit. He made no fire except in severe weather. Between eleven and four he walked, read papers, and paid visits. He pretended poverty—his whole fortune an annuity of six hundred francs, the remainder of old opulence—and visited his man of business quarterly. In truth a banker paid him one hundred and fifty francs every three months, remitted by Monsieur Bordin of Paris, the last of the procureurs du Chatelet. The chevalier demanded secrecy.Monsieur de Valois was welcome in Alencon’s best houses. His skill at cards, his stories, amiability, and approving grimace made gatherings feel incomplete without him. Young women treasured his compliments; his old-fashioned diminutives—“my heart,” “my jewel,” “my little pet”—had a grace few resisted. At cards he never complained, praised opponents when they lost, and with a snuff-box gesture silenced tedious dissertations on play. He tolerated poor players and made the best of them. His equability won admiration: “I do admire the Chevalier de Valois!”To grisettes he was paternal and flattering; he supplied trifles—chocolate, bonbons, ribbons—and they returned secret attachments and discretion. Mornings he amused himself with their gossip—his “petticoat gazettes.” Despite small winnings—he left salons with about six francs—he was the town’s darling.Yet there was more to his means. After the “glorious” sad days of July, some discovered his nightly winnings were about one hundred and fifty francs every three months and that he had the pluck to send himself his annuity to avoid appearing wholly destitute. Friends defended him as respectable and calumniated. Over about fifteen years he had amassed ten thousand and some odd hundred francs. With the Bourbon return an old friend, the Marquis de Pombreton, allegedly repaid twelve hundred pistoles lent long ago; this allowed him to consult moneyed friends and invest on the Grand-Livre at the right moment. He obtained, through unnamed influential persons, a pension of three hundred francs from the king’s privy purse and the cross of Saint-Louis, permitting him to take rank as a retired colonel for his service in the Catholic armies of the West.Thus, besides the fiction of his annuity, he had an honest income of a thousand francs. He changed little in lifestyle; the red ribbon of the cross completed his physiognomy. After 1802 he sealed letters with an old family seal and bore arms: Party of France, with mascles and a cross; motto: “Valeo.” Many envied his tranquil life—whist, boston, backgammon, reversi, piquet; snuff gracefully inhaled; tranquil walks. But he harbored an ambition: to marry a certain rich old maid, using her as a stepping-stone to the court. That explained his royal bearing and residence in Alencon.CHAPTER II. SUSANNAH AND THE ELDERSOne Wednesday morning in mid-spring the chevalier, dressing in his old green-flowered damask gown, heard the light step of a young girl climb the stair. Three taps, and without waiting she slipped into his room.“Ah! is it you, Suzanne?” said the chevalier, still stropping his razor. “What have you come for, my dear little jewel of mischief?”“I have come to tell you something which may perhaps give you as much pleasure as pain.”“Is it anything about Cesarine?”“Cesarine! much I care about your Cesarine!” she answered saucily.Suzanne was a work-girl at Madame Lardot’s. The wash-rooms occupied the ground floor; the tiny courtyard dried the fine linen of the town’s best families. The chevalier, claiming to know love-affairs from the linen’s condition, was discreet; he was never betrayed into epigrams that might close a salon to him. He frequented the work-room, bestowing trifles on his “little ducks,” who adored him. Women have an instinct for men who love them and ask nothing in return; the chevalier’s gallant protection—an echo of the seigneurs’ ways—made him beloved. He could no longer ruin himself for a mistress, so he gave toffees instead of bank-bills; they accepted gladly. They made him seem older and more respectable in public, but among themselves they perched on his shoulders like parrots. He enjoyed their household gossip and was amused by their “talking feuilletons” at breakfast.Suzanne was one of his favorites—clever, ambitious, handsome—reminiscent of a Sophie Arnould. She had a Norman, rubenesque beauty.“Well, my child, tell me your great or little adventure,” said the chevalier.He listened with a paternal manner; the shabby room—threadbare tapestry, tavern-paper profiles of Louis XVI and his family—exhaled an eighteenth-century air; libertine graces of his youth reappeared. He draped Suzanne before him, holding her legs between his knees; she indulged him but could be offish in the street.“We slander ourselves,” he said craftily; “we are as virtuous as that biblical girl whose name we bear; we can always marry as we please, but we are thirsty for Paris. We want to go see if the great capital hasn’t some young Chevalier de Valois with a carriage, diamonds, an opera-box.” He counseled her to go to Paris at an old bachelor’s expense—“an old bachelor is the natural money-box of a young girl”—but warned: “don’t drag me into it. I haven’t a penny to get you out of trouble. If I marry Mademoiselle Cormon and become rich, of course I would prefer you.”“Monsieur le chevalier, I assure you, you are mistaken,” she began, blushing; the chevalier had already guessed her plan.“Yes, yes! I understand: you want me to believe it. Well! I do believe it. But take my advice: go to Monsieur du Bousquier. Don’t you take linen there? He’s an old bachelor and very rich; if you’re clever you can go to Paris at his expense. Twist him round your finger; he fears scandal and is ambitious.” At these words Suzanne hurried off—to du Bousquier.Du Bousquier belonged to an old Alencon family, had gone to Paris, engaged in public business, and made a fortune as commissary of provisions to the armies from 1793 to 1799. He lived magnificently, was intimate with Barras and Fouche, and had aimed for ministerial rank by supporting a secret party until Marengo. His speculations failed when Kellermann and Desaix altered events; Napoleon disfavored contractors who had speculated on his defeat, and du Bousquier was ruined. From his past he retained only twelve hundred francs a year placed on the Grand Livre. He returned to Alencon furious with Bonaparte and was received among royalists though shunned by the leading nobility. He had offered himself to Mademoiselle Armande and been refused. Now middle-aged, he was plump, with strong calves and a broad chest, but his voice was stifled, as if cutting moist wood. He favored the liberal party in Alencon, manipulating elections and harming the Restoration; his hatred of royalists was patient and inexhaustible.The chevalier had turned Suzanne upon du Bousquier because the two old bachelors were secret rivals for the hand of Mademoiselle Cormon. Though their designs were discreet, both hoped for this wealthy old maid. The chevalier had never asked her hand; du Bousquier had and been refused. Their war was courteous in the chevalier’s manner, less so in du Bousquier’s, yet they met nearly every evening on the same ground.Suzanne tripped from the rue du Cours to du Bousquier’s house in the rue du Cygne, where he had bought a grey Jura-stone house about five years before. It preserved some furniture and decorations of his former splendor; provincial neglect made the whole a jumble—magnificence marred by dirt. He lived as a man of leisure with a single Breton servant, Rene, a ninny trained to rub floors and black boots; Rene was a glutton and a trusty confidant.“’Tisn’t your day. We haven’t any linen for the wash,” Rene grunted when Suzanne entered. She left him and went upstairs.Du Bousquier, in bed, listened as Suzanne sat swinging curtain-rings.“What is it, my charmer?” he said, sitting up.“Monsieur,” she said gravely, “I know how ridiculous it is in a poor girl to nag a man. But I can be of use to you if you’ll attach yourself to me as I am to you. If we went to Paris you’d see what I could do with a man of your mind and capacity; they are remaking the government. Perhaps this misfortune is a piece of luck. Who and what are you working for now?”“For myself, of course!” cried du Bousquier.“Monster! you’ll never be a father!” she retorted.Du Bousquier, torn between joy, distrust, and calculation, had long planned to marry Mademoiselle Cormon for her property and social standing. That marriage would have advanced his political career; but Suzanne’s sudden offer bewildered him. Without it, he might have married her and led the liberal party openly; with it, he hesitated. He rubbed his night-cap and thought. Finally: “I’m not to be taken in with such bosh, not I!”—the proud remark of a man who prided himself on never being duped by women.“Remember, Monsieur du Bousquier,” Suzanne replied, “I have offered you my hand with dignity. I shan’t beg. I can find other husbands. But I cannot stay in Alencon: my mother would beat me, Madame Lardot would turn me off. If I go to Paris I must have money for the journey and for—you know what.”This struck du Bousquier more than the chevalier; had she truly been desperate she would have braved a thousand deaths before asking. “Will you really go to Paris, then?” he asked.“Yes, monsieur.”Du Bousquier lamented his debts—payments on the house, bills for painter and upholsterer. He offered three hundred francs. Suzanne pretended to depart.“Stop, where are you going?” said du Bousquier uneasily. “The devil take me if I ever did anything more than rumple her collar, and, lo and behold! she makes THAT a ground to put her hand in one’s pocket!”“I’m going to Madame Granson, treasurer of the Maternity Society,” Suzanne said. “They save many poor girls in my condition from suicide.”“Madame Granson!” Du Bousquier, alarmed, gave her a key and told her to take from his secretary a bag containing about six hundred francs—“it is all I possess.”“If you deceive me, Suzanne,” he cried as she handled the sack.“Monsieur,” she answered impertinently, “wouldn’t you have given me money if I had asked for it?” Recalled to gallantry, du Bousquier kissed her; she left, cursing his stinginess. She hid the sack in an osier gamebag, already thinking that the Maternity Society, presided over by Mademoiselle Cormon, might be induced to complete the sum she had calculated for Paris—one thousand francs—and, in revenge for du Bousquier’s parsimony, perhaps to entangle him in scandal. With a mind quick to mischief, she went to Madame Granson, composing an expression of deepest dejection.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Madame Granson, widow of a lieutenant-colonel of artillery killed at Jena, lived on a meagre pension of nine hundred francs and three hundred francs from a small property; her savings had been spent educating and supporting a son. Their lodging in the rue du Bercail was a modest ground-floor apartment typical of a provincial town: a street door above three steep steps, a passage to an interior courtyard, a gallery staircase, a dining-room and kitchen on one side, and a multifunctional salon that served as the widow’s bedroom on the other.Athanase Granson, twenty-three, slept in an attic and added six hundred francs to his mother’s income through a post in the mayor’s office, obtained via his relation Mademoiselle Cormon; he was placed in charge of the archives. Madame Granson, plainly dressed and seated beneath a portrait of the lieutenant-colonel, kept strict provincial decorum while watching the street. At the moment mother and son breakfasted simply—coffee, bread and butter, radishes. To understand how much Suzanne’s visit would mean to Madame Granson, certain private motives of both mother and son must be explained.Athanase was thin, pale, of medium height, with a hollow face and two black eyes like bits of coal—features that suggested repressed talent. Irregular lines, a prominent chin, a well-shaped forehead and the melancholy cast of poverty hinted at an imprisoned spirit. In any place but Alençon his appearance might have attracted aid from those who recognize ability in obscurity. He was contemplative; timidity and the chill of provincial life stifled action. Poverty intensified a savage pride in him—noble but obstructive at life’s outset. He belonged to the kind of talent that doubts and is easily discouraged: more thinker than actor, liable to sudden resolves others might call madness.Contempt for poverty crushed Athanase; solitude enervated him; without encouragement his powers languished. His library work passed unnoticed; he kept his ambitions hidden, fearing they might harm him. Beneath all lay a secret passion that hollowed his cheeks: he loved his distant cousin, Mademoiselle Cormon, whom the Chevalier de Valois and du Bousquier also pursued. His love began in calculation—Cormon was thought rich—and he imagined marriage would provide the steady livelihood to free him for fame and secure his mother’s comfort. That motive degraded his feeling in his own eyes; he also feared ridicule for a young man of twenty-three loving an “old maid” of forty.Yet in provincial life such a marriage could be genuine. Where manners are fixed and opportunities few, marriage is often a necessity; improvident liaisons alarm provincial parents and ruin a young man’s prospects. A poor artist or scholar cannot rely on the provinces to marry for anything but material comfort; the aim to combine love and sustenance is usually denied. Athanase’s initial practical motive turned into real passion as he studied Cormon and, by the charm of habit, began to see only her perfections.For a young man the senses are powerful; in Athanase’s isolation Mademoiselle Cormon became the single figure presented to him, concentrating all light upon her. His secret feeling broadened and deepened silently, swelling hour by hour, and with it his timidity.Madame Granson suspected the truth and calculated candidly. She saw advantages in the match: a talented young husband would honor the family and neighborhood. She judged the obstacles—his poverty and Cormon’s age—formidable but surmountable by patience. Like other provincials, she watched for propitious circumstances and quietly plotted. She distrusted du Bousquier but concealed her tactics from her son.Thus Suzanne’s lie, confided to Madame Granson, became a powerful weapon in the widow’s hands. As treasurer of the Maternity Society, Madame Granson could spread news under the guise of charity while gathering support for “the chaste Suzanne,” using the scandal to wound du Bousquier. The Chevalier de Valois anticipated how events might turn but could not foresee the scandal they would become.At breakfast Athanase, leaning on his elbow, twirled his spoon and looked about the poor room—red brick floor, straw chairs, a painted wooden buffet, and chequered curtains. His pallid face, framed in black hair and lit by the window, was the first thing Suzanne saw when she entered. Suzanne, a grisette familiar with misery and heartache, felt an instantaneous electric attraction—an inexplicable flash that convinces one, “It is he!”—a presentiment of shared love. Often reason quells such moments, but here Suzanne was struck by a genuine feeling that dispersed the cynicism of her dissipated life. What had seemed a joke the night before now seemed to her a grave misdeed; she recoiled at her own readiness to deceive. Yet practical considerations—the young man’s poverty, hopes of enriching herself, perhaps destiny—checked this sentiment.She asked for a private interview with Madame Granson, who led her to the bedchamber. When Suzanne emerged and looked at Athanase again, tears came to her eyes. Madame Granson was radiant: she had at last a terrible weapon against du Bousquier and promised the seduced girl the support of charitable ladies, foreseeing a storm over the guilty man’s head.“My dear child,” said Madame Granson to her son, “we dine to-night with Mademoiselle Cormon; do take pains with your appearance. Put on that handsome frilled shirt and your green coat of Elbeuf cloth. I have my reasons.” She urged him to study the Chevalier de Valois’s easy manners and to soften his stiff air. Athanase listened, surprised but obedient; he rose, took his cap, and went to the mayor’s office, wondering if his mother suspected his secret.He walked past the rue du Val-Noble, where Mademoiselle Cormon lived—a daily pleasure—and fancied himself beside her: how little she knew that a young man loved her, would be faithful, let her manage her fortune, and yet could not speak out. “Suppose I were to speak to her this evening?” he thought.Suzanne returned to her mother’s, thinking of Athanase, and, like many who long to help an adored man beyond their powers, considered using her body as a stepping-stone for his advancement. Her falsehood had achieved its purpose: a few days later she left town with money and good clothes, including a green reps dress and a green bonnet lined with pink given by Monsieur de Valois—a gift she preferred even to money—and set off joyfully for Paris. Alençon lamented her misfortunes as the ladies of the Charity and Maternity Societies showed lively sympathy. Though Suzanne belonged to the class absorbed by Paris, she remained in the more decent strata of gallantry; in her time, as Monsieur de Valois said, “Woman no longer existed,” yet she styled herself “Madame du Val-Noble.” One distinguished Restoration journalist who shaped public opinion took her under his protection and might marry her.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In nearly all the smaller French prefectures there exists a salon that gathers the notable, well-considered people of the community who are not the very highest in society. The master and mistress of such an establishment are among the town’s leaders, received wherever they choose to visit; no public fête, formal or diplomatic dinner occurs without them. But the château people—the heads of great estates, the highest personages—do not go to their houses; social intercourse between them is maintained by cards and occasional reciprocal dinners or soirées.This salon, where the lesser nobility, clergy, and magistracy meet, exerts great influence. Judgment and mind of the region reside in that solid, unostentatious society, where each man knows his neighbor’s resources and where indifference to luxury and dress prevails—trifles compared to securing ten or twelve acres of pasture, often the subject of long schemes. Immoveable in its prejudices, this circle follows a beaten track, accepting nothing from Paris without long trial; it rejects cashmeres as it rejects speculative investments; it scoffs at fashions and novelties, reads little, and is indifferent to science, literature, or industrial inventions. It insists on removing a prefect who does not suit it; if administration resists, it isolates him, like bees walling up a snail in wax.Gossip in this society often becomes solemn verdict. Young women seldom appear there; when they do it is to seek approbation of their conduct—a consecration of self-importance. The supremacy of one house wounds other natives, who console themselves by calculating the profit from its entertainments. If no fortune large enough exists to keep an open house, the leading people choose a neutral meeting-place, as at Alençon, where the public salon convenes in the residence of an inoffensive old maid whose settled life offends no one.For years the upper classes of Alençon met at the house of Mademoiselle Rose‑Marie‑Victoire Cormon. Her fortune, unknown to her, was coveted by Madame Granson, her second cousin, and by two old bachelors whose hopes we have seen: the Chevalier de Valois and the Chevalier du Bousquier. Rose lived with her maternal uncle, the venerable Abbé de Sponde, once her guardian and now her heir. The Cormon family had been among the province’s most considerable bourgeois families, allied with nobility—stewards to the Duc d’Alençon, magistrates, bishops among their members. Her maternal grandfather, Monsieur de Sponde, had been elected to the States‑General by the Nobility; her father, Monsieur Cormon, by the Tiers‑État, though neither accepted the mission. For a century daughters of the family had married provincial nobles, and the Cormons appeared in many genealogies; no bourgeois family seemed so near nobility.The house where Mademoiselle Cormon lived had always belonged to the family, built in Henry IV’s time by Pierre Cormon, steward to the last Duc d’Alençon. Though it produced little revenue and was costly to maintain, such an aristocratic private dwelling in a provincial town naturally attracted envy and temptation. The mansion, solid and ancient, stood in rue du Val‑Noble. Built of granite with diamond‑cut blocks, it had one clear story above the ground floor and a steep roof with projecting windows framed in oak. Two gables were surmounted by leaden bouquets, once a bourgeois symbol of status. Stables, coach‑house, kitchens, and laundry flanked the courtyard.A side of the porte‑cochère opened to a modest courtyard garden—roses, pinks, lilies, Spanish broom, pots of pomegranate and myrtle in season—kept with a scrupulous, almost Dutch neatness, betraying the life of an old maid who, lacking other occupation, introduced meticulous order into a region proud of professing indifference to comfort. The Chevalier de Valois or du Bousquier, mounting the double stairway, thought the house fit for a peer of France and that the mayor ought to live there.A glass door from the portico led into an antechamber—a red‑tiled, wainscoted gallery housing family portraits, cloaks, clogs, overshoes, umbrellas, and pelisses of guests; benches lined the walls for servants and a stove countered the north wind. The house was divided into two equal parts. Toward the courtyard were the staircase well, a large dining‑room overlooking the garden, and a pantry communicating with the kitchen. On the other side was the salon with four windows and two smaller rooms—a boudoir looking on the garden, and an office looking on the courtyard. The upper floor contained a full family apartment and a suite where the Abbé de Sponde lived. Garrets housed rats and mice whose nocturnal restlessness Mademoiselle Cormon lamented with comic surprise. The garden, about half an acre, bordered the shallow Brillante river, though near Val‑Noble its waters were stained by dyehouses and loaded with refuse; beyond rose the houses of various trades. Tall pottery vases and trimmed linden hedges completed the modest, tranquil setting around the venerable Cormon edifice: nothing pretentious, but nothing transitory.The ground floor, wholly devoted to reception, embodied the provincial spirit. The great square salon had four windows, modestly cased in gray woodwork. A single oblong mirror above the fireplace bore a decorative Dawn led by the Hours; windows were draped with green damask curtains looped by heavy cords. Furniture covered with tapestry, painted woodwork in fashions of a prior century, chairs with La Fontaine scenes, some mended tapestry—everything showed preservation rather than novelty. A huge beam divided the ceiling; an old rock‑crystal chandelier hung swathed in green gauze. On the mantel were Sèvres‑blue vases, old girandoles, and a clock whose bronze‑gilt group illustrated Sedaine’s popular Le Déserteur: soldiers bringing a deserter and a fainting young woman, displaying a taste for moralized decoration. Portraits by Rigaud and pastels by Latour hung on the walls. Four card tables, a backgammon board, and a piquet table occupied the room—the only ceiled chamber in the house.The dining‑room, paved in black‑and‑white stone with painted beams, contained an enormous marble‑topped sideboard necessary for the provincial war on the stomach. Frescoed walls, varnished cane seats, and plain wood doors gave it a patriarchal air. The provincial genius preserves everything: nothing is new or decrepit; a cold precision pervades the house.This Cormon house is an archetype of burgher houses in Normandy, Brittany, Maine, and Anjou and helps explain manners and customs. Life within was calm and monotonously regular. A library below river level housed well‑bound, dusty books, chosen from regional presses; transport costs limited purchases to desirable, well‑selected works.Mademoiselle Cormon’s society numbered about one hundred and fifty persons. Some visited country estates or traveled, but many came every night unless elsewhere invited, with others present by duty or habit; all were mature in age; few had traveled; most had spent their lives in the provinces, and some had taken part in the chouannerie. The latter were beginning to speak openly of that war now that rewards were being distributed to defenders of the cause. Monsieur de Valois, a mover in the last uprising, had recently related stratagems used against Hulot, an old republican commander stationed at Alençon.Women in this society took little trouble with dress except on Wednesdays, when Mademoiselle Cormon gave a dinner and guests invited the previous Wednesday returned their “visit of digestion.” Wednesdays were gala days; the assembly was numerous; visitors appeared in ribbons; some women brought sewing or knitting; young girls made patterns for Alençon point lace to pay their expenses. Certain husbands attended out of policy; young men were few; whispers were impossible without attracting attention, so there was no danger of clandestine love‑making.Every evening at six the antechamber filled. Each habitué brought cane, cloak, and lantern. Habits were patriarchal: if the Abbé de Sponde lay down, or Mademoiselle Cormon retired, neither Josette the maid nor Jacquelin the man‑servant nor Mariette the cook informed them; the first comer received the second, and whist, piquet, or boston began when company sufficed, without awaiting the Abbé or Mademoiselle. If dark, Josette or Jacquelin lit candles at the first bell; seeing the salon lighted, the abbé would slowly descend. Backgammon and piquet, three boston tables, and the whist table filled—twenty‑five or thirty persons; occasionally forty appeared. Between eight and nine servants accompanied their masters home; short of a revolution no one remained after ten. Guests departed in groups, discoursing on the game or continuing conversations on land, wills, quarrels among heirs, or aristocratic haughtiness—much like Parisians leaving a theatre.Provincial life’s faithful repetition may seem happiness to some. Du Bousquier, the society’s born statistician, estimated the salon’s habitués commanded 131 votes in the electoral college and mustered eighteen hundred thousand francs a year from nearby landed estates.Alençon’s society was not entirely represented by this salon; higher aristocracy had its own circles, and the receiver‑general’s house acted like a government inn where society danced, plotted, flirted, and supped. These salons communicated through mixed individuals with Mademoiselle Cormon’s house, which nevertheless sat in judgment on the others, criticizing their dinners’ luxury, their ices at balls, and the women’s behavior and fashions.As sovereign of this coterie, Mademoiselle Cormon naturally attracted two ambitious men as shrewd as the Chevalier de Valois and du Bousquier—each seeing in her an electoral or official advancement. To both she meant election as deputy—peerage for the noble, receiver‑generalship for the purveyor. A leading salon is hard to create; here was one already established. To marry Mademoiselle Cormon was to reign in Alençon. Athanase Granson, among her suitors, was the one who truly loved her person as well as her fortune.A singular drama plays out in these unremarked rivalries circling a woman who never guessed they existed, despite her eager desire to be married. Yet it is not hard to see why, in spite of fortune and suitors, she remained unmarried. First, she always wished to marry a nobleman; from 1788 to 1798 political circumstances were hostile. Though she wanted a husband of condition, she was terrified of the Revolutionary tribunal. These equal sentiments kept her motionless. The uncertainty pleased unmarried women as long as they felt young and able to choose. During the Consulate and the Empire many widows appeared and heiresses far outnumbered suitable bachelors. When the Consulate restored order, external difficulties again complicated Rose’s prospects. She refused old men and would not marry very young ones, fearing ridicule.Provincial families married sons early to avoid conscription, and she obstinately refused soldiers—she would not give a man up to the Emperor; she wanted him for herself. Thus, from 1804 to 1815 she found no suitable match among young men. Besides her predilection for nobility, she had an excusable mania: to be loved for herself. She set tests for suitors; her nets were so subtle that unlucky aspirants failed without realizing it. A single heedless word or a joke sufficed to condemn a man: one had no heart or delicacy, another told lies, a third was irreligious, a fourth sought money under the cloak of marriage; others showed hereditary gout or immoral antecedents. Like the Church, she demanded a noble priest at her altar; she wanted to be married for imaginary defects she would afterwards redeem by revealing concealed virtues. But nobles met common souls who prioritized interest and lacked understanding of her nobler calculations.As she approached what is called a “second youth,” her distrust increased. She presented herself unfavorably and behaved so that last wooers hesitated to bind themselves to a woman whose virtuous blind‑man’s‑buff required penetration men who wanted virtue ready‑made did not possess. Fear of marrying for her money made her suspicious beyond reason. She turned to the rich, who sought great marriages; she feared the poor, whom she saw as lacking disinterestedness. After each disappointment she grew to despise mankind, adopting a secret misanthropy which tinged her conversation with bitterness and her eyes with severity. Celibacy hardened her manners; she strove to sanctify herself in despair: noble vengeance, she cut for God the rough diamond rejected by man. Public opinion judged her accordingly; society assumed her rejections had secret causes—deformity or hidden faults—but she was as pure as a saint, healthy, and full of kindness; Nature had intended her for motherhood.Mademoiselle Cormon did not have classical beauty. Her charm was the rustic buxom freshness called la beauté du diable. Her feet were broad, legs sinewy, a stout waist, and plump arms gave a Norman outline. Projecting undecided eyes gave a sheepish simplicity; an aquiline nose contrasted with a narrow forehead; thick red lips showed kindliness while her forehead suggested a lack of ideas. Chestnut hair of extraordinary length added vigor. In a ball‑dress she might have seemed handsome; officers admired her robust figure. But as years passed, plumpness saturated her body; no corset could restore youthful proportions. Her bosom’s amplitude overwhelmed when she stooped; her chin doubled, shortening her neck and hindering carriage. No wrinkles, but folds of flesh; jests said she powdered her skin like an infant’s.This ample person attracted Athanase. Many elegant, indebted men would have welcomed making Mademoiselle Cormon happy. But at forty she had fortified virtue by stern religious practices. A confessor had, for three years, directed her to maceration; he advised scourging, which modern medicine would find absurd. These practices cast a monastic tint over Rose, whose white skin took a yellow tone; a down darkened her upper lip and her temples grew shiny; decadence began. Rumor said she suffered rushes of blood to the head. She confided her ills to the Chevalier de Valois, who would pull out his snuff‑box and say: “The right composing draught, my dear lady, is a good and kind husband.”The violence of hope made Rose afraid to look a man in the face lest he perceive her feelings. By wilfulness—perhaps from tests—she treated those she felt attracted to ungraciously, fearful of seeming foolish. Society took her behavior for revenge for a refusal and made fun of her abortive marriages. When 1815 began, she was forty‑two; desire for marriage bordered on monomania because she saw chances of children slipping away. She loved wholesomely and simply—Catholic, chaste, incapable of coquetry.For months she counted on chance: disbanded imperial officers and reorganized royal forces sent many officers home—some on half‑pay—who might have suited her by character and rank. But those returning were either too old or too young, too Bonapartist or too dissipated; their situations mismatched Rose’s rank, fortune, and morals. She prayed for a husband with whom she could be piously happy; none came. Conversations in her salon kept her informed of strangers’ arrivals, fortunes, rank, and habits; but Alençon is not a town of travellers. She despaired; her eye grew savage. Monsieur de Valois, aware that fidelity to a first attachment is considered a pledge for the future in feminine ethics, responded with the same snuff‑box gesture that made her uneasy.The Abbé de Sponde could not aid her matrimonial manoeuvres. The worthy man, now seventy, attributed the Revolution’s disasters to Providence punishing a dissolute Church. He adopted an ascetic life, hiding charity, prayers, and penances. Detached from earthly interests, his mind turned to sufferers and the Church’s needs; he left property management to his niece, who gave him its income while he spent surpluses in secret alms. The abbé loved his niece and regarded her as a daughter, but was abstracted and unable to conceive the flesh’s agitations; accustomed to reverence him, Rose dared not tell him of her desires. He would scarcely have liked a husband introduced. His silence was affable and benevolent; tall, spare, grave, solemn, his presence carried sacred authority. He was fond of the Voltairean chevalier; relics of nobility and clergy recognized each other by generous traits. The chevalier was unctuous with the abbé as he was paternal with grisettes.Some may imagine Mademoiselle Cormon employed every artifice to secure marriage—dress, coquetries—but she did not. She was heroic and immovable in her high‑necked chemisette. Her gowns were made by Alençon’s two hump‑backed sisters, who had taste. She refused airy deceits of elegance, choosing substance in all things. Those who laugh at her would have called her sublime for sincere feeling regardless of form.Critics say no girl is so ignorant as not to angle for men, and that Mademoiselle Cormon is an impossible type; yet faith and piety are still strong in Brittany and old Alençon. Devotion allows no subtleties: Mademoiselle Cormon trod the path of salvation, preferring prolonged virginity’s sorrows to trickery and sin. In a woman armed with a scourge, virtue will not compromise; consequently love and self‑interest must seek her resolutely. Devotion can produce moral myopia: pious persons turn so to celestial things that they become blind to many earthly matters. The Chevalier de Valois found it hard to decide whether stupidity makes one pious or piety makes one stupid. But whether stupidity or sanctity, their sorrows deserve pity like those of genius—stupidity is more abundant socially.Thus Rose was judged virginally ignorant. She failed to read Athanase Granson’s eyes—shy herself, she never suspected shyness in others; she did not recognize quavering tones as unuttered sentiment. Capable of inventing refinements that hindered earlier marriage, she could not perceive such refinements in Athanase. That a devoted soul may be blind to a noble soul is unsurprising: heart qualities differ from intellectual ones as genius differs from nobility of soul.This good creature, who grieved at making yearly preserves for only her uncle and herself, was becoming almost ridiculous. Sympathy and mockery mingled: some pitied her goodness, others derided her defects. People speculated on who would inherit her fine property and her uncle’s savings. For a while she was suspected of being, despite appearances, an “original”—dangerous in the provinces, where originality, meaning ideas not understood by others, is not permissible; provinces demand equality of mind and manner.After 1804 the phrase “married like Mademoiselle Cormon” became proverbial in Alençon for ridiculous failures. It was Parisian sarcasm that so excellent a woman should excite laughter. She received the whole society at her house, was charitable, pious, incapable of malice, and embodied the spirit and habits of the place; she had never left them and loved their prejudices and interests.Despite an income of eighteen thousand francs from land—a considerable provincial fortune—she lived on a footing with less‑rich families. When she went to her country place at Prébaudet she rode in an old wicker carriolle hung on two white leather straps, drawn by a wheezy mare and hardly protected by rusty leather curtains. Jacquelin cared for it as if it were the finest coupé in Paris. Mademoiselle valued it, having used it for twelve years—a fact she noted with triumphant thrift. Most inhabitants were grateful she did not humiliate them by ostentation; had she imported a calèche from Paris they would have gossiped more about it than her matrimonial failures. In the provinces people look to results and care little about the elegance of means so long as they function.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################To complete the picture of the household’s internal habits, one must place around Mademoiselle Cormon and the Abbé de Sponde the figures of Jacquelin, Josette, and Mariette, who provided for the uncle and niece’s comfort.Jacquelin, about forty, short, fat, ruddy, with a Breton-sailor face, had been in their service twenty-two years. He waited at table, groomed the mare, gardened, blacked the abbé’s boots, ran errands, chopped wood, drove the carriole, fetched oats, straw, and hay from Prébaudet, and dozed in the antechamber evenings. He was in love with Josette, a thirty-year-old, short, plump woman whose face, “like a dirty apricot,” showed sense and character; it was said she ruled her mistress. Mademoiselle would have dismissed Josette had she married Jacquelin, so the pair resigned themselves to saving their wages and loving silently, awaiting—like Jews awaiting the Messiah—some distant change. Mariette, the cook of fifteen years’ standing, knew how to prepare the most esteemed dishes of Alençon.The fat old Norman mare, Pénélope, drawn to Prébaudet by Mademoiselle Cormon, deserves mention: she had served the family eighteen years and was treated with the same affectionate mania as any human servant. Kept carefully and fed regularly, Mademoiselle and Jacquelin hoped to use her ten years more. The household’s four dependents—raised and lowered in mutual subservience to the beast—moved each day with mechanical accuracy. As they said, “they had eaten their white bread first.” Mademoiselle Cormon, driven by a fixed idea, grew hard to please, petty, and nagging—less by nature than to occupy her active mind. Without husband or children, she fell back on minute details: napkins marked “Z” before the “O’s,” whether Pénélope had her oats at two o’clock, a neglected dust-layer, a poorly made toast, Josette’s delay in closing blinds that faded furniture. Small things became grounds for serious quarrels. Once Josette gave her the “Journée du Chrétien” instead of the “Quinzaine de Pâques”; Mademoiselle left church and returned home in embarrassment; the town heard of the calamity. She would admonish Josette gently: “Such a thing must never happen again.”These little quarrels served as catharses for Mademoiselle Cormon, and in secret she was steadied by them. Josette and Jacquelin accepted the mood swings as weather-changes: “It is fine to-day,” or “It rains,” without arraigning the heavens. Each morning they wondered in what humor Mademoiselle would rise, as farmers wonder at dawn mists.She had ended, naturally, contemplating herself in the infinite pettinesses of life: herself and God, her confessor and the weekly wash, her preserves and church services, and her uncle to care for, occupied her feeble intellect. Her perfect health magnified the slightest digestive trouble. She lived under the iron rod of medical practice, taking quarterly precautionary doses strong enough, it was said, to have killed Pénélope but seeming to rejuvenate her mistress. A pimple on her satiny shoulder occasioned endless inquiries into the week’s alimentary articles until Josette triumphantly attributed it to a high hare or to Mariette’s over-seasoning: “No doubt, no doubt, it was the hare!”Four times a year she spent a number of days at her estate, Prébaudet. In mid-May she liked to see how her apple-trees had “snowed” (fallen blossoms promising an abundant cider harvest), attend winter repairs, order the digging of flower-beds and the vegetable garden that supplied her table. Every season had its business. Before departing she always gave a farewell dinner to intimate friends, though she expected to see them again within weeks. Her departure was the town’s news: shopkeepers repeated it from door to door—“So Mademoiselle Cormon is going to Prébaudet!”—with all manner of provincial commentary.The Wednesday Suzanne chose to reveal her scandal happened to be such a farewell Wednesday, when Mademoiselle was driving Josette distractedly packing. Madame Granson had spent the morning visiting houses, and the malicious Chevalier de Valois was playing piquet with Mademoiselle Armande, who governed aristocratic salons. The scene was set: the chevalier and Madame Granson wanted to see how Mademoiselle Cormon, in her double capacity as marriageable woman and president of the Maternity Society, would receive the news. Du Bousquier, the innocent suitor, walked on the promenade, beginning to suspect Suzanne had tricked him.On gala days Mademoiselle Cormon’s table was laid about half-past three; the fashionable dined at four. One of her chief pleasures was seeing herself dressed as mistress of the house to receive guests. When fully equipped, a ray of hope would glide into her heart; she felt that nature had not provided in vain and that some brave man would present himself. She revelled in inspecting the dining-room, the oblong table set with a snow-white cloth and twenty covers, the squadron of bottles with honorable labels, the names on little paper bits in the trembling hand of the abbé—the only duty he assumed. After these preliminaries she would go in her finery to her uncle, who took his walk on the terrace overlooking the Brillante and the birds in the coppice. In waiting she never joined the Abbé de Sponde without asking some ridiculous question to rouse him from his meditative silence; she regarded it as her duty to talk and make herself agreeable, a practice her spiritual director, the Abbé Couturier, had advised. She perspired in her corset thinking up ideas; her efforts produced such absurdities as: “No one can be in two places at once—unless it is a little bird,” which somehow provoked a debate on the apostles’ ubiquity. Such talk made the beaux esprits call her “that good Mademoiselle Cormon”—a polite way of saying she was rather a fool; but many of her peers took it literally, answering, “Yes; oh yes! Mademoiselle Cormon is an excellent woman.”When conversation flagged the Chevalier de Valois, who loved to assist women, would transform her sayings into wit by paradoxically sustaining them; once, when she could not distinguish an ox from a bull he was rescued by the chevalier claiming the only difference was as between sheep and lamb. He often covered her retreats so well that she seemed never to have said anything silly. She took his help as proof of her own adequacy and grew complacent in her ignorance, acquiring a self-possession that gave her speeches a solemnity like patriotic absurdities.Approaching her uncle one day, she tried to draw him into a matrimonial discussion.“Uncle,” she said, leaning on his arm, “if everything here below happens according to the will of God, there must be a reason for everything.”“Certainly,” replied the abbé.“Then if I remain unmarried—supposing that I do—God wills it?”“Yes, my child.”“And yet, as nothing prevents me from marrying to-morrow if I choose, His will can be destroyed by mine?”“That would be true if we knew what was really the will of God,” replied the former prior of the Sorbonne. “Observe, my daughter, that you put in an if.”Such childish logic perplexed her. “But, uncle, God did not make women intending them not to marry; otherwise they ought all to stay unmarried; if not, they ought all to marry. There’s great injustice in the distribution of parts.”“You are blaming the Church, which declares celibacy the better way to God,” replied the abbé.“But if the Church is right, and all the world were good Catholics, wouldn’t the human race come to an end, uncle?”“You have too much mind, Rose; you don’t need so much to be happy.”That remark pleased her and confirmed her good opinion of herself.Guests arrived and the familiarities between servants and company began: Mariette telling Monsieur du Ronceret she had cooked cauliflowers au gratin “expressly for you, for Mademoiselle knows how you like them”; Josette informing Madame Granson that “Mademoiselle has thought of you, and there’s fish for dinner”; the Chevalier de Valois, in his easy, condescending tone, asking Mariette if there was some dainty he should reserve himself for. Du Bousquier was not invited. Mademoiselle Cormon, faithful to her ambivalence, treated the fifty-year-old suitor ill though she felt inexplicable sentiments toward him. She had refused him yet sometimes repented; a presentiment haunted her that she might yet marry him, and terror prevented her desiring it. Madame Granson and the chevalier, observing her naive glances toward du Bousquier, resolved to ruin his hopes.Two tardy guests—Monsieur du Coudrai, recorder of mortgages, and Monsieur Choisnel, the notary—kept dinner waiting. As they arrived, Jacquelin announced “They are all in the garden.” The company strolled the terrace above the river, looking at water-plants, mosaics of currents, houses across the Sarthe with old wooden galleries and little gardens where clothes dried; a charming provincial landscape.The chevalier scanned faces; he had introduced his firebrand into the chief houses. The scandal about Suzanne and du Bousquier had not yet been openly discussed: provincials distill gossip, waiting the right moment. Whispers circulated: “You have heard?” “Yes.” “Du Bousquier?” “And that handsome Suzanne.” “Does Mademoiselle Cormon know of it?” “No.” “Ha!”The chevalier, noting Madame Granson’s triumphant look, suspected not only her joy but self-interest. He watched Athanase and saw, in a furtive look at Mademoiselle Cormon, a flash that set his plans awry. He offered his arm to Mademoiselle Cormon, whom he revered. She, secretly wishing for the alliance, admired the name de Valois as a social object, though his seeming purity and indifference worried her; she wanted a husband a little dissipated—someone with experience to purify. Observers have remarked pious women’s leaning toward scamps; Mademoiselle’s wish shows this: she would have liked a man to combine the chevalier’s nobility with du Bousquier’s vigor.To explain what followed, two matters agitated the town, matters in which du Bousquier was implicated. One concerned the rector of Alençon, who had once taken the constitutional oath but was winning Catholics by high virtues. He had supporters among the middle classes rallied by du Bousquier, while Mademoiselle Cormon and the Abbé de Sponde, belonging to the uncompromising “little Church,” refused to receive him. The second was du Bousquier’s idea of building a theatre in Alençon; Athanase Granson and other young clerks championed the project. The chevalier urged Mademoiselle Cormon to save Athanase from this influence.“Mademoiselle,” he said, “you have so much sense and judgment in social proprieties and influence with his mother; ought you not to use it to save him from perdition? He is irreligious, liberal, agitating for a theatre, the dupe of that disguised republican du Bousquier.”Mademoiselle responded that his mother said he had so much mind, “and yet he can’t say two words; he stands planted before me as mum as a post—”“Which doesn’t think at all!” cried the recorder, and the chevalier led Mademoiselle to a flower-pot to speak privately. He warned that lads raised in lycées had bad morals, were marched about in uniform, and, he implied, Athanase’s shyness and pale looks foretold a sad end. He urged her to warn the mother that her son might lose his place in the mayor’s office for such associations.Madame Granson, who had heard part of the talk, followed Mademoiselle, hoping to learn the outcome. But Jacquelin announced dinner in, and the recorder of mortgages, sensing his opportunity, offered his arm and cut off Monsieur de Valois. The chevalier then addressed Madame Granson directly, warning that Athanase’s theatre intrigues and rector sympathies could cost his job; she gratefully promised to enlighten him.The chevalier had long seen Athanase’s unyielding republican convictions—rooted in artistic sorrows and a bitter view of society—and knew he would persist. To remain faithful to the Left in Alençon was to earn Mademoiselle Cormon’s aversion.Thus this outwardly peaceful society was internally agitated by craft and passion like any diplomatic circle. The guests sat and ate as provincials, with appetites undisturbed by Parisian sumptuary fads. Toward the end of the first course Mademoiselle Cormon made the celebrated remark that would be repeated for two years: in the debate against du Bousquier—whom the chevalier and others depicted as a pernicious intriguer—she had innocently said, “As for me, I thought that Monsieur du Bousquier cared chiefly for childish things.” The phrase had enormous success: it undermined the Princess Goritza’s dignity and astonished the chevalier into silent admiration; he applauded with fingers as they do at the Opera.Prompted by the chevalier, du Bousquier was caricatured as a monster, a Pere Gigogne who had supplied the Foundling Hospital for fifteen years, his immoral habits explained as the cause of Parisian revels. The chevalier, leading the cancan, speculated that a du Bousquier might marry a “Suzette,” invoking Madame du Barry and the Emperor Joseph; Mademoiselle Cormon corrected him with naïve historicism. The Abbé de Sponde, touched with charitable solemnity, urged succor for the poor girl now unlikely to marry. The judge, Madame Granson, and others volleyed puns and jests until the Abbé de Sponde’s grave “Nor can he be a foster-father” quieted them.Usually a man is ignorant of town rumors; du Bousquier remained superbly unaware of Suzanne’s revelations and was jaunty at coffee. Mademoiselle Cormon, embarrassed, chose to lecture Athanase with platitudes about royalist politics and morality.
