Athanase listened with a stupid air, gazing at her enormous bust and swelling in his love, which transformed her shrill voice into a soft murmur and her flat remarks into witticisms. Love, the narrator notes, is a maker of false coin, turning copper into gold.“Will you promise me?” she asked Athanase, and the words pierced his absorbed mind. Had he spoken then, he might have revealed his passion; but his shy absorption ruined him.“What have you been saying to Mademoiselle Cormon?” demanded his mother.“Nothing.”Madame Granson, who believed du Bousquier forever lost in Mademoiselle’s esteem after that evening, thought little more of the remark. The salon later filled: four tables set for piquet, others for backgammon; the group gathered by the fire in evening finery, each repeating their farewell: “So you are going tomorrow to Prébaudet?” “Yes, I really must.”Mademoiselle Cormon appeared preoccupied; Madame Granson noticed she was thinking.“What are you thinking of, cousin?” she asked.“I am thinking,” Mademoiselle replied, “of that poor girl. As president of the Maternity Society, I will give you fifty francs for her.”“Fifty francs!” cried Madame Granson in astonishment. Mademoiselle never gave so much, but her sudden generosity showed du Bousquier had advanced in her estimation.“Du Bousquier is not only a monster, he is a villain,” said Madame Granson. “When a man has done a wrong like that, he ought to pay the indemnity. Isn’t it his place rather than ours to look after the girl?—who, to tell you the truth, seems rather questionable.”Mademoiselle, who could not quite grasp how hearts work, said innocently that perhaps one could be calumniated for loving Athanase, yet be loved truly. Madame Granson, exasperated by her cousin’s naiveté, tried to explain the physical side of things: “It is impossible for Suzanne to love du Bousquier. And if the heart counts for nothing in this affair—” “But, cousin, what do people love with if not their hearts?” inquired the old maid, widening her eyes in honest puzzlement.Madame Granson advised a show of displeasure: not receiving du Bousquier in her house until he married would manifest exemplary morals. Mademoiselle promised to consult her uncle and the Abbé Couturier upon her return from Prébaudet.The lights, the women in best clothes, the solemn assembly made Mademoiselle Cormon proud: to many it was the best of Parisian society. Du Bousquier, whistling the pastoral air, was the centre of curiosity. Some declared him strong and misjudged; others saw danger in him. The chevalier, going home, planned to spread a rumor of his marriage to Mademoiselle Cormon to secure attention and advantage, to use his connections to become mayor and then deputy, dreaming of peerage and power, imagining how the marriage would serve his ambitions.When Madame Granson reached home she fiercely argued with her son Athanase, who could not see the link between his political opinions and his love—a quarrel the household had never known before.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The next day Mademoiselle Cormon, packed into the old carriole with Josette and a pyramid of parcels, drove up rue Saint-Blaise toward Prebaudet; an event hurried on her marriage—an event unlooked for by Madame Granson, du Bousquier, Monsieur de Valois, or Mademoiselle Cormon herself. Chance is a great artificer.The day after her arrival at Prebaudet, about eight o’clock, while she was listening at breakfast to reports from her keeper and gardener, Jacquelin burst into the dining-room.“Mademoiselle,” he cried, out of breath, “Monsieur l’abbé sends you an express—the son of Mère Grosmort—with a letter. The lad left Alençon before daylight; he ran like Penelope! Can’t I give him a glass of wine?”“What can have happened, Josette? Do you think my uncle can be—”“He couldn’t write if he were,” said Josette, guessing her mistress’s fears.“Quick! quick!” cried Mademoiselle Cormon, on reading the first lines. “Tell Jacquelin to harness Penelope—Get ready, Josette; pack up everything in half an hour. We must go back to town—”“Jacquelin!” called Josette, excited by the look on her mistress’s face.Jacquelin entered to say, “But, mademoiselle, Penelope is eating her oats.”“What does that signify? I must start at once.”“But, mademoiselle, it is going to rain.”“Then we shall get wet.”“Finish your coffee at any rate, mademoiselle; don’t excite your blood; just see how red you are.”“Am I red, Josette?” she said, going to a mirror whose quicksilver was peeling and which inverted her features.“Good heavens!” thought Mademoiselle Cormon. “Suppose I should look ugly! Come, Josette; dress me at once; I want to be ready before Jacquelin has harnessed Penelope. If you can’t pack my things in time, I will leave them here rather than lose a single minute.”If you have comprehended the monomania to which the desire of marriage had brought Mademoiselle Cormon, you will share her emotion. The uncle’s sudden missive announced that Monsieur de Troisville, of the Russian army during the Emigration, grandson of one of his old friends, desired to retire to Alençon and had asked the abbé’s hospitality because of that friendship. Alarmed at the responsibility, the absent-minded abbé entreated his niece to return instantly and help receive this guest; the viscount’s letter had been delayed, and he might arrive that very night.After reading this, how could she fail to hasten? The keeper and gardener, witnesses to her excitement, stood aside. For the first time the mistress who oversaw Prebaudet in every detail said, to their stupefaction, “Do what you like.” Josette watched her mistress fly down the staircase like an elephant made to fly.In spite of a driving rain, Mademoiselle Cormon drove away from Prebaudet, leaving her factotums with reins and worries. Jacquelin dared not hasten Penelope’s usual trot; impatient, the mistress ordered him sharply to drive at a gallop, whip if necessary; she feared not having time to arrange the house suitably to receive Monsieur de Troisville. She imagined the grandson about forty; a soldier just from service was doubtless a bachelor; with her uncle’s aid she resolved not to let Monsieur de Troisville leave their house as he entered. Though Penelope galloped, Mademoiselle Cormon, absorbed in visions of trousseau and wedding-day, declared again and again that Jacquelin made no way. She twisted in the carriole, unresponsive to Josette’s questions, talking to herself as if revolving important designs.The carriole reached Alençon’s main street. Her sudden return in pouring rain created an uproar: Penelope at a full gallop, Jacquelin’s grin, the early hour, the parcels half-packed, and Mademoiselle Cormon’s impatience were remarked. The Troisville property lay between Alençon and Mortagne; Josette, by a word her mistress let fall, surmised the expected de Troisville must be between forty and forty-two, a bachelor, neither rich nor poor. Mademoiselle Cormon beheld herself Vicomtesse de Troisville.“And to think my uncle told me nothing! thinks of nothing! inquires nothing!” she muttered.Under such circumstances old maids become keen-witted, bold, and promissory. Immediately Alençon was perturbed. Cooks, shopkeepers, street-passers spread the news; it rose to upper circles. Soon the cry, “Mademoiselle Cormon has returned!” burst like a bomb. Jacquelin opened the great green gate and closed it in sign of mourning; during Mademoiselle Cormon’s absence the evening assemblies had not taken place. The faithful invited the Abbe de Sponde to their houses; Monsieur de Valois repaid the debt by inviting him to dine at the Marquis d’Esgrignon’s. Jacquelin led the carriage to the portico.“Mariette!” cried Mademoiselle Cormon.“Mademoiselle!” exclaimed Mariette, closing the gate.“Has the gentleman arrived?”“No, mademoiselle.”“Where’s my uncle?”“He is at church, mademoiselle.”Jacquelin and Josette helped their mistress out; at the portico she surveyed her courtyard with satisfaction.“Come, Mariette, leave that gate alone; I want you.”“There’s something in the wind,” whispered Jacquelin.“Mariette, what provisions have you in the house?” asked Mademoiselle Cormon, already faint with apprehension.“I haven’t anything,” replied Mariette, hands on hips. “Mademoiselle knows that during her absence Monsieur l’abbé dines out every day.”“He thinks of nothing! he ought to have told you to go to market. Mariette, go at once; and without wasting money, don’t spare it; get all there is that is good and delicate. Go to the diligence office and see if you can send for pâtés; and I want shrimps from the Brillante. What o’clock is it?”“A quarter to nine.”“Good heavens! Mariette, don’t stop to chatter. The person my uncle expects may arrive at any moment. If we had to give him breakfast, where should we be with nothing in the house?”Mariette turned back to Penelope in a lather, and looked at Jacquelin as if to say, “Mademoiselle has put her hand on a husband this time.”“Now, Josette,” continued the old maid, “let us see where we had better put Monsieur de Troisville to sleep.”With what joy she said, “Put Monsieur de Troisville to sleep.” How many ideas in those few words! The old maid was bathed in hope.“Will you put him in the green chamber?”“The bishop’s room? No; that’s too near mine. All very well for monseigneur.”“Give him your uncle’s room.”“Oh, that’s so bare; it is indecent.”“Well, then, why not arrange a bed in your boudoir? It is easily done; and there’s a fireplace. Moreau can find a bed to match the hangings.”“You are right, Josette. Go yourself to Moreau; consult with him what to do; I authorize you to get what is wanted. If the bed could be put up to-night without Monsieur de Troisville observing it, I should like it. If Moreau won’t engage to do this, then I must put Monsieur de Troisville in the green room, though he would be very near me.”Josette was departing when recalled.“Stop! explain to Jacquelin,” she cried. “Tell him to go to Moreau; I must be dressed! Fancy if Monsieur de Troisville surprised me as I am now! and my uncle not here to receive him! Oh, uncle, uncle! Come, Josette; dress me at once.”“But Penelope?” said Josette.“Always Penelope! Penelope this, Penelope that! Is Penelope the mistress of this house?”“But she is all of a lather, and she hasn’t had time to eat her oats.”“Then let her starve!” cried Mademoiselle Cormon; “provided I marry,” she thought.Hearing this, Josette stood speechless, then ran down the portico steps.“The devil is in her, Jacquelin,” she murmured.Thus all things conspired on this fateful day. The town was already agitated by five extraordinary circumstances accompanying her return: the pouring rain; Penelope at a gallop in a lather; the early hour; parcels half-packed; and the excited old maid. But when Mariette raided the market for the best things; when Jacquelin sought a bed at the principal upholsterer two doors from the church—there was matter for the gravest conjectures. These events were discussed everywhere; within two days Alençon had been agitated as in the critical days of 1814 and 1815. Some good women feared the world was ending. The question resolved into: “What is happening at the Cormons?”The Abbe de Sponde, adroitly questioned when he left Saint-Leonard’s, replied that he expected the Vicomte de Troisville, a nobleman who had served Russia during the Emigration and was returning to settle. From two to five o’clock a labial telegraphy went on; all learned that Mademoiselle Cormon had at last found a husband by letter, and was about to marry the Vicomte de Troisville. Rumors varied: some said the vicomte was a poor younger son; others, a Russian general with a great fortune; some accused the abbe of arranging things. All who had a right to call upon Mademoiselle Cormon determined to do so that very evening.During this commotion, Mademoiselle was not less agitated; new emotions seized her. She looked at her salon, dining-room, and boudoir with cruel apprehensions. A demon sneered at her old-fashioned luxury: items admired since youth suddenly seemed aged and absurd. She feared the viscount’s smile of contempt might reflect age upon her; she would gladly have spent half her savings to refit the house if a fairy wand could do it.“Madame la Vicomtesse de Troisville,” she said to herself; “a noble name! Our property will go to a good family, at any rate.”She fell prey to an irritation that made every nerve quiver. Her blood, lashed by hope, was in motion; she felt able to converse, if necessary, with Monsieur de Troisville.It is needless to relate with detail the activity of Josette, Jacquelin, Mariette, Moreau, and his agents. It was like ants about their eggs. All routine care was gone over, scrubbed and brushed. The china of ceremony saw the light; the damask linen marked “A, B, C” was drawn from triple wrappings. Above all, Mademoiselle Cormon's sacrifices included three bottles of Madame Amphoux’s famous liqueurs, dear to gourmets. Thanks to her lieutenants, she was soon ready. Furniture, cookery, provisions, all munitions of war, and reserve forces were prepared. Jacquelin, Mariette, and Josette had orders to appear in full dress. The garden was raked. The old maid would have liked to arrange the nightingales’ finest trills.At about four o’clock, as the Abbe de Sponde returned and Mademoiselle considered she had in vain set her table with the best plate and linen, the click-clack of a postillion was heard in the Val-Noble.“’Tis he!” she said, the whip’s snap echoing in her heart.A post-chaise with a single gentleman made such sensation descending rue Saint-Blaise that boys and grown persons followed it to the Cormon gate. Jacquelin, who foresaw his own marriage in his mistress’s, opened the gates. The postilion, a friend, made a fine turn and drew up before the portico. The abbé greeted his guest, whose chaise was emptied with highwayman speed; the chaise was rolled into the coach-house, gates closed, and all signs of Monsieur de Troisville’s arrival vanished. Never did two chemicals blend more rapidly than the hotel Cormon absorbed the Vicomte de Troisville.Mademoiselle, whose heart beat like a lizard caught, sat heroically on her salon sofa. Josette opened the door; the Vicomte de Troisville, followed by the Abbe de Sponde, presented himself to the spinster.“Niece, this is Monsieur le Vicomte de Troisville, the grandson of one of my old schoolmates; Monsieur de Troisville, my niece, Mademoiselle Cormon.”“Ah! that good uncle; how well he does it!” thought Rose-Marie-Victoire.The Vicomte de Troisville might be painted in two words as du Bousquier ennobled. Between them there was the difference separating vulgar from noble style. The viscount’s strength had elegance; his figure preserved magnificent dignity. He had blue eyes, black hair, an olive skin, and looked about forty-six—a handsome Spaniard preserved in the ice of Russia. His manner, carriage, and attitude denoted a diplomat who had seen Europe. His dress was that of a well-bred traveller. As he seemed fatigued, the abbé offered to show him his room, and was amazed when his niece threw open the boudoir door, transformed into a bedroom.Mademoiselle Cormon and her uncle left the stranger to attend to his affairs, aided by Jacquelin bringing luggage, and went to walk beside the river until the guest had made his toilet. Although the Abbe de Sponde was still absent-minded, Mademoiselle was not less preoccupied. They walked in silence. She had never met any man as seductive as this Olympean viscount. She might have said, as the Germans, “This is my ideal!” Instead she felt bound from head to foot and could only think “Here’s my affair!” Then she flew to Mariette to know if the dinner could be delayed without loss.“Uncle, your Monsieur de Troisville is very amiable,” she said on return.“Why, niece, he hasn’t as yet said a word.”“But you can see it in his ways, his manners, his face. Is he a bachelor?”“I’m sure I don’t know,” replied the abbé, thinking of a recent discussion on mercy. “Monsieur de Troisville wrote he wanted to buy a house here. If he was married, he wouldn’t come alone on such an errand,” he added carelessly, not conceiving his niece might think of marriage.“Is he rich?”“He is a younger son of the younger branch,” said her uncle. “His grandfather commanded a squadron, but this young man’s father made a bad marriage.”“Young man!” exclaimed the old maid. “It seems to me, uncle, that he must be at least forty-five.” She wanted to equate their years.“Yes,” said the abbé; “but to a poor priest of seventy, Rose, a man of forty seems a youth.”All Alençon now knew Monsieur de Troisville had arrived. The traveller rejoined his hosts and admired the Brillante, garden, and house.“Monsieur l’abbé,” he said, “my whole ambition is to have a house like this.” The old maid fancied a declaration lurked in that speech and lowered her eyes. “You must enjoy it very much, mademoiselle,” added the vicomte.“How could it be otherwise? It has been in our family since 1574,” replied Mademoiselle Cormon. “It is built on piles,” she added.Jacquelin announced dinner. Monsieur de Troisville offered his arm to the happy woman, who endeavored not to lean too heavily; she feared to seem forward.“Everything is so harmonious here,” said the viscount, as he sat.“Yes, our trees are full of birds, which give us concerts for nothing; no one ever frightens them; and the nightingales sing at night,” said Mademoiselle Cormon.“I was speaking of the interior of the house,” remarked the viscount, not troubling to observe Mademoiselle Cormon, and therefore not perceiving her dulness. “Everything is so in keeping,—the tones of color, the furniture, the general character.”“But it costs a great deal; taxes are enormous,” responded the excellent woman.“Ah! taxes are high, are they?” said the viscount, preoccupied.“I don’t know,” replied the abbé. “My niece manages the property of each of us.”“Taxes are not of much importance to the rich,” said Mademoiselle Cormon, not wishing to be thought miserly. “As for the furniture, I shall leave it as it is, and change nothing,—unless I marry; and then, of course, everything here must suit the husband.”“You have noble principles, mademoiselle,” said the viscount with a smile. “You will make one happy man.”“No one ever made to me such a pretty speech,” thought the old maid.The viscount complimented her on the excellence of her service and the admirable arrangements of the house, remarking he had supposed the provinces behind the age; on the contrary, he found them, as the English say, “very comfortable.”“What can that word mean?” she thought. “Well, courage!” she said. “I can’t be expected to answer a foreign language—But,” she continued aloud, feeling eloquence under the pressure to be agreeable, “we have a very brilliant society here, monsieur. It assembles at my house, and you shall judge of it this evening, for some of my faithful friends have no doubt heard of my return and your arrival. Among them is the Chevalier de Valois, a seigneur of the old court, a man of infinite wit and taste; then there is Monsieur le Marquis d’Esgrignon and Mademoiselle Armande, his sister”—(she bit her tongue)—“a woman remarkable in her way. She resolved to remain unmarried in order to leave all her fortune to her brother and nephew.”“Ah!” exclaimed the viscount. “Yes, the d’Esgrignons,—I remember them.”“Alençon is very gay,” continued the old maid. “There’s much amusement: the receiver-general gives balls; the prefect is an amiable man; and Monseigneur the bishop sometimes honors us with a visit—”“Well, then,” said the viscount with a smile, “I have done wisely to come back, like the hare, to die in my form.”“Yes,” she said. “I, too, attach myself or I die.”Conversation continued in generalities. By some mysterious faculty, Mademoiselle Cormon found, under the pressure to be agreeable, all the phrases and opinions of the Chevalier de Valois. The viscount, well-bred, did not speak of the dinner’s excellence; his silence was praise. As he drank the wines Jacquelin served, he seemed to feel he was with friends and met them with pleasure; for the true connoisseur enjoys, he does not applaud. He inquired the price of land and houses, made Mademoiselle Cormon describe the confluence of the Sarthe and the Brillante; he expressed surprise that the town lay so far from the river, and seemed much interested in the topography.The silent abbé left his niece to throw the dice of conversation; and she truly felt she pleased Monsieur de Troisville, who smiled and had committed himself more during dinner than her eager suitors had done in ten days. Imagine the little attentions: she watched his every look and served him as if he were a cherished lover's return. She did not depreciate herself; instead she set every sail, assumed the bearing of the queen of Alençon, and boasted of her preserves. She fished for compliments, seeing that she pleased; her eager desire had transformed her almost into a woman.At dessert she heard sounds denoting the arrival of her usual guests. She called the abbé’s and the vicomte’s attention; it was curiosity. Impatient to show herself, Mademoiselle Cormon told Jacquelin to serve coffee and liqueurs in the salon, where he displayed a magnificent Dresden liqueur-stand seen only twice a year. This was noted by the company ready to gossip.“The deuce!” muttered du Bousquier. “Madame Amphoux’s liqueurs, only served at the four church festivals!”“Undoubtedly the marriage was arranged a year ago by letter,” said the chief-justice du Ronceret. “The postmaster tells me his office has received letters postmarked Odessa for more than a year.”Madame Granson trembled. The Chevalier de Valois, though he had dined with the appetite of four men, turned pale on the left of his face. Feeling he might betray himself, he said hastily,—“Don’t you think it is very cold today? I am almost frozen.”“The neighborhood of Russia, perhaps,” said du Bousquier.The chevalier looked at him as if to say, “Well played!”Mademoiselle Cormon appeared radiant and triumphant; the company thought her handsome. Since early morning her blood had been whirling and nerves agitated by the presentiment of a crisis. These combined made her so unlike herself. With joy she presented the viscount to the chevalier, the chevalier to the viscount, and all Alençon to Monsieur de Troisville.By accident the viscount and chevalier, aristocrats by nature, came instantly into unison; they recognized each other as men of the same sphere and conversed before the fireplace. A circle formed and listened in silence. Picture Mademoiselle Cormon pouring coffee for her suitor with her back to the fireplace.Monsieur de Valois: “Monsieur le vicomte has come, I am told, to settle in Alençon?”Monsieur de Troisville: “Yes, monsieur, I am looking for a house.” [Mademoiselle Cormon, cup in hand, turns round.] “It must be a large house” [Mademoiselle offers him the cup] “to lodge my whole family.” [The old maid’s eyes are troubled.]Monsieur de Valois: “Are you married?”Monsieur de Troisville: “Yes, for the last sixteen years, to a daughter of the Princess Scherbellof.”Mademoiselle Cormon fainted; du Bousquier, who saw her stagger, sprang forward and received her in his arms; someone opened the door and allowed him to pass with his burden. The republican, instructed by Josette, found strength to carry the old maid to her bedroom, where he laid her on the bed. Josette, with scissors, cut the terribly tight corset. Du Bousquier flung water on Mademoiselle Cormon’s face and bosom, which, freed from the corset, overflowed like the Loire in flood. The poor woman opened her eyes, saw du Bousquier, and gave a cry of modesty. Du Bousquier retired, leaving six women, headed by Madame Granson, radiant with joy, to care for the invalid.What had the Chevalier de Valois been doing? Faithful to his system, he had covered the retreat.“That poor Mademoiselle Cormon,” he said to Monsieur de Troisville, gazing at the assembly whose laughter was repressed by his aristocratic glances, “her blood is horribly out of order; she wouldn’t be bled before going to Prebaudet, and see the result!”“She came back this morning in the rain,” said the Abbe de Sponde, “and she may have taken cold. It won’t be anything; it is only a little upset she is subject to.”“She told me yesterday she had not had one for three months, adding that she was afraid it would play her a trick at last,” said the chevalier.“Ha! so you are married?” thought Jacquelin as he looked at Monsieur de Troisville, who quietly sipped his coffee.The faithful servant espoused his mistress’s disappointment; he divined it, and promptly carried away Madame Amphoux’s liqueurs, offered to a bachelor and not to the husband of a Russian woman.All these details were noticed and laughed at. The Abbe de Sponde knew Monsieur de Troisville’s object, but, absent-minded, forgot and did not suppose his niece could be interested. The viscount, preoccupied with his own errand and like many husbands not eager to talk of his wife, had had no occasion to say he was married; he would naturally suppose Mademoiselle Cormon knew.Du Bousquier reappeared and was questioned. One of the six women came down and announced Mademoiselle Cormon was much better, that the doctor had come. She intended to stay in bed, as bleeding was necessary. The salon was full. The ladies discussed the tragi-comic scene—embellished, extended, embroidered—destined on the morrow to occupy all Alençon.“That good Monsieur du Bousquier! how well he carried you!” said Josette to her mistress. “He was really pale at the sight of you; he loves you still.”That speech closed the solemn evening.Next morning every circumstance of the comedy was known, and—let it be to the town’s shame—they caused inextinguishable laughter. But on that day Mademoiselle Cormon, much benefited by bleeding, would have seemed sublime had scoffers seen her dignity and Christian resignation as she gave her arm to her involuntary deceiver for breakfast. Cruel jesters! why not have seen her say to the viscount,—“Madame de Troisville will have difficulty in finding a suitable house; do me the favor, monsieur, of accepting the use of mine while you seek yours.”“But, mademoiselle, I have two sons and two daughters; we should greatly inconvenience you.”“Pray do not refuse me,” she said earnestly.“I made you the same offer in the answer I wrote to your letter,” said the abbé; “but you did not receive it.”“What, uncle! then you knew—”The poor woman stopped. Josette sighed. Neither the viscount nor the abbé observed anything amiss. After breakfast the Abbe de Sponde carried off his guest, as agreed, to show him houses in Alençon.Left alone in the salon, Mademoiselle Cormon said to Josette, distressed, “My child, I am now the talk of the whole town.”“Well, then, mademoiselle, you should marry.”“But I am not prepared to make a choice.”“Bah! if I were you, I should take Monsieur du Bousquier.”“Josette, Monsieur de Valois says he is so republican.”“They don’t know what they say, your gentlemen: sometimes they declare that he robbed the republic; he couldn’t love it if he did that,” said Josette, departing.“That girl has an amazing amount of sense,” thought Mademoiselle Cormon, who remained alone, prey to perplexities.She saw plainly that a prompt marriage was the only way to silence the town. Each of the two old bachelors understood the situation; each resolved that morning to call and in bachelor fashion “press his point.” Monsieur de Valois considered such an occasion demanded a painstaking toilet; he took a bath and groomed with extraordinary care. For the first and last time Cesarine saw him apply a suspicion of rouge. Du Bousquier, coarse and brisk-willed, paid no attention to dress, and arrived first.Little things decide fortunes. Kellerman’s charge at Marengo, Blücher’s arrival at Waterloo, Louis XIV.’s disdain for Prince Eugene—all these turning points show how small neglects alter fate. A touch of rouge destroyed the Chevalier de Valois’s hopes; while he made this last touch, the rough du Bousquier entered the desolate old maid’s salon. This entrance produced a thought favorable to the republican, though in other respects the chevalier had advantages.“God wills it!” she said, piously, seeing du Bousquier.“Mademoiselle, you will not, I trust, think my eagerness importunate. I could not trust my stupid René to bring news of your condition, and therefore I have come myself.”“I am perfectly recovered,” she replied, with emotion. “I thank you, Monsieur du Bousquier,” she added after a pause and in a significant tone, “for the trouble you have taken, and for that which I gave you yesterday—”She remembered being in his arms, and that again seemed an order from heaven. She had been seen for the first time by a man with her laces cut, her treasures forced from their casket.“I carried you with such joy that you seemed to me light.”Mademoiselle Cormon looked at du Bousquier as she had never looked at any man. Encouraged, the purveyor cast upon the old maid a glance that reached her heart.“I would,” he said, “that that moment had given me the right to keep you forever”—she listened delighted—“as you lay fainting upon that bed, you were enchanting. I have never in my life seen a more beautiful person,—and I have seen many handsome women. Plump ladies are superb to look upon; they have only to show themselves and they triumph.”“I fear you are making fun of me,” said the old maid, “and that is not kind when all the town will misinterpret what happened yesterday.”“As true as my name is du Bousquier, mademoiselle, I have never changed in my feelings toward you; and your first refusal has not discouraged me.”Her eyes lowered. A moment of cruel silence followed, and then Mademoiselle Cormon decided. She raised her eyelids; tears flowed, and she gave du Bousquier a tender glance.“If that is so, monsieur,” she said trembling, “promise me to live in a Christian manner, and not oppose my religious customs, but to leave me the right to select my confessors, and I will grant you my hand”; she held it out.Du Bousquier seized the good fat hand and kissed it solemnly.“But,” she added, “one thing more I must require of you.”“If it is possible, it is granted,” replied the purveyor.“Alas!” returned the old maid. “For my sake, I must ask you to take upon yourself a sin which I feel to be enormous,—for to lie is one of the capital sins. But you will confess it, will you not? We will do penance together.” They looked at each other tenderly. “Besides, it may be one of those lies which the Church permits as necessary—”“Can she be as Suzanne says she is?” thought du Bousquier. “What luck! Well, mademoiselle, what is it?” he asked.“That you will take upon yourself to—”“What?”“To say this marriage has been agreed upon between us for the last six months.”“Charming woman,” said the purveyor, willing to devote himself, “such sacrifices can be made only for a creature adored these ten years.”“In spite of my harshness?” she asked.“Yes, in spite of your harshness.”“Monsieur du Bousquier, I have misjudged you.”Again she held out the fat red hand, which he kissed again.At that moment the door opened; the betrothed pair looked round to see who entered and beheld the delightful, but tardy, Chevalier de Valois.“Ah!” he said on entering, “I see you are about to be up, fair queen.”She smiled at the chevalier, feeling a weight upon her heart. Monsieur de Valois, remarkably young and seductive, had the air of a Lauzun reentering the apartments of the Grande Mademoiselle in the Palais-Royal.“Hey! dear du Bousquier,” said he in a jaunty tone, sure of success, “Monsieur de Troisville and the Abbe de Sponde are examining your house like appraisers.”“Faith!” said du Bousquier, “if the Vicomte de Troisville wants it, it is his for forty thousand francs. It is useless to me now. If mademoiselle will permit—must soon be known—Mademoiselle, may I tell it?—Yes! Well, then, be the first, my dear Chevalier, to hear of the honor that mademoiselle has done me, the secret I have kept for some months. We shall be married in a few days; the contract is drawn, and we shall sign it to-morrow. You see, therefore, my house in the rue du Cygne is useless to me. I have been secretly seeking a purchaser; and the Abbe de Sponde, who knew that, naturally took Monsieur de Troisville to see the house.”This falsehood seemed true and the chevalier believed it. That “my dear chevalier” was du Bousquier’s revenge for a thousand little past shafts. In triumph, he ran his hands through his hair and knocked aside his false front.“I congratulate you both,” said the chevalier with agreeable air; “and I wish that the marriage may end like a fairy tale: They were happy ever after, and had—many—children!” So saying, he took snuff. “But, monsieur,” he added satirically, “you forget—you are wearing a false front.”Du Bousquier blushed. The false front was hanging half a dozen inches from his skull. Mademoiselle Cormon raised her eyes, saw the skull in its nudity, and lowered them, abashed. Du Bousquier cast upon the chevalier the most venomous look a toad ever darted.“Dogs of aristocrats who despise me,” he thought, “I’ll crush you some day.”The chevalier thought himself recovered. But Mademoiselle Cormon did not understand the connection the chevalier intimated between his wish and the false front. Besides, her word was passed and her hand given. Monsieur de Valois saw all was lost. The innocent woman, with the two now silent men before her, wished in duty to amuse them.“Why not play a game of piquet together?” she said artlessly.Du Bousquier smiled and went to fetch the piquet table as future master of the house. Whether the chevalier lost his head or wanted to study his disaster, he allowed himself to be led like a lamb to the slaughter. He had received the most violent blow a man might suffer; any noble would have lost his senses for less.The Abbe de Sponde and Monsieur de Troisville soon returned. Mademoiselle Cormon rose, hurried into the antechamber, and took her uncle aside to tell him her resolution. Learning that the rue du Cygne house suited the viscount, she begged her future husband to tell him her uncle knew it was for sale. She dared not confess the lie to the absent-minded abbé. The lie prospered better than a virtuous action. That evening all Alençon heard the news. The town had had as much to think of as during the fatal years 1814–1815. Some laughed; others admitted the marriage. The middle classes rejoiced, viewing it as a victory. The next day the Chevalier de Valois said cruelly among friends,—“The Cormons end as they began; there’s only a hand’s breadth between a steward and a purveyor.”############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The news of Mademoiselle Cormon’s choice stabbed poor Athanase Granson to the heart; yet he gave no outward sign. He learned of the marriage at the house of the chief-justice, du Ronceret, where his mother was playing boston. Madame Granson thought him pale, but he had been so all day from rumors already reaching him.Mademoiselle Cormon was the card on which Athanase had staked his life; a cold presentiment of catastrophe weighed on him. When imagination magnifies misfortune beyond endurance, a long-cherished hope balks, faith fails in self and future, and the man is lost. Athanase, a product of the Imperial system and its fatalistic schooling, sat still, eyes fixed on Madame du Ronceret’s cards in a stupor that passed for indifference; his mother was deceived. This apparent coldness explained his refusal to make the usual sacrifices for a marriage whose political overtones—“liberal” opinions, the current fashionable label—had complicated private matters.After that fatal evening the young man wandered the picturesque regions of the Sarthe—the windmills, gay river meadows, well-grouped trees—seeking the solitude he loved. Provincials care little for a fine view; they lack poesy. Athanase sat beneath a poplar, vacant-eyed; neighbors said to Madame Granson, “Something is the matter with your son.” She hinted he meditated some great work.He ceased to have political opinions and no longer took part in politics; sometimes he seemed gay in a satirical way that insulted the world with individual scorn. He interested few; no friend or woman came to dry his tears; they fell into the Sarthe. Had the gorgeous Suzanne crossed his path, many young miseries might have been born; they would surely have loved.Suzanne did come. Her ambition had been fired by the tale of the Marquis de Montauran’s adventure with Marie de Verneuil at the tavern of the More. Fascinated by beauty and the story, Suzanne longed to play upon men. Months after her hasty departure she passed through her native town with an artist, wanting to see Fougeres and the scene of the Marquis’s tragedy. She fancied arriving in Alencon so well equipped none would recognize her; she hoped to put her mother beyond necessity and to send Athanase, delicately, a sum of money.A month passed in uncertainties about Mademoiselle Cormon’s marriage. Unbelievers denied it; believers affirmed it. The sale of du Bousquier’s house to the Marquis de Troisville seemed to show otherwise, but that too was explained away. Choisnel, Mademoiselle Cormon’s usual notary, heard nothing about a contract; yet on the twentieth day Monsieur Lepressoir, the notary of the liberals, went to her house and the contract was signed.This was the first of numerous sacrifices Mademoiselle Cormon would make to her husband. Du Bousquier bore deep hatred toward Choisnel, whom he blamed for a prior refusal of Mademoiselle Armande’s hand—an event he thought had influenced Mademoiselle Cormon’s delay. Mademoiselle received anonymous letters and learned, to her astonishment, that Suzanne was, as to du Bousquier, as truly a virgin as herself; the seducer with the false toupet had never been hero of any such adventure. Mademoiselle Cormon wrote to Suzanne, and Suzanne, who had heard of the proposed marriage, acknowledged her trick, sent a thousand francs to the Maternity Society, and did what harm she could to the old purveyor. The society resolved henceforth to assist only misfortunes that had happened, not those about to.Despite gossip, the banns were published, and Athanase prepared the deeds. As propriety demanded, the bride retired to Prebaudet, where du Bousquier, with sumptuous bouquets, visited every morning and returned home for dinner.On a dull rainy June morning, Mademoiselle Cormon and the Sieur du Bousquier were married at noon in the parish church of Alencon, in sight of the whole town. They drove from the mayor’s office to the church in an open caleche secretly sent from Paris—the loss of the old carriole being viewed as a local calamity. People feared the triumph of Parisian luxury, a rise in rents and provisions, and invasion by foreign furniture. Jacquelin charged ten sous to show the new vehicle. A pair of horses bought in Normandie alarmed the Ronceret circle, who argued, however absurdly, that buying horses themselves would prevent outsiders from taking their money. Penelope succumbed to pleurisy six weeks before the marriage.Madame du Bousquier entered the church with her left foot—a dreadful omen as “Left” was gaining political meaning. The priest opened his book by chance at the De Profundis. No wedding party followed; the pair departed immediately for Prebaudet. Parisian customs, the community said, were about to triumph over time-honored provincial ways. Jacquelin and Josette’s wedding, gay and heartening, alone refuted the sinister prophecies.Du Bousquier used proceeds of his former residence to restore and modernize the hotel Cormon, decided to remain two seasons at Prebaudet, and took the Abbe de Sponde with them. The town feared “comfort.” One morning the bridegroom drove in from Prebaudet in a fine tilbury with Rene livery at his side; he had deposited his wife’s savings on the Grand-Livre and, by playing for a rise, made himself a personal fortune nearly as large as his wife’s.These forebodings were superseded by a still more fatal event connected with the marriage.On the evening of the ceremony, Athanase and his mother sat before a little fagot fire. Madame Granson proposed they go to the du Roncerets’; Athanase, in a voice like childhood, asked his mother to stay a little longer, calling her “my dear mamma.” She consented, preferring to listen to his projects rather than play boston.He clung to her with desperate tenderness, kissed her eyes and gray hair. Though he tried to deceive her about a fatal resolution, he revealed he wished to leave Alencon, to suffer away from her. She refused to be parted from him; he insisted. She offered money hidden in her petticoat for his outfit.After a brief outing to du Ronceret’s, where his mother said he had “great independence of heart,” Athanase left for the Sarthe, sat beneath his poplar on two heavy stones, reviewed the glorious future that would no longer come, and in a resolute, nearly soundless act glided into the deep pool he had long selected.Madame Granson returned at half-past nine to find a letter: “My good mother, I have departed; don’t be angry with me.” She thought he had gone off cleverly and went to bed in peace.The Sarthe, having risen suddenly the previous morning, had brought eels and, with them, the fisherman’s net at the very place Athanase had flung himself. At six in the morning the fisherman drew in his net, and with it the young body. Friends prepared the mother for the remains. The news of the suicide caused great excitement in Alencon: the poor young man of genius had no protector the night before and a thousand voices cried, “I would have helped him.” The Chevalier de Valois explained the suicide in a spirit of revenge, revealing Athanase’s sincere love for Mademoiselle Cormon. Madame Granson, guided by the chevalier, recalled many details that confirmed him. The story became touching; many women wept. Madame Granson’s grief was silent, concentrated, and little understood. Mothers mourn in two ways—public mourning for sons admired by society, and the deeper private mourning known only to the one who has loved and shared a child’s innermost life.Before Madame du Bousquier returned to town, Madame du Ronceret had driven to Prebaudet to fling this corpse upon the roses of the bride’s joy and sober her bridal month. On the road back to Alencon, Madame du Bousquier met Madame Granson at the corner of rue Val-Noble; the mother’s dying look, full of maledictions and reproach, struck the poor woman to the heart.The evening after the catastrophe Madame Granson, though hostile to the town’s rector, sought him out. After placing her son in his shroud with her own hands, she found the modest priest putting away flax and yarns in an outer room—charity that saved many who could not beg. He took her to his dining-room; she noticed his frugal living.“Monsieur l’abbé,” she sobbed, “I have come to implore you—”“I know what brings you,” he replied. “I must trust you and your relation Madame du Bousquier to pacify Monseigneur the Bishop at Seez. Yes, I will pray for your unhappy child; yes, I will say the masses. But avoid scandal; give no opportunity for evil-judging persons. I alone, without other clergy, at night—”“Yes, as you think best; if only he may lie in consecrated ground,” she said, kissing his hand.Toward midnight a coffin was clandestinely borne to the parish church by four young men, comrades of Athanase. A few friends, veiled women, and half a dozen intimates attended. The rector, assisted by a discreet choirboy, said the mortuary mass. The body was noiselessly carried to a corner of the cemetery; a black wooden cross without inscription marked its place. Athanase lived and died in shadow. No voice blamed the rector; the bishop was silent. The piety of the mother redeemed the impiety of the son’s last act.Months later, half beside herself with grief, Madame Granson resolved to see the spot where her son had drowned—perhaps to recover thoughts of him beneath that poplar or to see what his eyes had seen last. As she stood there she saw a woman approach, weeping: it was Suzanne. Told of the catastrophe that morning at the hotel du More, Suzanne had planned to send Athanase several thousand francs under a false pretense had he lived. Seeing Madame Granson, she whispered as she passed, “I loved him!”Suzanne did not leave Alencon without changing the bride’s orange blossoms to rue; she declared Madame du Bousquier would always be Mademoiselle Cormon, revenging poor Athanase and her dear chevalier with one cutting tongue.Alencon then witnessed a slower suicide: the Chevalier de Valois died in life for fourteen years. Three months after the du Bousquier marriage, his linen was frayed, his hair unkempt; his elegance collapsed. He ate, snuffed, lost teeth; wrinkles and pallor marked him. The cavalier who had once maintained appearances with meticulous care now showed the power of the soul over the body; with hope deserted, he grew old overnight. His nose, once delicate, smeared with tobacco; his vanity gone, he revealed the pains he had formerly taken. His clever sayings grew rare, but the appetite remained; he languidly took pinches of snuff and alarming dinners. His intercourse with the Princess Goritza lessened.One day he appeared in Mademoiselle Armande’s salon with his calf of leg on his shin-bone; that bankruptcy of grace struck Alencon with horror. The chevalier, who had waited and watched for Mademoiselle Cormon for ten years and missed his game, frightened society by his decay. A mortified grisette revealed his mornings’ details, and he now passed for a libertine; liberals blamed him for foundlings once attributed to du Bousquier. The faubourg Saint-Germain pitied him and gathered him closer, bringing back a few smiles; but enmity piled on du Bousquier. Eleven persons deserted the Cormon salon for the d’Esgrignons.The old maid's marriage defined two parties in Alencon. The salon d’Esgrignon represented upper aristocracy (the returning Troisvilles attached themselves to it); the Cormon salon under du Bousquier represented that class of opinions which, without being truly liberal or resolutely royalist, had given birth to votes like the famous 221. The salon du Ronceret, secretly allied to Cormon, was boldly liberal.The Abbe de Sponde, after returning from Prebaudet, suffered inwardly and told no word to his niece but to Mademoiselle Armande he confessed he would have preferred the Chevalier de Valois to Monsieur du Bousquier. The abbe mourned the loss of his little grove and lindens—symbols of his home and prayers—cut down for an English garden. Du Bousquier, acting on the Abbe Couterier’s authorization, had committed wrongs to please his wife, but the restorations destroyed the venerable dignity and patriarchal air of the old house. The salon’s bourgeois dignity no longer existed when turned to white and gold with mahogany and blue satin; columns in stucco, glass doors, Greek moldings—a mélange of styles—produced magnificence out of season.The town gabbled at first, but months later took pride in the luxury; rich manufacturers restored houses, modern furniture arrived, and astral lamps were seen.The Abbe de Sponde perceived the secret unhappiness this marriage imposed on his niece. The character of noble simplicity that had ruled their lives was lost the first winter, when du Bousquier gave two balls every month; the abbe prayed on his knees while the revels lasted. Political tone in the salon was perverted. The abbe fathomed du Bousquier; he shuddered at his imperious tone and saw his niece lose control of her property—left only the linen and table to manage—while Jacquelin became coachman, Rene groom, and the Parisian chef took over. The triumph of the will is intoxicating to narrow minds; one must have been a dismissed minister to know the bitter pain of losing power.At first du Bousquier constrained himself out of respect for the abbe and to avoid vexing him, but it was his hand that dealt the blow sending the old priest to his grave. If you view "intolerance" as firmness of principle, and do not condemn the Catholic soul of the Abbe de Sponde, you can understand his sorrow when he saw in his niece’s salon the apostate priest reconciled with the rector—the renunciation of orthodoxy that to him was treachery. Du Bousquier, secretly ambitious to lay down the law to the town, reconciled the minister of Saint-Leonard with the parish rector; his wife thought he had accomplished peace, but the abbe saw perfidy. The bishop visited du Bousquier; orthodoxy seemed to expire in the diocese. The abbe died when orthodoxy thus expired in the diocese.In 1819 the abbe’s property increased Madame du Bousquier’s income to twenty-five thousand francs, not counting Prebaudet or the Val-Noble house. Du Bousquier returned to his wife the capital of her savings, and made her use it to buy lands contiguous to Prebaudet, enlarging the domain considerably. He passed for one of the department’s richest men—a constant candidate of the liberals, missing electoral victories by slim margins, then ostentatiously repudiating liberals by seeking ministerial royalist election. This rancorous republican, mad with ambition, resolved to rival and ruin the royalism and aristocracy of Alencon when they regained power. He strengthened himself with the Church by a deceitful feint of piety: accompanying his wife to mass, giving to convents, aiding congregations, siding with clergy. Secretly supported by liberals, protected by the Church, calling himself a constitutional royalist, he kept beside the aristocracy to ruin it—he succeeded.Among other benefits he gave money to revive point d’Alencon manufacture, renewed the linen trade, and the town gained a factory. Insulating himself upon popular interests, he risked little—backed by fortune he could wait. He posed as a banker, a partner in enterprises taking security; he promoted insurance companies, public conveyances, petitions for roads and bridges. The government, warned, considered this encroachment, and a struggle began injudiciously; authorities yielded as community interest demanded. Du Bousquier embittered provincial nobility against court nobility and the peerage; he brought constitutional royalists to side with the “Journal des Debats” and M. de Chateaubriand against the throne—an ungrateful opposition based on ignoble interests that was one cause of journalism’s triumph in 1830.Thus du Bousquier, common to his class, watched royalty’s funeral. After 1830 he became receiver-general, relying on relations with the Duc d’Orleans and Monsieur de Folmon, receiving about eighty thousand francs a year. In the public eye he was respectable, steady, upright, and obliging. Alencon owed him its industrial connection and the sudden proliferation of private carriages. Burghers and landowners, alarmed at first that prices might rise, later found revenues helped by increased style of living. Du Ronceret’s remark, “Du Bousquier is a very strong man,” was adopted by the countryside.But unhappily for his wife, that saying had a double meaning. The public politician did not resemble the domestic husband. This great citizen, generous to the world, was a despot at home, utterly devoid of conjugal affection. Astute, hypocritical, sly—this Cromwell of the Val-Noble—behaved as he did to aristocrats: velvet glove on iron hand. His wife bore no children; Suzanne’s remark and the chevalier’s insinuations thus seemed justified. The liberal bourgeoisie and church party blamed Madame du Bousquier, saying she was too old when she married and that pregnancy would be dangerous. Friends praised du Bousquier to his wife, saying she was lucky, he’d make her queen of the department. Misjudged by society, the pious woman found opportunity at home to display virtues. She lived in tears but presented a placid face. She told herself she had loved the Chevalier de Valois but had married du Bousquier; Athanase’s death pursued her like remorse. Her uncle’s death made her life more sorrowful; sorrow spread slow as oil, imbibing into her being.The Chevalier de Valois contrived to reveal du Bousquier’s baseness to her. The chevalier, expert in love, divined the married man. Du Bousquier’s salon was closed to him and to those who had slighted the Cormon mansion; he was impervious to ridicule, with vast fortune and power. To please her husband, Madame du Bousquier had broken off relations with the d’Esgrignon household, visiting only briefly when her husband left for Paris. Three years after her marriage, at the Abbe de Sponde’s death, Mademoiselle Armande led Madame du Bousquier to Mr. d’Esgrignon’s. Finding the Chevalier de Valois there, he took her hand and in a tender, fatherly manner questioned her happiness. Surrounded by Mademoiselle Armande, Madame de Troisville, and the marquise, they gently contrived to draw from her, in naïve confession, the secrets of her house—her inability to have children and the deceptions of her marriage. The chevalier thus rendered du Bousquier for a time ridiculous and odious; ridicule waned, gossip died, but the incident envenomed du Bousquier’s hatred for the house of Esgrignon.By way of reprisals, du Bousquier, who had created the journal "Courrier de l’Orne," inserted: “Bonds to the amount of one thousand francs a year will be paid to any person who can prove the existence of one Monsieur de Pombreton before, during, or after the Emigration.” He turned vengeance into public pen.Although her marriage was essentially negative, Madame du Bousquier saw some advantages: a remarkable man about her was preferable to solitude. Du Bousquier was preferable to a dog or canaries. He showed for his wife a sentiment more real and less selfish than that felt by servants or hopeful heirs. Later she regarded her husband as an instrument of divine wrath for past desires: she had brought sorrow on Madame Granson and hastened her uncle’s death; obedient to religion that bids one kiss the rod, she praised him publicly but wept in prayer.With feelings bruised and immolated, compelled to make her husband happy and attached to him by habit and a dutiful affection, her life became perpetual contradiction. She had married a man she hated but whom she must care for. Often she walked with angels when du Bousquier ate her preserves with satisfaction. She watched to see his slightest wish satisfied, seeing in his small attentions proofs of his oblivious duty. When he kissed her forehead and called her “a good woman,” tears came to her eyes. Du Bousquier likely made some concessions, enough to earn her respect; Catholic virtue did not require total dissimulation. Often she sat mutely and listened as men she admired ranged against the Church; she shuddered at the ruin she foresaw. A stupid word from her was cut short by his glance.The worries stupefied her; she found it easier and more dignified to concentrate on inner thoughts and resign herself to a life purely animal—slave-like submission as meritorious. She gave herself to severe religious practices and ceased thinking of Satan’s vanities. She presented to the world a union of Christian virtue; du Bousquier was certainly one of the luckiest men in France.“She will be a simpleton to her last breath,” said the former collector, who nevertheless dined with her twice a week.This history would be incomplete without noting the coincidence of the Chevalier de Valois’s death with that of Suzanne’s mother. The chevalier died with the monarchy, in August, 1830. He had joined Charles X.’s cortege at Nonancourt and piously escorted it to Cherbourg with the Troisvilles and others. He had carried fifty thousand francs—the sum of his savings—offering it to a faithful friend of the king for transmission, speaking of his approaching death and declaring the money came originally from the king’s goodness. Whether his zeal moved the abandoned Bourbon is unknown; Cesarine, residuary legatee, received only six hundred francs a year from his estate. The chevalier returned to Alencon, weakened by grief and fatigue, and died on the very day Charles X. arrived on a foreign shore.Madame du Val-Noble and her protector, fearing liberal vengeance, lingered in the village where Suzanne’s mother died. At the chevalier’s sale, Suzanne bought the famous snuff-box’s portrait of Princess Goritza for a thousand francs; two years later a collector obtained it from Madame du Val-Noble. If the dead could know what happens after them, the chevalier’s head would blush upon its left cheek.If this history pricks possessors of relics to make codicils and bequeath such souvenirs to loving hands, it will serve the romantic portion of the community. It contains a higher moral: the need for a new species of education, chairs of anthropology—Germany outstripping France here. Modern myths are even less understood than ancient ones; myths press us from every point, serving all theories. If Mademoiselle Cormon had been a reader or student, had there been in the Orne a professor of anthropology, or even if she had read Ariosto, the frightful disasters of her conjugal life might never have occurred. She would probably have known why Angelica prefers Medoro to Orlando—Medoro as the mythic courtier, Orlando the myth of impotent revolutions. We publish this opinion of a pupil of Monsieur Ballanche without assuming responsibility.No information reached us about the fate of the negroes’ heads in diamonds. You may see Madame du Val-Noble every evening at the Opera; thanks to the chevalier’s education she has almost the air of a well-bred woman.Madame du Bousquier still lives; is not that as much as to say she still suffers? After sixty—the age women allow confessions—she told Madame du Coudrai she had never been able to endure the idea of dying an old maid.ADDENDUMThe following personages appear in other stories of the Human Comedy.  Beatrix       The Unconscious Humorists     Granson, Athanase       The Government Clerks (mentioned only)     Lenoncourt, Duc de       The Lily of the Valley       Cesar Birotteau       The
