His fears were justified: disaster hovered.Indifferent to political reactions, David tried to remain neutral on burning issues. But in the provinces men of business professed politics to secure patronage; the Cointet Brothers deliberately cultivated monarchical opinion. They fasted on Fridays, kept Lent, haunted the cathedral, cultivated the clergy. When devotional books came back into demand, the Cointets profited handsomely. They slandered David as a Liberal and as immoral because of his father’s reputation; they argued they needed the business for their families and urged customers away.These tales worked. The monopoly of prefectorial and diocesan work gradually passed to the Cointet Brothers; David’s remaining circulation fell by half as rivals started a second local sheet of announcements. The Cointets grew richer from their devotional books and offered to buy Séchard’s printing-paper and the departmental announcements.The news sent David’s father rushing from Marsac. “Leave me to manage the Cointets,” he said; “don’t you meddle.” He saw what the Cointets meant and took alarm. If David gave up the paper, the connection would be lost; all depended on the paper. The Cointets had tried to ruin the Séchards by accusing them of Liberalism, giving them a plank to cling to—the Liberal customers should keep the Séchards. “Sell the paper! Why, you might as well sell the stock-in-trade and the license!”Old Séchard, fond of his son, now sided with him—at least in appearance—bringing his son to the front as a peasant brings in his wife. He asked the Cointets sixty thousand francs for the printing business so as not to ruin his son. David was unwilling; offers were wrung from the Cointets until, after effort, they offered twenty-two thousand francs for the Charente Chronicle. But David had to pledge thenceforward to print no newspaper under penalty of thirty thousand francs damages.That deal was the deathblow to the Séchard establishment; but the old vinegrower cared little. To pocket the ready money he consented to abandon his share of the business but not the premises; the rent remained at the famous twelve hundred francs per annum.After the sale, he came into town rarely, pleading age, though he still loved his stock-in-trade. The old foreman, who had gone to the rival establishment, knew exactly how much this fatherly generosity was worth; the old fox meant to reserve a right to interfere and had arranged to appear in the bankruptcy as a privileged creditor for arrears of rent.The causes of David’s heedlessness illustrate his character. A few days after taking over the paternal office he met an old school friend in dire poverty. Lucien Chardon, about twenty-one, son of a surgeon-major, had been brought up in scientific habits. His father, a chemist by bent, had run a retail druggist business and died having worked on a gout specific; the great discovery that should have brought wealth was lost with him. The father had married a beautiful woman of the Rubempré family, who had been saved from the guillotine in 1793 by the claim she was pregnant. The marriage had been made in straitened circumstances; their children inherited the mother’s beauty but were left almost destitute when the father died.M. Chardon’s devotion to science might have led him to give his children a good education. The family had been accustomed to a comfortable income; now poor, the mother and daughter accepted humble employments. Mme. Chardon sold the druggist’s shop and, on small capital income of three hundred francs invested, earned seven francs a week as a monthly nurse, taking the name Madame Charlotte to spare Lucien embarrassment. Lucien’s sister Eve worked for a laundress and earned modest wages. The combined incomes—Mme. Chardon’s rentes of three hundred and the women’s scant earnings—amounted to about eight hundred francs a year for three persons, barely adequate.Lucien, once a brilliant pupil at Angoulême grammar school and in the third form when David left, had been trained by a father’s passion for natural science. Worn out by penury and impatient at twenty, he accepted David’s generous offer of forty francs a month to come and learn the printer’s reader’s work—David had no real need of a reader but saved Lucien from despair. Their school friendship renewed, their bond deepened through shared hopes and misfortune. Both possessed intelligence and literary taste; Lucien aimed at speculative natural science and literary fame; David, meditative and poetic, was drawn to natural science too. They soon became almost brothers in devotion. David glimpsed Eve’s fair face and loved, and, seeing the mother and sister’s blind devotion to Lucien, was moved to add his support; he believed in Lucien’s genius with exaggerated confidence.In a moment of urgent need the two ruminated on ways to make a fortune. Lucien recalled two of his father’s ideas: a chemical method to refine sugar and an American vegetable fibre for making paper à la chinoise, promising huge savings in raw material. David seized on the paper-fibre idea, seeing a fortune and treating Lucien as benefactor.Their ruling thoughts and inner lives unfitted them for the practical running of a printing-house. Instead of making fifteen to twenty thousand francs as the Cointets did, Séchard & Son made barely three hundred francs per month, from which the foreman’s salary, Marion’s wages, rent and taxes had to be paid; David himself scarcely made twelve hundred francs a year. Active businesspeople would have bought new type and machinery and sought cheap Paris orders; but master and foreman, absorbed in intellectual pursuits, were content with whatever business trickled in.The Cointets, understanding David’s habits, did not anathematize him now; policy required letting the business flicker on so it wouldn’t be taken over by a stronger rival. They sent prospectuses and job-printing work to the Séchards, a practice akin to mail-coach owners setting up sham opposition coaches to keep rivals out. Unwittingly, David owed his commercial existence to their cunning. They behaved fairly in appearance but were tactically clever.Both inside and out, the condition of the Séchard establishment testified to the old “bear’s” sordid avarice, who never spent a sou on repairs. The old house had weathered sun and rain until it resembled a venerable tree trunk, seamed and cracked. The front, brick and stone, bent beneath a worm-eaten roof of curved pantiles. Decrepit casements, heavy shutters, iron cross bars—there was no more dilapidated house in Angoulême. Picture the workshop: lit at either end and dark in the middle; walls plastered with handbills and begrimed by thirty years of handling; a cobweb of cordage across the ceiling; stacks of paper, old-fashioned presses, slabs for weighting damp sheets, rows of cases, and two dens in the corners where the master printer and foreman sat—and you will have some idea of the life lived by the two friends.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################One day early in May, 1821, David and Lucien stood by the window looking into the yard. It was nearly two o’clock and a few men were going out to dinner. David waited until the apprentice had shut the street door with the bell fastened; then, as if the smells of paper, ink and presses had grown intolerable, he led Lucien into the yard and they sat under the vines, keeping the office and door in view. Sunbeams played among the trellised shoots, making an aureole about their heads and heightening the contrast between their faces and characters.David’s frame belonged to the fighter: broad shoulders, a full chest, thick black hair, and a high-colored swarthy face on a thick neck that at first suggested rough solidity. On closer view the lines about his lips, the dimple in his chin, the square nostrils and broad irregular cleft, and above all his steady eyes—lit by an absorbing love—revealed the real character: a thoughtful, strenuous melancholy. He was a spirit who discerned horizons on either side and saw to the end of winding ways, quick to analyze joys still only ideas and quick to turn from them in disgust. One expected flashes of genius and saw the ashes of an extinguished volcano—hopes quenched beneath the sense of social annihilation to which low birth and lack of fortune condemn so many loftier minds. Near this poor printer, who loathed a handicraft so allied to intellectual work, stood Lucien, graceful as a sculptured Bacchus.Lucien’s face had antique distinction: a Greek profile, velvet-white skin, blue eyes set in pearly whites, dewy and fresh as a child’s. Those eyes shone beneath long chestnut lashes and penciled brows. Silken down on his cheeks and bright curling hair gleamed golden in the sunlight. A divine graciousness transfigured his white temples; a matchless nobleness set its seal on a short, raised chin. The smile about his coral lips—a sorrowing angel’s smile—contrasted with even teeth. His shapely hands, slender figure, and feminine hips might make one think him a girl in disguise, a trait common to keen-witted men. Yet his restless mind often stood on the lower ground of diplomatists who hold that success justifies any means. One misfortune of great intellects is that they comprehend both good and evil.Both judged society by a lofty standard because their social position lay at the lowest end of the scale; unrecognized power avenged lowly station by viewing the world from on high. Still, such flights left them more bitter and hopeless. Lucien had read much and compared; David had thought much and deeply. Despite David’s robust, country-bred look, his mind was melancholic and somewhat morbid—he lacked confidence; but Lucien, despite an almost effeminate figure, possessed a high degree of Gascon temperament—rash, brave, adventurous, inclined to see the bright side and shrink from the dark. He would stoop at no crime if anything could be gained, and laugh at vice that served as a stepping-stone. For the moment these tendencies were checked partly by the fair illusions of youth and partly by the enthusiasm that leads every man in love with glory to prefer nobler methods at first. Lucien struggled with himself and his desires, not with the baseness of others; he was at strife with his own power. The brilliancy of his intellect attracted David keenly. David admired his friend yet kept him out of scrapes into which the furie française might lead him.David, with a well-balanced mind and timid nature at variance with a strong constitution, had the persistence of the Northern temper; though he saw all difficulties, he vowed to conquer and never give way. In him apostolic unswerving virtue was softened by pity springing from inexhaustible indulgence. In the old friendship one was the worshiper—David; Lucien ruled him like a woman sure of love, and David loved to give way. He felt that his friend’s physical beauty implied real superiority; he accepted himself as coarser clay. “The ox for patient labor in the fields, the free life for the bird,” he thought. “I will be the ox, and Lucien shall be the eagle.”For three years they had shared destinies bright with promise. Together they read great recent works—Schiller, Goethe, Byron, Scott, Jean-Paul, and others—warming themselves at these fires; tried their powers in abortive creations, work laid aside and taken up again with renewed enthusiasm. Incessantly they worked with youthful vitality; comrades in poverty, comrades in consuming love of art and science, they forgot the hard present, minds bent on laying foundations of future fame.“Lucien,” said David, “do you know what I have just received from Paris?” He drew a tiny volume from his pocket. “Listen!” David read André de Chénier’s Idyll Néere, then Le Malade, the Elegy on a Suicide, another elegy in the classic taste, and the last two Iambes.“So that is André de Chénier!” Lucien exclaimed. “It fills one with despair!” he cried when David surrendered the book, overcome. “A poet rediscovered by a poet!” he added, reading the prefatory signature. “After Chénier had written those poems, he thought he had written nothing worth publishing,” David told him.Lucien read aloud the fragment L’Aveugle and two Elegies; on meeting the line, “If they know not bliss, is there happiness on earth?” he pressed the book to his lips and both men shed tears, for the two friends were lovers and fellow-worshipers.The vine-stems were changing with spring; they covered the rifted walls of the old house, with fluted columns, knots, bas-reliefs and little grotesques—fairy-work in a gloomy yard. Fancy had scattered flowers and crimson gems over the place, and Chénier’s Camille became for David an Eve to worship, for Lucien a great lady to whom he paid homage. Poetry had shaken out her starry robe above the workshop where “monkeys” and “bears” were grotesquely busy among types and presses. Five o’clock struck, but the friends felt neither hunger nor thirst; life had turned to a golden dream, and all the treasures of the world lay at their feet. Far on the horizon lay the blue streak to which Hope points in storm and stress; a siren voice sounded, calling, “Come, spread your wings; through that streak of gold or azure lies the way of escape from evil fortune!”Just then the low glass door of the workshop opened, and out came Cérizet, an apprentice David had brought from Paris. He introduced a stranger who saluted politely and spoke to David.“This, sir, is a monograph I desire to print,” he said, producing a huge package of manuscript. “Will you oblige me with an estimate?”“We do not undertake work on such a scale, sir,” David answered without looking at the manuscript. “You had better see the Messieurs Cointet about it.”“Still we have a very pretty type which might suit it,” Lucien put in, taking up the roll. “Leave your commission and call again to-morrow, and we will give an estimate.”“Have I the pleasure of addressing M. Lucien Chardon?”“Yes, sir,” said the foreman.“I am fortunate to meet a young poet destined to such greatness,” returned the author. “Mme. de Bargeton sent me here.”Lucien flushed at the name and stammered gratitude for Mme. de Bargeton’s interest. David noticed his friend’s embarrassed flush and left him in conversation with the country gentleman, the author of a monograph on silk-culture, prompted by vanity to print the effort for fellow-members of the local agricultural society.When the author had gone, David spoke.“Lucien, are you in love with Mme. de Bargeton?”“Passionately.”“But social prejudices set you as far apart as if she were in Pekin and you in Greenland.”“The will of two lovers can rise victorious over all things,” said Lucien, lowering his eyes.“You will forget us,” returned the alarmed lover, as Eve’s fair face rose before his mind.“On the contrary, I have perhaps sacrificed my love to you,” cried Lucien.“What do you mean?”“In spite of my love, and motives that might secure a footing in her house, I have told her I will never go thither again unless another is made welcome too, a man whose gifts are greater than mine—David Séchard, my brother, my friend. I shall find an answer waiting when I go home. All the aristocrats may have been asked to hear me read this evening, but I shall not go if the answer is negative, and I will never set foot in Mme. de Bargeton’s house again.”David brushed tears from his eyes and wrung Lucien’s hand. The clock struck six.“Eve must be anxious; good-bye,” Lucien added abruptly and hurried away. David stood overcome by an emotion deep for his age and position—the friends were like two young swans with wings unclipped by provincial experience.“Heart of gold!” David exclaimed as his eyes followed Lucien across the workshop.Lucien went down to L’Houmeau along the broad Promenade de Beaulieu, the Rue du Minage, and Saint-Peter’s Gate. It was the longest way round, so of course Mme. de Bargeton’s house lay on the route. So delicious it was to pass under her windows, though she knew nothing of his presence, that for two months he had gone round daily by the Palet Gate into L’Houmeau.Under the trees of Beaulieu he saw how far the suburb lay from the city. Custom had raised barriers harder to surmount than the steep steps Lucien descended. Youth and ambition had thrown a flying-bridge of glory across the gulf between the city and suburb, yet Lucien was uneasy over his lady’s answer like any favorite who fears to fall. This must be clear to those unfamiliar with the manners of a city divided into upper and lower town; therefore it is necessary to enter upon topographical details, for comprehension of Mme. de Bargeton’s position.The old city of Angoulême perches aloft on a crag like a sugar-loaf, overlooking the plain where the Charente winds through meadows. The crag is an outlying spur on the Périgord side of a long ridge, ending abruptly above the road from Paris to Bordeaux; the Rock of Angoulême marks three picturesque valleys. The ramparts, great gateways and ruined fortress on the summit still witness to the stronghold’s importance during the Religious Wars. But this old strength is a source of modern weakness: Angoulême could not spread down to the Charente, and shut in between ramparts and steep crag, the old town was condemned to stagnation.Government attempts to extend the town by building a Prefecture, a Naval School, and barracks opened roads, but private enterprise had been beforehand. The suburb of L’Houmeau sprang up at the foot of the crag and along the riverside where the road from Paris to Bordeaux runs. The great paper-mills of Angoulême, established centuries ago on the Charente and its branches, and other water-based industries clustered there where the river’s fall served their needs. The largest state factory of marine ordnance was at Ruelle some six miles away. Carriers, wheelwrights, posthouses, inns, and every trade that lives by road or river crowded Lower Angoulême to avoid the ascent. Naturally tanneries, laundries, and waterside trades stood within reach of the Charente; along the banks lay stores of brandy and warehouses full of water-borne raw material; the carrying trade lined the quays with buildings.Thus the Faubourg of L’Houmeau grew into a busy, prosperous city—a second Angoulême rivaling the upper town, the residence of powers that be—though L’Houmeau remained an appendage of the city above. The noblesse and officialdom dwelt on the crag; trade and wealth remained below. No love is lost between these two sections anywhere, and in Angoulême it was hard to say which detested the other more. Under the Empire the machinery worked fairly smoothly, but the Restoration intensified the feud.Nearly every house in the upper town is inhabited by noble or old burgher families who live on incomes—an autochthonous nation that suffers no aliens. After two hundred years of residence, a family from another district may be adopted, but in the eyes of the aboriginal race they are newcomers of yesterday. Prefects, receivers-general, and various administrations had tried to tame the ancient families perched aloft like wary ravens; the families were always willing to accept invitations to dinners and dances, but as to admitting strangers to their houses they were inexorable. Ready to scoff and disparage, jealous and niggardly, marrying only among themselves, they formed a serried phalanx to keep out intruders. Of modern luxury they had no notion; as for sending a boy to Paris, they thought it certain ruin. Such sagacity measures their old-world manners—thick-headed Royalism infected with bigotry rather than zeal, all stagnating together, motionless as their town on a rock. Yet Angoulême enjoyed a great regional reputation for educational advantages, and neighbors sent daughters to its boarding schools and convents.It is easy to imagine the influence of class sentiment holding Angoulême aloof from L’Houmeau. The merchant classes were rich, the noblesse usually poor. Each side took revenge in scorn. “He is a man of L’Houmeau!” a shopkeeper of the upper town would say of a lower-suburb merchant with an accent words cannot describe. When the Restoration defined the noblesse’s position, holding out hopes realizable only by complete topsy-turvydom, the distance between Angoulême and L’Houmeau, already more marked than that between hill and plain, widened further. The better families, all devoted to the Government, grew more exclusive here than elsewhere. “The man of L’Houmeau” became little better than a pariah. Hence the deep, smothered hatred that broke out ugly in the insurrection of 1830 and destroyed durable social elements in France. As the Court nobles’ overweening haughtiness detached the provincial noblesse from the throne, so did these last alienate the bourgeoisie from the royal cause by behavior that galled their vanity.So “a man of L’Houmeau,” a druggist’s son, in Mme. de Bargeton’s house was nothing less than a little revolution. Who was responsible? Lamartine and Victor Hugo, Casimir Delavigne and Canalis, Béranger and Chateaubriand—old and young names in literature, Liberals and Royalists alike—must share the blame, for Mme. de Bargeton loved art and letters—an eccentric taste much deplored in Angoulême. In justice to the lady it is necessary to sketch the previous history of a woman born to shine, left by unlucky circumstances in the shade, a woman whose influence decided Lucien’s career.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################M. de Bargeton descended from Mirault, an alderman of Bordeaux ennobled under Louis XIII for long service. His son, Mirault de Bargeton, became an officer in Louis XIV’s household and married a large fortune; by Louis XV’s reign his son dropped Mirault and was known simply as M. de Bargeton. That descendant dissipated the family wealth. Two brothers returned to trade, so the Mirault name still appears among Bordeaux merchants. The Bargeton lands in Angoumois, in the barony of Rochefoucauld, and the town house in Angoulême, the Hôtel Bargeton, were entailed and thus passed to the prodigal’s grandson. The Revolution stripped him of seignorial rights, leaving rents of about ten thousand francs a year. Had earlier prudence prevailed, the family might have risen to a marquisate or even a dukedom; instead, by 1805 the present M. de Bargeton thought himself fortunate to marry Mlle. Marie-Louise-Anaïs de Nègrepelisse, daughter of a noble reduced to the quiet life of a manor-house. The Nègrepelisses traced their blood to an old southern family; a Nègrepelisse had once been among St. Louis’s hostages, and the elder branch became d’Espard by marriage in Henri Quatre’s time. M. de Nègrepelisse, a younger son of a younger son, lived off his wife’s property near Barbezieux, farming shrewdly, distilling brandy, and accumulating silver crowns.In the remote countryside unusual events shaped Mme. de Bargeton’s taste for music and reading. During the Revolution Abbé Niollant, the Abbé Roze’s best pupil, took refuge at the manor-house of Escarbas and brought his compositions. The hospitable gentleman was rewarded: the Abbé undertook the education of his host’s daughter. Without him she might have been left to herself or to an ignorant servant. The Abbé, a musician and man of letters fluent in Italian and German, taught Naïs those languages, counterpoint, and explained masterpieces of French, German, and Italian literature. In enforced seclusion he also taught her Latin and Greek and touched on natural science. A mother might have moderated the effects of such an education on a spirited girl; the Abbé’s artistic temperament, admirable though it was, inclined him to liberty of judgment and breadth of view that, while pardoned in society for depth and originality, could lead private minds astray. He was both good-hearted and an enthusiast, and his pride in his pupil—like an author’s pride—flattered her vanity. Isolated from peers, she learned fearless criticism and ready judgment, qualities useful in men but ill-suited to a woman destined for domestic life. Although the Abbé counseled modesty and graciousness, Naïs formed a high opinion of herself and robust contempt for ordinary people. With no social intercourse to enforce restraint, her bold ideas showed in manner and expression; there was a cavalier air about her more fit to adventurous lives than to provincial society.M. de Nègrepelisse was miserly; he would have given his daughter’s books to save a sick bullock and would not give her two farthings beyond her allowance for an educational need. The Abbé died in 1802 before her marriage—he likely would not have approved of it—leaving her father anxious about managing her. Naïs had strong opinions about marriage and little inclination toward submitting to the commonplace men she knew. She wished to rule rather than obey and preferred the freedom of a lover who pleased her to the obedience marriage entailed.Fearing a mésalliance, M. de Nègrepelisse resolved to marry his daughter for his own peace. He wanted a noble or country gentleman: not too clever, not meddling with trustees’ accounts, sufficiently dull to let Naïs have her way, and able to take her without a dowry. Such a son-in-law was hard to find. M. de Bargeton appeared the only fit man: forty, much worn by amorous dissipations, regarded as of feeble intellect, but possessing commonsense enough to manage his affairs and breeding enough to avoid social blunders in Angoulême. Naïs was shown how to govern such a husband and was delighted at the prospect of liberty under the guise of conventional marriage, hoping to use her wit and beauty to gain connections in Paris. M. de Bargeton saw the match as brilliant, expecting to profit from the Nègrepelisse estates; but to a dispassionate observer the elder M. de Nègrepelisse seemed likely to outlive his son-in-law.Mme. de Bargeton was thirty-six; her husband fifty-eight and looked much older. She still appeared young, could wear rose-colored dresses, and had loose hair. Their income, about twelve thousand francs, placed them among the half-dozen great fortunes in Angoulême excluding merchants and officials. They had to remain in Angoulême until Mme. de Nègrepelisse’s inheritance came to hand and they could go to Paris. Meanwhile they were attentive to the elder M. de Nègrepelisse, who kept them waiting so long that the son-in-law predeceased him. Naïs’s intellectual gifts and inner riches profited her little in provincial life; isolated from stimulating society, her strong faculties hardened into absurdities. Pride, enthusiasm, and other admirable qualities, when untempered by exposure to greater minds, turned into affectation, exaggeration, and petty pretensions. Knowledge stagnated, taste corrupted, and noble natures were stifled by provincial meanness.Mme. de Bargeton began to overstate every feeling, to make spectacles of her emotions; she used inflated language and neologisms, and she indulged in ceaseless admiration and dramatic reactions to trifles. Her enthusiasm was boundless and indiscriminate: she wept and rhapsodized over every sensation, magnified minor events, and exhausted herself in chronic admiration or dislike. She fancied herself heroic and picturesque, envied dramatic lives, and longed for any destiny more striking than the clear, simple life she led. She adored Byron and Rousseau and any life that seemed romantic or grand. Many called her a harmless lunatic; a shrewder observer might discern these extravagances as fragments of a grandeur that had broken off before fulfillment—love without a lover.The first eighteen years of her marriage can be summarized: she lived on herself and distant hopes. Finding the income too narrow for a promised Paris life, she faced the prospect of spending her days among men who could inspire neither passion nor intellectual companionship. She had no chance encounters to relieve this fate; when the Empire reached its height and Napoleon’s troops marched, her curiosity and hopes revived. At a military ridotto she fell in love with an officer, a sub-lieutenant of good family, who, with dreams of glory, received a token of affection from her: a portrait he wore at Wagram. On that battlefield a shell destroyed the object that recorded her young beauty and the soldier was killed, a grief that left a lasting veil of sadness. This pain deepened the sense of having missed youth’s joys and reinforced her inner austerity. Pride saved her from degrading provincial intrigues; she would have perished of grief had she been debased by the petty men of her town. She lived by poetry and by the great foreign books and essayists that reached France after 1815: they delighted but could not soften or broaden her mind. She became rigid, her dignity turning into a stilted manner and affectation; she ruled her circle with foibles that courtiers adored.Into this setting came Lucien, introduced by a newcomer who stirred Mme. de Bargeton’s monotony. The job of controller of excise fell vacant, and M. de Barante appointed a man whose adventurous life opened the door to Mme. de Bargeton’s salon: M. de Châtelet. Born Sixte Châtelet, he took the particle after 1806 and was one of those agreeable young men who skirted conscription by proximity to the Imperial court. He had been private secretary to an Imperial Highness—an office for which he possessed many outward qualifications: handsome, adept at billiards and dance, passable as an amateur actor, ready with a ballad or a quatrain, and superficial in many arts. He could accompany a singer at the piano, improvise verse, sketch, copy landscapes, and color costumes. His talents were precisely those useful where women exercised influence. He claimed diplomacy as his strong point; it suited men who could say little and keep mystery. He was made a Baron and sent to Cassel as envoy-extraordinary, and Napoleon used him as a courier in a European crisis. Promised a ministerial post in Westphalia, he lost it when the Empire fell and, discouraged, went to Egypt with General de Montriveau. A chain of accidents separated them; Châtelet wandered among Arab tribes and was even sold and resold as a captive, his talents useless among nomads. Eventually he reached Muscat’s territory, boarded an English vessel, and returned to Paris before his former companion. His misfortunes and connections recommended him to the President of the Council, who placed him in M. de Barante’s department until a controllership appeared, and so he came to Angoulême.Baron Sixte du Châtelet learned the manners of the town and took his cue. He affected the air of a world-weary man, habitually raising his hand to his forehead to suggest chronic pain and interesting travels. Socially, he was on good terms with the authorities—the general, prefect, receiver-general, and bishop—but he remained frigid, polite, and slightly supercilious in every house, like a man out of place awaiting favors. When curiosity about him rose, he studied men and women: he found the men nought and saw Mme. de Bargeton as the person to cultivate. He judged music the key to her house—a place where strangers were rarely received—so he procured a Mass by Miroir and learned it on the piano. One Sunday, while Angoulême attended cathedral, he played the organ and sent the ignorant into ecstasies; Mme. de Bargeton, impressed, lamented the lack of opportunity for duets with such a musician and sought him out. By this artifice the adroit baron obtained entrée he could not have secured by direct request.Thus Châtelet entered the queen of Angoulême’s circle and paid her marked attention. At forty-five he saw her dormant youth ready to revive and treasures to be turned to account: perhaps a rich widow, perhaps a political connection through the Nègrepelisse family, perhaps marriage. High-born Angoulême gasped at this intrusion: Mme. de Bargeton’s salon was a sacred space in a society that prided itself on purity. The only outsider usually intimate there was the bishop; the prefect was received only rarely; the receiver-general never dined at the Hôtel de Bargeton—she went to his concerts but did not accept his hospitality. For such genteel minds the appearance of a mere controller of excise in her circle shocked precedence and propriety.Those who understand petty vanities across social levels will appreciate how the bourgeoisie revered the Hôtel de Bargeton: from afar it looked like a miniature Louvre, the glory of Angoumois’ Hôtel de Rambouillet; yet inside it gathered the intellectual poverty and decayed gentility of the surrounding countryside.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Political opinion in the provinces was expressed in loud commonplaces; the Quotidienne was tepidly loyal, and Louis XVIII branded a Jacobin. The women were awkward, silly, ill-dressed, lacking in toilette, talk, flesh, or spirit. Châtelet could hardly have tolerated this society but for his designs on Mme. de Bargeton. Yet the ruined nobility, with old manners and knowledge of tradition, masked many shortcomings. Nobility of feeling seemed more real in the country than in Paris. These provincial Royalists resembled tarnished old silver: antiquated but weighty; their fixed political opinions were a kind of fidelity, their exclusiveness elevating their social value. Each noble had a market price to townsmen, a token of status.Some women, flattered by M. du Châtelet, saw in him what their men lacked and were soothed. They hoped to succeed to “Imperial Highness.” Purists thought du Châtelet might be seen at Mme. de Bargeton’s but nowhere else. He tolerated much insolence but cultivated the clergy and the queen of Angoulême, bringing her the newest books and reading poetry aloud. Together they pretended ecstasy over Romantics; he endured them with patience unusual in a man of the Imperial school, though he hardly understood them. Mme. de Bargeton welcomed the literary renaissance, rejoiced in the Bourbon restoration, and was pleased by M. de Chateaubriand’s calling Victor Hugo “a sublime child.” She pined for Paris and its great men. Du Châtelet, seeing his chance, told her that in Angoulême there was “another sublime child,” a brilliant young poet from L’Houmeau—Lucien—whose verses surpassed the Parisian galaxy, though he knew it not. The headmaster had shown the Baron some admirable lines. Mme. de Bargeton, starved for genius in her provincial circle, was ecstatic and demanded to see the lad; du Châtelet negotiated Lucien’s appearance.For poor provincials the social distances were vast; Lucien’s heart leapt when his headmaster announced that the gates of the Hôtel de Bargeton would open for him. Only his sister knew. Eve, thrifty housekeeper and laundress, devoted savings to procure thin shoes and a new suit, made a frill for his shirt, and cautioned him about manners—no elbows on tables in aristocratic precincts. She accompanied him to St. Peter’s Gate and watched until he reached the Promenade where du Châtelet waited. For Eve, Lucien in Mme. de Bargeton’s house meant the dawn of success; she did not foresee that where ambition begins ingenuous feeling ends.The Hôtel was less grand than imagined: mellow country stone, dismal from the street, plain inside with a neat provincial courtyard and sober, convent-like rooms in good repair. Lucien mounted the old staircase, crossed an antechamber, and entered a wainscoted drawing-room beyond a dim salon. The carved eighteenth-century woodwork was painted gray, monochrome friezes and crimson damask trimmed the walls; old-fashioned furniture crouched under covers. Mme. de Bargeton sat on a sofa, lit by two wax candles.She did not rise but twisted gracefully, smiling on the new poet. He found her dignified; she found him poetry incarnate—his diffidence and beauty enchanted her. She wore a coif of slashed black velvet, a medieval head-dress that heightened her dignity; red-gold curls escaped to frame a white throat and an oval, ardent face. Her refined fingers summoned him to sit close; M. du Châtelet ensconced himself in an easy-chair, and Lucien realized he was alone with Mme. de Bargeton.Those three hours passed to Lucien like a dream he wished prolonged. He adored her feminine exaltation and energy, and was blind to faded cheeks or hard patches of paint; his imagination clung to dazzling particulars. He had brought no verses; she did not ask for them because she meant him to come again to read them. M. Sixte du Châtelet, however, perceived a rival and, mortified, tried to assert himself. He walked with Lucien down to Beaulieu and, with a flourish of self-importance, offered advice and tales of his conquests, speaking as one who would destroy a rival. Yet his bluster barely intimidated the young poet.Lucien returned often enough to grow used to condescension. When nobles called on Naïs and found him present, they greeted him with the patronizing graciousness of the well-bred toward inferiors; Lucien mistook civilities for kindness until their tone stirred his gall and a bitter Republicanism—a phase of many aspirants—began to ferment. Nevertheless, he would endure anything for Naïs.Naïs prophesied boundless fame for him. She flattered and exalted him, held him near as reader and secretary, and cared more for him than she expected. Her conduct mixed familiarity and capricious pride: sometimes a lofty patroness, sometimes tender. The oscillation of dread and hope tormented Lucien, the torture of first love. After two months as benefactress, confidences came: she softened terms of address to “dear,” and he, bolder, called her Naïs; she rebuked him, and he then called her Louise—privileged intimacy that delighted him.One evening she hid a portrait, telling Lucien the sad tale of Cante-Croix’s stainless but cut-short love, provoking his jealousy and despair. When she raised scruples of duty or appearances he could not analyze; he was too boyish and vowed to live for her rather than die for her. Louise then demanded verses for her album—pretext for quarrel in case of tardiness—but when she read his stanzas she was moved and changed. The verses she praised seemed to her evidence of genius, and she kissed him on the forehead. She spoke of perfecting him—teaching languages, refining manners—reasons to keep him perpetually in her orbit. She resumed music for his sake, played Beethoven to send him into ecstasy, and questioned whether such happiness was not enough; he answered “Yes.”Townsfolk buzzed. The week before, she had asked Lucien to dine with the Bargetons as a third, and the town reacted with outrage. Some cried moral decay attributable to Liberal doctrines. Du Châtelet, haughty, joked about Lucien’s mother being Madame Charlotte the monthly nurse—an apparent slur on the poet’s origins. Mme. de Chandour hurried to remonstrate with Mme. de Bargeton. The latter met gossip with composure: genius is often born of humble stock; if they had no money, what would they do? She turned misfortune into virtue, her wit and persistence disarming critics.That evening she used caustic wit before a crowded salon, arguing that because nobles could not themselves produce Molières or Voltaires, they must accept that great men might be upholsterers’ or clockmakers’ sons; genius was not the preserve of birth. Her eccentricity and defense quelled the storm. When Lucien appeared before the whist-tables he was brought forward like a prince she meant to protect, and he faced the cold hostility of those who vowed to expel him with insolence.Naïs had won a victory but weakened her supremacy. Rumor spread; Amélie de Chandour, following du Châtelet’s counsel, set a rival salon on Wednesdays. The habitués were wedded to routine and the mistress of the staircase. The president of the agricultural society saved face by remarking that pre-Revolution nobles admitted men like Dulcos and Grimm—nobodies—but they never received tax-collectors; Châtelet, a tax-collector, remained unacceptable. Du Châtelet, affronted by the slur and envious of Lucien, declared he would possess Mme. de Bargeton; yet in a twist of tactics he sided with her and declared himself Lucien’s friend, feting the poet with a grand dinner. He invited officials—the prefect, receiver-general, colonel, head of the Naval School, president of the Court—and had Lucien recite “The Dying Sardanapalus.” Applause was plentiful; the headmaster extolled the lines as superior to Jean-Baptiste Rousseau. Sixte du Châtelet, thinking a hothouse of adulation might ruin the poet, watched with the calculating interest of a rake waiting to strike.From then on vague reports of a great man in Angoumois circulated. Mme. de Bargeton was praised for her patronage, and she sought general sanction by arranging a soirée with ices, tea, and cakes—novelties in a town where tea was sold mostly as medicine. She summoned the flower of aristocracy to hear Lucien read. She warned him of the social cabal and counselled that only the strong could survive these obstacles. She proposed he abandon his patronymic for the noble name Rubempré, offering to secure royal sanction through her relation, the Marquise d’Espard. The words “King” and “Marquise” dazzled Lucien; baptism into a noble name seemed requisite.Mme. de Bargeton traversed social strata to show him that such a change would elevate him. She urged Lucien to renounce the equality rhetoric of ’89, fed his taste for social distinction, and tamed David’s commonsense. The once-rabid Liberal turned into a secret Monarchist: he longed for rank, luxury, and fame and swore to win laurels to lay at his lady’s feet, even if blood must be spilled. He would conquer at all costs. To prove his courage he confessed his present hardships, the shackles of poverty borne with pride, his long days of work for David, nights of study. His ardor recalled the colonel of twenty-six; Louise’s eyes softened; Lucien seized a hand and kissed it with the frenzy of lover and poet; she allowed him to press his lips to her forehead.Mme. de Bargeton’s arguments—men of genius must sacrifice family and law to grow—fell on Lucien’s worst inclinations and spread corruption in his heart. She invoked Bernard Palissy, Louis XI, Fox, Napoleon, Columbus, Caesar—talking up the right of genius to set itself above law and to take what it needed. For Lucien, with desires hot, all means seemed admissible. Unconsciously he stood between genius’s promise and a shameful end in the hulks; on one hand the prop of prophetic Sinai, on the other the hideous shame of Gomorrah.Louise loosened his provincial bonds so effectively that he resolved on an experiment to secure her love without risk of rebuff. The forthcoming soirée offered a chance. Ambition and love intertwined: he loved and meant to rise. Society’s banquet early aroused ambition, robbing youth of its original charm and corrupting generous thoughts into mercenary scheming. Lucien persuaded himself his plotting was for David’s sake.He wrote a long, impassioned letter to Louise—copied thrice—detailing his father’s thwarted genius, his own grinding poverty, praising his sister as an angel and David as a future Cuvier, friend, and father-figure. He implored her to do for David what she had done for him, to lift David into his deserved place. The letter was a blend of boyish casuistry, naive eloquence, and romantic pleading—the kind of revelation women love for its unconscious display of the writer’s heart.Lucien left the letter with the housemaid and spent the day at the office reading proofs, supervising orders, and attending to the printing-house, saying nothing to David. Youth retains its reticence; perhaps he feared David’s clear eyes. But when he later read Chénier’s poems with David, the secret rose in his heart under the sting of reproach, like an unhealed wound probed.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################And now try to understand Lucien’s troubled thoughts as he left Angoulême. Was the great lady angry with him? Would she receive David? Had he, in his ambition, plunged back into the depths of L’Houmeau? Before he kissed Louise’s forehead he had time to measure the distance between a queen and her favorite; he knew how far he had climbed in five months, and although David might leap the same ground at once, Lucien did not yet grasp how completely the lower orders were excluded from that upper world. A second indiscretion would ruin Mme. de Bargeton: once accused of liking canaille, Louise would be shunned like a leper. Naïs might have broken moral law and been defended by clergy and aristocracy, but admitting humble people to her house was an unforgivable sin. The powerful are forgiven their faults; once they abdicate, they pay. And what was it but abdication to receive David?Lucien’s aristocratic instinct found other obstacles. Fine manners can be acquired—Racine had a noble air, Corneille looked like a cattle-dealer—but David lacked the trifles Nature had given Lucien: a graceful figure, distinguished features, a certain ring in the voice. Lucien, of gentle blood on his mother’s side and with a high-arched foot, could imagine Mme. de Bargeton’s repressed smile at David’s appearance. Not ashamed of his friend, he resolved to be prudent and risk no repeat of such experiments.Thus, after the hour of poetry and self-sacrifice came the hour of worldly wisdom and scheming. Back in L’Houmeau he wished he had not written the letter; he would have taken it back if he could. Down the vista of the future he glimpsed the inexorable law that nothing succeeds like success, and it cost him to step down from the first rung of the ladder he meant to climb. Pictures of his simple life rose before him—David, generous and self-sacrificing; his mother proud in her lowly lot; his sister submissive and gracious; his own unspoiled conscience—and the past bloomed.He told himself it was nobler to hew his way through hostile society by repeated strokes than to reach the goal through a woman’s favor. Sooner or later genius would shine; others had tamed society; women would love him when that day came. He flung his schemes to the wind and blamed himself. Lucien was easily moved between extremes.He had none of the scholar’s love of retreat; for a month he had felt shame at seeing POSTEL (LATE CHARDON), PHARMACEUTICAL CHEMIST, in yellow letters on green over the shop. It offended him that his father’s name should be posted where every carriage passed. Every evening, after closing the ugly iron gate and going up to Beaulieu to give his arm to Mme. de Bargeton among the dandies, he chafed at the disparity between his lodging and his fortune.“I love Mme. de Bargeton; perhaps soon she will be mine, yet here I live in this rat-hole!” he thought as he descended into the little yard behind the shop. Bundles of boiled herbs lay along the wall, an apprentice scoured a caldron, and M. Postel, in a laboratory apron, inspected some chemical product while watching the door.A strong scent of camomile and peppermint pervaded the yard and the poor dwelling reached by a short ladder with ropes for handrails. Lucien’s room was an attic under the roof.“Good-day, sonny,” said M. Postel, the provincial tradesman. “Are you pretty middling? I have been experimenting on treacle, but it would take a man like your father to find what I am looking for. Ah! he was a famous chemist, he was! If I had only known his gout specific, you and I should be rolling along in our carriage this day.”Postel, thick-headed but kind, never failed to allude to Chardon senior’s unlucky secrecy—words that stabbed Lucien. He began to find Postel vulgar, though he blessed him for his kindness; honest Postel had helped the widow and children more than once.“Is there a letter for me?”“Yes, a letter that smells like balm! It is lying on the corner near my desk.”Mme. de Bargeton’s letter among physic bottles in a druggist’s shop! Lucien sprang to rescue it.“Be quick, Lucien! your dinner has been waiting an hour!” a sweet voice called through a half-opened window; but Lucien did not hear.“That brother of yours has gone crazy, mademoiselle,” said Postel, lifting his face.The old bachelor, a miniature brandy-cask of a man with a ruddy, pitted face, became ceremonious and amiable at the sight of Eve, showing thoughts of espousing his predecessor’s daughter. “Your sister is uncommonly pretty, and you are not so bad looking neither! Your father did everything well,” he often said.Eve was tall, dark-haired, blue-eyed; though showing signs of vigour, she was gentle, tender, and devoted. Her frank innocence, simplicity, and spotless character won David’s heart; since they first met a repressed, single-hearted love had grown between them in the German fashion—quietly, without fervid protestations. In secret they thought of each other as if barred by something jealous; they hid feelings as if it wronged Lucien. David, lacking confidence, could not believe Eve could care for him; Eve, penniless and proud, was shy. Rumors said Marsac was worth more than eighty thousand francs; old Séchard had savings and bought bits of land, but perhaps David alone knew nothing of his father’s means—he cared little for the estate, seeing Marsac only at vintage time when his father boasted.David was a solitary student; his timid love sought encouragement. He stood in awe of Eve and was awkward, eager to hurry away. He repressed his passion; often he would reach the green iron railings of L’Houmeau and turn back. Eve read the little he showed and was proud of his reverence, but it was his faith in Lucien that drew her most. Their love’s mute delights were like wildflowers: glances like water-flowers, words vanishing like briar scents—tender blossoms of two rare natures. Eve saw strength beneath David’s weakness; a single word might bind them closer.Eve opened the door, and Lucien sat at the little X-shaped trestle table. There was no tablecloth; the household owned three silver spoons and forks, and Eve laid them all for her dearly loved brother.“What have you there?” she asked when she set a dish and put the extinguisher on the portable stove.Lucien did not answer. Eve garnished a little plate with vine-leaves and set a jug of cream.“There, Lucien, I have had strawberries for you.”But Lucien was absorbed in his letter. Eve sat beside him without murmur; in a sister’s love there is pleasure to serve without ceremony.“Oh! what is it?” she cried as she saw tears in his eyes.“Nothing, nothing, Eve,” he said, and putting his arm about her, drew her close and kissed her forehead and throat with surprising warmth.“You are keeping something from me.”“Well, then—she loves me.”“I knew very well that you kissed me for somebody else,” Eve pouted, flushing.“We shall all be happy,” cried Lucien, swallowing great spoonfuls of soup.“We?” echoed Eve, with the presentiment that had crossed David’s mind. “You will not care so much about us now.”“How can you think that, if you know me?”Eve clasped his hand, carried off the empty plate and brought the dish she had made. Lucien read his letter again instead of eating; Eve discreetly respected his silence. Here is the letter:—“MY FRIEND,—Why should I refuse to your brother in science the help that I have lent you? All merits have equal rights in my eyes; but you do not know the prejudices of those among whom I live. We shall never make an aristocracy of ignorance understand that intellect ennobles. If I have not sufficient influence to compel them to accept M. David Séchard, I am quite willing to sacrifice the worthless creatures to you. It would be a perfect hecatomb in the antique manner. But, dear friend, you would not, of course, ask me to leave them all in exchange for the society of a person whose character and manner might not please me. I know from your flatteries how easily friendship can be blinded. Will you think the worse of me if I attach a condition to my consent? In the interests of your future I should like to see your friend, and know and decide for myself whether you are not mistaken. What is this but the mother’s anxious care of my dear poet, which I am in duty bound to take?LOUISE DE NEGREPELISSE.”Lucien had no suspicion of the arts by which polite society frames a “Yes” on the way to a “No.” He took the note for victory. David should go to Mme. de Bargeton’s house! David would shine in the majesty of his genius! He raised his head proudly; his face radiated hope, and Eve told him he looked handsome.“If that woman has any sense, she must love you! And if so, to-night she will be vexed, for all the ladies will try all sorts of coquetries on you. How handsome you will look when you read your Saint John in Patmos! If only I were a mouse, and could just slip in and see it! Come, I have put your clothes out in mother’s room.”The mother’s room testified to self-respecting poverty: white curtains, a strip of cheap green carpet, a chest of drawers, a looking-glass, a few walnut chairs. A clock on the chimney-piece told of vanished prosperity. Eve’s adjoining closet held a narrow bed, a low chair, and a work-table; the door always stood open for air. Though poor, the atmosphere was sedate and studious.Lucien was tying his cravat when David’s step sounded in the little yard. The young printer appeared in a hurry.“Well, David!” cried Lucien, “we have gained the day! She loves me! You shall come too.”“No,” David said with confusion. “I came to thank you, but I have been thinking seriously. My life is cut out for me, Lucien. I am David Séchard, printer to His Majesty in Angoulême, my name on bills posted on every wall. For people of that class, I am an artisan, a craftsman who lives over a shop in the Rue de Beaulieu at the corner of the Place du Mûrier. I have not the wealth of a Keller nor the name of a Desplein—powers the nobles ignore. How can I prove my claim to this sudden elevation? I should only make myself a laughing-stock for nobles and bourgeoisie alike. As for you, your position is different. A foreman is not committed. You are gaining knowledge indispensable for later; you can explain your present work by your future. You can leave your place tomorrow and begin something else; nobody has pigeon-holed you. Take advantage of your social maiden fame to walk alone and grasp honors. Enjoy all pleasures. I wish you luck; I shall enjoy your success; you will be like a second self. Yes, in my mind I shall live your life. You shall have the holiday life; I will lead the tradesman’s life of sober toil, patient scientific research.“You shall be our aristocracy,” he continued, looking at Eve. “If you totter, I will steady you. If you suffer treachery, find refuge in our hearts; love there will never change. Influence and favor might fail us if we were two; we should stand in each other’s way; go forward, you can tow me after you if it comes to that. I shall dedicate my life to yours. The thing you did—risked losing your benefactress, your love perhaps, rather than forsake me—that little thing was so great—ah, well, Lucien, that alone would bind me to you forever if we were not brothers already. Have no remorse; be not concerned about seeming to take the larger share. This one-sided bargain is exactly to my taste. Suppose you give me a pang now and again, who knows that I shall not be your debtor all my life?”He glanced timidly at Eve; her eyes were full of tears; she saw all beneath the surface.“In fact,” he added, “you are well made, you have a graceful figure, you wear your clothes with an air, you look like a gentleman in that blue coat with yellow buttons and plain nankeen trousers; I should look like a workingman among those people, awkward and out of my element, or say foolish things—or say nothing. But you can take your mother’s name; call yourself Lucien de Rubempré; I am and shall be David Séchard. Everything tells for you there and against me. Women will worship that angel face of yours; won’t they, Eve?”Lucien sprang up and flung his arms about David. David’s humility had dispelled many doubts. It was one of those rare moments when forces in us are sweetly strung and every chord gives full resonance.Yet this noble goodness increased Lucien’s human tendency to take himself as the center. He had all the faults of a spoiled eldest son: his mother and sister concentrated tenderness on him, David was devoted; Mme. de Bargeton was doing her best to foster the same tendency by inducing him to forget his family and friend. He was far from doing so yet; but there was reason to fear that as his sphere widened his thought would be how to maintain himself in it.When emotion subsided, David suggested Lucien’s Saint John in Patmos might be too biblical for such an audience. For his first appearance before the exacting Charente public, Lucien seemed nervous. David advised taking André de Chénier and substituting certain pleasure for dubious delight. Lucien was a perfect reader; listeners would enjoy him, and his modesty would serve him. Like most young people, they endowed the world with their own intelligence and virtues; youth not yet gone astray is pitiless for others’ sins and magnanimous in faith.The two lovers were left alone. David, embarrassed and timid, half wished, half feared Eve’s praise; he longed to run away, for modesty is not exempt from coquetry. He was afraid to utter a word that might seem to beg for thanks; everything put him in a false position, so he held his tongue and looked guilty. Eve guessed his agony and enjoyed the pause; when David wrung his hat as if to go, she smiled.“Monsieur David,” she said, “if you are not going to Mme. de Bargeton’s, we can spend the time together. It is fine; shall we take a walk along the Charente? We will have a talk about Lucien.”David longed to fling himself at her feet. Eve’s tone solved his difficulties: her suggestion was better than praise; it was the first grace offered by love.“But give me time to dress!” she said as David made to go.David went out; he, who had never known one tune from another, hummed. Honest Postel heard him and suspected Eve’s feelings for the printer.Small things that evening made a great impression on Lucien; his character was peculiarly susceptible to first impressions. Like all inexperienced lovers, he arrived so early that Louise was not in the drawing-room; M. de Bargeton was there alone. Lucien had begun to practice the small deceits lovers pay for married happiness—deceits through which a woman learns her power—but he had not met the lady’s husband.M. de Bargeton’s intellect was limited, poised between harmless vacancy and excessive stupidity. To be agreeable he adopted an opera-dancer’s smile as his sole expression. Satisfied, he smiled; dissatisfied, he smiled again. He smiled at good and evil alike; when obliged to express approval a complacent laugh reinforced the smile; otherwise he hardly spoke. A tête-à-tête embarrassed him, and he rescued himself by returning to the artless ways of childhood—thinking aloud about the smallest personal sensations. He never talked weather or commonplaces; he plunged you into intimate trivialities—“I took veal this morning to please Mme. de Bargeton, and my stomach has been uneasy since.” Such remarks permitted no discussion; a “Yes” or “No” and conversation fell dead. Then he implored help with a gaze that said, “You were saying?” He loved bores who talked about themselves and listened with delicate interest, so twaddlers credited him with more understanding than he showed. When others could find nobody to listen they went to him, sure of his eulogistic smile. Mme. de Bargeton’s rooms were always crowded, and generally her husband felt at ease. He watched arrivals, helped his wife bring guests, laid in wait for departures to take them to the door with that eternal smile. When conversation grew lively, he stood happy and mute, planted like a swan, listening to political talk or watching card-players without notion of the game; or he walked about taking snuff to promote digestion. Anaïs was the bright side of his life; she made it unspeakably pleasant. Stretched in his armchair he watched admiringly while she played hostess. He tried to see the point in her remarks, and often succeeded only after delay; his smiles appeared like a delayed explosion. His respect for his wife almost amounted to adoration. She had drilled him into military subordination; he yielded passive obedience. “Go and call on Monsieur So-and-So,” she would say, and he went forthwith, like a soldier at command. He stood at attention in her presence and waited motionless for orders.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################There was talk of nominating the mute gentleman for a deputy. Lucien had scarcely begun to fathom this inscrutable man; he had not frequented the house long. M. de Bargeton, stretched full-length in his great chair, seemed to see and understand everything; his silence increased his dignity, and his figure filled Lucien with awe. Imaginative natures magnify; Lucien took him for a formidable sphinx rather than a mere sentinel and thought it necessary to conciliate him.“I am the first comer,” he said, bowing with more respect than usual.“That is natural enough,” replied M. de Bargeton.Lucien took the remark for an epigram; he imagined the lady’s husband jealous and reddened, striving to compose himself.“You live in L’Houmeau,” M. de Bargeton observed, “and people who live a long way off always come earlier than those who live near by.”“What is the reason of that?” asked Lucien politely.“I don’t know,” M. de Bargeton answered, relapsing into immobility.“You have not cared to find out,” Lucien continued; “one who could observe might discover the cause.”“Ah!” said M. de Bargeton—“final causes! Eh! eh!...”The conversation died; Lucien racked his brains to revive it.“Mme. de Bargeton is dressing, no doubt,” he began, shuddering at the silliness.“Yes, she is dressing,” the husband naturally answered.Lucien looked at the ceiling, at the little chandelier stripped of gauze and filled with wax candles, covers removed from furniture revealing faded damask—preparations that meant something. He glanced at his boots, misgave about his costume, and fixed on a Japanese jar, seeking some hobby of M. de Bargeton’s to humor him.“You seldom leave the city, monsieur?” he ventured.“Very seldom.”Silence again. M. de Bargeton watched Lucien’s slightest movements like a suspicious cat; the young man’s presence disturbed him. Each was afraid of the other.“Can he feel suspicious of my attentions?” thought Lucien; “he seems anything but friendly.”His unease eased when the old man-servant announced “M. du Châtelet.” The Baron entered at ease, greeted Bargeton, and favored Lucien with the fashionable slight nod the poet called purse-proud impertinence.Sixte du Châtelet wore dazzling white trousers, pumps, thread stockings, an eyeglass with a black ribbon over a white waistcoat and a fashionable black coat. He was the faded beau his antecedents suggested: dye hiding grizzled hair, a travel tan, and the retained air of an Imperial Highness’ charming private secretary. He inspected Lucien’s clothes from head to foot, then coolly returned his eyeglass to his pocket with a look that said, “I am satisfied.” Lucien, eclipsed by the inland-revenue elegance, hoped to shine later with poetic airs; but M. de Bargeton’s imagined hostility stung him. The Baron seemed to bring the weight of fortune to humiliate his poverty.M. de Bargeton, having little to say, kept a question in reserve.“Well, monsieur,” he asked Châtelet, “is there anything fresh? anything people are talking about?”“The latest thing is M. Chardon,” Châtelet said maliciously. “Ask him. Have you brought some charming poet for us?” he added, adjusting his side curl.“I should have asked you whether I had succeeded,” Lucien answered; “you have been before me in the field of verse.”“Pshaw!” said the Baron. “A few vaudevilles, lines for music, and my long Epistle to a Sister of Bonaparte will not hand down my name to posterity.”At that moment Mme. de Bargeton appeared in full toilette: a Jewess’ turban with an Eastern clasp, cameos gleaming through gauze at her shoulders, short sleeves displaying bracelets. Lucien was charmed by the theatrical dress. M. du Châtelet lavished fulsome compliments that pleased her; she was glad to be praised in Lucien’s hearing but scarcely glanced at him, meeting Châtelet with a civility that kept him at a distance.Guests began to arrive. First the Bishop and his Vicar-General, dignified and reverend though unlike—one tall and attenuated, the other short and fat. Both were impassive; their silence and reserve were supposed to hide great intellects.Close after them came Mme. de Chandour and her husband, Stanislas, a ci-devant young man slim at forty-five with a sieve-like countenance. His cravat presented two menacing points, his coat-tails were awry, a cut-away waistcoat showed a swelling starched shirt fastened with massive gold studs. Exaggerated dress and talk made him almost a living caricature: he admired himself, verified buttons, checked his hair, surveyed the women with happy flinging airs—a king of the poultry-yard—pleasant to the aristocratic circle. His eighteenth-century grossness procured success with women; his coxcomb superciliousness intrigued them and challenged M. du Châtelet.Amélie de Chandour, short, plump, fair and dark-haired, was a poor actress; her loud voice matched her restless head, feathered or flowered. She talked without depth and had asthma to wheeze away her efforts.M. de Saintot, Astolphe, President of the Agricultural Society, tall, stout, high-colored, usually appeared with his wife Élisa, called Lili—a lady with a withered-fern face, solemn, pious, and trying at cards. Astolphe was supposed to be a scientific man; in truth he was ignorant but had compiled articles on sugar and brandy for a Dictionary of Agriculture by pillage of others’ work. It was believed he was writing on modern husbandry; though he locked himself in his study mornings, he had not produced much of late. If callers arrived, he contrived to be found rummaging papers, yet spent time on puerilities—reading newspapers, cutting corks, drawing on blotting-paper. He’d turn to Cicero to find anything applicable to current events and drag a quotation into conversation, prompting the remark, “Astolphe is a well of learning.” The belief in his abilities sustained itself by rumor.After this pair came M. de Bartas, Adrien, who boomed his song in a bass voice and made prodigious musical claims. His self-conceit rested on solfeggi; he admired his singing and talked music to the point of monomania. Animated only by that subject, he was miserable until begged to sing. After bellowing an air he strutted and accepted praise, moving from group to group for more; when compliments ceased, he returned to the song’s difficulties or extolled the composer.M. Alexandre de Brébian produced heroic sepia sketches, disfiguring friends’ walls and spoiling albums. He and M. de Bartas arrived together, each with the other’s wife on his arm in a cross-cornered arrangement that gossip said they carried to extremes. Mesdames Charlotte de Brébian and Joséphine de Bartas—Lolotte and Fifine—cared as much for a scarf or color reconciliation as for any serious matter, aiming to look like Parisiennes while neglecting their homes. Their husbands availed an artist’s privilege to dress as they pleased; provincial dowdiness on them contrasted with their wives’ doll-like finery, making them like stage supernumeraries at third-rate theatre weddings.One of the queerest figures was M. le Comte de Senonches, Jacques, a mighty hunter, lean and sunburned, haughty as a boar, suspicious as a Venetian, jealous as a Moor, yet intimate with M. du Hautoy—Francis, the friend of the house.Madame de Senonches, Zéphirine, was tall and fine-looking though her complexion showed pimples of liver complaint, making her exacting. Slender and delicate, she affected languid manners but revealed passion and the consciousness that every caprice would be gratified.Francis had forsaken a consulship in Valence and sacrificed diplomatic prospects to live near Zéphirine in Angoulême. He managed the household, educations, languages, and fortunes of M. and Mme. de Senonches with complete devotion. Noble, administrative, and bourgeois Angoulême had long looked askance at this perfect union of three persons, but the conjugal trinity became so pleasing that had M. du Hautoy suggested marriage he would have seemed monstrously immoral. Mme. de Senonches had a lady companion and goddaughter, Mlle. de la Haye; her excessive attachment to Françoise de la Haye raised surmises of disquieting mysteries, and despite discrepancies in dates, people thought Françoise bore a striking likeness to Francis du Hautoy.When “Jacques” was shooting nearby, people asked after Francis, and Jacques would discourse on his steward’s ailments and speak of his wife in the second place. Such blindness in a jealous man amused friends who drew him out on the topic. M. du Hautoy was a finical dandy whose minute self-care degenerated into mincing affectation: he fussed about coughs, appetite, digestion, and sleep. Zéphirine made a valetudinarian of him; she doctored and coddled him, fed him delicacies, embroidered his waistcoats and handkerchiefs until he became a kind of Japanese idol. Their understanding was perfect: Zizine consulted Francis with a look; he took his ideas from her eyes. They frowned and smiled together, seeming to take counsel even before making the simplest remark.The largest landowner was the Marquis de Pimentel; between him and his wife they had forty thousand livres and spent winters in Paris. This evening they arrived in their calèche with neighbors, the Baron and Baroness de Rastignac, their party, the Baroness’ aunt and two charming, penniless daughters carefully brought up and simply dressed to show natural loveliness.These first families met a reception of chilling silence; respect paid them was tinged with jealousy, especially since Mme. de Bargeton paid marked attention to these guests. The two families kept aloof from provincial gossip, belonged to no clique, lived quietly, and maintained dignified reserve. M. de Pimentel and M. de Rastignac were addressed by full names, and no length of acquaintance had brought their wives and daughters into Angoulême’s select coterie; both families were too near the Court to risk provincial follies.The Prefect and the General in command arrived last, with a country gentleman who had brought a treatise on silkworms to David that morning. Evidently a mayor or canton gentleman, his fine estate sufficed for gentility; but he was unused to polite society, uneasy in his clothes, at a loss with his hands, shifting from foot to foot, half rising and sitting as people spoke to him. He seemed ready to perform menial service: obsequious, nervous, grave by turns, laughing eagerly at every joke, listening with servility, then assuming a knowing air when imagining others laughed at him. His treatise weighed on his mind; he tried repeatedly to talk silkworms but met M. de Bartas, who talked music, and M. de Saintot, who quoted Cicero, until late in the evening Mme. and Mlle. du Brossard showed sympathy.Mme. and Mlle. du Brossard were interesting yet pitiable: as poor as they were noble, their dress betrayed carefully hidden penury. The daughter, a big, heavy seven-and-twenty, was lauded as a good pianist by her mother in the clumsiest way. Mme. du Brossard, anxious to marry off Camille, declared her child liked everything desirable—roving life, then country farmhouses—adapting to any suitor’s taste. Mother and daughter bore a pinched dignity learned from experience; they had been objects of benevolent egoism and had sounded the void beneath consoling formulas the world offers the unfortunate.M. de Séverac, fifty-nine and a childless widower, spoke of his silkworm nurseries; Mme. du Brossard enthused, “My daughter has always been fond of animals... she will grasp anything in a moment. Did she not understand one day the inverse ratio of the squares of distances!” This remark brought the conversation between Mme. du Brossard and M. de Séverac to a glorious close after Lucien’s reading that night.A few habitués slipped in, as did one or two eldest sons: shy, mute young men tricked out in jewelry, highly honored by an invitation to the literary solemnity; the boldest chatted with Mlle. de la Haye. The women arranged themselves in a circle, the men stood behind them. It was a quaint assemblage of wrinkled faces and heterogeneous costumes but very alarming to Lucien; his heart beat fast under everyone’s look. Mme. de Bargeton welcomed illustrious Angoulême personages with ostentatious courtesy, but the uncomfortable feeling was aggravated by a trifling matter that would shock a young man unused to the world. Lucien noticed that no one except Louise, M. de Bargeton, the Bishop, and some few who wished to please the hostess called him M. de Rubempré; to the formidable audience he was M. Chardon. Lucien’s courage sank under inquisitive lips; he read his plebeian name in their movements and heard anticipatory criticisms in blunt provincial fashion. He had not expected such pin-pricks; it put him more out of humor with himself. Impatient to begin the reading, he hoped to assume an attitude to end his mental torment; but Jacques told Mme. de Pimentel of his sport, Adrien expounded Rossini to Mlle. Laure de Rastignac, and Astolphe, with a newspaper paragraph on a patent plow, lectured the Baron. Lucien, luckless poet, did not know there was scarce a soul in the room besides Mme. de Bargeton who could understand poetry. The matter-of-fact assembly was there by misapprehension; for the most part they did not know why they had come. Certain words—“beauty,” “glory,” “poetry”—bewitch even the coarsest intellect.When everyone had arrived and the buzz faded after M. de Bargeton, obedient to his wife, made the circuit like a beadle tapping the pavement with his wand, securing silence at last, Lucien went to the round table near Mme. de Bargeton. A fierce thrill ran through him. He announced in an uncertain voice that, to prevent disappointment, he would read the masterpieces of a great poet discovered only recently (although André de Chénier’s poems had appeared in 1819, no one in Angoulême had heard of him). Everybody took the announcement one way—it was a shift of Mme. de Bargeton’s to save the poet’s self-love and put the audience at ease.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Lucien began with Le Malade, and the poem drew a murmur of applause; but when he followed with L’Aveugle the strain proved too great for the average intellect. Only artists or those with an artistic temperament can follow such a reading; to render poetry by voice so that listeners grasp it requires devout attention and an intimate alliance between reader and audience. That sympathy was lacking, and Lucien seemed like an angel trying to sing of heaven amid the chucklings of hell. A man in a field that stimulates him can be acutely aware of everything; a musician or poet knows at once whether his audience listens, feeling it as a plant revives or droops under favorable or unfavorable conditions. The men who had come with their wives were discussing their own affairs; by the laws of acoustics every murmur rang in Lucien’s ear; he noticed the gaps made by spasmodic jaw-work and teeth that seemed to grin defiance at him.Searching, like the dove in the deluge, for any spot to rest on, he saw nothing but rows of impatient faces. Their owners had clearly come together to discuss practical matters; except for Laure de Rastignac, the Bishop, and two or three young men, they all looked bored. Those who understand poetry strive to develop the seeds of another poetry quickened by the poet’s; this glacial audience, so far from attaining the spirit, did not even listen to the letter.Lucien felt discouraged; damp with chill perspiration, he was steadied by a glowing glance from Louise, but his poet’s heart bled. “Do you find this very amusing, Fifine?” inquired the wizened Lili. “Don’t ask me; I cannot keep my eyes open when anyone reads aloud,” she answered. “I hope Naïs will not give us poetry often in the evenings,” said Francis. “If I am obliged to attend while somebody reads aloud after dinner, it upsets my digestion.” “It was very well declaimed,” said Alexandre, “but I like whist better.” After that quip, some card-players decided the reader’s voice needed a rest, and a couple of couples slipped away into the card-room. But Louise, the Bishop, and pretty Laure besought Lucien to continue, and he caught his audience again with Chénier’s spirited reactionary iambes. Several, borne away by his impassioned delivery, applauded without understanding the sense; people of that sort mistake vociferation for meaning, as a coarse palate is tickled by strong spirits.During the interval, while they had ices, Zéphirine sent Francis to examine the volume and told Amélie that the poetry was in print. Amélie brightened. “Why, that is easily explained,” she said. “M. de Rubempré works for a printer. It is as if a pretty woman should make her own dresses,” she added, looking at Lolotte. “He printed his poetry himself!” the women murmured. “Then why does he call himself M. de Rubempré?” inquired Jacques. “If a noble takes a handicraft, he ought to lay his name aside.” “So he did,” said Zizine, “but his name was plebeian; he took his mother’s, which is noble.” “Well, if his verses are printed, we can read them for ourselves,” said Astolphe.This stupidity complicated matters until Sixte du Châtelet condescended to inform these unlettered folk that the prefatory announcement was not oratorical flourish but fact: the poems had been written by a Royalist brother of Marie-Joseph Chénier, the Revolutionary leader. All Angoulême, except Mme. de Rastignac, her two daughters, and the Bishop — who had genuinely felt the grandeur of the poetry — were mystified and took offense at the hoax. There was a smothered murmur, but Lucien did not heed it. The intoxication of the poetry had seized him; he was far from the hateful world, striving to render in speech the music that filled his soul, seeing the faces about him through a cloudy haze. He read the sombre Elegy on the Suicide, lines in the taste of a bygone day, pervaded by sublime melancholy; then he turned to the page where the line occurs, “Thy songs are sweet, I love to say them over,” and ended with the delicate idyll Néere.Mme. de Bargeton sat with one hand buried in her curls, heedless of the havoc she wrought among them, gazing with unseeing eyes, alone in her drawing-room, lost in delicious dreaming; for the first time in her life she had been transported to the sphere that was hers by nature. Judge, therefore, how unpleasantly she was disturbed by Amélie, who took it upon herself to express the general wish. “Naïs,” she broke in, “we came to hear M. Chardon’s poetry, and you are giving us poetry out of a book.
