The extracts are very nice, but the ladies feel a patriotic preference for the wine of the country; they would rather have it.” Astolphe added lightly to Châtelet, “The French language does not lend itself very readily to poetry, does it? Cicero’s prose is a thousand times more poetical to my way of thinking.” “The true poetry of France is song, lyric verse,” Châtelet answered. “Which proves that our language is eminently adapted for music,” said Adrien.“I should very much like to hear the poetry that has cost Naïs her reputation,” said Zéphirine; “but after receiving Amélie’s request in such a way, it is not very likely that she will give us a specimen.” “She ought to have them recited in justice to herself,” said Francis. “The little fellow’s genius is his sole justification.” “You have been in the diplomatic service,” said Amélie to M. du Châtelet, “go and manage it somehow.” “Nothing easier,” said the Baron.The Princess’ private secretary, used to such petty manoeuvres, went to the Bishop and contrived to bring him to the fore. At the Bishop’s entreaty, Naïs had no choice but to ask Lucien to recite his own verses for them, and the Baron received a languishing smile from Amélie as the reward of his prompt success. “Decidedly, the Baron is a very clever man,” she observed to Lolotte. But Amélie’s previous acid remark about women who made their own dresses rankled in Lolotte’s mind. “Since when have you begun to recognize the Emperor’s barons?” she asked, smiling.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Lucien had attempted to deify his beloved in an ode titled “TO HER!” and recited it proudly under Mme. de Bargeton’s protection. But Mme. de Bargeton, who viewed society from an intellectual height, looked anxious as he proceeded.The poem pictured an angel visiting earth to bring solace, Love blinded by excessive light, and a luminous pathway in heaven marking the angelic flight. Amélie coquetted; the Baron, arbiter of taste, sneered that the verse was a Christianized Ossian, full of inflated words and strained rhymes. Zéphirine called the ode obscure despite the plain dedication; Francis likened the archangel’s armor to thin gauze. The women, eager for poets to flatter them like angels, professed polite delight while remaining inwardly cold.Lolotte ordered Adrien not to congratulate the poet, and Adrien obeyed. Zéphirine told Francis that love was a poem to be lived, not written; Stanislas agreed, flattering himself. Amélie, hoping Naïs would be humbled, attacked Lucien’s origins—mocking his family’s humble trades—and the company joined to humiliate him. Francis, seeing a chance for a public blow, engineered a subtler attack through the Bishop.He had told the Bishop that Lucien’s mother, a modest woman of uncommon powers, supplied subjects for her son’s verses and that Lucien idolized her. Having planted this idea, Francis left the Bishop to make the intended allusion in conversation.When Francis and the Bishop rejoined the group, interest sharpened. Lucien, shy and unaccustomed to their manners, was embarrassed by questions and being called alternately M. Chardon and M. de Rubempré. He mistakenly addressed M. de Senonches as M. Lili, provoking rude laughter and Mme. de Bargeton’s crimsoned shame. Senonches muttered displeasure. Zéphirine compared Lucien to M. de Cante-Croix. Mme. de Bargeton, speaking to the Marquise de Pimentel, philosophized on glory’s attraction and on why women are drawn to greatness. The Marquise laughed and sided with Naïs.The Marquise and Marquis spoke lightly to Lucien; Lolotte asked if he worked quickly, as one might ask a joiner about a box. Stung, Lucien took courage from Mme. de Bargeton’s reply: poetry did not grow in his head like grass in their courtyards. The Bishop, sanctifying poetry, laid a hand on Lucien’s head and declared him a poet marked by Fate.Grateful for the championing, Lucien thanked the Bishop with a look, unaware that the prelate’s next comment would wound him. Mme. de Bargeton’s penetrating glances increased her rivals’ fury.Lucien spoke of the poet’s suffering, the difficulty of making ideas clear, the need for experience and deep feeling to create true characters—he named great literary creations as fruits of such throes. When Châtelet asked what Lucien would create, Lucien replied that to proclaim it would be vanity and that such works required long experience; he hinted bitterly that he had only begun. The Bishop’s joke—“Your excellent mother might assist you”—elicited delighted smiles of caste and M. de Bargeton’s familiar laugh.Mme. de Bargeton retorted that a poet who turned to the Bible for inspiration indeed had a mother in the Church, and challenged Lucien to declaim Saint John in Patmos or Belshazzar’s Feast, mockingly invoking Rome’s role. The women smiled at the Latin.Lucien faltered under the blows, sank, then rose resolved to conquer this world. He prepared to declaim Saint John in Patmos; but the card-tables called the company back to amusement, and they showed contempt by returning to their games, leaving Lucien and Mme. de Bargeton alone. The social world of Angoulême, conscious of its ignorance of poetry, clustered about the Pimentels and Rastignacs to hear their verdict; the Marquis and Marquise’s approval—“not bad for provincial poetry”—spread and became malicious.Music followed: Châtelet mangled du Bartas and Figaro; Châtelet sang a Chateaubriand ballad; duets and schoolroom songs filled the evening. Hurt by the company’s contempt for her poet, Mme. de Bargeton withdrew to her boudoir, followed by the Bishop and Mlle. de Rastignac, who was enchanted by the poetry.There, with no one to watch, Mme. de Bargeton murmured that they had not understood him; she loved to repeat his lines. Lucien took comfort; she urged him to endure, asserting that suffering is the price of immortality and that struggle gives eagle wings room to spread. She begged him to remember, when he reached greatness, the minds disinherited by fate and to sing of lives choked and unblossomed—allegories of souls that remember Heaven. The Bishop compared such a theme to the Song of Songs; Laure de Rastignac suggested it. The Vicar-General declared the sacred poem of France unwritten and prophesied success for the man who should write for religion. Mme. de Bargeton claimed the task for Lucien, seeing a dawn of vision in his eyes.During this quieter talk, Amélie, Fifine, Adrien, and Francis reentered with Mme. de Rastignac to draw Naïs back for music. They gleefully told the drawing-room that M. de Rubempré was to recite Saint John in Patmos; the word “biblical” became mockery, and Lucien excused himself for a lapse of memory. When he reappeared, no one noticed him; the poet’s halo had been plucked away. The landowners and pretentious set had no use for him; the women, jealous of Mme. de Bargeton, looked on him with icy scorn.“So this is society!” Lucien thought as he took the long way home to L’Houmeau, walking to ease his anger and muse on answers that had come too late. Far from disheartened, his ambition burned: like many who reach a higher sphere before they belong, he vowed any sacrifice to remain. As he paced toward the Bordeaux road, he fancied Eve and David by the river and hurried to meet them.Eve had dressed simply but becomingly; David, shy at her beauty, resolved to speak of himself and their future. They walked across the Bridge of Saint Anne along the Charente until Eve broke the silence with small talk about the evening. David, thinking of Lucien, expressed anxiety: he feared Mme. de Bargeton was spoiling Lucien for hard work and would either make him her plaything or, if indifferent, ruin him. Lucien loved pleasure and would not endure the privations required for great literary work; society would stimulate desires beyond his means.Eve, undismayed, promised to work with her mother to support Lucien until success came. David then proposed: to marry Eve, give up his present life, and devote himself and any future fortune to Lucien’s support. He argued Lucien should not be a foreman nor live in L’Houmeau; together they could keep Lucien until he made his way to Paris. He pledged that love and the desire to help Lucien would give him the perseverance to pursue a profitable invention he had been researching—something in paper-making—if Eve would marry him and keep his confidence while he worked.Moved, Eve confessed she had never thought of any other man. David admitted his faults and modest means, and disclosed a plan based on his knowledge of chemistry and the paper trade. He explained the economic problem: since the Empire’s fall, calico had become common and linen rags—used for paper—were scarce and dear. Paper-makers used a mix of linen and cotton rags; cotton paper is soft, creases easily, and dissolves in water, whereas linen makes durable paper. If cheap, durable paper could be made from a common vegetable fiber—like Chinese paper from bamboo—the cost and weight of books could be drastically reduced, advancing literature and printing. David had studied earlier attempts and recent discoveries: the Chinese used bamboo; European experiments had been gradual; the transition from rag-based to machine-made paper had been slow. He mentioned web-paper and inventions by Robert d’Essonne and Didot-Saint-Léger and that the press industry longed for a substitute for linen rags, since cotton predominated in England. If linen could be replaced with a local vegetable fiber processed by machinery, books would be cheaper and lighter—allowing a wider diffusion of knowledge.David sketched the paper-making process concisely: rag-pickers gather rags; merchants sort and stock them; mills wash and reduce rags to pulp; the pulp is strained through a mould to set sheet size, then pressed and dried. He recounted his employment with the Didots and conversations about Chinese paper, and how the insight that the Chinese used a native vegetable fiber led him to consider French reeds and other local plants. He acknowledged the difficulty of imitating Chinese handwork mechanically—Chinese labor was cheap and their handwork gave surface quality—but argued that an industrial replacement could revolutionize European paper-making.As David explained, Lucien arrived, distraught. He related his humiliation: the company’s sneers, the Bishop’s epigram, the ridicule of his origins, and his fear that Mme. de Bargeton would be both his consolation and his ruin. He believed she loved him as he loved her, that she comforted him and would never give him up. David and Eve, absorbed in their own happy news—their engagement and David’s plan to support Lucien—at first did not notice Lucien’s shock that Mme. de Bargeton’s attachment might complicate his future. Lucien had pictured Mme. de Bargeton as his path to elevation; the tangled prospects jarred him. But David’s warm description of the planned household—first floor for his wife, second for Lucien, rooms above the shed for Mme. Chardon—reassured Lucien. The brother’s taste for the burgher life returned; the glow of the aristocratic world faded. They walked back together, reconciled, Lucien joining in the lovers’ joy.They returned late; Postel, uneasy, saw light in Eve’s room and asked Lucien if anything was wrong. Lucien told him that his mother had consented to Eve’s engagement, and Postel slammed his window in despair that he had not earlier asked for Mlle. Chardon.David did not go back to Angoulême to sleep; he walked through the night to his father’s house at Marsac. At sunrise he found his father among the vines. After a brief domestic exchange, David declared his purpose: he was to marry Eve Chardon, and he sought his father’s consent and permission to build a second floor and rooms above the shed to house their projected household.The father, a practical vine-grower, immediately thought of money but remained skeptical. He grumbled about taxes, boasted of making more puncheons per acre, and scoffed at book-learning. He consented to the marriage but had no money to give and assumed Eve must be rich, hoping for funds to pay back his rent. When David revealed Eve had as little as his mother—i.e., nothing—the father suggested other, wealthier matches and railed at David’s education for leading him away from practical advantage. He offered to seek a miller’s widow with land or arrange other profitable unions, but refused to expend anything on building. Though David urged his father to build, the old man refused, saying he had no cash and that such expenditure would be wasted. He would, however, consent to the marriage and not press the rent, leaving David to fund any improvements himself.David left Marsac disappointed; he perceived he could not count on his father for help and that his plans must be achieved by his own means.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In Angoulême that day everybody spoke of the Bishop’s epigram and Mme. de Bargeton’s reply. By evening the details had swelled into a triumph for the poet. A few drops of the storm fell among the bourgeoisie; young men glanced after Lucien as he passed through Beaulieu, and he caught phrases that fed his vanity.“There is a lucky young fellow!” said Petit-Claud, an attorney’s clerk who had been at school with Lucien and spoke to him with patronizing airs.“Yes, he certainly is,” answered one who had been at the reading; “he is good-looking, has some brains, and Mme. de Bargeton is quite wild about him.”Lucien waited impatiently for a chance to see Louise. He had news of his sister’s marriage to tell, and he wanted to seek the mistress of his destiny. After the previous evening’s soirée, he hoped for more kindness from Louise, and he was not mistaken. Mme. de Bargeton met him with a vehemence that struck the novice in love as passionate. She yielded to his kisses, wiped the pearls of sweat from his brow, and called him a poet crowned.“If only you could have seen your face while you were reading,” she cried, using the tu since yesterday. “There were sparks in your eyes! From your lips fell the golden chains that bind men’s hearts. You shall read Chénier to me; he is the lover’s poet. You shall not be unhappy; I will not have it. Yes, dear angel, I will make an oasis for you—sometimes busy, sometimes languid, indolent, full of work, and musing by turns. But never forget you owe your laurels to me; let that thought be my guerdon for the sufferings I must endure. The world will not spare me any more than it has spared you; it avenges itself on all happiness it cannot share. I shall always be a mark for envy—did you not see that last night? The bloodthirsty insects are quick to drain every wound they pierce. But I was happy; I lived. It is so long since all my heartstrings vibrated.”Tears flowed and Lucien kissed her hand. His mother, sister, David, and Louise all soothed and caressed his vanity; each helped raise the imaginary pedestal on which he had set himself. His friends’ praise and his enemies’ fury fixed him more firmly in an unreal world. A handsome young man full of promise readily accepts flattering estimates; only sharp lessons teach him his ordinariness.“My beautiful Louise, do you mean in very truth to be my Beatrice, a Beatrice who condescends to be loved?”Louise raised her eyes. “If you show yourself worthy—one day!” she said with an angelic smile that belied the reserve. “Are you not happy? To be the sole possessor of a heart, to speak freely at all times, with the certainty of being understood—that is happiness.”“Yes,” he answered.“Child!” she laughed. “You have something to tell me, have you not? You came in absorbed in thought, my Lucien.”He told her, trembling, that David loved his sister Eve, that Eve loved David, and that they were to be married. “Poor Lucien!” said Louise. “Were you afraid you’d be blamed, as if you were to be married! What is your family to me? Lovers are for each other their whole family. Suppose my father married his cook—would that trouble you? Be great, find a way to win fame—that is our affair!”This selfish reply made Lucien the happiest of mortals. While Louise convinced him by fantastic reasoning that they two were alone in the world, M. de Bargeton came in. Lucien frowned but Louise signaled him to stay for dinner and to read André de Chénier until people arrived for their evening cards.“You will give her pleasure,” said M. de Bargeton, “and me also. Nothing suits me better than listening to reading aloud after dinner.”Cajoled by Louise and M. de Bargeton and waited on with the respect shown a favored guest, Lucien lingered at the Hôtel de Bargeton. He began to think of the luxuries he now enjoyed as rightful accessories of Lucien de Rubempré. He felt secure because of Louise’s love and M. de Bargeton’s weakness, and as the rooms filled, he assumed a lordly air which Louise encouraged. He tasted the delights of despotic sway and liked to play the lion of the little town. Some who noticed his airs concluded, as the old saying ran, that he had come to the last term with the lady. Amélie, who came with M. du Châtelet, was sure of the deplorable fact, in a corner of the drawing-room where the jealous and envious gathered.“Do not call Naïs to account for the vanity of a youngster who thinks he has set foot in society,” said Châtelet. “He does not know the difference between the silence of real passion and the patronizing graciousness due to his good looks and talent. It would be too bad if women were blamed for the desires they inspire. He certainly is in love, but Naïs—”“Oh! Naïs,” echoed the perfidious Amélie, “Naïs is well enough pleased. A young man’s love makes a woman young again; she acts a girl’s hesitation and manners, not dreaming she is ridiculous. Just look! Think of a druggist’s son giving himself conqueror’s airs with Mme. de Bargeton.”“Love knows naught of high or low degree,” hummed Adrien.The next day every house in Angoulême discussed the intimacy between M. Chardon (alias de Rubempré) and Mme. de Bargeton. Though the actual guilt amounted to a few kisses, the world chose to believe the worst. Mme. de Bargeton paid for her sovereignty: society’s erratic judgments set different standards for different people. So one glance between Mme. de Bargeton and Lucien outweighed twelve years of Zizine’s connection with Francis; a squeeze of the hand drew down all the thunders of the Charente upon the lovers.David had brought a small hoard from Paris and set it aside for his marriage and to build a second floor in his father’s house. He built a timbered second story for Lucien to avoid straining the old walls, taking pleasure in making the rooms where Eve would live as brave as might be.It was a time of blithe happiness for the friends. Lucien was tired of provincial shabbiness and treated a five-franc piece as a fortune, but he bore hardships without complaint. His moody looks were succeeded by radiant hope. He saw a star above his head, dreamed of a great future, and built it on M. de Bargeton’s tomb. M. de Bargeton, troubled by indigestion now and then, cherished the delusion that such discomfort was cured by hearty supper.By September Lucien had ceased to be a printer’s foreman; he was M. de Rubempré, housed sumptuously compared with the tumbledown attic in L’Houmeau where “young Chardon” had lived; he dined four times a week with Mme. de Bargeton. A friendship had grown between him and the Bishop; he went to the palace. His occupations put him on a level with the highest rank; his name would one day be among the great names of France; and as he moved in his pretty sitting-room, charming bedroom, and tastefully furnished study, he consoled himself for drawing thirty francs a month from his mother’s and sister’s earnings by thinking that An Archer of Charles IX., the historical romance he had written for two years, and a volume of verse titled Marguerites would spread his fame and repay them. Grown great in his own eyes and hearing echoes of his future name, he accepted present sacrifices with noble assurance; he smiled at his poverty and relished these last days of penury.Eve and David had set Lucien’s happiness before their own. They delayed their wedding to paper and paint their rooms and buy furniture; Lucien’s affairs came first. He was so engaging, had winning ways, and such gracious impatience that his cause was always won before he spoke. This unlucky gift of fortune ruins many: the world is predisposed toward youth and good looks and protects those who please it with a selfish good-nature that flings alms to any appealing beggar. Many a grown child basks instead of using favor profitably; they imagine deceptive smiles will always meet them, and the world finally strips them bare, like an elderly coquette or a stray rag in the gutter.Eve had wished for the delay; she meant to establish a household on a strict budget. But who could refuse a brother who said, “How I wish I could sew!” David, sober and observant, shared the devotion. Since Lucien’s triumph David had watched him with misgivings, fearing he would change and look down on homely ways. Once David tried him, and Lucien chose home pleasures over the great world, and David exclaimed, “They will not spoil him for us!” They arranged country picnics in provincial fashion—a walk along the Charente, dinner on the grass; on big occasions they dined at a compromise restaurant and sometimes spent five francs. David gave his brother infinite credit for forsaking Mme. de Bargeton and grand dinners for these days; the whole party made much of the great man of Angoulême.Matters had gone so far that the new home was nearly ready, and David had gone to Marsac to persuade his father to come to the wedding, hoping the old man might relent at sight of his daughter-in-law and contribute toward expenses, when an event occurred that changed things in the small town.Lucien and Louise had a spy in Châtelet, a man who watched with hatred and avarice for a chance to make scandal. Sixte wanted Mme. de Bargeton compromised so she should be “lost”; he posed as her humble confidant, admired Lucien in the Rue du Minage, and disparaged him elsewhere. Naïs had gradually given him les petites entrées, for she no longer mistrusted her elderly admirer; but Châtelet had taken too much for granted—love was still platonic, to the despair of Louise and Lucien.Love affairs often start badly. Two creatures launch into tactics; they talk instead of acting, skirmish instead of besieging. They grow tired and expend longing in empty space. Provincial life keeps desire unsatisfied and maintains lovers’ arguments on the intellectual plane. A system of minute espionage underlies provincial life; every house is transparent and outrageously scandalous constructions are put on innocent intercourse. The world, which blames superficially, often contributes to the scandals it decries; many a woman takes extreme steps only after unjust accusation, and Mme. de Bargeton was now on the verge of this position.The obstacles of such passion were like Lilliput’s bonds—trifles that made movement impossible. Mme. de Bargeton must always be visible. If she had denied herself to visitors when Lucien was with her, it would have been all over with her; she might as well have run away with him at once. They sat in the boudoir, now so familiar to Lucien that he felt a right to be there; but the doors stood open, and everything was arranged with the utmost propriety. M. de Bargeton pervaded the house like a cockchafer; it never entered his head that his wife could wish to be alone with Lucien. If he had been the only person in the way Naïs could have got rid of him—or given him something to do—but he was not the only one; visitors flocked as curiosity increased. The servants came and went promiscuously; any change in household ways was tantamount to a confession, and Angoulême still hung in doubt.Mme. de Bargeton could not set foot outside her house without the whole town knowing whither she went. To take a walk alone with Lucien would have been a decided measure; it would have been less dangerous to shut herself up with him. There would have been comments if Lucien had stayed till midnight; within and without she lived in public.Like many women newly carried away by passion, Louise discovered her difficulties one by one. They frightened her, and her terror reacted on the fond talk that fills lovers’ hours. Weary of living in public and pushed to extremities by a tyranny that afforded no pleasures sweet enough to compensate, she thought of Escarbas and of seeing her aged father.Châtelet did not believe in innocence. He lay in wait, watched Lucien into the house, and followed a few minutes later with M. de Chandour, the most indiscreet of the clique; putting that gentleman first, he hoped to find a surprise. His part required appearing neutral to prompt the actors in his drama. To give himself countenance he attached himself to the jealous Amélie, better to lull suspicion in Lucien and Mme. de Bargeton, who was not without perspicacity. To spy he contrived an old controversy with M. de Chandour; Amélie goaded them to argument because she longed to know the truth. Some intimate friends of the house dropped in during the argument. Stanislas and Châtelet vied in backing up their opinions with observations, each trying to enlist partisans. “What do you think?” they asked each neighbor. These polemics kept Mme. de Bargeton and Lucien well in sight.At length Châtelet remarked that when he went with M. de Chandour and found Lucien there, there was nothing suspicious; the boudoir door stood open, the servants came and went. Stanislas vowed he would cross the room on tiptoe the next day, and perfidious Amélie held him to his bargain.For Lucien that morrow was the day he resolved to be extravagant. The poet who had once sat bashfully in the boudoir-sanctuary had been transformed into an urgent lover. Six months had brought him level with Louise; now he would fain be her lord and master. He left home determined to be vehement, to cry that he had lost his head and could not write. The horror some women feel for premeditation does honor to their delicacy; they prefer surrender on impulse.Mme. de Bargeton read fixed purpose in Lucien and resolved to baffle him, urged partly by contradiction and partly by an exalted conception of love. She valued herself as a sovereign lady, a Beatrice; she enthroned herself and meant that Lucien should win her by prowess—eclipse Lamartine, Scott, and Byron. She regarded her love as a stimulating power; the desire she had kindled should give him energy to win glory. Like many provincials, she desired to give herself as the reward of prolonged service—a trial of constancy to judge her lover.Lucien began with vehement petulance, and Louise took a lofty tone and delivered a long oration:“Was that your promise to me, Lucien? Do not sow regrets in the present to poison my after life. Do not spoil the future, and do not spoil the present! Is not my whole heart yours? What more must you have? Can it be your love is influenced by the clamor of the senses, when it is the noblest privilege of the beloved to silence them? For whom do you take me? Am I not your Beatrice? If I am not more than a woman for you, I am less than a woman.”“That is just what you might say to a man if you cared nothing at all for him,” cried Lucien, frantic.“If you cannot feel all the sincere love underlying my ideas, you will never be worthy of me.”“You are throwing doubts on my love to escape responding,” cried Lucien, and he flung himself weeping at her feet.The poor boy cried in earnest at the prospect of remaining at paradise’s gate. “You have never loved me!” he cried.“You do not believe what you say,” she answered, flattered by his violence.“Then give me proof that you are mine,” said the disheveled poet.Just then Stanislas came up unheard. He beheld Lucien in tears, half reclining on the floor with his head on Louise’s knee. The attitude was suspicious enough; he turned sharply on Châtelet, who stood at the salon door. Mme. de Bargeton sprang up, but the spies beat a precipitate retreat like intruders; she was not quick enough for them.“Who came just now?” she asked the servants.“M. de Chandour and M. du Châtelet,” said Gentil, her old footman.Mme. de Bargeton went back, pale and trembling, to her boudoir. “If they saw you just now, I am lost,” she told Lucien.“So much the better!” exclaimed the poet, and she smiled to hear the cry, so full of selfish love.In the provinces a story is aggravated by the way it is told. Everyone knew in a moment that Lucien had been detected at Naïs’s feet. M. de Chandour, elated by the part he played, first told the great news at the club, then from house to house; Châtelet, hastening to say he had seen nothing, egged Stanislas on to talk. Stanislas, thinking himself witty, added a little each time. Everyone flocked to Amélie’s that evening, for exaggerated versions were circulating among the Angoulême nobility. Women and men eagerly sought the truth; the loudest hands before faces were Mesdames Amélie, Zéphirine, Fifine, and Lolotte, each with heavy indictments. There were variations in every key.“Well, well,” said one lady, “poor Naïs! have you heard about it? I do not believe it myself; she is far too proud to be anything but a patroness to M. Chardon. Still, if it is true, I pity her.”“She makes herself frightfully ridiculous; she is old enough to be M. Lulu’s mother. The little poet is twenty-two at most; and Naïs, between ourselves, is quite forty.”“For my part,” said M. du Châtelet, “I think M. de Rubempré’s position in itself proves Naïs’ innocence. A man does not go down on his knees to ask for what he has already.”“That is as may be!” said Francis, with levity that brought Zéphirine’s disapproving glance.“Do just tell us how it really was,” they besought Stanislas and formed a small secret committee.Stanislas had concocted a facetious story with pantomime that made matters worse. “It is incredible! At midday? Naïs was the last person I would have suspected! What will she do now?”Châtelet took Mme. de Bargeton’s part, but defended her so ill that he stirred gossip instead of quelling it.Lili, disconsolate over the fall of the fairest angel in the Angoumoisin hierarchy, went in tears to the palace. When Châtelet was convinced the whole town was agog, he went to Mme. de Bargeton’s where there was one whist game that night, and asked Naïs for a little talk in the boudoir. They sat on the sofa, and Châtelet began in an undertone—“You know what Angoulême is talking about, of course?”“No.”“I am your friend and bound to put you in a position to silence slanders, invented no doubt by Amélie, who regards herself as your rival. I came to your door this morning with that monkey of a Stanislas; he was ahead and came so far” (pointing to the boudoir door); “he says he saw you and M. de Rubempré in such a position that he could not enter; he turned on me bewildered and hurried me away before I had time to think. If I had known, I would not have stirred till I had cleared up the matter and exonerated you, but it would have proved nothing to go back then.“Whether Stanislas’ eyes deceived him or he is right, he must have made a mistake. Dear Naïs, do not let that dolt trifle with your life, your honor, your future; stop his mouth at once. You know my position here. I have need of all these people, but still I am entirely yours. Dispose of a life that belongs to you. I have said everywhere that I was at the door and saw nothing. If you are asked for the name of the person who told you this gossip, pray use me. I should be proud to be your champion; but, between ourselves, M. de Bargeton is proper to ask Stanislas for an explanation.... Suppose young Rubempré behaved foolishly, a woman’s character ought not to be at the mercy of the first hare-brained boy who flings himself at her feet. That is what I have been saying.”Naïs bowed and looked thoughtful. She was weary of provincial life, and Châtelet’s words turned her mind to Paris. “Dispose of me, I repeat,” he added.“Thank you,” she answered.“What do you think of doing?”“I shall see.”A prolonged pause. “Are you so fond of that young Rubempré?”A proud smile stole over her lips; she folded her arms and fixed her gaze on the curtains. Châtelet went out; he could not read that high heart.Later, when Lucien and the four old gentlemen who came for whist had left without troubling themselves about tittle-tattle, M. de Bargeton prepared for bed and opened his mouth to bid his wife good-night, when she stopped him.“Come here, dear, I have something to say to you,” she said solemnly.He followed her into the boudoir.“Perhaps I have done wrongly,” she said, “to show a warm interest in M. de Rubempré, which he, as well as the stupid people here, has misinterpreted. This morning Lucien threw himself at my feet with a declaration, and Stanislas happened to come in just as I told the boy to get up. A woman, under any circumstances, has claims which courtesy prescribes to a gentleman; but in contempt of these, Stanislas has been saying he came unexpectedly and found us in an equivocal position. I was treating the boy as he deserved. If the young scatterbrain knew of the scandal caused by his folly, he would go to insult Stanislas and compel him to fight. That would simply be a public proclamation of his love. I need not tell you that your wife is pure; but if you think, you will see it is dishonoring for both you and me if M. de Rubempré defends her. Go at once to Stanislas and ask him to give you satisfaction for his insulting language; mind, you must not accept any explanation short of a full and public retraction in the presence of witnesses of credit. In this way you will win back the respect of right-minded people; you will behave like a man of spirit and a gentleman, and you will have a right to my esteem. I shall send Gentil on horseback to Escarbas; my father must be your second; old as he is, I know he will trample this puppet under foot who has smirched the reputation of Nègrepelisse. You have the choice of weapons, choose pistols; you are an admirable shot.”“I am going,” said M. de Bargeton, and he took his hat and cane.“Good, that is how I like a man to behave, dear; you are a gentleman,” she said, touched, and made the old man happy and proud by putting up her forehead for a kiss. When the carriage gateway shut with a clang behind him, tears came into her eyes despite herself.“How he loves me!” she thought. “He clings to life, poor dear man, and yet he would give his life for me.”M. de Bargeton was not troubled by the prospect of facing a man the next day and looking into the muzzle of a pistol; only one thing made him uncomfortable on the way to M. de Chandour’s—he quaked inwardly.“What shall I say?” he thought; “Naïs really ought to have told me what to say,” and he racked his brains to compose a speech.But people like M. de Bargeton, often silent for lack of scope, behave at great crises with ready solemnity. If they say little, they usually say little foolish; their lack of confidence makes them ponder words to the point of being concise and unexpectedly apt. So M. de Bargeton bore himself like a man of uncommon sense and spirit, and justified those who held he was a philosopher.He reached Stanislas’ house at nine, bowed silently to Amélie before a room full of people, and greeted others with that simple smile which under the circumstances seemed profoundly ironical. There followed a great silence, like the pause before a storm. Châtelet had returned and looked significantly from M. de Bargeton to Stanislas, whom the injured gentleman accosted politely.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Châtelet knew what a visit at that hour meant: old M. de Bargeton was invariably in bed. Clearly Naïs had set the feeble arm in motion. Intimate in the house, Châtelet took M. de Bargeton aside.“Do you wish to speak to Stanislas?”“Yes,” said the old gentleman, pleased to find a go‑between.“Very well; go into Amélie’s bedroom,” said the controller of excise, pleased at the prospect of a duel that might make Mme. de Bargeton a widow and put a barrier between her and Lucien. Châtelet then went to M. de Chandour.“Stanislas,” he said, “here comes Bargeton to call you to account, no doubt, for the things you have been saying about Naïs. Go into your wife’s room, and behave, both of you, like gentlemen. Keep it quiet and make a great show of politeness—behave with phlegmatic British dignity.”A minute later Stanislas and Châtelet went to Bargeton.“Sir,” said the injured husband, “do you say that you discovered Mme. de Bargeton and M. de Rubempré in an equivocal position?”“M. Chardon,” corrected Stanislas with ironic emphasis; he did not take Bargeton seriously.“So be it,” answered the other. “If you do not withdraw your assertions at once before the company now in your house, I must ask you to look for a second. My father‑in‑law, M. de Nègrepelisse, will wait upon you at four o’clock tomorrow morning. Both of us may as well make our final arrangements, for the only way out is the one I have indicated. I choose pistols, as the insulted party.”The speech, long rehearsed, was delivered without excitement. Stanislas turned pale. “After all, what did I see?” he wondered.Put between the shame of recanting and the mute terror Bargeton inspired, Stanislas chose the remote peril.“All right. Tomorrow morning,” he said, thinking it might be arranged somehow.They rejoined the room. Everybody scanned their faces; Châtelet smiled, M. de Bargeton looked in his element, and Stanislas looked ghastly. Some women guessed the conference’s nature, and the whisper, “They are going to fight!” circulated. Half the room thought Stanislas in the wrong—his pallor and manner convicted him of falsehood; the other half admired Bargeton. Châtelet maintained a solemn, mysterious air. Bargeton stayed a few minutes, scrutinized faces, then retired.“Have you pistols?” Châtelet asked Stanislas in a whisper; Stanislas shook violently.Amélie, understanding, felt ill, and the women took her into her bedroom. There was sensation; everybody talked at once. The men stopped in the drawing‑room, declaring with one voice that M. de Bargeton was within his rights.“Would you have thought the old fogy capable of acting like this?” asked M. de Saintot.“But he was a crack shot when young,” said the pitiless Jacques. “My father often told me of Bargeton’s exploits.”“Pooh! Put them at twenty paces, and they will miss each other if you give them cavalry pistols,” said Francis, addressing Châtelet.Châtelet stayed after the rest had gone to reassure Stanislas and his wife, and to explain that all would go off well. In a duel between a man of sixty and one of thirty‑five, the advantage lay with the younger.Early next morning, as Lucien sat at breakfast with David, who had come back alone from Marsac, Mme. Chardon entered with a scared face.“Well, Lucien,” she said, “have you heard? Everyone is talking, even the market. M. de Bargeton nearly killed M. de Chandour this morning in M. Tulloy’s meadow; people are making puns on the name. It seems M. de Chandour said he found you with Mme. de Bargeton yesterday.”“It is a lie! Mme. de Bargeton is innocent,” cried Lucien.“I heard about the duel from a countryman who saw it from his cart. M. de Nègrepelisse came at three to be Bargeton’s second; he told Chandour that if anything happened to his son‑in‑law he would avenge him. A cavalry officer lent the pistols and tried them repeatedly. Du Châtelet tried to prevent practice, but they referred to the officer; he said that unless they meant to behave like children, they ought to have serviceable pistols. The seconds put them at twenty‑five paces. Bargeton looked as if out for a walk. He fired first; the ball lodged in Chandour’s neck, and he dropped before he could return the shot. The house‑surgeon says Chandour will have a wry neck for the rest of his days. I came to tell you how it ended, lest you go to Mme. de Bargeton’s or show yourself in Angoulême, for some of Chandour’s friends might call you out.”As she spoke, the apprentice brought in Gentil, Bargeton’s footman, with a note for Lucien from Louise.“You have doubtless heard,” she wrote, “of the duel between Chandour and my husband. We shall not be at home today. Be careful; do not show yourself. I ask this in the name of the affection you bear me. Would it not be best to spend this melancholy day listening to your Beatrice, whose whole life has been changed by this event, who has a thousand things to say to you?”“Luckily, my marriage is fixed for the day after tomorrow,” said David, “and you will have an excuse for not going to Mme. de Bargeton’s so often.”“Dear David,” returned Lucien, “she asks me to go today; I ought to do as she wishes; she knows better than we do how I should act now.”“Then is everything ready here?” asked Mme. Chardon.“Come and see,” cried David, delighted to show the transformation of the first floor. Everything was new and fresh, pervaded by the sweet influences of early married days, crowned by the wreath of orange blossoms and the bridal veil; days when love’s springtide finds expression in material things, and everything is white and spotless.“Eve’s home will be fit for a princess,” said the mother, “but you have spent too much, you have been reckless.”David smiled, but Mme. Chardon had touched a sore spot. The cost far exceeded his estimates; he could not afford to build beyond the shed. His mother‑in‑law must wait for the home he meant. Nothing is more painful to a generous nature than failing to keep such promises; it mortified the little vanities of affection. David hid his embarrassment to spare Lucien; he feared Lucien might be overwhelmed by the sacrifices made for his sake.“Eve and her girl friends have been working hard,” said Mme. Chardon. “The wedding clothes and house linen are ready. The girls, without letting her know, covered the mattresses with white twill and rose‑colored piping. So pretty! It makes one wish one were going to be married.”Mother and daughter had spent all their savings to furnish David’s home with things a young bachelor never thinks of. They knew he was furnishing splendidly; something had been said about ordering a Limoges dinner‑service, and the two women strove to make Eve’s contributions worthy of David. This emulation in love and generosity would bring husband and wife into difficulties at the outset, yet everything looked homely and comfortable—luxury in a town as behind the times as Angoulême then was.As soon as Lucien saw his mother and David enter the bedroom with blue‑and‑white draperies and neat furniture, he slipped away to Mme. de Bargeton. He found Naïs at table with her husband; Bargeton’s early walk had sharpened his appetite, and he breakfasted quite unconcernedly after all that had passed. Lucien saw the dignified face of M. de Nègrepelisse, the old provincial noble, a relic of the old French nobility, sitting beside Naïs.When Gentil announced M. de Rubempré, the white‑haired old man gave him a keen, curious glance; the father was anxious to form an opinion of the man his daughter had singled out. Lucien’s beauty impressed him; he could not repress an approving look, but seemed to regard the affair as a flirtation, a passing fancy. Breakfast over, Louise left her father and Bargeton together; she beckoned Lucien to follow.“Dear,” she said, half glad, half melancholy, “I am going to Paris, and my father is taking Bargeton back with him to the Escarbas, where he will stay during my absence. Mme. d’Espard (a Blamont‑Chauvry) has great influence and influential relations. The d’Espards are connections of ours; if she acknowledges the relationship, I shall cultivate her; she may procure a place for Bargeton. At my solicitation, it might be desired at Court that he represent the Charente, a step toward his election here. If he were a deputy, it would further other steps I wish to take in Paris. You, my darling, have brought about this change in my life. After this morning’s duel, I must shut up my house for some time; people will side with the Chandours against us. In our position, in a small town, absence is the only way to soften bad feeling. But I shall either succeed and never see Angoulême again, or I shall not succeed and then wait in Paris until I can spend summers at the Escarbas and winters in Paris. It is the only life for a woman of quality; I have waited too long. One day will suffice for our preparations; tomorrow night I set out. You are coming with me, are you not? You shall start first. I will overtake you between Mansle and Ruffec, and we shall soon be in Paris. There is the life for a man with anything in him. We are only at our ease among equals; we are uncomfortable in other society. Paris, besides, is the capital of the intellectual world, the stage on which you will succeed; overleap the gulf that separates us quickly. You must not let your ideas grow rancid in the provinces; put yourself into communication at once with the great men of the nineteenth century. Try to stand well with the Court and those in power. No honor, no distinction, comes to talent that perishes for lack of light in a little town; tell me any great work executed in the provinces! See how Jean‑Jacques, sublime in his poverty, felt the irresistible attraction of that sun of the intellectual world which produces ever‑new glories and stimulates intellect—Paris, where men rub against one another. What is it but your duty to take your place in the succession of pléiades that rise generation after generation? You have no idea how much being brought into a high social light helps a clever young man. I will introduce you to Mme. d’Espard; it’s not easy to get into her set; but you meet all the greatest people at her house—cabinet ministers and ambassadors, great orators, peers and men of influence, wealthy or famous people. A young man with looks and more than sufficient genius could fail to excite interest only by very bad management.“There is no pettiness about those who are truly great; they will support you; and when you have a high position, your work will rise in public opinion. The great problem for the artist is putting himself in evidence. There will be hundreds of chances of making your way, sinecures, a pension from the civil list. The Bourbons are fond of encouraging letters and the arts; you must be a religious poet and a Royalist poet at the same time. Not only is it the right course, but it is the way to get on. Do the Liberals give places and rewards and make the fortunes of men of letters? Take the right road and reach the goal of genius. You have my secret; do not breathe it, and prepare to follow me. Would you rather not go?”To Lucien, listening to these alluring words and bewildered by that rapid bird’s‑eye view of Paris, it seemed as if he had been using only half his brain and suddenly found the other half; his ideas widened. He saw himself stagnating in Angoulême like a frog under a stone. Paris and her splendors rose before him: the Eldorado of provincial imaginings, with golden robes and the royal diadem, arms outstretched to talent. Great men would greet him there. Everything smiled upon genius. There were no jealous booby‑squires to invent gibes and humiliate a man of letters; no stupid indifference to poetry. Paris was the fountain‑head; there the poet was brought into the light and paid. Publishers should no sooner read An Archer of Charles IX than open their cash‑boxes with “How much do you want?” And besides all this, he understood that this journey with Mme. de Bargeton would virtually give her to him; that they should live together.At “Would you rather not go?” tears came to his eyes; he flung his arms about Louise, held her tightly, and kissed her passionately. Suddenly he checked himself, as if memory had struck.“Great heavens!” he cried, “my sister is to be married the day after tomorrow!”That exclamation was the last expiring cry of noble and single‑hearted boyhood. The strong ties that bind young hearts to home and early friendship were to be severed.“Well,” cried the haughty Nègrepelisse, “and what has your sister’s marriage to do with the progress of our love? Have you set your mind so much on being best man at a wedding of tradespeople and workingmen that you cannot give up these exalted joys for my sake? A great sacrifice, indeed!” she went on, scornfully. “This morning I sent my husband out to fight in your quarrel. There, sir, go; I am mistaken in you.”She sank fainting upon the sofa. Lucien went to her, entreating pardon, cursing his family, his sister, and David.“I had such faith in you!” she said. “M. de Cante‑Croix had an adored mother; but to win a letter from me, and the words, ‘I am satisfied,’ he fell in the thick of the fight. And now, when I ask you to take a journey with me, you cannot think of giving up a wedding dinner for my sake.”Lucien was ready to kill himself; his desperation was so genuine that Louise forgave him, though she made him feel he must redeem his mistake.“Come, come,” she said, “be discreet, and to‑morrow at midnight be upon the road, a hundred paces out of Mansle.”Lucien felt the globe shrink; he went back to David’s house, hopes pursuing him like Furies, for he had glimmerings of endless difficulties, summed up in the appalling words, “Where is the money to come from?”He stood in such terror of David’s perspicacity that he locked himself into his pretty new study until he could recover; his head swam in this new position. So he must leave the rooms just furnished at such cost, and all the sacrifices had been made in vain. Then it occurred to him his mother might take the rooms and save David the heavy expense of building at the yard’s end; his departure would be a convenience. He found many urgent reasons for sudden flight; there is no such Jesuit as the desire of your heart. He hurried to tell his sister in L’Houmeau and take counsel. As he reached Postel’s shop, he bethought himself that if all else failed he could borrow enough to live on for a year from his father’s successor.“Three francs per day will be abundance if I live with Louise,” he thought; “it is only a thousand francs for a year. And in six months’ time I shall have plenty of money.”Under seal and promise of secrecy, Eve and her mother heard Lucien’s confidences. Both women began to cry as they heard his ambitious plans; when he asked why they were troubled, they said every penny had been spent on table‑linen, house‑linen, Eve’s wedding clothes, and many things David overlooked. They had been glad to do it, for David had made a marriage‑settlement of ten thousand francs on Eve. Lucien then spoke of a loan; Mme. Chardon undertook to ask M. Postel to lend a thousand francs for a year.“But, Lucien,” said Eve, a thought clutching her heart, “you will not be here at my wedding! Oh! come back; I will put it off a few days. Surely she will give you leave to come back in a fortnight if you go now? Surely she would spare you a week when we brought you up for her? We shall have no luck if you are not at the wedding.... But will a thousand francs be enough?” she asked, interrupting. “Your coat suits you divinely, but you have only that one! Only two fine shirts; the other six are coarse; three of your white ties are common muslin, only two lawn cravats, your handkerchiefs not good. Where will you find a sister in Paris to get up your linen in one day? You will want much more. You have just one pair of new nankeen trousers; last year’s are tight; you will be obliged to have clothes made in Paris, and Paris prices are not Angoulême’s. You have only two presentable white waistcoats; I have mended the others already. Come, I advise you to take two thousand francs.”David came in as she spoke and apparently heard the last two words; he looked at the brother and sister and said nothing.“Do not keep anything from me,” he said at last.“Well,” exclaimed Eve, “he is going away with her.”Mme. Chardon came in again, and, not seeing David, began at once:“Postel is willing to lend you the thousand francs, Lucien,” she said, “but only for six months; and he wants a bill endorsed by your brother‑in‑law, for he says you give him no security.”She turned and saw David; there was a deep silence. The Chardons felt ashamed for having abused David’s goodness. Tears stood in the young printer’s eyes.“Then you will not be here at our wedding,” he began. “You are not going to live with us! And here have I been squandering all that I had! Oh, Lucien, as I came along, bringing Eve her little bits of wedding jewelry, I did not think I should be sorry I spent the money on them.” He brushed his hand over his eyes as he drew the tiny morocco‑covered boxes from his pocket and set them on the table.“Oh! why do you think so much for me?” protested Eve, giving him a divinely sweet smile that belied her words.“Mamma, dear,” said David, “just tell M. Postel I will put my name to the bill, for I can tell from your face, Lucien, that you have quite made up your mind.”Lucien’s head sank dejectedly; there was a pause, then he said, “Do not think hardly of me, my dear, good angels.”He put his arms about Eve and David, drew them close, and held them tightly as he added, “Wait and see what comes of it, and you shall know how much I love you. What is the good of our high thinking, David, if it does not enable us to disregard petty ceremonial in which the law entangles our affections? Shall I not be with you in spirit despite distance? Shall we not be united in thought? Have I not a destiny to fulfil? Will publishers come here to seek my Archer of Charles IX and the Marguerites? A little sooner or later I shall be obliged to do as I am doing to‑day, should I not? And shall I ever find a better opportunity? Does not my success depend upon my entrance into life in Paris through the Marquise d’Espard’s salon?”“He is right,” said Eve; “you yourself said he ought to go to Paris at once.”David took Eve’s hand and led her into the narrow little room where she had slept for seven years.“Love, you were saying just now that he would want two thousand francs?” he murmured in her ear. “Postel is only lending one thousand.”Eve looked anguish at him; he read her meaning.“Listen, my adored Eve, we are making a bad start. Yes, my expenses have taken all my capital; I have just two thousand francs left, and half will be needed to carry on the business. If we give your brother the thousand francs, it will mean giving away our bread; we shall live in anxiety. If I were alone, I know what I should do; but we are two. Decide for us.”Eve, distraught, sprang to his arms and kissed him tenderly through tears: “Do as you would if you were alone; I will work to earn the money.”Despite the most impassioned kiss ever given and taken by betrothed lovers, David left Eve overcome, and went out to Lucien.“Do not worry,” he said; “you shall have your two thousand francs.”“Go see Postel,” said Mme. Chardon, “for you must both sign the bill.”When Lucien and David returned unexpectedly, they found Eve and her mother on their knees in prayer. The women felt sure Lucien’s departure would bring realization of many hopes; at the moment they only felt how much they lost in his parting, and the coming days would be filled with fears for Lucien.“If you could ever forget this sight,” David said in Lucien’s ear, “you would be the basest of men.”David, no doubt, thought these brave words needed; Mme. de Bargeton’s influence seemed less to be feared than Lucien’s unlucky instability—he was so easily led. Eve soon packed Lucien’s clothes; the Fernando Cortez of literature carried but little baggage. He wore his best overcoat, his best waistcoat, and one of the two fine shirts. The whole of his linen, the celebrated coat, and his manuscript made a small package. To hide it from Mme. de Bargeton, David proposed to send it by coach to a paper merchant he dealt with and wrote asking him to keep the parcel until Lucien sent for it.Despite Mme. de Bargeton’s precautions, Châtelet learned she was leaving Angoulême; to discover whether she traveled alone or with Lucien, he sent his man to Ruffec to watch every carriage changing horses there.“If she is taking her poet with her,” he thought, “I have her now.”Lucien set out before daybreak the next morning. David went with him. David had hired a cabriolet, pretending he was going to Marsac on business, a deception that seemed probable. The two friends went to Marsac and spent part of the day with the old “bear.” As evening came they set out again, and at dawn waited on the road beyond Mansle for Mme. de Bargeton. When the seventy‑year‑old traveling carriage he had often seen in the coach‑house appeared, Lucien felt more deeply moved than ever; he sprang into David’s arms.“God grant that this may be for your good!” said David; he climbed into the shabby cabriolet and drove away with dread clutching his heart; he had terrible presentiments of Lucien’s fate in Paris.ADDENDUMNote: Two Poets is part one of a trilogy. The second part is A Distinguished Provincial at Paris and details Lucien’s further adventures. Part three is titled Eve and David and continues their story. In other addendum references parts one and three are combined under the title Lost Illusions.The following personages appear in other stories of the Human Comedy.Bargeton, Madame de (see Châtelet, Baronne du)Cérizet — Eve and David; Though the woman seemed occupied with the nine- or ten-year-old child whose long black hair she toyed with, she watched every look her companion gave. Their bond was not mere love; misery—perhaps the strongest tie—drew their anxious attention.The stranger had a broad, serious head, thick hair like those in Carracci paintings; the jet black was streaked with white. His noble, proud features were marred by a hardness. Despite an erect, strong figure he looked over sixty. His worn clothes were foreign. His wife’s faded, once-beautiful face showed deep sadness, though she forced smiles when he looked. The little girl stood despite weariness, sun-tanned, her Italian cast marked by large black eyes beneath arched brows, native nobleness, and simple grace. Passersby felt moved by the plain despair; when the stranger noticed attention he looked so savage that bold loungers hurried off.After standing some time, the tall stranger wiped his face as if to clear his thoughts. He drew a dagger from his breast and gave it to his companion, saying in Italian, “I will see if the Bonapartes remember us.” He walked slowly to the palace entrance, where a consular guard stopped him and refused to permit discussion. Seeing his obstinacy, the sentinel presented his bayonet. Fortune favored the stranger: the guard was changing, and the corporal pointed to the post commander.“Let Bonaparte know that Bartolomeo di Piombo wishes to speak with him,” the Italian said to the captain.The officer insisted one could not see the First Consul without a written request. Bartolomeo persisted that the soldier must go to Bonaparte. The officer refused, citing regulations. Bartolomeo frowned, casting a terrible look as if charging him with the consequences of refusal. Then he remained under the portico between garden and courtyard. Those who will things intensely are often aided by chance: at that moment a carriage drew up and Lucien Bonaparte, minister of the interior, emerged.“Ah, Loucian, it is lucky for me I have met you!” cried the stranger in Corsican patois. Lucien recognized him and, after a word, took the Corsican away.Murat, Lannes, and Rapp were in the First Consul’s cabinet. As Lucien entered with Piombo, conversation ceased. Lucien took Napoleon by the arm and led him to a window recess. Napoleon signaled Murat and Lannes to retire; Rapp pretended not to see and lingered. Bonaparte spoke sharply and Rapp left reluctantly, though he waited near the adjoining salon. The First Consul opened the door suddenly and found his aide-de-camp close to the wall.“Do you choose not to understand me?” said the First Consul. “I wish to be alone with my compatriot.”“A Corsican!” replied the aide-de-camp. “I distrust those fellows too much to—”Bonaparte smiled and pushed him aside. “Well, what has brought you here, my poor Bartolomeo?” he asked.“To ask asylum and protection from you, if you are a true Corsican,” replied Bartolomeo roughly.“What ill fortune drove you from the island? You were the richest—”“I have killed all the Portas,” said the Corsican in a deep voice, frowning.The First Consul stepped back in surprise.“Do you mean to betray me?” cried Bartolomeo, with a dark look at Bonaparte. “Do you know that there are still four Piombos in Corsica?”Lucien seized his compatriot’s arm and shook it. “Did you come here to threaten the savior of France?” he said.Bonaparte signaled Lucien to be silent, then looked at Piombo. “Why did you kill the Portas?”“We had been friends,” the man replied. “The Barbantis reconciled us. The day after we drank to end our quarrels I left for Bastia. The Portas stayed in my house and set fire to my vineyard at Longone. They killed my son Gregorio. My daughter Ginevra and my wife had taken the sacrament that morning and escaped; the Virgin protected them. When I returned my house was ashes. I found Gregorio’s body by accident at night and knew the Portas had done it. I went to the woods, called together all the men who had ever served me—do you hear me, Bonaparte?—and we marched to the Portas’ vineyard. We arrived at five; at seven they were all before God. Giacomo says Eliza Vanni saved a child, Luigi. But I myself bound Luigi to his bed before setting fire to the house. I left the island with my wife and child without learning whether Luigi is alive.”Bonaparte listened with curiosity but not surprise. Lucien asked how many there had been.“Seven,” replied Piombo. “All of them persecuted you in olden times.”The brothers showed no hatred. “Ha! you are no longer Corsicans!” cried Piombo in despair. “Farewell. In other days I protected you,” he added reproachfully. “Without me your mother would never have reached Marseille,” he said to Bonaparte, who stood silent and thoughtful, his elbow on a mantel-shelf.“As a matter of duty, Piombo,” said Napoleon at last, “I cannot take you under my wing. I have become the leader of a great nation; I command the Republic; I am bound to execute the laws.”“Ha! ha!” said Bartolomeo scornfully.“But I can shut my eyes,” Bonaparte continued. “The tradition of the vendetta will long prevent law's reign in Corsica,” he added to himself. “But it must be destroyed, at any cost.”After a pause he spoke more personally. “Live here, in Paris,” he said to Bartolomeo. “We will know nothing of this affair. I will cause your property in Corsica to be bought to give you enough to live on for the present. Later, we will think of you. But remember, no more vendetta! There are no woods here to fly to. If you play with daggers you must expect no mercy. Here, the law protects all citizens; no one is allowed to do justice for himself.”“He has made himself the head of a singular nation,” said Bartolomeo, taking Lucien’s hand and pressing it. “But you have both recognized me in misfortune, and I am yours henceforth, for life or death. You may dispose as you will of the Piombos.”At these words his Corsican brow unbent and he looked pleasantly about him. “You are not badly off here,” he said, smiling, “You are all in red, like a cardinal.”“Your success depends on yourself; you can have a palace too,” Bonaparte replied, watching him keenly. “It will often happen that I shall need some faithful friend in whom I can confide.”A sigh of joy heaved the Corsican’s chest. He held out his hand to the First Consul. “The Corsican is in you still.”Bonaparte smiled and looked at the man who seemed to bring a breath from the island that had saved him—the land he would never see again. He signaled to his brother, who then took Piombo away. Lucien asked about the family’s finances. Piombo showed his wife and Ginevra sitting on a heap of stones.“We came from Fontainebleau on foot; we have not a single penny,” he said.Lucien gave him his purse and told him to come the next day so arrangements could be made for their comfort. The value of Piombo’s Corsican property, if sold, would scarcely maintain him honorably in Paris.Fifteen years elapsed between Piombo’s arrival in Paris with his family and the following event, which would be scarcely intelligible without this account of the preceding circumstances.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Servin, one of our notable artists, was the first to open a studio for young girls who wished to take painting lessons.About forty, of strict morals and wholly devoted to his art, he had married for inclination the dowerless daughter of a general. At first the mothers brought their daughters; then, seeing the care he took to deserve their confidence, they were content to send them alone.He meant to accept only young ladies of wealthy, well-placed families to avoid complaints about social mixing. He even refused girls who wished to become professional artists, for they required a different, more technical instruction. His prudence and skill, and the security mothers felt that their daughters would be among well-bred companions, made him an esteemed teacher. When a young girl wished to learn to draw, friends invariably advised: “Send her to Servin’s.”Servin thus became a specialty for feminine art; like Herbault for bonnets or Leroy for gowns. It was believed a young woman trained by Servin could judge the Musée, make a striking portrait, copy an old master, or paint a genre picture. He satisfied the aristocracy’s educational needs and, though he kept relations with the best Parisian families, remained independent and patriotic, preserving among them that easy, brilliant, half-ironical tone and freedom of judgment which characterize painters.He had taken careful precautions in arranging the studio. The entrance to the attic above his apartments was walled up. To reach this retreat—a private space—one had to climb a small spiral stair within his rooms. The studio occupied nearly the whole attic under the roof, an unexpectedly vast gallery lit from above through enormous panes with green linen shades artists used to temper light. Dark-gray walls bore caricatures and rapid heads; a small stove, shelves with dusty plaster models—Niobe, a smiling Venus, yellowed écorchés—lay figures, frames without paintings and paintings without frames gave the room that studio physiognomy of jumble and bareness: poverty and riches, care and neglect. The vast atelier, where everything seems small, had something of an operatic coulisse; ancient garments, armor, fragments of stuff and machinery lay about. Yet there was something mysteriously grand: genius and death, Diana and Apollo beside a skull—beauty and destruction—colors glowing in shadows. An artist’s head, symbolically.On a bright July morning a dozen easels rose like masts beneath two rays that traced gold lines in the dust. Several young girls animated the scene by varied expressions, attitudes, and dresses. Strong shadows cast by the green serge curtains, arranged for each easel, produced piquant light-and-shade effects. This group was the prettiest picture in the room.A fair young girl, simply dressed, sat apart, working bravely and seeming in dread of mishap. No one looked at or spoke to her; she was the prettiest, most modest, and apparently the least rich among them. Two principal groups, distinctly separated, showed two cliques, two minds even here, where rank and fortune might seem forgotten.The young girls, sitting or standing amid color-boxes, handling dazzling palettes, painting, laughing, talking, and singing, formed a spectacle unknown to men. One proud, haughty, capricious girl with black hair cast her glance here and there; another light-hearted with chestnut hair smiled and seemed thoughtless; a third was dreamy and melancholy; her neighbor, tall and indolent with long Asiatic eyes, said little and reflected. Another, like the jocoso of a Spanish play, watched the rest with a comprehensive glance, making them laugh and seeming to rule the first group—daughters of bankers, notaries, and merchants—rich but conscious of the cutting slights from the aristocratic set. The latter were led by Mademoiselle Amélie Thirion, daughter of one of the King’s ushers—a little creature as silly as she was vain—proud of court connections, pretentious, always late and very dressed, and demanding low voices.In this aristocratic group were several girls with exquisite figures and distinguished features but little simplicity or candor; their faces lacked frankness; polite manners had formed their character, and social pleasures had dulled sentiment and developed egotism. Yet when the whole class assembled, childlike heads could be seen among them—pure, virgin faces with half-open lips and spotless teeth; a virgin smile flickered. The studio then resembled not a studio but a group of angels on a cloud.By mid-day on this occasion, Servin had not appeared. For days he had spent most of his time in another studio, finishing a picture for the Exposition. All at once Amélie Thirion, leader of the aristocrats, began to speak low and earnestly to her neighbor. A great silence fell on the patrician group; the commercial party, surprised, became equally silent, trying to discover the subject. The secret of the young ultras soon became clear.Amélie rose, took an easel near hers, carried it away from the noble group, and placed it close to a board partition that separated the studio from the attic’s extreme end where broken casts, defaced canvases, and winter wood were kept. Amélie’s action caused a murmur; she hastily rolled aside the stool, the box of colors, and even the Prudhon picture her absent pupil had been copying. After this coup d’état the Right began to work in silence, but the Left discoursed at length.“What will Mademoiselle Piombo say to that?” asked one of Matilde Roguin’s companions.“She’s not a temper to say anything,” replied Matilde; “but fifty years hence she’ll remember the insult as if done the night before, and revenge it cruelly. She is a person I don’t want to be at war with.”“The slight these young ladies mean to put upon her is the more unkind,” said another; “because yesterday Mademoiselle Ginevra was very sad. Her father, they say, has just resigned. They ought not to add to her trouble; I think our ultras act more from jealousy than party spite.”“I have a great mind to get Mademoiselle Piombo’s easel and place it next to mine,” said Matilde. She rose, but sat down again. “With a character like hers one can’t tell how she would take civility; better wait.”The steps of someone climbing the narrow stairway sounded. “Here she comes!” went from mouth to mouth; then absolute silence reigned.To understand the importance of Amélie Thirion’s ostracism, add that this scene occurred toward the end of July, 1815. The second return of the Bourbons had shaken friendships that had held under the first Restoration. Families, almost all divided in opinion, renewed many deplorable scenes that stain history in times of civil war. Children and old men shared the monarchical fever. Discord glided beneath roofs; distrust dyed words and actions of the most intimate friends.Ginevra Piombo adored Napoleon; how then could she hate him? The emperor was her compatriot and the benefactor of her father. Baron di Piombo had been among those who helped most in the return from Elba. Incapable of denying his political faith and anxious to confess it, the old baron remained in Paris among enemies. Ginevra made no secret of the grief the second Restoration caused her family. The only tears she had shed so far had been drawn from her by news of Napoleon’s captivity on the Bellerophon and Labédoyère’s arrest.The studio’s aristocratic girls belonged to the most devoted royalist families in Paris. It is hard now to convey the exaggerations and horror of the Bonapartists then prevalent. However petty Amélie’s action may seem now, it was a natural expression of prevailing hatred. Ginevra, one of Servin’s first pupils, had occupied the place now taken from her since her first day. The aristocratic circle had gradually surrounded her. To drive her from a spot that in some sense belonged to her was not only to insult her, but to inflict an artistic wound; artists have predilect spots where they work.Nevertheless, political prejudice was not the chief motive of the Right clique. Ginevra, the ablest of Servin’s pupils, was an object of intense jealousy. The master admired her talents and character; she outshone others. Without anyone able to explain the ascendancy she obtained, she exercised a prestige not unlike Bonaparte’s over his soldiers.The aristocracy of the studio had for days resolved upon the fall of this queen, but none had yet openly avoided the Bonapartist. Mademoiselle Thirion’s act was a decisive stroke intended to force others to become open accomplices in her hatred. Though Ginevra was sincerely loved by several royalists, most were indoctrinated at home with party ideas and felt it best to keep aloof.On Ginevra’s arrival she was received in profound silence. Of all the young women who came to Servin’s studio, she was the handsomest, tallest, and best made. Her carriage and demeanor were noble and graceful, commanding respect. Her face, instinct with intelligence, seemed to radiate light, inspired with the enthusiasm peculiar to Corsicans—without excluding calm. Her long hair and black eyes and lashes expressed passion; the corners of her mouth and the slightly marked lips showed a kindliness strong beings derive from conscious strength.By a singular caprice, the charm of her face was contrasted by a marble forehead bearing an almost savage pride, from which seemed to emanate Corsican moral instincts. All the rest of her person—the simplicity and ease of her Lombard beauty—was so seductive that it was difficult to hurt her. She inspired attachment so strong that her old father had her accompanied to and from the studio. The only defect of this poetic creature came from the very power of a fully developed beauty; she looked a woman. She had refused marriage out of love for her parents, feeling necessary to their comfort. Her taste for painting took the place of passions that usually absorb her sex.“You are very silent today, mesdemoiselles,” she said after advancing a little among her companions. “Good-morning, my little Laure,” she added in a soft, caressing voice, approaching the young girl painting apart. “That head is strong,—the flesh tints a little too rosy, but the drawing is excellent.”Laure looked tenderly at Ginevra; their faces showed mutual affection. A faint smile brightened the Italian’s lips; she walked slowly to her easel, glanced at the drawings and pictures, and greeted the girls of the first group, not observing the unusual curiosity. She was like a queen in her court; she paid no attention to the profound silence of the patricians and passed before them without a word. She sat before her easel, opened her color-box, took up her brushes, drew on her brown sleeves, arranged her apron, looked at her picture, examined her palette—apparently not thinking of what she was doing. All heads in the banking group turned toward her. If the Thirion camp did not show impatience frankly, their sidelong glances were none the less directed at Ginevra.“She hasn’t noticed it!” said Mademoiselle Roguin.At this instant Ginevra shifted from meditation and turned toward the aristocrats. She measured the distance between her and them at a glance but said nothing.“It hasn’t occurred to her they meant to insult her,” said Matilde; “she neither colored nor turned pale. How vexed these girls will be if she likes her new place as well as the old! You are out of bounds, mademoiselle,” she added aloud.The Italian pretended not to hear; perhaps she really did not. She rose abruptly, walked along the side of the partition that separated the adjoining closet from the studio, and seemed to examine the sash through which her light came—so much importance did she give to it that she mounted a chair to raise the green serge higher. Reaching that height, her eye fell upon a slight opening in the partition—the real object of her curiosity. The glance she cast through it was like a miser discovering Aladdin’s treasure. Then she sprang down, returned to her place, changed the position of her picture, pretended dissatisfaction with the light, pushed a table close to the partition, placed a chair on it, climbed lightly to the summit of this frail erection, and again looked through the crevice. She cast but one glance into the space beyond, lit through a skylight; what she saw produced so strong an effect she tottered.“Take care, Mademoiselle Ginevra, you’ll fall!” cried Laure.All the girls watched the imprudent climber; fear of their coming to her gave her courage; she recovered her equilibrium and replied, balancing on the shaking chair: “Pooh! it is more solid than a throne!”She then secured the curtain, came down, pushed the chair and table as far as possible from the partition, returned to her easel, and seemed to arrange it to suit the light. Her picture, however, was not in her mind; she was wholly bent on getting near the closet door where she finally settled. Sitting there she could distinctly hear a sound that had excited her curiosity the evening before and had whirled her imagination across vast fields of conjecture. She recognized the firm, regular breathing of a man she had just seen asleep. Through the opening she had seen the Imperial eagle; upon the flock bed, faintly lighted from above, lay the form of an officer of the Guard. She guessed all. Servin was hiding a proscribed man.She now trembled lest any companion come near and, by the regular breath or some deeper one, reveal the political victim. She resolved to keep her place by that door, trusting her wits to baffle danger.“Better that I should be here,” she thought, “to prevent some luckless accident, than leave that poor man at the mercy of a heedless betrayal.”This was the secret of the indifference Ginevra had seemed to show to the move of her easel. She was inwardly pleased because the change enabled her to gratify her curiosity; besides, she was too preoccupied to perceive the motive for her removal.Nothing is more mortifying to young girls than to see an insult miss its effect through the contempt or indifference of the intended victim. Hatred to an enemy seems to grow as that enemy is raised above us. Ginevra’s behavior was an enigma; friends and enemies were equally surprised; her friends claimed all virtues for her except forgiveness of injuries. Though occasions for that vice rarely arose in a studio, the specimens she had sometimes shown of vindictiveness left a strong impression.After many conjectures, Mademoiselle Roguin concluded the Italian’s silence showed grandeur of soul; the banking circle, inspired, formed a project to humiliate the aristocracy. They succeeded, by a fire of sarcasms, in humbling the Thirion party.Madame Servin’s arrival put a stop to the struggle.
