The last rays of sun faded from Ginevra’s face as she sat sleeping in her chair, holding her child on her breast.“Wake, my dear one,” he said, not seeing the infant, who at that moment shone with a strange pallor.Hearing his voice, the poor mother opened her eyes, met Luigi’s look, and smiled; but Luigi cried out in horror; he scarcely recognized his wife, half mad. With a savage gesture he showed her the gold. Ginevra laughed mechanically, then cried in a dreadful voice, “The child, Luigi, he is cold!”She looked at her son and swooned. The little Bartolomeo was dead. Luigi took his wife in his arms; after laying her on the bed he went out to seek help.“Oh! my God!” he cried as he met his landlord on the stairs. “I have gold, and my child has died of hunger, and his mother is dying, too! Help me!”He returned like one distraught to his wife, leaving the mason and neighbors to gather a few things for needs they had been too proud to reveal.Luigi had cast his gold upon the floor and knelt by the bed. Ginevra, delirious, murmured, “Father! take care of my son, who bears your name.”“Oh, my angel! be calm,” Luigi said, kissing her; “our good days are coming back.”“My Luigi,” she said, looking at him with extraordinary attention, “listen. I feel that I am dying. My death is natural; I suffered too much; besides, such happiness as mine must be paid for. Yes, Luigi, be comforted. I have been so happy that if I lived again I would accept our fate. I am a bad mother; I regret you more than my child—My child!” she added hollowly.Two tears escaped her dying eyes, and suddenly she pressed the little body she could not warm.“Give my hair to my father, in memory of his Ginevra,” she said. “Tell him I have never blamed him.”Her head fell on her husband’s arm.“No, you cannot die!” cried Luigi. “The doctor is coming. We have food. Your father will take you home. Prosperity is here. Stay with us, angel!”Her faithful heart, full of love, grew cold. Ginevra turned her eyes to him she loved, though she was conscious of nothing else. Confused images passed before her; memory of earth faded. She knew Luigi was there and clasped his hand tightly as if to hold herself from some precipice.“Dear,” she said at last, “you are cold; I will warm you.”She tried to put his hand upon her heart, and died.Two doctors, a priest, and neighbors came into the room, bringing what might save the poor couple and calm despair. They made some noise entering; afterwards an awful silence filled the room.While that scene took place, Bartolomeo Piombo and his wife sat in their antique chairs at the corners of a vast fireplace in their salon. A friend had just left; the chair he had occupied remained between the two Corsicans. The clock told midnight. The old couple had lost the ability to sleep; they sat silent, like persons in their dotage, gazing at things they did not see. Their deserted salon, filled with memories, was feebly lit by a single expiring lamp. Without the hearth’s spark they might soon be in total darkness.Piombo cast glances at the empty chair—glances full of remorse—for it was Ginevra’s seat. Elisa Piombo watched expressions cross her husband’s pallid face; tonight they seemed threatening and melancholy, making his soul incomprehensible to her.Would Bartolomeo yield at last to the memories that chair awakened? Had he been struck to see a stranger occupy it, used for the first time since his daughter left? Had the hour of his mercy struck—the hour Elisa had prayed and waited for?These thoughts shook the mother. For an instant her husband’s countenance became so terrible she trembled at having spoken of Ginevra. The wintry night blew snow sharply against the blinds; the old woman dropped her head to hide tears. Suddenly a sigh burst from the old man; he seemed crushed. She risked speaking—for the second time in three years—of his daughter.“Ginevra may be cold,” she said softly.Piombo quivered.“She may be hungry,” she continued.The old man dropped a tear.“Perhaps she has a child and cannot suckle it; her milk is dried up!” said the mother in despair.“Let her come! let her come to me!” cried Piombo. “Oh! my precious child, thou hast conquered me.”The mother rose as if to fetch her daughter. At that instant the door opened noisily, and a man, his face no longer human, stood before them.“Dead! Our two families were doomed to exterminate each other. Here is all that remains of her,” he said, laying Ginevra’s long black hair upon the table.The old people shook as if struck by lightning.Luigi no longer stood before them.“He has spared me a shot, for he is dead,” Bartolomeo slowly said, gazing at the ground.ADDENDUMThe following personages appear in other stories of the Human Comedy.- Bidault (known as Gigonnet) — The Government Clerks; Gobseck; Cesar Birotteau; The Firm of Nucingen; A Daughter of Eve- Bonaparte, Napoleon — The Gondreville Mystery; Colonel Chabert; Domestic Peace; The Seamy Side of History; A Woman of Thirty- Bonaparte, Lucien — The Gondreville Mystery- Camusot de Marville, Madame — Cesar Birotteau; Jealousies of a Country Town; Scenes from a Courtesan’s Life; Cousin Pons- Magus, Elie — A Marriage Settlement; A Bachelor’s Establishment; Pierre Grassou; Cousin Pons- Murat, Joachim, Prince — The Gondreville Mystery; Colonel Chabert; Domestic Peace; The Country Doctor- Rapp — The Gondreville Mystery- Roguin — Cesar Birotteau; Eugenie Grandet; A Bachelor’s Establishment; Pierrette- Thirion — Cesar Birotteau; Jealousies of a Country Town- Tiphaine, Madame — Pierrette- Vergniaud, Louis — Colonel Chabert############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################################ MIDDLE OF STORY ################################################################ MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ######################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ######################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################By Honore De BalzacTranslated by Katharine Prescott WormeleyDEDICATIONTo my dear Alexandre de Berny.His old friend,De Balzac.Many tales, rich in incident or shaped by chance, carry their own setting and can be told without losing charm. But some human incidents require the heart to give them life; certain delicate, almost anatomical touches cannot be reproduced except through an equally delicate rendering. Some portraits demand the infusion of a soul; they mean nothing unless the subtlest expression of a speaking countenance is given. There are things we cannot say or do without the aid of secret harmonies produced by a day, an hour, a fortunate conjunction, or an inward tendency.Such mysterious revelations are needed to tell this simple history, which we hope will interest the naturally grave and reflective who find sustenance in tender emotions. If the writer, like the surgeon beside his dying friend, feels a species of reverence for his subject, should not the reader share that inexplicable feeling? Is it so difficult to be in unison with the vague, nervous sadness that casts gray tints about us—a semi-illness whose gentle sufferings are often pleasing? If the reader sometimes thinks of lost dear ones, if he is alone, if the day wanes or the night has come, let him read on; otherwise he should lay aside this book. If he has never buried a good old relative, infirm and poor, he will not understand these pages, which to some will seem redolent of musk, to others colorless and virtuous as Florian. In short, the reader must have known the luxury of tears, felt the silent pangs of passing memory, the vision of a dear yet far-off Shade—memories that bring regret for all earth has swallowed up, with smiles for vanished joys.Believe that the writer would not, for the wealth of England, steal from poesy a single lie to embellish this narrative. The following is a true history, on which you may safely spend the treasures of your sensibility—if you have any.In these days the French language has as many idioms and represents as many idiosyncrasies as there are varieties of men in the great family of France. It is curious and amusing to hear the different interpretations or versions of the same thing by the various species that compose the genus Parisian—“Parisian” used merely to generalize our remark.If you say to an individual of the species Practical, “Do you know Madame Firmiani?” he will present that lady by this inventory: “Fine house in the rue du Bac, salons handsomely furnished, good pictures, one hundred thousand francs a year, husband formerly receiver-general of the department of Montenotte.” Thus the Practical man—rotund, fat, usually dressed in black—will project his lower lip, nodding as if to add: “Solid people, those; nothing to be said against them.” Ask no further; Practical men settle everyone’s status by figures, incomes, or solid acres.Turn to the right and ask that same question of the species Lounger. “Madame Firmiani?” he says; “yes, I know her; I go to her parties; receives Wednesdays; highly creditable house.” Madame Firmiani is metamorphosed into a house! but not a pile of stones—“house” in the Lounger’s language carries an indefinable idiom. The Lounger, spare, with an agreeable smile and more acquired cleverness than wit, stoops to your ear and adds with a shrewd glance: “I have never seen Monsieur Firmiani. His social position is that of looking after property in Italy. Madame Firmiani is a Frenchwoman, and spends her money like a Parisian. She has excellent tea. It is one of the few houses where you can amuse yourself; the refreshments are exquisite. It is very difficult to get admitted; therefore, of course, one meets only the best society in her salons.” Here the Lounger takes a pinch of snuff; he inhales slowly and seems to say: “I go there, but don’t expect me to present you.”Evidently the Lounger considers Madame Firmiani keeps a sort of inn, without a sign.“Why do you want to know Madame Firmiani? Her parties are as dull as the Court itself. What is the good of possessing a mind unless to avoid such salons, where stupid talk and foolish little ballads are the order of the day.” You have questioned an Egotist, a species that would keep the universe under lock and key and let nothing be done without their permission. They are unhappy if others are happy; they forgive nothing but vices, downfalls, frailties, and like none but protégés. Aristocrats by inclination, they make themselves democrats out of spite, preferring to consort with inferiors as equals.“Oh, Madame Firmiani, my dear fellow! she is one of those adorable women who serve as Nature’s excuse for all the ugly ones she creates. Madame Firmiani is enchanting, and so kind! I wish I were in power and possessed millions that I might—” (here a whisper). “Shall I present you?” The speaker is of the Student species, bold among men and timid in the boudoir.“Madame Firmiani?” cries another, twirling his cane. “I’ll tell you what I think of her; she is a woman between thirty and thirty-five; faded complexion, handsome eyes, flat figure, contralto voice worn out, much dressed, rather rouged, charming manners; in short, the remains of a pretty woman still worth the trouble of a passion.” This is the Fop, who has just breakfasted, doesn’t weigh his words, and is about to mount his horse. At that moment Fops are pitiless.“Magnificent collection of pictures in her house; go and see them by all means,” answers another. “Nothing finer.” You have questioned a Connoisseur. He leaves you to go to Perignon’s or Tripet’s. To him, Madame Firmiani is a collection of painted canvases.A Woman: “Madame Firmiani? I don’t wish you to visit her.” Rich in meanings—dangerous woman! a siren! dresses well, has taste; gives other women sleepless nights. Your informant belongs to the genus Spiteful.An Attache to an embassy: “Madame Firmiani? Isn’t she from Antwerp? I saw her ten years ago in Rome; she was very handsome then.” Attaches have a mania for speaking in Talleyrand’s style. Their wit is so refined the point is often imperceptible; they are like billiard-players who avoid hitting the ball with consummate dexterity. These individuals are usually taciturn, and when they talk it is only about Spain, Vienna, Italy, or Petersburg. Names of countries act like springs in their minds; press them, and the ringing of their changes begins.“That Madame Firmiani sees a great deal of the faubourg Saint-Germain, doesn’t she?” says one who desires to belong to the class Distinguished. She gives the “de” to everybody—Monsieur Dupin senior, Monsieur Lafayette; she flings it right and left and humiliates many. This woman spends her life striving to know and do “the right thing”; but, for her sins, she lives in the Marais, and her husband is a lawyer—a lawyer before the Royal courts, however.“Madame Firmiani, monsieur? I do not know her.” This man belongs to the species Duke. He recognizes none but women presented at court. Pray excuse him, he was one of Napoleon’s creations.“Madame Firmiani? surely she used to sing at the Opera-house.” Species Ninny. Individuals of this species have an answer for everything; they will tell lies sooner than say nothing.Two old ladies, wives of former magistrates: The First (cap with bows, wrinkled face, sharp nose, hard voice, prayer-book in hand): “What was that Madame Firmiani’s maiden name?”—The Second (small face red as a crab-apple, gentle voice): “She was a Cadignan, my dear, niece of the old Prince de Cadignan, consequently cousin to the present Duc de Maufrigneuse.”Madame Firmiani is a Cadignan. She might lack virtue, wealth, or youth, but she would still be a Cadignan; it is a prejudice always alive and working.An Original: “My dear fellow, I’ve seen no galoshes in her antechamber; consequently you can visit her without compromising yourself, and play cards there without fear; if there are any scoundrels in her salons, they are people of quality and come in carriages; such persons never quarrel.”Old man of the genus Observer: “If you call on Madame Firmiani, you will find a beautiful woman sitting at her ease by the corner of her fireplace. She will scarcely rise to receive you—she only does that for women, ambassadors, dukes, and persons of great distinction. She is very gracious, possesses charm; she converses well, and likes to talk on many topics. There are many indications of a passionate nature about her; but she has so many adorers that she cannot have a favorite. If suspicion rested on two or three intimates, we might say one or other was the ‘cavaliere servente’; but it does not. The lady is a mystery. She is married, though none of us have seen her husband. Monsieur Firmiani is altogether mythical; he is like that third post-horse for which we pay though we never behold it. Madame has the finest contralto voice in Europe, so say judges; but she has never been heard to sing more than two or three times since she came to Paris. She receives much company, but goes nowhere.”The Observer speaks as an Oracle. His words, anecdotes, and quotations must be accepted as truths, under pain of being thought without social education or intelligence, and of causing him to slander you with zest in twenty salons where he is indispensable. The Observer is forty, never dines at home, declares himself no longer dangerous to women, wears a maroon coat, and has a place reserved for him in several boxes at the “Bouffons.” He is sometimes confounded with the Parasite; but he has filled too many real functions to be thought a sponger; moreover he possesses a small estate in a certain department, the name of which he has never been known to utter.“Madame Firmiani? why, my dear fellow, she was Murat’s former mistress.” This man belongs to the Contradictors—persons who note errata in memoirs, rectify dates, correct facts, bet a hundred to one, and are certain about everything. You can detect them in some gross blunder in the course of a single evening. They will tell you they were in Paris at the time of Mallet’s conspiracy, forgetting that half an hour earlier they described how they had crossed the Berezina. Nearly all Contradictors are “chevaliers” of the Legion of honor; they talk loudly, have retreating foreheads, and play high.“Madame Firmiani a hundred thousand francs a year? nonsense, you are crazy! Some people will persist in giving millions with the liberality of authors, to whom it costs nothing to dower their heroines. Madame Firmiani is simply a coquette, who has lately ruined a young man, and now prevents him from making a fine marriage. If she were not so handsome she wouldn’t have a penny.”Ah, that one—of course you recognize him—belongs to the species Envious. There is no need to sketch him; the species is as well known as that of the felis domestica. But how explain the perennial vigor of envy?—a virtue that brings nothing in!############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Persons in society, literary men, honest folk—in short, individuals of all species—were promulgating in January 1824 so many different opinions about Madame Firmiani that it would be tedious to list them all. A man who wanted to know her, yet could not or would not visit her house, could learn from hearsay that she was a widow or a wife, silly or wise, virtuous or the reverse, rich or poor, soulless or full of feeling, handsome or plain—as many Madame Firmianis as there are species in society or sects in Catholicism. Frightful reflection! We are all like lithographic blocks from which various copies can be drawn—the proofs resembling us more or less according to imperceptible shading—so that our reputation depends (barring the calumnies of friends and the witticisms of newspapers) on the balance struck by our critics between Truth that limps and Falsehood to which Parisian wit gives wings.Madame Firmiani, like other noble women who make their hearts a sanctuary and disdain the world, was likely to be misjudged by Monsieur de Bourbonne, an old country magnate with reason to think of her that winter. He belonged to the class of provincial planters—men living on their estates, keeping close accounts, bargaining with the peasantry. Thus employed, a man becomes sagacious in spite of himself. The old gentleman had come to Paris from Touraine to satisfy his curiosity about Madame Firmiani. He was a man of honor and breeding. His sole heir was a nephew he greatly loved, in whose interests he planted his poplars. When a man thinks without annoyance about his heir, watching trees grow finer for his future benefit, affection grows too with every blow of the spade; though not common, this feeling is to be met with in Touraine.This cherished nephew, Octave de Camps, descended from the famous Abbé de Camps—well known to bibliophiles and learned men. Provincials have the habit of reputably condemning any young man who sells inherited estates. This antiquated prejudice had interfered with the stock-jobbing the government encouraged. Without consulting his uncle, Octave had lately sold an estate to the Bande Noire, a mysterious association of speculators that bought landed estates, cut them up, and sold parcels to peasants or others. The château de Villaines would have been pulled down were it not for the remonstrances the old uncle made to the representatives of the “Pickaxe company.” To increase the old man’s wrath, a distant relative—one of those cousins of small means and much astuteness about whom shrewd provincials remark, “No lawsuits for me with him!”—had, as it were by accident, come to visit Monsieur de Bourbonne and incidentally told him of his nephew’s ruin.Monsieur Octave de Camps, he said, having wasted his means on a certain Madame Firmiani, was now reduced to teaching mathematics for a living, while awaiting his uncle’s death, not daring to let him know of his dissipations. This distant cousin, a sort of Charles Moor, was not ashamed to give this fatal news to the old gentleman as he sat by his fire, digesting a profuse provincial dinner.But heirs cannot always rid themselves of uncles. The uncle refused to believe the story, and came victorious from the attack of indigestion produced by his nephew’s biography. Some shocks affect the heart, others the head; in this case the cousin’s blow fell on the digestive organs and did little harm, for the old man’s stomach was sound. Like a true disciple of Saint Thomas, Monsieur de Bourbonne came to Paris, unknown to Octave, resolved to make full inquiries as to his nephew’s insolvency. Having many acquaintances in the faubourg Saint-Germain, he heard so much gossip, so many facts and falsities, about Madame Firmiani that he resolved to be presented to her under the name of de Rouxellay, that of his estate in Touraine. The astute old gentleman was careful to choose an evening when he knew Octave would be engaged in finishing a piece of work which was to pay him well—for this so-called lover of Madame Firmiani still went to her house; a circumstance that seemed difficult to explain. As to Octave’s ruin, that, unfortunately, was no fable, as Monsieur de Bourbonne at once discovered.Monsieur de Rouxellay was not at all like the provincial uncle at the Gymnase. Formerly in the King’s guard, a man of the world and a favorite among women, he knew how to present himself in society with the courteous manners of the olden time; he could make graceful speeches and understand the Charter. Though he loved the Bourbons with noble frankness, believed in God as a gentleman should, and read nothing but the Quotidienne, he was not as ridiculous as the liberals of his department would fain have him. He could hold his own in the court circle, provided no one talked to him of “Moses in Egypt,” nor of the drama, or romanticism, nor of railways. He himself had never got beyond Voltaire, Buffon, Payronnet, and the Chevalier Gluck, the Queen’s favorite musician.“Madame,” he said to the Marquise de Listomère, who was on his arm as they entered Madame Firmiani’s salons, “if this woman is my nephew’s mistress, I pity him. How can she live in the midst of this luxury, and know that he is in a garret? Hasn’t she any soul? Octave is a fool to have given up such an estate as Villaines for a—”Monsieur de Bourbonne belonged to the species Fossil, and used the language of the days of yore.“But suppose he had lost it at play?”“Then, madame, he would at least have had the pleasure of gambling.”“And do you think he has had no pleasure here? See! look at Madame Firmiani.”The brightest memories of the old man faded at the sight of his nephew’s so-called mistress. His anger died away at the gracious exclamation that escaped him as he looked at her. By one of those fortunate accidents that happen only to pretty women, it was a moment when all her beauties shone with peculiar lustre, perhaps due to the wax-lights, to the charming simplicity of her dress, and to the ineffable atmosphere of elegance that surrounded her. One must have studied the transitions of an evening in a Parisian salon to appreciate the imperceptible lights and shades which color a woman’s face and vary it. There comes a moment when, content with her toilet, pleased with her own wit, delighted to be admired, and feeling herself the queen of a salon full of remarkable men who smile to her, the Parisian woman reaches a full consciousness of her grace and charm; her beauty is enhanced by the looks she gathers in—a mute homage which she transfers with subtle glances to the man she loves. At moments like these a woman is invested with supernatural power and becomes a magician, a charmer, without herself knowing that she is one; involuntarily she inspires the love that fills her own bosom; her smiles and glances fascinate. If this condition, which comes from the soul, can give attraction even to a plain woman, with what radiance does it not invest a woman of natural elegance, distinguished bearing, fair, fresh, with sparkling eyes, and dressed in a taste that wrings approval from artists and her bitterest rivals.Have you ever met a woman whose harmonious voice lends to her speech the same charm that emanates from her manners? A woman who knows how to speak and to be silent, whose words are happily chosen, whose language is pure, and who concerns herself in your interests with delicacy? Her raillery is caressing, her criticism never wounds; she neither discourses nor argues, but she likes to lead a discussion and stop it at the right moment. Her manner is affable and smiling, her politeness never forced, her readiness to serve others never servile; she reduces the respect she claims to a soft shadow; she never wearies you, and you leave her satisfied with her and with yourself. Her charming grace is conveyed to all the things with which she surrounds herself. Everything about her pleases the eye; in her presence you breathe, as it were, your native air. This woman is natural. There is no effort about her; she is aiming at no effect; her feelings are shown simply, because they are true. Frank herself, she does not wound the vanity of others; she accepts men as God made them; pitying the vicious, forgiving defects and absurdities, comprehending all ages, and vexed by nothing, because she has had the sense and tact to foresee all. Tender and gay, she gratifies before she consoles. You love her so well that if this angel did wrong you would be ready to excuse her. If, for your happiness, you have met with such a woman, you know Madame Firmiani.After Monsieur de Bourbonne had talked with her for ten minutes, sitting beside her, his nephew was forgiven. He perceived that whatever the actual truth might be, the relation between Madame Firmiani and Octave covered some mystery. Returning to the illusions that gild the days of youth, and judging Madame Firmiani by her beauty, the old gentleman became convinced that a woman so innately conscious of her dignity as she appeared to be was incapable of a bad action. Her dark eyes told of inward peace; the lines of her face were so noble, the profile so pure, and the passion he had come to investigate seemed so little to oppress her heart, that the old man said to himself, while noting all the promises of love and virtue given by that adorable countenance, “My nephew is committing some folly.”Madame Firmiani acknowledged to twenty-five. But the Practicals proved that having married the invisible Firmiani (then a highly respectable individual in the forties) in 1813, at the age of sixteen, she must be at least twenty-eight in 1825. However the same persons also asserted that at no period of her life had she ever been so desirable or so completely a woman. She was now at an age when women are most prone to conceive a passion, and to desire it, perhaps, in their pensive hours. She possessed all that earth sells, all that it lends, all that it gives. The Attaches declared there was nothing of which she was ignorant; the Contradictors asserted that there was much she ought to learn; the Observers remarked that her hands were white, her feet small, her movements a trifle too undulating. But, nevertheless, individuals of all species envied or disputed Octave’s happiness, agreeing, for once in a way, that Madame Firmiani was the most aristocratically beautiful woman in Paris.Still young, rich, a perfect musician, intelligent, witty, refined, and received by the Princesse de Blamont-Chauvry, that oracle of the noble faubourg, loved by her rivals the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse her cousin, the Marquise d’Espard, and Madame de Macumer,—Madame Firmiani gratified all the vanities which feed or excite love. She was therefore sought by too many men not to fall a victim to Parisian malice and its charming calumnies, whispered behind a fan or in a safe aside. It was necessary to quote the remarks given at the beginning of this history to bring out the true Firmiani in contradistinction to the Firmiani of society. If some women forgave her happiness, others did not forgive her propriety. Now nothing is so dangerous in Paris as unfounded suspicions—for the reason that it is impossible to destroy them.This sketch of a woman who was admirably natural gives only a faint idea of her. It would need the pencil of an Ingres to render the pride of that brow, with its wealth of hair, the dignity of that glance, and the thoughts betrayed by the changing colors of her cheeks. In her were all things; poets could have found an Agnes Sorel and a Joan of Arc, also the woman unknown, the Soul within that form, the soul of Eve, the knowledge of the treasures of good and the riches of evil, error and resignation, crime and devotion, the Donna Julia and the Haïdee of Lord Byron.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The former guardsman lingered impertinently after the other guests had left; Madame Firmiani found him still in an armchair at two in the morning, determined to remain like a fly. As she rose, the old gentleman said, “Madame, I am the uncle of Monsieur Octave de Camps.”Madame Firmiani sat again, visibly moved. Despite his experience, he could not tell whether her pallor came from shame or pleasure; delicate women often betray delicious emotions by modesty. Monsieur de Bourbonne, less generous in interpretation, was distrustful.“Well, monsieur?” she asked, giving him a glance that questioned more than it revealed.“In my province they tell me my nephew has ruined himself for you and lives in a garret while you are in silk and gold,” he said bluntly. “Forgive my rustic sincerity; it may be useful for you to know of these calumnies.”“Stop, monsieur,” she interrupted imperatively. “I know all that. You are too polite to continue and too gallant—if old-fashioned—to question me. It would be ridiculous to defend myself. I hope you will think well of my character and believe the profound contempt I feel for money, although I was married, without fortune, to a man of immense wealth. It is nothing to me whether your nephew is rich or poor; I receive him because I esteem him. All my friends respect each other; I have not the philosophy to admit those I do not esteem. My guardian angel preserves in me a profound aversion to tattle and dishonesty.”Her voice rose and then settled, finishing with calm self-possession.“I am almost Octave’s father,” the old man said humbly, “and I ask your pardon for the question I now venture to put, giving you my word of honor that your answer shall die here”—laying his hand on his heart—“Are these rumors true; do you love Octave?”“To any other man I would answer by a look,” she replied. “But to you—since you are almost his father—I ask what you would think of a woman who answered? To avow our love for one we love—when he loves us—ah! that may be; but even when sure of being loved forever, believe me, it is an effort for us and a reward to him.” She rose, bowed, and withdrew into her private rooms, the doors closing like speech.“Ah! the mischief!” he thought; “what a woman! she is either sly or an angel.” He left in his hired coach, the horses stamping while the coachman slept on his box, cursing his tardy passenger.Next morning the old gentleman mounted the stairs to Octave’s garret in the rue de l’Observance. The young professor, who had been up all night, was astonished to see his uncle. Monsieur de Bourbonne sat down and reproached him in a comic, affectionate torrent: since when was it fashionable to laugh at uncles who have twenty-six thousand francs a year? Had he not been an indulgent uncle? Had he ever refused money? He warned Octave he had been the most accommodating uncle in France and at least intended to make him the prettiest estate in Touraine one day. Yet the nephew was living like a lackey, perhaps ruined by gambling or the Bourse.“Have you been gambling? lost money?” the uncle demanded. “Say, ‘Uncle, I’m a wretch,’ and I’ll hug you. But if you lie to me I’ll sell my property, buy an annuity, and go back to the evils of my youth—if I can.”“I saw Madame Firmiani yesterday,” he added, kissing his fingers. “She is charming. You have my consent and approbation, if that will do you any good. Have you ruined yourself for her?”“Yes, uncle.”“Aha! the jade! In my time women of the court were cleverer at ruining a man than the courtesans, but she—she is of the last century!”“You are mistaken,” Octave said gravely. “Madame Firmiani deserves esteem and all adoration.”“Youth! always the same,” the uncle cried. But when Octave produced a letter that would explain nearly all, Monsieur de Bourbonne, without spectacles, told him to read it aloud.Octave began reading Madame Firmiani’s letter:“My beloved—”“Hey, then you are still intimate with her?” the uncle interrupted.“Yes, of course,” Octave replied.“You haven’t parted from her?”“Parted! We are married.”“Heavens!” cried the uncle. Octave read on, some passages moving him.In the letter she asked why he was sad and confessed her struggle between love and conscience. She explained that since learning of his father’s injustice—how he had deprived others of their property—her view of him was tarnished. She could not respect a man who knowingly held property obtained by deceit. A wife’s love needs reverence and esteem in the man she loves; she could not sacrifice her conscience even for love. She urged him to consult his conscience and, if the accusation were true, to make restitution. She insisted this act should spring from his own moral conviction, not from her urging; a man who truly loves a woman respects the sanctity of her trust.Octave’s eyes filled with tears. The uncle urged him to continue; Octave said the rest was for lovers.Monsieur de Bourbonne softened: “I too have loved: ‘et ego in Arcadia.’ But I don’t understand why you give lessons in mathematics.”Octave explained: as his father’s heir he had been tainted by that capital. After reading the letter, a revolution took place within him. He felt shame when driving or eating, a sense that the possessions were not honestly his. He rushed to Madame Firmiani; together they calculated what was due to the Bourgneufs. Against her advice, he condemned himself to pay three percent interest, but his funds were insufficient. She offered, and he accepted, her savings.“You’re saving money too?” the uncle said, surprised.“Don’t laugh,” Octave answered. Her position had obliged prudence. Her husband had gone to Greece in 1820 and died three years later; legal proof of his death and the will leaving property to her had been missing—lost in the troubles of Greece, where consulates and registers were unreliable. Unsure whether she might be forced to give up her fortune, she lived austerely. Octave wanted to acquire property of his own to provide for his wife if she lost hers.“But why didn’t you tell me all this?” the uncle asked. “I love you enough to pay your debts. I’ll be the traditional uncle and avenge myself!”“Let me get rich by my own industry,” Octave pleaded. “If you truly want to help, make me an allowance of two or three thousand francs a year until I can undertake the enterprise I have in mind. I give lessons so I may not live at the cost of anyone. If you knew how happy I was making restitution! I found the Bourgneufs living miserably. The old father was a lottery agent; the daughters worked, the mother was ill. I entered as the accomplice in a crime and left as a man who had purged his father’s memory. The visit was like a drama. To bring sudden opulence to a family sitting by the light of a single miserable lamp—no words can describe it. My extreme justice seemed to them almost unjust. If there is a Paradise, my father is happy there now. As for me, I am loved as none was ever loved. Madame Firmiani gives me more than happiness; she has inspired in me a delicacy of feeling I lacked. I call her my dear conscience. I find honesty profitable; I hope to get rich by an industrial scheme.”“You have your mother’s soul,” the uncle said, his eyes filling for his sister.Just then a carriage arrived despite the narrow street. “There she is!” Octave cried. Madame Firmiani entered, mildly annoyed to see Monsieur de Bourbonne, then smiling explained she had come to entreat her husband to accept her fortune. The Austrian Embassy had just sent a document proving Monsieur Firmiani’s death and the will, which his valet had kept to give her. “Octave, you can accept it all; you are richer than I, for you have treasures here”—she laid her hand on his heart—“to which none but God can add.” Overcome with happiness, she laid her head on his breast.“My dear niece,” said the old man, “in my day we made love; in yours, you love. You women are all that is best in humanity; you are not even guilty of your faults, for they come through us.”ADDENDUMThe following personages appear in other stories of the Human Comedy.Blamont-Chauvry, Princesse de — The Thirteen; Madame Firmiani; The Lily of the ValleyBourbonne, De — Madame Firmiani; The Vicar of ToursCamps, Octave de — Madame Firmiani; The Member for ArcisCamps, Madame Octave de — Madame Firmiani; The Government Clerks; A Woman of Thirty; A Daughter of Eve; The Member for Arcis############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################CENTER OF THE STORY############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ##################################################################### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #################################################################### MIDDLE OF THE STORY.############################################################################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################The Rue du Tourniquet-Saint-Jean, once one of the darkest, most tortuous lanes near the Hôtel de Ville, wound around the little gardens of the Préfecture de Police and ended at the Rue Martroi by a wall since pulled down. A turnstile that gave the street its name stood until 1823, when the Municipality built a ballroom on the neighboring garden for the fête of the Duc d’Angoulême.Even at its widest, where it opened on the Rue de la Tixeranderie, the Rue du Tourniquet measured under six feet. In rain the gutter water ran deep at the house bases, sweeping dust and refuse into corners where carts could not reach; the inhabitants trusted storms to wash their miry alley. In summer the sun lit this blackness for a few minutes, never drying the permanent damp that rose to the first story. Residents who lit lamps at five in June kept them burning through winter. A traveler approaching the Marais along the quays, past the Rue du Chaume and the Rues de l’Homme-Armé, des Billettes, and des Deux-Portes, might think he had passed through cellars all the way.Many old Paris streets were such damp labyrinths, where antiquaries find curiosities. On the corner where the Rue du Tourniquet met the Rue de la Tixeranderie two iron ring clamps remained, relics of chains the watch once strung nightly. The corner house testified to the unhealthiness of such dwellings: its cellar arches rose about two feet above ground, the entrance required three outside steps, and the door was crowned by a closed arch whose keystone bore a female head and time-eaten arabesques. Three windows, sills about five feet from the ground, lit a small set of rooms facing the Rue du Tourniquet, protected by strong iron bars curved outward below like a baker's.By day a passer-by could see little; only in high summer, when light strengthened, might one discern in the second room two beds hung with green serge side by side in an old-fashioned alcove. But by about three, when candles were lighted, the nearer pane showed an old woman on a stool tending a brazier to simmer a porter’s-wife stew; a few utensils hung against the wall in the twilight. An old trestle table, bare of linen, held pewter spoons and her dish; three wretched chairs furnished the multipurpose kitchen-dining room. Above the chimney hung a small mirror, a tinder-box, three glasses, matches, and a large cracked white jug. In spite of the gloom, the floor, utensils, and fireplace testified to cleanliness and thrift.The old woman’s pale, withered face matched the street’s darkness and mustiness. Sitting motionless, as inseparable from the house as a snail from its shell, her countenance—alert with a vague mischief—was framed in a net cap that barely covered white hair; her fine gray eyes were quiet as the street, and wrinkles ran like cracks in the walls. Whether born to poverty or fallen from better days, she seemed resigned to melancholy.From sunrise till dark, except when cooking or buying provisions, she sat by the farther window opposite a young girl. At any hour a passer-by could see the needlewoman in an old red velvet chair bending over an embroidery frame, stitching indefatigably. The mother had a green pillow and busied herself with handmade net, but with feeble sight; an antiquated spring-grip pair of spectacles rested on her nose. By night they set a lamp between them, its light concentrated by two globe-shaped water bottles, letting the elder see the network on her pillow and the younger the pattern’s delicate details.The window’s outward bend held a box of earth on the sill where sweet-peas, nasturtiums, a sickly honeysuckle, and convolvulus twined up the iron bars. These etiolated plants bore a few pale flowers, adding sadness and sweetness to the framed picture of the two figures.Even the most selfish observer would carry away a perfect image of working-class Parisian women’s lives: the embroideress evidently lived by her needle. Many passing through the turnstile wondered how a girl could keep her color living in a cellar. A student crossing to the Quartier Latin might liken this vegetative existence to ivy clinging to chill walls, or to peasants who toil and die unknown. A house-owner, valuing the property, would ask, “What will become of those two women if embroidery goes out of fashion?”Among the men obliged to pass this street for the Hôtel de Ville or Palais de Justice, some might have been charitable. A widower or an Adonis of forty, often glimpsing these sad lives, might reckon on the mother and daughter’s poverty and hope to make the innocent work-woman his at little cost—her nimble fingers, youthful figure, and pale skin (a charm due to the sunless street) drawing admiration. Perhaps an honest clerk with twelve hundred francs a year, seeing diligence and purity, waited only for better prospects to unite his life to hers, to bring a man’s arm and calm affection, pale like the window flowers, to uphold the home.Vague hope kept life in the mother’s dim gray eyes. Each morning after a frugal breakfast she took up her pillow—chiefly for show—laid her spectacles on a little mahogany worktable as old as herself, and watched from about half-past eight till ten the regular passers, noting gait, dress, and countenance as if trying to attract attention to her daughter. This small review was as good as a play to her, perhaps her sole amusement.The daughter rarely looked up. Modesty, or the painful consciousness of poverty, kept her eyes fixed on the frame; only some exclamation from her mother showed her small features. Then a clerk in a new coat or one appearing with a woman might catch sight of the girl’s slightly upturned nose, rosy mouth, and gray eyes, bright despite toil. Late hours left pale circles beneath each eye on her fresh cheeks.The child seemed made for love and cheerfulness: love had drawn two perfect arches above her eyelids and given her a mass of chestnut hair she might hide under like a tent; cheerfulness widened her nostrils into quivering animation, carved dimples in her cheeks, and made her quick to forget troubles—cheerfulness, the blossom of hope, giving strength to face life’s barren path.Her hair was always neatly dressed. As Paris needlewomen, she thought her toilet complete after brushing smooth and tucking the short curls at each temple against her white skin. The pretty growth at the nape and a clear brown line suggested youth and charm; an observer seeing her bent over work might deem her a coquette. This promise caught more than one young man’s eye, who turned in vain hope to glimpse that modest countenance.“Caroline, there is a new face that passes regularly by, and not one of the old ones to compare with it.”These words, spoken in a low voice by her mother one August morning in 1815, vanquished the young needlewoman’s indifference, and she looked out; the stranger was gone.“Where has he flown to?” she asked.“He will come back no doubt at four; I shall see him coming, and will touch your foot with mine. I am sure he will come back; he has been through the street regularly for the last three days; but his hours vary. The first day he came by at six o’clock, the day before yesterday it was four, yesterday as early as three. I remember seeing him occasionally some time ago. He is some clerk in the Préfet’s office who has moved to the Marais.—Why!” she exclaimed after glancing down the street, “our gentleman of the brown coat has taken to wearing a wig; how much it alters him!”The gentleman of the brown coat seemed to close the daily procession; the old woman put on her spectacles and took up her work with a sigh, glancing at her daughter with a look that mingled admiration, gratitude, hope, and pride in such a pretty child.At about four the old lady pushed her foot against Caroline’s, and the girl looked up quickly enough to see a new actor, whose regular advent would thenceforth lend variety to the scene. He was tall and thin, wore black, a man of about forty, with a certain solemnity; as his piercing hazel eye met the old woman’s dull gaze, he made her quake, for she felt as though he had the gift of reading hearts, or had much practice in it, and his presence seemed as icy as the air of this dank street. Was the dull, sallow complexion of that ominous face due to excess of work, or to delicate health?The old woman supplied many answers; but next day Caroline discerned the lines of long mental suffering on a brow quick to frown. His hollow cheeks bore sorrow’s seal. Though the girl’s expression at first was one of lively but innocent curiosity, it took on gentle sympathy as the stranger receded like a last relation following a funeral.The weather was so hot and the gentleman so absent-minded that he had taken off and forgotten his hat as he passed the squalid street. Caroline noted how his brushed hair altered his countenance. The strong, uncharming impression he made was unlike any other passenger; for the first time she felt pity for someone besides herself and her mother. She said nothing to her mother’s absurd conjectures, drew her needle in silence, and only regretted not seeing him more closely, looking forward to the morrow to form a definite opinion.Indeed it was the first time a passer-by had stirred much thought in her. She usually replied to her mother’s hypotheses with a smile; the old woman eyed every passer as a potential protector. If these crude suggestions did not provoke evil thoughts, Caroline’s indifference had been borne by the persistent toil consuming her youth and threatening the clearness of her eyes and the bloom of her cheeks.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For two months the “Black Gentleman”—as they called him—moved erratically; he did not always pass down the Rue du Tourniquet at the same hour. The old woman sometimes saw him in the evening when he had not come in the morning; he lacked the punctuality of the clerks who served Madame Crochard. Except on the first occasion, when his glance had alarmed the old mother and his eyes rested on the odd picture of the two female gnomes in the window, he seemed lost in thought, looking at the ground or straight ahead as if reading the future in the fog of the Rue du Tourniquet. There were, besides two carriage-gates and a dark ironmonger’s, only barred windows lighting staircases, so his lack of curiosity could not be blamed on rivals; Madame Crochard was piqued to see her “Black Gentleman” always absorbed. One mid-September morning, however, Caroline Crochard’s lively face, fresh among belated flowers and faded leaves, gleamed against the room’s dark background; the daily scene brightened with contrasts and the pretty needlewoman at work. The stranger, perhaps roused by the clatter her irritated mother made with bobbins, glanced up and studied this living picture.Their eyes met briefly but meaningfully; both knew they would think of each other. When he passed again at four, Caroline heard his step and they looked steadily at one another with evident purpose; his kind expression made him smile, and Caroline blushed, which Madame Crochard noted with satisfaction. After that memorable afternoon the Gentleman in Black passed twice daily with rare exceptions, observed by mother and daughter. From his irregular hours they judged he was not a precise subordinate.Through the first three winter months they saw each other twice a day for the few moments it took him to cross the stretch by their door and windows. Day by day the brief meetings took on a friendly, then fraternal, tone. They seemed to understand each other at once, and by studying each other’s faces learned to know them well. It became, as if, a duty of the Unknown to visit Caroline; if he failed to give the half-smile or cordial glance she expected, her day was marred—like an old man who must read his paper each morning. The charm of these meetings was mutual; neither could hide vexation or fatigue from the other.“He must have had trouble yesterday,” Caroline would think when his features altered. “Oh, he has been working too hard!” she decided from another shade. He could tell when she had spent Sunday finishing a dress, when the quarter-day approached and her face darkened, when she had sat up late, and he marked how the gloom that dimmed her delicate features slowly lifted as their acquaintance warmed. When winter killed the window plants and the window was closed, he noted the concentration of light at Caroline’s head, the small fire and the frosty redness of the two women’s faces showing the home’s poverty; if his face ever showed pity, the girl met it with assumed cheerfulness.Yet their feelings remained unspoken; no incident revealed how deep they were in each other. They had never heard each other speak, and both kept at a distance as if closer acquaintance might bring disaster. Was it shyness or friendship that restrained them? Fear of encountering selfishness, or the city’s mutual distrust? Each perhaps hoped to preserve an illusion: he seeming to fear a vulgar word from lips as fresh as a flower, she feeling unworthy of a mysterious person who evidently had power and wealth.Madame Crochard, reproachful at her daughter’s indecision, now showed sulkiness to the “Black Gentleman,” on whom until then she had smiled with benignant servility. She complained of being forced to cook at her age, of catarrh and rheumatism, and could not promise the same number of ells of net as before.By late December, when bread was dear and the 1816 corn shortage began to bite hard, the stranger saw secret sorrow in Caroline’s face which his kind smiles could not dispel. He noticed the dimness in her eyes from long hours at night. One night near month’s end he passed the Rue du Tourniquet at one in the morning. In the silence he heard Madame Crochard’s lachrymose voice and Caroline’s sadder tone mingling with sleet. He crept along slowly and, risking trouble with the police, stopped beneath the window to listen through the largest hole in the yellow muslin curtain. He saw a sheet of paper on the table by the lamp and water-filled globes—a writ. Madame Crochard wept; Caroline’s voice was thick.“Why be so heartbroken, mother? Monsieur Molineux will not sell us up or turn us out before I have finished this dress; only two nights more and I shall take it home to Madame Roguin.”“And supposing she keeps you waiting as usual? And will the money for the gown pay the baker too?”The stranger, versed in reading faces, fancied the mother’s grief false while the daughter’s was genuine; he turned away, came back, and peeped again. Madame Crochard lay in bed; Caroline bent over her frame, embroidering with indefatigable diligence. On the table with the writ lay a triangular hunch of bread, placed to sustain her through the night and remind her of the reward of industry. Tremulous with pity, the stranger slipped his purse through a cracked pane so it would fall at the girl’s feet, then fled with tingling cheeks.Next morning the shy, melancholy stranger passed with a preoccupied look, but could not avoid Caroline’s gratitude: she had opened her window and pretended to dig in the snow-buried window-box so he would not see her only through the glass. Her eyes brimmed with tears as she bowed, saying with a look, “I can only repay you from my heart.”The Gentleman in Black affected not to understand this gratitude. That evening, when he passed, Caroline mended the window with a sheet of paper and smiled, showing her pearly teeth like a promise. Thenceforth the Stranger went another way and was no more seen in the Rue du Tourniquet.One early May day, as Caroline watered a honeysuckle root, she caught sight of a narrow strip of cloudless blue and said gaily to her mother, “Mamma, we must go tomorrow for a trip to Montmorency!” She had hardly spoken when the Gentleman in Black passed, sadder than ever. Caroline’s innocent look might have seemed an invitation. Indeed, the next day, at the corner of the Rue du Faubourg Saint-Denis and the Rue d’Enghien, Madame Crochard, dressed in a claret merino pelisse, silk bonnet, and striped shawl, found her Unknown standing like a man waiting for his wife. A smile lit his face at sight of Caroline in plum-colored gaiters and a white dress tossed by a breeze that showed her graceful form. Her rice-straw bonnet lined with pink silk shaded a forehead white as snow; her hair parted in two brown bands gave an expression of innocence. She seemed lit from heaven; when she saw him, radiant hope brightened her. The Stranger, in doubt perhaps whether to be her escort, had his decision made by her delight. He hired a vehicle for Saint-Leu-Taverny and offered seats beside him. The mother accepted, but soon complained of the heat and her cough, making civil speeches about being an inconvenience. By Saint-Denis she seemed fast asleep; her snores seemed dubious to the Stranger, who frowned suspiciously.“Oh, she is fast asleep,” Caroline said guilelessly; “she never ceased coughing all night. She must be very tired.”Her companion said nothing but looked at the girl with a smile that seemed to mean, “Poor child, you little know your mother!”Despite distrust, as they passed the poplar avenue toward Eaubonne, the Stranger, perhaps loosened by the bright sky, pure country air, and the first green scents, entered on a conversation with his young companion as aimless as the branches’ sway, as devious as butterflies in the azure air, illogical as the fields’ murmur, and full of a mysterious love. At that season the country is like a bride in her marriage robe; no heart remains unmoved on leaving the Marais for Montmorency’s smiling valley, lifting a charmed gaze to eyes as infinite and loving as the view.He found Caroline sprightly rather than witty, affectionate but ill educated; her giddy laugh hid genuine feeling. Under his shrewd questioning she spoke with the effusiveness of the lower classes, not guarded as people of the world. Her frankness brightened his face, renewing a youthful look beneath former sadness. Gradually his sternness softened to a handsome youthfulness that made Caroline proud and happy. Her light prattle lifted the last veil from his real youth and character; he seemed to relinquish ideas that haunted him and to show the liveliness beneath his solemnity.Their talk grew so intimate that by the time the carriage stopped at the straggling houses of Saint-Leu, Caroline was calling him Monsieur Roger. Then, for the first time, the old mother awoke.“Caroline, she has heard everything!” Roger whispered suspiciously.Caroline replied with an exquisite smile of disbelief, dispelling the cloud his fear of some plot had caused. Madame Crochard, amazed at nothing, approved everything and followed them into the park, where the young pair wandered through meadows and fragrant copses famed by Queen Hortense.“How lovely!” Caroline cried when, on the green ridge where Montmorency’s forest begins, she saw the valley: combes sheltering villages, a horizon of blue hills, church towers, meadows sending a murmur like the ocean. They walked beside an artificial stream to the Swiss valley and its chalet once sheltering Hortense and Napoleon. Caroline sat with pious reverence on the mossy bench where emperors had rested. Madame Crochard wished to see a rustic bridge and left them, saying she would not go out of sight.“What, poor child!” Roger asked, “have you never longed for wealth and luxury? Have you never wished to wear the beautiful dresses you embroider?”“It would not be truth,” Caroline answered, “if I said I never think how happy the rich must be. Oh yes! I often fancy, especially as I fall asleep, how glad I should be to see my poor mother no longer compelled to go out, whatever the weather, to buy provisions at her age. I should like her to have a servant to bring her coffee sweetened with white sugar, and to see her wearing out her eyes over her favorite novels rather than twisting bobbins. And a little good wine. In short, I should like her comfortable—she is so good.”“Then she has shown you great kindness?”“Oh yes,” the girl said with conviction, then paused as they watched Madame Crochard shaking her finger at them from the bridge. Caroline added, “She sold her last silver forks to apprentice me to the old maid who taught me to embroider. And my poor father! What did she not go through to make him end his days in happiness!” She shivered at remembrance and hid her face; “Well! come! let us forget past sorrows!” she rallied, blushing when Roger too seemed moved but daring not to look at him.“What was your father?” he inquired.“He was an opera-dancer before the Revolution,” she replied simply, “and my mother sang in the chorus. My father, who led stage figures, was present at the siege of the Bastille. Recognized by some assailants, who asked whether he could lead a real attack, since he led on stage, he accepted, led the insurgents, and was rewarded with the rank of captain in the Sambre-et-Meuse army, where he rose to colonel. But at Lützen he was so badly wounded that, after a year’s suffering, he died in Paris. The Bourbons returned; my mother got no pension, and we sank into such misery that we had to work. She has been ailing, poor dear, and I have never known her so little resigned; she complains much, and I cannot wonder, for she once knew an easy life. I cannot pine for delights I have never known; I have but one wish.”“And that is?” Roger asked eagerly.“That women may continue to wear embroidered net dresses, so I may never lack work.”Her frankness touched him and softened his look toward Madame Crochard as she slowly returned.“Well, children, have you had a long talk?” the old woman asked with half-laughing indulgence. “When I think, Monsieur Roger, that the ‘little Corporal’ has sat where you are sitting,” she went on, “Poor man! how my husband worshiped him! Ah! Crochard did well to die; he could not have borne to think of him where they have sent him!”Roger put his finger to his lips; the good woman went on very gravely, unhooking a bit of her bodice to show a ribbon and cross tied round her neck by black ribbon, “they shall never hinder me from wearing what he gave to my poor Crochard, and I will have it buried with me.”At that seditious speech, Roger suddenly rose, and they returned to the village through park walks. He left briefly to order a meal at the best Taverny eating-house, then returned to fetch them and led them through the forest alleys.Dinner was cheerful. Roger was no longer the melancholy figure of the Rue du Tourniquet but a confiding young man ready to take life as it came, like the two hardworking women who might lack bread the next day; his smile was affectionate and childlike.After the meal ended with a few glasses of champagne, Roger proposed they join the village ball under the chestnuts, where he and Caroline danced together. Their hands met sympathetically, their hearts beat with the same hopes, and under the blue sky and slanting, rosy sunset beams, their eyes sparkled with fires that to them made heaven pale. The power of an idea and desire seemed boundless; nothing seemed impossible. In such moments the soul foresees nothing but happiness. The day had created memories neither had known before. Would the source prove more beautiful than the river, the desire more enchanting than its fulfilment?“So the day is already at an end!” Caroline sighed when the dance finished, noticing Roger’s face shade into sadness.“Why should you not be as happy in Paris as here?” she asked. “Is happiness only found at Saint-Leu? I think I can never be unhappy anywhere.”Roger, struck by her unguarded words, felt what she did; for the first time since their first glance, they shared the same idea. Frightened by the mutual silence, they went to the carriage. Before entering, they playfully took hands and ran down the dark avenue in front of Madame Crochard. When they could no longer see the white net cap, Roger, in a tremulous voice, said, “Caroline!”Startled, the girl drew back a few steps, understanding the invitation; she held out her hand, which he passionately kissed, then she withdrew it quickly, standing on tiptoe to see her mother.Madame Crochard affected blindness, playing the supernumerary, as if by a remnant of her old stage parts.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The adventures of these two young people were not continued in the Rue du Tourniquet. To see Roger and Caroline again we must leap into modern Paris, where newly built houses contain apartments seemingly made for newlyweds on their honeymoon. There the paper and paint are as fresh as the couple, decorations in blossom like their love; everything suits youthful notions and ardent wishes.Halfway down the Rue Taitbout, in a house whose stone walls were still white, where the hall columns and doorway were spotless and the inner walls shone with the neat painting our recent intimacy with English ways had brought into fashion, there was, on the second floor, a small set of rooms fitted as though the architect had known their use. A simple airy ante-room, with a stucco dado, opened into a combined drawing- and dining-room. From the drawing-room came a pretty bedroom, with a bathroom beyond. Every chimney-shelf bore a fine mirror elegantly framed; doors were crowded with tasteful arabesques and the cornices were in the best style. Any amateur would have discerned the sense of distinction and decorative fitness that marks modern French architects’ work.For more than a month Caroline had lived in this apartment, furnished by an upholsterer guided by an artist. A short description of the principal room will show the pleasures Caroline enjoyed when Roger installed her. Gray hangings trimmed with green silk adorned her bedroom; seats covered with light-colored wool sateen were easy and fashionable; a simple inlaid chest of drawers held toilet treasures; a matching writing-table served for love-letters on scented paper; an antique-draped bed, with elegant muslin hangings, suggested thoughts of love; the window curtains, drab silk with green fringe, were half drawn to subdue the light; a bronze clock represented Love crowning Psyche; and a Gothic-carpeted red ground set off the rest. A small dressing-table before a long glass was where the needlewoman sat, out of patience with Plaisir, the famous hairdresser.“Do you think you will have done today?” she asked.“Your hair is so long and so thick, madame,” replied Plaisir.Caroline smiled. The man’s flattery doubtless revived in her the memory of the praises Roger lavished on the beauty of her hair.When the hairdresser finished, a waiting-maid came to counsel her about the dress Roger would like best. It was the beginning of September 1816 and cold; she chose a green grenadine trimmed with chinchilla. Dressed, Caroline flew into the drawing-room and opened a window, then stepped onto the elegant balcony that adorned the front. There she stood, arms crossed, in a charming attitude—not to display herself to passersby but to look out on the Boulevard at the foot of the Rue Taitbout. This side view let her catch glimpses of elegant carriages and crowds swept past like silhouettes. Not knowing whether Roger would arrive in a carriage or on foot, she watched both pedestrians and tilburies—the light cabs introduced by the English.After a quarter of an hour, neither her keen eye nor her heart had announced him. Scorn and love swept over her face; disdain and indifference for all others showed in her features as if the passersby were ants. Her gray eyes, bright with fun, now flamed. Given over to passion, she avoided admiration as much as some cultivate it; she cared for no eyes below her, only one face and one thought. When the spotted head of a certain bay horse crossed the strip between the rows of houses, Caroline shivered and stood on tiptoe, hoping to recognize the white traces and the tilbury’s color. It was he.Roger turned the corner, saw the balcony, whipped his horse to a gallop, and stopped at the bronze-green door he knew as well as his master did. The maid, having heard her mistress’ exclamation, opened at once. Roger rushed up, clasped Caroline to him, and embraced her with the effusive feeling natural to lovers who rarely meet. They went, by common impulse and with arms about each other, into the quiet fragrant bedroom; a settee stood ready by the fire. For a moment they looked at each other in silence, expressing happiness by clasped hands and a fond gaze.“Yes, it is he!” she said at last. “Yes, it is you. Do you know, I have not seen you for three long days—an age!—But what is the matter? You are unhappy.”“My poor Caroline—”“There, you see! ‘poor Caroline’—”“No, no, do not laugh, my darling; we cannot go to the Feydeau Theatre together this evening.”Caroline pouted briefly, then the pout vanished.“How absurd I am! How can I think of the play when I see you? Is not the sight of you the only spectacle I care for?” she cried, pushing her fingers through Roger’s hair.“I am obliged to go to the Attorney-General’s. We have a knotty case. He met me in the great hall at the Palais; and as I am to plead, he asked me to dine with him. But, my dearest, you can go to the theatre with your mother, and I will join you if the meeting breaks up early.”“To the theatre without you!” she cried in amazement; “enjoy any pleasure you do not share! O my Roger! you do not deserve a kiss,” she added, throwing her arms round his neck with artless passion.“Caroline, I must go home and dress. The Marais is some way off, and I still have business to finish.”“Take care what you are saying, monsieur,” she interrupted. “My mother says that when a man begins to talk about his business, he is ceasing to love.”“Caroline! Am I not here? Have I not stolen this hour from my pitiless—”“Hush!” she said, laying a finger on his mouth. “Don’t you see I am in jest?”Back in the drawing-room, Roger’s eye fell on an object brought home that morning by the cabinetmaker. Caroline’s old rosewood embroidery frame, by which she and her mother had earned their bread when they lived in the Rue du Tourniquet-Saint-Jean, had been refitted and polished, and a net dress of elaborate design already stretched on it.“Well, then, my dear, I shall do some work this evening. As I stitch, I shall fancy myself gone back to those early days when you used to pass by without a word, though not without a glance; the days when the memory of your look kept me awake all night. Oh my dear old frame—the best piece of furniture in my room, though you did not give it me!” she said, seating herself on Roger’s knees; he, overcome, had dropped into a chair. “Listen. All I can earn by my work I mean to give to the poor. You have made me rich. How I love that pretty home at Bellefeuille, less for what it is than because you gave it me! But tell me, Roger, I should like to call myself Caroline de Bellefeuille—can I? Is it legal or permissible?”At Roger’s small affirmative grimace—for he hated the name Crochard—Caroline jumped for joy and clapped her hands.“I feel,” she said, “as if I should more especially belong to you. Usually a woman gives up her own name and takes her husband’s—” An idea forced itself on her and made her blush. She took Roger’s hand and led him to the open piano. “Listen,” she added, “I can play my sonata now like an angel!” Her fingers were already running over the ivory keys when she felt herself seized round the waist.“Caroline, I ought to be far from hence!”“You insist on going? Well, go,” she said, with a pretty pout, but she smiled as she looked at the clock and exclaimed joyfully, “At any rate, I have detained you a quarter of an hour!”“Goodbye, Mademoiselle de Bellefeuille,” he said, with the gentle irony of love.She kissed him and saw him to the door; when his steps had died on the stairs she ran to the balcony to watch him get into the tilbury, gather up the reins, catch a parting look, hear the crack of his whip and the sound of wheels on the stones, watch the handsome horse, the master’s hat, the tiger’s gold lace, and at last stand gazing long after the dark corner of the street had eclipsed the vision.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Five years after Mademoiselle Caroline de Bellefeuille had taken up her abode in the pretty house in the Rue Taitbout, we again look in on one of those home-scenes which tighten the bonds of affection between two persons who truly love. In the middle of the blue drawing-room, in front of the window opening to the balcony, a little boy of four made a tremendous noise whipping the rocking-horse, whose curved supports did not move fast enough to please him; his pretty face, framed in fair curls that fell over his white collar, smiled up like a cherub’s at his mother when she said from an easy-chair, “Not so much noise, Charles; you will wake your little sister.”The inquisitive boy suddenly got off his horse, and treading on tiptoe as if afraid of the sound of his feet on the carpet, came up with one finger between his little teeth, and standing in one of those childish attitudes that are so graceful because they are so perfectly natural, raised the muslin veil that hid the rosy face of a little girl sleeping on her mother’s knee.“Is Eugenie asleep, then?” he said, quite astonished. “Why is she asleep when we are awake?” he added, looking up with large, liquid black eyes.“That only God can know,” replied Caroline, with a smile.The mother and boy gazed at the infant, only that morning baptized.Caroline, now about four-and-twenty, showed the ripe beauty which had expanded under the influence of cloudless happiness and constant enjoyment. In her the Woman was complete.Delighted to obey her dear Roger’s every wish, she had acquired the accomplishments she had lacked; she played the piano fairly well, and sang sweetly. Ignorant of the customs of a world that would have treated her as an outcast, and which she would not have cared for even if it had welcomed her—for a happy woman does not care for the world—she had not caught the elegance of manner or learned the art of conversation, abounding in words and devoid of ideas, which is current in fashionable drawing-rooms; on the other hand, she worked hard to gain the knowledge indispensable to a mother whose chief ambition is to bring up her children well. Never to lose sight of her boy, to give him from the cradle that training of every minute which impresses on the young a love of all that is good and beautiful, to shelter him from every evil influence and fulfil both the painful duties of a nurse and the tender offices of a mother,—these were her chief pleasures.The coy and gentle being had from the first day so fully resigned herself never to step beyond the enchanted sphere where she found all her happiness, that, after six years of the tenderest intimacy, she still knew her lover only by the name of Roger. A print of the picture of the Psyche lighting her lamp to gaze on Love in spite of his prohibition hung in her room, and constantly reminded her of the conditions of her happiness. Through all these six years her humble pleasures had never importuned Roger by a single indiscreet ambition, and his heart was a treasure-house of kindness. Never had she longed for diamonds or fine clothes, and had again and again refused the luxury of a carriage which he had offered her. To look out from her balcony for Roger’s cab, to go with him to the play or make excursions with him, on fine days in the environs of Paris, to long for him, to see him, and then to long again,—these made up the history of her life, poor in incidents but rich in happiness.As she rocked the infant, now a few months old, on her knee, singing the while, she allowed herself to recall the memories of the past. She lingered more especially on the months of September, when Roger was accustomed to take her to Bellefeuille and spend the delightful days which seem to combine the charms of every season. Nature is equally prodigal of flowers and fruit, the evenings are mild, the mornings bright, and a blaze of summer often returns after a spell of autumn gloom. During the early days of their love, Caroline had ascribed the even mind and gentle temper, of which Roger gave her so many proofs, to the rarity of their always longed-for meetings, and to their mode of life, which did not compel them to be constantly together, as a husband and wife must be. But now she could remember with rapture that, tortured by foolish fears, she had watched him with trembling during their first stay on this little estate in the Gatinais. Vain suspiciousness of love! Each of these months of happiness had passed like a dream in the midst of joys which never rang false. She had always seen that kind creature with a tender smile on his lips, a smile that seemed to mirror her own.As she called up these vivid pictures, her eyes filled with tears; she thought she could not love him enough, and was tempted to regard her ambiguous position as a sort of tax levied by Fate on her love. Finally, invincible curiosity led her to wonder for the thousandth time what events could be that led so tender a heart as Roger’s to find his pleasure in clandestine and illicit happiness. She invented a thousand romances to avoid recognizing the true reason, which she had long suspected but tried not to believe in. She rose, and carrying the baby in her arms, went into the dining-room to superintend the preparations for dinner.It was the 6th of May 1822, the anniversary of the excursion to the Park of Saint-Leu, which had been the turning-point of her life; each year it had been marked by heartfelt rejoicing. Caroline chose the linen to be used, and arranged the dessert. Having attended with joy to these details, which touched Roger, she placed the infant in her pretty cot and went out on to the balcony, whence she presently saw the carriage which her friend, as he grew to riper years, now used instead of the smart tilbury of his youth. After submitting to the first fire of Caroline’s embraces and the kisses of the little rogue who addressed him as papa, Roger went to the cradle, looked at his little sleeping daughter, kissed her forehead, and then took out of his pocket a document covered with black writing.“Caroline,” said he, “here is the marriage portion of Mademoiselle Eugenie de Bellefeuille.”The mother gratefully took the paper, a deed of gift of securities in the State funds.“But why,” said she, “have you given Eugenie three thousand francs a year, and Charles no more than fifteen hundred?”“Charles, my love, will be a man,” replied he. “Fifteen hundred francs are enough for him. With so much for certain, a man of courage is above poverty. And if by chance your son should turn out a nonentity, I do not wish him to be able to play the fool. If he is ambitious, this small income will give him a taste for work.—Eugenie is a girl; she must have a little fortune.”The father then turned to play with his boy, whose effusive affection showed the independence and freedom in which he was brought up. No shyness between the father and child interfered with the charm which rewards a parent for his devotion; and the cheerfulness of the little family was as sweet as it was genuine. In the evening a magic-lantern displayed its illusions and mysterious pictures on a white sheet to Charles’ great surprise, and more than once the innocent child’s heavenly rapture made Caroline and Roger laugh heartily.Later, when the little boy was in bed, the baby woke and craved its limpid nourishment. By the light of a lamp in the chimney corner, Roger enjoyed the scene of peace and comfort, and gave himself up to the happiness of contemplating the sweet picture of the child clinging to Caroline’s white bosom as she sat, as fresh as a newly opened lily, while her hair fell in long brown curls that almost hid her neck. The lamplight enhanced the grace of the young mother, shedding over her, her dress, and the infant, the picturesque effects of strong light and shadow.The calm and silent woman’s face struck Roger as a thousand times sweeter than ever, and he gazed tenderly at the rosy, pouting lips from which no harsh word had ever been heard. The very same thought was legible in Caroline’s eyes as she gave a sidelong look at Roger, either to enjoy the effect she was producing on him, or to see what the end of the evening was to be. He, understanding the meaning of this cunning glance, said with assumed regret, “I must be going. I have a serious case to be finished, and I am expected at home. Duty before all things—don’t you think so, my darling?”Caroline looked him in the face with an expression at once sad and sweet, with the resignation which does not, however, disguise the pangs of a sacrifice.“Good-bye, then,” said she. “Go, for if you stay an hour longer I cannot so lightly bear to set you free.”“My dearest,” said he with a smile, “I have three days’ holiday, and am supposed to be twenty leagues away from Paris.”A few days after this anniversary of the 6th of May, Mademoiselle de Bellefeuille hurried off one morning to the Rue Saint-Louis, in the Marais, only hoping she might not arrive too late at a house where she commonly went once a week.
